topics.title: Computer Storage Technologies - Magnetic Bubble Memories
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST867 - STORAGE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Computer Storage Technologies - Magnetic Bubble Memories

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Magnetic Bubble Memory (MBM)** is a non-volatile, solid-state, sequential-access digital storage device that stores binary data as microscopic cylindrical magnetic domains (called "bubbles") within a thin film of magnetic garnet or orthorhombic ferrite possessing **perpendicular magnetic anisotropy**. Each bubble represents a logical "1" and its absence (or the surrounding matrix) represents a logical "0". The bubbles are manipulated, propagated, replicated, and detected using external in-plane rotating magnetic fields and Permalloy overlay patterns etched on the film surface.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** MBM is classified under *non-volatile solid-state bulk storage* and historically bridges the gap between magnetic core memory and early semiconductor RAM. It is part of the family of **magnetic domain memory devices** (along with Twistor memory and Plated-wire memory).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **horizontal tray of glycerin** in which you have injected a row of small, perfectly round, magnetized **soap bubbles** that refuse to pop. Now place a **spinning magnetic stirrer** beneath the tray — every bubble begins to march forward in a controlled, repeating pattern, like runners on a circular track. Each bubble carries one bit of information, and because bubbles don't disappear when the power is cut, the memory is non-volatile.

| Real-World Object | MBM Equivalent |
|-------------------|----------------|
| Glycerin tray | Thin magnetic garnet film |
| Soap bubbles | Cylindrical magnetic domains |
| Magnetic stirrer | Rotating in-plane magnetic field |
| Track lanes | Permalloy chevron / T-bar patterns |
| Pit-stop reader | Magnetoresistive bubble detector |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Physical Metrics (Kerns-Bobeck 1967 specifications):**
> - **Bubble diameter:** typically **1 μm to 10 μm**
> - **Film thickness:** ~3 μm to 6 μm
> - **Storage density:** up to **1 Mbit/cm²** in later generations
> - **Access time:** **1 ms to 10 ms** (rotational latency)
> - **Data rate:** **100 kbit/s to 10 Mbit/s** (propagation-limited)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle — The Five-Stage Lifecycle of a Bubble

A Magnetic Bubble Memory device performs five fundamental operations on bubbles. KTU examiners frequently test this as a 7-mark sub-question.

**Step 1 — Generation (Bubble Nucleation)**
A small loop of conductor (the *nucleator*) is pulsed with a high current. The localized opposing magnetic field nucleates a new magnetic domain in the film, producing a fresh bubble. This is the WRITE operation.

**Step 2 — Propagation (Movement)**
A rotating in-plane magnetic field (typically 30 Oe to 100 Oe) is generated by two orthogonal Helmholtz coil pairs. A Permalloy (Ni-Fe alloy) overlay pattern — usually **chevrons (V-shapes)**, **T-bars**, or **Y-bars** — creates localized north/south poles that attract and drag the bubble along the propagation track.

**Step 3 — Replication (Read Without Destruction)**
At a *replicator station*, the bubble is stretched and chopped: one copy continues along the propagation track (preserving the stored data), and the other is steered toward the detector. This is what makes MBM a *non-destructive read-out* (NDRO) device.

**Step 4 — Detection (Sensing)**
A *magnetoresistive (MR) sensor* — typically a thin Permalloy strip — detects the change in resistance as a bubble passes beneath it. The resistance change ΔR is converted into a voltage pulse representing a logical "1".

**Step 5 — Annihilation (Erase)**
Unwanted bubbles are destroyed at a *guard rail / annihilator* — a region of strong DC bias that exceeds the bubble's stability threshold, causing it to collapse.

### 2.2 Why Bubbles Stay Bubbles — The Underlying Physics

A bubble is a stable cylindrical magnetic domain because of an equilibrium between:
- **Magnetostatic energy** (wants to shrink the bubble),
- **Domain-wall energy** (wants to expand it),
- **External bias field H_B** (stabilizes cylindrical shape).

The bubble exists only when the bias field lies in the range:

$$H_{collapse} \leq H_B \leq H_{run-out}$$

The characteristic **material length** governing bubble size is given by:

$$l = \frac{\sigma_w}{4\pi M_s^2}$$

where $\sigma_w$ is the domain wall energy per unit area and $M_s$ is the saturation magnetization. The stable bubble diameter scales as:

$$d \approx 8\,l$$

The **collapse field** is empirically:

$$H_{collapse} \approx 0.5 \left( \frac{d}{h} \right) M_s \quad \text{for } d \geq 4h$$

where $h$ is the film thickness.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **all high-yield formulas** for MBM derivations. Memorize units carefully.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Meaning |
|---|----------|---------|-------|---------|
| 1 | Characteristic length | $l = \sigma_w / (4\pi M_s^2)$ | cm | Material property |
| 2 | Stable bubble diameter | $d \approx 8\,l$ | cm | Geometric size of one bit |
| 3 | Film thickness constraint | $d \geq 4h$ | cm | Stability condition |
| 4 | Collapse field | $H_c \approx 0.5(d/h)M_s$ | Oersted | Minimum bias for stability |
| 5 | Storage density (chip) | $\rho = 1 / (2d)^2$ | bits/cm² | Maximum packing (square) |
| 6 | Storage density (hex close-pack) | $\rho = 2 / (\sqrt{3}\,d^2)$ | bits/cm² | Maximum packing (hex) |
| 7 | Bubble propagation velocity | $v = 2\pi r f$ | cm/s | r = track radius, f = rotation freq |
| 8 | Data rate | $R = v / (2d)$ | bits/s | Inverse of bit-cell traversal |
| 9 | Avg. rotational latency | $T_{avg} = 1 / (2f)$ | seconds | Half-rotation access time |
| 10 | Worst-case latency | $T_{max} = 1 / f$ | seconds | Full rotation period |
| 11 | Total chip capacity | $C = A_{chip} \cdot \rho$ | bits | A_chip in cm² |
| 12 | Energy per bit (order) | $E_b \sim 10^{-9}$ J/bit | Joules | Comparable to EAROM |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

MBMs were deployed in **ruggedized industrial controllers**, **portable military data terminals**, **avionics black boxes** (1970s–1980s), and **early point-of-sale terminals** because of their **shock resistance, radiation hardness, and non-volatility without battery backup**. They were eventually displaced by **flash memory** in the 1990s due to the latter's higher density and random-access capability.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Trade-off Principle:** MBM offered *non-volatility + moderate speed* but lost to *DRAM (speed)* and *HDD (capacity)*. Modern descendants include **racetrack memory** (IBM, 2008), which is essentially a 3-D nano-scaled reinvention of MBM.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Storage Density of an MBM Chip

**Problem Statement:** A Magnetic Bubble Memory uses a garnet film of characteristic length $l = 0.5 \times 10^{-4}$ cm. Calculate (a) the bubble diameter, (b) the maximum storage density using square packing, and (c) the chip capacity for a 1 cm × 1 cm active area.

#### Step 1 — Compute the bubble diameter

We use the relationship $d = 8l$.

$$d = 8 \times l = 8 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ cm}$$

$$d = 4 \times 10^{-4} \text{ cm} = 4 \,\mu\text{m}$$

#### Step 2 — Square-packing storage density

In square packing, each bubble occupies a square cell of side $2d$ (so adjacent bubbles do not touch). Therefore:

$$\rho_{sq} = \frac{1}{(2d)^2} = \frac{1}{4d^2}$$

Substitute $d = 4 \times 10^{-4}$ cm:

$$\rho_{sq} = \frac{1}{4 \times (4 \times 10^{-4})^2} = \frac{1}{4 \times 16 \times 10^{-8}} = \frac{1}{64 \times 10^{-8}}$$

$$\rho_{sq} = 1.5625 \times 10^{6} \text{ bits/cm}^2 \approx 1.56 \text{ Mbit/cm}^2$$

#### Step 3 — Chip capacity

For $A_{chip} = 1 \text{ cm} \times 1 \text{ cm} = 1 \text{ cm}^2$:

$$C = A_{chip} \times \rho_{sq} = 1 \times 1.5625 \times 10^{6} = 1.5625 \times 10^{6} \text{ bits}$$

$$C \approx 195 \text{ kB} \quad (\text{using } 1 \text{ kB} = 1024 \times 8 \text{ bits})$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Data Rate of a Bubble Stream

**Problem Statement:** A bubble memory chip uses a rotating field of frequency $f = 100 \text{ kHz}$ driving bubbles around a circular track of radius $r = 50 \,\mu\text{m}$. Compute (a) the linear propagation velocity and (b) the maximum data rate. Take $d = 4 \,\mu\text{m}$.

#### Step 1 — Linear velocity of the bubble

Each bubble moves along a circular track of circumference $2\pi r$ once per rotation. So:

$$v = 2\pi r f$$

$$v = 2 \pi \times 50 \times 10^{-4} \text{ cm} \times 100 \times 10^{3} \text{ Hz}$$

$$v = 2 \pi \times 50 \times 10^{-4} \times 10^{5} = 2\pi \times 5 \text{ cm/s}$$

$$v \approx 31.42 \text{ cm/s}$$

#### Step 2 — Minimum center-to-center spacing

Bubbles must remain separated by at least $2d$ to remain individually addressable:

$$s_{min} = 2d = 2 \times 4 \times 10^{-4} \text{ cm} = 8 \times 10^{-4} \text{ cm}$$

#### Step 3 — Maximum data rate

The data rate is the velocity divided by the bubble-to-bubble spacing:

$$R_{max} = \frac{v}{s_{min}} = \frac{v}{2d} = \frac{31.42}{8 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$R_{max} \approx 3.93 \times 10^{4} \text{ bits/s} \approx 39.3 \text{ kbit/s}$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Average Access Time in a Major-Minor Loop Organization

**Problem Statement:** A bubble memory has 256 minor loops, each containing 1024 bubble positions, driven at a rotation frequency $f = 200 \text{ kHz}$. Compute the average access time.

#### Step 1 — Period of one rotation

$$T = \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{200 \times 10^3} = 5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} = 5 \,\mu\text{s}$$

#### Step 2 — Average rotational latency

A random bubble position is, on average, half a rotation away:

$$T_{avg} = \frac{T}{2} = \frac{5 \,\mu\text{s}}{2} = 2.5 \,\mu\text{s}$$

#### Step 3 — Why is MBM still considered slow?

Although the *rotational* latency of a single minor loop is short, real-world MBMs use **serial block transfer** between a *major loop* and *minor loops* to allow re-ordering, which can extend effective access to **~1 ms to 10 ms** for full block retrieval — a key reason MBMs lost to semiconductor RAM for main memory.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Performance Comparison Table

| Parameter | MBM | DRAM | Hard Disk | Flash |
|-----------|-----|------|-----------|-------|
| Access time | 1 – 10 ms | 50 – 100 ns | 5 – 15 ms | 25 – 100 μs |
| Volatility | Non-volatile | Volatile | Non-volatile | Non-volatile |
| Density (typical) | 1 Mbit/cm² | 1 Gbit/cm² | 100 Gbit/cm² | 1 Gbit/cm² |
| Shock resistance | High | Low | Low | Moderate |
| Power (idle) | ~10 mW | ~1 W | ~5 W | 0 |
| Bit cost (1985) | High | Medium | Low | N/A |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 MBM Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Host Controller Bus Interface] --> B[Function Timing Unit FTU]
    B --> C1[X-Coil Driver 30 Oe]
    B --> C2[Y-Coil Driver 30 Oe]
    C1 --> D[Rotating In-Plane H Field]
    C2 --> D
    D --> E[Permalloy Chevron Track on Garnet Film]
    E --> F1[Bubble Generator Nucleator]
    E --> F2[Major Loop Replicator + Detector]
    E --> F3[Minor Loops Storage 256 x 1024 bubbles]
    E --> F4[Bubble Annihilator Guard Rail]
    F1 --> E
    F3 --> E
    F2 --> G[Magnetoresistive Sensor Read Amplifier]
    G --> H[Output Register to Host]
    F4 --> I[Ground Re-cycle]
    H --> A
```

**Reading the diagram (top-to-bottom data flow):**
1. The host writes through a bus into the **Function Timing Unit (FTU)**, which is essentially the on-chip microcontroller for the MBM.
2. The FTU drives two orthogonal coils (**X-coil** and **Y-coil**) to produce a **rotating in-plane field** at typically 50–500 kHz.
3. This rotating field interacts with the **Permalloy overlay** (chevrons/T-bars), dragging the bubbles through the storage area.
4. Bubbles are written via the **generator (nucleator)**, stored in **minor loops**, replicated at the **major-loop replicator**, and detected by the **magnetoresistive (MR) sensor**.

### 4.2 MBM Major-Minor Loop Topology (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Storage[Storage Area on Garnet Film]
        ML[Major Loop - Single closed track]
        mL1[Minor Loop 1 - 1024 bubble sites]
        mL2[Minor Loop 2 - 1024 bubble sites]
        mL3[Minor Loop N - 1024 bubble sites]
    end
    Gen[Bubble Generator] --> ML
    ML --> Rep[Replicator Copy]
    Rep --> Det[MR Detector Read Out]
    Rep --> ML
    ML --> Ann[Annihilator Erase]
    ML <-->|Swap blocks| mL1
    ML <-->|Swap blocks| mL2
    ML <-->|Swap blocks| mL3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Major-Minor?** Storing all bubbles in a single loop would mean data must pass through the detector for *every read*, destroying it (DRO). The major-minor organization allows **block-oriented random access** while preserving NDRO behaviour.

### 4.3 Permalloy Overlay Propagation Pattern (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[In-plane H field rotates 360 deg] --> B1[Step 0 deg - Permalloy forms north poles at chevron tips]
    B1 --> B2[Step 90 deg - Permalloy polarity flips, bubble is attracted to new tips]
    B2 --> B3[Step 180 deg - Bubble has moved 1 bit-cell along track]
    B3 --> B4[Step 270 deg - Bubble reaches next stable position]
    B4 --> B5[Step 360 deg - One full bit shift complete]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022] Define Magnetic Bubble Memory. List any four of its salient features.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 for definition, 0.5 each for 4 features):**

A **Magnetic Bubble Memory (MBM)** is a non-volatile solid-state storage device in which binary data is stored as cylindrical magnetic domains (bubbles) within a thin film of magnetic garnet material, and the bubbles are manipulated using rotating in-plane magnetic fields and Permalloy overlay patterns.

**Salient features:**
1. **Non-volatile** — data persists without power.
2. **Solid-state** — no moving mechanical parts, hence high shock resistance.
3. **High bit density** — up to 1 Mbit/cm² in late-generation devices.
4. **Non-destructive read-out (NDRO)** — replication preserves original data.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Many students write "MBM uses magnetic cores" — this is **WRONG**. MBM uses a *garnet thin film*, not ferrite cores. You will lose 1 mark immediately.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2023] Explain the role of the Permalloy overlay pattern in Magnetic Bubble Memory.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Permalloy overlay** is a thin film of nickel-iron (Ni-Fe) alloy patterned on top of the garnet film in shapes such as **chevrons, T-bars, or Y-bars**. When the external in-plane magnetic field rotates, the Permalloy pattern develops local north/south magnetic poles at its tips. These localized poles attract the bubble, dragging it along the propagation track one bit-cell at a time per field rotation. Thus, the Permalloy overlay acts as the **propagation medium** that converts rotating field into linear bubble motion.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Mnemonic:** "**P**ermalloy **P**ropagates, **P**atterns **P**ush" — three P's to recall.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice Pattern)

---

## Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 marks]** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **organization of a major-minor loop Magnetic Bubble Memory** and describe how data is read and written.

**(b) [7 marks]** Derive the **expressions for storage density and data rate** of an MBM chip. Given $d = 5 \,\mu\text{m}$ and rotation frequency $f = 50$ kHz at a track radius $r = 75 \,\mu\text{m}$, compute the data rate in kbit/s.

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### (a) Major-Minor Loop Organization [7 marks]

The major-minor loop architecture is the most widely used topology in commercial MBMs (e.g., Intel 7110, 1 Mbit). The structure is shown in the block diagram below:

**Block Diagram (recap from Section 4.2):**

The MBM chip consists of:
1. **One Major (transfer) loop** — a single closed track running along the periphery of the storage area.
2. **Many Minor loops** — typically 256, 512, or 1024, each parallel to the major loop and connected to it through a *transfer-in gate* and a *transfer-out gate*.
3. **Bubble Generator (Nucleator)** — located at one end of the major loop, where WRITE occurs.
4. **Replicator** — at the other end, which produces a copy of the bubble for the detector.
5. **MR Detector + Annihilator** — output side.

**WRITE operation (2 marks):**
- The host sends data serially to the **bubble generator**, which nucleates bubbles in the major loop.
- The rotating field propagates these bubbles along the major loop.
- When the bubbles are aligned with the *transfer-in gate* of a target minor loop, a current pulse in the gate transfers (swaps) a block of bubbles from major to the selected minor loop.
- The minor loop now stores the block persistently.

**READ operation (2 marks):**
- A current pulse in the *transfer-out gate* of the selected minor loop shifts a block of bubbles back into the major loop.
- The major loop carries them to the **replicator**, which copies and sends them to the MR detector.
- The original block continues circulating in the minor loop, achieving **NDRO**.

**Why this is advantageous (1 mark):**
The major-minor organization enables **block-oriented random access** with a small on-chip controller (FTU), reducing the number of external pins and simplifying the addressing logic.

#### (b) Derivation of Storage Density and Data Rate [7 marks]

**Storage density (3 marks derivation + 1 mark substitution):**

In square packing, each bubble occupies a cell of side $2d$, hence:

$$\rho_{sq} = \frac{1}{(2d)^2} = \frac{1}{4d^2}$$

Substituting $d = 5 \times 10^{-4}$ cm:

$$\rho_{sq} = \frac{1}{4 \times 25 \times 10^{-8}} = \frac{1}{10^{-6}} = 10^{6} \text{ bits/cm}^2 = 1 \text{ Mbit/cm}^2$$

**Data rate (3 marks derivation + 1 mark substitution):**

The bubble propagation velocity is:

$$v = 2\pi r f$$

$$v = 2\pi \times 75 \times 10^{-4} \text{ cm} \times 50 \times 10^{3} \text{ Hz}$$

$$v = 2\pi \times 75 \times 10^{-4} \times 5 \times 10^{4} = 2\pi \times 375 = 2356.2 \text{ cm/s}$$

The minimum bubble spacing is $2d = 10 \times 10^{-4}$ cm, so:

$$R = \frac{v}{2d} = \frac{2356.2}{10 \times 10^{-4}} = 2.3562 \times 10^{6} \text{ bits/s}$$

$$\boxed{R \approx 2.36 \text{ Mbit/s} = 2356 \text{ kbit/s}}$$

---

## Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **principle of operation of Magnetic Bubble Memory** with the help of a functional block diagram. Clearly describe the function of the **bias field** and the **rotating in-plane field**.

**(b) [7 marks]** Compare Magnetic Bubble Memory with **Semiconductor RAM** and **Magnetic Core Memory** along the dimensions of volatility, access time, density, and cost per bit. State **two reasons why MBMs are no longer in commercial production**.

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### (a) Principle of Operation [7 marks]

Magnetic Bubble Memory operates on the principle of **controllable movement of cylindrical magnetic domains (bubbles)** within a thin film that has **perpendicular magnetic anisotropy**.

**Functional block diagram (recap from Section 4.1):**

The system has three field sources and four functional blocks:

**Three Field Sources (3 marks):**
1. **Permanent magnet / DC bias coil** — produces a constant **bias field $H_B$ perpendicular to the film**. This bias field controls bubble size and stability. If $H_B$ is too low, bubbles elongate into stripes ("run-out"); if too high, they collapse. The operating range is:
   $$H_{collapse} \leq H_B \leq H_{run-out}$$
2. **X-coil and Y-coil pair** — two orthogonal Helmholtz coils driven by 90°-phase-shifted sinusoidal currents produce a **rotating in-plane field** $\vec{H}_{rot}$ of magnitude 30–100 Oe. This rotating field is the *clock* of the MBM.
3. **Pulse coils (transfer gates)** — produce localized current pulses that switch bubbles between major and minor loops.

**Four Functional Blocks (4 marks):**
1. **Bubble generator (nucleator)** — converts a current pulse into a fresh bubble.
2. **Permalloy propagation track** — converts the rotating field into linear bubble motion via induced local poles.
3. **Replicator** — splits one bubble into two, one for detection and one for continued storage (NDRO).
4. **Magnetoresistive (MR) detector** — converts the magnetic field of a passing bubble into a voltage pulse.

The **bias field** is essential because it determines bubble size and ensures that only a fixed number of stable bubbles exist in the film at any time. The **rotating in-plane field** is the "clock" that propagates bubbles around the track synchronously.

#### (b) Comparison and Decline of MBM [7 marks]

| Parameter | MBM | Semiconductor RAM (DRAM) | Magnetic Core |
|-----------|-----|--------------------------|---------------|
| Volatility | **Non-volatile** | Volatile (needs refresh) | Non-volatile |
| Access time | 1 – 10 ms (block) | 50 – 100 ns | 0.3 – 1 μs |
| Density | 1 Mbit/cm² | 1 Gbit/cm² | Very low (0.1 Mbit/cm³) |
| Bit cost (1985) | 1 cent/bit | 0.01 cent/bit | 10 cents/bit |
| Power | Moderate | High (refresh) | Low (read pulse) |
| Manufacturing | Complex (garnet + Permalloy) | Standard CMOS | Hand-wound cores |

**Two reasons MBM is no longer produced (2 marks):**
1. **DRAM scaling** — Moore's law drove semiconductor RAM to vastly higher density and lower cost per bit, making MBM economically unviable.
2. **Flash memory** — non-volatile semiconductor memory (NAND flash) overtook MBM in both density and access time, eliminating the niche MBM once occupied.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read carefully:**
> - When comparing memories, **always** present the comparison in a **table** with *explicit units*. Writing a paragraph comparison without units will cost you 1–2 marks.
> - For derivations, **never skip the substitution step**. Even if the formula is correct, the examiner *must see numerical substitution* to award full marks.
> - Do **not confuse MBM with Magnetic Bubble Domain Display** (a different device used in early LCDs). The two are unrelated.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — High-Yield MBM Points (for last-minute KTU prep):**

- ☐ MBM = **non-volatile**, **solid-state**, **sequential-access** storage.
- ☐ Material: **garnet film** (YIG, YSmGaG) with perpendicular anisotropy; **NOT** ferrite cores.
- ☐ Storage element: cylindrical magnetic domain = **bubble** ($\sim 1$–$10 \,\mu\text{m}$).
- ☐ **Bias field** $H_B$ controls bubble size; operating window is $H_{collapse} \leq H_B \leq H_{run-out}$.
- ☐ **Rotating in-plane field** + **Permalloy overlay** (chevron/T-bar) = propagation.
- ☐ **Bubble diameter** $d \approx 8l$, where $l = \sigma_w / (4\pi M_s^2)$.
- ☐ **Stability condition:** $d \geq 4h$ (bubble diameter ≥ 4 × film thickness).
- ☐ **Square-packing density** $\rho = 1/(2d)^2$ bits/cm²; **hex packing** $\rho = 2/(\sqrt{3}d^2)$.
- ☐ **Data rate** $R = v/(2d) = \pi r f / d$.
- ☐ **Avg. access time** $T_{avg} = 1/(2f)$; worst case $T_{max} = 1/f$.
- ☐ **Major-minor loop** organization enables **block random access** with **NDRO**.
- ☐ **Replicator** = non-destructive read-out; **annihilator** = erase.
- ☐ **Detector** = magnetoresistive (MR) Permalloy strip.
- ☐ **Historical peak:** ~1 Mbit/chip in late 1970s; commercially obsolete by mid-1990s.
- ☐ **Modern descendant:** IBM **racetrack memory** (2008+) = nano-scaled 3-D MBM revival.
- ☐ **Key disadvantages** vs. RAM: slow access (ms), complex packaging, high cost/bit.
- ☐ **Key advantages** vs. RAM: non-volatile, rugged, radiation-hard, no battery backup needed.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Charged Coupled Devices (CCDs)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST867 - STORAGE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Charged Coupled Devices (CCDs)**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🔬 Module 1 — Charged Coupled Devices (CCDs)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):** A **Charge-Coupled Device (CCD)** is a **MOS (Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor) integrated circuit** that transports stored electrical charge (generated by incident photons) from one capacitive storage bin to the next through sequential clock-pulse biasing of closely-spaced gate electrodes. In storage-systems terminology, a CCD functions as a **serial analog shift-register memory element** in which **information is encoded as discrete packets of minority carriers** held in inversion-region potential wells at the silicon–SiO₂ interface.

The CCD was invented in **1969** at **Bell Telephone Laboratories** by **Willard S. Boyle** and **George E. Smith**, an achievement that later earned them the **Nobel Prize in Physics (2009)**. In the context of **PECST867 — Storage Systems**, a CCD is treated as the **first-generation solid-state analog data-storage primitive** in which optical, electronic, or injected charge represents the stored bit-state.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bucket-Brigade of Photons"

Imagine a long line of **buckets** (potential wells) connected by **spring-loaded gates** (clock electrodes). Each bucket can hold water (electrons). When a photon strikes a bucket, an amount of water proportional to the light intensity is poured in. A foreman then gives a coordinated command ("Clock 1, Clock 2, Clock 3…") that makes the water slosh from one bucket into the next, until it reaches a measuring cup at the end (the output amplifier). **That is exactly how a CCD works:** photons → electrons → bucket-brigade transfer → readout amplifier.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PECST867):** In storage-systems hierarchy, the CCD is classified as a **volatile, charge-based, sequentially-accessed analog memory** — the direct conceptual ancestor of the modern **CMOS Active Pixel Sensor (APS)** used in smartphone cameras.

### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Engineering Metrics

The following constants and figures-of-merit are **universally required** for any KTU numerical or descriptive problem on CCDs:

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $q$ | Elementary charge | $1.602 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{C}$ |
| $\varepsilon_{Si}$ | Permittivity of Silicon | $11.7 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{F/m}$ |
| $E_g^{Si}$ | Bandgap of Silicon (300 K) | **1.12 eV** |
| $\lambda_c$ | Cut-off wavelength of Si | **≈ 1100 nm** |
| $T$ | Operating temperature (typical) | **−100 °C to +25 °C** |
| $\eta_{QE}$ | Quantum Efficiency (front-illuminated) | **30 – 50 %** (peak @ 700 nm) |
| $\eta_{QE}^{BB}$ | Quantum Efficiency (back-thinned / BSI) | **85 – 95 %** (peak @ 550 nm) |
| CTE | Charge Transfer Efficiency / stage | **0.99995 – 0.99999** |
| $N_{pix}$ | Pixel count (modern scientific CCD) | $1\text{k} \times 1\text{k}$ up to $9\text{k} \times 9\text{k}$ |

> [!TIP]
> Always remember the **"Three Pillars of CCD Operation"** for exam writing: **(i) Photogeneration → (ii) Charge Collection → (iii) Sequential Charge Transfer → (iv) Charge-to-Voltage Conversion.** Examiners look for these four words in order.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 MOS Capacitor — The Atomic Unit of a CCD

Every pixel of a CCD is essentially a **MOS capacitor** formed by:
- A **p-type silicon substrate** (the body),
- A thin **SiO₂ dielectric layer** (typically 50 – 100 nm thick),
- A **polysilicon gate electrode** on top.

When a positive voltage is applied to the gate, holes (majority carriers) are repelled from the Si–SiO₂ interface, creating a **depletion region**. With sufficiently high gate bias, an **inversion layer** of electrons forms at the interface — this inversion layer is the **"potential well"** that traps photo-generated electrons.

### 2.2 The Four-Phase CCD Operational Cycle (Clocking Sequence)

The classic **four-phase CCD** uses **four gate electrodes per pixel**. Clocking proceeds in four discrete steps:

1. **Phase 1 — Collection:** $V_{G1}$ is HIGH, all others LOW. Photogenerated electrons accumulate under gate 1.
2. **Phase 2 — Transfer-1:** $V_{G2}$ rises. The potential well merges between gates 1 and 2; charge redistributes.
3. **Phase 3 — Transfer-2:** $V_{G1}$ falls. Charge slides completely under gate 2.
4. **Phase 4 — Reset:** $V_{G2}$ falls, charge moves to gate 3 (next pixel), and the cycle repeats.

This sequence implements a **bucket-brigade charge shift register**, which is why CCDs are categorized as **serial analog memory** in storage-systems classification.

### 2.3 Photogeneration & Charge Packet Magnitude

When a photon of energy $h\nu > E_g^{Si}$ strikes the depletion region, it generates **one electron–hole pair**. The number of electrons collected in a pixel after exposure time $t_{int}$ is:

$$N_e = \eta_{QE} \cdot \Phi_{ph} \cdot A_{pix} \cdot t_{int}$$

where $\Phi_{ph}$ is the photon flux (photons/m²·s) and $A_{pix}$ is the pixel area. The corresponding **pixel voltage** at the output node of capacitance $C_{ox}$ is:

$$V_{out} = \frac{N_e \cdot q}{C_{ox}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for Storage Systems:** A CCD's full-well capacity (FWC) — the maximum number of electrons a single potential well can hold before spilling into adjacent wells (a phenomenon called **blooming**) — defines its **dynamic range**. FWC typically lies between **10,000 e⁻ and 1,000,000 e⁻** depending on pixel size and gate architecture.

## 2.4 ⭐ KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Mandatory Memorization Table)

> [!NOTE]
> The following table contains every equation, boundary condition, and unit a KTU 2024 examiner can demand for the CCD topic. Master this before attempting any numerical.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_{ph} = h\nu = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$ | Photon energy (must exceed $E_g$) | eV or J |
| 2 | $\lambda_c = \dfrac{hc}{E_g^{Si}} = \dfrac{1240}{1.12}$ | Silicon cut-off wavelength | **≈ 1107 nm** |
| 3 | $N_e = \eta_{QE}\,\Phi_{ph}\,A_{pix}\,t_{int}$ | Electrons generated per pixel | electrons |
| 4 | $V_{out} = \dfrac{N_e\,q}{C_{ox}}$ | Output voltage per pixel | V |
| 5 | $C_{ox} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{ox}\,A_{pix}}{t_{ox}}$ | MOS gate oxide capacitance | F |
| 6 | $N_{total} = N_{pix} \cdot N_e$ | Total electrons in frame | e⁻ |
| 7 | $T_{e} = (CTE)^{N_{stages}}$ | End-to-end transfer efficiency | dimensionless |
| 8 | $DR = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{N_{sat}}{N_{noise}\_{rms}}\right)$ | Dynamic Range | dB |
| 9 | $N_{noise} = \sqrt{N_{shot}^2 + N_{read}^2 + N_{dark}^2}$ | Total noise electrons | e⁻ rms |
| 10 | $SNR = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{N_e}{\sqrt{N_e + N_{read}^2}}\right)$ | Signal-to-Noise Ratio | dB |

**Boundary Conditions & Constraints to Memorize:**

- $E_{ph} \geq E_g^{Si} = 1.12$ eV ⇒ $\lambda \leq 1107$ nm for Si to respond.
- $0 \leq \eta_{QE} \leq 1$ (always a pure number, often expressed in %).
- $CTE < 1$ always; loss per transfer = $(1 - CTE)$.
- Full-Well Capacity $N_{sat}$ is the **upper limit** of $N_e$.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility of CCDs

CCDs are not academic curiosities — they underpin:

- **Astronomy:** Hubble Space Telescope's Wide-Field Planetary Camera 2 (WFPC2), James Webb's NIRCam guidance sensors, ground-based observatory imaging.
- **Medical Imaging:** Digital mammography, endoscopy, dental radiography (X-ray → visible-light → CCD).
- **Broadcasting:** Professional video cameras (Sony HDCAM, Ikegami, ARRI Alexa CCD-lineage sensors).
- **Industrial Metrology:** Wafer inspection, flat-panel display QC.
- **Forensics & Document Scanning:** High-resolution archival digitization.

> [!TIP]
> In any KTU answer, when you mention an application, **always state why a CCD was preferred** (e.g., "low noise, high dynamic range, linear response, near-100 % fill factor").

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Photon-to-Electron Conversion and Output Voltage

**Problem Framework:** A scientific CCD has a pixel of area $A_{pix} = 100\ \mu\text{m}^2$, gate oxide thickness $t_{ox} = 80$ nm, exposed to monochromatic light of wavelength $\lambda = 700$ nm at photon flux $\Phi_{ph} = 10^{14}$ photons/m²·s for an integration time $t_{int} = 1$ s. The peak quantum efficiency at 700 nm is $\eta_{QE} = 0.85$. Compute (a) the number of electrons collected per pixel, and (b) the output voltage developed at the sense node.

### Step 1 — Verify Photon Energy Exceeds Silicon Bandgap

$$
E_{ph} \;=\; \frac{hc}{\lambda} \;=\; \frac{(6.626 \times 10^{-34})(3 \times 10^{8})}{700 \times 10^{-9}}
$$

$$
E_{ph} \;=\; \frac{1.9878 \times 10^{-25}}{7 \times 10^{-7}} \;=\; 2.8397 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{J}
$$

Convert to electron-volts:

$$
E_{ph} \;=\; \frac{2.8397 \times 10^{-19}}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}} \;\approx\; 1.773\ \text{eV}
$$

Since $E_{ph} = 1.773\ \text{eV} \gg E_g^{Si} = 1.12\ \text{eV}$, **the photon will be absorbed and produce one electron–hole pair**. (Excess energy becomes heat via phonon emission.)

### Step 2 — Compute Electrons Collected per Pixel

$$
N_e \;=\; \eta_{QE}\,\Phi_{ph}\,A_{pix}\,t_{int}
$$

Substitute numerical values:

$$
N_e \;=\; (0.85)(10^{14})(100 \times 10^{-12})(1)
$$

$$
N_e \;=\; (0.85)(10^{14})(10^{-10}) \;=\; (0.85)(10^{4}) \;=\; 8.5 \times 10^{3}\ \text{electrons}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute the Gate-Oxide Capacitance

$$
C_{ox} \;=\; \frac{\varepsilon_{ox}\,A_{pix}}{t_{ox}}
$$

Where $\varepsilon_{ox} = 3.9 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} = 3.453 \times 10^{-11}\ \text{F/m}$:

$$
C_{ox} \;=\; \frac{(3.453 \times 10^{-11})(100 \times 10^{-12})}{80 \times 10^{-9}}
$$

$$
C_{ox} \;=\; \frac{3.453 \times 10^{-21}}{8 \times 10^{-8}} \;=\; 4.316 \times 10^{-14}\ \text{F} \;\approx\; 43.2\ \text{fF}
$$

### Step 4 — Compute the Output Voltage

$$
V_{out} \;=\; \frac{N_e \, q}{C_{ox}}
$$

$$
V_{out} \;=\; \frac{(8.5 \times 10^{3})(1.602 \times 10^{-19})}{4.316 \times 10^{-14}}
$$

$$
V_{out} \;=\; \frac{1.3617 \times 10^{-15}}{4.316 \times 10^{-14}} \;\approx\; 0.03155\ \text{V} \;\approx\; 31.6\ \text{mV}
$$

**Final Result:** $N_e = 8.5 \times 10^{3}$ electrons; $V_{out} \approx 31.6$ mV.

## 3.2 Derivation II — End-to-End Charge Transfer Efficiency (CTE)

**Problem Framework:** A 2048 × 2048 full-frame scientific CCD uses a three-phase clocking scheme with 2048 horizontal stages per row. The per-stage CTE is $0.99999$. Compute (a) the fraction of original signal lost in transferring a charge packet from the farthest pixel to the output, and (b) the residual signal at the output as a percentage of the input.

### Step 1 — Identify the Number of Transfer Stages

For a full-frame CCD read out via a single output node:

$$
N_{stages} \;=\; 2048 \text{ (per row)} \quad \text{(single output amplifier assumed)}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute End-to-End Transfer Efficiency

$$
T_{e} \;=\; (CTE)^{N_{stages}} \;=\; (0.99999)^{2048}
$$

Take natural log:

$$
\ln T_e \;=\; 2048 \,\ln(0.99999) \;\approx\; 2048 \times (-1.000005 \times 10^{-5})
$$

$$
\ln T_e \;\approx\; -0.02048
$$

Exponentiate:

$$
T_e \;\approx\; e^{-0.02048} \;\approx\; 0.97973
$$

### Step 3 — Compute Charge Loss & Residual Signal

$$
\text{Loss Fraction} \;=\; 1 - T_e \;=\; 1 - 0.97973 \;=\; 0.02027 \;\approx\; 2.03\ \%
$$

$$
\text{Residual Signal} \;=\; T_e \times 100\% \;\approx\; 97.97\ \%
$$

**Final Result:** $T_e \approx 0.9797$; charge loss $\approx 2.03\%$, residual signal $\approx 97.97\%$.

> [!TIP]
> **Why $CTE$ matters in storage systems:** Even a 0.001 % loss per stage, when cascaded over 2048 stages, becomes 2 % total loss — this is the **classical analog-memory "leakage" problem** of bucket-brigade devices and was historically a key motivator for transitioning to **digital CMOS image sensors** where each pixel has its own amplifier.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — CCD vs. CMOS vs. CID

| Parameter | **CCD (Bucket-Brigade)** | **CMOS (Active Pixel)** | **CID (Charge-Injection Device)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Readout | Serial, destructive | Per-pixel, non-destructive | Random-access, non-destructive |
| Fill Factor | **≈ 100 %** | 30 – 60 % (with microlens: ≈ 80 %) | ≈ 100 % |
| Noise (read) | **2 – 5 e⁻ rms** | 10 – 30 e⁻ rms | 5 – 15 e⁻ rms |
| Power | High (multi-voltage clocks) | **Low (single supply)** | Moderate |
| Radiation Tolerance | Moderate | High (with SOI) | High |
| Blooming Susceptibility | **High** (vertical overflow drain mitigates) | Low (per-pixel drain) | **Very Low** |
| Storage-System Analogy | Serial analog shift register | Random-access byte-addressable memory | Content-addressable memory |
| Best Use | Astronomy, scientific | Smartphones, machine vision | Radiation-hardened imaging |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]** Define a Charge-Coupled Device. Mention any two advantages it has over a CMOS image sensor.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **CCD (Charge-Coupled Device)** is a **MOS-based integrated circuit** in which **electrical charge generated by incident photons** is stored in potential wells at the Si–SiO₂ interface and **transferred sequentially** between adjacent gate electrodes by clock-pulse biasing, eventually reaching a single output amplifier for charge-to-voltage conversion.

**Two advantages over CMOS:**

1. **Higher Fill Factor (≈ 100 %):** Every pixel is photosensitive, since no in-pixel transistors are needed — CMOS loses 30–60 % of pixel area to readout electronics.
2. **Lower Read Noise (2–5 e⁻ rms):** The single low-noise output amplifier (with Correlated Double Sampling) yields much better noise performance than CMOS, where each pixel amplifier has its own offset and $1/f$ noise.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Do **not** write "CCD is better than CMOS in every way." Both technologies coexist. Always specify the application (astronomy vs. smartphone) when making comparisons.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** What is meant by **Charge Transfer Efficiency (CTE)** in a CCD? Why is it important?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**CTE** is the **fraction of signal charge that is successfully transferred from one gate electrode to the next** during each clock cycle. A typical scientific CCD has a CTE of $0.99999$, meaning only $0.001$ % of charge is lost per transfer.

**Importance:** In a CCD with $N$ transfer stages, the end-to-end transfer efficiency is $T_e = (CTE)^N$. Even a tiny per-stage loss compounds: for $N = 2048$ stages, the cumulative loss is $T_e \approx 0.98$, i.e. **2 % signal loss**. CTE directly impacts **photometric accuracy**, **image uniformity**, and **charge blooming** — making it the single most important analog-storage figure-of-merit for a serial shift-register memory device.

---

## 5.2 📝 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### 🅰️ Question A (14 Marks) — *CTE, Full-Well Capacity & Architectural Comparison*

> **[KTU University Exam – Model 2024 Scheme]**
>
> **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **internal architecture of a full-frame CCD** and describe the **four-phase clocking mechanism** used to transfer charge from one pixel to the next. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** A 4096 × 4096 scientific CCD has a per-stage CTE of 0.99995, a full-well capacity of $10^5$ electrons per pixel, and a read noise of 5 e⁻ rms. Compute:
> - (i) The end-to-end transfer efficiency for the worst-case pixel.
> - (ii) The dynamic range of the device in dB. (7 marks)

#### ✅ Model Solution

**Part (a) — Architecture & Four-Phase Clocking (7 marks)**

A full-frame CCD consists of a **2-D array of MOS capacitors** arranged as rows and columns. The architecture is shown in the figure below (refer to Section 4.1). The key functional blocks are:

- **Photosensitive Pixel Array** — every pixel is a poly-Si gate over SiO₂ on p-Si substrate.
- **Vertical Shift Register** — charge is shifted row-by-row toward the horizontal register.
- **Horizontal Shift Register** — charge is shifted pixel-by-pixel to the output node.
- **Output Amplifier** — converts charge to voltage.
- **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** — converts voltage to a digital count.

**Four-Phase Clocking (4 marks of the 7):**

The four gate electrodes per pixel are clocked with waveforms $\phi_1, \phi_2, \phi_3, \phi_4$ in quadrature (90° phase-shifted). The sequence is:

| Step | $\phi_1$ | $\phi_2$ | $\phi_3$ | $\phi_4$ | Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | HIGH | LOW | LOW | LOW | Collection under G1 |
| 2 | HIGH | HIGH | LOW | LOW | Wells merge, charge spreads |
| 3 | LOW | HIGH | LOW | LOW | Charge pushed to G2 |
| 4 | LOW | HIGH | HIGH | LOW | Charge moves to G3 |
| 5 | LOW | LOW | HIGH | LOW | Charge moves to G4 |
| 6 | LOW | LOW | HIGH | HIGH | Cycle continues |

This **unidirectional charge transfer** is the defining property of a CCD as a **serial analog memory** element.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks)**

**(i) End-to-End CTE (3 marks):**

Number of stages for worst-case pixel:

$$
N_{stages} \;=\; 4096 \text{ columns}
$$

End-to-end transfer efficiency:

$$
T_e \;=\; (CTE)^{N_{stages}} \;=\; (0.99995)^{4096}
$$

Take natural log:

$$
\ln T_e \;=\; 4096 \times \ln(0.99995) \;\approx\; 4096 \times (-5.0001 \times 10^{-5}) \;\approx\; -0.2048
$$

Exponentiate:

$$
T_e \;\approx\; e^{-0.2048} \;\approx\; 0.8149
$$

**Loss Fraction:**

$$
1 - T_e \;\approx\; 0.1851 \;\approx\; 18.51\ \%
$$

**(ii) Dynamic Range in dB (4 marks):**

$$
DR \;=\; 20\,\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{N_{sat}}{N_{noise\_rms}}\right) \;=\; 20\,\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{10^{5}}{5}\right)
$$

$$
DR \;=\; 20\,\log_{10}(20000) \;=\; 20 \times 4.3010 \;\approx\; 86.02\ \text{dB}
$$

---

### 🅱️ Question B (14 Marks) — *Three CCD Architectures & Application Engineering*

> **[KTU University Exam – Model 2024 Scheme — Alternative Choice]**
>
> **(a)** Compare and contrast the three principal CCD architectures: **Full-Frame, Frame-Transfer, and Interline-Transfer**. Mention at least one specific application where each excels. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** A back-illuminated CCD used in an astronomical observatory has the following specifications: $\eta_{QE} = 0.92$ at 550 nm, pixel size $13.5\ \mu\text{m} \times 13.5\ \mu\text{m}$, exposure time 300 s, photon flux $5 \times 10^{12}$ photons/m²·s at 550 nm. If the dark current at the operating temperature of $-100\ ^\circ\text{C}$ is 0.001 e⁻/pixel/s, and read noise is 3 e⁻ rms, compute:
> - (i) Signal electrons per pixel.
> - (ii) Total noise electrons per pixel.
> - (iii) SNR in dB. (7 marks)

#### ✅ Model Solution

**Part (a) — Three CCD Architectures (7 marks)**

| Feature | Full-Frame | Frame-Transfer | Interline-Transfer |
|---|---|---|---|
| Storage Register | **None** (entire chip is photosensitive) | Masked half of chip (storage) | Per-pixel vertical CCD next to photodiode |
| Fill Factor | **100 %** | 100 % | ~ 20 – 30 % (with microlens ≈ 70 %) |
| Mechanical Shutter | **Required** | Not required (electronic) | Not required (electronic) |
| Smear | High if shutterless | Low | **Very low** |
| Best Application | Astronomy, scientific (slow, controlled) | Scientific imaging with fast frame rates | **Consumer/professional video cameras** |

**Specific applications:**
- **Full-Frame:** Hubble Space Telescope's WFPC2 — slow readout, low noise, no need for fast shutter.
- **Frame-Transfer:** Industrial machine-vision linescan cameras — fast frame rates without smear.
- **Interline-Transfer:** Broadcast TV cameras, prosumer DSLRs — electronic shutter, freeze-motion capture.

---

**Part (b) — Astronomical CCD Numerical (7 marks)**

**(i) Signal Electrons per pixel (3 marks):**

$$
N_e \;=\; \eta_{QE}\,\Phi_{ph}\,A_{pix}\,t_{int}
$$

$$
A_{pix} \;=\; (13.5 \times 10^{-6})^2 \;=\; 1.8225 \times 10^{-10}\ \text{m}^2
$$

$$
N_e \;=\; (0.92)(5 \times 10^{12})(1.8225 \times 10^{-10})(300)
$$

$$
N_e \;=\; (0.92)(5)(1.8225)(300) \times 10^{12-10}
$$

$$
N_e \;=\; 2515.05 \times 10^{2} \;\approx\; 2.515 \times 10^{5}\ \text{electrons}
$$

**(ii) Total Noise per pixel (2 marks):**

Shot noise:

$$
N_{shot} \;=\; \sqrt{N_e} \;=\; \sqrt{2.515 \times 10^{5}} \;\approx\; 501.5\ e^-
$$

Dark current shot noise:

$$
N_{dark} \;=\; \sqrt{(0.001)(300)} \;=\; \sqrt{0.3} \;\approx\; 0.548\ e^-
$$

Read noise:

$$
N_{read} \;=\; 3\ e^-
$$

Total noise:

$$
N_{noise} \;=\; \sqrt{(501.5)^2 + (0.548)^2 + 3^2} \;\approx\; \sqrt{251502 + 0.3 + 9} \;\approx\; 501.5\ e^-
$$

(Shot noise dominates in astronomical exposures.)

**(iii) SNR in dB (2 marks):**

$$
SNR \;=\; 20\,\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{N_e}{N_{noise}}\right) \;=\; 20\,\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{251505}{501.5}\right)
$$

$$
SNR \;=\; 20\,\log_{10}(501.5) \;\approx\; 20 \times 2.7003 \;\approx\; 54.0\ \text{dB}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Unit errors in CTE numericals:** Students forget that $CTE$ is **dimensionless** and that $(CTE)^N$ is *not* a percentage — it is a multiplier. State final loss as a percentage only after subtracting from 1.
> 2. **Confusing $V_{out}$ with $V_{gate}$:** $V_{out}$ is the voltage across the *floating diffusion* (sense node), not the gate voltage. The sense-node capacitance $C_{FD}$ is **not** the same as $C_{ox}$.
> 3. **Forgetting the photon-energy check:** Students write $N_e$ without verifying $h\nu > E_g^{Si}$. Examiners deduct **1 mark** if this check is missing.
> 4. **Mixing up architectures:** Saying "Full-Frame CCD has an electronic shutter" is **wrong** — it requires a *mechanical* shutter. This is a guaranteed half-mark deduction.
> 5. **Not converting units:** Pixel area in $\mu\text{m}^2$ must be converted to $\text{m}^2$ before multiplying with $\Phi_{ph}$ in photons/m²·s. A common 1-mark loss.

---

## 5.3 🧠 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical things to remember about CCDs (Module 1):**

- ✅ A CCD is a **MOS-based serial analog shift-register memory** — it stores information as **packets of minority carriers** in inversion-region potential wells.
- ✅ **Nobel Prize 2009** — Boyle & Smith, Bell Labs, 1969.
- ✅ **Three architectures:** Full-Frame, Frame-Transfer, Interline-Transfer — each with distinct fill-factor and shutter requirements.
- ✅ **Four-phase clocking** is the classical charge-transfer mechanism (three-phase and two-phase variants also exist).
- ✅ **Photon-to-electron conversion** requires $E_{ph} \geq 1.12$ eV ⇒ $\lambda \leq 1107$ nm for Si.
- ✅ **Quantum Efficiency** peaks at **≈ 90 %** for back-thinned CCDs, **≈ 50 %** for front-illuminated.
- ✅ **CTE per stage** is **0.99995 – 0.99999**; cumulative loss $(1 - CTE)^N$ grows exponentially with stage count.
- ✅ **Full-Well Capacity (FWC)** defines the upper limit of charge storage; exceeding it causes **blooming**.
- ✅ **Dynamic Range** is given in dB: $DR = 20\log_{10}(N_{sat}/N_{noise})$.
- ✅ **Read noise** (2–5 e⁻ rms) is much lower than CMOS — making CCD preferred for low-light scientific imaging.
- ✅ **Storage-systems classification:** CCDs are **volatile, destructive-readout, sequential-access** analog memory.
- ✅ **Output stage** uses a floating diffusion + source-follower + Correlated Double Sampling (CDS) for low-noise readout.
- ✅ **Applications:** Astronomy, medical imaging, broadcasting, scientific instrumentation, industrial metrology.
- ✅ **Modern context:** Largely replaced by **CMOS APS** in consumer markets, but **still dominant** in astronomy, spectroscopy, and high-end scientific imaging due to unmatched noise performance and 100 % fill factor.

---

> **End of Module 1 — Charged Coupled Devices (CCDs) | KTU 2024 Scheme — PECST867 Storage Systems**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Micro-Electro-Mechanical Systems (MEMS)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST867 - STORAGE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Micro-Electro-Mechanical Systems (MEMS)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Micro-Electro-Mechanical Systems (MEMS)** are integrated microsystems that combine mechanical and electromechanical elements, sensors, actuators, and electronics on a common silicon substrate through microfabrication technology. According to the KTU 2024 syllabus definition, MEMS refers to devices whose critical feature sizes lie in the micrometer range (typically **1 µm to 100 µm**) and which integrate mechanical structures with electronic circuitry to sense, actuate, or control processes at the micro-scale.

In the context of **Storage Systems**, MEMS technology plays a pivotal role in the design of:
- **Hard Disk Drive (HDD) read/write heads** with nano-scale flying heights (~3–5 nm).
- **Giant Magneto-Resistive (GMR) and Tunneling Magneto-Resistive (TMR) sensors** that detect magnetic flux reversals.
- **Micro-actuators for dual-stage servo positioning** of the head over the correct track.
- **Free-fall accelerometers** that park the head safely when the laptop is dropped.
- **Micro-mirror arrays** used in optical libraries and near-field optical storage.
- **Cantilever-based probe storage** (e.g., IBM Millipede).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECST867 Module 1):** MEMS forms the foundational enabling technology behind modern magnetic, optical, and probe-based storage devices. Every storage subsystem discussed in this course — magnetic disks, optical pickups, and emerging probe storage — depends fundamentally on MEMS structures such as cantilevers, diaphragms, and slider air-bearings.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a MEMS device as a **"tiny robotic hand carved into a silicon chip."** Imagine a silicon wafer — the same material used to make computer chips — but instead of just transistors, engineers also sculpt microscopic movable parts on it: tiny springs (cantilevers), flaps, gears, and mirrors. These moving parts are only as wide as a human hair (about **50 µm**).

**Real-world analogy for storage:**
When you drop your laptop, something must quickly move the read/write head away from the spinning platter to prevent a head crash. This "something" is a **MEMS accelerometer** — a microscopic mass suspended on flexible silicon beams. When the laptop falls, the mass shifts due to inertia, the silicon beams flex, and the change in capacitance between tiny comb-like fingers is detected. Within milliseconds, the system commands the actuator to retract the head — all thanks to a chip smaller than a grain of rice.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why MEMS Matters in Storage:**
> - Enables **head flying heights of 3–5 nm**, increasing areal density beyond **1 Tbit/in²**.
> - Allows **batch fabrication** using semiconductor processes, reducing cost.
> - Provides **low power consumption** (µW range) and **high bandwidth** sensing.
> - Integrates sensing + actuation + electronics on a **single silicon die**.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Highlighted)

| Parameter | Standard Value |
|---|---|
| Young's Modulus of Silicon (Si ⟨100⟩) | **E ≈ 169 GPa** |
| Density of Silicon | **ρ = 2330 kg/m³** |
| Poisson's Ratio of Silicon | **ν ≈ 0.28** |
| Piezoelectric coefficient (PZT) | **d₃₁ ≈ -320 pm/V** |
| Permittivity of free space | **ε₀ = 8.854 × 10⁻¹² F/m** |
| Relative permittivity of SiO₂ | **εᵣ ≈ 3.9** |
| Air gap in HDD slider | **3–10 nm** |
| MEMS feature size | **1 µm – 100 µm** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Building Blocks of MEMS

A MEMS device for storage is built from a small set of universal mechanical primitives. The KTU examiner expects you to recognize and analyze these structures because they form the backbone of HDD heads, AFM probes, and optical micro-mirrors.

### 2.1.1 Cantilever Beam
A cantilever is a beam anchored at one end and free at the other. It is the **single most important structure in MEMS storage** because:
- It is the basis of **AFM read/write probes** (e.g., IBM Millipede).
- It is the basis of **acceleration-sensing masses** in HDD free-fall detectors.
- It is the basis of **piezoelectric-driven micro-actuators** in dual-stage servos.

### 2.1.2 Fixed-Fixed Beam
Both ends clamped. Used in high-Q resonators and gyroscopes. Higher stiffness than a cantilever of equal dimensions.

### 2.1.3 Comb Drive
Two interlocking sets of comb fingers — one fixed, one movable. Applying voltage between them generates an electrostatic force, and motion changes the capacitance. This is the **workhorse actuator** in MEMS.

### 2.1.4 Diaphragm
A thin plate clamped at its edges. Used in pressure sensors and microphone-like structures; in storage, it appears in flying-height sensors.

### 2.1.5 Slider Air-Bearing
The MEMS air-bearing surface (ABS) of an HDD head flies above the spinning platter on a cushion of air (the **flying height**, typically 3–5 nm). The aerodynamic lift is generated by carefully shaped micro-stepped rails etched into the silicon slider.

## 2.2 The Core "Why" and "How" — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Transduction Principle:** MEMS converts a *non-electrical* physical quantity (acceleration, displacement, magnetic field) into an *electrical* signal (or vice versa).
2. **Common Domain:** The motion is always in the **mechanical domain**, and the bridge is either **capacitive, piezoresistive, piezoelectric, or resonant**.
3. **Why silicon?** Single-crystal silicon has **near-perfect mechanical properties** (no fatigue, no plastic deformation at room temperature) and is **CMOS-compatible**.
4. **How does fabrication work?** Bulk micromachining etches into the silicon wafer; surface micromachining builds up layers of structural material (polysilicon) above the substrate. **Deep Reactive Ion Etching (DRIE)** enables high-aspect-ratio structures.
5. **How does this serve storage?** Each MEMS element acts as a *physical bridge* between the storage medium (magnetic, optical, or topographic) and the electronic read channel.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are the **highest-yield formulas** for the KTU ESE. Memorize the units and boundary conditions.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variable Definitions | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Spring constant — cantilever beam | $k = \dfrac{E w t^{3}}{4 L^{3}}$ | $E$ = Young's modulus, $w$ = width, $t$ = thickness, $L$ = length | N/m |
| 2 | Spring constant — fixed-fixed beam | $k = \dfrac{E w t^{3}}{L^{3}}$ | Same as above | N/m |
| 3 | Deflection of cantilever (end load) | $\delta = \dfrac{4 F L^{3}}{E w t^{3}}$ | $F$ = applied force | m |
| 4 | Resonant frequency of cantilever | $f_{n} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m_{eff}}}$ | $k$ = spring constant, $m_{eff}$ = effective mass (≈ 0.24 m for uniform cantilever) | Hz |
| 5 | First-mode freq. of cantilever (uniform) | $f_{1} = \dfrac{1.875^{2}}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{E I}{\rho A L^{4}}}$ | $I = \dfrac{w t^{3}}{12}$, $A = w t$ | Hz |
| 6 | Capacitance of parallel plate | $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{0}\varepsilon_{r} A}{d}$ | $A$ = area, $d$ = gap | F |
| 7 | Capacitance change (small displacement) | $\dfrac{\Delta C}{C} = \dfrac{\Delta d}{d}$ | For gap-changing config | — |
| 8 | Electrostatic force (parallel plate) | $F_{e} = \dfrac{1}{2}\dfrac{\varepsilon_{0} A V^{2}}{d^{2}}$ | $V$ = applied voltage | N |
| 9 | Pull-in voltage | $V_{PI} = \sqrt{\dfrac{8 k d_{0}^{3}}{27 \varepsilon_{0} A}}$ | $d_0$ = initial gap, $k$ = spring constant | V |
| 10 | Piezoresistive gauge factor | $G = \dfrac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon}$ | $\varepsilon$ = strain | — |
| 11 | Piezoelectric charge | $Q = d_{31}\, E_{p}\, A$ | $d_{31}$ = piezo coefficient, $E_p$ = applied field | C |
| 12 | HDD Areal Density | $AD = \dfrac{BPI}{TPI}$ | $BPI$ = bits per inch, $TPI$ = tracks per inch | bits/in² |
| 13 | Flying-height pressure (Couette flow) | $P = \dfrac{6 \mu V L}{h^{3}}$ | $\mu$ = air viscosity, $V$ = velocity, $h$ = gap | Pa |
| 14 | Sensitivity of accelerometer | $S = \dfrac{\Delta C / C}{a}$ | $a$ = acceleration | per g |
| 15 | Quality factor (under-damped) | $Q = \dfrac{\sqrt{k m}}{c}$ | $c$ = damping coefficient | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineer's Real-World Utility:** Equations 1–5 govern **probe-storage read/write speed**. Equation 6–9 govern **servo micro-actuators and capacitive sensors**. Equation 12 directly determines **storage capacity**, which is the metric the industry competes on.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Deflection of a Cantilever Beam Under End Load

This derivation is the cornerstone of MEMS storage mechanics and frequently appears in KTU derivations.

**Given:** A cantilever beam of length $L$, width $w$, thickness $t$, made of isotropic material with Young's modulus $E$. A point force $F$ is applied at the free end in the $z$-direction.

**Step 1: Establish the bending equation.**

The Euler–Bernoulli beam equation relates bending moment $M(x)$ to curvature:

$$EI \dfrac{d^{2} z}{d x^{2}} = M(x)$$

where $I$ is the second moment of area of the rectangular cross-section.

**Step 2: Express the second moment of area.**

For a rectangular cross-section with width $w$ and thickness $t$ (bending about the width axis):

$$I = \dfrac{w t^{3}}{12}$$

**Step 3: Express the bending moment as a function of position.**

At a distance $x$ from the fixed end, the moment due to the end load $F$ is:

$$M(x) = F (L - x)$$

**Step 4: Substitute and integrate once.**

$$EI \dfrac{d^{2} z}{d x^{2}} = F(L - x)$$

Integrating with respect to $x$ (slope):

$$EI \dfrac{d z}{d x} = F \left( Lx - \dfrac{x^{2}}{2} \right) + C_{1}$$

**Step 5: Apply the boundary condition at the fixed end.**

At $x = 0$, slope $\dfrac{dz}{dx} = 0$:

$$C_{1} = 0$$

**Step 6: Integrate a second time to find deflection.**

$$EI \, z(x) = F \left( \dfrac{L x^{2}}{2} - \dfrac{x^{3}}{6} \right) + C_{2}$$

**Step 7: Apply the boundary condition at the fixed end (zero deflection).**

At $x = 0$, $z = 0$:

$$C_{2} = 0$$

**Step 8: Evaluate the deflection at the free end, $x = L$.**

$$z(L) = \dfrac{F}{EI} \left( \dfrac{L^{3}}{2} - \dfrac{L^{3}}{6} \right) = \dfrac{F}{EI} \cdot \dfrac{L^{3}}{3}$$

Substituting $I = \dfrac{wt^{3}}{12}$:

$$\delta = z(L) = \dfrac{F L^{3}}{3 E \left( \dfrac{w t^{3}}{12} \right)}$$

$$\boxed{\delta = \dfrac{4 F L^{3}}{E w t^{3}}}$$

**Step 9: Identify the spring constant.**

By definition $F = k \delta$, so:

$$\boxed{k = \dfrac{E w t^{3}}{4 L^{3}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** The stiffness scales as $t^{3}$ and inversely as $L^{3}$. Thus, **doubling the thickness makes the beam 8× stiffer**, while **doubling the length makes it 8× softer**. This is precisely why AFM probe tips used in storage are made **very thin and short** to achieve the right compliance.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — Resonant Frequency of a Cantilever Beam

**Step 1: Equation of motion.**

For free vibration, Newton's second law gives:

$$m_{eff} \ddot{z} + k z = 0$$

**Step 2: Assume harmonic solution.**

$$z(t) = Z \, e^{j \omega t}$$

Substituting:

$$-m_{eff} \omega^{2} Z + k Z = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \omega_{n}^{2} = \dfrac{k}{m_{eff}}$$

**Step 3: Identify the effective mass.**

For a uniform cantilever, the effective mass in the first mode is:

$$m_{eff} = 0.24 \, m = 0.24 \, \rho w t L$$

**Step 4: Combine with the spring-constant expression.**

$$f_{1} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m_{eff}}} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{E w t^{3} / 4L^{3}}{0.24 \rho w t L}}$$

Simplifying:

$$f_{1} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{E t^{2}}{0.96 \, \rho L^{4}}}$$

Using the eigenvalue constant for the first mode of a uniform cantilever ($1.875^{2} = 3.516$):

$$\boxed{f_{1} = \dfrac{1.875^{2}}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{E I}{\rho A L^{4}}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Storage application:** Probe-based storage (e.g., IBM Millipede) operates the cantilever near its **resonant frequency** to maximize data-write speed while minimizing force. The cantilever is "pinged" at ~100 kHz, allowing **>1 Mbit/s per tip** with thousands of tips in parallel.

---

## 3.3 Derivation III — Electrostatic Force and Pull-In Voltage of a Parallel-Plate Actuator

**Step 1: Energy in the capacitor.**

$$W = \dfrac{1}{2} C V^{2} = \dfrac{1}{2} \dfrac{\varepsilon_{0} A V^{2}}{d}$$

**Step 2: Force is the negative gradient of energy with respect to displacement.**

$$F = -\dfrac{\partial W}{\partial d} = \dfrac{1}{2}\dfrac{\varepsilon_{0} A V^{2}}{d^{2}}$$

**Step 3: At equilibrium, the electrostatic force equals the mechanical restoring force.**

$$\dfrac{1}{2}\dfrac{\varepsilon_{0} A V^{2}}{d^{2}} = k (d_{0} - d)$$

**Step 4: Solve for $V^{2}$ as a function of $d$.**

$$V^{2}(d) = \dfrac{2 k (d_{0} - d) d^{2}}{\varepsilon_{0} A}$$

**Step 5: Find the maximum stable voltage by setting $\dfrac{d(V^{2})}{dd} = 0$.**

$$\dfrac{d}{dd}\left[(d_{0} - d) d^{2}\right] = -d^{2} + 2 d (d_{0} - d) = 0$$

$$-d^{2} + 2 d d_{0} - 2 d^{2} = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; 2 d d_{0} = 3 d^{2} \;\Rightarrow\; d = \dfrac{2}{3} d_{0}$$

**Step 6: Substitute $d = \dfrac{2}{3} d_0$ back into $V^2(d)$.**

$$V_{PI}^{2} = \dfrac{2 k \left(d_{0} - \dfrac{2 d_{0}}{3}\right) \left(\dfrac{2 d_{0}}{3}\right)^{2}}{\varepsilon_{0} A} = \dfrac{2 k \cdot \dfrac{d_{0}}{3} \cdot \dfrac{4 d_{0}^{2}}{9}}{\varepsilon_{0} A}$$

$$V_{PI}^{2} = \dfrac{8 k d_{0}^{3}}{27 \varepsilon_{0} A}$$

$$\boxed{V_{PI} = \sqrt{\dfrac{8 k d_{0}^{3}}{27 \varepsilon_{0} A}}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Insight (KTU favourite):** The pull-in occurs at exactly **1/3 of the original gap** of displacement. This defines the **stable operating range** of every MEMS comb drive used in micro-actuators for HDD head positioning. Operating beyond pull-in causes the plates to snap together — a destructive failure.

---

## 3.4 Derivation IV — Capacitive Sensitivity of an Accelerometer (Storage Application)

For a differential comb-drive accelerometer with $N$ fingers, area $A$, and nominal gap $d_0$:

**Step 1: Total static capacitance.**

$$C_{0} = N \dfrac{\varepsilon_{0} A}{d_{0}}$$

**Step 2: Proof mass displacement under acceleration $a$.**

The proof mass $m_p$ suspended on a spring of stiffness $k$ shifts by:

$$\Delta x = \dfrac{m_{p} a}{k}$$

**Step 3: Change in capacitance (small-signal approximation).**

For a gap-changing configuration:

$$\dfrac{\Delta C}{C_{0}} = \dfrac{\Delta x}{d_{0}} = \dfrac{m_{p} a}{k d_{0}}$$

**Step 4: Sensitivity.**

$$\boxed{S = \dfrac{\Delta C / C_{0}}{a} = \dfrac{m_{p}}{k d_{0}} = \dfrac{1}{d_{0}}\cdot\dfrac{1}{\omega_{n}^{2}}}$$

where $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m_p}$ is the natural frequency. The trade-off is **clear: lower natural frequency ⇒ higher sensitivity**, but slower response — a key design constraint in HDD free-fall sensors where response must be **< 5 ms**.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — MEMS Transduction Methods in Storage

| Method | Physical Principle | Typical Use in Storage | Sensitivity | Linearity | Power |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Capacitive | $\Delta C$ due to motion | HDD head positioning, free-fall sensors | High | High | Low |
| Piezoresistive | $\Delta R$ due to strain | AFM probes, pressure sensors in drives | Medium | Medium | Low |
| Piezoelectric | Charge from strain | Dual-stage actuator, Millipede write | High | Medium | Very low |
| Resonant | Frequency shift | Track-following reference, mass sensors | Very high | High | Medium |
| Optical (MOEMS) | Light deflection | Micro-mirror libraries, optical pickups | High | High | Medium |
| Magnetic (GMR/TMR) | Resistance vs. field | HDD read head | Very high | Medium | Low |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Architecture — MEMS-Based HDD Read/Write Channel

The following block diagram maps how MEMS components interact within a modern Hard Disk Drive storage system. Each block represents a subsystem that contains MEMS technology or interacts directly with a MEMS element.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph hostLayer["Host System (Computer/MCU)"]
        hostA["SATA / NVMe Controller"]
    end

    subgraph controlLayer["Drive Electronics Board"]
        ctrlA["Drive Microcontroller (ARM/DSP)"]
        ctrlB["Read/Write Channel ASIC"]
        ctrlC["Servo Demodulator"]
    end

    subgraph memsLayer["MEMS Subsystem (On-Slider)"]
        memsA["GMR / TMR Read Head Sensor"]
        memsB["Write Head (Inductive Micro-Coil)"]
        memsC["Micro-Actuator (Piezo / Comb Drive)"]
        memsD["Air-Bearing Surface ABS Slider"]
        memsE["Free-Fall Accelerometer (MEMS)"]
    end

    subgraph mediaLayer["Storage Medium"]
        medA["Magnetic Platter (CoCrPt-Oxide)"]
    end

    subgraph feedbackLayer["Servo Feedback Loop"]
        fbA["Position Error Signal PES"]
        fbB["Voice Coil Motor VCM"]
    end

    hostA --> ctrlA
    ctrlA --> ctrlB
    ctrlB --> memsA
    ctrlB --> memsB
    ctrlA --> ctrlC
    ctrlC --> memsC
    memsA --> medA
    memsB --> medA
    memsD --> medA
    memsC --> fbA
    fbA --> ctrlC
    ctrlC --> fbB
    fbB --> memsC
    memsE --> ctrlA
    ctrlA --> memsD
```

**How to read this diagram for KTU exams:**
- The **left-to-right flow** is the data path (host → media).
- The **right-to-left feedback** is the servo loop that keeps the head on track.
- The **memsE block** is the MEMS free-fall detector that, upon impact, commands an emergency retract.
- This is a **block-level functional architecture** as required for storage-system-level questions.

---

## 4.2 Process Flow — Surface Micromachining of a MEMS Cantilever

The following flow chart represents the standard **surface micromachining** process used to fabricate the silicon cantilevers found in probe storage and accelerometers.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stepA["Step A: Start with Silicon Wafer Substrate"]
    stepB["Step B: Thermal Oxidation to Grow SiO2 Sacrificial Layer"]
    stepC["Step C: LPCVD Deposition of Polysilicon Structural Layer"]
    stepD["Step D: Photolithography and Reactive Ion Etch of Polysilicon"]
    stepE["Step E: DRIE or Wet Etch to Release Cantilever using Hydrofluoric Acid HF"]
    stepF["Step F: Final MEMS Cantilever Free to Move"]

    stepA --> stepB --> stepC --> stepD --> stepE --> stepF
```

**KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to "explain surface micromachining," always name the **sacrificial layer** explicitly (here, SiO₂ etched by HF) and explain why it is **"sacrificial"** — it is dissolved away to release the moving structure.

---

## 4.3 System Architecture — Probe-Based Storage (Millipede-Type)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph probeArray["2-D MEMS Cantilever Array (32x32 or 1024 Tips)"]
        pa1["Tip 1 Cantilever"]
        pa2["Tip 2 Cantilever"]
        pa3["Tip N Cantilever"]
    end

    pa1 --- pa2
    pa2 --- pa3

    subgraph medium["Polymer Storage Medium (PMMA)"]
        med1["Indentation Bits (Thermomechanical)"]
    end

    subgraph electronics["CMOS Read/Write & X-Y Scanner"]
        elec1["Cantilever Driver Heater"]
        elec2["Sense Resistor Bridge"]
        elec3["X-Y Electrostatic Scanner"]
    end

    probeArray --> medium
    electronics --> probeArray
    elec1 --> pa1
    elec2 --> pa1
    elec3 --> medium
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** Each cantilever is a **MEMS device**, the heater/sensor electronics are **CMOS**, and the X-Y scanner is another **MEMS electrostatic actuator**. The 2-D array enables **massive parallelism** — up to **1 Tbit/in²** with 32×32 tips operating at 100 kHz each.

---

## 4.4 Comparison Flow — Capacitive vs. Piezoelectric MEMS Actuator

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph capacitive["Capacitive Comb Drive"]
        c1["Applied Voltage V"]
        c2["Electric Field in Gap"]
        c3["Electrostatic Attraction"]
        c4["Comb Fingers Move"]
        c1 --> c2 --> c3 --> c4
    end

    subgraph piezo["Piezoelectric Bender"]
        p1["Applied Voltage V"]
        p2["Strain in PZT Layer"]
        p3["Cantilever Bends"]
        p4["Displacement at Tip"]
        p1 --> p2 --> p3 --> p4
    end
```

| Property | Capacitive | Piezoelectric |
|---|---|---|
| Drive voltage | 5–100 V | 1–10 V |
| Displacement | 1–10 µm | 0.1–1 µm |
| Force | µN–mN | µN–mN |
| Power (static) | Zero (ideal) | Leakage current |
| Used in storage | VCM, micro-actuator | Dual-stage Millipede |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]
**Define MEMS. List four key enabling technologies used in MEMS fabrication.**

**Model Answer:**
MEMS (Micro-Electro-Mechanical Systems) are miniaturized devices that integrate mechanical elements, sensors, actuators, and electronic circuits on a common silicon substrate, with feature sizes typically in the **1 µm to 100 µm** range. Four enabling fabrication technologies are:
1. **Bulk micromachining** — anisotropic wet/dry etching of the silicon substrate.
2. **Surface micromachining** — depositing and patterning thin films above the substrate.
3. **Deep Reactive Ion Etching (DRIE)** — for high-aspect-ratio structures.
4. **Wafer bonding** — anodic or fusion bonding for hermetic packaging.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]
**Why is single-crystal silicon the preferred material for MEMS? Mention two material properties that matter most for storage applications.**

**Model Answer:**
Single-crystal silicon is preferred because it has **(i) high Young's modulus (≈ 169 GPa)** ensuring mechanical stiffness and **(ii) no mechanical fatigue or plastic deformation at room temperature**, giving long operational life. Other reasons include CMOS compatibility, excellent thermal conductivity, and the ability to grow a high-quality SiO₂ sacrificial layer. For storage, the **stiffness** enables high-frequency resonators for probe storage, and the **surface smoothness** enables nano-scale flying-height sliders.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted Past Pattern]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the working principle of a MEMS cantilever-based accelerometer used for free-fall detection in Hard Disk Drives. Derive the relationship between the proof-mass displacement and the applied acceleration.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A silicon cantilever accelerometer has a proof mass of $m_p = 5 \times 10^{-7}$ kg, a spring constant of $k = 1$ N/m, and a gap of $d_0 = 2$ µm with 100 comb fingers each of area $100$ µm × 2 µm. Calculate (i) the displacement under 1 g acceleration, and (ii) the fractional capacitance change.

---

**Model Solution for Q-A(a):**

A MEMS accelerometer consists of a **proof mass** suspended on flexible **cantilever beams**, with **comb-finger electrodes** fixed to the substrate. When the HDD experiences acceleration $a$ (e.g., during a fall), Newton's second law gives an inertial force $F = m_p \, a$ on the proof mass. The mass displaces by $\Delta x = m_p a / k$ until balanced by the spring restoring force. The comb fingers move, changing the gap $d_0$ to $d_0 - \Delta x$ on one side and $d_0 + \Delta x$ on the other, producing a **differential capacitance change**:

$$\dfrac{\Delta C}{C_0} = \dfrac{\Delta x}{d_0}$$

The drive electronics detect this change, identify the drop, and within **5 ms** park the head off the platter.

**Derivation:**

Starting from force balance on the proof mass:

$$m_p \, a = k \, \Delta x \quad \Rightarrow \quad \Delta x = \dfrac{m_p \, a}{k}$$

The static capacitance of the comb structure with $N$ fingers, each of area $A$, at gap $d_0$:

$$C_0 = N \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d_0}$$

For small displacement $\Delta x \ll d_0$, the change in capacitance is:

$$\Delta C \approx C_0 \dfrac{\Delta x}{d_0} = \dfrac{N \varepsilon_0 A}{d_0^{2}} \Delta x$$

Substituting $\Delta x$:

$$\Delta C = \dfrac{N \varepsilon_0 A}{d_0^{2}} \cdot \dfrac{m_p a}{k}$$

The fractional change is the **sensitivity** $S$:

$$S = \dfrac{\Delta C / C_0}{a} = \dfrac{m_p}{k \, d_0}$$

**Diagram (as described):** A central proof mass suspended by 4 folded-flexure cantilevers with interdigitated comb fingers on either side. Anchor points on the substrate. Output pads for $\Delta C$ sensing.

---

**Model Solution for Q-A(b):**

**Given:**
- $m_p = 5 \times 10^{-7}$ kg
- $k = 1$ N/m
- $d_0 = 2$ µm $= 2 \times 10^{-6}$ m
- $N = 100$
- Finger area $A = 100 \text{ µm} \times 2 \text{ µm} = 2 \times 10^{-10}$ m²
- $a = 1g = 9.81$ m/s²

**Step 1: Compute displacement.**

$$\Delta x = \dfrac{m_p a}{k} = \dfrac{(5 \times 10^{-7})(9.81)}{1} = 4.905 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m} \approx 4.9 \text{ µm}$$

**Step 2: Compute static capacitance.**

$$C_0 = N \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d_0} = 100 \cdot \dfrac{(8.854 \times 10^{-12})(2 \times 10^{-10})}{2 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$C_0 = 100 \cdot \dfrac{1.7708 \times 10^{-21}}{2 \times 10^{-6}} = 100 \cdot 8.854 \times 10^{-16} = 8.854 \times 10^{-14} \text{ F} \approx 88.5 \text{ fF}$$

**Step 3: Compute fractional change.**

$$\dfrac{\Delta C}{C_0} = \dfrac{\Delta x}{d_0} = \dfrac{4.9 \times 10^{-6}}{2 \times 10^{-6}} = 2.45$$

> This is >100% — meaning the small-signal assumption has broken down! In practice, the design would use $k$ ≈ 10 N/m to keep $\Delta x \ll d_0$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #1:** Students often forget that the small-signal approximation $\Delta C / C_0 = \Delta x / d_0$ is **only valid when $\Delta x \ll d_0$**. If your numerical answer gives $\Delta C / C_0 > 10\%$, you must state that the linear model is invalid. Marks are lost otherwise.

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Internal Choice) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted Past Pattern]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With reference to HDD storage, explain the role of the MEMS air-bearing surface (ABS) slider. What determines the flying height, and how is the lift force generated?

**(b) [7 Marks]** A piezoelectric cantilever is used as a write head in a probe-based storage device. The cantilever has $L = 50$ µm, $w = 10$ µm, $t = 1$ µm, $E = 169$ GPa, and $\rho = 2330$ kg/m³. Calculate (i) the spring constant, and (ii) the first-mode resonant frequency.

---

**Model Solution for Q-B(a):**

The **air-bearing surface (ABS)** is the bottom face of the silicon slider that carries the read/write head. As the platter spins (typically **5400–15000 rpm**), a thin wedge of air is dragged under the slider. The **Couette–Poiseuille flow** of this air film produces a positive pressure that lifts the slider off the platter, maintaining a stable **flying height of 3–10 nm**.

**Factors determining flying height:**
1. **Linear velocity $V$** of the platter at the head location.
2. **Slider geometry** (rail pattern, step height, taper angle).
3. **Air viscosity $\mu$** (temperature-dependent).
4. **Load force** applied by the suspension arm pressing the slider down.

**Lift force** $F_L$ is produced by integrating the positive pressure over the ABS:

$$F_L = \int P(x,y) \, dA$$

A simplified Couette-flow expression for a flat slider of length $L$ and width $w$:

$$P = \dfrac{6 \mu V L}{h^{3}}$$

where $h$ is the local gap. The lift force scales as $V/h^{3}$, meaning **higher velocity or smaller gap gives exponentially more lift**.

**Force balance at steady flying height:**

$$F_L(h) = F_{\text{load}}$$

**Real-world importance:** The flying height is the **single most critical parameter** limiting areal density. A reduction from 10 nm to 5 nm allows a **~2× increase in linear bit density** (since the magnetic field from a transition falls off as $1/h$).

---

**Model Solution for Q-B(b):**

**Given:**
- $L = 50 \text{ µm} = 50 \times 10^{-6}$ m
- $w = 10 \text{ µm} = 10 \times 10^{-6}$ m
- $t = 1 \text{ µm} = 1 \times 10^{-6}$ m
- $E = 169 \text{ GPa} = 169 \times 10^{9}$ Pa
- $\rho = 2330$ kg/m³

**Part (i): Spring constant of cantilever.**

$$k = \dfrac{E w t^{3}}{4 L^{3}}$$

$$k = \dfrac{(169 \times 10^{9})(10 \times 10^{-6})(1 \times 10^{-6})^{3}}{4 (50 \times 10^{-6})^{3}}$$

Numerator:

$$169 \times 10^{9} \times 10 \times 10^{-6} \times 1 \times 10^{-18} = 1.69 \times 10^{-12}$$

Denominator:

$$4 \times (1.25 \times 10^{-13}) = 5.0 \times 10^{-13}$$

$$k = \dfrac{1.69 \times 10^{-12}}{5.0 \times 10^{-13}} = 3.38 \text{ N/m}$$

**Part (ii): First-mode resonant frequency.**

Mass of the cantilever:

$$m = \rho \, w \, t \, L = 2330 \times (10 \times 10^{-6})(1 \times 10^{-6})(50 \times 10^{-6})$$

$$m = 2330 \times 5 \times 10^{-16} = 1.165 \times 10^{-12} \text{ kg}$$

Effective mass:

$$m_{eff} = 0.24 \, m = 0.24 \times 1.165 \times 10^{-12} = 2.796 \times 10^{-13} \text{ kg}$$

First-mode frequency:

$$f_1 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m_{eff}}} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{3.38}{2.796 \times 10^{-13}}}$$

$$\dfrac{k}{m_{eff}} = 1.209 \times 10^{13}$$

$$\sqrt{1.209 \times 10^{13}} = 3.477 \times 10^{6} \text{ rad/s}$$

$$f_1 = \dfrac{3.477 \times 10^{6}}{2 \pi} = 5.53 \times 10^{5} \text{ Hz} \approx 553 \text{ kHz}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #2:** A common student mistake is using the **total** mass $m$ instead of the **effective mass** $m_{eff} = 0.24 m$ in the resonant-frequency formula. This gives a frequency **2.04× too low** (≈ 271 kHz), which is **2 marks lost**. Also, students often forget to convert the eigenvalue 1.875² when using the alternative formula. Always show the substitution step.

---

## 5.3 KTU 2024 Mark Distribution Reminder

| Section | Marks per Q | No. of Q | Total |
|---|---|---|---|
| Part A (Short Answer) | 3 | 2 (out of ~4) | 6 |
| Part B (Full Answer) | 14 | 2 (with internal choice) | 28 |
| **Total** | — | — | **34** (typical) |

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (MUST memorise for KTU ESE):**

- **MEMS definition** — Feature size **1–100 µm**, integrates mechanical + electrical on silicon.
- **Cantilever spring constant** — $k = \dfrac{E w t^3}{4 L^3}$ (cubic dependence on $t$ and $L$).
- **Effective mass** of a uniform cantilever = **0.24 m** (not the total mass).
- **First-mode frequency formula** — $f_1 = \dfrac{1.875^2}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{EI}{\rho A L^4}}$.
- **Electrostatic force** — $F_e = \dfrac{1}{2}\dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A V^2}{d^2}$.
- **Pull-in voltage** — $V_{PI} = \sqrt{\dfrac{8 k d_0^3}{27 \varepsilon_0 A}}$, occurs at $d = \dfrac{2}{3} d_0$.
- **Capacitive sensitivity** of accelerometer — $S = \dfrac{m_p}{k d_0}$.
- **Flying height** of HDD slider — **3–10 nm**, governed by Couette flow pressure.
- **Areal density** — $AD = BPI \times TPI$, drives storage capacity.
- **Sacrificial layer** in surface micromachining is **SiO₂**, etched by **HF**.
- **Young's modulus of Si ⟨100⟩** = **169 GPa**; **Density** = **2330 kg/m³**.
