- **Probe storage** (Millipede) uses thermomechanical writing on a **PMMA polymer** medium with **2-D cantilever arrays**.
- **Free-fall detection** response time must be **< 5 ms** to prevent head crash.
- **Piezo coefficient** of PZT $d_{31} \approx -320$ pm/V; piezoelectric cantilevers used as **dual-stage actuators** in HDDs.
- **Comb drive** is the standard electrostatic actuator; $N$ fingers multiplies the force and capacitance by $N$.
- **DRIE** is the process that makes high-aspect-ratio MEMS structures possible.
- Always state **units** in numerical answers (KTU explicitly awards unit-mark).
- Always check the **small-signal assumption** when using $\Delta C / C_0 = \Delta x / d_0$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Flash Memories
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST867 - STORAGE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Flash Memories

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Flash Memory** is a non-volatile semiconductor memory technology that stores data in **floating-gate MOSFET** cells. It is electrically erasable and programmable, allowing data to be retained even when power is removed. The term "flash" refers to the fact that the entire memory array (or a large block of it) can be erased in a single operation, or a "flash," unlike older EEPROMs which required byte-level erasure.

Flash memory serves as the foundational storage technology in nearly all modern portable electronics, solid-state drives (SSDs), USB drives, memory cards (SD, microSD, CF), and embedded systems. It is organized into two principal architectures:

1. **NOR Flash** — random access, byte-erasable, used for code execution (e.g., BIOS, firmware).
2. **NAND Flash** — sequential/page-based access, block-erasable, used for high-density data storage (e.g., SSDs, smartphones).

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU 2024 Metrics to Memorize:**
> - **SLC (Single-Level Cell)** — stores 1 bit/cell.
> - **MLC (Multi-Level Cell)** — stores 2 bits/cell.
> - **TLC (Triple-Level Cell)** — stores 3 bits/cell.
> - **QLC (Quad-Level Cell)** — stores 4 bits/cell.
> - **Endurance:** SLC ≈ **100,000 P/E cycles**; MLC ≈ **10,000**; TLC ≈ **1,000–3,000**; QLC ≈ **1,000**.
> - **Data Retention:** typically **10 years** at room temperature.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **water tank with a controllable gate** sitting above a drainage pipe:

- The **water level** inside the tank represents the **threshold voltage** of the cell.
- The **floating gate** is the tank itself — it is electrically isolated, so water (electrons) can stay trapped for years.
- **Program (Write) operation** = pumping water (electrons) into the tank through a one-way valve. This raises the water level, making the cell harder to "drain" (read as a '0' or '1' depending on level).
- **Erase operation** = opening a release valve that drains all water at once from the entire tank block. This is the "flash" — entire sectors get emptied together.
- **Read operation** = gently checking whether water flows out under a small test pressure. If water flows, the level is low (erased); if it does not, the tank is full (programmed).

> **Key Insight:** Because the floating gate is **electrically isolated** (wrapped in oxide), trapped electrons can stay for **10+ years** without a power source. This is what makes flash *non-volatile*.

## 1.3 Cell Architecture — The Floating-Gate MOSFET

The fundamental building block is a **floating-gate transistor**, which has *two* gates stacked above the channel:

- **Control Gate (CG)** — the top gate, electrically connected to the word line.
- **Floating Gate (FG)** — completely surrounded by a thin layer of silicon dioxide (SiO₂) — the oxide. It is electrically **floating** (no connection).

Between the floating gate and the channel lies the **tunnel oxide** (≈ 7–10 nm). Between the floating gate and control gate lies the **inter-poly oxide** (or ONO — Oxide-Nitride-Oxide).

> [!NOTE]
> The **threshold voltage** $V_{TH}$ of the cell shifts depending on whether charge is stored on the floating gate. This shift is what encodes binary data.

| Cell State | Floating Gate Charge | Threshold Voltage $V_{TH}$ | Logical Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| Erased | No electrons (neutral) | Low (≈ 0 V to 1 V) | '1' (in NAND) |
| Programmed | Electrons present | High (≈ 4 V to 7 V) | '0' (in NAND) |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Core Operations of Flash Memory

### 2.1.1 Program (Write) Operation
The goal is to **inject electrons** into the floating gate, raising $V_{TH}$. Two physical mechanisms are used:

- **Hot Carrier Injection (HCI)** — predominantly used in **NOR flash**. A high drain-to-source voltage accelerates electrons in the channel; some gain enough energy ("hot" electrons) to overcome the Si–SiO₂ energy barrier (~3.2 eV) and get trapped on the floating gate.
- **Fowler–Nordheim (F-N) Tunneling** — used in **NAND flash**. A high voltage (~15–20 V) is applied across the control gate and substrate, creating a strong electric field across the thin tunnel oxide. Electrons **quantum-mechanically tunnel** through the oxide into the floating gate.

### 2.1.2 Erase Operation
Always performed using **Fowler–Nordheim tunneling**, but in the **reverse direction**:
- The substrate (or source) is raised to a high positive voltage (~20 V).
- The control gate is grounded (0 V).
- Electrons tunnel **out** of the floating gate back to the substrate.
- Erase is always done in **blocks** (typically 16 KB to 4 MB), never byte-by-byte.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** Erase is a *block* operation. To modify a single byte, the entire block must be read, modified in a buffer, erased, and rewritten. This is why flash requires a **Flash Translation Layer (FTL)** with garbage collection.

### 2.1.3 Read Operation
A small voltage is applied to the control gate, and the **drain current** is sensed. If the cell is programmed (high $V_{TH}$), the read voltage is insufficient to turn it on → no current → bit value detected. If erased (low $V_{TH}$), current flows → opposite bit value detected.

## 2.2 NOR vs NAND Flash — The Critical Comparison

| Parameter | NOR Flash | NAND Flash |
|---|---|---|
| **Architecture** | Cells connected in **parallel** to bit lines | Cells connected in **series** (like a NAND gate) |
| **Access Type** | **Random access** (byte-level) | **Sequential / page access** |
| **Erase Granularity** | Block (typically 64–256 KB) | Block (16 KB – 4 MB) |
| **Program Granularity** | Byte or word | Page (typically 4–16 KB) |
| **Read Speed** | Very fast (~50–100 ns) | Fast, but page-oriented |
| **Write/Erase Speed** | Slow (≈ 1 s per block) | Faster (≈ 1–2 ms per page) |
| **Density** | Lower (larger cell size) | Higher (smaller cell, ~40% area of NOR) |
| **Endurance** | ~100,000 cycles | ~10,000 cycles (SLC); ~1,000 (TLC) |
| **Use Case** | Firmware boot, BIOS | SSDs, smartphones, USB drives |
| **Interface** | Addressable memory bus | I/O-mapped, command-based |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **cheat sheet** for all standard numerical problems on Flash Memories in KTU 2024 ESE.

| # | Quantity | Formula / Expression | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cell capacity (bits/cell) | $C_{cell} = \log_2(L)$ where $L$ = number of voltage levels | bits | SLC: $C=1$, MLC: $C=2$, TLC: $C=3$, QLC: $C=4$ |
| 2 | Die capacity (per die) | $N_{cells} \times C_{cell}$ | bits | $N_{cells}$ = total cells |
| 3 | Program Time (per page) | $t_{prog} = N_{pages} \times t_{page\_prog}$ | seconds | $t_{page\_prog} \approx 200$–$500\,\mu s$ for NAND |
| 4 | Erase Time (per block) | $t_{erase} = t_{block\_erase}$ | seconds | $t_{erase} \approx 1$–$2$ ms (NAND) |
| 5 | Total Throughput (write) | $T = \frac{N_{pages} \times S_{page}}{t_{prog} + t_{erase}}$ | MB/s | $S_{page}$ = page size in bytes |
| 6 | Endurance | $E = P/E\ \text{cycles}$ | cycles | Depends on cell type |
| 7 | Bit Error Rate (raw) | $BER = \frac{N_{err}}{N_{total}}$ | dimensionless | Typical: $10^{-6}$ to $10^{-3}$ |
| 8 | RBER (per cell per read) | $RBER = \frac{N_{reads\_failed}}{N_{total\_reads}}$ | dimensionless | Used in ECC decisions |
| 9 | Effective Endurance with ECC | $E_{eff} = E \times \left(1 - \frac{t_{retention}}{T_{retention,max}}\right)$ | cycles | Retention-aware |
| 10 | Cost per Bit | $c = \frac{C_{die}}{N_{bits}}$ | $/bit | Used in cost optimization |
| 11 | F-N Tunneling Current density | $J_{FN} = A E_{ox}^2 e^{-B/E_{ox}}$ | A/cm² | $A,B$ = material constants |
| 12 | Energy per Program Operation | $E_{prog} = V_{prog} \cdot I_{prog} \cdot t_{prog}$ | Joules | $V_{prog} \approx 15$–$20$ V |

> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** These formulas drive SSD controller firmware design, endurance prediction in data centers, and storage-class memory (SCM) benchmarks. Wear-leveling algorithms (e.g., dynamic and static) use endurance formulas to balance writes across blocks.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Threshold Voltage Shift Due to Floating-Gate Charge

The threshold voltage of a flash cell is determined by the charge stored on the floating gate. Let us derive the relationship between $V_{TH}$ and the floating-gate charge $Q_{FG}$.

### Setup
Consider an n-channel floating-gate MOSFET. The control gate is at potential $V_{CG}$, the floating gate is at potential $V_{FG}$, and the source is grounded. Let:

- $C_{CG}$ = capacitance between control gate and floating gate.
- $C_{ox}$ = capacitance between floating gate and channel/substrate.
- $Q_{FG}$ = charge (electrons) stored on the floating gate.

### Step 1: Charge Neutrality at the Floating Gate
Since the floating gate is electrically isolated, the total charge on it must balance the capacitive coupling from the control gate and the channel. Applying Kirchhoff's current law (steady state, no current path):

$$
Q_{FG} = C_{CG} \cdot (V_{CG} - V_{FG}) + C_{ox} \cdot (V_{channel} - V_{FG})
$$

### Step 2: Solve for the Floating-Gate Potential $V_{FG}$
Rearranging for $V_{FG}$ (assuming $V_{channel} \approx 0$ at the onset of strong inversion near the source):

$$
V_{FG} = \frac{C_{CG} \cdot V_{CG} - Q_{FG}}{C_{CG} + C_{ox}}
$$

### Step 3: Substitute into the Standard Threshold Equation
The threshold condition for a regular MOSFET states that the channel surface potential must reach $2\phi_F$ (twice the Fermi potential). This condition is expressed in terms of the voltage at the gate (here, the floating gate):

$$
V_{FG} = V_{TH0} + 2\phi_F
$$

where $V_{TH0}$ is the threshold voltage of a *standard* MOSFET (no floating gate).

### Step 4: Combine to Find the Effective Threshold
Substituting the expression for $V_{FG}$ from Step 2:

$$
V_{FG} = \frac{C_{CG} \cdot V_{CG} - Q_{FG}}{C_{CG} + C_{ox}}
$$

Rearranging to solve for $V_{CG}$ at the threshold condition:

$$
V_{CG} \cdot C_{CG} = (V_{TH0} + 2\phi_F)(C_{CG} + C_{ox}) + Q_{FG}
$$

Therefore the new (apparent) threshold voltage seen at the control gate is:

$$
V_{TH} = V_{TH0} + 2\phi_F + \frac{Q_{FG}}{C_{CG}}
$$

### Step 5: Final Compact Form
Defining the **coupling ratio** $\alpha = \dfrac{C_{CG}}{C_{CG} + C_{ox}}$ (a value typically between 0.5 and 0.7 in real cells), we obtain the **canonical flash cell equation**:

$$
\boxed{V_{TH} = V_{TH0} + \frac{Q_{FG}}{C_{CG}}}
$$

> **Physical Interpretation:**
> - If $Q_{FG} = 0$ (erased cell), $V_{TH} = V_{TH0}$ (low).
> - If electrons are stored ($Q_{FG} < 0$ by convention, since electrons are negative), $V_{TH}$ *increases* (becomes more positive). Wait — careful! Since $Q_{FG}$ carries the sign of the actual charge, with electrons being negative, $Q_{FG} < 0$, the equation actually yields $V_{TH} < V_{TH0}$ in some sign conventions. **KTU's standard convention is:** the *magnitude* of the threshold shift is $\Delta V_{TH} = \dfrac{\vert Q_{FG} \vert}{C_{CG}}$.

> This is the equation flash memory engineers use to size coupling capacitors and predict cell behavior.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Total Memory Capacity of a NAND Die

A NAND die is organized in a hierarchical structure: **Die → Plane → Block → Page → Cell**.

Let:
- $N_{dies} = 1$ (single die)
- $N_{planes}$ = number of planes per die
- $N_{blocks}$ = number of blocks per plane
- $N_{pages}$ = number of pages per block
- $N_{cells}$ = number of cells per page
- $C_{cell}$ = bits per cell (SLC = 1, MLC = 2, TLC = 3, QLC = 4)

### Step 1: Number of Pages per Die

$$
N_{pages}^{total} = N_{planes} \times N_{blocks} \times N_{pages}
$$

### Step 2: Total Cells per Die

$$
N_{cells}^{total} = N_{pages}^{total} \times N_{cells}
$$

### Step 3: Total Bits per Die

$$
N_{bits} = N_{cells}^{total} \times C_{cell}
$$

### Step 4: Capacity in Bytes and GB

$$
\text{Capacity} = \frac{N_{bits}}{8}\ \text{bytes} = \frac{N_{bits}}{8 \times 2^{30}}\ \text{GB}
$$

### Example Calculation
Suppose a NAND die has:
- $N_{planes} = 2$
- $N_{blocks} = 2048$ per plane
- $N_{pages} = 256$ per block
- $N_{cells} = 16{,}384$ per page
- $C_{cell} = 2$ (MLC)

Then:

$$
N_{pages}^{total} = 2 \times 2048 \times 256 = 1{,}048{,}576
$$

$$
N_{cells}^{total} = 1{,}048{,}576 \times 16{,}384 = 1.717986 \times 10^{10}
$$

$$
N_{bits} = 1.717986 \times 10^{10} \times 2 = 3.435974 \times 10^{10}\ \text{bits}
$$

$$
\text{Capacity} = \frac{3.435974 \times 10^{10}}{8 \times 2^{30}} \approx 4\ \text{GB}
$$

> **Result:** A die with these parameters stores approximately **4 GB of MLC data** — consistent with early 2010s NAND die specifications.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Endurance Degradation Model

Endurance is the most critical reliability metric. As P/E cycles accumulate, the tunnel oxide degrades, leading to **stress-induced leakage current (SILC)** and electron trap buildup. A simplified model for the *remaining endurance* is:

### Step 1: Oxide Damage per Cycle
Each P/E cycle damages $N_{dmg}$ traps in the oxide:

$$
N_{traps}(n) = N_0 + n \cdot N_{dmg}
$$

where $n$ is the number of P/E cycles so far, and $N_0$ is the initial defect count.

### Step 2: Threshold Voltage Shift Over Time
The cumulative $V_{TH}$ shift is:

$$
\Delta V_{TH}(n) = \frac{q \cdot N_{traps}(n)}{C_{ox}}
$$

where $q$ is the electron charge.

### Step 3: Read Window Budget
A flash cell must maintain a minimum $V_{TH}$ separation $\Delta V_{win}$ between adjacent states (e.g., between '00' and '01' in MLC). The cell fails when:

$$
\Delta V_{TH}(n) > \Delta V_{win}
$$

### Step 4: Solve for Maximum Cycles (Endurance)

$$
\boxed{N_{endurance} = \frac{C_{ox} \cdot \Delta V_{win} - q \cdot N_0}{q \cdot N_{dmg}}}
$$

> **Engineering Insight:** Modern controllers use **dynamic wear leveling** to keep $n$ uniformly distributed across all blocks, maximizing die-level endurance to ~1–2× the per-block rating.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Cell Technology

| Parameter | SLC | MLC | TLC | QLC |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Bits/cell | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 |
| Voltage levels | 2 | 4 | 8 | 16 |
| Endurance (P/E cycles) | 100,000 | 10,000 | 1,000–3,000 | 500–1,000 |
| Read time (μs) | 25 | 50 | 75 | 100 |
| Program time (μs) | 200 | 600 | 1,200 | 1,800 |
| Cost/bit | High (1×) | ~0.5× | ~0.33× | ~0.25× |
| Data retention (years) | 10 | 10 | 5–7 | 3–5 |
| Typical use | Enterprise | Consumer SSD | Consumer SSD | Archival SSD |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: NAND Flash Die

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["NAND FLASH DIE"] --> B1["PLANE 0"]
    A --> B2["PLANE 1"]

    B1 --> C1["BLOCK 0"]
    B1 --> C2["BLOCK 1"]
    B1 --> C3["BLOCK N"]

    C1 --> D1["PAGE 0 4 KB Data + Spare"]
    C1 --> D2["PAGE 1 4 KB Data + Spare"]
    C1 --> D3["PAGE K"]

    D1 --> E1["WORD LINE WL 0 16K Cells"]
    D1 --> E2["WORD LINE WL 1 16K Cells"]
    D1 --> E3["BIT LINE BL Sense Amps"]

    E1 --> F1["FLOATING GATE MOSFET CELL"]
    E1 --> F2["FLOATING GATE MOSFET CELL"]
    E1 --> F3["FLOATING GATE MOSFET CELL"]

    A --> G1["ROW DECODER"]
    A --> G2["COLUMN DECODER"]
    A --> G3["PAGE BUFFER 16 KB"]
    A --> G4["CHARGE PUMP 20V VPP"]
    A --> G5["CONTROL LOGIC CMD Interface"]

    G5 --> H1["HOST CONTROLLER SSD Firmware"]
    H1 --> H2["FLASH TRANSLATION LAYER FTL"]
    H2 --> H3["Wear Leveling"]
    H2 --> H4["Garbage Collection"]
    H2 --> H5["Error Correction ECC LDPC"]
```

**Description:** This block diagram shows the hierarchical structure of a NAND die — from the full die down to individual floating-gate cells. The host controller issues commands through the control logic; the charge pump generates the high voltages (15–20 V) needed for program/erase. The FTL manages wear leveling, garbage collection, and ECC.

---

## 4.2 Operational Flow: Read / Program / Erase

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1["HOST COMMAND"] --> A2["FTL ROUTING"]

    A2 --> B1["READ COMMAND"]
    A2 --> B2["PROGRAM WRITE COMMAND"]
    A2 --> B3["ERASE COMMAND"]

    B1 --> C1["Apply Vread 4-5V to CG"]
    C1 --> C2["Sense Drain Current"]
    C2 --> C3{"Current flows?"}
    C3 -- Yes --> C4["Bit = 1 Erased"]
    C3 -- No --> C5["Bit = 0 Programmed"]

    B2 --> D1["Apply Vprog 15-20V to CG"]
    D1 --> D2["Channel ON Source Grounded"]
    D2 --> D3["Hot Carriers or FN Tunneling"]
    D3 --> D4["Electrons Stored on FG"]
    D4 --> D5["VTH Increases Cell Programmed"]

    B3 --> E1["Apply 20V to Substrate"]
    E1 --> E2["Apply 0V to CG"]
    E2 --> E3["FN Tunneling in Reverse"]
    E3 --> E4["Electrons Exit FG"]
    E4 --> E5["VTH Resets Block Erased"]
```

**Description:** This diagram traces the exact electrical sequence for each of the three fundamental flash operations. Note that **erase** is done at the **block** level (entire block reset at once), while **program** is done at the **page** level (4–16 KB).

---

## 4.3 Floating-Gate Cell Cross-Section (Schematic Representation)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1["CONTROL GATE Word Line"]
    A2["INTER-POLY DIELECTRIC ONO"]
    A3["FLOATING GATE Stores Charge"]
    A4["TUNNEL OXIDE SiO2 7-10 nm"]
    A5["SILICON SUBSTRATE Channel P-well"]
    A6["SOURCE N+"]
    A7["DRAIN N+"]

    A1 --- A2
    A2 --- A3
    A3 --- A4
    A4 --- A5
    A5 --- A6
    A5 --- A7
```

**Description:** A vertical cross-section of the floating-gate MOSFET. The floating gate is **completely surrounded by oxide**, which is what traps electrons for years. The tunnel oxide is the thinnest layer (~7–10 nm) and is the path through which electrons tunnel during program/erase.

---

## 4.4 Wear Leveling & FTL Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    H1["HOST OS LBA Logical Block Address"] --> F1["FTL LOGICAL TO PHYSICAL MAP"]
    F1 --> F2["WEAR LEVELING ENGINE"]
    F2 --> F3["HOT DATA Frequent Writes"]
    F2 --> F4["COLD DATA Rare Writes"]
    F2 --> F5["GARBAGE COLLECTION"]
    F1 --> F6["ECC ENGINE LDPC BCH"]
    F1 --> F7["BAD BLOCK MANAGEMENT"]
    F6 --> P1["PHYSICAL NAND DIES 0 to N"]
    F5 --> P1
    F3 --> P1
    F4 --> P1
    F7 --> P1
```

**Description:** The FTL is the brain of an SSD. It translates logical addresses to physical addresses, performs wear leveling, runs garbage collection, and manages ECC. This is essential because flash cannot overwrite data in-place — it must erase before writing.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define flash memory. List the two main types and state one key difference between them.**

**Model Answer:**
> Flash memory is a non-volatile semiconductor memory that uses floating-gate MOSFET cells to store data. The two main types are **NOR flash** and **NAND flash**.
>
> **Key Difference:**
> - **NOR flash** has cells connected in **parallel**, enabling **random byte-level access** with fast read times. It is used for code/firmware storage.
> - **NAND flash** has cells connected in **series**, enabling **higher density and sequential page access**. It is used for mass storage (SSDs, USB drives, memory cards).
>
> *(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for naming both types, 1 for the key difference.)*

---

### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**What is the role of the floating gate in a flash memory cell?**

**Model Answer:**
> The floating gate is a **conducting layer completely surrounded by oxide** within a MOSFET structure. Its role is to **trap and store electrical charge (electrons)** that represents the data bit.
>
> The presence or absence of charge on the floating gate **modifies the threshold voltage ($V_{TH}$)** of the transistor, which is detected during the read operation to determine whether the cell stores a '0' or a '1'. Because the floating gate is electrically isolated, stored electrons can remain for **~10 years** without power, giving flash its non-volatile property.
>
> *(3 marks: 1 for structural description, 1 for charge storage role, 1 for $V_{TH}$ modulation and non-volatility.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain with neat diagrams the structure and operation of a **floating-gate MOSFET** used in flash memory. Discuss **hot carrier injection** and **Fowler–Nordheim tunneling** as the two charge-transfer mechanisms. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A NAND flash die has 2 planes, with 2048 blocks per plane and 128 pages per block. Each page contains 16,384 cells. If the cell stores 2 bits (MLC), calculate the **total capacity in GB**. If the endurance is 5,000 P/E cycles per block, what is the **total number of program/erase operations** the die can support over its lifetime (assuming all blocks are used equally)? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to A(a):

**Structure of the Floating-Gate MOSFET:**

A flash memory cell is essentially an n-channel MOSFET with an **additional floating gate** inserted between the control gate and the channel. The structure consists of:

1. **Source** and **Drain** (n⁺ regions) in a p-type substrate.
2. **Tunnel Oxide** — a thin layer of SiO₂ (~7–10 nm) above the channel.
3. **Floating Gate** — a polysilicon layer, completely surrounded by oxide and **electrically isolated**.
4. **Inter-poly Dielectric (ONO)** — oxide-nitride-oxide sandwich above the floating gate.
5. **Control Gate** — top gate connected to the word line.

*(2 marks for the structure & diagram description)*

**Operation:**

| Operation | Mechanism | Voltage Conditions |
|---|---|---|
| Program (HCI) | Hot carrier injection | $V_{D} \approx 5$ V, $V_{CG} \approx 10$ V (NOR flash) |
| Program (FN) | Fowler–Nordheim tunneling | $V_{CG} \approx 15$–$20$ V, source/substrate grounded (NAND flash) |
| Erase | Fowler–Nordheim tunneling (reverse) | Substrate at ~20 V, control gate at 0 V |
| Read | $V_{TH}$ sensing | Small $V_{CG} \approx 4$–5 V applied, drain current measured |

**Hot Carrier Injection (HCI):**
- Used predominantly in **NOR flash**.
- A high voltage is applied between drain and source, accelerating electrons across the channel.
- Some electrons gain kinetic energy > 3.2 eV (the Si–SiO₂ barrier) and become "hot."
- These hot electrons are injected **over** the energy barrier onto the floating gate.
- This raises the threshold voltage, marking the cell as programmed.

**Fowler–Nordheim (F-N) Tunneling:**
- Used in **NAND flash** for both program and erase.
- A strong electric field across the thin tunnel oxide (~10 MV/cm) causes electrons to **quantum-mechanically tunnel** through the oxide.
- During program, electrons tunnel **into** the floating gate.
- During erase, electrons tunnel **out** of the floating gate back to the substrate.
- This is reversible and consumes less current than HCI.

*(5 marks: 1 each for structure, operation table, HCI explanation, F-N explanation, comparison/comment.)*

---

#### Model Solution to A(b):

**Given:**
- $N_{planes} = 2$
- $N_{blocks/plane} = 2048$
- $N_{pages/block} = 128$
- $N_{cells/page} = 16{,}384$
- $C_{cell} = 2$ bits (MLC)
- Endurance $E = 5{,}000$ P/E cycles per block

**Step 1: Total Pages in the Die**

$$
N_{pages}^{total} = 2 \times 2048 \times 128 = 524{,}288\ \text{pages}
$$

**Step 2: Total Cells in the Die**

$$
N_{cells}^{total} = 524{,}288 \times 16{,}384 = 8.589934 \times 10^9\ \text{cells}
$$

**Step 3: Total Bits (MLC: 2 bits/cell)**

$$
N_{bits} = 8.589934 \times 10^9 \times 2 = 1.717987 \times 10^{10}\ \text{bits}
$$

**Step 4: Convert to GB**

$$
\text{Capacity} = \frac{1.717987 \times 10^{10}}{8 \times 2^{30}} = \frac{1.717987 \times 10^{10}}{8.589935 \times 10^9}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Capacity} \approx 2\ \text{GB}}
$$

**Step 5: Total P/E Operations Over Lifetime**

Total blocks in the die:

$$
N_{blocks}^{total} = 2 \times 2048 = 4096\ \text{blocks}
$$

Total lifetime P/E operations (assuming all blocks used equally):

$$
N_{PE}^{total} = 4096 \times 5{,}000 = 2.048 \times 10^7
$$

$$
\boxed{N_{PE}^{total} = 20{,}480{,}000\ \text{program/erase operations}}
$$

*(7 marks: 1 for setting up equations, 1 for total pages, 1 for total cells, 1 for bits, 1 for GB conversion, 1 for total blocks, 1 for total P/E operations.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** Compare **NOR and NAND flash** architectures in terms of cell connection, access method, density, endurance, and use case. Which is more suitable for storing a smartphone's operating system boot code, and why? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A TLC NAND flash SSD has a raw capacity of 256 GB. The over-provisioning factor is 7% and the controller reserves 1 GB for bad block replacement. Calculate the **user-addressable capacity**. If the SSD is rated for 1,500 P/E cycles and the write amplification factor (WAF) is 2.5, calculate the **total bytes that can be written (TBW)** over its lifetime, assuming an even write distribution. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to B(a):

| Parameter | NOR Flash | NAND Flash |
|---|---|---|
| **Cell Connection** | Cells in parallel between bit lines and source line | Cells in series (Drain-Source-Drain chain) — like a NAND gate |
| **Access Method** | Random byte access, addressable memory bus | Sequential page access, command-based I/O |
| **Density** | Lower (~40% larger cell) | Higher (~40% smaller cell, more bits/area) |
| **Erase Granularity** | 64–256 KB blocks | 16 KB – 4 MB blocks |
| **Endurance** | ~100,000 P/E cycles | ~1,000–10,000 P/E cycles (SLC/MLC) |
| **Read Speed** | ~50–100 ns (very fast) | ~25–100 μs (page-oriented) |
| **Write/Erase Speed** | Slow | Faster |
| **Use Case** | Boot code, BIOS, firmware | Mass storage (SSDs, USB, memory cards) |

**Most suitable for smartphone boot code: NOR flash.**

**Reason:**
1. Boot code requires **fast random read access** — NOR provides byte-level random access, allowing the CPU to fetch instructions directly via execute-in-place (XIP).
2. Boot code is **written rarely** (usually once at factory or OTA update), so NOR's **lower write speed** is not a concern.
3. NOR's **high endurance** ensures the boot code region survives many read cycles.
4. NAND would require the entire boot image to be loaded into RAM first (no XIP), increasing boot time and RAM consumption.

*(7 marks: 1 for each comparison row, 1 for the answer NOR, 1 for the three correct reasons.)*

---

#### Model Solution to B(b):

**Given:**
- Raw capacity = 256 GB
- Over-provisioning (OP) = 7%
- Bad block reserve = 1 GB
- Endurance = 1,500 P/E cycles
- WAF = 2.5

**Step 1: Capacity Lost to Over-Provisioning**

$$
C_{OP} = 256\ \text{GB} \times 0.07 = 17.92\ \text{GB}
$$

**Step 2: User-Addressable Capacity**

$$
C_{user} = 256\ \text{GB} - C_{OP} - 1\ \text{GB (bad blocks)}
$$

$$
C_{user} = 256 - 17.92 - 1 = 237.08\ \text{GB}
$$

$$
\boxed{C_{user} \approx 237.08\ \text{GB}}
$$

**Step 3: Total Raw P/E Operations**

Total blocks (conceptually):

$$
N_{PE}^{raw} = 1{,}500\ \text{cycles}
$$

**Step 4: Effective Host Writes per Cycle**

Because of write amplification factor (WAF), each host write causes WAF internal writes. Effective cycles per logical block:

$$
E_{eff} = \frac{1{,}500}{WAF} = \frac{1{,}500}{2.5} = 600\ \text{logical cycles}
$$

**Step 5: Total Host Bytes Writable (TBW)**

$$
TBW = C_{user} \times E_{eff} = 237.08\ \text{GB} \times 600
$$

$$
TBW = 142{,}248\ \text{GB} \approx 142.25\ \text{TB}
$$

$$
\boxed{TBW \approx 142.25\ \text{TB}}
$$

*(7 marks: 1 for OP calculation, 1 for user capacity, 1 for bad block subtraction, 1 for WAF definition, 1 for effective cycles, 1 for TBW formula, 1 for final answer with units.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in Flash Memory Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "program" and "erase":** Many students incorrectly state that *erase* uses hot carrier injection. **Correct:** HCI is only used in *programming* NOR flash. *Erase* always uses **Fowler–Nordheim tunneling in the reverse direction**. (-1 to -2 marks)
>
> 2. **Wrong unit conversions:** GB = $2^{30}$ bytes (not $10^9$). Always use $2^{30}$ for binary prefixes. Mixing GiB and GB leads to errors of ~7%. (-1 mark)
>
> 3. **Forgetting over-provisioning and WAF:** In TBW calculations, students often forget to subtract OP or to divide endurance by WAF. The WAF reflects that **one host write causes multiple internal NAND writes** — this is the single most common mistake. (-2 marks)
>
> 4. **Mixing up SLC, MLC, TLC, QLC bit counts:** $C_{cell} = \log_2(L)$. TLC = 3 bits, **not** 4. QLC = 4 bits. (-1 mark)
>
> 5. **Saying NOR has "no erase" or "byte erase":** NOR flash **does** have block erase, just smaller blocks than NAND. (-1 mark)

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Flash Memory — Rapid Revision Checklist**

- [x] **Flash Memory** is a **non-volatile** memory using **floating-gate MOSFETs** to store charge.
- [x] **Two types:** **NOR** (random access, code storage) and **NAND** (sequential, mass storage).
- [x] **Three operations:** **Program** (write), **Erase** (block-level), **Read** (sense $V_{TH}$).
- [x] **Two charge-transfer mechanisms:** **HCI** (NOR program) and **F-N Tunneling** (NAND program, all erase).
- [x] **Erase is ALWAYS block-level** — cannot erase a single byte.
- [x] **Cell types:** SLC (1 bit, 100k cycles), MLC (2 bits, 10k), TLC (3 bits, 1k–3k), QLC (4 bits, ~1k).
- [x] **Threshold voltage equation:** $V_{TH} = V_{TH0} + \dfrac{Q_{FG}}{C_{CG}}$.
- [x] **Endurance** degrades with P/E cycles due to **tunnel oxide damage** and **electron traps**.
- [x] **Wear leveling** is mandatory in SSDs to distribute writes across blocks.
- [x] **Flash Translation Layer (FTL)** handles logical-to-physical mapping, garbage collection, and ECC.
- [x] **Data retention:** ~10 years (SLC/MLC), ~5 years (TLC/QLC) at room temperature.
- [x] **Cost per bit:** QLC < TLC < MLC < SLC (inversely related to endurance).
- [x] **NAND architecture:** Die → Plane → Block → Page → Cell.
- [x] **Standard page size:** 4 KB to 16 KB. **Standard block size:** 16 KB to 4 MB.
- [x] **TBW formula:** $TBW = C_{user} \times \dfrac{E_{cycles}}{WAF}$.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Processing In Memory (PIM)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST867 - STORAGE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Processing In Memory (PIM)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Processing-in-Memory (PIM)** is a non-von-Neumann computing paradigm in which computational logic is physically co-located with, or integrated into, the memory array itself, thereby enabling arithmetic and logical operations to be executed directly on the data residing in memory. The objective of PIM is to mitigate the **Memory Wall** and the **Data Movement Bottleneck** that have dominated modern computing, where the energy and latency costs of shuttling data between the Central Processing Unit (CPU) and off-chip Dynamic Random-Access Memory (DRAM) often exceed the cost of the actual computation by orders of magnitude (typically **100x to 1000x**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PECST867):** *Processing-in-Memory (PIM) is an emerging computing architecture that places processing elements inside or in close physical proximity to memory dies (DRAM, SRAM, NAND, or ReRAM), eliminating the long, energy-hungry off-chip interconnects that characterize traditional von-Neumann systems. It encompasses both Near-Data Processing (NDP) and In-Memory Computing (IMC).*

The two principal classifications of PIM adopted in the KTU curriculum are:

1. **Near-Data Processing (NDP):** The processing element is placed *adjacent* to the memory array (e.g., in the logic die of a 3D-stacked DRAM), but still uses a separate data path inside the memory chip.
2. **In-Memory Computing (IMC):** The processing element is embedded *inside* the memory cell array, leveraging the analog/mixed-signal properties of memory devices (especially ReRAM/MRAM) to perform computation in place (e.g., matrix-vector multiplication via Ohm's law and Kirchhoff's current law).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

Imagine a giant post office system. In a **traditional von-Neumann architecture**, every single letter from every village must be physically trucked to a *single* central sorting office (the CPU). Even though the trucks are fast, the sheer volume of mail creates kilometer-long traffic jams on the highway (the memory bus). The sorting itself is lightning fast, but the trucks spend most of their time *stuck in traffic*.

In the **PIM architecture**, the sorting logic is moved *into* the regional post offices (the memory banks). Each regional office sorts the mail it holds, and only the final, already-sorted bundles are shipped to headquarters. The highway traffic collapses, fuel consumption drops dramatically, and the system becomes orders of magnitude more efficient.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Metrics to Memorize:**
> - Off-chip DRAM access energy ≈ **100 pJ/bit** (DRAM read) to **1300 pJ/bit** (DRAM write)
> - 32-bit integer ALU operation energy ≈ **1 pJ**
> - Hence, the cost of *fetching one byte* of data from DRAM is roughly equivalent to performing **100+ ALU operations**.
> - This ratio is the **fundamental economic justification** for PIM.

### 1.3 The Two Problems PIM Solves

| Problem | Description | Quantitative Manifestation |
|---|---|---|
| **Memory Wall** | Processor speed has grown ~2x every 1.5 years, but DRAM latency has improved <10% per decade | Compute throughput is throttled by memory bandwidth |
| **Data Movement Wall** | Energy spent moving data dominates total system energy | Up to **90%** of data-center accelerator energy is spent on data movement, not compute |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Von-Neumann Bottleneck — Why PIM Exists

A conventional computer is built on the **von-Neumann architecture (1945)**, which has three structural components:

- **CPU (Arithmetic Logic Unit + Registers + Control Unit)**
- **Memory Unit (DRAM hierarchy)**
- **Interconnect Bus (Address bus + Data bus)**

The defining feature of von-Neumann is the **separation of processing and storage**. Every instruction that the CPU executes requires data to be transferred *across* this bus. As transistor counts scaled with Moore's Law, this bus became the system's primary bottleneck.

The **total system energy** for a data-intensive workload can be modeled as:

$$E_{total} = E_{compute} + E_{data\_movement}$$

In modern systems executing workloads such as graph analytics, recommendation systems, and neural-network inference, the second term dominates:

$$E_{data\_movement} \gg E_{compute}$$

This inequality is the **economic and engineering rationale** for PIM.

### 2.2 Architectural Taxonomy of PIM

PIM implementations can be classified along **two orthogonal axes**:

#### Axis A — Location of Compute
- **Logic-layer PIM:** Computational units placed in a *separate logic die* but inside the same package (e.g., the base die of 3D-stacked High Bandwidth Memory, or HBM).
- **Array-internal PIM:** Computational units placed *inside* the memory cell array, often exploiting analog physics (e.g., ReRAM crossbars).
- **Memory-side PIM:** Computational units placed on the DRAM DIMM module itself (e.g., UPMEM PIM-DIMM).

#### Axis B — Memory Technology Used
- **DRAM-based PIM** (most mature commercially, e.g., Samsung HBM-PIM, SK Hynix AiM, UPMEM)
- **SRAM-based PIM** (used in caches and AI accelerators)
- **NAND-Flash-based PIM** (emerging, for SSD-internal analytics)
- **ReRAM/MRAM-based PIM** (analog matrix multiplication)

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table compiles **every quantitative expression** a KTU 2024 student must know for PIM-related exam questions.

| Symbol | Concept | Formula | Units | Engineering Interpretation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $AI$ | Arithmetic Intensity | $AI = \dfrac{W}{Q}$ | FLOPs/Byte | Ratio of useful work to data movement |
| $P$ | Achievable Performance | $P = \min(\pi, \beta \cdot AI)$ | FLOPs/sec | Bounded by either compute or memory |
| $\pi$ | Peak Compute Throughput | $\pi = f_{clk} \cdot N_{cores} \cdot \text{IPC}$ | FLOPs/sec | Hardware compute ceiling |
| $\beta$ | Peak Memory Bandwidth | $\beta = \dfrac{B_{bus} \cdot f_{bus}}{8}$ | Bytes/sec | Memory-side delivery ceiling |
| $E_{dm}$ | Data-Movement Energy | $E_{dm} = Q \cdot e_{bit}$ | Joules | Energy = bytes × per-bit cost |
| $E_{c}$ | Compute Energy | $E_{c} = W \cdot e_{op}$ | Joules | Energy = ops × per-op cost |
| $\eta$ | Energy-Efficiency Gain | $\eta = \dfrac{E_{c}}{E_{dm}}$ | Dimensionless | Justification ratio for PIM |
| $R$ | Reuse Factor | $R = \dfrac{\text{ops on data}}{\text{times data is fetched}}$ | ops/fetch | Higher $R$ = PIM more attractive |
| $T_{mem}$ | Memory Latency | $T_{mem} = T_{row} + T_{col} + T_{prop}$ | seconds | Time to deliver a single cache line |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Boundary Condition for the Roofline Model:**
> A workload is said to be **compute-bound** when $AI \geq \dfrac{\pi}{\beta}$, and **memory-bound** when $AI < \dfrac{\pi}{\beta}$. PIM is *most beneficial* for **memory-bound** workloads.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

PIM is not merely an academic concept — it is the architectural backbone of:

- **Data-center recommendation systems** (Meta, Google) where embedding tables exceed cache capacity.
- **Large Language Model inference** (KV-cache movement dominates total energy).
- **Genomic and graph analytics** (irregular memory access patterns).
- **Autonomous vehicle edge inference** (energy-budget constrained).
- **Database query acceleration** (in-memory analytics on columnar stores).

The **commercial milestones** a KTU examiner expects you to know:

| Year | Milestone | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| 2019 | UPMEM PIM-DIMM launched | First commercial DRAM-PIM product |
| 2021 | Samsung HBM-PIM announced | First 3D-stacked HBM with internal logic |
| 2022 | SK Hynix AiM (Accelerator-in-Memory) | GDDR6-based PIM for AI training |
| 2023 | Mythic Analog Matrix Processor | ReRAM-based analog PIM at scale |
| 2024 | Industry standardization (CXL-PIM, UCIe) | Disaggregated memory with PIM |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Memory Wall Inequality

The original Memory Wall inequality, formulated by Wulf and McKee (1995), quantifies the divergence between processor and memory speeds.

**Step 1 — Define the clock period of the processor.**

Let $f_{cpu}$ be the CPU clock frequency. The time per CPU cycle is:

$$T_{cpu} = \frac{1}{f_{cpu}}$$

**Step 2 — Define the DRAM access time.**

DRAM access time $T_{DRAM}$ has remained nearly constant over the past two decades (≈ **50–100 ns**), since it is dominated by the physical capacitance of bit-lines and sense-amplifier recovery time.

**Step 3 — Express the number of CPU cycles a memory access must wait.**

$$N_{stall} = \frac{T_{DRAM}}{T_{cpu}}$$

**Step 4 — Apply historical scaling.**

If $f_{cpu}$ doubles every 18 months (Moore's Law), but $T_{DRAM}$ improves by only 10% per decade, then:

$$N_{stall}(t) = N_{stall}(0) \cdot 2^{t/1.5}$$

**Step 5 — State the Memory Wall inequality.**

The system is memory-bound when the average time spent waiting for memory exceeds the time spent on computation:

$$\text{Memory Wall} \iff N_{stall} \cdot T_{cpu} > T_{cpu} \cdot \text{ops per memory access}$$

Equivalently, in the limit:

$$\lim_{t \to \infty} \frac{f_{cpu}}{1/T_{DRAM}} = \infty$$

This proves that the *relative* cost of memory access grows without bound, which is the mathematical justification for PIM.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — The Roofline Model for PIM

The Roofline Model (Williams, Waterman & Patterson, 2009) provides a unified performance bound for any computing system — including PIM.

**Step 1 — Define Arithmetic Intensity.**

The Arithmetic Intensity $AI$ of a kernel is the number of floating-point operations performed per byte of DRAM traffic:

$$AI = \frac{W}{Q}$$

where $W$ is the number of FLOPs and $Q$ is the number of bytes transferred.

**Step 2 — Define the memory-bound performance ceiling.**

A kernel cannot run faster than the rate at which memory supplies data:

$$P_{mem} = \beta \cdot AI$$

**Step 3 — Define the compute-bound performance ceiling.**

A kernel cannot run faster than the hardware's peak FLOP rate:

$$P_{compute} = \pi$$

**Step 4 — Combine the two ceilings.**

The actual achievable performance is the **minimum** of the two:

$$P_{actual} = \min(\pi, \beta \cdot AI)$$

**Step 5 — Identify the ridge point.**

The arithmetic intensity at which the two ceilings intersect is called the **ridge point**:

$$AI_{ridge} = \frac{\pi}{\beta}$$

**Step 6 — Quantify the PIM advantage.**

For a memory-bound workload ($AI < AI_{ridge}$), PIM increases $\beta$ by $k$ times (e.g., $k = 10$ for HBM-PIM vs. conventional DDR5). The speedup is:

$$S_{PIM} = \frac{P_{PIM}}{P_{baseline}} = \frac{\min(\pi, k\beta \cdot AI)}{\min(\pi, \beta \cdot AI)}$$

In the memory-bound regime:

$$S_{PIM} = k$$

This means a **10x bandwidth improvement from HBM-PIM yields exactly 10x speedup** for purely memory-bound kernels.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Energy Savings from Eliminating Off-Chip Traffic

**Step 1 — Establish the per-bit energy costs.**

Let $e_{DRAM} = 100$ pJ/bit (off-chip DRAM read) and $e_{PIM} = 1$ pJ/bit (intra-die data movement in PIM).

**Step 2 — Compute the energy for a workload with $Q$ bytes of data movement.**

Conventional system:

$$E_{conv} = Q \cdot 8 \cdot e_{DRAM} = 800 Q \text{ pJ}$$

PIM system (data stays in memory):

$$E_{PIM} = Q \cdot 8 \cdot e_{PIM} = 8 Q \text{ pJ}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the energy savings ratio.**

$$\eta = \frac{E_{conv}}{E_{PIM}} = \frac{800 Q}{8 Q} = 100$$

A **100x energy reduction** is a realistic order-of-magnitude estimate, and aligns with published industry benchmarks for UPMEM and Samsung HBM-PIM.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Workload Suitability for PIM

The following matrix classifies common workloads by their suitability for PIM acceleration, with quantitative justification:

| Workload Category | Arithmetic Intensity (AI) | Memory-Bound? | PIM Suitability | Expected Speedup |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| GEMM (dense matrix multiply) | 50–500 FLOPs/Byte | No (compute-bound) | Low | 1.0–1.5x |
| SpMV (sparse matrix-vector) | 0.5–5 FLOPs/Byte | Yes | **Very High** | 10–50x |
| Graph traversal (BFS/DFS) | 0.1–1 FLOPs/Byte | Yes | **Very High** | 20–100x |
| KV-cache attention | 0.2–2 FLOPs/Byte | Yes | **Very High** | 5–20x |
| Embedding table lookup | 0.01–0.1 FLOPs/Byte | Yes (extremely) | **Extreme** | 50–200x |
| Stream compaction | 0.5–2 FLOPs/Byte | Yes | **High** | 8–15x |
| Convolution (CNN) | 10–100 FLOPs/Byte | Mixed | Medium | 2–5x |
| Random number generation | 0 FLOPs/Byte | Pure memory | **Extreme** | 100x+ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Examination:** The *lower* the Arithmetic Intensity, the *more* attractive PIM becomes. A common exam trick is to ask "why is PIM less useful for GEMM?" — the answer is that GEMM is compute-bound, not memory-bound, so increasing $\beta$ does not help.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram 1 — Von-Neumann vs. PIM Architecture Comparison

The following Mermaid diagram contrasts the data-flow topology of a traditional system with a PIM-enabled system.

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph VonNeumann[Traditional Von Neumann System]
        CPU_A[CPU Socket]
        BusA[Off Chip Memory Bus]
        DRAM_A[External DRAM Modules]
        CPU_A -- Read Write Traffic --> BusA
        BusA -- Long Latency Access --> DRAM_A
    end

    subgraph PIMSystem[PIM Enabled System]
        Host_A[Host CPU Small]
        Link_A[CXL or PCIe Link]
        PIM_A[PIM Memory Module With Internal Compute]
        Host_A -- Control Commands Only --> Link_A
        Link_A -- Lightweight Commands --> PIM_A
    end

    VonNeumann -. Contrasted With .-> PIMSystem
```

### 4.2 Diagram 2 — 3D-Stacked HBM-PIM Internal Structure

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph HBMStack[3D Stacked HBM PIM Package]
        TopLogic[Top Logic Die With PIM Cores and Near Data ALUs]
        Layer1[DRAM Layer 1]
        Layer2[DRAM Layer 2]
        Layer3[DRAM Layer 3]
        Layer4[DRAM Layer 4]
        TopLogic --- Layer1
        Layer1 --- Layer2
        Layer2 --- Layer3
        Layer3 --- Layer4
    end

    Substrate[Silicon Interposer With TSV Connections]
    HBMStack --- Substrate
    HostGPU[Host CPU or GPU Socket]
    Substrate --- HostGPU
```

### 4.3 Diagram 3 — Decision Flow for Choosing PIM Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    Start([New Data Intensive Workload])
    Analyze{Is AI Less Than Ridge Point}
    PIMApplicable[Yes Workload Is Memory Bound]
    SkipPIM[No Workload Is Compute Bound]
    Analyze -- AI Below Threshold --> PIMApplicable
    Analyze -- AI Above Threshold --> SkipPIM
    ReuseCheck{Is Reuse Factor Greater Than 10}
    DRAM_PIM[Use DRAM Based PIM UPMEM or HBM PIM]
    AnalogPIM[Use ReRAM Based Analog PIM]
    ReuseCheck -- High Reuse --> DRAM_PIM
    ReuseCheck -- Matrix Heavy Low Reuse --> AnalogPIM
    PIMApplicable --> ReuseCheck
    SkipPIM --> End([Keep Conventional Architecture])
    DRAM_PIM --> End2([Deploy PIM and Measure Speedup])
    AnalogPIM --> End2
```

### 4.4 Diagram 4 — PIM Operation Sequence (Request Flow)

```mermaid
graph LR
    App[User Application]
    Runtime[PIM Aware Runtime Library]
    Driver[PIM Driver and Firmware]
    PIMCore[PIM Processing Core Inside Memory]
    MemCell[Target Memory Cells]
    App -- Step 1 Issue Kernel --> Runtime
    Runtime -- Step 2 Allocate PIM Threads --> Driver
    Driver -- Step 3 Send Command to DIMM --> PIMCore
    PIMCore -- Step 4 Read Data In Place --> MemCell
    PIMCore -- Step 5 Write Back Locally --> MemCell
    PIMCore -- Step 6 Return Reduced Result --> Driver
    Driver -- Step 7 Final Aggregation --> Runtime
    Runtime -- Step 8 Deliver Output --> App
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Aid:** Notice that in Diagram 4, *no data ever leaves the memory module during steps 4 and 5*. The only thing that traverses the long, off-chip bus is the *command* (going down) and a *small reduced result* (coming up). This is the fundamental energy-saving mechanism of PIM.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**

*Define Processing-in-Memory (PIM). List any two commercial examples of PIM systems.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Processing-in-Memory (PIM) is a non-von-Neumann computing architecture in which processing elements are integrated directly into memory dies (DRAM, SRAM, NAND, or ReRAM), enabling computation to be performed on data in place, thereby eliminating the energy and latency cost of off-chip data movement.

**Commercial Examples (1 mark — any two):**

1. **UPMEM PIM-DIMM** (2019) — DRAM-based, CPU host offloads tasks to in-DIMM cores.
2. **Samsung HBM-PIM** (2021) — 3D-stacked HBM with processing logic in the base die.
3. **SK Hynix AiM (Accelerator-in-Memory)** (2022) — GDDR6-based PIM.
4. **Mythic Analog Matrix Processor** — ReRAM-based analog PIM.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**

*What is the "Memory Wall"? Explain in two sentences why it motivates the adoption of PIM.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Memory Wall** refers to the ever-widening performance gap between processor throughput (which has grown exponentially with Moore's Law) and DRAM latency/bandwidth (which has improved only marginally over the past two decades) [1.5 marks].

It motivates PIM because, in modern workloads, the **time and energy spent moving data between the CPU and DRAM** often exceed the time and energy spent *computing* on that data. By placing compute units inside the memory, PIM eliminates the long, expensive off-chip interconnects that define this wall [1.5 marks].

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Arithmetic Intensity & Roofline Analysis

**[KTU University Exam — Sample Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Define **Arithmetic Intensity (AI)**. A memory-bound kernel performs **128 FLOPs** and requires **1024 bytes** of data movement. Compute its AI and state whether it is memory-bound or compute-bound, given $\pi = 100$ GFLOPs/s and $\beta = 25$ GB/s. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the same kernel, compute the **achievable performance** $P_{actual}$ and the **speedup** obtained by replacing the conventional memory with a **PIM module that provides 4x bandwidth** ($\beta_{PIM} = 100$ GB/s), assuming the kernel remains in the memory-bound regime. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Definition (2 marks):**

Arithmetic Intensity (AI) is the ratio of the number of floating-point operations performed by a kernel to the number of bytes transferred to/from main memory:

$$AI = \frac{W}{Q}$$

where $W$ is the total work in FLOPs and $Q$ is the data movement in bytes.

**Step 2 — Compute AI (2 marks):**

$$AI = \frac{128 \text{ FLOPs}}{1024 \text{ bytes}} = 0.125 \text{ FLOPs/byte}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the ridge point (2 marks):**

$$AI_{ridge} = \frac{\pi}{\beta} = \frac{100 \times 10^{9} \text{ FLOPs/s}}{25 \times 10^{9} \text{ bytes/s}} = 4 \text{ FLOPs/byte}$$

**Step 4 — Classify the workload (1 mark):**

Since $AI = 0.125 \ll AI_{ridge} = 4$, the kernel is **memory-bound**.

---

**Part (b) — (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute baseline $P_{actual}$ (2 marks):**

In the memory-bound regime:

$$P_{actual} = \beta \cdot AI = 25 \times 10^{9} \cdot 0.125 = 3.125 \text{ GFLOPs/s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute PIM $P_{PIM}$ (2 marks):**

$$P_{PIM} = \beta_{PIM} \cdot AI = 100 \times 10^{9} \cdot 0.125 = 12.5 \text{ GFLOPs/s}$$

**Step 3 — Compute speedup (2 marks):**

$$S_{PIM} = \frac{P_{PIM}}{P_{actual}} = \frac{12.5}{3.125} = 4$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation (1 mark):**

The workload is purely memory-bound, so the **4x increase in bandwidth yields exactly 4x speedup**, with zero overhead. This is the ideal case for PIM acceleration and is the central selling point of HBM-PIM.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Taxonomy, Energy Analysis & Real-World Systems

**[KTU University Exam — Sample Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the **two main classes of PIM** (Near-Data Processing and In-Memory Computing) with one commercial example of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A database query reads **8 GB** of data from off-chip DRAM. Compute the **off-chip data-movement energy** (assume $e_{DRAM} = 100$ pJ/bit) and the equivalent **number of 32-bit integer ALU operations** that could be performed with the same energy budget (assume $e_{ALU} = 1$ pJ per operation). What conclusion do you draw about the value of PIM for this workload? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — (7 marks):**

**1. Near-Data Processing (NDP) — 3.5 marks:**

NDP places a *separate* processing element *adjacent to* the memory array, typically in the logic die of a 3D-stacked memory or on the DIMM module. Data movement occurs *within* the memory package, but compute still uses a digital logic fabric. **Commercial example:** **Samsung HBM-PIM** or **UPMEM PIM-DIMM**. The processing elements are general-purpose, programmable cores that execute user-defined kernels.

**2. In-Memory Computing (IMC) — 3.5 marks:**

IMC embeds computation *inside* the memory cell array itself, often exploiting the analog physical properties of the cells (e.g., resistance of ReRAM). Matrix-vector multiplications are performed using **Ohm's law** ($V = IR$) and **Kirchhoff's current law** at every cross-point in parallel. **Commercial example:** **Mythic Analog Matrix Processor** (ReRAM-based) or research prototypes on **DRAM sense amplifiers**. IMC achieves parallelism at the level of individual memory cells, yielding massive energy savings for AI inference.

---

**Part (b) — (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Convert data to bits (1 mark):**

$$Q = 8 \text{ GB} = 8 \times 8 \times 10^{9} \text{ bits} = 64 \times 10^{9} \text{ bits}$$

**Step 2 — Compute off-chip energy (2 marks):**

$$E_{DRAM} = Q \cdot e_{DRAM} = 64 \times 10^{9} \cdot 100 \times 10^{-12} = 6.4 \text{ J}$$

**Step 3 — Compute equivalent ALU operations (2 marks):**

$$N_{ALU} = \frac{E_{DRAM}}{e_{ALU}} = \frac{6.4 \text{ J}}{1 \times 10^{-12} \text{ J}} = 6.4 \times 10^{12} \text{ operations}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation (2 marks):**

The system expends **6.4 J** purely on *data transport* for an 8 GB scan. That same energy could have executed **6.4 trillion 32-bit integer operations** *in situ* if compute were placed inside the memory. This demonstrates a **200x to 1000x** energy-efficiency opportunity, strongly justifying PIM for memory-bound analytical workloads such as database scans, OLAP, and embedding lookups.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting units:** Writing $AI$ as a pure number instead of **FLOPs/Byte** is a 0.5-mark deduction. Always state the unit.
> 2. **Confusing Near-Data Processing with In-Memory Computing:** NDP places compute *next to* memory (digital); IMC places compute *inside* the memory cell array (often analog). Examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 3. **Stating speedup without computing the ridge point first:** You must show that $AI < \pi/\beta$ *before* applying the speedup formula.
> 4. **Ignoring the compute-bound vs memory-bound regime:** A common trick is to give an AI *higher* than the ridge point and watch students erroneously apply the memory-bound speedup formula.
> 5. **Forgetting to mention commercial systems:** Naming only the academic concept without a product name (UPMEM, HBM-PIM, etc.) costs 1 mark per omission.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Examination**

- ☐ **PIM = Processing-in-Memory** — compute placed *inside or adjacent to* memory to defeat the Memory Wall.
- ☐ **Two main classes:** **NDP** (near data, digital) and **IMC** (in-array, often analog).
- ☐ **Memory technologies used:** **DRAM, SRAM, NAND, ReRAM/MRAM** — each with distinct trade-offs.
- ☐ **Memory Wall** = growing gap between CPU speed and DRAM speed; the key motivator.
- ☐ **Data Movement Energy** = 100–1000x larger than compute energy per operation.
- ☐ **Arithmetic Intensity** $AI = W/Q$ in **FLOPs/Byte** — lower AI ⇒ more attractive PIM candidate.
- ☐ **Roofline Model:** $P = \min(\pi, \beta \cdot AI)$ — ridge point at $AI_{ridge} = \pi/\beta$.
- ☐ **Memory-bound regime:** $AI < \pi/\beta$ ⇒ PIM speedup equals bandwidth multiplier.
- ☐ **Compute-bound regime:** $AI \geq \pi/\beta$ ⇒ PIM offers little benefit.
- ☐ **Commercial systems to memorize:** **UPMEM PIM-DIMM (2019), Samsung HBM-PIM (2021), SK Hynix AiM (2022), Mythic AMP (ReRAM)**.
- ☐ **Architectural formats:** Logic-layer PIM (3D-stacked), Array-internal PIM (crossbar), Memory-side PIM (DIMM).
- ☐ **HBM-PIM** uses TSVs (Through-Silicon Vias) and a base logic die with compute.
- ☐ **ReRAM crossbars** compute $y = Mx$ via Ohm's + Kirchhoff's laws in **one cycle** for the entire matrix.
- ☐ **Reuse Factor $R$** = ops per data fetch; high $R$ ⇒ DRAM-PIM, low $R$ ⇒ analog PIM.
- ☐ **Workloads best suited:** SpMV, BFS, KV-cache attention, embedding lookups, hash joins, stream compaction.
- ☐ **Workloads poorly suited:** Dense GEMM, large FFTs, dense CNN convolutions.
- ☐ **Energy saving** $\eta = e_{DRAM} / e_{PIM} \approx 100$ to $1000\times$.
- ☐ **Two fundamental equations** to remember by heart: $P = \min(\pi, \beta \cdot AI)$ and $AI_{ridge} = \pi/\beta$.
- ☐ **Key emerging standard:** **CXL (Compute Express Link)** + **UCIe** for disaggregated PIM.

---

**End of Module 1 Notes — Processing In Memory (PIM)**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Optical Storage
topics.content:
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### Formal Definition **Optical Storage** is a non-volatile data storage technology that uses a low-power laser beam to read (and in some cases write) data encoded as microscopic physical features — known as **pits** (depressions) and **lands** (flat areas) — embossed or burned onto the surface of a rotating polycarbonate disc. The presence or absence of light reflected back to a photodetector is translated into the binary digits **0** and **1**. The three dominant generations standardized under this technology are: | Generation | Media | Laser Wavelength | Numerical Aperture (NA) | Single-Layer Capacity | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1st Gen | **CD** (Compact Disc) | **780 nm** (Infrared) | **0.45** | **700 MB** (80 min audio) | | 2nd Gen | **DVD** (Digital Versatile Disc) | **650 nm** (Red) | **0.6** | **4.7 GB** | | 3rd Gen | **Blu-ray Disc (BD)** | **405 nm** (Blue-Violet) | **0.85** | **25 GB** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Optical Storage is the foundational block of secondary/removable storage. The capacity scaling rule you must memorize is governed by the **diffraction-limited spot size formula**: > $$D_{spot} \approx \frac{\lambda}{2 \cdot NA}$$ > Smaller wavelength ($\lambda$) and higher NA yield a smaller laser spot, allowing **smaller pits** and **tighter track pitch** → more data per disc. ### Conceptual Analogy — The "Whispering Gallery" of Light Imagine a vast circular gallery where a single tiny spotlight sweeps from the innermost ring to the outermost ring. Wherever the floor has a tiny "dip" (a pit), the light scatters and doesn't echo back. Wherever the floor is flat (a land), the light bounces back perfectly. An assistant standing at the center listens carefully — every echo is a **"1"** and every silence is a **"0"**. The disc spins at a steady pace, and the spotlight reads millions of these dips and flats per second, reconstructing music, video, or software. That, in essence, is how a **CD/DVD/Blu-ray player** works. > [!NOTE] > **Why pits and not magnetic domains?** Because light is a wave, and waves can be focused to a diffraction-limited spot much smaller than a magnetic domain, optical media achieves far higher areal density than floppy disks (1.44 MB) or even early hard drives — at the cost of **read-only or write-once** behavior in most consumer formats. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Physical Structure of an Optical Disc - **Substrate Layer:** A **1.2 mm** thick (CD/DVD) or **1.1 mm** thick (Blu-ray) polycarbonate plastic disc. It provides structural rigidity and a transparent window for the laser. - **Data Layer:** A thin metallic (aluminum for CD/DVD) or phase-change/organic dye layer where pits are physically stamped (ROM), burned (R), or rewritten (RW/RAM). - **Reflective Layer:** A sputtered silver/alloy layer that bounces the laser back to the photodetector. - **Protective Lacquer:** A UV-cured coating to prevent scratches. - **Label Side:** The top, printable surface — opposite the laser-reading side. The data is arranged in a **single continuous spiral track** that begins at the inner radius ($R_i$) and spirals outward to the outer radius ($R_o$). This is fundamentally different from magnetic hard drives, which use **concentric circles (cylinders)**. ### 2.2 Pit-and-Land Encoding Logic - A **pit** is a depression of depth approximately **$\lambda / 4$** (quarter-wavelength) into the polycarbonate. This depth causes **destructive interference** in the reflected light, so the photodetector sees a **low-intensity** signal. - A **land** is the flat region at the surface level — it reflects light constructively back to the photodetector, producing a **high-intensity** signal. - The data is encoded using **Non-Return-to-Zero Inverted (NRZI)** scheme: - A **transition** (pit → land or land → pit) = binary **1** - **No transition** (continuous pit or continuous land) = binary **0** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Minimum Pit Length Rule:** The shortest run-length (T) must be at least **3 channel bits** long in CD/DVD (and 2 in Blu-ray) to keep the photodetector signal detectable above noise. Maximum is **11T** (CD) or **14T** (DVD). ### 2.3 Pit Depth Justification — Why $\lambda/4$? When the laser reflects from a pit, it travels an extra round-trip distance of $2 \times d$ where $d$ is the pit depth. For **destructive interference** with the light reflected from a land: $$2d = \frac{\lambda}{2n} \quad \Rightarrow \quad d = \frac{\lambda}{4n}$$ where $n \approx 1.55$ is the refractive index of polycarbonate. This is why pit depth scales with laser wavelength. ### 2.4 Constant Linear Velocity (CLV) Unlike hard drives (which use **Constant Angular Velocity — CAV**), optical discs use **CLV**: the disc spins **slower at the outer edge** and **faster at the inner edge** so that the linear speed of the track under the laser remains constant. - **CD:** Linear velocity $v = 1.2$ m/s (75 sectors/second at the standard data rate) - **DVD:** $v = 3.49$ m/s - **Blu-ray:** $v \approx 4.9$ m/s (1x) to 25.9 m/s (6x) ### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Engineering Utility | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Diffraction-limited spot diameter | $D = \dfrac{\lambda}{2 \cdot NA}$ | meters | Governs minimum pit length | | 2 | Pit depth (destructive interference) | $d = \dfrac{\lambda}{4n}$ | meters | Justifies physical pit geometry | | 3 | Total spiral track length | $L = \dfrac{\pi (R_o^2 - R_i^2)}{p}$ | meters | Used in capacity derivation | | 4 | Approximate disc capacity | $C = L \times \rho$ | bits / bytes | Total stored information | | 5 | Linear density (data rate / velocity) | $\rho = \dfrac{R_b}{v}$ | bits/meter | Bits per unit track length | | 6 | Number of spiral turns | $N_{turns} = \dfrac{R_o - R_i}{p}$ | dimensionless | Spiral geometry | | 7 | Channel bit period | $T = \dfrac{v}{R_b}$ | seconds | Pit length timing | | 8 | Areal density | $\sigma = \dfrac{C}{\pi (R_o^2 - R_i^2)}$ | bits/m² | Storage efficiency metric | | 9 | Data rate (1x baseline) | CD: **1.4112 Mbps**, DVD: **11.08 Mbps**, BD: **36 Mbps** | Mbps | KTU expects these constants | | 10 | Capacity of DVD dual-layer | **8.5 GB** (4.7 + 3.8) | GB | Common KTU numerical | Where: - $\lambda$ = laser wavelength, $n$ = refractive index, $NA$ = numerical aperture - $R_o, R_i$ = outer/inner radius, $p$ = track pitch - $R_b$ = channel bit rate, $v$ = linear velocity, $\rho$ = linear density ### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Applications 1. **Software Distribution:** Operating systems, game installations (read-only mass replication). 2. **Archival Storage:** Write-Once-Read-Many (WORM) discs for legal, medical, and government records (50+ year lifespan). 3. **Movie Industry:** DVD and Blu-ray remain the dominant physical distribution medium for high-definition video. 4. **Boot Media:** Emergency recovery discs, firmware updates in automobiles and industrial machines. 5. **Aerospace:** Radiation-hardened optical storage in satellite payloads (immune to electromagnetic interference). --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: Total Track Length of a Spiral Disc We treat the spiral as a set of infinitesimally thin concentric rings, each of width $p$ (track pitch). For a ring at radius $r$ with width $dr$, the circumference is $2\pi r$. Integrating from $R_i$ to $R_o$: $$ \begin{aligned} L &= \int_{R_i}^{R_o} \frac{2\pi r}{p} \, dr \\[6pt] &= \frac{2\pi}{p} \int_{R_i}^{R_o} r \, dr \\[6pt] &= \frac{2\pi}{p} \left[ \frac{r^2}{2} \right]_{R_i}^{R_o} \\[6pt] &= \frac{\pi}{p} \left( R_o^2 - R_i^2 \right) \end{aligned} $$ **Logic step:** Each infinitesimal ring of width $p$ contributes one full track of length $2\pi r$, so the number of tracks in a strip of width $dr$ is $dr/p$, and each contributes $2\pi r$ to the total length. Integration sums them all. ### 3.2 Derivation 2: Approximate Capacity of a CD **Given (Red Book / Yellow Book standard):** - $R_o = 58$ mm, $R_i = 25$ mm - Track pitch $p = 1.6 \text{ μm} = 1.6 \times 10^{-3}$ mm - Channel bit rate $R_b = 4.3218$ Mbps - Linear velocity $v = 1.2$ m/s - User data efficiency (after EFM + CIRC + sync): $\eta \approx 1/3$ (i.e., 8 user bits → 17 channel bits including merging bits and sync overhead) **Step 1 — Total spiral length:** $$ \begin{aligned} L &= \frac{\pi (R_o^2 - R_i^2)}{p} \\[6pt] &= \frac{\pi \times (58^2 - 25^2) \text{ mm}^2}{1.6 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mm}} \\[6pt] &= \frac{\pi \times (3364 - 625)}{1.6 \times 10^{-3}} \text{ mm} \\[6pt] &= \frac{\pi \times 2739}{1.6 \times 10^{-3}} \text{ mm} \\[6pt] &= \frac{8603.2}{1.6 \times 10^{-3}} \text{ mm} \\[6pt] &= 5.377 \times 10^{6} \text{ mm} = 5.377 \text{ km} \end{aligned} $$ **Logic step:** Substituting the squared radii (in mm²) and dividing by track pitch (in mm) yields a length in mm, then converted to km. The ~5.4 km figure is the well-known CD spiral length. **Step 2 — Linear density (channel bits per mm):** $$ \begin{aligned} \rho &= \frac{R_b}{v} = \frac{4.3218 \times 10^{6} \text{ bits/s}}{1.2 \times 10^{3} \text{ mm/s}} \\[6pt] &= 3601.5 \text{ bits/mm} \end{aligned} $$ **Logic step:** Each second, $R_b$ channel bits are read; in that same second, the laser travels $v$ mm. So bits per mm = rate ÷ velocity. **Step 3 — Total channel bits:** $$ \begin{aligned} B_{channel} &= L \times \rho = 5.377 \times 10^{6} \text{ mm} \times 3601.5 \text{ bits/mm} \\[6pt] &\approx 1.937 \times 10^{10} \text{ channel bits} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Apply efficiency to get user bytes:** $$ \begin{aligned} C_{user} &= B_{channel} \times \eta \div 8 \\[6pt] &= 1.937 \times 10^{10} \times \frac{1}{3} \div 8 \\[6pt] &= 8.07 \times 10^{8} \text{ bytes} \approx 807 \text{ MB} \end{aligned} $$ The slight overshoot above the marketed 700 MB accounts for the lead-in, lead-out, and Table of Contents (TOC) overhead — the **usable** data area is indeed **≈ 700 MB**. ### 3.3 Derivation 3: Why Blu-ray Holds 25 GB **Given:** - $R_o = 60$ mm, $R_i = 24$ mm (data zone) - Track pitch $p = 0.32 \text{ μm} = 0.32 \times 10^{-3}$ mm - Channel bit length $T = 0.15 \text{ μm/bit}$ (minimum, BD-ROM) **Step 1 — Track length:** $$ \begin{aligned} L &= \frac{\pi (60^2 - 24^2)}{0.32 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{\pi \times 3024}{0.32 \times 10^{-3}} \text{ mm} \\[6pt] &= 29,687.7 \text{ m} \approx 29.7 \text{ km} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Bit density (≈ 1/T):** $$ \rho = \frac{1}{0.15 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mm}} \approx 6666 \text{ bits/mm} $$ **Step 3 — Raw bits and capacity:** $$ \begin{aligned} B_{channel} &= 29,687.7 \text{ m} \times 6666 \text{ bits/mm} = 1.979 \times 10^{11} \text{ bits} \\[6pt] C_{user} &= \frac{B_{channel} \times \eta_{BD}}{8} \approx \frac{1.979 \times 10^{11} \times 0.66}{8} \\[6pt] &\approx 1.63 \times 10^{10} \text{ bytes} \approx 16.3 \text{ GB} \end{aligned} $$ With **17% modulation overhead + error correction** at the practical layout, the **nominal 25 GB** (single-layer) is the marketed capacity. The takeaway for KTU: Blu-ray's 25 GB ÷ DVD's 4.7 GB ≈ **5.3×** — entirely traceable to its **smaller $\lambda$ (405 nm vs 650 nm) and tighter pitch (0.32 μm vs 0.74 μm)**. ### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix | Property | CD | DVD | Blu-ray | |---|---|---|---| | $\lambda$ | 780 nm | 650 nm | 405 nm | | $NA$ | 0.45 | 0.6 | 0.85 | | Spot diameter $\frac{\lambda}{2NA}$ | **0.87 μm** | **0.54 μm** | **0.24 μm** | | Track pitch | 1.6 μm | 0.74 μm | 0.32 μm | | Min pit length | 0.83 μm | 0.40 μm | 0.15 μm | | Disc thickness | 1.2 mm | 1.2 mm (bonded 0.6+0.6) | 1.1 mm | | Single-layer capacity | 700 MB | 4.7 GB | 25 GB | | Modulation | EFM (8→17) | EFMPlus (8→16) | 17PP | | Error correction | CIRC | RS-PC | LDC + BIS | --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram: Optical Drive Read Path ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Polycarbonate Disc
Spinning at CLV] --> B[Semiconductor Laser Diode
CD 780nm, DVD 650nm, BD 405nm] B --> C[Beam Splitter
Directional Coupler] C --> D[Objective Lens
High NA Focusing Optics] D --> A A -->|Reflected Light| D D --> C C --> E[Photodetector Array
Multi Segment] E --> F[RF Amplifier and Equalizer] F --> G[EFM EFMPlus or 17PP
Demodulator] G --> H[Error Correction
CIRC, RS PC, LDC+BIS] H --> I[Digital Data Stream
Audio Video File] style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc,color:#000000 style B fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000000 style C fill:#f0e1ff,stroke:#6600cc,color:#000000 style D fill:#f0e1ff,stroke:#6600cc,color:#000000 style E fill:#e1ffe1,stroke:#009900,color:#000000 style F fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000000 style G fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000000 style H fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000000 style I fill:#e1ffe1,stroke:#009900,color:#000000 ``` ### 4.2 Pit-Land Encoding State Machine ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Land Land --> Pit: Transition Equals Binary 1 Land --> Land: No Transition Equals Binary 0 Pit --> Land: Transition Equals Binary 1 Pit --> Pit: No Transition Equals Binary 0 Land --> [*] Pit --> [*] note right of Land Reflection detected Photodetector HIGH end note note right of Pit Destructive interference Pit depth lambda over 4n Photodetector LOW end note ``` ### 4.3 Cross-Generation Capacity Scaling Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD Start[Optical Storage Capacity] --> Wavelength[Reduce Laser Wavelength
780nm to 405nm] Start --> NA[Increase Numerical Aperture
0.45 to 0.85] Wavelength --> Spot[Smaller Diffraction Spot] NA --> Spot Spot --> Pitch[Tighter Track Pitch
1.6um to 0.32um] Spot --> Pit[Smaller Pit Length
0.83um to 0.15um] Pitch --> Density[Higher Areal Density] Pit --> Density Density --> CD[CD 700MB] Density --> DVD[DVD 4.7GB] Density --> BD[Blu-ray 25GB] style Start fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc,color:#000000 style Spot fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000000 style Density fill:#e1ffe1,stroke:#009900,color:#000000 style CD fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000000 style DVD fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000000 style BD fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000000 ``` ### 4.4 Disc Physical Layer Stack ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph BluRay_Stack[Blu-ray Disc Cross Section] L1[Label Side 0.1mm] --> L2[Polycarbonate Substrate 1.0mm] L2 --> L3[Data Layer Pits and Lands 405nm Sensitive] L3 --> L4[Sputtered Reflective Ag Alloy 100nm] L4 --> L5[UV Protective Lacquer 0.1mm] end subgraph DVD_Stack[DVD Disc Cross Section] D1[Label Side 0.1mm] --> D2[Polycarbonate 0.6mm] D2 --> D3[Data Layer] D3 --> D4[Adhesive Bonding Layer 0.05mm] D4 --> D5[Data Layer 2 if Dual Layer] D5 --> D6[Polycarbonate 0.6mm] end style L3 fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000000 style D3 fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000000 style D5 fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000000 ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **`[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`** **Q1. Why is the pit depth in an optical disc chosen as $\lambda / 4n$? What would happen if it were $\lambda / 2n$?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - The pit is a depression of depth $d$ in a polycarbonate substrate of refractive index $n \approx 1.55$. Light reflecting from the bottom of the pit travels an extra path of $2d$ compared to light reflecting from the surrounding land (1 mark). - For maximum destructive interference (minimum detected intensity at pit) the optical path difference must equal $\lambda/2$, hence $2d = \lambda/2n$, giving $d = \lambda/4n$ (1 mark). - If $d$ were $\lambda/2n$, the path difference would equal $\lambda$, causing **constructive interference** at the pit — making pits and lands indistinguishable and the disc unreadable (1 mark). --- > **`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`** **Q2. Differentiate between Constant Linear Velocity (CLV) and Constant Angular Velocity (CAV) in the context of optical storage.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **CLV (used in CD/DVD/BD):** The disc's rotational speed is varied — faster near the center, slower near the edge — so that the **linear track velocity under the laser remains constant** (1 mark). Benefit: uniform data density and constant data rate across the disc; drawback: variable seek time and complex motor control (1 mark). - **CAV (used in HDDs and modern CD-ROMs in partial-CAV mode):** The disc spins at a fixed RPM, so the **linear velocity is higher at the outer edge** than the inner edge. Data rate varies radially; simpler motor control (1 mark). --- ### Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** > **`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 – Model Paper]`** **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of an optical disc read system. Label the function of each block in the laser-to-data path. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A DVD-ROM uses a **650 nm** laser with a numerical aperture of **0.6**. The disc has an inner data radius of **22 mm** and an outer data radius of **120 mm**, with a track pitch of **0.74 μm**. The channel bit rate is **26.16 Mbps** at a linear velocity of **3.49 m/s**. Calculate: (i) the diffraction-limited laser spot diameter, (ii) the total spiral track length, (iii) the total channel bits on the disc, (iv) the minimum pit length (in μm). **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Block diagram and working (7 marks):** ``` Laser Diode → Beam Splitter → Objective Lens → Disc (Pit/Land) ↑ ↓ Photodetector ← RF Amplifier ← Reflected Light Path ↓ EFM/EFMPlus Demodulator → CIRC/RS-PC Decoder → Digital Data ``` **Step 1:** The **semiconductor laser diode** (650 nm red) emits a coherent, low-divergence beam. A **photodiode monitor** regulates output power via feedback (1 mark). **Step 2:** The **beam splitter** (a partially silvered prism or holographic optical element) directs the beam toward the objective lens and later channels the reflected beam toward the photodetector — keeping the return path optically isolated (1 mark). **Step 3:** The **objective lens** (NA = 0.6) focuses the laser through the 0.6 mm polycarbonate substrate to a diffraction-limited spot of ~0.54 μm diameter. A **servo-controlled voice-coil actuator** maintains focus (auto-focus) and tracks the spiral (tracking servo) (1 mark). **Step 4:** At a **pit**, the photodetector sees low intensity (destructive interference, since pit depth = $\lambda/4n$). At a **land**, it sees high intensity. The detector's output is a high-frequency analog waveform called the **HF (High Frequency) signal** or **Eye Pattern** (1 mark). **Step 5:** The **RF amplifier and equalizer** boost the signal and apply waveform shaping (1 mark). **Step 6:** The **EFMPlus demodulator** (in DVD) reverses the 8-to-16 modulation, recovering the original channel bit stream (1 mark). **Step 7:** The **error correction decoder** (Reed Solomon Product Code in DVD) corrects burst and random errors, delivering the final digital data to the host (1 mark). --- **(b) Numerical (7 marks):** **(i) Spot diameter (2 marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} D &= \frac{\lambda}{2 \cdot NA} = \frac{650 \times 10^{-9}}{2 \times 0.6} \\[6pt] &= \frac{650 \times 10^{-9}}{1.2} = 5.42 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m} = 0.542 \text{ μm} \end{aligned} $$ **(ii) Total track length (2 marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} L &= \frac{\pi (R_o^2 - R_i^2)}{p} = \frac{\pi (120^2 - 22^2)}{0.74 \times 10^{-3}} \text{ mm} \\[6pt] &= \frac{\pi \times (14400 - 484)}{0.00074} = \frac{\pi \times 13916}{0.00074} \\[6pt] &= 59,073.2 \text{ m} \approx 59.07 \text{ km} \end{aligned} $$ **(iii) Total channel bits (2 marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} \rho &= \frac{R_b}{v} = \frac{26.16 \times 10^{6}}{3.49 \times 10^{3}} = 7495.7 \text{ bits/mm} \\[6pt] B_{channel} &= L \times \rho = 59,073.2 \text{ m} \times 7495.7 \text{ bits/mm} \\[6pt] &= 59,073,200 \text{ mm} \times 7495.7 \text{ bits/mm} \\[6pt] &\approx 4.428 \times 10^{11} \text{ channel bits} \end{aligned} $$ **(iv) Minimum pit length (1 mark):** $$ \begin{aligned} T_{min} &= 3T = 3 \times \frac{v}{R_b} = 3 \times \frac{3.49 \text{ m/s}}{26.16 \times 10^{6} \text{ bits/s}} \\[6pt] &= 3 \times 1.334 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m} = 4.00 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m} = 0.40 \text{ μm} \end{aligned} $$ --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) [ALTERNATIVE]** > **`[KTU University Exam – May 2024 – Model Paper]`** **(a)** Compare the physical and encoding characteristics of **CD, DVD, and Blu-ray** discs in a structured table and explain why Blu-ray achieves a 5× capacity improvement over DVD despite using a disc of similar physical size. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A Blu-ray disc uses a **405 nm** laser with **NA = 0.85**. The minimum pit length is **0.15 μm** (2T in BD-ROM encoding).
