Determine: (i) the spot diameter and the channel bit rate at 1× linear velocity of **4.917 m/s**, (ii) the maximum linear density (bits/mm) and the minimum channel bit period, (iii) the number of spiral turns if the data zone spans from **24 mm to 58 mm** radius with track pitch **0.32 μm**. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Comparative analysis (7 marks):** - Present the **Analytical Matrix from Section 3.4** as the structured table (3 marks). - The 5× capacity jump from DVD (4.7 GB) to Blu-ray (25 GB) is a consequence of three independent factors (4 marks): 1. **Spot size reduction:** $\lambda/2NA$ drops from 0.54 μm (DVD) to 0.24 μm (BD) — a **2.25×** linear reduction, giving ~5× areal improvement. 2. **Track pitch tightening:** 0.74 μm → 0.32 μm (≈ **2.3×** denser). 3. **Pit length shrinking:** 0.40 μm → 0.15 μm (≈ **2.7×** shorter) allows more bits per mm of track. 4. **Cover layer thinning:** 0.6 mm → 0.1 mm cover, allowing the high-NA (0.85) lens to focus sharply without spherical aberration. **(b) Numerical (7 marks):** **(i) Spot diameter and channel bit rate (2 marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} D &= \frac{\lambda}{2 NA} = \frac{405 \times 10^{-9}}{2 \times 0.85} = 2.38 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m} = 0.238 \text{ μm} \\[6pt] R_b &= \frac{v}{T} = \frac{4.917}{0.15 \times 10^{-6} \div 2} = \frac{4.917}{7.5 \times 10^{-8}} = 6.556 \times 10^{7} \text{ bps} \approx 65.56 \text{ Mbps} \end{aligned} $$ *Note:* The **standard 1× Blu-ray rate is 36 Mbps**, but the question's parameters yield 65.56 Mbps — the student should plug the given numbers, not recall the marketing value. The "1×" of 36 Mbps is achieved at a slightly different linear velocity in the official spec. **(ii) Linear density and bit period (2 marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} \rho_{max} &= \frac{1}{T_{min}/2} = \frac{1}{0.075 \text{ μm}} = 13{,}333 \text{ bits/mm} \\[6pt] T_{min} &= \frac{1}{R_b} = \frac{1}{6.556 \times 10^{7}} = 1.525 \times 10^{-8} \text{ s} = 15.25 \text{ ns} \end{aligned} $$ **(iii) Number of spiral turns (3 marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} N_{turns} &= \frac{R_o - R_i}{p} = \frac{58 - 24}{0.32 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{34}{0.00032} \\[6pt] &= 106{,}250 \text{ turns} \end{aligned} $$ **Sanity check:** Total length $L = N_{turns} \times \pi (R_o + R_i) \approx 106250 \times \pi \times 82 \text{ mm} \approx 27{,}370$ m, consistent with Section 3.3's ~29.7 km estimate (the difference is due to the spiral-vs-rings approximation). --- ### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfalls That Cost Marks in Optical Storage Questions:** > 1. **Confusing minimum pit length with channel bit period T:** Minimum pit is **3T** (CD/DVD) or **2T** (Blu-ray), not T itself. Many students write "minimum pit = T" — instant loss of 1 mark. > 2. **Forgetting the refractive index $n \approx 1.55$ in pit depth:** The formula is $d = \lambda / 4n$, not $d = \lambda / 4$. Board examiners specifically test this nuance. > 3. **Mixing units in capacity calculations:** Track pitch is in **μm** but radius is in **mm**. A unit-conversion error cascading through $L$, $\rho$, and $C$ will cost 2-3 marks even with correct logic. > 4. **Mis-stating NA and wavelength pairs:** CD is **780 nm / 0.45**, DVD is **650 nm / 0.6**, Blu-ray is **405 nm / 0.85**. Mismatched pairs = 0 marks for that sub-question. > 5. **Stating capacity without considering overhead:** Raw capacity ≠ user capacity. Always mention **EFM/EFMPlus efficiency** (~33% for CD, ~50% for DVD) and error-correction overhead. > 6. **Drawing the read path with the photodetector on the laser side:** The detector must be on the **reflected** path, after the beam splitter. Reversed diagrams lose 1-2 marks. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Optical storage = laser-read pits and lands on a spiral polycarbonate disc.** - The three generations are **CD (780 nm), DVD (650 nm), Blu-ray (405 nm)** with progressively **higher NA (0.45 → 0.85)**. - **Spot diameter** $D = \lambda / 2NA$ is the **single most important formula** for the entire module — it directly governs minimum pit length and track pitch. - **Pit depth** $= \lambda / 4n$ (with $n \approx 1.55$) ensures destructive interference → low photodetector signal at pits. - **Encoding** uses NRZI: **transition = 1, no transition = 0**. Minimum run-length is **3T** (CD/DVD) or **2T** (BD). - **CLV** (not CAV) is used to maintain constant linear data rate; motor RPM decreases from inner to outer radius. - **Capacity** $C \approx L \times \rho = \frac{\pi(R_o^2 - R_i^2)}{p} \times \frac{R_b}{v}$, with user data reduced by modulation + ECC overhead. - **CD-ROM** = 700 MB, **DVD** = 4.7 GB (single-layer) / 8.5 GB (dual-layer), **Blu-ray** = 25 GB (single-layer) / 50 GB (dual-layer). - Blu-ray's high NA is enabled by a **0.1 mm cover layer** (vs 0.6 mm in DVD), reducing spherical aberration. - **EFM** (CD: 8→14 + 3 merge), **EFMPlus** (DVD: 8→16), **17PP** (BD) are the three modulation codes. - Error correction: **CIRC** (CD), **RS-Product Code** (DVD), **LDC + BIS** (Blu-ray). - The **read path** order is: Laser → Beam Splitter → Objective Lens → Disc → (reflected) → Photodetector → RF Amplifier → Demodulator → ECC Decoder → Data. - **Track pitch** decreases: 1.6 μm (CD) → 0.74 μm (DVD) → 0.32 μm (BD). - **Linear velocity** at 1×: 1.2 m/s (CD), 3.49 m/s (DVD), 4.92 m/s (BD). ---

topics.title: Data Deduplication in Storage Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST867 - STORAGE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Data Deduplication in Storage Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Data Deduplication (Dedup)** is a specialized data compression technique used in modern storage systems to eliminate redundant copies of repeating data, ensuring that only a *single unique instance* of each data segment is physically retained on the storage medium. It operates by comparing incoming data blocks or files against already-stored unique segments, and discarding any duplicate occurrences, while replacing them with a tiny *pointer/reference* that redirects to the original stored copy.

In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme / Storage Systems**, deduplication is formally defined under the umbrella of *Information Lifecycle Management (ILM)* and *Capacity Optimization* within the **SNIA (Storage Networking Industry Association)** reference architecture.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Syllabus Terminology (KTU 2024)**
> - **Data Redundancy** – The unnecessary existence of identical data segments occupying multiple physical storage locations.
> - **Single Instance Storage (SIS)** – A primitive form of deduplication where only one copy of a file is retained, often at the file level.
> - **Deduplication Ratio** – The ratio expressing how much redundant data was eliminated relative to the original dataset size.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **college library** that has 500 students, and every student submits a printed copy of the *same 200-page reference book* for an assignment. The librarian, instead of stacking 500 identical copies on shelves, keeps **one master copy** and hands out 500 small *index cards* (pointers) that say *"Read the book on Shelf B, Row 4"*. The library saves enormous physical space.

In storage terms:
- The **500 printed books** = redundant data blocks.
- The **one master copy** = the unique chunk stored once.
- The **500 index cards** = small references/pointers (typically a few bytes).
- The **library shelf space saved** = storage capacity reclaimed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why It Matters for KTU Examinations**
> Modern data centers report that **up to 60–90% of enterprise data is duplicated** (emails, VM snapshots, backups, source code repositories). Without deduplication, storage arrays would be flooded with redundant copies, causing **wasted capacity, increased power costs, and longer backup windows**.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in Deduplication

The following metrics are standardized by **SNIA** and are examinable under KTU 2024:

- **Deduplication Ratio** – A dimensionless ratio (e.g., 10:1) indicating how many copies of data were collapsed into one.
- **Data Reduction Ratio (DRR)** – Inverse formulation showing effective logical capacity saved.
- **In-line vs. Post-process** – Timing of when deduplication is performed (write-path vs. background).
- **Chunk Size** – Granularity of deduplication (typically **4 KB – 128 KB**).
- **Hash Function Strength** – Typically **128-bit or 256-bit** cryptographic hash values used as fingerprints.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Insight:** A deduplication ratio of **20:1** means 20 units of logical data require only **1 unit of physical storage**. Always quote the ratio in the standard `N:1` form.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic of Deduplication – Step-by-Step

A deduplication engine operates through a deterministic pipeline. The following structured steps represent the canonical flow adopted by commercial systems such as **Dell EMC Data Domain**, **NetApp FAS**, and **HPE StoreOnce**:

1. **Chunking**: The incoming data stream (file or block) is segmented into smaller logical units called *chunks* or *data segments*. Chunking strategies include *Fixed-Length Chunking* and *Content-Defined Chunking (CDC)*.

2. **Fingerprinting (Hashing)**: Each chunk is processed through a cryptographic hash function (commonly **SHA-256** or **MD5**) to generate a unique fixed-size identifier called a *fingerprint* or *hash signature*.

3. **Lookup in Index**: The generated fingerprint is queried against a *deduplication index* (often an in-memory hash table) to determine whether the chunk has been seen before.

4. **Comparison Decision**:
   - If **hash match found** → chunk is duplicate; only a *pointer* to the original is stored.
   - If **no match** → chunk is unique; the actual data and its fingerprint are written to the storage tier.

5. **Reference Counting**: The original chunk's reference count is incremented to track how many logical entities depend on it (important for safe deletion / garbage collection).

6. **Garbage Collection**: Periodically, chunks with **reference count = 0** (i.e., all logical references deleted) are physically purged to reclaim space.

### 2.2 Classification of Deduplication

Deduplication is classified along **two orthogonal axes** in the KTU syllabus:

#### 2.2.1 By Timing (When)

| Mode | Description | Pros | Cons |
|------|-------------|------|------|
| **In-line (Synchronous)** | Dedup occurs on the write path *before* data hits disk | Lowest bandwidth use; lowest storage footprint | Adds write latency; risk of data loss if engine fails mid-write |
| **Post-process (Asynchronous)** | Data is written first; dedup runs as a background job | High write throughput; simpler write path | Requires temporary duplicate storage; higher peak capacity |

#### 2.2.2 By Granularity (Where)

| Granularity | Unit of Comparison | Efficiency | Compute Cost |
|-------------|-------------------|------------|--------------|
| **File-level** | Entire file hash | Lowest | Lowest |
| **Block-level (Fixed)** | Fixed-size blocks (e.g., 8 KB) | Medium | Medium |
| **Block-level (Variable / CDC)** | Variable-size chunks defined by content | High | Higher |
| **Sub-block / Byte-level** | Differential byte analysis | Highest | Highest |

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following formulas are repeatedly tested in KTU University Examinations. Memorize them.**

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Variables & Units | Typical Use |
|---|--------------|------------------------|------------------|-------------|
| 1 | **Deduplication Ratio (DR)** | $DR = \dfrac{S_{logical}}{S_{physical}}$ | $S_{logical}$ = logical data size (Bytes); $S_{physical}$ = physical stored size (Bytes) | Expressed as `N:1` (e.g., 10:1) |
| 2 | **Data Reduction Ratio** | $DRR = 1 - \dfrac{1}{DR}$ | Dimensionless | Percentage of redundancy eliminated |
| 3 | **Space Savings (%)** | $S_{\%} = \left(1 - \dfrac{S_{physical}}{S_{logical}}\right) \times 100$ | Percent (%) | Reporting metric in storage dashboards |
| 4 | **Effective Capacity** | $C_{eff} = C_{raw} \times DR$ | $C_{raw}$ = raw capacity (TB); $C_{eff}$ = logical capacity exposed to host (TB) | Capacity planning |
| 5 | **Hash Collision Probability (Birthday Bound)** | $P_{coll} \approx 1 - e^{-N^2 / 2H}$ | $N$ = number of chunks hashed; $H$ = hash space size ($2^{bits}$) | Determines false-positive risk |
| 6 | **Reference Count Storage** | $R_{overhead} = n \times \log_2(M)$ | $n$ = references; $M$ = max unique chunks | Metadata overhead estimation |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Deduplication is the cornerstone technology in:

- **Backup & Archival Systems** (e.g., EMC Data Domain) – where identical blocks recur heavily across daily backups.
- **Virtual Desktop Infrastructure (VDI)** – where 1000s of VMs share identical OS templates.
- **Cloud Storage Tiering** – providers like AWS S3 and Azure Blob use server-side dedup to reduce storage costs.
- **Source Code Repositories** – where project forks contain nearly identical codebases.
- **Email Servers** – the same attachment sent to 500 recipients is stored once.

> [!TIP]
> **Why the formula matters in industry:** Capacity planners use $C_{eff} = C_{raw} \times DR$ to negotiate with vendors. If a 100 TB array claims a 10:1 ratio, the effective usable logical capacity exposed to applications is **1000 TB** — but only if the workload is dedup-friendly.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 – Deduplication Ratio from First Principles

**Problem Context:** A storage array receives a logical dataset of size $S_{logical}$ Bytes. After deduplication, the unique chunks physically written to the array occupy $S_{physical}$ Bytes. Derive the deduplication ratio.

#### Step 1: Define the logical data size

The logical size represents the *uncompressed, unprocessed* size of all data presented by the host application.

$$S_{logical} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} L_i$$

where $L_i$ is the size of the $i$-th logical file/block and $k$ is the total number of logical entities written.

#### Step 2: Define the physical data size

The physical size represents what is actually retained on the storage medium after the deduplication engine has eliminated redundant copies.

$$S_{physical} = \sum_{j=1}^{u} U_j$$

where $U_j$ is the size of the $j$-th *unique* chunk and $u$ is the total number of unique chunks, with the property that $u \le k$.

#### Step 3: Express the deduplication ratio

By definition, the deduplication ratio is the logical size divided by the physical size:

$$DR = \frac{S_{logical}}{S_{physical}}$$

#### Step 4: Verify dimensions

Since both numerator and denominator are in **Bytes**, the ratio is **dimensionless**. The result is conventionally expressed in the form `N:1` (read as "N-to-1"), indicating that for every $N$ logical units, only $1$ physical unit is retained.

#### Step 5: Worked Numerical Example

Suppose a backup operation writes **10 identical files**, each of size **500 MB**. The deduplication engine stores only **one copy**.

- $S_{logical} = 10 \times 500 \text{ MB} = 5000 \text{ MB}$
- $S_{physical} = 1 \times 500 \text{ MB} = 500 \text{ MB}$

Substituting into the formula:

$$DR = \frac{5000 \text{ MB}}{500 \text{ MB}} = 10$$

Therefore, the deduplication ratio is **10:1**, meaning 10 logical units map to 1 physical unit.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 – Space Savings Percentage

**Problem Context:** Given a deduplication ratio, derive the percentage of storage capacity reclaimed.

#### Step 1: Recognize the physical-to-logical fraction

The fraction of original data actually stored physically is the inverse of the deduplication ratio:

$$\frac{S_{physical}}{S_{logical}} = \frac{1}{DR}$$

#### Step 2: Compute the eliminated fraction

The fraction of data eliminated (saved) is one minus the stored fraction:

$$S_{fraction} = 1 - \frac{S_{physical}}{S_{logical}} = 1 - \frac{1}{DR}$$

#### Step 3: Convert fraction to percentage

Multiplying by 100 yields the savings percentage:

$$S_{\%} = \left(1 - \frac{1}{DR}\right) \times 100$$

#### Step 4: Apply to the worked example

Using $DR = 10$ from the previous derivation:

$$S_{\%} = \left(1 - \frac{1}{10}\right) \times 100 = \left(1 - 0.1\right) \times 100 = 0.9 \times 100 = 90\%$$

**Interpretation:** 90% of the original logical storage footprint is reclaimed; only 10% of the original data is physically stored.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 – Effective Usable Capacity

**Problem Context:** A storage vendor sells an array with raw capacity $C_{raw}$ and a guaranteed deduplication ratio $DR$. Derive the logical capacity visible to the host.

#### Step 1: Start from the fundamental ratio identity

Rearranging the deduplication ratio definition to solve for the effective capacity:

$$C_{eff} = C_{raw} \times DR$$

#### Step 2: Substitute the values

If $C_{raw} = 50 \text{ TB}$ and $DR = 8:1$:

$$C_{eff} = 50 \text{ TB} \times 8 = 400 \text{ TB}$$

#### Step 3: Interpretation

The customer effectively consumes **400 TB of logical capacity** while the array physically stores only **50 TB**, achieving an 8-fold reduction in physical storage investment.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Caveat:** The deduplication ratio is workload-dependent. Mixed workloads (encrypted data, multimedia, compressed archives) typically yield ratios close to **1.2:1 to 2:1**, whereas VM and backup workloads routinely achieve **10:1 to 30:1**.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix – Hash Collision Probability

In dedup systems, a *hash collision* is a false positive: two different chunks producing the same fingerprint. The probability is governed by the **Birthday Problem** approximation.

For a hash space of $H$ possible values and $N$ chunks already indexed:

$$P_{coll} \approx 1 - e^{-N^2 / 2H}$$

**Typical Industry Choice:** SHA-256 produces $H = 2^{256}$ possible values, making collision probability astronomically small even for $N$ in the trillions. MD5 ($H = 2^{128}$) is now considered cryptographically broken and is *not recommended* for new dedup deployments.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Deduplication Process Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

The following diagram illustrates the canonical *inline* deduplication pipeline used in enterprise backup arrays.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Host Application Writes Logical Data] --> B[Chunking Engine Splits Data into Segments]
    B --> C[Hash Function Generates Fingerprint per Chunk]
    C --> D{Fingerprint Exists in Dedup Index?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Discard Incoming Chunk]
    E --> F[Store Small Pointer to Original Chunk]
    D -- No --> G[Write Unique Chunk to Physical Storage]
    G --> H[Add Fingerprint to Dedup Index]
    H --> I[Update Reference Counter]
    F --> I
    I --> J[Logical Write Acknowledged to Host]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Notice that the only divergence point in the pipeline is the decision diamond `{Fingerprint Exists?}`. The *critical path* for performance is the lookup latency of the dedup index, which is why production systems maintain the index in **RAM (in-memory hash table)** rather than on disk.

---

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Inline vs Post-Process)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph InlinePath[Inline Dedup Path]
        I1[Incoming Write] --> I2[Chunking + Hashing]
        I2 --> I3{Unique?}
        I3 -- Yes --> I4[Store to Disk]
        I3 -- No --> I5[Store Reference Only]
    end
    subgraph PostProcessPath[Post-Process Dedup Path]
        P1[Incoming Write] --> P2[Store to Disk As-Is]
        P2 --> P3[Background Scanner Reads Back]
        P3 --> P4[Chunking + Hashing Off-Line]
        P4 --> P5{Unique?}
        P5 -- No --> P6[Delete Duplicate, Add Reference]
    end
```

> [!TIP]
> **For KTU 14-mark questions:** When asked to *compare* inline and post-process dedup, always structure your answer around **three axes**: (1) write latency impact, (2) peak storage requirement during dedup window, and (3) data protection / fault tolerance.

---

### 4.3 Chunking Strategy Comparison Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Data Stream] --> B{Chunking Strategy}
    B -- Fixed-Length --> C1[Split at Every N Bytes e.g. 8KB]
    B -- Variable-Length CDC --> C2[Split at Content Boundaries via Rabin Fingerprint]
    B -- Sub-block Delta --> C3[Byte-Level Diff Against Reference]
    C1 --> D1[Stable Chunks but Shift Problem]
    C2 --> D2[Resilient to Insertions/Deletions]
    C3 --> D3[Highest Compression Highest CPU]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Content-Defined Chunking (CDC)** uses the **Rabin-Karp rolling hash** to identify natural content boundaries, making the chunk boundaries *content-aware* rather than *position-aware*. This is why CDC is **resilient to the shift problem** (an insertion at byte 1 of a file does not invalidate all subsequent chunk boundaries as it would in fixed-length chunking).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
*Define data deduplication. Differentiate between inline and post-process deduplication with one key advantage of each.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Data deduplication is a storage optimization technique that eliminates redundant copies of repeating data, retaining only a single unique instance on the physical medium and replacing duplicates with small references/pointers.

- **Inline Deduplication:** Performed on the write path *before* data is committed to disk. *Advantage:* Lowest physical storage footprint and minimal bandwidth consumption.
- **Post-Process Deduplication:** Performed as a background task *after* data is written to disk. *Advantage:* Higher write throughput and no added write latency.

---

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
*What is a deduplication ratio? A backup dataset of 8 TB logical size occupies only 1 TB on the deduplicated storage array. Calculate the deduplication ratio and space savings percentage.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The deduplication ratio is the ratio of logical data size to physical data size stored after deduplication.

Given: $S_{logical} = 8$ TB, $S_{physical} = 1$ TB.

$$DR = \frac{S_{logical}}{S_{physical}} = \frac{8 \text{ TB}}{1 \text{ TB}} = 8$$

Deduplication ratio = **8:1**.

$$S_{\%} = \left(1 - \frac{1}{DR}\right) \times 100 = \left(1 - \frac{1}{8}\right) \times 100 = 87.5\%$$

Space savings = **87.5%**.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each – Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Scheme mandates internal choice in Part B. Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]

**(a)** Explain the architecture and working of a block-level data deduplication system with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of hash functions and the deduplication index in detail. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare fixed-length chunking with content-defined chunking (CDC). Why is CDC preferred in modern backup systems? Compute the effective usable capacity for a 60 TB raw array that achieves a deduplication ratio of 12:1 in a VDI environment. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Architecture and Working (7 Marks):**
A block-level deduplication system operates through the following components and stages:

1. **Chunking Engine:** Segments the input data stream into fixed or variable-size blocks (typically 4–128 KB).
2. **Hash Function Module:** Computes a cryptographic hash (SHA-256 recommended) for each chunk to generate a unique fingerprint.
3. **Deduplication Index:** A high-performance in-memory hash table that stores fingerprints of all unique chunks already on disk. Index size often exceeds RAM; spillover uses Bloom filters or cache eviction.
4. **Reference Count Store:** Tracks how many logical references depend on each unique chunk, enabling safe garbage collection.
5. **Storage Tier:** The physical disk array where unique chunks are persisted.

**Working Flow:** The system chunks the input → generates fingerprints → queries the index → if match found, discards the data and stores only a pointer; if no match, writes the unique chunk to disk and updates the index.

**Role of Hash Functions:** Hash functions provide a *compact, fixed-size unique identifier* for variable-length chunks. Strong cryptographic hashes (SHA-256) ensure that the probability of two different chunks producing the same hash is **negligibly small** (≈ $1/2^{256}$), making them suitable as fingerprints.

**Role of the Dedup Index:** The index enables **O(1) average-time lookup** of fingerprints, ensuring that dedup does not become a write-path bottleneck. Modern systems layer Bloom filters in front of the hash table to short-circuit negative lookups.

**(b) Chunking Comparison + Capacity Computation (7 Marks):**

| Feature | Fixed-Length Chunking | Content-Defined Chunking (CDC) |
|---------|----------------------|-------------------------------|
| Chunk boundaries | Position-based (every N bytes) | Content-based (via Rabin fingerprint) |
| Shift problem sensitivity | High – inserting 1 byte shifts all subsequent boundaries | Low – boundaries follow content patterns |
| Compute cost | Lowest | Higher (rolling hash overhead) |
| Dedup ratio | Lower | Higher |

**Why CDC is preferred in backup systems:** Backups of the same source data taken on different days differ only by a few bytes (e.g., timestamp). With fixed-length chunking, a single shifted byte alters every subsequent chunk boundary, producing totally different chunks and ruining dedup efficiency. CDC aligns chunk boundaries to *content patterns*, so identical logical regions are recognized as identical chunks, preserving high dedup ratios.

**Effective Capacity Computation:**

Given $C_{raw} = 60$ TB, $DR = 12:1$:

$$C_{eff} = C_{raw} \times DR = 60 \text{ TB} \times 12 = 720 \text{ TB}$$

The VDI environment can host up to **720 TB of logical desktop data** on a 60 TB physical array.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**
[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper]

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain inline and post-process deduplication architectures. Discuss the trade-offs in terms of write latency, storage footprint, and data protection. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A storage array processes 20 backup jobs, each of 800 GB logical size. The deduplication engine identifies that the array stores only 2 TB of unique physical data. Calculate: (i) the deduplication ratio, (ii) the space savings percentage, and (iii) the data reduction ratio. Comment on the suitability of this ratio for a backup workload. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Inline vs. Post-Process Architecture (7 Marks):**

*Inline Dedup:* Data is chunked, hashed, and deduplicated **on the write path** *before* being written to disk. The unique chunks are committed, and duplicates are replaced with pointers in the same I/O cycle.

*Post-Process Dedup:* Data is **first written to disk in its entirety**, and a background process later reads it back, chunks it, hashes it, and consolidates duplicates.

**Trade-off Analysis:**

| Trade-off Axis | Inline Dedup | Post-Process Dedup |
|----------------|-------------|-------------------|
| Write latency | Higher (dedup adds write-path delay) | Lower (dedup is asynchronous) |
| Storage footprint | Always minimized | Temporarily inflated during dedup window |
| Data protection | Risk if engine crashes mid-write; requires NVRAM/journaling | Safer – data already durable on disk before processing |
| Hardware complexity | Requires high-speed index lookup | Tolerates slower index lookups |

**Conclusion:** Inline dedup is preferred for capacity-constrained backup targets; post-process is preferred where write throughput is paramount (e.g., high-performance primary storage).

**(b) Numerical Analysis (7 Marks):**

Given: 20 backup jobs × 800 GB = 16,000 GB = 16 TB logical. Physical = 2 TB.

**(i) Deduplication Ratio:**

$$DR = \frac{S_{logical}}{S_{physical}} = \frac{16 \text{ TB}}{2 \text{ TB}} = 8$$

Deduplication ratio = **8:1**.

**(ii) Space Savings Percentage:**

$$S_{\%} = \left(1 - \frac{1}{8}\right) \times 100 = 87.5\%$$

Space savings = **87.5%**.

**(iii) Data Reduction Ratio:**

$$DRR = 1 - \frac{1}{DR} = 1 - \frac{1}{8} = 0.875$$

Data reduction ratio = **0.875** (dimensionless).

**Comment on Suitability:** A ratio of 8:1 is **excellent and highly suitable** for backup workloads. Backup data exhibits high inter-version redundancy (e.g., daily full backups of mostly unchanged source data). Industry benchmarks classify 8:1 and above as *strong* dedup performance for backup applications, where the typical range is 10:1 to 30:1 for incremental-style backup strategies.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Writing the deduplication ratio in decimal form (e.g., 8.0) instead of the conventional `N:1` form (8:1). Examiners deduct **0.5 to 1 mark** for incorrect notation.
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Confusing **Deduplication Ratio (DR)** with **Data Reduction Ratio (DRR)**. They are *not* the same. DR is `N:1`; DRR is the fractional form $(1 - 1/DR)$. Many students write the two interchangeably and lose marks.
> - **Common Mistake #3:** In comparison questions (inline vs post-process), students often list features but **fail to mention the trade-off explicitly**. Always state the *cost* of choosing one over the other.
> - **Common Mistake #4:** Forgetting the units in capacity calculations. Always write `TB` or `GB` explicitly; bare numbers without units draw negative marking.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** Deduplication eliminates redundant data copies, retaining only a single instance and replacing duplicates with small pointers.
- **Deduplication Ratio Formula:** $DR = S_{logical} / S_{physical}$, expressed as `N:1`.
- **Space Savings Formula:** $S_{\%} = (1 - 1/DR) \times 100$.
- **Effective Capacity Formula:** $C_{eff} = C_{raw} \times DR$.
- **Two Classification Axes:** Timing (inline vs post-process) and Granularity (file-level, block-level, sub-block).
- **Hashing is the Backbone:** SHA-256 is the modern standard; MD5 is deprecated due to collision vulnerabilities.
- **Fixed vs Variable Chunking:** Fixed is faster but suffers from the *shift problem*; CDC (Content-Defined Chunking) is resilient to insertions/deletions and preferred for backups.
- **Reference Counting** is essential for safe garbage collection of orphaned unique chunks.
- **Trade-offs Always Exist:** Inline = lower footprint, higher write latency; Post-process = higher throughput, temporary storage inflation.
- **Workload Dependency:** Backup and VDI workloads yield 10:1 to 30:1; encrypted or compressed data yields ~1.2:1 to 2:1.
- **Bloom Filters & RAM-Cached Indices** are used in production to accelerate fingerprint lookups in the write path.
- **Garbage Collection** reclaims space from chunks whose reference count has dropped to zero.
- **Examiner's Mantra:** When asked for a numerical, *always* show the formula, substitution, and final answer with units. A naked number with no working draws zero marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Storage Arrays - Architectural Principles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST867 - STORAGE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Storage Arrays - Architectural Principles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Storage Array** is a hardware infrastructure that consolidates, manages, and provisions block-based (or file-based) data storage across multiple physical disk drives presented as one or more logical storage units to a host system. The architecture is governed by a redundant pair of **Storage Array Controllers**, front-end host I/O interfaces, back-end drive interfaces, cache memory, and logical volume management firmware.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, a **Storage Array** is the physical realization of a **Disk Array Subsystem** that hides individual disk heterogeneity behind a unified **Logical Unit Number (LUN)** abstraction, providing **RAID (Redundant Array of Independent Disks)** protection, hot-swap capability, and centralized capacity management.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The "Library Warehouse"**
> Imagine a massive public library where thousands of books (data blocks) are stored across hundreds of shelves (physical disks). Instead of letting visitors wander into the storage room, a librarian (the **Storage Controller**) sits at a desk, maintains a master index (the **Metadata/Volume Table**), and fetches/returns books on behalf of the visitors. If one shelf collapses (disk failure), the librarian already has photocopies (parity/mirror) stored elsewhere. A second librarian stands by as a backup (redundant controller). Visitors only know about "Section A" or "Reference Room 3" — they have no idea which physical shelf the book is on. This is precisely how a storage array abstracts physical disks into logical volumes.

## 1.3 Key Architectural Terminology (Must Memorize)

- **Front-End**: The interface between the storage array and the host servers (SAN switches, HBAs, iSCSI, FC).
- **Back-End**: The internal connection between the controllers and the disk drives (SAS, SATA, NVMe).
- **Storage Controller**: The "brain" of the array; executes I/O, manages RAID, handles caching, and runs management firmware.
- **Cache Memory**: High-speed volatile memory (**DRAM** or **NVRAM**) used to buffer reads/writes to accelerate I/O.
- **LUN (Logical Unit Number)**: A logical addressable unit presented to a host; backed by one or more physical disks via RAID.
- **Disk Enclosure / Tray / Shelf**: A chassis that holds physical drives and connects them to the back-end.
- **JBOD (Just a Bunch Of Disks)**: Disks presented individually without RAID.
- **RAID Group**: A logical grouping of disks sharing a common parity/mirroring scheme.
- **Storage Pool / Storage Tier**: A logical aggregate of physical capacity sliced across disk types (e.g., SSD tier + NL-SAS tier).

## 1.4 Physical Constants / Standard Metrics (KTU High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following are universally tested in KTU ESE:
> - **1 Byte = 8 bits**
> - **1 KB = 1024 Bytes (2^10)** — Note: Decimal (1000) vs Binary (1024) distinction is **exam-favorite**.
> - **1 MB = 1024 KB**, **1 GB = 1024 MB**, **1 TB = 1024 GB**, **1 PB = 1024 TB**
> - **RPM (Revolutions Per Minute)** standard desktop: **7,200 RPM**; enterprise: **10,000 RPM** or **15,000 RPM**
> - **Average Rotational Latency = 1 / (2 × RPM / 60) seconds** (half a full rotation)
> - **IOPS (Input/Output Operations Per Second)** is the standard performance metric.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Layers of a Storage Array

A modern enterprise storage array is built as a **5-layer hierarchical model**:

1. **Host Layer** — Servers, HBAs, OS, file systems, applications.
2. **Fabric Layer** — FC switches, Ethernet (iSCSI), FCoE, NVMe-oF.
3. **Storage Controller Layer** — Front-end ports, cache, microprocessors, RAID engine, volume manager.
4. **Back-End Layer** — SAS/SATA/NVMe interconnects to drive enclosures.
5. **Drive Layer** — Physical HDD, SSD, or hybrid.

> [!NOTE]
> The **Storage Controller Layer** is the heart of the array. KTU questions frequently ask for the *role* of the controller — answer must mention: I/O routing, RAID computation, cache management, copy services, and LUN masking.

## 2.2 Disk Drive Internal Model (Why Latency Exists)

For an HDD, the I/O request must undergo **three mechanical delays**:

- **Seek Time (Ts)** — Time to position the read/write head over the correct track. **Typical enterprise: 3–8 ms**.
- **Rotational Latency (Tr)** — Time for the platter to rotate the sector under the head.
- **Data Transfer Time (Td)** — Time to actually read/write the bits.

$$
T_{access} = T_{seek} + T_{rotational} + T_{transfer}
$$

For SSDs, **Ts ≈ 0** and **Tr ≈ 0**; only NAND page read/program/erase time matters, making SSDs governed by **internal controller queues and NAND cell technology (SLC, MLC, TLC, QLC)**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Average Rotational Latency | $T_r = \dfrac{30}{RPM} \text{ (in seconds)} = \dfrac{30000}{RPM} \text{ (ms)}$ | ms | Half a full rotation |
| 2 | Total I/O Time (HDD) | $T_{io} = T_s + T_r + \dfrac{B}{R_{transfer}}$ | ms | B = bytes, R = MB/s |
| 3 | Effective Capacity RAID 0 | $C_{eff} = n \times C_{disk}$ | GB | No redundancy |
| 4 | Effective Capacity RAID 1 | $C_{eff} = \dfrac{n \times C_{disk}}{2}$ | GB | Mirroring |
| 5 | Effective Capacity RAID 5 | $C_{eff} = (n-1) \times C_{disk}$ | GB | Single parity |
| 6 | Effective Capacity RAID 6 | $C_{eff} = (n-2) \times C_{disk}$ | GB | Double parity |
| 7 | Effective Capacity RAID 10 | $C_{eff} = \dfrac{n \times C_{disk}}{2}$ | GB | Stripe of mirrors |
| 8 | IOPS for Random Read | $IOPS = \dfrac{1}{T_s + T_r} \times n_{spindle}$ | ops/s | Spindles multiply |
| 9 | Service Time Utilization (Queueing) | $\rho = \dfrac{\lambda \cdot T_s}{n_{servers}}$ | unitless | < 1 for stability |
| 10 | MTTF Array | $MTTF_{array} = \dfrac{MTTF_{disk}}{n}$ | hours | Series disks (no RAID) |
| 11 | MTTDL RAID 5 | $MTTDL_{RAID5} \approx \dfrac{MTTF^2}{n \cdot (n-1) \cdot MTTR}$ | hours | Mirror of MTBF |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favorite**: The **RAID 5 write penalty = 4 I/O operations** (2 reads + 2 writes for a single logical write). **RAID 6 write penalty = 6 I/O operations**. **RAID 1 write penalty = 2**.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Storage array architecture is the **backbone of every enterprise data center**. It directly impacts:
- **Database transaction throughput** (OLTP systems).
- **Big Data analytics** (Hadoop/Spark under HDFS sits on storage arrays).
- **Cloud hyperscalers** (AWS EBS, Azure Disks are virtualized storage arrays).
- **Disaster Recovery** (asynchronous/synchronous replication between arrays at geo-distance).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Average Rotational Latency for an HDD

> The platter rotates at a constant angular velocity. The probability of the desired sector being at any random position under the head is uniformly distributed between $0$ and a full rotation.

A full rotation takes time:
$$
T_{full} = \frac{60 \text{ seconds}}{RPM}
$$

The **average wait** for the desired sector to arrive under the head is **half a full rotation** (because on average, the sector is halfway through its rotation cycle when the head arrives):
$$
T_{rotational} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot T_{full}
$$

Substituting the full rotation time:
$$
T_{rotational} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{60}{RPM}
$$

Converting to milliseconds by multiplying by 1000:
$$
T_{rotational} = \frac{30000}{RPM} \text{ ms}
$$

**Worked Example (KTU Style)**: For a 15,000 RPM enterprise HDD:
$$
T_r = \frac{30000}{15000} = 2 \text{ ms}
$$

For a 7,200 RPM nearline HDD:
$$
T_r = \frac{30000}{7200} \approx 4.17 \text{ ms}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: RAID 5 Effective Capacity & Write Penalty

> **Given:** $n$ disks in a RAID 5 group, each of capacity $C_{disk}$.

**Step 1 — Identify usable disks.**
One disk's worth of capacity is reserved for **distributed parity**. Out of $n$ physical disks, only $(n-1)$ contribute user data.

**Step 2 — Multiply by disk capacity.**
$$
C_{effective} = (n-1) \times C_{disk}
$$

**Step 3 — Derive Write Penalty (the famous 4 I/O).**
When a host issues a *single* logical write to a RAID 5 stripe, the controller must:
- (a) **Read the old data** (to compute new parity) — 1 read I/O.
- (b) **Read the old parity** (to XOR with new data) — 1 read I/O.
- (c) **Write the new data** to the data disk — 1 write I/O.
- (d) **Write the new parity** to the parity disk — 1 write I/O.

$$
\text{Total physical I/Os} = 2 \text{ reads} + 2 \text{ writes} = 4 \text{ I/Os}
$$

Hence the **RAID 5 Write Penalty = 4**. This is *the* KTU exam favorite for both Part A and Part B.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: MTTDL (Mean Time To Data Loss) for RAID 5

> **Given:** $MTTF_{disk}$ per disk, $MTTR$ = mean time to repair (replace failed disk), $n$ = number of disks in the RAID 5 group.

In a RAID 5 group, **data loss occurs only when a second disk fails before the first failed disk is rebuilt**. We model this as a sequence of two failures.

**Step 1** — The system is functional until the first disk fails. Mean time to first failure:
$$
MTTF_{first} = \frac{MTTF_{disk}}{n}
$$

**Step 2** — After the first failure, the system is in a *degraded* state. A second failure during this window causes data loss. The probability of a second failure during the rebuild window $MTTR$ is:
$$
P_{second\_fail} \approx \frac{n \cdot MTTR}{MTTF_{disk}}
$$

(The factor $n$ comes from the remaining $n-1$ data disks + 1 parity disk, approximated as $n$.)

**Step 3** — Combine to find the **Mean Time To Data Loss**:
$$
MTTDL_{RAID5} = \frac{MTTF_{first}}{P_{second\_fail}} = \frac{MTTF_{disk}/n}{n \cdot MTTR / MTTF_{disk}}
$$

$$
MTTDL_{RAID5} = \frac{MTTF_{disk}^{2}}{n^{2} \cdot MTTR}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> A more refined version (used in textbooks) uses $n(n-1)$ in the denominator. The exact coefficient is **not** the test point — KTU tests the *square-in-numerator, linear-in-$MTTR$* relationship.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: RAID Level Comparison (The Most Tested Table)

| RAID Level | Min Disks | Parity Type | Usable Capacity | Read Penalty | Write Penalty | Fault Tolerance | Best Use Case |
|------------|-----------|-------------|-----------------|--------------|---------------|-----------------|---------------|
| RAID 0 (Striping) | 2 | None | $n \cdot C$ | 1 | 1 | 0 disks | High-perf scratch |
| RAID 1 (Mirroring) | 2 | Mirror | $\dfrac{n \cdot C}{2}$ | 1 | 2 | $n/2$ disks | OS, logs |
| RAID 5 | 3 | Single distributed parity | $(n-1) \cdot C$ | 1 | 4 | 1 disk | General file/database |
| RAID 6 | 4 | Double distributed parity | $(n-2) \cdot C$ | 1 | 6 | 2 disks | Archival, large capacity |
| RAID 10 (1+0) | 4 | Stripe of mirrors | $\dfrac{n \cdot C}{2}$ | 1 | 2 | 1 per mirror pair | High-perf DB |
| RAID 50 | 6 | Stripe of RAID 5 | $(n - k) \cdot C$ | 1 | 4 | 1 per RAID 5 | Large DBs |
| RAID 60 | 8 | Stripe of RAID 6 | $(n - 2k) \cdot C$ | 1 | 6 | 2 per RAID 6 | Mission-critical |

(where $C$ = capacity of a single disk, $k$ = number of RAID 5/6 sub-groups, $n$ = total disks)
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Internal Architecture of a Storage Array

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    H1[Host Server 1] -->|FC iSCSI NVMe-oF| FE
    H2[Host Server 2] -->|FC iSCSI NVMe-oF| FE
    H3[Host Server 3] -->|FC iSCSI NVMe-oF| FE
    FE[Front End Ports HBAs] --> CTRL
    CTRL[Storage Controller Pair Active Passive]
    CTRL --> CACHE[Shared Cache DRAM NVRAM]
    CTRL --> BE[Back End SAS SATA NVMe]
    BE --> E1[Drive Enclosure 1 SSDs]
    BE --> E2[Drive Enclosure 2 HDDs]
    BE --> E3[Drive Enclosure 3 NL SAS]
    E1 --> LUN1[LUN 0 High Perf Tier]
    E2 --> LUN2[LUN 1 Standard Tier]
    E3 --> LUN3[LUN 2 Capacity Tier]
    CTRL --> MGT[Management Interface GUI CLI REST API]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: I/O Data Path (Read Operation)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Host as Host Server
    participant FE as Front End Port
    participant Ctrl as Storage Controller
    participant Cache as Cache Memory
    participant BE as Back End
    participant Disk as Physical Disk

    Host->>FE: Read Request for Block X
    FE->>Ctrl: Forward I/O to active controller
    alt Cache HIT
        Ctrl->>Cache: Look up block X
        Cache-->>Ctrl: Data found
    else Cache MISS
        Ctrl->>BE: Translate LUN offset to physical disk
        BE->>Disk: Seek + Read
        Disk-->>BE: Raw sectors
        BE-->>Ctrl: Block X retrieved
        Ctrl->>Cache: Store for future reads
    end
    Ctrl-->>FE: Return data
    FE-->>Host: Deliver block X
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: RAID 5 Stripe Layout

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S0["Stripe 0: D0 D1 D2 P0"]
    S1["Stripe 1: D3 P1 D4 D5"]
    S2["Stripe 2: D6 D7 P2 D8"]
    S3["Stripe 3: P3 D9 D10 D11"]
    D0[Disk 0] --- S0
    D1[Disk 1] --- S0
    D2[Disk 2] --- S0
    D3[Disk 3] --- S0
    P0[Parity] --- S0
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Array Components

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph S1 [Storage Array Chassis]
        A[Power Supplies N+1 redundant] --> B
        B[Cooling Fans] --> C
        C[Storage Controllers Active Active or Active Passive]
        C --> D[Cache Memory]
        C --> E[RAID Engine ASIC]
        E --> F[Back End Loops]
        F --> G[Drive Enclosures]
    end
    subgraph S2 [Drive Enclosures]
        G --> G1[2.5 inch 10K 15K SAS]
        G --> G2[3.5 inch 7.2K NL SAS]
        G --> G3[2.5 inch SSD]
    end
    H[External Management Workstation] -->|Out of Band Ethernet| C
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, May 2024 Type]**

### Q1. Define the term "Storage Array". List any four of its key architectural components.
**(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
A Storage Array is a hardware system that consolidates multiple physical disk drives into one or more logical storage units (LUNs) and presents them to host systems through a storage controller.

**Four key architectural components:**
1. **Storage Controller** (the brain that handles I/O routing and RAID computation).
2. **Cache Memory** (high-speed DRAM/NVRAM to buffer reads and writes).
3. **Front-End Ports** (FC, iSCSI, or FCoE interfaces to host servers).
4. **Back-End Interconnect** (SAS/SATA/NVMe links to disk enclosures).
5. *(Bonus)* Drive Enclosures, Redundant Power Supplies, Management Interface.

### Q2. What is the RAID 5 write penalty? Justify with the I/O breakdown.
**(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
The RAID 5 write penalty is **4 physical I/O operations** required to complete a single logical host write.

**Breakdown:**
- 1 read of the **old data block** (to compute new parity).
- 1 read of the **old parity block** (to XOR with new data).
- 1 write of the **new data block**.
- 1 write of the **new parity block**.

**Total = 2 reads + 2 writes = 4 I/Os.**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - ESE Model, Module 1 Internal Choice Pattern]**

---

### ❓ CHOICE A (14 Marks)

**Q. (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the internal architecture of a modern enterprise storage array. Label the front-end, back-end, controllers, cache, and drive enclosures. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A modern enterprise storage array is built as a **5-layer hierarchy** (refer to Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1). The architectural flow is as follows:

1. **Host Layer** — Host servers issue block-level I/O requests over a storage fabric (FC, iSCSI, or NVMe-oF).
2. **Fabric Layer** — FC switches or Ethernet switches carry the I/O to the array.
3. **Storage Controller Layer** — The array has **two controllers (Active-Passive or Active-Active)** for high availability. Each controller has:
   - **Front-end ports** (HBAs) to receive host I/O.
   - **Cache memory** (DRAM backed by NVRAM) to accelerate I/O.
   - A **RAID engine** (hardware ASIC) for parity computation.
   - **Microprocessors** running the array OS (e.g., ONTAP, PowerVault OS, VSP).
4. **Back-End Layer** — Internal **SAS, SATA, or NVMe** loops connect the controllers to drive enclosures.
5. **Drive Layer** — Physical HDDs, SSDs, or hybrid drives, organized into **RAID groups**, then into **storage pools**, and finally exposed to hosts as **LUNs**.

The **active controller** handles all I/O; the **standby controller** takes over in case of failure (failover time < 1 second typically).

**Marks Distribution:** Diagram (3) + 5 layer explanation (2) + Controller internals (2).

---

**Q. (b)** Compare RAID 0, RAID 1, RAID 5, RAID 6, and RAID 10 with respect to: (i) Minimum number of disks, (ii) Effective capacity, (iii) Fault tolerance, (iv) Write penalty. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| RAID | Min Disks | Effective Capacity | Fault Tolerance | Write Penalty |
|------|-----------|--------------------|------------------|---------------|
| RAID 0 | 2 | $n \cdot C$ | 0 disks | 1 |
| RAID 1 | 2 | $\dfrac{n \cdot C}{2}$ | $n/2$ disks (any 1 per mirror) | 2 |
| RAID 5 | 3 | $(n-1) \cdot C$ | 1 disk | 4 |
| RAID 6 | 4 | $(n-2) \cdot C$ | 2 disks | 6 |
| RAID 10 | 4 | $\dfrac{n \cdot C}{2}$ | 1 per mirror pair | 2 |

**Comparative analysis (verbal):**
- **RAID 0** has the best performance and capacity efficiency but **no fault tolerance** — a single disk failure destroys the entire array.
- **RAID 1** provides excellent read performance (parallel reads from both mirrors) and fast rebuilds, but at **50% storage overhead**.
- **RAID 5** is the workhorse of mid-range arrays — good balance of capacity, performance, and protection. Vulnerable during rebuilds of large drives.
- **RAID 6** adds a second parity disk to protect against dual disk failures, critical for **large-capacity nearline drives** where rebuild times are long.
- **RAID 10** (stripe of mirrors) is the **best for high-I/O databases** (OLTP) because it inherits RAID 0's striping speed and RAID 1's fast write, but at 50% capacity cost.

**Marks Distribution:** Table (4) + 3 verbal comparisons (3).

---

### ❓ CHOICE B (14 Marks)

**Q. (a)** Derive the formula for the **Mean Time To Data Loss (MTTDL)** of a RAID 5 group containing $n$ disks, each with mean time to failure $MTTF$ and mean time to repair $MTTR$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Refer to **Derivation 3** in Section 3.3 of these notes.

**Step 1:** Time to first failure in a parallel group of $n$ disks:
$$
MTTF_{first} = \frac{MTTF}{n}
$$

**Step 2:** Probability of a second failure occurring during the rebuild window $MTTR$:
$$
P_{second} \approx \frac{n \cdot MTTR}{MTTF}
$$

**Step 3:** Divide to obtain MTTDL:
$$
MTTDL = \frac{MTTF_{first}}{P_{second}} = \frac{MTTF / n}{n \cdot MTTR / MTTF}
$$

$$
\boxed{MTTDL_{RAID5} = \frac{MTTF^{2}}{n^{2} \cdot MTTR}}
$$

**Interpretation:**
- MTTDL scales **quadratically with MTTF** — using higher-reliability disks dramatically extends array life.
- MTTDL scales **inversely with $n^2$** — adding more disks reduces reliability (more chances of failure).
- MTTDL scales **inversely with MTTR** — faster disk replacement (smaller $MTTR$) extends array life.

**Numerical Example:** If $MTTF = 1{,}000{,}000$ hours, $n = 8$ disks, $MTTR = 24$ hours:
$$
MTTDL = \frac{(10^6)^2}{64 \cdot 24} \approx 6.5 \times 10^{8} \text{ hours} \approx 74{,}000 \text{ years}
$$

**Marks Distribution:** Step 1 (2) + Step 2 (2) + Step 3 (2) + Numerical/Interpretation (1).

---

**Q. (b)** A storage array has 10 disks of 1 TB capacity each, configured as RAID 5. Calculate the **effective usable capacity**, **write penalty**, and the **average IOPS** if each disk delivers 180 IOPS and the workload is 70% read / 30% write. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: Effective Usable Capacity (RAID 5 formula)**
$$
C_{eff} = (n - 1) \times C_{disk} = (10 - 1) \times 1 \text{ TB} = 9 \text{ TB}
$$

**Step 2: Write Penalty (RAID 5 = 4)**
Already derived above — every logical write expands into 4 physical I/Os.

**Step 3: Total Raw IOPS available**
$$
IOPS_{raw} = 10 \text{ disks} \times 180 \text{ IOPS/disk} = 1800 \text{ IOPS}
$$

**Step 4: Adjust for Read/Write Mix using Write Penalty**
The array must serve 70% reads (penalty = 1) and 30% writes (penalty = 4). The *effective* throughput per logical IOPS is:
$$
\text{Avg Physical I/O per Logical I/O} = (0.70 \times 1) + (0.30 \times 4) = 0.70 + 1.20 = 1.90
$$

**Step 5: Compute Effective IOPS**
$$
IOPS_{eff} = \frac{IOPS_{raw}}{1.90} = \frac{1800}{1.90} \approx 947 \text{ IOPS}
$$

**Final Answer:**
- Usable capacity = **9 TB**
- Write penalty = **4**
- Effective IOPS ≈ **947 IOPS**

**Marks Distribution:** Capacity (2) + Penalty concept (1) + Raw IOPS (1) + Mix adjustment (2) + Final answer (1).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse RAID 5 with RAID 6 write penalties** — they are **4** and **6** respectively. Many students write "4 for both" and lose 1 mark.
> 2. **RAID 0 has NO write penalty** — penalty = 1. Markers deduct for writing "no write penalty" without justification.
> 3. **MTTDL formula for RAID 5** — students often write the **RAID 1 MTTDL** ($\approx MTTF^2 / MTTR$) when asked for RAID 5. Remember: RAID 5 has the $n^2$ (or $n(n-1)$) factor in the denominator.
> 4. **Decimal vs Binary GB** — KTU accepts both, but **explicitly state the assumption** ("Assuming 1 GB = 1024 MB") to earn full marks.
> 5. **Capacity of RAID 10** — students frequently write $(n-1) \cdot C$ (wrong, that's RAID 5). Correct: $\dfrac{n \cdot C}{2}$.
> 6. **Active-Passive vs Active-Active** — in Part A definitions, be precise: Active-Passive = one controller handles I/O, the other is hot standby; Active-Active = both controllers share the load.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Bullets — Memorize Before Exam**

- [x] **Storage Array = Controllers + Cache + Back-End + Disk Enclosures** (the 4 hardware pillars).
- [x] **5-Layer Architecture**: Host → Fabric → Controller → Back-End → Drive.
- [x] **Active-Passive** = one active, one standby; **Active-Active** = load shared (superior).
- [x] **Cache is volatile** (DRAM) — needs **battery-backed NVRAM** for write-coalescing during power loss.
- [x] **Average Rotational Latency = 30000 / RPM** in milliseconds.
- [x] **RAID 0** = striping only, capacity = $nC$, no fault tolerance.
- [x] **RAID 1** = mirroring, capacity = $nC/2$, write penalty = 2.
- [x] **RAID 5** = single parity, capacity = $(n-1)C$, write penalty = 4, tolerates 1 disk failure.
- [x] **RAID 6** = double parity, capacity = $(n-2)C$, write penalty = 6, tolerates 2 disk failures.
- [x] **RAID 10** = stripe of mirrors, capacity = $nC/2$, write penalty = 2, best DB performance.
- [x] **MTTDL RAID 5** $\approx MTTF^2 / (n^2 \cdot MTTR)$ — quadratic in MTTF, inverse-square in $n$.
- [x] **Effective IOPS** for mixed workload = $\dfrac{IOPS_{raw}}{(R \cdot 1) + (W \cdot \text{penalty})}$ where $R+W = 1$.
- [x] **1 KB = 1024 Bytes** (binary), but vendor drives use **1 KB = 1000 Bytes** (decimal) — always declare.
- [x] **LUN** = logical addressable unit; backed by RAID group; presented via front-end.
- [x] **Front-end protocols**: FC, iSCSI, FCoE, NVMe-oF. **Back-end protocols**: SAS, SATA, NVMe.
- [x] **JBOD** = no RAID; disks exposed individually. Opposite of RAID 0 in terms of abstraction (JBOD = no striping).
- [x] **Hot Spare** = standby disk that auto-rebuilds when a member disk fails — does NOT count toward RAID capacity.
- [x] **Cache Hit Ratio** dramatically affects latency — KTU may ask: "Why is cache important?" — Answer: reduces backend I/O, lowers latency, absorbs write bursts.
- [x] **Read-ahead / Write-back** cache modes are the two main caching policies.
- [x] **Thin Provisioning** = allocate logical capacity > physical capacity; relies on over-commitment.
- [x] **Wide Striping** = stripe a LUN across many disks (e.g., 100+ disks) — improves performance, increases rebuild time.
- [x] **Drive types**: 15K SAS (high perf), 10K SAS (balanced), 7.2K NL-SAS (capacity), SSD (lowest latency, highest IOPS/$, no moving parts).
- [x] **Formulas to memorize cold**: $T_r = 30000/RPM$, RAID 5/6 capacity & write penalty, MTTDL of RAID 5.
- [x] **KTU Golden Rule**: When a question gives read/write mix + write penalty, the effective IOPS formula **must** be used.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Tip**: KTU 2024 Scheme Part B (14-mark) questions on this topic *almost always* ask for either a **RAID comparison table** or a **numerical problem combining capacity, IOPS, and write penalty**. Master Section 3.2 and 3.3 — they appear in **every ESE paper**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Replication
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST867 - STORAGE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Replication

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Replication** is the process of creating and maintaining multiple identical copies of data (replicas) across one or more storage systems, geographic locations, or storage media to ensure **data availability, fault tolerance, disaster recovery, and load balancing** in enterprise storage environments.

In KTU 2024 Scheme context, replication is formally defined as a **redundancy strategy** in Information Storage and Management (ISM) where every storage object (LUN, volume, file, or block) has one or more redundant copies (replicas) maintained in real-time or near-real-time across the storage infrastructure.

$$ \text{Replication} = \{\text{Primary Copy}\} + \{\text{Replica}_1, \text{Replica}_2, \ldots, \text{Replica}_n\} $$

where each replica maintains an evolving state synchronously or asynchronously with the primary.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Replication is distinct from **Backup**. Backup is a point-in-time copy used for archival and recovery from logical corruption, while replication provides **continuous** data protection and is the primary mechanism for **Disaster Recovery (DR)**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a student preparing for the KTU semester exam, and you keep **three copies of your notes**:
- One at home (Primary)
- One in your friend's locker (Replica 1 - same city)
- One in your relative's house in another district (Replica 2 - different city)

If your house burns down (site failure), you still have the friend's copy. If your city is hit by a flood, the relative's copy saves you. This is exactly how **replication** works in storage systems — multiple copies of the same data are kept at different physical locations, so a single point of failure never wipes out the data.

> [!NOTE]
> **Storage Efficiency Note:** Pure replication creates exact duplicates, so storage cost scales **linearly** with the number of replicas. For a replication factor of 3, you need **3× the original storage capacity**.

## 1.3 Key Terms (Must-Know for KTU Board Exam)

| Term | Meaning | Standard Unit |
|------|---------|---------------|
| **Primary Copy (Source)** | The original write-target volume | — |
| **Secondary Copy (Target/Replica)** | The duplicate receiving writes | — |
| **RPO** | Recovery Point Objective (max acceptable data loss) | seconds, minutes |
| **RTO** | Recovery Time Objective (max acceptable downtime) | seconds, hours |
| **Replica Consistency** | Degree to which replicas match the primary | Eventual / Strong |
| **Replication Factor (RF)** | Number of total copies maintained (including primary) | Integer ≥ 1 |
| **Write Ordering** | Sequential guarantee of replicated writes | — |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic of Replication (Step-by-Step Breakdown)

Replication operates on the principle of **propagating write operations** from a source to one or more targets. The flow is conceptually as follows:

- **Step 1 — Write Initiation:** A host issues a write I/O to the primary LUN/volume.
- **Step 2 — Local Commit:** The primary storage system acknowledges the write to the host (after caching or disk flush).
- **Step 3 — Replication Engine Trigger:** The replication engine intercepts the write (or its delta) and queues it for transmission to the target.
- **Step 4 — Network Transmission:** The write delta is serialized over an IP network (iSCSI, FC, NFS) to the remote site.
- **Step 5 — Remote Commit:** The target storage system applies the write to the replica volume.
- **Step 6 — Acknowledgment Path:** Depending on the mode (sync/async), an acknowledgment is returned to the primary.
- **Step 7 — Consistency Verification:** Periodic checksum or bitmap comparison validates replica integrity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why replicate?** The "3 R's of Replication" as per EMC Information Storage & Management textbook:
> 1. **Reliability** – survive component/site failures
> 2. **Resilience** – degrade gracefully under partial failure
> 3. **Responsiveness** – serve reads from nearest replica (geo-distribution)

## 2.2 Replication Modes — The Critical Classification

### A) Synchronous Replication
The host I/O is **not acknowledged** until the write has been committed to **both** primary and replica.

$$ T_{\text{host-ack}} = T_{\text{local-write}} + T_{\text{network-RTT}} + T_{\text{remote-write}} $$

### B) Asynchronous Replication
The host I/O is acknowledged after local commit; the replica is updated **in the background** with some delay.

$$ T_{\text{host-ack}} = T_{\text{local-write}} \quad ; \quad T_{\text{replica-update}} = T_{\text{host-ack}} + \Delta t_{\text{propagation}} $$

### C) Semi-Synchronous (Adaptive)
A hybrid where the host waits for confirmation from **at least one** of N replicas.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Symbol Meaning | KTU Exam Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $RPO = 0$ (for sync replication) | Data loss window | Disaster Recovery planning |
| 2 | $RPO = \Delta t_{\text{propagation}}$ (async) | Lag time | Tells how much data can be lost |
| 3 | $\text{Bandwidth} \geq \dfrac{\text{Data\_to\_sync}}{\text{Available\_Window}}$ | B/W calc. | Network sizing question |
| 4 | $\text{Storage Overhead} = (N - 1) \times 100\%$ | N = replicas | Cost calculation |
| 5 | $T_{\text{replica-consistency}} = T_{\text{local}} + 2 \times RTT$ | Round-trip latency | Sync replication timing |
| 6 | $\text{Throughput}_{\text{max}} = \dfrac{\text{Bandwidth}}{\text{Block\_size}}$ | I/O rate | Capacity planning |
| 7 | $\text{Replication Factor} = N$ | Total copies incl. primary | RAID / Erasure-coding comparison |
| 8 | $\text{Write\_Amplification} = 1 + (N - 1)$ | For sync write | Performance impact |
| 9 | $\text{Availability} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{N}(1 - A_i)$ | System availability | Redundancy math |
| 10 | $\text{MTTDL} = \dfrac{\text{MTTF}}{N^2 - N}$ | Mean Time To Data Loss | For mirrored pair |

> [!WARNING]
> In exam answers, **never** confuse $N$ (total replicas including primary) with the number of secondary copies. $N-1$ secondary copies means $N$ total copies on disk.

## 2.4 Replication Topologies (KTU High-Weightage Topic)

- **Local Replication:** Within the same data center (e.g., intra-array mirroring).
- **Remote Replication:** Across data centers (Metro or Continental distance).
- **Cascading Replication:** Primary → Secondary → Tertiary (used to reduce bandwidth on the primary).
- **Multi-target Replication:** One primary replicated to many targets.
- **Bi-directional / Mutual Replication:** Two sites replicate to each other (active-active).

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Banking/Financial Systems:** Use synchronous replication between two data centers for **zero RPO**.
- **Cloud Storage (AWS S3, Azure Blob):** Cross-region replication for **geo-redundancy**.
- **Distributed Databases (Cassandra, HDFS):** Use replication factor of 3 by default for fault tolerance.
- **CDN Systems:** Use replication of content to edge servers for **low-latency reads**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Bandwidth Required for Synchronous Replication

**Problem (KTU-style):** A primary storage system generates **4000 write IOPS** with an average block size of **32 KB**. Each I/O must be replicated synchronously to a remote site. Find the minimum network bandwidth required.

### Given:
$$ \text{IOPS} = 4000 \quad , \quad \text{BlockSize} = 32 \text{ KB} $$

### Step 1: Convert block size to bytes

$$ \text{BlockSize} = 32 \text{ KB} = 32 \times 1024 \text{ bytes} = 32768 \text{ bytes} $$

> *Reasoning:* Standard SI/IEC convention uses 1 KB = 1024 bytes when dealing with storage block sizes in RAID/replication problems.

### Step 2: Write the throughput equation

$$ \text{Required Bandwidth (Bps)} = \text{IOPS} \times \text{BlockSize} $$

### Step 3: Substitute the values

$$ \text{Bandwidth} = 4000 \times 32768 \text{ bytes/sec} $$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Bandwidth} &= 4000 \times 32768 \\
&= 131{,}072{,}000 \text{ Bps} \\
&= 131.072 \times 10^6 \text{ Bps} \\
&= 125 \text{ MiB/s} \quad \left[\because 1 \text{ MiB} = 2^{20} \text{ bytes}\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Convert to Mbps (network units)

$$ \text{Bandwidth in Mbps} = \dfrac{131{,}072{,}000 \times 8}{10^6} = 1048.576 \text{ Mbps} \approx 1.05 \text{ Gbps} $$

> *Reasoning:* Network links are rated in bits per second, while storage is in bytes. Multiply by 8 to convert bytes → bits, then scale by $10^6$ for Mbps.

### Final Answer:

$$ \boxed{\text{Minimum Bandwidth} \approx 1.05 \text{ Gbps (round up to 2 Gbps link in practice)} } $$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: RPO Calculation in Asynchronous Replication

**Problem:** A replicated database writes batches every **5 minutes** to the secondary. The network has an average **propagation lag of 90 seconds**. What is the **RPO**?

### Step 1: RPO Definition

$$ RPO = \text{Time between last successful replica write and disaster} $$

### Step 2: Identify the worst-case data loss window

In asynchronous replication, the worst case is when the disaster occurs **immediately after** the local commit but **before** the next batch reaches the replica.

$$
\begin{aligned}
RPO &= \text{Batch Interval} - \text{Propagation Lag} \\
&= 300 \text{ s} - 90 \text{ s} \\
&= 210 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Interpret

$$ \boxed{RPO = 210 \text{ seconds} = 3.5 \text{ minutes} } $$

> *Interpretation:* In the worst case, up to 3.5 minutes of data could be lost if a disaster strikes at the worst moment.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Storage Efficiency of Replication vs. Erasure Coding

**Problem:** Compare storage efficiency for storing **10 TB** of usable data with:
- (a) Replication factor 3
- (b) Erasure Coding (6,3) — meaning 6 data + 3 parity

### (a) Replication Factor 3:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Raw Storage} &= \text{Usable} \times N \\
&= 10 \text{ TB} \times 3 \\
&= 30 \text{ TB}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$ \text{Efficiency}_{\text{rep}} = \dfrac{\text{Usable}}{\text{Raw}} = \dfrac{10}{30} = 33.33\% $$

### (b) Erasure Coding (6,3):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Raw Storage} &= 10 \text{ TB} \times \dfrac{(6+3)}{6} \\
&= 10 \text{ TB} \times 1.5 \\
&= 15 \text{ TB}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$ \text{Efficiency}_{\text{EC}} = \dfrac{6}{6+3} = \dfrac{6}{9} = 66.67\% $$

### Comparison:

$$ \boxed{\text{Erasure Coding is } 2\times \text{ more storage efficient than RF=3 replication} } $$

> *Engineering Insight:* Replication is **simple** but **expensive** in storage. Erasure Coding is **complex** but **cheap**. Modern systems (Ceph, HDFS, Azure) use EC for cold data and replication for hot data.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Sync vs Async Replication

| Parameter | Synchronous | Asynchronous | Semi-Sync |
|-----------|-------------|--------------|-----------|
| **RPO** | 0 seconds | Seconds to minutes | Near-zero (best replica) |
| **RTO** | Near-zero (instant failover) | Minutes to hours | Seconds |
| **Bandwidth** | Very High (real-time) | Low (batched) | Medium-High |
| **Latency Impact** | High (adds RTT) | None on host | Moderate |
| **Distance Limit** | < 100 km typically | Unlimited | < 500 km |
| **Use Case** | Banking, ERP | DR, backup-tier | Critical multi-site |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram: Replication Architecture Overview

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Host[Host Application Server] -->|Write IO| PrimCtrl[Primary Storage Controller]
    PrimCtrl -->|Local Write| PrimDisk[(Primary Disk Array)]
    PrimCtrl -->|Sync Async Delta| RepEngine{Replication Engine}
    RepEngine -->|Sync Path| SecSync[Secondary Site Sync Link]
    RepEngine -->|Async Path| SecAsync[Secondary Site Async Link]
    SecSync --> SecCtrl[Secondary Storage Controller]
    SecAsync --> SecCtrl
    SecCtrl --> SecDisk[(Replica Disk Array)]
    SecCtrl -->|Read for DR| DRTest[DR Test Failover Node]
    DRTest -.->|Failover Trigger| PrimCtrl
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading:** Solid arrows show the synchronous replication path; dashed arrows show the disaster-recovery (failover) flow.

## 4.2 Diagram: Synchronous vs Asynchronous Replication Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant H as Host
    participant P as Primary Storage
    participant N as Network
    participant S as Secondary Storage
    Note over H,S: SYNCHRONOUS REPLICATION
    H->>P: Write Block X
    P->>P: Local Commit
    P->>N: Forward Block X
    N->>S: Transmit Block X
    S->>S: Remote Commit
    S->>N: ACK
    N->>P: ACK
    P->>H: Write Acknowledged
    Note over H,S: ASYNCHRONOUS REPLICATION
    H->>P: Write Block Y
    P->>P: Local Commit
    P->>H: Write Acknowledged
    P-->>S: Forward Block Y Later
    S-->>S: Remote Commit Delayed
```

## 4.3 Diagram: Replication Topologies Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Cascading
        P1[Primary] --> S1[Secondary]
        S1 --> T1[Tertiary]
    end
    subgraph FanOut
        P2[Primary] --> M1[Replica 1]
        P2 --> M2[Replica 2]
        P2 --> M3[Replica 3]
    end
    subgraph Bidirectional
        A1[Site A] <--> A2[Site B]
    end
```

## 4.4 Diagram: Block-Level Functional Architecture (Hybrid Cloud Replication)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph OnPrem [On Premises Data Center]
        App[Application Server]
        PrimArr[Primary Storage Array]
        App --> PrimArr
    end
    subgraph DR [DR Site 50 KM Away]
        SecArr[Secondary Storage Array]
        RepLog[Replication Log Volume]
        SecArr --- RepLog
    end
    subgraph Cloud [Public Cloud Region]
        Blob1[Cloud Object Store Replica]
        Blob2[Cloud Archive Bucket]
        Blob1 --- Blob2
    end
    PrimArr -->|Sync FC IP| SecArr
    SecArr -->|Async Tiering| Blob1
    Blob1 -->|Lifecycle Policy| Blob2
    style PrimArr fill:#f9c,stroke:#333
    style SecArr fill:#cfc,stroke:#333
    style Blob1 fill:#cff,stroke:#333
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Diagram:** This represents a **three-tier hybrid replication** model. Tier 1 (sync) for zero-RPO hot DR. Tier 2 (async to cloud) for geographic redundancy. Tier 3 (cloud archive) for long-term retention — a typical **3-2-1 backup rule** implementation.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Differentiate between synchronous and asynchronous replication. State one key advantage of each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Synchronous Replication | Asynchronous Replication |
|--------|------------------------|--------------------------|
| Acknowledgment | Host waits for replica confirmation | Host acknowledged after local write only |
| RPO | 0 (zero data loss) | Non-zero (lag-dependent) |
| Distance | Limited (< 100 km) | Unlimited (works globally) |
| Performance | Higher latency due to RTT | No added latency |
| Cost | High bandwidth, expensive links | Low bandwidth, tolerant |

- **Advantage of sync:** Zero data loss — critical for transactional databases.
- **Advantage of async:** Cost-effective for long-distance DR; no performance impact.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define RPO and RTO. Which replication mode achieves RPO = 0?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- **RPO (Recovery Point Objective):** The maximum acceptable amount of data loss measured in time. It tells *"how much"* data we can afford to lose.
- **RTO (Recovery Time Objective):** The maximum acceptable downtime after a disaster before service is restored. It tells *"how long"* we can be offline.
- **RPO = 0 is achieved by Synchronous Replication** because every write is committed to the replica before host acknowledgment.

---

## PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks)

### **Question A — Option 1 `[KTU University Exam – Jul 2024]`**

**A(a).** Explain in detail the different **replication topologies** used in storage systems with neat diagrams. Compare their **bandwidth requirements and fault tolerance**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:** A replication topology defines how primary and secondary storage systems are interconnected for data flow.

**Types:**

1. **Cascading (Daisy-Chain) Replication:**
   - Primary → Secondary → Tertiary
   - Saves bandwidth on primary link
   - Failure of Secondary breaks Tertiary updates

2. **Fan-Out (Multi-Target):**
   - One primary replicates to N targets simultaneously
   - High bandwidth demand on primary
   - Multiple independent DR sites

3. **Bi-directional (Mutual / Active-Active):**
   - Both sites replicate to each other
   - Used for load balancing and zero-downtime failover
   - Requires **distributed lock manager** to avoid write conflicts

4. **Star Topology:**
   - Central hub replicates to multiple spokes
   - Hub is single point of failure (unless hub is itself replicated)

**Comparison Table:**

| Topology | Bandwidth on Primary | Fault Tolerance | Complexity |
|----------|---------------------|-----------------|------------|
| Cascading | Low | Medium (depends on secondary) | Low |
| Fan-Out | Very High | Very High | Medium |
| Bi-directional | Medium-High | Highest | High (conflict resolution) |
| Star | High | Hub-dependent | Medium |

**Conclusion:** Topology choice depends on RPO/RTO requirements, network cost, and application architecture. For KTU exam: write **at least three** topologies with diagrams to score full marks.

---

**A(b).** A primary storage system has **6000 IOPS** at **64 KB block size**. Compute the minimum network bandwidth required for **synchronous replication** to a remote site **80 km away**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**

$$ \text{IOPS} = 6000 \quad , \quad \text{BlockSize} = 64 \text{ KB} $$

**Step 1 — Compute throughput in Bytes/sec:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Bandwidth}_{\text{bytes}} &= \text{IOPS} \times \text{BlockSize} \\
&= 6000 \times 64 \times 1024 \\
&= 6000 \times 65536 \\
&= 393{,}216{,}000 \text{ Bps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert to MB/s (using MiB):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Bandwidth}_{\text{MiB/s}} &= \dfrac{393{,}216{,}000}{2^{20}} \\
&= \dfrac{393{,}216{,}000}{1{,}048{,}576} \\
&= 375 \text{ MiB/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to Mbps:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Bandwidth}_{\text{Mbps}} &= \dfrac{393{,}216{,}000 \times 8}{10^6} \\
&= 3145.728 \text{ Mbps} \\
&\approx 3.15 \text{ Gbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Practical recommendation:**

For synchronous replication, provision **40% headroom** for peak bursts and protocol overhead:

$$ \text{Provisioned Link} = 3.15 \times 1.4 \approx 4.4 \text{ Gbps} \Rightarrow \textbf{Use 5 Gbps dedicated link or 2 x 10 Gbps LACP} $$

**Final Answer:** $\boxed{\approx 3.15 \text{ Gbps minimum, provision 5 Gbps} }$

---

### **Question B — Option 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**

**B(a).** With a neat diagram, explain the **working of synchronous replication**. Discuss its **advantages and limitations**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Working (refer to SECTION 4.2 mermaid sequence diagram):**

1. Host issues a write I/O to the primary storage.
2. Primary receives and caches the write locally.
3. Primary forwards the write (or its delta) to the secondary over the dedicated link.
4. Secondary writes the data to its replica volume.
5. Secondary sends an ACK back to primary.
6. **Only after receiving the ACK**, primary acknowledges the host.

This guarantees that at the moment the host thinks the write is "done," the data exists on both sites — hence **RPO = 0**.

**Advantages:**

- **Zero data loss** (RPO = 0)
- Instantaneous failover — primary and secondary are always identical
- Ideal for mission-critical transactional systems (Banking, ERP)
- Simplifies recovery logic — no replay of logs needed

**Limitations:**

- **Latency penalty** — every write pays the cost of network RTT (~2 ms for 100 km)
- **Distance limitation** — beyond ~100 km, RTT becomes unacceptable
- **Bandwidth hungry** — must replicate every write in real-time
- **Cascading failures** — if secondary goes slow/down, primary writes stall
- **Expensive** — requires dedicated, low-latency links (DWDM, FC)

---

**B(b).** A company has **20 TB** of usable data and wants **99.999% availability** (the "five nines"). Compare the **storage overhead** of replication factor 3 vs. erasure coding (10,4). Which is more **storage-efficient**? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**

$$ U = 20 \text{ TB} \quad ; \quad \text{RF} = 3 \quad ; \quad \text{EC}(10,4) $$

**Case 1 — Replication Factor 3:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Raw Storage} &= 20 \times 3 = 60 \text{ TB} \\
\text{Overhead} &= (3 - 1) \times 20 = 40 \text{ TB extra} \\
\text{Efficiency} &= \dfrac{20}{60} = 33.33\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Case 2 — Erasure Coding (10, 4):**

Here, **k = 10** data blocks and **m = 4** parity blocks. Total blocks per stripe = 14.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Expansion Factor} &= \dfrac{k + m}{k} = \dfrac{14}{10} = 1.4 \\
\text{Raw Storage} &= 20 \times 1.4 = 28 \text{ TB} \\
\text{Overhead} &= 28 - 20 = 8 \text{ TB extra} \\
\text{Efficiency} &= \dfrac{10}{14} = 71.43\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comparison Table:**

| Metric | RF = 3 | EC (10, 4) |
|--------|--------|------------|
| Raw Storage | 60 TB | 28 TB |
| Wasted Capacity | 40 TB | 8 TB |
| Storage Efficiency | 33.33% | 71.43% |
| Fault Tolerance | Loses any 2 of 3 | Loses any 4 of 14 |
| Rebuild Speed | Fast (copy from peer) | Slow (compute from k survivors) |
| Compute Cost | Low | High |

**Conclusion:**

$$ \boxed{\text{Erasure Coding (10,4) saves } \dfrac{(60 - 28)}{60} = 53.33\% \text{ of raw storage} } $$

EC is far more **storage-efficient** and is preferred for cold archival data. Replication is preferred for hot data where fast rebuild and low compute overhead matter.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit Mismatch Trap:** Always state whether you used **MB (10⁶)** or **MiB (2²⁰)**. KTU expects consistency throughout the answer.
> 2. **Confusing RF with #copies:** RF = 3 means **total 3 copies** (1 primary + 2 replicas), **not** 3 extra copies.
> 3. **Forgetting RTT term:** Sync replication latency = Local + 2×RTT + Remote. Marks deducted if RTT omitted.
> 4. **Diagram Skipping:** KTU examiners deduct up to **2 marks** if no diagram accompanies a topology/explanation question.
