> 5. **Writing only formulas without interpretation:** Always end numerical problems with a one-line **engineering interpretation**.

---

## 📌 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- ✅ **Replication** = maintaining multiple data copies across storage systems for availability and DR.
- ✅ **Synchronous replication** gives **RPO = 0** but is limited by **network latency and distance (< 100 km)**.
- ✅ **Asynchronous replication** is **distance-independent** but introduces **non-zero RPO**.
- ✅ **RPO** = max data loss; **RTO** = max downtime; both are **business-driven**, not technical.
- ✅ **Bandwidth formula:** $BW = \text{IOPS} \times \text{Block Size}$ — always convert to **bits/sec** for network.
- ✅ **Replication Factor 3** gives **33.33% storage efficiency**; **Erasure Coding (10,4)** gives **71.43%**.
- ✅ **Cascading replication** saves bandwidth on the primary link but creates dependency chain.
- ✅ **Bi-directional (active-active)** needs **distributed locking** to prevent split-brain.
- ✅ For KTU exam: always draw the diagram, define terms first, then derive.
- ✅ Most-asked topics: Sync vs Async, RPO/RTO calculation, Bandwidth sizing, Topology comparison.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Local Snapshot
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST867 - STORAGE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Local Snapshot

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

A **Local Snapshot** is a logical, point-in-time, read-only image of a primary logical unit (LUN/Volume) that is created and maintained on the **same physical storage array** as the source data. It uses metadata pointer redirection rather than physical block duplication at the moment of creation, thereby consuming negligible primary capacity and completing in near-zero elapsed time.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Anchor:** A snapshot is **not a backup**; it is a *temporal reference* to data state. The source volume and the snapshot share the same physical storage pool, differing only in the pointer tables that map logical block addresses (LBAs).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Time-Travel Bookmark" Model

Imagine you are writing a 500-page engineering thesis on a single shared Google Document. Before performing a major restructure, you press **"Version History → Name Current Version"**. You do **not** duplicate the document. The system simply records a *bookmark* pointing to the current state of every word. If you later accidentally delete Chapter 4, you can roll back to the bookmarked state and recover exactly what was there.

| Analogy Element | Real-World Mapping in Storage |
|:---|:---|
| The Google Document | The source LUN / Volume |
| The "Named Version" | The Local Snapshot |
| The bookmark pointer table | The Snapshot Pointer/Metadata Table |
| The act of editing after the bookmark | Copy-on-Write (CoW) or Redirect-on-Write (RoW) |
| The deleted chapter recovery | Restore from Snapshot |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Local snapshots reside *inside* the same RAID group / storage pool as the source. This makes recovery extremely fast (no network hop, no remote replication lag) but does **not** protect against array-level disasters (fire, controller failure, site loss) — for that, *Remote Snapshots* are required.

### 1.3 Core Functional Properties of a Local Snapshot

The following five properties are universally tested in KTU examinations:

1. **Point-in-Time Consistency** — Captures data state at a specific instant (often enforced by application flush or array-level freeze).
2. **Space-Efficient** — Initial creation consumes only metadata; physical space is allocated only when source blocks change.
3. **Read-Only / Redirectable** — The snapshot itself cannot be written to (it represents frozen past state); however, the *source* continues to mutate freely.
4. **Same-Array Residency** — Source and snapshot live in the same disk pool, ensuring sub-second restore times.
5. **Logical / Virtual Nature** — It is a metadata construct, not a physical clone, until blocks diverge.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic: The Three Pillars of Snapshot Implementation

A storage array can implement a local snapshot using one of three architectural models. KTU examiners frequently ask comparative questions on these.

#### Pillar 1: Copy-on-Write (CoW)
- **Trigger condition:** A write I/O arrives for a block that is currently shared between source and snapshot.
- **Action sequence:**
  1. The array reads the *original* block from its current location.
  2. The original block is copied into a **reserved snapshot area** (pre-allocated or thin-provisioned pool).
  3. The pointer table for the source is updated to point to a new location.
  4. The user's new write is committed to the new location.
- **Net effect:** The snapshot always sees the *old* value; the source always sees the *new* value.

#### Pillar 2: Redirect-on-Write (RoW) — also called "Write-Anywhere"
- **Trigger condition:** A write I/O arrives for any block (even unmodified ones) after the snapshot is taken.
- **Action sequence:**
  1. The new data is written to a **new, free location** in the pool.
  2. The source's pointer table is updated to point to this new block.
  3. The snapshot's pointer table **remains unchanged**, continuing to reference the old block.
- **Net effect:** All new writes go to fresh locations; the snapshot's pointers never need rewriting.

#### Pillar 3: Full Clone / Split-Mirror
- A **complete physical copy** of the source volume is created.
- All blocks are duplicated bit-for-bit at creation time.
- High initial cost in time and space; zero ongoing overhead.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following table consolidates every quantitative relationship you must memorize for the ESE.

| **Parameter** | **Formula / Expression** | **Units** | **Engineering Meaning** |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| Snapshot creation time (CoW/RoW) | $T_{create} \approx T_{metadata}$ (typically **< 1 second**) | seconds | Time to write the snapshot pointer table |
| CoW physical space overhead | $S_{cow} = N_{changed} \times B_{size}$ | MB / GB | Where $N_{changed}$ = number of blocks modified since snapshot |
| RoW physical space overhead | $S_{row} = N_{written} \times B_{size}$ | MB / GB | All post-snapshot writes accumulate here |
| Total pool consumption | $S_{total} = S_{source} + S_{snapshot\_reserve}$ | GB | Source size + snapshot reserved capacity |
| Restore time (full volume) | $T_{restore} = \dfrac{D_{size}}{R_{disk}}$ | seconds | $D_{size}$ = data size, $R_{disk}$ = disk read throughput |
| Storage efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{D_{logical}}{D_{physical}} \times 100\%$ | percent | Ratio of usable user data to total physical capacity |
| Read I/O on unchanged block | $T_{read} = 1 \times T_{disk\_seek}$ | ms | Single seek — no extra penalty |
| Read I/O on changed block (CoW) | $T_{read} = 1 \times T_{disk\_seek}$ (from snapshot area) | ms | No penalty, just different LBA |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Examiner Note:** The vertical bar symbol $\vert$ is used above (e.g., in $N_{changed} \vert B_{size}$) **only in inline math mode**. Inside markdown tables, always use `\vert` or `\mid` to prevent the table parser from misinterpreting it as a column separator.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Database Rollback:** Oracle and SQL Server leverage snapshot integration for near-instantaneous point-in-time recovery (PITR).
- **Dev/Test Cloning:** Mounting a snapshot as a writable LUN (using *snap-clone* / *linked clone*) provides developers a sandbox identical to production in seconds.
- **Backup Window Reduction:** Backup software reads the frozen snapshot while production continues writing, eliminating the nightly backup window.
- **Analytics Offloading:** BI tools run heavy read queries against the snapshot, isolating analytical load from OLTP performance.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Maximum Physical Overhead of a CoW Snapshot

**Problem Statement:** Given a source LUN of size $S$ composed of $N$ blocks each of size $B$, derive the **maximum** physical storage consumed by a single Copy-on-Write snapshot at the extreme case where every block of the source has been overwritten.

**Step 1 — Establish baseline block count.**
Every storage volume is divided into fixed-size logical blocks. The total number of blocks is the volume size divided by the block size.

$$
N = \frac{S}{B}
$$

**Step 2 — Define the "changed block" set.**
Let $\mathcal{C}$ denote the set of block indices that have been modified since the snapshot was created. In the *worst case* (entire volume rewritten), the cardinality of this set equals the total number of blocks.

$$
N_{changed} = \vert \mathcal{C} \vert_{max} = N
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the CoW overhead equation.**
For every changed block, the original content must be preserved in the snapshot reserve area. Therefore the snapshot reserve space is the cardinality of the changed set multiplied by the block size.

$$
S_{cow} = N_{changed} \times B
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the worst-case cardinality.**
Replacing $N_{changed}$ with its maximum value $N$ from Step 2:

$$
S_{cow,\max} = N \times B
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $N$ from Step 1.**
Replacing $N$ with $\dfrac{S}{B}$:

$$
S_{cow,\max} = \frac{S}{B} \times B
$$

**Step 6 — Algebraic simplification.**
The block size terms cancel out completely:

$$
S_{cow,\max} = S
$$

**Step 7 — Compute total pool consumption at worst case.**
The total physical capacity consumed is the original source plus the snapshot reserve:

$$
S_{total,\max} = S + S_{cow,\max} = S + S = 2S
$$

**Conclusion:** A CoW snapshot, in its absolute worst case (full volume rewrite), consumes **100% of source size**, doubling the physical footprint. In typical enterprise workloads, the change rate is 5–20% per day, so real overhead is a fraction of source size.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Restore Time Estimation

**Problem Statement:** A 1 TB (terabyte) volume snapshot must be fully restored onto the source LUN. Given a SATA disk read throughput of 150 MB/s, derive the restore time.

**Step 1 — Convert terabytes to megabytes.**
1 TB = 1024 GB = 1,048,576 MB (binary convention used by most storage vendors).

$$
D_{size} = 1 \text{ TB} = 1{,}048{,}576 \text{ MB}
$$

**Step 2 — Identify disk throughput.**

$$
R_{disk} = 150 \text{ MB/s}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply restore time formula.**

$$
T_{restore} = \frac{D_{size}}{R_{disk}}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute numerical values.**

$$
T_{restore} = \frac{1{,}048{,}576 \text{ MB}}{150 \text{ MB/s}}
$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate division.**

$$
T_{restore} \approx 6{,}990.5 \text{ seconds}
$$

**Step 6 — Convert to hours for engineering intuition.**

$$
T_{restore} \approx \frac{6{,}990.5}{3600} \approx 1.94 \text{ hours}
$$

**Conclusion:** Full restore of a 1 TB volume over a single 150 MB/s SATA link takes nearly **2 hours**. This is why production systems use multiple parallel disks (RAID) or SSDs (500+ MB/s) to reduce restore windows.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: CoW vs RoW Trade-off Table

| **Criterion** | **Copy-on-Write (CoW)** | **Redirect-on-Write (RoW)** |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Write I/O latency penalty | **Yes** — each first-write triggers a read + copy + write (3× I/O) | **No** — writes go directly to new location |
| Read I/O latency on snapshot | **Slight penalty** — block may reside in snapshot reserve area | **Slight penalty** — pointer dereferencing overhead |
| Snapshot space efficiency | **Better** — only modified blocks consume space | **Worse** — all post-snapshot writes accumulate |
| Best suited for | Read-heavy workloads, infrequent snapshots | Write-heavy workloads, frequent snapshots |
| Used by (examples) | VMware VMFS, ZFS, NetApp WAFL (older) | NetApp WAFL (newer), Windows Server 2012+ |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram 1: Local Snapshot Creation & Copy-on-Write Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User issues snapshot command] --> B[Array freezes source pointer table]
    B --> C[Create new pointer table for snapshot referencing same blocks]
    C --> D[Snapshot creation complete near zero seconds]
    D --> E[Application issues write to Block X]
    E --> F{Is Block X shared with snapshot?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Read original Block X from disk]
    G --> H[Copy original to snapshot reserve pool]
    H --> I[Write new data to new location]
    I --> J[Update source pointer to new location]
    F -- No --> K[Write directly to free location]
    K --> J
    J --> L[Operation complete]
```

### 4.2 Diagram 2: Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Snapshot

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph srcPool[Source Volume Pool]
        A1[Source Pointer Table] --> A2[Original Block 1]
        A1 --> A3[Original Block 2]
        A1 --> A4[Original Block 3]
    end
    subgraph snapPool[Snapshot Reserve Pool]
        B1[Snapshot Pointer Table] --> A2
        B1 --> A5[Preserved Old Block 2]
        B1 --> A4
    end
    subgraph newWrites[New Write Region]
        A1 --> A6[New Block 2 location]
    end
    A5 -.preserves old state.-> B1
    A6 -.holds new state.-> A1
```

### 4.3 Diagram 3: Restore from Snapshot Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1[Disaster detected: data corruption] --> R2{Recovery scope?}
    R2 -- Single file --> R3[Mount snapshot as read-only volume]
    R3 --> R4[Copy required file back to source]
    R2 -- Full volume --> R5[Promote snapshot to new source LUN]
    R5 --> R6[Swap logical unit identifiers at host level]
    R2 -- Point-in-time PIT --> R7[Restore entire volume from snapshot]
    R7 --> R8[Source now references old pointer table]
    R4 --> R9[Recovery complete]
    R6 --> R9
    R8 --> R9
```

### 4.4 Diagram 4: Local Snapshot Lifecycle States

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Created: snapshot command
    Created --> Active: blocks diverge on writes
    Active --> Active: more writes accumulate
    Active --> Reverting: revert source to snapshot
    Active --> Cloning: promote to writable clone
    Reverting --> Restored: pointer swap complete
    Cloning --> Independent: full physical copy
    Restored --> [*]
    Independent --> [*]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define a local snapshot in a storage system. List any two advantages of using a local snapshot over a full clone.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A local snapshot is a point-in-time, logical copy of a source volume that resides on the **same storage array**, implemented using pointer redirection rather than physical block duplication.

**Advantages over a full clone:**
1. **Near-instantaneous creation** — Only metadata pointers are written; physical space is allocated lazily as source blocks change.
2. **Space efficiency** — A full clone requires 100% of source size immediately, whereas a snapshot typically consumes only 5–20% overhead proportional to change rate.

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark each for the two advantages.)*

---

#### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Distinguish between Copy-on-Write (CoW) and Redirect-on-Write (RoW) snapshot implementations.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Basis | Copy-on-Write | Redirect-on-Write |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Operation | Original block copied to reserve before overwrite | New data written to new location; old block left in place |
| Write penalty | First write is 3× I/O (read + copy + write) | Single write I/O |
| Space consumption | Only modified blocks tracked | All post-snapshot writes consume new space |

*(Award 1 mark per correct distinguishing row, 3 rows = 3 marks.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the operational mechanism of a Copy-on-Write (CoW) local snapshot with a suitable block diagram. Describe what happens to the source and snapshot when:
   (i) A read I/O is issued to an unchanged block.
   (ii) A write I/O is issued to a block that is shared between source and snapshot.   **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A production LUN of size **500 GB** is protected by a local CoW snapshot. After 24 hours, the change rate observed is **8%** of the total blocks. Calculate:
   (i) The physical space consumed by the snapshot reserve area, assuming a block size of **4 KB**.
   (ii) The total storage pool consumption.
   (iii) The restore time if the disk subsystem delivers **200 MB/s** sequential read throughput.   **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Operational Mechanism (7 marks):**
- The array maintains **two pointer tables**: one for the source LUN, one for the snapshot. (1 mark)
- At snapshot creation, the snapshot pointer table is created as an exact duplicate of the source pointer table. (1 mark)
- Both tables initially reference the same physical blocks. (1 mark)
- **(i) Read on unchanged block:** No special action; the I/O is served directly from the existing physical block. Both source and snapshot continue to see the same data. (1 mark)
- **(ii) Write on shared block:** The array performs the **Copy-on-Write protocol**:
   - Step 1: The original block is read from its current location. (1 mark)
   - Step 2: The original is copied to the snapshot reserve pool. (1 mark)
   - Step 3: The new data is written to a fresh block. (1 mark)
   - Step 4: The source pointer table is updated; the snapshot pointer table remains pointing to the preserved old block. (1 mark)

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Total number of blocks in the 500 GB LUN.**

$$
N = \frac{S}{B} = \frac{500 \text{ GB}}{4 \text{ KB}}
$$

Convert 500 GB to KB: $500 \times 1024 \times 1024 = 524{,}288{,}000$ KB.

$$
N = \frac{524{,}288{,}000}{4} = 131{,}072{,}000 \text{ blocks}
$$

**Step 2 — Number of changed blocks (8% change rate).**

$$
N_{changed} = 0.08 \times 131{,}072{,}000 = 10{,}485{,}760 \text{ blocks}
$$

**Step 3 — Snapshot reserve space.**

$$
S_{cow} = N_{changed} \times B = 10{,}485{,}760 \times 4 \text{ KB} = 41{,}943{,}040 \text{ KB}
$$

Convert to GB: $41{,}943{,}040 \div 1{,}048{,}576 = 40$ GB.

**Answer (i): Snapshot reserve = 40 GB.** (2 marks)

**Step 4 — Total storage pool consumption.**

$$
S_{total} = 500 \text{ GB} + 40 \text{ GB} = 540 \text{ GB}
$$

**Answer (ii): Total pool consumption = 540 GB.** (2 marks)

**Step 5 — Restore time calculation.**

$$
T_{restore} = \frac{D_{size}}{R_{disk}} = \frac{500 \text{ GB}}{200 \text{ MB/s}}
$$

Convert 500 GB to MB: $500 \times 1024 = 512{,}000$ MB.

$$
T_{restore} = \frac{512{,}000 \text{ MB}}{200 \text{ MB/s}} = 2{,}560 \text{ seconds}
$$

Convert to minutes: $2{,}560 \div 60 \approx 42.67$ minutes.

**Answer (iii): Restore time ≈ 42 minutes 40 seconds.** (3 marks for the 3-step calculation, 1 mark for final unit conversion)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – May 2023 Model]**

**(a)** Compare and contrast **Local Snapshots** with **Local Clones (Full Mirrors)**. Discuss the architectural differences, space utilization, creation time, and typical use cases for each.   **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A financial services firm maintains a 2 TB mission-critical database. The storage administrator wants to take a local snapshot every hour for 24 hours, then retain only the most recent snapshot for an additional 7 days. The estimated hourly change rate is **2%** of the total volume. Calculate:
   (i) The total snapshot reserve space required at the end of 24 hours if **all 24 hourly snapshots are retained**.
   (ii) The final space consumption after 7 days if only the last snapshot is retained.
   (iii) Comment on whether this strategy is sustainable on a storage pool of **5 TB** usable capacity.   **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Local Snapshot vs Local Clone (7 marks):**

| Parameter | Local Snapshot | Local Clone |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Architecture** | Pointer redirection; metadata-based | Full physical bit-for-bit copy |
| **Creation time** | Near-instantaneous (sub-second) | Proportional to volume size (minutes to hours) |
| **Space utilization** | Lazy allocation; 5–20% overhead typical | 100% of source size immediately |
| **Mutability** | Read-only (frozen past state) | Writable; independent of source |
| **Recovery use case** | Quick rollback, point-in-time recovery | Long-term archival, independent workload |
| **Resource intensity** | Low I/O at creation, ongoing write penalty (CoW) | High I/O at creation, no ongoing penalty |

*(Award 1 mark per parameter row, 1 mark for overall architectural summary = 7 marks.)*

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Total number of blocks in 2 TB volume (block size 4 KB assumed standard).**

$$
N = \frac{2 \text{ TB}}{4 \text{ KB}} = \frac{2{,}097{,}152 \text{ MB}}{4 \text{ KB}} = 536{,}870{,}912 \text{ blocks}
$$

**Step 2 — Number of changed blocks per hour (2% change rate).**

$$
N_{changed/hr} = 0.02 \times 536{,}870{,}912 = 10{,}737{,}418.24 \text{ blocks}
$$

**Step 3 — Snapshot reserve per snapshot (per hour).**

$$
S_{cow/hr} = 10{,}737{,}418 \times 4 \text{ KB} \approx 40 \text{ GB}
$$

**Step 4 — Total reserve after 24 hours (all retained, assuming independent change sets).**

$$
S_{cow,24hr} = 24 \times 40 \text{ GB} = 960 \text{ GB}
$$

**Answer (i): 960 GB total snapshot reserve.** (2 marks)

**Step 5 — Space after 7 days with only last snapshot retained.**

$$
S_{final} = 2 \text{ TB (source)} + 40 \text{ GB (last snapshot)} = 2{,}048 \text{ GB} + 40 \text{ GB} = 2{,}088 \text{ GB}
$$

Convert to TB: $2{,}088 \div 1{,}024 \approx 2.04$ TB.

**Answer (ii): ≈ 2.04 TB.** (2 marks)

**Step 6 — Sustainability check on 5 TB pool.**

If 24 hourly snapshots are retained, total consumption = $2 \text{ TB} + 0.96 \text{ TB} = 2.96$ TB, which is **within** the 5 TB pool. (2 marks)

If retention grows to many days with all snapshots kept, the linear growth $24 \times 40$ GB = 960 GB/day will exhaust 5 TB within approximately 3 days.

**Answer (iii): Sustainable for 24-hour retention, but NOT sustainable for multi-week retention without a snapshot deletion policy or thin-provisioned reserve pool.**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit conversion error (–2 marks):** Students often write "500 GB = 500,000 MB" using decimal convention. Storage vendors use **binary (1 GB = 1,024 MB)**. Always use $1024$ in KTU numericals unless explicitly told otherwise.
> 2. **Confusing snapshot with backup (–1 mark):** A snapshot is **not** a substitute for backup. It resides on the same array and cannot survive array-level failure.
> 3. **Forgetting the source size in total pool calculation (–1 mark):** Total pool = source + snapshot reserve, not just snapshot reserve.
> 4. **Writing the clone as "more reliable" (–1 mark):** Clones and snapshots serve different purposes; reliability is not a direct comparison axis.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Local Snapshot = Same-array, point-in-time, metadata-based copy.**
- Creation time is **near-zero**; space is allocated **lazily** on first write.
- **CoW**: Read original → Copy to reserve → Write new → Update source pointer.
- **RoW**: Write new data to fresh location → Update source pointer only.
- **Full Clone**: Bit-for-bit physical copy; high initial cost, zero ongoing overhead.
- Worst-case CoW space = 100% of source size (full rewrite scenario).
- Typical enterprise overhead = **5–20%** of source per snapshot.
- Restore time $T_{restore} = D_{size} / R_{disk}$; always convert GB↔MB using **1024**.
- Local snapshots do **NOT** protect against array failure → need replication for DR.
- Use cases: rollback, Dev/Test sandbox, backup offload, PITR, analytics isolation.
- VMware VMFS, ZFS, NetApp WAFL, Windows Server all support local snapshots natively.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Redundant Arrays of Independent Disks (RAID) - RAID 0
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST867 - STORAGE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Redundant Arrays of Independent Disks (RAID) - RAID 0

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**RAID 0 (Striping without Parity)** is the most fundamental RAID level defined under the Berkeley RAID taxonomy (Patterson, Gibson & Katz, 1988). It implements **data striping** — the technique of dividing a logical block of data into smaller segments and distributing these segments across multiple physical disks in a round-robin fashion — **without** any form of redundancy, mirroring, or parity protection.

Formally, if a logical file $F$ of size $S$ bytes is striped across $n$ disks using a stripe unit (chunk size) of $C$ bytes, the data is partitioned into $k = \lceil S/C \rceil$ stripe units, and stripe unit $i$ is written to disk $(i \bmod n)$. The mapping function can be expressed as:

$$Disk_{target}(i) = i \bmod n$$

$$Offset_{within\_disk}(i) = \lfloor i / n \rfloor \cdot C$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** RAID 0 is categorized as a **non-redundant RAID level** (RAID Level 0). It is the *baseline* against which all other RAID levels (1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 10, 50, 60) are benchmarked in terms of performance and capacity efficiency.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Multi-Lane Highway

Imagine a single-lane rural road where one truck at a time must deliver a 100-tonne cargo load. Delivery is painfully slow because the truck must carry *everything* sequentially.

Now, picture the same cargo split into 4 equal parts of 25 tonnes each, and four trucks depart *simultaneously* on a **4-lane highway**, each truck assigned to a fixed lane. The total cargo reaches the destination **~4× faster**, because the workload is **parallelized** across independent transport channels.

This is precisely how **RAID 0** works:
- The **highway lanes** = physical disks (4 disks in our analogy)
- The **cargo chunks** = stripe units (block-sized fragments of the file)
- The **trucks** = concurrent I/O operations
- The **highway** = the disk controller / channel

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Intuition:** RAID 0 offers **NO data protection**. In our highway analogy, if *any one lane collapses* (one disk fails), the cargo on that lane is lost *forever*, and the remaining trucks cannot reconstruct the missing 25 tonnes. This is why RAID 0 is suited only for **non-critical, performance-hungry workloads** (e.g., scratch disks, render farms, gaming, scientific compute caches).

### 1.3 Key Terminology & Standard Metrics

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Stripe Unit (Chunk Size)** | The smallest block of data written to a single disk. Typical values: 16 KB, 32 KB, 64 KB, 128 KB, 256 KB. |
| **Stripe Width** | The total number of disks across which a stripe is spread. For RAID 0, stripe width equals the array size $n$. |
| **Stripe** | The collection of $n$ stripe units (one from each disk) that share the same logical offset. |
| **Array Capacity** | The sum of usable capacities of all member disks. |
| **Fault Tolerance** | The ability to survive disk failure(s) without data loss. RAID 0 has **zero** fault tolerance. |
| **MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures)** | The expected operational time before a failure. RAID 0 has a *reduced* MTBF compared to a single disk. |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** The KTU paper frequently tests the *distinction* between **stripe unit (chunk)** and **stripe**. A stripe spans all $n$ disks; a stripe unit is the data on *one* disk within a stripe. Confusing these is a guaranteed mark-loser.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Mechanics — How RAID 0 Works Step-by-Step

1. **Logical Request Reception:** The host OS issues a read/write request for a logical block address (LBA) range.
2. **Address Translation:** The RAID controller's mapping logic computes:
   - Which stripe units (chunks) are involved.
   - Which target disk each chunk belongs to using the modulo rule.
   - The physical offset on that disk.
3. **Parallel Dispatch:** Independent I/O commands are issued *concurrently* to all member disks.
4. **Completion & Assembly:** The controller collects the responses and re-assembles the original logical block for delivery to the host.

### 2.2 The 'Why' Behind Striping — Performance Engineering Rationale

- **Mechanical Latency Reduction:** A single HDD has a seek time + rotational latency of typically **5–15 ms**. By spreading a sequential I/O across $n$ disks, the *effective* rotational latency per byte is divided by $n$ because the heads on different disks are mechanically independent.
- **Aggregate Throughput Scaling:** If a single disk delivers $T$ MB/s, the array delivers up to $n \times T$ MB/s for *large sequential* workloads.
- **Pipeline Effect:** While one disk is performing a seek, another can be transferring data. This **overlapped operation** is what makes striping powerful.

### 2.3 Trade-Off Triangle (The CAP Triangle of RAID)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every RAID level navigates a trade-off between three competing properties. RAID 0 **sacrifices reliability entirely** in exchange for **maximum capacity** and **maximum performance**. This is its defining characteristic.

```
            CAPACITY
               /\
              /  \
             /    \
            / RAID \
           /   0   \
          /________\
   PERFORMANCE ---- RELIABILITY
   (MAX)            (ZERO)
```

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Property | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Minimum Disks** | $n_{min} = 2$ | disks | RAID 0 is undefined for $n=1$ |
| 2 | **Usable Capacity** | $C_{usable} = n \times D$ | GB / TB | $D$ = capacity of smallest disk (homogeneous arrays assumed) |
| 3 | **Capacity Efficiency** | $\eta = 100\%$ | percent | No overhead — all disk space is usable |
| 4 | **Sequential Read Throughput** | $R_{seq} = n \times R_{single}$ | MB/s | Ideal case, large-block sequential I/O |
| 5 | **Sequential Write Throughput** | $W_{seq} = n \times W_{single}$ | MB/s | Ideal case |
| 6 | **Random Read IOPS** | $IOPS_{rand} = n \times IOPS_{single}$ | ops/s | Best when requests are uniformly distributed across disks |
| 7 | **Random Write IOPS** | $IOPS_{rand\_w} = n \times IOPS_{single}$ | ops/s | Same as random read in RAID 0 (no parity compute) |
| 8 | **Fault Tolerance** | $F = 0$ disks | disks | Any single disk failure = total array failure |
| 9 | **Array MTBF** | $MTBF_{array} = MTBF_{single} / n$ | hours | Failure rate *adds* across disks |
| 10 | **Stripe Unit Count** | $k = \lceil S / C \rceil$ | units | $S$ = file size, $C$ = chunk size |
| 11 | **Stripe Count** | $N_{stripes} = \lceil k / n \rceil$ | stripes | Total stripes in the array |
| 12 | **Read Latency (best case)** | $L_{read} = T_{seek} + T_{rot} + (C / T_{transfer})$ | ms | Single chunk read from one disk |

> [!CAUTION]
> **Pipe-Symbol Warning (for Markdown):** All absolute values / divisions in the formulas above use the explicit `×` or `/` symbol — **never** the vertical pipe `|` — to preserve table integrity when rendered on the KTU e-learning portal.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

RAID 0 is deployed in scenarios where **speed >> safety**:

- **Scientific Computing / HPC clusters** — intermediate scratch space for simulation outputs.
- **Video Editing & Rendering Workstations** — Adobe Premiere, DaVinci Resolve, Blender render farms leverage striping for real-time 4K/8K playback.
- **Gaming Rigs** — dual-NVMe RAID 0 to minimize game load times.
- **Database TempDB / Swap Volumes** — transient, regenerable data structures.
- **Live Streaming Edge Buffers** — high-write-throughput transient caches.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Array Capacity

**Problem Statement:** Given an array of $n$ identical disks, each of capacity $D$ GB, derive the usable capacity of a RAID 0 array.

**Step 1: Define the storage contribution of each disk.**
In RAID 0, *every* disk in the array is fully utilized as a data-bearing member. Unlike RAID 1 (mirroring) or RAID 5 (parity), no disk is reserved for redundancy or parity.

$$\text{Usable space on disk } i = D \quad \text{for } i = 1, 2, \dots, n$$

**Step 2: Sum the contributions across all disks.**

$$C_{usable} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} D = n \cdot D$$

**Step 3: Express the capacity efficiency.**

$$\eta = \frac{C_{usable}}{n \cdot D} \times 100\% = \frac{n \cdot D}{n \cdot D} \times 100\% = 100\%$$

**Conclusion:** RAID 0 has a **100% capacity efficiency** — the highest of any RAID level.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Sequential Read Throughput

**Problem Statement:** A RAID 0 array consists of $n$ disks. Each disk can sustain a sequential read throughput of $R_{single}$ MB/s. Derive the maximum array read throughput.

**Step 1: Identify the bottleneck structure.**
A single disk's throughput is limited by:
- Interface bandwidth (e.g., SATA = 600 MB/s, NVMe PCIe 4.0 = ~7000 MB/s)
- Areal density × rotational speed (for HDDs)
- Internal controller queue depth

**Step 2: Recognize that disks operate in parallel.**
The RAID controller issues $n$ independent read commands (one per disk) *simultaneously*. The total throughput is the sum of concurrent data streams.

$$R_{array} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} R_i$$

**Step 3: Apply the homogeneous assumption (all disks identical).**

$$R_i = R_{single} \quad \forall i \in \{1, 2, \dots, n\}$$

**Step 4: Finalize the formula.**

$$R_{array} = n \cdot R_{single}$$

**Step 5: Validate with units.**

$$[\text{MB/s}] = [\text{disks}] \times [\text{MB/s per disk}] = n \cdot R_{single} \quad \checkmark$$

**Numerical Example:**
Let $n = 4$, $R_{single} = 150$ MB/s (typical 7200 RPM HDD).

$$R_{array} = 4 \times 150 = 600 \text{ MB/s}$$

This is precisely the **theoretical** peak; real-world overheads (controller, protocol) typically yield 85–95% of this.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Array MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures)

**Problem Statement:** Each disk has an MTBF of $M_{single}$ hours. Derive the MTBF of a RAID 0 array of $n$ disks.

**Step 1: Convert MTBF to failure rate.**
The failure rate $\lambda$ is the reciprocal of MTBF.

$$\lambda_{single} = \frac{1}{M_{single}}$$

**Step 2: Model the array as a series reliability system.**
In a series system, **any single component failure causes system failure**. RAID 0 is the *purest* series system: one disk down = array down.

$$\lambda_{array} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i$$

**Step 3: Apply the homogeneous assumption.**

$$\lambda_{array} = n \cdot \lambda_{single} = \frac{n}{M_{single}}$$

**Step 4: Invert to obtain MTBF.**

$$MTBF_{array} = \frac{1}{\lambda_{array}} = \frac{M_{single}}{n}$$

**Numerical Example:**
A typical enterprise HDD has $M_{single} = 1{,}000{,}000$ hours. For a 4-disk RAID 0:

$$MTBF_{array} = \frac{1{,}000{,}000}{4} = 250{,}000 \text{ hours}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall:** Students often *increase* MTBF when adding disks (mistakenly thinking redundancy applies). In RAID 0, the opposite is true — **MTBF decreases linearly with $n$**. This is a classic differentiator between RAID 0 and RAID 1/5/6 questions.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Stripe Address Decomposition

**Problem Statement:** A host requests logical block $L$ of size $B$ bytes. The array has $n$ disks with chunk size $C$. Determine the target disk and offset.

**Step 1: Convert logical block address to byte offset.**

$$\text{Byte\_offset} = L \cdot B$$

**Step 2: Determine which stripe unit contains this byte.**

$$\text{Stripe\_unit\_index} = \left\lfloor \frac{\text{Byte\_offset}}{C} \right\rfloor$$

**Step 3: Compute the target disk via modulo.**

$$\text{Disk\_ID} = \text{Stripe\_unit\_index} \bmod n$$

**Step 4: Compute the offset within that disk.**

$$\text{Disk\_offset} = \left\lfloor \frac{\text{Stripe\_unit\_index}}{n} \right\rfloor \cdot C$$

**Worked Numerical Example:**
- $n = 3$ disks, $C = 64$ KB, host requests byte offset = **200,000 bytes**.
- Stripe unit index = $\lfloor 200000 / 65536 \rfloor = \lfloor 3.05 \rfloor = 3$
- Disk ID = $3 \bmod 3 = 0$ → **Disk 0**
- Disk offset = $\lfloor 3 / 3 \rfloor \times 65536 = 1 \times 65536 = 65{,}536$ bytes

---

### 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Choose RAID 0

| Criterion | RAID 0 Verdict | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| Mission-critical data | ❌ NEVER | Zero fault tolerance |
| Maximum I/O throughput required | ✅ Excellent | Linear scaling with $n$ |
| Maximizing usable capacity | ✅ Best (100%) | No redundancy overhead |
| Budget-constrained high-speed scratch | ✅ Ideal | Cheap performance |
| Boot drive for production server | ❌ Avoid | OS failure = total downtime |
| Read-heavy, regenerable data | ✅ Good | E.g., CDN edge cache |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 RAID 0 Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Host["HOST OPERATING SYSTEM\nLogical Block Request"]
    
    Controller["RAID CONTROLLER\nAddress Translation Module\nModulo N Mapper"]
    
    Bus["INTERNAL I/O BUS\nSATA SAS OR NVMe FABRIC"]
    
    D0["DISK 0\nStripe Unit 0\nStripe Unit 3\nStripe Unit 6"]
    D1["DISK 1\nStripe Unit 1\nStripe Unit 4\nStripe Unit 7"]
    D2["DISK 2\nStripe Unit 2\nStripe Unit 5\nStripe Unit 8"]
    D3["DISK 3\nStripe Unit N\nStripe Unit N+1\nStripe Unit N+2"]
    
    Output["ASSEMBLED LOGICAL BLOCK\nReturned To Host"]
    
    Host --> Controller
    Controller --> Bus
    Bus --> D0
    Bus --> D1
    Bus --> D2
    Bus --> D3
    D0 --> Output
    D1 --> Output
    D2 --> Output
    D3 --> Output
    
    style Host fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff,stroke:#1e3a8a
    style Controller fill:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff,stroke:#7c2d12
    style Bus fill:#374151,color:#ffffff,stroke:#374151
    style D0 fill:#065f46,color:#ffffff,stroke:#065f46
    style D1 fill:#065f46,color:#ffffff,stroke:#065f46
    style D2 fill:#065f46,color:#ffffff,stroke:#065f46
    style D3 fill:#065f46,color:#ffffff,stroke:#065f46
    style Output fill:#581c87,color:#ffffff,stroke:#581c87
```

> **Reading the diagram:** A logical file is sliced into stripe units (S0, S1, S2, …). The RAID controller dispatches them in round-robin order — S0 → Disk 0, S1 → Disk 1, S2 → Disk 2, S3 → Disk 3, S4 → Disk 0, and so on. The four disks operate **concurrently**, halving (or quartering) the wall-clock time for large sequential I/O.

### 4.2 RAID 0 Logical-to-Physical Address Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    LogicalFile["LOGICAL FILE F\nSize S bytes"]
    
    Chop["STRIPING LOGIC\nSplit into chunks of C bytes"]
    
    S0["CHUNK 0\nOffset 0 to C-1"]
    S1["CHUNK 1\nOffset C to 2C-1"]
    S2["CHUNK 2\nOffset 2C to 3C-1"]
    S3["CHUNK 3\nOffset 3C to 4C-1"]
    S4["CHUNK 4\nOffset 4C to 5C-1"]
    S5["CHUNK 5\nOffset 5C to 6C-1"]
    
    Mod0["TARGET DISK = 0 mod 4 = 0"]
    Mod1["TARGET DISK = 1 mod 4 = 1"]
    Mod2["TARGET DISK = 2 mod 4 = 2"]
    Mod3["TARGET DISK = 3 mod 4 = 3"]
    Mod4["TARGET DISK = 4 mod 4 = 0"]
    Mod5["TARGET DISK = 5 mod 4 = 1"]
    
    D0Phys["DISK 0 PHYSICAL SECTORS\nChunk 0, Chunk 4, Chunk 8 ..."]
    D1Phys["DISK 1 PHYSICAL SECTORS\nChunk 1, Chunk 5, Chunk 9 ..."]
    D2Phys["DISK 2 PHYSICAL SECTORS\nChunk 2, Chunk 6, Chunk 10 ..."]
    D3Phys["DISK 3 PHYSICAL SECTORS\nChunk 3, Chunk 7, Chunk 11 ..."]
    
    LogicalFile --> Chop
    Chop --> S0
    Chop --> S1
    Chop --> S2
    Chop --> S3
    Chop --> S4
    Chop --> S5
    
    S0 --> Mod0 --> D0Phys
    S1 --> Mod1 --> D1Phys
    S2 --> Mod2 --> D2Phys
    S3 --> Mod3 --> D3Phys
    S4 --> Mod4 --> D0Phys
    S5 --> Mod5 --> D1Phys
    
    style LogicalFile fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    style Chop fill:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff
    style D0Phys fill:#065f46,color:#ffffff
    style D1Phys fill:#065f46,color:#ffffff
    style D2Phys fill:#065f46,color:#ffffff
    style D3Phys fill:#065f46,color:#ffffff
```

### 4.3 Failure Cascade Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Healthy["RAID 0 ARRAY\n4 DISKS HEALTHY\nCapacity = 4D\nThroughput = 4x R\nMTBF = M / 4"]
    
    Fail["DISK 2 FAILS\nOR HARDWARE ERROR"]
    
    Collapsed["RAID 0 ARRAY COLLAPSED\nCapacity = 0 BYTES\nThroughput = 0 MB/s\nDATA = LOST\nRECOVERY = NONE"]
    
    Healthy --> Fail
    Fail --> Collapsed
    
    style Healthy fill:#065f46,color:#ffffff
    style Fail fill:#b91c1c,color:#ffffff
    style Collapsed fill:#7f1d1d,color:#ffffff
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Visual takeaway:** A RAID 0 array exhibits an **all-or-nothing** failure mode. There is no graceful degradation, no rebuild, and no recovery path. The instant a single disk fails, the entire logical volume is **unrecoverable** (in the absence of an external backup).

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define RAID 0. List its key advantages and one major disadvantage.*

**Model Answer:**

**RAID 0 (Striping)** is a disk array architecture that distributes data uniformly across multiple physical disks in fixed-size chunks called **stripe units**, using a round-robin mapping function $(Disk_{target} = chunk\_index \bmod n)$, without storing any redundant copy or parity information.

**Advantages:**
- Maximum **read/write performance** — throughput scales linearly with the number of disks $n$.
- **100% capacity efficiency** — no storage overhead.
- Simple controller logic — lowest implementation cost.

**Disadvantage:**
- **Zero fault tolerance**: failure of any single disk causes total data loss for the entire array.

**Marks Distribution (as per KTU key):** Definition (1M) + Two advantages (1M) + One disadvantage (1M).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*Differentiate between "stripe unit" and "stripe" in the context of RAID 0 with a suitable diagram description.*

**Model Answer:**

A **stripe unit** (or *chunk*) is the **smallest contiguous block of data** written to a single disk during striping. Its size $C$ is a configurable parameter (e.g., 64 KB).

A **stripe** is the **collection of $n$ stripe units, one from each member disk**, that together occupy the same logical position in the array. For a 4-disk RAID 0, a single stripe comprises 4 stripe units — one on each of Disk 0, Disk 1, Disk 2, and Disk 3.

In essence, the stripe unit is the **per-disk** data quantum, while the stripe is the **array-wide** logical quantum.

**Marks Distribution:** Stripe unit definition (1M) + Stripe definition (1M) + Relationship/diagram description (1M).

---

### 5.2 PART B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> **KTU Pattern Note:** Each Part B question is internally choice-based — students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each sub-part carries 7 marks.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the architecture and data mapping mechanism of RAID Level 0. Discuss why striping improves I/O performance. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Architecture:** RAID 0 consists of $n \geq 2$ independent disks connected to a RAID controller. The controller exposes a single logical volume to the host OS, hiding the physical disk boundaries. Data is divided into stripe units of size $C$ and distributed across disks using the modulo rule: $Disk = chunk\_index \bmod n$.

**Diagram (textual representation):**

```
Logical File:  [  S0  |  S1  |  S2  |  S3  |  S4  |  S5  |  S6  |  S7  ]
                  ↓       ↓       ↓       ↓       ↓       ↓       ↓       ↓
Disk 0:        [  S0  |      |      |  S3  |      |      |  S6  |      ]
Disk 1:        [      |  S1  |      |      |  S4  |      |      |  S7  ]
Disk 2:        [      |      |  S2  |      |      |  S5  |      |      ]
Disk 3:        [      |      |      |      |      |      |      |      ]
```

**Why striping improves I/O performance:**
1. **Parallelism:** All $n$ disks can service I/O requests simultaneously, multiplying aggregate throughput by up to $n$.
2. **Latency hiding:** While one disk is performing a seek, others are transferring data, eliminating idle mechanical time.
3. **Load balancing:** The round-robin distribution spreads I/O uniformly, preventing any single disk from becoming a hotspot.

**Marks Split:** Architecture (2M) + Diagram (2M) + Performance justification (3M).

---

**(b)** A RAID 0 array is built with 4 disks, each of capacity 500 GB and MTBF of 1,200,000 hours. Compute:
  (i) Total usable capacity.
  (ii) Array MTBF.
  (iii) Sequential read throughput if a single disk delivers 180 MB/s. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Total Usable Capacity:**
$$C_{usable} = n \times D = 4 \times 500 = 2000 \text{ GB}$$

**(ii) Array MTBF:**
$$MTBF_{array} = \frac{M_{single}}{n} = \frac{1{,}200{,}000}{4} = 300{,}000 \text{ hours}$$

**(iii) Sequential Read Throughput:**
$$R_{array} = n \times R_{single} = 4 \times 180 = 720 \text{ MB/s}$$

**Marks Split:** (i) 2M + (ii) 2M + (iii) 2M + Units/neatness (1M).

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the term "stripe unit size" in RAID 0. How does the choice of stripe unit size affect read/write performance for **(i) large sequential I/O** and **(ii) small random I/O**? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Stripe unit size** is the granularity at which the RAID controller partitions a logical file before distributing it across member disks. It is typically configured as a power of 2 (e.g., 16 KB, 64 KB, 256 KB).

**Effect on Large Sequential I/O:**
- **Large stripe units (e.g., 256 KB):** Each disk transfers a big chunk before the controller switches to the next disk, minimizing the per-disk command overhead and seek count → **higher throughput**.
- **Small stripe units:** Frequent disk switching increases controller overhead, slightly reducing throughput.

**Effect on Small Random I/O:**
- **Small stripe units (e.g., 16 KB):** A single 4 KB random read is served by **one** disk, leaving the other $n-1$ disks free to handle *concurrent* independent requests → **higher IOPS** and better parallelism.
- **Large stripe units:** A small random I/O may still touch only one disk (no benefit), but the wasted chunk space becomes a larger fraction of the request, increasing read-modify-write amplification and reducing effective efficiency.

**Marks Split:** Definition (2M) + Sequential analysis (2.5M) + Random analysis (2.5M).

---

**(b)** A 6-disk RAID 0 array receives a write request for a 384 KB file with a configured stripe unit size of 64 KB. Show the complete chunk-to-disk mapping and identify how many disks are engaged in the operation. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Number of stripe units (chunks).**
$$k = \lceil S / C \rceil = \lceil 384 / 64 \rceil = 6 \text{ chunks}$$

**Step 2: Apply modulo mapping for $n = 6$ disks.**

| Chunk Index | Target Disk (i mod 6) | Offset on Disk (KB) |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | 0 |
| 1 | 1 | 0 |
| 2 | 2 | 0 |
| 3 | 3 | 0 |
| 4 | 4 | 0 |
| 5 | 5 | 0 |

**Step 3: Identify engagement.**
**All 6 disks are engaged concurrently.** Each disk receives exactly one 64 KB chunk, written in parallel.

**Step 4: Effective write throughput.**
$$W_{effective} = 6 \times W_{single}$$

For a 384 KB file, the wall-clock write time equals the single-disk time for a 64 KB write — a **6× speedup** over a single disk.

**Marks Split:** Chunk computation (2M) + Mapping table (3M) + Engagement analysis (2M).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Patterns in RAID 0 Questions:**
> 1. **Writing RAID 1 properties for RAID 0** — Do not mention mirroring, fault tolerance, or "data redundancy" in a RAID 0 answer. RAID 0 has *none* of these.
> 2. **Confusing "stripe" with "stripe unit"** — This is the single most common KTU examiner complaint. Use precise terminology.
> 3. **Forgetting to divide MTBF by $n$** — Many students wrongly state that adding disks *increases* reliability. The opposite is true for RAID 0.
> 4. **Ignoring the modulo rule in chunk-mapping problems** — Always show the formula $Disk = i \bmod n$ explicitly, not just the answer.
> 5. **Forgetting units** — A 7-mark numerical problem without units (GB, MB/s, hours) typically loses 1 mark per missing unit.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — RAID 0**

- ✅ **Full Name:** Redundant Array of Independent Disks — **Level 0 (Striping)**
- ✅ **Minimum Disks:** 2 (undefined for $n=1$)
- ✅ **Capacity Formula:** $C = n \times D$ (sum of all disks)
- ✅ **Capacity Efficiency:** **100%** (best in class)
- ✅ **Throughput Formula:** $R = n \times R_{single}$ (linear scaling)
- ✅ **IOPS Formula:** $IOPS = n \times IOPS_{single}$
- ✅ **Fault Tolerance:** **0 disks** — any failure = total data loss
- ✅ **MTBF Formula:** $MTBF_{array} = MTBF_{single} / n$ (degrades with scale)
- ✅ **Mapping Rule:** $Disk_{target} = chunk\_index \bmod n$
- ✅ **Stripe vs Stripe Unit:** Stripe = array-wide quantum; Stripe Unit = per-disk quantum
- ✅ **Use Cases:** Scratch space, render farms, gaming, scientific compute, non-critical caches
- ✅ **Avoid For:** OS drives, databases, mission-critical archives, production boot volumes
- ✅ **Berkeley Origin:** Patterson, Gibson & Katz (1988) — the seminal RAID paper
- ✅ **Trade-off Position:** Sacrifices reliability for **maximum** performance + capacity
- ✅ **Failure Mode:** All-or-nothing (no graceful degradation, no rebuild)
- ✅ **Chunk Size Trade-off:** Large = sequential throughput; Small = random IOPS

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: RAID 2
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST867 - STORAGE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** RAID 2

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**RAID 2** (Redundant Array of Independent Disks – Level 2) is a disk organization technique that employs **bit-level striping** across multiple data disks combined with a dedicated set of **Error-Correcting Code (ECC) disks** based on **Hamming Codes**. Unlike RAID 1, which mirrors whole blocks, RAID 2 distributes every individual bit of a word across separate physical disks and uses the remaining disks to store Hamming parity bits capable of correcting single-bit errors and detecting double-bit errors in real time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** RAID 2 is the *only* RAID level (among levels 0–6) that relies on **Hamming Error Correction Codes (ECC)** rather than simple parity. It was proposed by Patterson, Gibson, and Katz (1988) but is **never used in modern commercial systems** because of its high cost and requirement for synchronized spindles.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a classroom where a teacher dictates a sentence word-by-word to **four students** sitting in a row, but each student only writes down **one letter** of every word. The fifth, sixth, and seventh students do not write letters — they continually compute a "checksum" (Hamming code) of the letters already written. If any single student makes a typo on a single letter, the checksum students can mathematically detect *which* student made the mistake and *automatically correct* it, all without asking the teacher to repeat the sentence.

In this analogy:
- **Students 1–4 (Data Disks):** each holds one bit of the data word.
- **Students 5–7 (ECC Disks):** continuously compute and store Hamming parity bits.
- **One bad student (failed disk):** the remaining checksum students can pinpoint and correct the bad bit instantly.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The "correction" is performed at the **bit level**, not at the block level. This is why RAID 2 was historically the only level that could *correct* (not just detect) single disk errors transparently.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics

| Metric | Value (KTU Convention) |
|---|---|
| Striping Unit | **1 bit** (sometimes 1 byte in textbooks) |
| ECC Scheme | **Hamming Code** (SEC-DED) |
| Parity Disks | **log₂(D) + 1**, where D = number of data disks |
| Minimum Disks | **3** data + 3 ECC = 7 (most cited example) |
| Spindle Synchronization | **Required (mandatory)** |
| Modern Usage | **Obsolete / Theoretical only** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Architecture (Bulleted Logic Steps)

1. **Bit-Level Striping:** A single byte or word of data is split into individual bits and written across D data disks simultaneously. Disk 1 receives bit 0, Disk 2 receives bit 1, ..., Disk D receives bit D−1.
2. **Hamming Code Generation:** For every data word of `m` bits, the controller computes `p` parity bits (where `p` is the smallest integer satisfying `2^p ≥ m + p + 1`). These `p` bits are written to `p` dedicated ECC disks.
3. **Synchronized Spindles:** All disks must rotate in *perfect* lockstep. If Disk 2 is half a rotation behind Disk 1, the controller cannot reassemble the word. This is the principal reason RAID 2 is impractical.
4. **Single-Bit Error Correction (SEC):** On every read, the controller recomputes the Hamming code from the retrieved data bits. The syndrome (XOR of recomputed and stored parity) yields a unique non-zero binary number that points to the exact failed bit position — which is then flipped automatically.
5. **Double-Bit Error Detection (DED):** If two bits are wrong, the syndrome pattern indicates an uncorrectable error (a special "double-error" syndrome is generated), preventing silent data corruption.
6. **Read Operation:** Controller reads D + p disks in parallel → reconstructs word → verifies/corrects.
7. **Write Operation:** Controller writes D data bits + p ECC bits simultaneously. Smallest write is still 1 word (D bits), not 1 bit, due to ECC recomputation overhead.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **exam-tested** for KTU Storage Systems. Master these before attempting any numerical problem.

| Parameter | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Data Disks | $D$ | Number of disks holding user data |
| ECC Disks | $p = \lceil \log_2(D + p + 1) \rceil$ | Minimum parity disks for Hamming code |
| Standard Lookup | $p = \lceil \log_2(D) \rceil + 1$ | Quick approximation (used in KTU) |
| Total Disks | $D + p$ | Spindles required |
| Usable Capacity | $D \times S_{\min}$ | Where $S_{\min}$ is the smallest disk in the array |
| Storage Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{D}{D+p} = \dfrac{D}{D + \lceil \log_2(D) \rceil + 1}$ | Fraction of raw capacity usable |
| Fault Tolerance | **1 disk** (SEC) | Can correct exactly one disk failure |
| Read Transfer Rate | $R_{\text{read}} = \dfrac{D}{D+p} \times R_{\text{disk}}$ | Slightly lower than single disk |
| Write Transfer Rate | $R_{\text{write}} = \dfrac{D}{D+p} \times R_{\text{disk}} \times \text{ECC\_overhead}$ | Degraded by ECC recomputation |
| Min I/O Request | 1 word (D bits) | No sub-word partial writes allowed |
| Hamming Constraint | $2^p \geq D + p + 1$ | Fundamental Hamming inequality |

### 2.3 KTU Pre-Computed Disk Count Reference Table

| Data Disks ($D$) | ECC Disks ($p$) | Total Spindles | Efficiency $\eta$ |
|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| 4 | 3 | 7 | 57.1% |
| 8 | 4 | 12 | 66.7% |
| 16 | 5 | 21 | 76.2% |
| 32 | 6 | 38 | 84.2% |
| 64 | 7 | 71 | 90.1% |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this table matters:** As D increases, RAID 2's efficiency approaches 100%. This is the *only* RAID level where ECC overhead shrinks *relatively* with scale — but the absolute number of ECC disks still grows logarithmically.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Historically, RAID 2 was implemented by **Thinking Machines Corporation** in the late 1980s for the Connection Machine supercomputer. Today, RAID 2 is **commercially obsolete** for three reasons:
- Cheap RAM allows whole-block buffering (no need for bit-level ECC).
- Modern disks have built-in sector-level ECC — application-level Hamming is redundant.
- Synchronized spindle requirements are mechanically infeasible with consumer drives.

Its **theoretical importance** in KTU exams lies in illustrating how Hamming codes enable single-disk **correction** (not just detection) — a principle later simplified in **RAID 3** (byte-level parity, 1 disk) and **RAID 5** (block-level rotating parity).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Number of ECC Disks Required for D Data Disks

**Given:** A RAID 2 array uses Hamming codes. We must find the minimum number of parity disks $p$ such that the syndrome can uniquely identify **any** single-bit error among $D + p$ bits.

**Step 1 — Count the syndromes needed.**
A syndrome is a binary number of $p$ bits. The total number of distinct non-zero syndromes is $2^p - 1$.
One syndrome must be reserved for the "no error" condition, and the remaining $2^p - 1$ syndromes must uniquely identify each of the $D + p$ bit positions.

$$
2^p - 1 \;\geq\; D + p
$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange into the canonical Hamming inequality.**

$$
2^p \;\geq\; D + p + 1
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the smallest integer $p$.**
The smallest $p$ that satisfies the inequality gives the minimum ECC disk count. This is solved by inspection (KTU allows direct lookup from the standard table).

**Step 4 — Verify with D = 4.**
Try $p = 3$:

$$
2^3 = 8 \;\geq\; 4 + 3 + 1 = 8 \;\;\checkmark
$$

Try $p = 2$:

$$
2^2 = 4 \;\not\geq\; 4 + 2 + 1 = 7
$$

So $p = 3$ is confirmed. **Total disks = 4 + 3 = 7.**

**Step 5 — Verify with D = 8.**
Try $p = 4$:

$$
2^4 = 16 \;\geq\; 8 + 4 + 1 = 13 \;\;\checkmark
$$

Try $p = 3$:

$$
2^3 = 8 \;\not\geq\; 8 + 3 + 1 = 12
$$

So $p = 4$. **Total disks = 8 + 4 = 12.** ∎

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Usable Storage Capacity

**Given:** A RAID 2 array uses $D$ data disks and $p$ ECC disks, each of size $C$ bytes. Compute the effective user storage.

**Step 1 — Total raw capacity of the array.**

$$
C_{\text{raw}} = (D + p) \times C
$$

**Step 2 — Usable capacity (only data disks are user-accessible).**

$$
C_{\text{usable}} = D \times C
$$

**Step 3 — Storage efficiency.**

$$
\eta = \frac{C_{\text{usable}}}{C_{\text{raw}}} = \frac{D}{D+p}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical example (D = 4, p = 3, C = 100 GB).**

$$
C_{\text{raw}} = 7 \times 100 = 700 \;\text{GB}
$$

$$
C_{\text{usable}} = 4 \times 100 = 400 \;\text{GB}
$$

$$
\eta = \frac{400}{700} = 0.5714 = 57.14\%
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation.** Out of every 7 disks purchased, only 4 (57.14%) are useful for user data. The remaining 3 disks are consumed by Hamming ECC overhead. ∎

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Hamming Code Bit Position Placement (D=4, p=3)

This is the **most frequently asked KTU sub-question** on RAID 2. We must show which data bit each parity bit covers.

**Step 1 — Label the 7 bit positions 1 through 7 in binary.**

| Position | Binary | Type |
|---:|---:|:---|
| 1 | 001 | p1 |
| 2 | 010 | p2 |
| 3 | 011 | d1 |
| 4 | 100 | p4 |
| 5 | 101 | d2 |
| 6 | 110 | d3 |
| 7 | 111 | d4 |

**Step 2 — Apply the Hamming rule.** Parity bit $p_k$ covers every position whose binary representation has a `1` in the $k$-th bit.

- $p_1$ (position 1, binary 001) covers positions with bit-0 = 1: **1, 3, 5, 7** → checks `d1, d2, d4` and itself.
- $p_2$ (position 2, binary 010) covers positions with bit-1 = 1: **2, 3, 6, 7** → checks `d1, d3, d4` and itself.
- $p_4$ (position 4, binary 100) covers positions with bit-2 = 1: **4, 5, 6, 7** → checks `d2, d3, d4` and itself.

**Step 3 — Resulting parity equations (even parity convention).**

$$
p_1 = d_1 \oplus d_2 \oplus d_4
$$

$$
p_2 = d_1 \oplus d_3 \oplus d_4
$$

$$
p_4 = d_2 \oplus d_3 \oplus d_4
$$

**Step 4 — Syndrome calculation on read.**

$$
s_1 = p_1 \oplus d_1 \oplus d_2 \oplus d_4
$$

$$
s_2 = p_2 \oplus d_1 \oplus d_3 \oplus d_4
$$

$$
s_4 = p_4 \oplus d_2 \oplus d_3 \oplus d_4
$$

The 3-bit syndrome $(s_4, s_2, s_1)$ directly equals the position number of the bit in error. For example, if $d_3$ flips, syndrome = `(110)` = position 6 → correct bit 6. ∎

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Throughput Analysis

**Given:** Each disk has transfer rate $R$ MB/s and access time $T_a$ ms.

**Step 1 — Read throughput (parallel access of D+p disks).**

$$
T_{\text{read}} = T_a + \frac{S_{\text{word}}}{D \cdot R}
$$

Because the data word is split into $D$ simultaneous bit-reads, transfer time is divided by $D$.

**Step 2 — Write throughput (must read-modify-write all ECC disks).**

$$
T_{\text{write}} = (D+p) \cdot T_a + \frac{S_{\text{word}}}{D \cdot R} + p \cdot T_{\text{ECC\_compute}}
$$

**Step 3 — Example.** D=4, p=3, R=100 MB/s, $T_a$=5 ms, word=4 bits (= 0.5 bytes). Transfer time is negligible compared to access time.

$$
T_{\text{read}} \approx 5 \text{ ms} + 0 \approx 5 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
T_{\text{write}} \approx 7 \times 5 \text{ ms} \approx 35 \text{ ms}
$$

Reads are extremely fast (single rotation, parallel bits), but writes are **7× slower** than a single disk because of the spindle count and ECC recomputation. This is one of RAID 2's classic disadvantages. ∎

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: RAID 2 Architecture (D=4, p=3)

```mermaid
graph LR
    ctrl[Disk Controller] --> d1[Data Disk 1 bit 0]
    ctrl --> d2[Data Disk 2 bit 1]
    ctrl --> d3[Data Disk 3 bit 2]
    ctrl --> d4[Data Disk 4 bit 3]
    ctrl --> p1[ECC Disk 1 p1]
    ctrl --> p2[ECC Disk 2 p2]
    ctrl --> p4[ECC Disk 3 p4]
    d1 -.syndrome check.-> s1[Syndrome Decoder]
    d2 -.syndrome check.-> s1
    d3 -.syndrome check.-> s1
    d4 -.syndrome check.-> s1
    p1 -.syndrome check.-> s1
    p2 -.syndrome check.-> s1
    p4 -.syndrome check.-> s1
    s1 --> fix[Single Bit Error Corrector]
    fix --> host[Host Computer]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart: Read Operation with Hamming Correction

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Host Issues Read Request] --> B[Controller Addresses All D + p Disks]
    B --> C[Disks Rotate in Sync]
    C --> D[Read 1 Bit from Each Disk]
    D --> E[Assemble D-bit Data Word]
    E --> F[Recompute Hamming Parity]
    F --> G{Compare with Stored ECC}
    G -->|Syndrome = 0| H[No Error Return Word]
    G -->|Syndrome = position k| I[Flip Bit at Position k]
    I --> J[Return Corrected Word]
    G -->|Double Error Syndrome| K[Report Uncorrectable Error]
    H --> L[Deliver to Host]
    J --> L
    K --> M[Initiate Alert to OS]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Comparison Block: RAID 2 vs. Other RAID Levels

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph RAID2[RAID 2 Characteristics]
        a1[Bit Level Striping]
        a2[Hamming ECC]
        a3[Multiple Parity Disks]
        a4[Synchronized Spindles]
        a5[SEC DED Capability]
    end
    subgraph RAID0[RAID 0]
        b1[Block Level Striping]
        b2[No Parity]
        b3[Zero Fault Tolerance]
    end
    subgraph RAID3[RAID 3]
        c1[Byte Level Striping]
        c2[Single Parity Disk]
        c3[Synchronized Spindles]
    end
    subgraph RAID5[RAID 5]
        d1[Block Level Striping]
        d2[Rotating Parity]
        d3[Asynchronous Spindles Allowed]
    end
```

### 4.4 Mermaid State Machine: Error Detection States

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> Reading: Read request
    Reading --> Compute: Bits retrieved
    Compute --> NoError: Syndrome equals zero
    Compute --> SingleError: Syndrome non zero and valid
    Compute --> DoubleError: Syndrome indicates double fault
    NoError --> Idle
    SingleError --> Correct: Flip bit at syndrome position
    Correct --> Idle
    DoubleError --> Alert: Notify OS
    Alert --> Rebuild: Replace failed disk
    Rebuild --> Idle
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

*Define RAID 2. Why is it rarely used in modern storage systems even though it provides single-disk error correction?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** RAID 2 is a disk organization scheme that uses **bit-level striping** across multiple data disks combined with **Hamming Error-Correcting Code (ECC) disks**. The Hamming code allows the controller to automatically detect and correct any single-bit error (and detect any double-bit error) in real time without interrupting host operations.

**Why rarely used (1.5 marks):** RAID 2 is rarely used because:
1. It requires **synchronized spindle rotation** across all disks, which is mechanically expensive and impractical with off-the-shelf drives.
2. The number of ECC disks is large for small arrays (e.g., 3 ECC disks for 4 data disks), making the **storage efficiency poor (57.1%)**.
3. Modern disks already have **built-in sector-level ECC**, so application-level Hamming correction is redundant.
4. Smallest I/O transfer unit is one full data word (D bits), causing inefficient small-write performance.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

*State the Hamming code constraint equation used in RAID 2 and explain its significance.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Equation (1.5 marks):**

$$
2^p \;\geq\; D + p + 1
$$

where $D$ is the number of data disks and $p$ is the number of ECC (parity) disks.

**Significance (1.5 marks):**
The $2^p$ on the left side represents the total number of distinct syndrome patterns (including the "no error" state) that a $p$-bit Hamming code can generate. The right side, $D + p + 1$, represents the total bit positions that must be uniquely identified (D data bits + p parity bits + 1 no-error state). The inequality guarantees that **every single-bit error produces a unique, non-zero syndrome** pointing to the exact failed bit position, enabling automatic single-error correction (SEC).

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]**

**(a)** For a RAID 2 array with 8 data disks, calculate:
   (i) The number of ECC disks required. (3 marks)
   (ii) The total number of disks in the array. (1 mark)
   (iii) The storage efficiency if each disk is 200 GB. (3 marks)

**(b)** Explain with a neat block diagram how RAID 2 achieves single-error correction using Hamming codes. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a)(i) Number of ECC disks for D = 8 (3 marks):**

We need the smallest $p$ such that $2^p \geq D + p + 1 = 8 + p + 1 = p + 9$.

Try $p = 3$: $2^3 = 8 \not\geq 12$. ✗
Try $p = 4$: $2^4 = 16 \geq 13$. ✓

**∴ Number of ECC disks = 4.**

**(a)(ii) Total disks (1 mark):**

$$
D + p = 8 + 4 = \mathbf{12 \text{ disks}}
$$

**(a)(iii) Storage efficiency (3 marks):**

Usable capacity:

$$
C_{\text{usable}} = D \times C = 8 \times 200 = 1600 \text{ GB}
$$

Raw capacity:

$$
C_{\text{raw}} = 12 \times 200 = 2400 \text{ GB}
$$

Efficiency:

$$
\eta = \frac{1600}{2400} = 0.6667 = \mathbf{66.67\%}
$$

**(b) Block diagram and single-error correction explanation (7 marks):**

**Architecture:** A RAID 2 array with $D$ data disks and $p$ ECC disks operates as follows:

1. **Write path:** The host issues a write of a $D$-bit data word. The controller splits the word into individual bits and writes bit $i$ to Data Disk $i+1$ (for $i = 0, 1, \ldots, D-1$). Simultaneously, the controller computes the $p$ Hamming parity bits using:

$$
p_k = \bigoplus_{\text{positions covered by } p_k} d_j
$$

These $p$ bits are written to the $p$ ECC disks.

2. **Read path:** The controller reads one bit from each of the $D + p$ disks in parallel. It assembles the $D$-bit data word and recomputes the Hamming parity to obtain a $p$-bit syndrome $S = (s_p, s_{p-1}, \ldots, s_1)$.

3. **Syndrome interpretation:**
   - If $S = 0$: no error occurred; return the word to the host.
   - If $S \neq 0$ and $S$ is a valid position number ($1 \leq S \leq D + p$): a single-bit error has occurred at position $S$. The controller **flips the bit at position $S$ automatically** and returns the corrected word.
   - If $S$ indicates a double-error pattern: the controller reports an uncorrectable error to the host OS.

**Example for D = 4, p = 3:** If $d_3$ (position 6) flips, the recomputed syndrome equals `(110)` = 6, exactly pinpointing the failed bit. The controller flips bit 6 and returns the corrected word transparently.

This is the **defining feature** of RAID 2 — single-disk failure is corrected *without host intervention* and *without data loss*, a property unmatched by RAID 0, 1, or 3 at the time of its invention.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper 1]**

**(a)** Compare RAID 2 with RAID 3 and RAID 5 in terms of striping unit, parity mechanism, spindle synchronization, fault tolerance, and minimum number of disks. Prepare a comparison table and justify why RAID 2 is considered commercially obsolete. (7 marks)

**(b)** A RAID 2 system uses 16 data disks of 250 GB each. Determine:
   (i) The minimum number of Hamming ECC disks required. (2 marks)
   (ii) The total raw capacity and usable capacity. (2 marks)
   (iii) The Hamming parity equations if the data bits are labelled $d_1$ through $d_{16}$ and the parity disks are $p_1, p_2, p_4, p_8$. (3 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Comparison Table and Justification (7 marks):**

| Parameter | RAID 2 | RAID 3 | RAID 5 |
|---|---|---|---|
| Striping Unit | **1 bit** | 1 byte | 1 block (sector) |
| Parity Mechanism | **Hamming ECC** (multiple parity disks) | Single XOR parity disk | Rotating XOR parity across all disks |
| Spindle Sync | **Required** | Required | Not required |
| Fault Tolerance | 1 disk (SEC-DED) | 1 disk (parity only) | 1 disk |
| Min Disks | 7 (4D + 3P) | 3 (2D + 1P) | 3 (2D + 1P) |
| Error Type Handled | **Corrects** single-bit error | Detects only | Detects only |
| Write Penalty | High (all ECC recomputed) | High (single parity disk bottleneck) | Moderate (read-modify-write of 2 blocks) |

**Why RAID 2 is obsolete (justification):**
- Synchronized spindles are mechanically infeasible with consumer drives.
- Hamming ECC overhead is prohibitive at small scales (e.g., 3 ECC for 4 data = 43% waste).
- Modern disks have **on-board sector ECC** that handles bit errors transparently.
- RAID 3 and RAID 5 achieve similar fault tolerance with **far less complexity** and **no spindle synchronization**.

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

**(b)(i) Minimum ECC disks for D = 16 (2 marks):**

We need the smallest $p$ such that $2^p \geq 16 + p + 1 = p + 17$.

Try $p = 5$: $2^5 = 32 \geq 22$. ✓
Try $p = 4$: $2^4 = 16 \not\geq 21$. ✗

**∴ $p = 5$ ECC disks.** (Note: KTU also accepts $p = \lceil \log_2 16 \rceil + 1 = 4 + 1 = 5$, which matches.)

**(b)(ii) Capacities (2 marks):**

Total disks = $D + p = 16 + 5 = 21$.

$$
C_{\text{raw}} = 21 \times 250 = 5250 \text{ GB}
$$

$$
C_{\text{usable}} = 16 \times 250 = 4000 \text{ GB}
$$

**(b)(iii) Parity equations (3 marks):**

The five parity bits $p_1, p_2, p_4, p_8, p_{16}$ correspond to the bit positions whose binary representation has the corresponding power-of-2 bit set.

Using the standard Hamming rule, each parity bit covers the data positions whose index (in binary) contains a 1 in the corresponding bit position. The general form is:

$$
p_k = \bigoplus_{j \in \text{positions where bit } k \text{ of } j \text{ is 1}} d_j
$$

Concretely for the 16 data bits labelled $d_1 \ldots d_{16}$ (positions 3 to 18 in the 21-bit word):

$$
p_1 = d_1 \oplus d_2 \oplus d_4 \oplus d_5 \oplus d_7 \oplus d_8 \oplus d_{10} \oplus d_{11} \oplus d_{13} \oplus d_{14} \oplus d_{16}
$$

(All data bits whose position number is **odd** — bit 0 of their index is 1.)

$$
p_2 = d_1 \oplus d_3 \oplus d_4 \oplus d_6 \oplus d_7 \oplus d_9 \oplus d_{10} \oplus d_{12} \oplus d_{13} \oplus d_{15} \oplus d_{16}
$$

(Bit 1 of index = 1.)

$$
p_4 = d_2 \oplus d_3 \oplus d_4 \oplus d_6 \oplus d_7 \oplus d_8 \oplus d_{10} \oplus d_{11} \oplus d_{12} \oplus d_{14} \oplus d_{15} \oplus d_{16}
$$

$$
p_8 = d_5 \oplus d_6 \oplus d_7 \oplus d_8 \oplus d_{13} \oplus d_{14} \oplus d_{15} \oplus d_{16}
$$

$$
p_{16} = d_9 \oplus d_{10} \oplus d_{11} \oplus d_{12} \oplus d_{13} \oplus d_{14} \oplus d_{15} \oplus d_{16}
$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in RAID 2 Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing bit-level vs. block-level striping.** Many students incorrectly write that RAID 2 stripes by block. RAID 2 is *bit-level* — say it explicitly.
> 2. **Forgetting the "+1" in the Hamming inequality.** The correct form is $2^p \geq D + p + 1$, **not** $2^p \geq D + p$. That "+1" accounts for the all-zero "no-error" syndrome.
> 3. **Using $p = \log_2(D)$ instead of $p = \lceil \log_2(D) \rceil + 1$.** Always take the *ceiling* and *add 1*. For D=4, $p = 3$, not $p = 2$.
> 4. **Ignoring the D + p + 1 nuance.** If a question gives D=10 and you write p=4 because $\lceil \log_2 10 \rceil = 4$, you will be marked correct **only if** you verify $2^4 = 16 \geq 10 + 4 + 1 = 15$. Always verify.
> 5. **Writing parity equations for the wrong position numbering.** Position 1, 2, 4, 8, ... are parity positions, **not** data positions. Mixing them up costs full marks on the equation sub-part.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for RAID 2 (KTU Module 1):**

- **Definition:** Bit-level striping + Hamming ECC disks. **State this verbatim** in the opening line of any 7-mark answer.
- **Hamming Constraint:** $2^p \geq D + p + 1$ — memorize this equation.
- **Quick ECC count:** $p = \lceil \log_2(D) \rceil + 1$ for D ≥ 4.
- **Standard 7-disk example:** D = 4 data, p = 3 ECC, total = 7, efficiency = 57.14%.
- **Bit positions in Hamming word:** 1, 2, 4, 8, 16, ... are parity; others are data.
- **Parity equation rule:** $p_k$ covers all positions whose binary index has a 1 in bit position $k$.
- **Syndrome interpretation:** $(s_p \ldots s_1)_2$ equals the position number of the failed bit.
- **SEC-DED:** Single Error Correction – Double Error Detection.
- **Mandatory spindle synchronization** — this is the single biggest reason RAID 2 is obsolete.
- **Storage efficiency formula:** $\eta = D / (D + p)$. Always express as a percentage in answers.
- **Comparison anchor:** RAID 2 = bit-level + multiple Hamming parity; RAID 3 = byte-level + 1 parity; RAID 5 = block-level + rotating parity.
- **Modern relevance:** Hamming code principles survive in **ECC RAM** and **disk sector ECC**, even though RAID 2 itself is not implemented.
- **Historical credit:** Invented by Patterson, Gibson, and Katz in their seminal 1988 paper "A Case for Redundant Arrays of Inexpensive Disks (RAID)."
- **Exam tip:** When asked "Why not RAID 2?", always mention *all four* reasons: spindle sync, ECC overhead, built-in disk ECC, and complex controller logic.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: RAID 3
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST867 - STORAGE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** RAID 3

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**RAID 3 (Redundant Array of Independent Disks, Level 3)** is a standard RAID architecture that employs **byte-level striping** of data across $N$ data disks combined with a **single dedicated parity disk**. The parity information is computed using the bitwise **XOR (Exclusive-OR)** operation across corresponding bytes of the data disks and stored on the dedicated parity disk.

$$P[i] \;=\; D_0[i] \;\oplus\; D_1[i] \;\oplus\; D_2[i] \;\oplus\; \dots \;\oplus\; D_{N-1}[i]$$

where $D_k[i]$ is the $i^{th}$ byte stored on data disk $k$, and $P[i]$ is the corresponding parity byte. The minimum configuration requires **3 disks** ($N \geq 2$ data disks and 1 parity disk). All disks must operate with **synchronized spindle rotation** because data is distributed at the byte (or sub-byte) level.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Terminology Checklist**
> - **Striping Unit (Stripe Width):** 1 byte (in pure RAID 3) or 1 bit (in some textbooks calling it "RAID 3" with bit-striping — that variant is often renamed **RAID 2** in canonical literature).
> - **Redundancy Mechanism:** Dedicated parity disk (Single Parity).
> - **Fault Tolerance:** 1 disk failure (rebuilds from remaining $N$ disks).
> - **Spindle Sync:** Mandatory (rotational lock-step).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The "Split-Notebook" with a Master Accountant**
> Imagine you are writing a story in a notebook. Instead of writing sentence-by-sentence on the same notebook, you tear out each **individual letter** and hand it to a different typist. Five typists (data disks) each get one letter of every word, while a sixth typist (the **parity typist**) records the **total count of letters** on each line — *not the actual letters themselves*, just the *sum* (XOR).
> - If one typist's notebook burns in a fire, the Master Accountant (RAID controller) can **reconstruct** every missing letter because the parity typist's count still tells how to combine the others.
> - The catch: every typist must write in perfect **lock-step** (synchronized spindles) because letter-1 of page-1, letter-1 of page-2, etc., must all be written at the same physical offset.
> - The drawback: the parity typist becomes the **bottleneck** of the office, just like the dedicated parity disk bottlenecks RAID 3.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics at a Glance

| Metric | Value / Range |
|---|---|
| Minimum disks | **3** (2 data + 1 parity) |
| Practical minimum | 5 (4 data + 1 parity) |
| Striping granularity | **1 byte** |
| Redundancy type | Dedicated parity (1 disk) |
| Fault tolerance | **1 disk** failure |
| Read penalty (small) | High (all spindles must sync) |
| Sequential transfer rate | **Very High** |
| Random I/O performance | **Poor** |
| Storage efficiency | $\dfrac{N}{N+1}$ |
| ECC method | Parity (XOR) — can be augmented with **Hamming code** for multi-bit error detection |

> [!TIP]
> In several KTU reference slides, the term *"Hamming code ECC"* is associated with RAID 3. This refers to the *extended* use of Hamming parity — the standard RAID 3 parity provides **Single Error Detection (SED)** only. A true Hamming-encoded RAID 3 can detect 2-bit errors and correct 1-bit errors, but the common university-level treatment of RAID 3 uses only the XOR parity.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Architecture — Layered Breakdown

RAID 3 can be understood in three logical layers:

1. **Application / Host Layer**
   - The host OS issues a *logical* read or write request for a contiguous block of bytes.
2. **RAID Controller Layer (Middle Layer)**
   - **Stripe Decomposer:** Splits the requested block into byte-sized fragments.
