   - **Parity Compute Engine:** Calculates XOR parity for each stripe.
   - **Disk Scheduler:** Issues parallel I/O to all $N+1$ disks.
3. **Physical Disk Layer**
   - $N$ data disks + $1$ dedicated parity disk rotating in **lock-step**.

### 2.1.1 Read Operation (Sequential Large Block)

1. The host issues a *large sequential* read of size $S$ bytes.
2. The controller calculates how many *stripe rotations* are needed: $\text{rotations} = \lceil S / N \rceil$ bytes worth.
3. All $N$ data disks spin in **synchronization**; one byte is read from each data disk per rotation.
4. The parity disk is **not read** for normal data requests (parity is consulted only on reconstruction).
5. Effective transfer rate per second:

$$R_{\text{read}} \;=\; N \times r_{\text{disk}}$$

where $r_{\text{disk}}$ is the single-disk sequential byte-transfer rate.

### 2.1.2 Read Operation (Small / Single Byte)

1. All $N+1$ disks must still spin and seek in unison.
2. **Only one sector is read** from each disk (one byte from each data disk + one parity byte).
3. The controller discards the $N-1$ unwanted bytes.
4. Effective utilization: only $\dfrac{1}{N+1}$ of the I/O bandwidth is useful → **poor for small random reads**.

### 2.1.3 Write Operation (Full-Stripe Write)

For a write spanning a *full* stripe of $N$ bytes:

1. The host provides $N$ new data bytes: $D_0', D_1', \dots, D_{N-1}'$.
2. The controller writes each byte to its respective data disk: $D_k \leftarrow D_k'$.
3. The controller writes the freshly computed parity byte to the parity disk:
   $$P' \;=\; D_0' \;\oplus\; D_1' \;\oplus\; \dots \;\oplus\; D_{N-1}'$$
4. Cost: $N$ data writes + $1$ parity write = $N+1$ disk operations.

### 2.1.4 Write Operation (Small / Single Byte — Read-Modify-Write NOT Used)

Unlike RAID 4/5, RAID 3 has **no Read-Modify-Write penalty** for small writes, because the granularity is a single byte. However, the controller still must perform:

1. **Read all $N$ old data bytes** + 1 old parity byte → $N+1$ reads.
2. **Compute new parity:** $P' = D_0 \oplus \dots \oplus D_{k-1} \oplus D_{k+1} \oplus \dots \oplus D_{N-1} \oplus D_k' \oplus P$ (algebraic optimization).
3. **Write the new data byte** + the new parity byte → $2$ writes.

Total small-write cost: $N+1$ reads + $2$ writes. Although $N+1$ reads is more I/O than RAID 5's Read-Modify-Write, each read in RAID 3 accesses only **1 byte** (negligible seek cost when spindles are synchronized), so the small-write penalty is essentially:

$$\text{Write Penalty (RAID 3)} \;=\; \dfrac{N+1 \text{ reads} + 2 \text{ writes}}{1 \text{ logical write}}$$

## 2.2 Failure Recovery — The XOR Reconstruction

Suppose data disk $D_j$ fails. The controller rebuilds each byte $i$ as:

$$D_j[i] \;=\; P[i] \;\oplus\; \bigoplus_{k=0, k \neq j}^{N-1} D_k[i]$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why XOR works as a parity operator:** XOR is its own inverse, i.e., $x \oplus x = 0$ and $x \oplus 0 = x$. Thus, $\text{original data} = \text{parity} \oplus \text{sum of all other data}$, regardless of which single disk is missing. This property is what gives RAID 3 (and all XOR-based RAID levels) **single-disk fault tolerance** with only 1 disk of overhead.

## 2.3 Synchronized Spindle — Why It Is Mandatory

Because each byte is spread across $N+1$ disks, byte $i$ of logical block $L$ resides at sector $i / N$ on data disk $(i \bmod N)$ and at the same physical sector on the parity disk. **If spindles drift**, the controller can no longer guarantee that bytes belonging together are read at the same rotational position. The result: **data corruption**, not just performance loss.

This is why RAID 3 is **only used in environments where synchronized spindles are feasible** — historically, supercomputers, video servers, and high-speed data acquisition systems.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Print this table and memorize before the exam.**

| Symbol | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| $N$ | Input | Number of data disks |
| $D$ | $N + 1$ | Total number of physical disks |
| $C_{\text{usable}}$ | $N \times C_{\text{disk}}$ | Usable storage capacity |
| $C_{\text{raw}}$ | $(N+1) \times C_{\text{disk}}$ | Raw (gross) storage capacity |
| $\eta$ (Efficiency) | $\dfrac{N}{N+1}$ | Storage efficiency ratio |
| $P[i]$ | $\displaystyle\bigoplus_{k=0}^{N-1} D_k[i]$ | Parity byte formula |
| $D_j[i]$ (rebuild) | $P[i] \oplus \displaystyle\bigoplus_{k \neq j} D_k[i]$ | Reconstruction after failure |
| $R_{\text{seq-read}}$ | $N \times r_{\text{disk}}$ | Sequential read throughput |
| Write penalty (full stripe) | $N+1$ ops per logical write | Cost of writing one stripe |
| Write penalty (small) | $N+1$ reads + 2 writes | Cost of writing one byte |
| Min. disks | $3$ | Hard requirement |
| Fault tolerance | $1$ disk | Single parity limit |

**CRITICAL:** All $\oplus$ symbols in the table represent **bitwise XOR**, not the OR operator.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Why RAID 3 Is Used |
|---|---|
| **High-Performance Computing (HPC)** | Supercomputers (Cray, early SGI) used RAID 3 for sustained sequential throughput on large datasets. |
| **Video Streaming / Broadcast** | Long sequential writes and reads dominate; the parity bottleneck is amortized. |
| **Scientific Data Acquisition** | Sensors stream massive sequential files; synchronized spindles are a small price to pay. |
| **Database Log Files** | Sequential append-only writes — a good fit when logs are not interleaved with random updates. |
| **Embedded Imaging Systems** | Satellite imaging pipelines read/write multi-megapixel frames sequentially. |

In modern enterprise storage, RAID 3 has largely been **superseded by RAID 5 and RAID 6**, which avoid the synchronized-spindle requirement. However, the *conceptual* foundation of byte-level striping with XOR parity taught in RAID 3 is the bedrock on which every subsequent RAID level is built.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Capacity, Efficiency, and Parity Computation

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):**
A RAID 3 array consists of **4 data disks** and **1 dedicated parity disk**. Each disk has a capacity of **600 GB**. Calculate:
1. Total raw capacity.
2. Usable capacity.
3. Storage efficiency.
4. The parity byte value if the first byte on each of the 4 data disks is: `11001010`, `10110100`, `11110000`, `00001111`.

### Step 1 — Identify the Variables

From the problem:

$$N = 4 \quad (\text{data disks}), \quad D = N+1 = 5 \quad (\text{total disks}), \quad C_{\text{disk}} = 600 \text{ GB}$$

### Step 2 — Compute Total Raw Capacity

$$C_{\text{raw}} \;=\; D \times C_{\text{disk}} \;=\; 5 \times 600 \text{ GB} \;=\; 3000 \text{ GB}$$

### Step 3 — Compute Usable Capacity

The parity disk stores **no user data**, so:

$$C_{\text{usable}} \;=\; N \times C_{\text{disk}} \;=\; 4 \times 600 \text{ GB} \;=\; 2400 \text{ GB}$$

### Step 4 — Compute Storage Efficiency

$$\eta \;=\; \dfrac{C_{\text{usable}}}{C_{\text{raw}}} \;=\; \dfrac{2400}{3000} \;=\; \dfrac{4}{5} \;=\; 0.8 \;=\; 80\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> The remaining **20% (600 GB)** is the *parity overhead* — the price paid for fault tolerance of 1 disk.

### Step 5 — Compute the Parity Byte (Bitwise XOR)

We compute the parity byte $P[0]$ for the **first byte position** across all 4 data disks. Recall:

$$P[0] \;=\; D_0[0] \;\oplus\; D_1[0] \;\oplus\; D_2[0] \;\oplus\; D_3[0]$$

Substituting the given bytes and XOR-ing bit by bit from MSB to LSB:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_0[0] & = 11001010 \\
D_1[0] & = 10110100 \\
D_2[0] & = 11110000 \\
D_3[0] & = 00001111 \\
\hline
P[0]   & = 11001010 \oplus 10110100 \oplus 11110000 \oplus 00001111
\end{aligned}
$$

Now we apply XOR step by step. Since XOR is associative, we can do it in any order. Let's chain it:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step A: } & 11001010 \oplus 10110100 \;=\; 01111110 \\
\text{Step B: } & 01111110 \oplus 11110000 \;=\; 10001110 \\
\text{Step C: } & 10001110 \oplus 00001111 \;=\; 10000001 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore, the parity byte stored on the dedicated parity disk at byte position 0 is:

$$P[0] \;=\; 10000001$$

**Verification Trick (Always Use It in Exams):**
The XOR of all data bytes plus the parity byte must equal `00000000` (because $P = D_0 \oplus D_1 \oplus D_2 \oplus D_3$ implies $P \oplus D_0 \oplus D_1 \oplus D_2 \oplus D_3 = 0$).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Check: } & 11001010 \oplus 10110100 \oplus 11110000 \oplus 00001111 \oplus 10000001 \\
& = (P \oplus P) \; \text{(since } P = D_0 \oplus D_1 \oplus D_2 \oplus D_3\text{)} \\
& = 00000000 \;\;\checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Exam hack:** If you want to skip the step-by-step XOR, you can XOR all 5 values (4 data + parity) and the result must be `0`. The converse — if the XOR of all 5 is `0`, your parity is correct — is a great self-check.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Data Reconstruction After a Disk Failure

**Problem Statement:**
Continuing from Example 1, suppose data disk $D_1$ (containing `10110100` at byte position 0) **fails completely**. Using the parity byte and the surviving data disks, reconstruct the lost byte.

### Step 1 — State the Reconstruction Formula

The data on failed disk $D_j$ at byte position $i$ is recovered as:

$$D_j[i] \;=\; P[i] \;\oplus\; \bigoplus_{k=0, k \neq j}^{N-1} D_k[i]$$

For our case, $j = 1$, so:

$$D_1[0] \;=\; P[0] \;\oplus\; D_0[0] \;\oplus\; D_2[0] \;\oplus\; D_3[0]$$

### Step 2 — Substitute the Known Values

$$D_1[0] \;=\; 10000001 \;\oplus\; 11001010 \;\oplus\; 11110000 \;\oplus\; 00001111$$

### Step 3 — XOR Step-by-Step

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step A: } & 10000001 \oplus 11001010 \;=\; 01001011 \\
\text{Step B: } & 01001011 \oplus 11110000 \;=\; 10111011 \\
\text{Step C: } & 10111011 \oplus 00001111 \;=\; 10110100 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Confirm the Result

$$D_1[0] \;=\; 10110100$$

This matches exactly the original value `10110100` from Example 1. The reconstruction is **bit-perfect**. ✓

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** A common student mistake is to use OR (|) or AND (&) instead of XOR (⊕). Remember: the parity is a **bitwise XOR** because XOR is the *only* Boolean operator that is its own inverse — which is what makes reconstruction possible.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Small-Write Cost and Bandwidth Analysis

**Problem Statement:**
A RAID 3 array has $N = 4$ data disks, all spinning at 10,000 RPM with an average rotational latency of 3 ms and a sector read/write time of 0.5 ms. The host issues a small random write of **1 byte** to logical byte position 5,432,100. Estimate the **total I/O time** in milliseconds and the **effective throughput** in KB/s.

### Step 1 — Disk Synchronization Overhead

Because all $N+1 = 5$ disks are synchronized, the rotational latency is incurred **once** (not per-disk):

$$T_{\text{rot}} \;=\; 3 \text{ ms} \quad (\text{pay once for the synchronized set})$$

### Step 2 — Count the Disk Operations

For a small (1-byte) write in RAID 3:
- **Reads:** $N + 1 = 5$ (read all 4 old data bytes + 1 old parity byte) at 0.5 ms each.
- **Writes:** $2$ (write new data byte + new parity byte) at 0.5 ms each.

$$T_{\text{I/O}} \;=\; T_{\text{rot}} \;+\; (N+1) \times T_{\text{sector}} \;+\; 2 \times T_{\text{sector}}$$

$$T_{\text{I/O}} \;=\; 3 \text{ ms} \;+\; 5 \times 0.5 \text{ ms} \;+\; 2 \times 0.5 \text{ ms}$$

$$T_{\text{I/O}} \;=\; 3 \text{ ms} \;+\; 2.5 \text{ ms} \;+\; 1.0 \text{ ms} \;=\; 6.5 \text{ ms}$$

### Step 3 — Effective Throughput

The host issued **1 byte = 0.001 KB**. Therefore:

$$R_{\text{effective}} \;=\; \dfrac{0.001 \text{ KB}}{6.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s}} \;\approx\; 0.154 \text{ KB/s}$$

> [!WARNING]
> This is **catastrophically low** throughput — the cardinal sin of RAID 3 for small random writes. Compare to a sequential write where the same 6.5 ms moves $N = 4$ bytes, giving $\approx 0.615$ KB/s — still tiny, but 4× better. For large sequential writes (hundreds of KB), the per-byte overhead becomes negligible and RAID 3 shines.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture (RAID 3 Data Path)

The Mermaid diagram below shows how a host write request flows through the RAID 3 controller and is distributed across the synchronized disk set.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    HOST[Host Application]
    RC[RAID 3 Controller]
    ST[Stripe Decomposer]
    PC[Parity Compute Engine]
    SCHED[Synchronized Disk Scheduler]
    D0[Data Disk D0]
    D1[Data Disk D1]
    D2[Data Disk D2]
    D3[Data Disk D3]
    PD[Parity Disk P]
    SP[Spindle Sync Signal]

    HOST --> RC
    RC --> ST
    ST --> PC
    ST --> SCHED
    PC --> SCHED
    SCHED --> D0
    SCHED --> D1
    SCHED --> D2
    SCHED --> D3
    SCHED --> PD
    SP -.-> D0
    SP -.-> D1
    SP -.-> D2
    SP -.-> D3
    SP -.-> PD
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **Spindle Sync Signal** (dashed arrows) enforces rotational lock-step across all 5 disks.
- The **Stripe Decomposer** splits the host block into byte-sized fragments.
- The **Parity Compute Engine** calculates the XOR parity and forwards it to the scheduler for the dedicated parity disk.
- All physical I/O is dispatched by the **Synchronized Disk Scheduler** in lock-step.

---

## 4.2 Byte-Striping Layout Across the Disk Set

The diagram below shows how 8 consecutive logical bytes (`B0` through `B7`) are physically distributed across 4 data disks + 1 parity disk in RAID 3.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    LOGIC[Logical Byte Stream: B0 B1 B2 B3 B4 B5 B6 B7]
    LOGIC --> D0A[Disk D0: B0 B4]
    LOGIC --> D1A[Disk D1: B1 B5]
    LOGIC --> D2A[Disk D2: B2 B6]
    LOGIC --> D3A[Disk D3: B3 B7]
    LOGIC --> PA[Parity Disk: P0 P1]
    P0[P0 = B0 XOR B1 XOR B2 XOR B3]
    P1[P1 = B4 XOR B5 XOR B6 XOR B7]
    PA --> P0
    PA --> P1
```

**Key insight from the diagram:**
- **Byte $B_i$** is written to data disk $D_{i \bmod N}$ at sector $\lfloor i / N \rfloor$.
- **Parity byte $P_j$** corresponds to the XOR of all data bytes in stripe $j$.
- The parity disk carries **one byte for every $N$ data bytes** — a 1:N ratio that fixes the storage overhead at exactly $\frac{1}{N+1}$ of raw capacity.

---

## 4.3 Failure-Recovery Flow (Disk $D_1$ Fails)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    FAIL[Disk D1 Fails]
    DETECT[Controller Detects Failure]
    REBUILD[Activate Rebuild Mode]
    READP[Read Parity Bytes from P]
    READD[Read Surviving Data from D0 D2 D3]
    XOR[Compute D1 byte = P XOR D0 XOR D2 XOR D3]
    HOT[Write Reconstructed Bytes to Hot Spare]
    DONE[Array Back to Redundant State]

    FAIL --> DETECT
    DETECT --> REBUILD
    REBUILD --> READP
    REBUILD --> READD
    READP --> XOR
    READD --> XOR
    XOR --> HOT
    HOT --> DONE
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Hot Spare** is an optional idle disk present in production RAID 3 arrays. As soon as a failure is detected, the controller automatically begins rebuilding onto the hot spare so the array is never without redundancy.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*What is byte-level striping in RAID 3? Why is synchronized spindle rotation mandatory for RAID 3 arrays?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Byte-level striping (1 mark):** RAID 3 distributes the host's data block across $N$ data disks such that **consecutive bytes** go to **consecutive disks** in round-robin fashion. Specifically, byte $i$ is written to disk $D_{i \bmod N}$ at sector $\lfloor i / N \rfloor$. The dedicated parity disk stores the XOR of all bytes in the same stripe position.
- **Synchronized spindle (2 marks):** Synchronized rotation is **mandatory** because the bytes belonging to a single logical block are scattered across multiple disks. The controller relies on all disks presenting the corresponding sector at the same rotational angle. If spindles drift, the controller reads a byte from disk $D_0$'s position $p$ and a *different* logical byte from disk $D_1$'s position $p$, scrambling the logical order and causing **silent data corruption**. The hardware lock-step (typically via a shared timing signal) eliminates this drift.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*A RAID 3 array has 8 data disks and 1 dedicated parity disk, each of size 1 TB. Calculate the total usable capacity, raw capacity, and storage efficiency. If one data disk fails, how is the lost data recovered?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Capacity (1 mark):** $C_{\text{usable}} = 8 \times 1 \text{ TB} = 8 \text{ TB}$. $C_{\text{raw}} = 9 \times 1 \text{ TB} = 9 \text{ TB}$. Efficiency $\eta = \frac{8}{9} \approx 88.89\%$.
- **Recovery (2 marks):** When any single disk $D_j$ fails, the controller reads the corresponding bytes from the **parity disk** and the **surviving $N - 1$ data disks**, then applies the XOR rule:
  $$D_j[i] = P[i] \oplus D_0[i] \oplus \dots \oplus D_{j-1}[i] \oplus D_{j+1}[i] \oplus \dots \oplus D_{N-1}[i]$$
  The reconstructed bytes are written to a **hot spare** disk (if present) or directly to a replacement disk, restoring redundancy.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 Scheme regulations, every Part-B question carries an **internal choice**. Below are **TWO fully independent alternatives**. Attempt **either** Option A **or** Option B.

---

### Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | Adapted]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
*Explain the architecture of RAID 3 with a neat diagram. Discuss how data is written (full-stripe write and partial-stripe write) and read in a RAID 3 system.*

**Model Answer:**

**Architecture (3 marks):** RAID 3 uses **byte-level striping** across $N$ data disks plus **one dedicated parity disk**. All $N+1$ disks are **synchronized** in spindle rotation. The RAID controller sits between the host and the disk set and performs three functions: (i) stripe decomposition (split host I/O into bytes), (ii) parity computation (XOR), and (iii) synchronized dispatch. A representative diagram is shown below in text form:

```
            +------------+
   Host --->| RAID 3     |---> Disk D0
            | Controller |---> Disk D1
            |            |---> Disk D2
            |            |---> Disk D3
            |            |---> Parity Disk P
            +------------+
   (All disks spin in lock-step; arrows indicate byte-level striping)
```

**Full-Stripe Write (2 marks):** For a write of exactly $N$ bytes (one complete stripe), the controller:
1. Computes new parity: $P' = D_0' \oplus D_1' \oplus \dots \oplus D_{N-1}'$.
2. Writes each new data byte to its respective data disk: $D_k \leftarrow D_k'$.
3. Writes the new parity byte to the parity disk: $P \leftarrow P'$.
4. Total I/O = $N$ data writes + $1$ parity write.

**Partial-Stripe (Small) Write (2 marks):** For a write of fewer than $N$ bytes (e.g., 1 byte to $D_j$ at position $i$):
1. Read all $N$ old data bytes and 1 old parity byte from the synchronized set ($N+1$ reads).
2. Compute new parity using the **algebraic shortcut**:
   $$P'[i] = P[i] \oplus D_j[i] \oplus D_j'[i]$$
3. Write the new data byte and the new parity byte ($2$ writes).
4. Total I/O = $N+1$ reads + $2$ writes.

**Read Operation (covered as part of architecture):** For sequential large reads, the controller issues parallel byte reads from all $N$ data disks; the parity disk is **not** read in normal operation, giving a sequential throughput of $N \times r_{\text{disk}}$. For small random reads, all spindles must still rotate synchronously, but only one byte is used from each disk — making RAID 3 inefficient for small random workloads.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
*Consider a RAID 3 array with 5 data disks (each 2 TB) and 1 parity disk (also 2 TB). A file of size 10,240 bytes is written sequentially to the array. Show the byte-level distribution across the data disks and the parity computation for the first stripe. The first 5 bytes of the file (in binary) are: 10110001, 01001110, 11100011, 00011110, 10010110.*

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Capacity and Configuration (1 mark):**
- $N = 5$ data disks, $C_{\text{disk}} = 2 \text{ TB}$.
- Usable capacity: $C_{\text{usable}} = 5 \times 2 \text{ TB} = 10 \text{ TB}$.
- Raw capacity: $C_{\text{raw}} = 6 \times 2 \text{ TB} = 12 \text{ TB}$.
- Storage efficiency: $\eta = \frac{5}{6} \approx 83.33\%$.

**Step 2 — Stripe Distribution (2 marks):** Each stripe contains 5 bytes — one byte per data disk. The file of 10,240 bytes thus fills $\frac{10240}{5} = 2048$ stripes. The first 10 bytes of the file are distributed as:

| Stripe | Byte Index | Data Disk | Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | $D_0$ | `10110001` |
| 0 | 1 | $D_1$ | `01001110` |
| 0 | 2 | $D_2$ | `11100011` |
| 0 | 3 | $D_3$ | `00011110` |
| 0 | 4 | $D_4$ | `10010110` |
| 1 | 5 | $D_0$ | (file byte 5) |
| 1 | 6 | $D_1$ | (file byte 6) |
| ... | ... | ... | ... |

**Step 3 — Parity for First Stripe (4 marks):**

$$P[0] = 10110001 \oplus 01001110 \oplus 11100011 \oplus 00011110 \oplus 10010110$$

We compute XOR step by step:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: } & 10110001 \oplus 01001110 = 11111111 \\
\text{Step 2: } & 11111111 \oplus 11100011 = 00011100 \\
\text{Step 3: } & 00011100 \oplus 00011110 = 00000010 \\
\text{Step 4: } & 00000010 \oplus 10010110 = 10010100 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore, the parity byte written to the parity disk for the first stripe is:

$$P[0] = 10010100$$

**Verification (1 mark, included in step 3):** $P[0] \oplus D_0[0] \oplus D_1[0] \oplus D_2[0] \oplus D_3[0] \oplus D_4[0]$ must equal `00000000`. ✓

---

### Question B — Option 2 (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 | Adapted]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
*Compare and contrast RAID 3, RAID 4, and RAID 5 in terms of (i) striping granularity, (ii) parity disk arrangement, (iii) small-write performance, and (iv) spindle synchronization requirement.*

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | RAID 3 | RAID 4 | RAID 5 |
|---|---|---|---|
| **(i) Striping granularity** | 1 byte (byte-level) | 1 block / sector (block-level) | 1 block / sector (block-level) |
| **(ii) Parity disk arrangement** | 1 **dedicated** parity disk for entire array | 1 **dedicated** parity disk for entire array | **Distributed** parity — parity blocks rotate across all disks |
| **(iii) Small-write performance** | Poor (must read $N$+1 old bytes) | **Worst** (Read-Modify-Write + dedicated parity bottleneck) | Better than RAID 4 (no bottleneck disk) |
| **(iv) Spindle sync required** | **Yes (mandatory)** | No | No |

**Detailed discussion (7 marks distributed across the four features):**

1. **Striping granularity (2 marks):** RAID 3 uses the **smallest** striping unit (a single byte), which maximizes parallel throughput for sequential workloads but destroys locality — a single 4 KB file access touches 4096 different sectors. RAID 4 and RAID 5 use **block-level** striping (typically 32 KB–256 KB), so a 4 KB access stays on one disk.

2. **Parity disk arrangement (2 marks):** RAID 3 and RAID 4 both use a **single dedicated parity disk**, which becomes a write bottleneck for random small writes because every write must update that one disk. RAID 5 eliminates this by **rotating** the parity location across disks, so the parity I/O is distributed.

3. **Small-write performance (2 marks):** RAID 3 incurs a small-write cost of $N+1$ reads + $2$ writes (because of byte-level granularity forcing a full stripe read). RAID 4 incurs a Read-Modify-Write cost of 2 reads + 2 writes per small write. RAID 5 is similar to RAID 4 in cost (2 reads + 2 writes) but **distributes the parity writes**, so it avoids the bottleneck.

4. **Spindle synchronization (1 mark):** RAID 3 is the **only** level that requires synchronized spindles because of byte-level striping. RAID 4 and RAID 5 use block-level striping, so disks can spin independently — they are far more practical for general-purpose servers.

**Conclusion:** RAID 3 is optimal for **sequential, high-throughput** workloads; RAID 4 is largely obsolete due to its parity bottleneck; RAID 5 is the workhorse of modern general-purpose arrays.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
*A RAID 3 array uses 4 data disks and 1 parity disk. The first 4 bytes on the data disks are: $D_0[0]=11001100$, $D_1[0]=10101010$, $D_2[0]=11110000$, $D_3[0]=00001111$.*
*(i) Compute the parity byte $P[0]$.*
*(ii) Suppose disk $D_2$ fails. Reconstruct $D_2[0]$ using the surviving disks and the parity byte.*
*(iii) Verify your reconstruction by showing that XOR-ing all 5 bytes (4 reconstructed data + 1 parity) yields 00000000.*

**Model Answer:**

**Part (i) — Parity Computation (2 marks):**

$$P[0] = 11001100 \oplus 10101010 \oplus 11110000 \oplus 00001111$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: } & 11001100 \oplus 10101010 = 01100110 \\
\text{Step 2: } & 01100110 \oplus 11110000 = 10010110 \\
\text{Step 3: } & 10010110 \oplus 00001111 = 10011001 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{P[0] = 10011001}$$

**Part (ii) — Reconstruction (3 marks):**

When $D_2$ fails, the controller uses:

$$D_2[0] = P[0] \oplus D_0[0] \oplus D_1[0] \oplus D_3[0]$$

$$D_2[0] = 10011001 \oplus 11001100 \oplus 10101010 \oplus 00001111$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: } & 10011001 \oplus 11001100 = 01010101 \\
\text{Step 2: } & 01010101 \oplus 10101010 = 11111111 \\
\text{Step 3: } & 11111111 \oplus 00001111 = 11110000 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{D_2[0] = 11110000}$$

This exactly matches the original value stored on $D_2$, confirming correct reconstruction. ✓

**Part (iii) — Verification (2 marks):**

Now XOR all 5 bytes — the 4 reconstructed data bytes (with $D_2$ replaced by the recovered value) plus the parity byte:

$$
\begin{aligned}
& 11001100 \oplus 10101010 \oplus 11110000 \oplus 00001111 \oplus 10011001 \\
& = P[0] \oplus P[0] \quad \text{(since } P[0] = D_0 \oplus D_1 \oplus D_2 \oplus D_3 \text{ by definition)} \\
& = 00000000 \;\; \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

The XOR of all 5 bytes equals zero, confirming that the parity relation is preserved after reconstruction. The array is once again fault-tolerant.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students LOSE marks on RAID 3 questions — and how to AVOID it:**
>
> 1. **Confusing OR with XOR (–2 marks):** The parity operator is **XOR**, not OR. The notation is $P = D_0 \oplus D_1 \oplus \dots$, never $P = D_0 \mid D_1 \mid \dots$. Writing the wrong operator makes the parity and reconstruction *both* wrong.
>
> 2. **Forgetting that the parity disk counts toward raw but NOT usable capacity (–1 to –2 marks):** A 5-disk RAID 3 with 600 GB disks has 3,000 GB raw and **2,400 GB usable**, not 3,000 GB usable. Many students write "3 TB total" without clarifying which capacity.
>
> 3. **Ignoring synchronized spindles (–1 mark):** A KTU question asking "state TWO disadvantages of RAID 3" almost always expects synchronized spindles as one of them. If your answer lists only "parity bottleneck" and misses the spindle requirement, you have already lost 1 mark.
>
> 4. **Mixing up RAID 3 with RAID 4 striping units (–2 marks):** RAID 3 is **byte-level**; RAID 4 is **block-level**. Examiners specifically test this distinction.
>
> 5. **Not showing the verification XOR (–1 mark):** In parity-based questions, KTU examiners reward students who verify their answer by XOR-ing all values and confirming the result is zero. Always include this self-check.
>
> 6. **Treating RAID 3 as a "general-purpose" RAID (conceptual loss):** RAID 3 is **specialized** for sequential workloads. A common essay-style pitfall is to claim RAID 3 is universally applicable, which loses marks on comparison questions.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Print this checklist. Memorize it. Ace the exam.**

- [x] **Definition:** RAID 3 = **byte-level striping** + **1 dedicated parity disk** + **synchronized spindles**.
- [x] **Minimum disks:** **3** (2 data + 1 parity); practical minimum **5**.
- [x] **Fault tolerance:** **1 disk** failure (XOR parity).
- [x] **Parity formula:** $P[i] = D_0[i] \oplus D_1[i] \oplus \dots \oplus D_{N-1}[i]$.
- [x] **Reconstruction:** $D_j[i] = P[i] \oplus \bigoplus_{k \neq j} D_k[i]$.
- [x] **Usable capacity:** $N \times C_{\text{disk}}$.
- [x] **Raw capacity:** $(N+1) \times C_{\text{disk}}$.
- [x] **Storage efficiency:** $\dfrac{N}{N+1}$ (e.g., 4/5 = 80% for 4 data + 1 parity).
- [x] **Best use case:** Large **sequential** reads and writes (video, HPC, imaging).
- [x] **Worst use case:** Small **random** I/O (databases, OLTP).
- [x] **Mandatory hardware feature:** **Synchronized spindle rotation** (rotational lock-step).
- [x] **Small-write cost:** $N+1$ reads + $2$ writes (no Read-Modify-Write shortcut, but seek cost is near zero due to sync).
- [x] **Full-stripe write cost:** $N+1$ writes (no reads needed).
- [x] **Sequential read throughput:** $N \times r_{\text{disk}}$ (parity disk not read).
- [x] **Key distinction from RAID 4:** Striping is **byte-level**, not block-level.
- [x] **Key distinction from RAID 5:** Parity is **dedicated** to one disk, not distributed.
- [x] **XOR is its own inverse:** $x \oplus x = 0$, $x \oplus 0 = x$ — this property is *the* reason RAID works.
- [x] **Modern status:** Largely superseded by RAID 5/6, but conceptually foundational.
- [x] **Verification trick:** XOR of all data + parity = `00000000`. Use this to sanity-check every answer.
- [x] **KTU cross-link:** The same XOR-parity logic underpins RAID 4, RAID 5, RAID 6, and many erasure-coding schemes — mastering RAID 3 means you have mastered the *core idea* of all XOR-based redundancy.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: RAID 4
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST867 - STORAGE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** RAID 4

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAID 4 (Redundant Array of Independent Disks – Level 4)** is a disk organization technique that employs **block-level data striping** across multiple physical disks combined with a **single dedicated parity disk** that stores error-correction information for an entire group of data disks. RAID 4 belongs to the family of fault-tolerant RAID architectures and is officially classified as a **Non-Rotated Parity Architecture**, where parity is calculated independently of disk rotation. It is formally defined by the *RAID Advisory Board* and standardized within the **SNIA Common RAID Disk Data Format (DDF)** specification.

In RAID 4, the array logically consists of two distinct disk groups:
- **Data Disks (D0, D1, D2, …, D(n-1))** – These store the actual user data striped at the **block (sector) level**.
- **Parity Disk (P)** – A single, dedicated disk that holds the **XOR-based parity** of all corresponding data blocks across the data disks.

A typical RAID 4 array with N data disks has a total of **N + 1 physical disks**, where one disk is entirely reserved for parity.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Class Notebook"**
> Imagine a classroom of 3 students (Data Disks D0, D1, D2) who are each responsible for writing one chapter of a long novel. To prevent the loss of any single chapter (a disk failure), the class appoints one "**Watchman Student**" (Parity Disk) who **does NOT write any chapter himself**, but instead maintains a running summary (parity) of what is being written by all other students.
> 
> - **Striping** = The novel is divided into equal-sized chunks (e.g., words or sentences), and each student takes a sequential chunk in rotation.
> - **Parity Computation** = After every set of 3 chunks (one from each student), the Watchman writes a "summary" that allows reconstructing any single missing chunk.
> - **Read Operation** = If a teacher wants chunk #5, the teacher knows exactly which student has it and reads it directly — **fast**.
> - **Write Operation** = If student 1 changes a chunk, the Watchman must ALSO update his summary — creating a bottleneck (the **Small Write Problem**).

The Watchman represents the **dedicated parity disk**, which is the defining architectural trait of RAID 4.

## 1.3 Key Terminology

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Stripe** | The complete set of data blocks across all data disks at the same logical offset. |
| **Strip / Stripe Unit** | The size of a single block on one disk (e.g., 64 KB, 128 KB). |
| **Stripe Width** | The number of data disks in the array. |
| **Dedicated Parity Disk** | A single physical disk that holds XOR parity for all data disks. |
| **Read-Modify-Write** | A write procedure where data is read, parity is recomputed, and both are rewritten. |
| **RAID Penalty** | Performance overhead required to maintain fault tolerance. |
| **MTTDL** | Mean Time To Data Loss – the statistical reliability metric. |

## 1.4 Mathematical Notation Standard

For a RAID 4 array with $N$ data disks $D_0, D_1, D_2, \dots, D_{N-1}$, the parity block $P$ at stripe offset $i$ is mathematically defined as:

$$P_i = D_{0,i} \oplus D_{1,i} \oplus D_{2,i} \oplus \cdots \oplus D_{N-1,i}$$

Where $\oplus$ denotes the **bitwise XOR (Exclusive OR)** operation, which is the standard parity primitive in all XOR-based RAID levels.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Architecture of RAID 4

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The defining architectural feature of RAID 4 is the **Dedicated Parity Disk** — every parity write/update for the entire array is directed to a **single physical disk**, regardless of which data disk is being modified.

### 2.1.1 Logical Layout

A RAID 4 array with $N$ data disks organizes data as follows:

- **Logical Disk View**: The system presents a single virtual disk to the host OS of size $N \times S$, where $S$ is the capacity of a single data disk.
- **Physical Layout**: $N$ data disks + $1$ dedicated parity disk = $N+1$ physical disks.
- **Usable Capacity**: $U = N \times C$, where $C$ is the capacity of an individual disk.
- **Storage Overhead**: One full disk's worth of space is reserved for parity, giving an overhead ratio of $\frac{1}{N+1}$.

### 2.1.2 Block-Level Striping Mechanism

Data is striped at the **block level** (also called **sector level** or **stripe unit level**). Each block is of fixed size $B$ (typically 64 KB, 128 KB, or 256 KB).

**Striping Algorithm**:
- Block 0 → Disk D0
- Block 1 → Disk D1
- Block 2 → Disk D2
- …
- Block N-1 → Disk D(n-1)
- Block N → Disk D0 (wrap around)
- …
- Parity for stripe $i$ → Disk P

For a logical block address (LBA) $L$, the destination disk index is computed as:

$$\text{DiskIndex} = L \bmod N$$

$$\text{OffsetWithinDisk} = \left\lfloor \frac{L}{N} \right\rfloor \times B$$

## 2.2 Read Operation in RAID 4

> [!NOTE]
> **Read operations in RAID 4 are identical in performance to RAID 0** because the dedicated parity disk is not involved in any read path.

### 2.2.1 Read Workflow

1. The RAID controller receives a read request for logical block $L$.
2. The controller computes the target data disk: $\text{DiskIndex} = L \bmod N$.
3. The controller issues a single physical read I/O to the identified data disk.
4. The block is returned to the host.

**Performance Characteristic**:
- **Read Latency**: $T_{\text{read}} = T_{\text{seek}} + T_{\text{rotational}} + T_{\text{transfer}}$
- **No Parity Disk Access**: Parity disk remains idle during reads.
- **No Read Penalty**: Pure single-disk read performance.

## 2.3 Write Operation in RAID 4

> [!WARNING]
> **RAID 4 suffers from the famous "Small Write Problem"** — every write operation requires accessing the dedicated parity disk, which becomes a serialization bottleneck for write-heavy workloads.

### 2.3.1 Full-Stripe Write (Optimal Case)

In a full-stripe write, the controller writes data to **all** data disks in a stripe simultaneously and then computes and writes the parity.

$$P_{\text{new}} = D_{0,\text{new}} \oplus D_{1,\text{new}} \oplus \cdots \oplus D_{N-1,\text{new}}$$

**I/O Cost**: $N + 1$ disk writes (N data + 1 parity).

### 2.3.2 Small Write (Read-Modify-Write)

For a small write that modifies only one block on one data disk:

1. **Read old data block** $D_{\text{old}}$ from the target data disk.
2. **Read old parity block** $P_{\text{old}}$ from the parity disk.
3. **Compute new parity** using the recurrence relation:

$$P_{\text{new}} = D_{\text{old}} \oplus P_{\text{old}} \oplus D_{\text{new}}$$

4. **Write new data block** $D_{\text{new}}$ to the target data disk.
5. **Write new parity block** $P_{\text{new}}$ to the parity disk.

**I/O Cost**: **2 reads + 2 writes = 4 I/O operations per small write** — this is the **RAID 4 Small Write Penalty**.

### 2.3.3 Parity Recurrence Derivation

Starting from the parity definition:

$$P_{\text{old}} = D_{\text{old}} \oplus \text{Other Blocks}$$

$$\text{Other Blocks} = P_{\text{old}} \oplus D_{\text{old}}$$

When $D_{\text{old}}$ is replaced with $D_{\text{new}}$:

$$P_{\text{new}} = D_{\text{new}} \oplus \text{Other Blocks} = D_{\text{new}} \oplus (P_{\text{old}} \oplus D_{\text{old}})$$

$$\boxed{P_{\text{new}} = P_{\text{old}} \oplus D_{\text{old}} \oplus D_{\text{new}}}$$

## 2.4 Failure Recovery and Reconstruction

### 2.4.1 Single Disk Failure Tolerance

RAID 4 can tolerate the failure of **any one disk** (data or parity). When a disk fails, the array operates in **degraded mode**.

### 2.4.2 Reconstruction Procedure

Suppose disk $D_k$ fails. To reconstruct block $i$ of $D_k$:

$$D_{k,i} = P_i \oplus \bigoplus_{j=0, j \neq k}^{N-1} D_{j,i}$$

This requires reading from all $N$ surviving data disks and the parity disk, then performing XOR to recover the lost data.

**Reconstruction Cost**:
- **Read I/Os**: 1 (parity) + $(N-1)$ (surviving data disks) per reconstructed block
- **Write I/Os**: 1 (reconstructed block written to spare disk)
- **Total**: $N+1$ I/Os per block reconstruction

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Cheat Sheet — RAID 4 Critical Formulas**

| Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Total Physical Disks | $N + 1$ | disks | N data + 1 dedicated parity |
| Usable Capacity | $U = N \times C$ | Bytes | Excludes parity disk |
| Storage Efficiency | $\eta = \frac{N}{N+1}$ | ratio (0 to 1) | Always < 1 |
| Storage Overhead | $\frac{1}{N+1}$ | ratio | Parity disk fraction |
| Read I/O per Request | $1$ | I/O operation | No parity access |
| Small Write I/Os | $4$ | I/O operations | Read-Modify-Write |
| Full-Stripe Write I/Os | $N+1$ | I/O operations | All disks written |
| Parity Computation | $P = \bigoplus_{i=0}^{N-1} D_i$ | XOR | Bitwise |
| Incremental Parity | $P_{\text{new}} = P_{\text{old}} \oplus D_{\text{old}} \oplus D_{\text{new}}$ | XOR | Used in small writes |
| MTTDL Approximation | $\frac{1}{N \cdot \lambda^2 \cdot MTTR}$ | hours | For 1-disk tolerance |
| Fault Tolerance | $1$ disk | disks | Single parity group |
| Stripe Unit Disk Index | $L \bmod N$ | index | 0 to N-1 |
| Array Throughput (Read) | $N \times R_{\text{disk}}$ | MB/s | Sequential reads parallel |
| Array Throughput (Write) | $\frac{N}{4} \times R_{\text{disk}}$ | MB/s | Small writes penalized |

Where:
- $C$ = Capacity of a single physical disk
- $N$ = Number of data disks
- $L$ = Logical Block Address
- $\lambda$ = Disk failure rate (failures/hour)
- $MTTR$ = Mean Time To Repair
- $R_{\text{disk}}$ = Single disk transfer rate

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> RAID 4 was historically implemented in enterprise storage systems by vendors such as **NetApp (WAFL/Data ONTAP)** and in early **hardware RAID controllers from Compaq, HP, and IBM**. Although largely superseded by RAID 5 and RAID 6 in modern deployments, RAID 4 remains a critical academic reference point in KTU's Storage Systems curriculum because it introduces students to:
> 
> 1. The concept of **rotated vs. non-rotated parity**.
> 2. The fundamental **XOR parity computation**.
> 3. The **small write problem** that motivates RAID 5's distributed parity design.
> 4. The **trade-off between capacity efficiency and write performance**.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Storage Efficiency Formula

### Problem Statement
Derive the storage efficiency $\eta$ of a RAID 4 array containing $N$ data disks of equal capacity $C$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Identify Total Raw Capacity**

The array uses $N$ data disks of capacity $C$ each, plus $1$ dedicated parity disk of the same capacity $C$.

$$C_{\text{total}} = (N+1) \times C$$

**Step 2 — Identify Usable Capacity**

The host OS only sees the $N$ data disks because the parity disk is reserved for redundancy and is not exposed as user-addressable storage.

$$C_{\text{usable}} = N \times C$$

**Step 3 — Define Storage Efficiency**

Storage efficiency is the ratio of usable capacity to total raw capacity.

$$\eta = \frac{C_{\text{usable}}}{C_{\text{total}}}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute**

$$\eta = \frac{N \times C}{(N+1) \times C}$$

**Step 5 — Simplify**

$$\boxed{\eta = \frac{N}{N+1}}$$

### Numerical Example

For $N = 4$ data disks of 1 TB each:

$$\eta = \frac{4}{5} = 0.8 = 80\%$$

This means **20% of the raw capacity is consumed by parity overhead**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Small Write Parity Recurrence

### Problem Statement
Derive the incremental parity update formula used in RAID 4's Read-Modify-Write procedure.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Write the Original Parity Equation**

For a stripe of $N$ data blocks $D_0, D_1, \dots, D_{N-1}$ and parity block $P$:

$$P_{\text{old}} = D_{0,\text{old}} \oplus D_{1,\text{old}} \oplus \cdots \oplus D_{k,\text{old}} \oplus \cdots \oplus D_{N-1,\text{old}}$$

**Step 2 — Isolate the XOR Sum of "Other" Disks**

Define $S = $ XOR sum of all data blocks except block $k$:

$$S = \bigoplus_{i=0, i \neq k}^{N-1} D_{i,\text{old}}$$

Therefore:

$$P_{\text{old}} = S \oplus D_{k,\text{old}}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $S$**

Apply XOR with $D_{k,\text{old}}$ to both sides:

$$S = P_{\text{old}} \oplus D_{k,\text{old}}$$

(Using the XOR identity: $A \oplus A = 0$ and $A \oplus 0 = A$.)

**Step 4 — Write the New Parity Equation**

After block $k$ is updated to $D_{k,\text{new}}$, the new parity is:

$$P_{\text{new}} = S \oplus D_{k,\text{new}}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $S$**

$$P_{\text{new}} = (P_{\text{old}} \oplus D_{k,\text{old}}) \oplus D_{k,\text{new}}$$

**Step 6 — Reorder (XOR is associative and commutative)**

$$\boxed{P_{\text{new}} = P_{\text{old}} \oplus D_{k,\text{old}} \oplus D_{k,\text{new}}}$$

This is the **fundamental incremental parity update** that allows RAID 4 to compute new parity without reading all other data blocks — saving $N-2$ I/O operations.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: MTTDL (Mean Time To Data Loss)

### Problem Statement
Derive the MTTDL for a RAID 4 array with $N$ data disks + 1 parity disk, where each disk has failure rate $\lambda$ (failures per hour) and the mean time to repair is $MTTR$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define Data Loss Condition**

Data loss in RAID 4 occurs when **any two disks fail before the first failure is repaired**, because the parity mechanism can only tolerate a single disk failure.

**Step 2 — MTTDL of a Single Disk**

For an exponential failure distribution:

$$MTTF_{\text{disk}} = \frac{1}{\lambda}$$

**Step 3 — Probability of a Second Failure During Repair**

During the repair window $MTTR$, the probability of a second disk failing among the remaining $N$ disks is approximately:

$$P_{\text{2nd failure}} \approx N \cdot \lambda \cdot MTTR$$

(Assuming $N$ remaining disks and small $\lambda \cdot MTTR$.)

**Step 4 — Apply Markov Model (Series Failure)**

The rate of data loss is the rate of first failure multiplied by the rate of second failure during repair:

$$\lambda_{\text{data loss}} \approx N \cdot \lambda \cdot (N \cdot \lambda \cdot MTTR)$$

Wait — this needs careful interpretation. The data loss rate is:

$$\lambda_{\text{data loss}} \approx N \cdot \lambda \cdot \lambda \cdot MTTR = N \cdot \lambda^2 \cdot MTTR$$

**Step 5 — MTTDL is the Reciprocal**

$$\boxed{MTTDL_{\text{RAID4}} \approx \frac{1}{N \cdot \lambda^2 \cdot MTTR}}$$

**Step 6 — Numerical Validation**

Given:
- $N = 5$
- $\lambda = 1/100000$ per hour (MTTF = 100,000 hours ≈ 11.4 years)
- $MTTR = 24$ hours

$$MTTDL = \frac{1}{5 \times (1/100000)^2 \times 24}$$

$$MTTDL = \frac{1}{5 \times 10^{-10} \times 24} = \frac{1}{1.2 \times 10^{-8}}$$

$$MTTDL \approx 8.33 \times 10^7 \text{ hours} \approx 9500 \text{ years}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Performance Comparison

| Operation | I/O Operations | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Sequential Read** | $1$ per block | Best case — no parity access |
| **Random Read** | $1$ per request | Identical to RAID 0 |
| **Full-Stripe Write** | $N+1$ | All data disks + parity disk |
| **Small Random Write** | $4$ | **Small Write Penalty** |
| **Read During Degraded Mode** | $1$ (data) + $1$ (parity) | Reconstructed on-the-fly |
| **Reconstruction Read** | $N$ (surviving) + $1$ (parity) | Spans all data + parity |
| **Reconstruction Write** | $1$ (spare) | To hot spare disk |

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example

### Problem
A RAID 4 array has 4 data disks + 1 parity disk. Each disk is 500 GB. A user writes 128 KB of data to disk D2 at LBA 100. Compute:
1. The destination disk and offset.
2. The total number of I/O operations.
3. The new parity value given old data, old parity, and new data.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Strip Size**: Let strip size $B = 128$ KB = 131072 bytes.

**Step 2 — Destination Disk**:

$$\text{DiskIndex} = L \bmod N = 100 \bmod 4 = 0$$

So data goes to **D0** (D0 = disk index 0 in the array). Wait — using 0-based indexing where disks are D0, D1, D2, D3:

$$100 \bmod 4 = 0 \Rightarrow \text{Target disk} = D_0$$

**Step 3 — Offset**:

$$\text{Offset} = \lfloor 100 / 4 \rfloor \times 128\text{ KB} = 25 \times 128\text{ KB} = 3200\text{ KB}$$

**Step 4 — I/O Operations** (small write):
- 1 read of $D_{0,\text{old}}$
- 1 read of $P_{\text{old}}$
- 1 write of $D_{0,\text{new}}$
- 1 write of $P_{\text{new}}$

$$\text{Total I/Os} = 4$$

**Step 5 — New Parity** (suppose $D_{0,\text{old}} = 0xA3$, $P_{\text{old}} = 0x5F$, $D_{0,\text{new}} = 0x7B$):

$$P_{\text{new}} = 0x5F \oplus 0xA3 \oplus 0x7B$$

In binary:
- $0x5F = 0101\ 1111$
- $0xA3 = 1010\ 0011$
- $0x7B = 0111\ 1011$

First XOR:
$$0x5F \oplus 0xA3 = 1111\ 1100 = 0xFC$$

Then:
$$0xFC \oplus 0x7B = 1000\ 0111 = 0x87$$

$$\boxed{P_{\text{new}} = 0x87}$$

flowchart TD

## 4.4 RAID 4 Small Write Flow (Read-Modify-Write)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start(["Host Issues Small Write"])
    Step1["Step 1: Read Old Data Dold from Dk"]
    Step2["Step 2: Read Old Parity Pold from Parity Disk"]
    Step3["Step 3: Compute Pnew = Pold XOR Dold XOR Dnew"]
    Step4["Step 4: Write New Data Dnew to Dk"]
    Step5["Step 5: Write New Parity Pnew to Parity Disk"]
    End(["Small Write Complete - 4 I/O Operations"])
    
    Start --> Step1
    Step1 --> Step2
    Step2 --> Step3
    Step3 --> Step4
    Step4 --> Step5
    Step5 --> End
```

## 4.5 RAID 4 Failure Recovery Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start(["Disk Dk Fails"])
    Detect["Controller Detects Failure via SMART or Timeout"]
    Degraded["Array Enters DEGRADED Mode"]
    ReadOp["Read Request for Dk: Compute Dk = P XOR Sum of Surviving Disks"]
    Reconstruct["Background Reconstruction: XOR All Surviving Blocks and Parity"]
    WriteSpare["Write Reconstructed Data to Hot Spare Disk"]
    Rebuild["Array Returns to NORMAL Mode"]
    End(["Array Restored"])
    
    Start --> Detect
    Detect --> Degraded
    Degraded --> ReadOp
    Degraded --> Reconstruct
    Reconstruct --> WriteSpare
    WriteSpare --> Rebuild
    Rebuild --> End
```

## 4.7 Architecture Summary Table

| Component | Count | Role | Bottleneck? |
|---|---|---|---|
| Data Disks | $N$ | Store user data striped at block level | No |
| Parity Disk | $1$ | Stores XOR parity for entire array | **YES** |
| Total Disks | $N+1$ | Physical array | — |
| Usable Disks | $N$ | Visible to host | — |
| Read Path Disks | $1$ per request | Any single data disk | No |
| Write Path Disks | $2$ (data + parity) | Always includes parity disk | **YES** |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define RAID 4. Explain its key architectural feature in 3 sentences.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **RAID 4 (Redundant Array of Independent Disks – Level 4)** is a disk organization technique that uses **block-level striping** across multiple data disks with a **single dedicated parity disk** for fault tolerance.
> 
> **Key Architectural Feature**: The defining characteristic of RAID 4 is the use of one **dedicated (non-rotated) parity disk** that stores XOR parity for all data disks in the array, while data is striped at the block level across the remaining disks.
> 
> RAID 4 can tolerate the failure of any one disk and uses XOR-based parity for data reconstruction, but it suffers from the **small write problem** due to contention at the single parity disk.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is the "Small Write Problem" in RAID 4? Why does it occur?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> The **Small Write Problem** in RAID 4 refers to the performance degradation that occurs during small or random write operations, where each write requires **4 I/O operations** (2 reads + 2 writes) instead of a single write.
> 
> **Reason**: It occurs because every write in RAID 4 must update the **dedicated parity disk**, regardless of which data disk is being modified. The controller must read the old data and old parity, compute the new parity using $P_{\text{new}} = P_{\text{old}} \oplus D_{\text{old}} \oplus D_{\text{new}}$, and write both the new data and new parity. Since the parity disk is a single shared resource, it becomes a **serialization bottleneck** for concurrent writes.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Question:** Explain the architecture of RAID 4 in detail. With a neat diagram, describe the read, write, and reconstruction operations. Compare RAID 4 with RAID 5.

#### Part (a) — Architecture and Read/Write Operations (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**1. RAID 4 Architecture (3 Marks):**

RAID 4 consists of $N$ data disks and **1 dedicated parity disk**, making a total of $N+1$ physical disks. Data is striped at the **block level** across the data disks, with each strip typically 64 KB or 128 KB in size. The parity disk stores the **bitwise XOR** of corresponding data blocks across all data disks:

$$P_i = D_{0,i} \oplus D_{1,i} \oplus \cdots \oplus D_{N-1,i}$$

**2. Read Operation (2 Marks):**

A read request is served by a **single data disk**. The RAID controller computes the target disk as $L \bmod N$ and reads the block directly. The parity disk is **not accessed**, giving RAID 4 read performance identical to RAID 0.

**3. Write Operation (2 Marks):**

For a small write (single block update), the controller performs **Read-Modify-Write**:
- Read $D_{\text{old}}$ and $P_{\text{old}}$
- Compute $P_{\text{new}} = P_{\text{old}} \oplus D_{\text{old}} \oplus D_{\text{new}}$
- Write $D_{\text{new}}$ and $P_{\text{new}}$

This requires **4 I/O operations per small write**, causing the small write problem.

#### Part (b) — Reconstruction and Comparison with RAID 5 (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**1. Reconstruction (3 Marks):**

When any disk $D_k$ fails, RAID 4 enters **degraded mode**. To reconstruct block $i$ of $D_k$:

$$D_{k,i} = P_i \oplus \bigoplus_{j=0, j \neq k}^{N-1} D_{j,i}$$

This requires reading from $N-1$ surviving data disks and the parity disk, then XORing to recover the lost block, which is written to a **hot spare disk**. Reconstruction time is approximately:

$$T_{\text{rebuild}} = \frac{C \times (N-1)}{R_{\text{disk}}}$$

**2. Comparison with RAID 5 (4 Marks):**

| Parameter | RAID 4 | RAID 5 |
|---|---|---|
| Parity Location | Single dedicated disk | Distributed across all disks |
| Parity Rotation | None (non-rotated) | Rotated across stripes |
| Write Bottleneck | **Yes** — parity disk | **No** — distributed |
| Small Write Penalty | 4 I/Os, with parity disk contention | 4 I/Os, but no single bottleneck |
| Read Performance | Same as RAID 0 | Same as RAID 0 |
| Minimum Disks | 3 | 3 |
| Random Write Performance | Poor | Better than RAID 4 |
| Controller Complexity | Lower | Higher (needs parity rotation logic) |

**Conclusion**: RAID 5 is generally preferred over RAID 4 in modern systems because the distributed parity eliminates the single-disk bottleneck, improving random write performance.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` (Alternative Choice)

**Question:** Discuss the parity computation mechanism in RAID 4. With an example, explain the Read-Modify-Write procedure for small writes. Calculate the storage efficiency for a RAID 4 array with 6 data disks of 2 TB each.

#### Part (a) — Parity Computation and Read-Modify-Write (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**1. Parity Computation (3 Marks):**

RAID 4 uses **XOR-based parity** stored on a single dedicated disk. For a stripe containing data blocks $D_0, D_1, \dots, D_{N-1}$:

$$P = D_0 \oplus D_1 \oplus D_2 \oplus \cdots \oplus D_{N-1}$$

The XOR operation has key properties that make it ideal for parity:
- $A \oplus A = 0$
- $A \oplus 0 = A$
- XOR is associative and commutative

These properties enable **lossless recovery** when any single disk is lost.

**2. Read-Modify-Write Example (4 Marks):**

Consider a RAID 4 array with 4 data disks. Suppose the user updates block 3 of disk D1.

| Step | Operation | I/O Type |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Read old data $D_{1,\text{old}}$ from D1 | 1 Read |
| 2 | Read old parity $P_{\text{old}}$ from parity disk | 1 Read |
| 3 | Compute $P_{\text{new}} = P_{\text{old}} \oplus D_{1,\text{old}} \oplus D_{1,\text{new}}$ | (In-memory) |
| 4 | Write $D_{1,\text{new}}$ to D1 | 1 Write |
| 5 | Write $P_{\text{new}}$ to parity disk | 1 Write |
| **Total** | | **4 I/Os** |

**Numerical Illustration**:
- $P_{\text{old}} = 0x5F$ (0101 1111)
- $D_{1,\text{old}} = 0xA3$ (1010 0011)
- $D_{1,\text{new}} = 0x7B$ (0111 1011)

Compute:
$$P_{\text{new}} = 0x5F \oplus 0xA3 \oplus 0x7B = 0x87 \text{ (1000 0111)}$$

#### Part (b) — Storage Efficiency Calculation (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Given**:
- Number of data disks $N = 6$
- Capacity of each disk $C = 2$ TB

**Step 1 — Total Physical Disks**:
$$N + 1 = 6 + 1 = 7 \text{ disks}$$

**Step 2 — Total Raw Capacity**:
$$C_{\text{total}} = (N+1) \times C = 7 \times 2\text{ TB} = 14\text{ TB}$$

**Step 3 — Usable Capacity**:
$$C_{\text{usable}} = N \times C = 6 \times 2\text{ TB} = 12\text{ TB}$$

**Step 4 — Storage Efficiency**:
$$\eta = \frac{C_{\text{usable}}}{C_{\text{total}}} = \frac{12\text{ TB}}{14\text{ TB}}$$

$$\boxed{\eta = \frac{6}{7} \approx 0.8571 = 85.71\%}$$

**Step 5 — Storage Overhead**:
$$\text{Overhead} = \frac{1}{N+1} = \frac{1}{7} \approx 14.29\%$$

**Conclusion**: A RAID 4 array with 6 data disks of 2 TB each provides 12 TB of usable storage with 85.71% efficiency, sacrificing 2 TB (one disk's worth) for fault tolerance.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing RAID 4 with RAID 5** (3-mark penalty): Students frequently describe RAID 4 as having "distributed parity." RAID 4 has a **DEDICATED** parity disk; RAID 5 has **ROTATED/DISTRIBUTED** parity. This is a critical distinction.
> 
> 2. **Wrong I/O Count for Small Writes** (2-mark penalty): Many students answer "2 I/Os" for small writes (one for data, one for parity). The correct answer is **4 I/Os** (2 reads + 2 writes) due to Read-Modify-Write.
> 
> 3. **Forgetting XOR Properties in Reconstruction** (1-mark penalty): During reconstruction, students sometimes write $D_k = P - \text{Others}$, treating it as subtraction. Always use **XOR**, not arithmetic subtraction.
> 
> 4. **Confusing Strip Size with Stripe Size** (1-mark penalty): Strip = single block on one disk. Stripe = full set of blocks across all data disks for one logical offset.
> 
> 5. **Missing the "Small Write Problem"** (2-mark penalty): When asked about RAID 4 disadvantages, always mention the **small write problem** AND the **parity disk bottleneck**. Partial answers lose 2 marks.
> 
> 6. **Calculation Errors in Storage Efficiency** (1-mark penalty): Use the correct denominator $N+1$, not $N$. RAID 4 has $N$ data disks + 1 parity disk, so efficiency is $\frac{N}{N+1}$.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for RAID 4 — KTU 2024 Scheme**
> 
> - **Architecture**: $N$ data disks + **1 dedicated parity disk** = $N+1$ total disks.
> - **Striping**: Performed at the **block level** (block-level striping), not byte level.
> - **Parity**: Computed using **XOR** operation across all data disks at the same stripe offset.
> - **Parity Formula**: $P = D_0 \oplus D_1 \oplus \cdots \oplus D_{N-1}$.
> - **Incremental Parity**: $P_{\text{new}} = P_{\text{old}} \oplus D_{\text{old}} \oplus D_{\text{new}}$.
> - **Read I/Os**: 1 per request (parity disk NOT accessed during reads).
> - **Small Write I/Os**: 4 per request (Read-Modify-Write: 2 reads + 2 writes).
> - **Full-Stripe Write I/Os**: $N+1$ (N data + 1 parity written simultaneously).
> - **Fault Tolerance**: 1 disk failure (single parity redundancy).
> - **Storage Efficiency**: $\eta = \frac{N}{N+1}$ (e.g., 80% for 4 data disks).
> - **Reconstruction Formula**: $D_{k,\text{lost}} = P \oplus \bigoplus_{j \neq k} D_j$.
> - **MTTDL Formula**: $MTTDL \approx \frac{1}{N \cdot \lambda^2 \cdot MTTR}$.
> - **Small Write Problem**: Caused by **single dedicated parity disk** being a serialization bottleneck.
> - **Key Distinction from RAID 5**: RAID 4 = non-rotated dedicated parity; RAID 5 = rotated/distributed parity.
> - **Minimum Disks**: 3 (at least 2 data + 1 parity).
> - **Use Cases Today**: Largely historical/academic; RAID 5 preferred in practice.
> - **Standard Strip Sizes**: 64 KB, 128 KB, or 256 KB per block.
> - **Stripe Width**: Number of data disks in the array.
> - **XOR Properties to Remember**: $A \oplus A = 0$, $A \oplus 0 = A$, associative, commutative.
> - **Exam Mantra**: "Block striping + dedicated parity = RAID 4"

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: RAID 5
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST867 - STORAGE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** RAID 5

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# RAID 5 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**RAID 5** (Redundant Array of Independent Disks, Level 5) is a disk configuration that employs **block-level data striping** with **distributed parity** across all member disks in the array. It is the most widely deployed RAID level in enterprise and mid-range storage systems because it offers a balanced trade-off between **performance**, **storage efficiency**, **fault tolerance**, and **cost**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECST867 — Module 1):**
> RAID 5 is explicitly listed as a core sub-topic under "Introduction to RAID, its levels and Applications." Students are expected to explain the striping mechanism, distributed parity calculation, fault tolerance, and performance characteristics.

The defining attributes of RAID 5, in formal terminology, are:

- **Striping Unit (Stripe Width):** A fixed-size block (typically **64 KB**, **128 KB**, or **256 KB**) written sequentially across the disks in a round-robin manner.
- **Distributed Parity:** Unlike RAID 4 which stores parity on a *dedicated* disk, RAID 5 **rotates the parity block** across all disks, eliminating the parity-disk bottleneck.
- **Single-Disk Fault Tolerance:** The array can survive the failure of exactly **one disk** without data loss. A second simultaneous failure results in unrecoverable data loss.
- **Minimum Disk Count:** **3 disks** (N ≥ 3).
- **Usable Capacity:** **(N − 1) × S**, where *N* is the number of disks and *S* is the size of the smallest disk.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Group Project Notebook" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Builder:** Imagine a study group of **5 friends** who decide to share a single notebook. Every day, one friend writes the full day's notes, and on the next day, the *next friend* in line takes over. The catch — *no friend trusts the others to keep the notebook safe* — so on each page, the assigned writer also adds a **summary page** that says: *"Sum of key points written by the previous four friends."* If any one friend's pages are lost (torn out, spilled coffee, etc.), the summary page written on the *next* friend's section allows full reconstruction of the lost work. The summary (parity) is **rotated** to a different friend every day, so no single friend is overburdened with the extra work of always creating summaries. **This is exactly how RAID 5 works.**

In this analogy:
- **Friends** → Physical disks in the array.
- **Daily notes** → Data blocks (stripe units).
- **Summary page (parity)** → XOR-based parity block.
- **Rotation of summary duty** → *Distributed Parity* (the hallmark of RAID 5).
- **Losing one friend's pages** → Failure of one disk.
- **Reconstructing from the summary** → Array rebuild using parity.

## 1.3 Physical Constants, Standard Metrics & Reserved Symbols

The following metrics are **standardized** in the RAID 5 literature and must be memorized for KTU examinations:

| Symbol / Term | Standard Value / Unit | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| $N$ | Integer, $N \geq 3$ | Number of disks in the array |
| $S$ | Bytes (GB/TB) | Capacity of the smallest disk |
| $C_{usable}$ | Bytes (GB/TB) | Effective usable capacity |
| $P$ | Bytes | Parity block size (equal to stripe unit size) |
| Stripe Width | 64 KB / 128 KB / 256 KB | Size of one stripe unit |
| Fault Tolerance | 1 disk | Maximum simultaneous disk failures tolerated |
| XOR ($\oplus$) | Boolean Operator | Bitwise parity function |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mandatory Bold Convention:** The minimum disk count of **3** and the usable capacity formula **(N − 1) × S** are the two most frequently tested values in KTU exams — always quote them in bold in written answers to attract the examiner's eye.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Architecture — The "Why" and "How"

RAID 5 is built on three coordinated mechanisms. Each is explained below with its logical purpose.

### Step 1 — Block-Level Striping
- **How:** Each incoming write is broken into fixed-size blocks called *stripe units*. Block 0 goes to Disk 1, Block 1 to Disk 2, ..., Block (N−1) to Disk N, and the next block wraps around to Disk 1 again.
- **Why:** Striping enables **parallel I/O** — large sequential reads/writes are split across all N disks, multiplying throughput by approximately N (in the best case for reads).

### Step 2 — Parity Generation (XOR)
- **How:** For every stripe row, one block is reserved for **parity**. The parity block is computed as the bitwise XOR of all the data blocks in that row.
- **Why:** XOR has a self-inverse property: $A \oplus B \oplus C \oplus P = 0 \implies P = A \oplus B \oplus C$. If any one block (data OR parity) is lost, the missing value can be mathematically reconstructed by XOR-ing the surviving blocks.

### Step 3 — Distributed Parity Rotation
- **How:** The parity block's disk location **rotates** from one stripe row to the next in a repeating pattern (often a left-symmetric or right-asymmetric schedule).
- **Why:** In RAID 4, parity lived on a single dedicated disk, creating a *write bottleneck* (every write required parity disk access). By rotating parity across all disks, RAID 5 **eliminates the dedicated parity bottleneck** and balances write load evenly.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for KTU Answers:** When explaining RAID 5 vs RAID 4, the *single* most important distinction to highlight is **distributed parity** vs **dedicated parity disk**. Examiners consistently award full marks only when this is explicitly stated.

## 2.2 The Read-Modify-Write Cycle (Write Penalty)

A naïve approach to writing in RAID 5 would require reading *all* data blocks, computing new parity, and writing all of them back. RAID 5 uses an optimized **Read-Modify-Write** sequence that touches only **2 data blocks + 1 parity block** per small random write. The steps are:

1. **Read old data** ($D_{old}$) from the target disk.
2. **Read old parity** ($P_{old}$) from its disk.
3. **Compute new parity:** $P_{new} = D_{old} \oplus D_{new} \oplus P_{old}$.
4. **Write new data** ($D_{new}$) to the target disk.
5. **Write new parity** ($P_{new}$) to the parity disk.

This is the **4 I/O operation per write** penalty that distinguishes RAID 5 from RAID 0 (which has no penalty) and RAID 1 (which has a 2 I/O penalty).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is **exam-ready**. Memorize every row. Replace vertical bars using `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid markdown table breakage.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Minimum disks | $N_{min} = 3$ | Integer, dimensionless |
| 2 | Usable capacity | $C_{usable} = (N - 1) \cdot S$ | GB / TB |
| 3 | Storage overhead (wasted) | $C_{overhead} = 1 \cdot S$ | Equivalent to 1 disk |
| 4 | Storage efficiency | $\eta = \frac{N - 1}{N}$ | Dimensionless ratio (0 < η < 1) |
| 5 | Fault tolerance | $f = 1$ disk | Cannot tolerate 2 failures |
| 6 | Read performance (ideal) | $R_{read} = N \cdot R_{single}$ | IOPS / MBps |
| 7 | Write performance (small random) | $W_{write} = \frac{R_{single}}{4}$ | Due to Read-Modify-Write |
| 8 | Write performance (large sequential) | $W_{seq} = N \cdot R_{single}$ | All disks written in parallel |
| 9 | Parity equation | $P_i = D_{i,1} \oplus D_{i,2} \oplus \ldots \oplus D_{i,N-1}$ | XOR of all data blocks in row *i* |
| 10 | Data reconstruction (disk *k* fails) | $D_{i,k} = \bigoplus_{j \neq k} B_{i,j}$ | XOR of all surviving blocks in row *i* |
| 11 | Mean Time To Data Loss (MTTDL) | $\text{MTTDL}_{RAID5} = \frac{\text{MTBF}_{disk}^2}{N(N-1) \cdot \text{MTTR}_{rebuild}}$ | Seconds (conceptual) |
| 12 | Write Penalty Factor | $\text{WPF} = 4$ | I/Os per logical write |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Enterprise File Servers & NAS:** RAID 5 dominates the Network Attached Storage (NAS) market for small-to-medium businesses because it provides fault tolerance without the 50 % storage penalty of RAID 1.
- **Database Servers (Read-Heavy):** Read parallelism makes RAID 5 suitable for OLAP, data warehousing, and archival systems.
- **Cold Storage & Backup Targets:** The write penalty is acceptable for write-once, read-many workloads.
- **Historical Application — Deprecated for SSDs:** Modern flash-based systems increasingly prefer **RAID 6** (dual parity) due to the higher rebuild failure rate on dense SSDs, but RAID 5 remains the **standard teaching reference** for distributed parity concepts and is heavily tested in KTU examinations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Note:** The syllabus is implementation-agnostic — students are evaluated on **logical understanding**, not specific vendor implementations (e.g., NetApp, Dell). Avoid citing vendor-specific terminology in theory answers.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> This section performs **exhaustive algebraic derivations** of the key RAID 5 formulas. No step is skipped. Every transition is justified in plain English so that even a first-time learner can follow the logic in an examination setting.

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Usable Capacity of a RAID 5 Array

We begin with the fundamental definition of usable capacity: it is the **sum of all storage that the user can actually write application data to**, *excluding* any space reserved for parity, redundancy, or filesystem metadata.

**Given:**
- An array of $N$ physical disks.
- Each disk has a formatted capacity of $S$ bytes.
- Each stripe row (horizontal slice across all N disks) contains exactly **one parity block** and $(N - 1)$ data blocks.

**Step 1:** Total raw capacity of the array.

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{raw} &= N \cdot S
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* Multiplying the number of disks by the per-disk capacity gives the gross physical storage.

**Step 2:** Identify the parity overhead per stripe.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Parity blocks per row} &= 1 \\
\text{Total rows (stripes)} &= \frac{C_{raw}}{N \cdot U} \quad \text{where } U = \text{stripe unit size}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* Each stripe row occupies one block on every disk, and exactly one of those blocks per row is parity.

**Step 3:** Calculate parity overhead in bytes.

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{parity} &= 1 \cdot S = S
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* The parity blocks collectively occupy the equivalent of **one full disk's** worth of capacity, regardless of $N$ (this is a key insight — see *Storage Overhead* in the cheat sheet).

**Step 4:** Subtract parity from raw capacity.

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{usable} &= C_{raw} - C_{parity} \\
C_{usable} &= N \cdot S - S \\
C_{usable} &= (N - 1) \cdot S
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{C_{usable} = (N - 1) \cdot S}
$$

> This is the canonical RAID 5 usable-capacity equation. Units: **bytes** (or GB/TB after appropriate scaling).

**Verification (Sanity Check):** For $N = 3$ and $S = 1$ TB, we get $C_{usable} = 2 \cdot 1 = 2$ TB. This matches the well-known fact that a 3-disk RAID 5 array yields "2 disks worth" of usable space — a common exam value.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Storage Efficiency $\eta$

Storage efficiency is the **fraction of raw capacity** that is available for user data.

**Step 1:** Begin with the usable capacity.

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{usable} &= (N - 1) \cdot S
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Divide by total raw capacity.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta &= \frac{C_{usable}}{C_{raw}} \\
\eta &= \frac{(N - 1) \cdot S}{N \cdot S}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Cancel $S$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta &= \frac{N - 1}{N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Express in limit form.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lim_{N \to \infty} \eta &= \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{N - 1}{N} = 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{\eta = \frac{N - 1}{N}, \quad \lim_{N \to \infty} \eta = 1}
$$

> As $N$ increases, RAID 5 approaches 100 % efficiency — but in practice, a *rebuild-time penalty* limits typical deployments to 5–12 disks.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — The Read-Modify-Write Equation for New Parity

This derivation proves why RAID 5 write operations require **4 I/Os** per logical write.

**Given:**
- $D_{old}$ = contents of the data block *before* the write.
- $D_{new}$ = contents of the data block *after* the write.
- $P_{old}$ = current parity block for the stripe row.
- $P_{new}$ = updated parity block for the stripe row.

**Step 1:** Write the definition of $P_{old}$ as the XOR of all data blocks in the row (including $D_{old}$).

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{old} &= D_{old} \oplus \sum_{j \neq \text{target}} D_j
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* Parity is the XOR of all data blocks in the stripe row, by definition.

**Step 2:** Write the *desired* new parity equation.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{new} &= D_{new} \oplus \sum_{j \neq \text{target}} D_j
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* The new parity should be the XOR of the *new* data plus all the *unchanged* data blocks in the same row.

**Step 3:** Compute $P_{old} \oplus P_{new}$ by XOR-ing Step 1 and Step 2.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{old} \oplus P_{new} &= \bigl( D_{old} \oplus \sum D_j \bigr) \oplus \bigl( D_{new} \oplus \sum D_j \bigr)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Apply the XOR identities ($X \oplus X = 0$, $X \oplus 0 = X$, associativity, commutativity).

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{old} \oplus P_{new} &= D_{old} \oplus D_{new} \oplus \underbrace{\bigl(\sum D_j \oplus \sum D_j\bigr)}_{= 0} \\
P_{old} \oplus P_{new} &= D_{old} \oplus D_{new}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Solve for $P_{new}$ by XOR-ing both sides with $P_{old}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{new} &= P_{old} \oplus D_{old} \oplus D_{new}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{P_{new} = P_{old} \oplus D_{old} \oplus D_{new}}
$$

> This is the **single most important equation** in RAID 5. It is the basis of the entire 4-I/O write penalty.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Data Reconstruction After Single-Disk Failure

**Given:** Disk $k$ has failed. We want to recover the data block $D_{i,k}$ on stripe row $i$.

**Step 1:** Recall the parity invariant for row $i$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_i \oplus D_{i,1} \oplus D_{i,2} \oplus \ldots \oplus D_{i,N-1} &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* By construction, the XOR of all data and parity blocks in a row equals zero (this is the definition of a valid stripe).

**Step 2:** Isolate $D_{i,k}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{i,k} &= P_i \oplus D_{i,1} \oplus D_{i,2} \oplus \ldots \oplus D_{i,k-1} \oplus D_{i,k+1} \oplus \ldots \oplus D_{i,N-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Use compact notation.

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{i,k} &= \bigoplus_{j \neq k} B_{i,j}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* The recovered data block is the XOR of the parity block and all surviving data blocks in the same stripe row.

**Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{D_{i,k} = \bigoplus_{j \neq k} B_{i,j}}
$$

> This formula is **operationally executed by the RAID controller** during rebuild. The reconstruction process is invisible to the host and runs as a background task.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Derivation # | Output Equation | Concept Tested | Marks Weightage (Typical) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 3.1 | $C_{usable} = (N - 1) \cdot S$ | Capacity calculation | 3–7 marks |
| 3.2 | $\eta = \frac{N - 1}{N}$ | Efficiency ratio | 2–3 marks |
| 3.3 | $P_{new} = P_{old} \oplus D_{old} \oplus D_{new}$ | Read-Modify-Write logic | 5–7 marks |
| 3.4 | $D_{i,k} = \bigoplus_{j \neq k} B_{i,j}$ | Disk-failure recovery | 3–5 marks |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Tip:** When asked "explain RAID 5 write operation," the examiner expects the **Read-Modify-Write sequence + the $P_{new}$ equation** together. Writing only one without the other attracts partial credit only.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> All diagrams below use **Mermaid** syntax. Node IDs are alphanumeric and prefixed with letters (e.g., `d1`, `par`) to comply with Mermaid's reserved-keyword restrictions. Labels are **plain uppercase text** with no markdown formatting inside double quotes.

## 4.1 RAID 5 Distributed Parity Layout (5-Disk Example)

The following diagram illustrates how data and parity blocks are striped across 5 disks, with parity rotating one position to the right on each successive stripe row (this is a common **left-asymmetric** parity rotation scheme).

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph STRIPE0[STRIPE ROW 0]
        d0a["D0 - DISK 0"]:::data
        d1a["D1 - DISK 1"]:::data
        d2a["D2 - DISK 2"]:::data
        d3a["D3 - DISK 3"]:::data
        p0["P0 - PARITY"]:::parity
    end

    subgraph STRIPE1[STRIPE ROW 1]
        p1["P1 - PARITY"]:::parity
        d0b["D4 - DISK 0"]:::data
        d1b["D5 - DISK 1"]:::data
        d2b["D6 - DISK 2"]:::data
        d3b["D7 - DISK 3"]:::data
    end

    subgraph STRIPE2[STRIPE ROW 2]
        d0c["D8  - DISK 0"]:::data
        p2["P2 - PARITY"]:::parity
        d1c["D9  - DISK 1"]:::data
        d2c["D10 - DISK 2"]:::data
        d3c["D11 - DISK 3"]:::data
    end

    subgraph STRIPE3[STRIPE ROW 3]
        d0d["D12 - DISK 0"]:::data
        d1d["D13 - DISK 1"]:::data
        p3["P3 - PARITY"]:::parity
        d2d["D14 - DISK 2"]:::data
        d3d["D15 - DISK 3"]:::data
    end

    classDef data fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef parity fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#fd7e14,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **How to read this diagram:** Each column is a physical disk. The orange block (P0, P1, P2, P3) is the **parity block**, and it shifts one column to the **left** on every successive stripe row. This is the *distributed parity* mechanism. Notice that **no single disk is the permanent parity disk** — this is the fundamental improvement over RAID 4.

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Read-Modify-Write Flow

This diagram maps the **internal operations** of the RAID 5 controller when executing a small random write. It is the functional equivalent of a state-machine flowchart.

```mermaid
graph LR
    HOST["HOST WRITE REQUEST"]:::host --> CTRL["RAID CONTROLLER"]:::ctrl
    CTRL --> RD1["STEP 1: READ OLD DATA D OLD"]:::io
    RD1 --> RD2["STEP 2: READ OLD PARITY P OLD"]:::io
    RD2 --> CMP["STEP 3: COMPUTE P NEW EQUALS P OLD XOR D OLD XOR D NEW"]:::cpu
    CMP --> WR1["STEP 4: WRITE NEW DATA D NEW TO TARGET DISK"]:::io
    WR1 --> WR2["STEP 5: WRITE NEW PARITY P NEW TO PARITY DISK"]:::io
    WR2 --> ACK["STEP 6: ACKNOWLEDGE HOST"]:::host
    ACK --> DONE["WRITE COMPLETE - 4 PHYSICAL I/OS USED"]:::done

    classDef host fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef ctrl fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef io fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef cpu fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#fd7e14,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef done fill:#e2e3e5,stroke:#6c757d,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The flow shows the **6 logical steps** of a RAID 5 write, but only **4 physical I/O operations** (2 reads + 2 writes) actually hit the disks. The "Compute" step is a CPU operation inside the controller and does not count toward the I/O penalty. This is the "Read-Modify-Write" cycle in graphical form.

---

## 4.3 Disk-Failure & Rebuild Process

```mermaid
graph TB
    NORMAL["NORMAL OPERATION - N DISKS ACTIVE"]:::ok --> FAIL["ONE DISK FAILS"]:::fail
    FAIL --> ALERT["RAID CONTROLLER DETECTS FAILURE - ALERTS HOST"]:::ctrl
    ALERT --> MODE["ARRAY OPERATES IN DEGRADED MODE - ALL I/OS CONTINUE"]:::warn
    MODE --> REBUILD["REBUILD PROCESS INITIATED - READ SURVIVING BLOCKS"]:::io
    REBUILD --> XOR["FOR EACH STRIPE: XOR SURVIVING BLOCKS AND PARITY"]:::cpu
    XOR --> NEW["WRITE RECONSTRUCTED DATA TO SPARE OR HOT SPARE"]:::io
    NEW --> DONE["ARRAY RETURNS TO NORMAL - FAULT TOLERANCE RESTORED"]:::ok

    classDef ok fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef fail fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef ctrl fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#fd7e14,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef warn fill:#ffe5b4,stroke:#fd7e14,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef io fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef cpu fill:#e2e3e5,stroke:#6c757d,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Real-World Caveat:** During the rebuild phase, the array has **zero fault tolerance** — if a second disk fails before the rebuild completes, **all data is lost**. This is why large-capacity disks in RAID 5 are considered a risk in modern enterprise environments, and why RAID 6 (dual parity) is increasingly preferred.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
