The payload of 2112 bytes plus overhead ~42 bytes = 2154 bytes per frame on the wire.

$$ \text{Frame Size} = 2154 \text{ bytes} = 2154 \times 8 = 17232 \text{ bits} $$

$$ \text{Frame Transmission Time} = \frac{17232 \text{ bits}}{4.25 \times 10^9 \text{ bps}} $$

$$ \text{Frame Transmission Time} = 4.0546 \times 10^{-6} \text{ seconds} \approx 4.05 \mu s $$

**Step 2: Use the BB_Credit formula.**

The minimum number of credits needed to keep the link full is:

$$ \text{BB\_Credit}_{\min} = \left\lceil \frac{\text{RTT}}{\text{Frame Transmission Time}} \right\rceil $$

**Step 3: Substitute the values.**

$$ \text{BB\_Credit}_{\min} = \left\lceil \frac{500 \mu s}{4.05 \mu s} \right\rceil $$

$$ \text{BB\_Credit}_{\min} = \left\lceil 123.46 \right\rceil = 124 $$

**Step 4: Add safety margin (typically +20%).**

$$ \text{BB\_Credit}_{\text{recommended}} = 124 \times 1.20 \approx 149 \approx 150 $$

> **Result:** The link requires a minimum of **124 BB_Credits** (recommended **150 credits**) to fully utilize a 4GFC connection over 50 km of single-mode fiber.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** A 50 km single-mode FC link with default 8–10 BB_Credits will be **severely underutilized** (only ~10% of the bandwidth usable). This is why **longwave SFPs with extended BB_Credits** (e.g., 60–150 credits) exist.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Component Selection Decision Tree

This matrix helps KTU students answer design-style questions in exams.

| Requirement | Recommended Component | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Connecting a single server to a single storage array (test lab) | **Point-to-Point FC + 1 HBA + 1 cable** | Lowest cost, sufficient for non-critical setup |
| 10 servers sharing 2 storage arrays in a small business | **FC Switch (8–24 ports) + dual-port HBAs** | Cost-effective, zoning enables isolation, multipath via dual HBA |
| Enterprise data center, 100+ servers, mission-critical | **FC Director + dual-port HBAs + redundant fabrics** | High port count, full redundancy, zero-downtime maintenance |
| Two data centers 50 km apart, replication needed | **Longwave SFP+ on FC Director + Extended BB_Credits** | Long-distance optical, sufficient credits to fill the wire |
| Legacy 2GFC environment with limited budget | **FC-AL Hub** | Cheaper, but no concurrency, no scalability |
| Virtualization host (VMware ESXi) with 100 VMs | **HBA with NPIV support + FC Switch with NPIV** | NPIV lets a single HBA appear as multiple virtual HBAs, one per VM |
| All-Flash Array deployment with NVMe | **32G/64G FC + FC-NVMe capable switches** | Highest throughput, lowest latency |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 FC SAN Architecture — Block-Level Functional Flow

The following Mermaid block diagram shows how all FC SAN components interact, mapped to the FC protocol stack. This is a **block-level functional architecture flow** showing logical interactions, not a physical drawing.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SERVER["SERVER NODE FC-4 LAYER"]
        APP["Application / OS"]
        SCSI["SCSI Initiator Driver"]
        HBA["HBA PCIe Card FC-0 to FC-2"]
        WWNH["WWN 20 00 00 0C 50 A4 1B 7C"]
    end

    subgraph PHYS["PHYSICAL MEDIA FC-0"]
        SFP1["SFP Plus Transceiver 850nm or 1310nm"]
        FIBER["Optical Fiber OM3 OM4 OS1 OS2"]
        SFP2["SFP Plus Transceiver Receiver"]
    end

    subgraph SWITCH["FC SWITCH or DIRECTOR FC-1 FC-2 FC-3"]
        FPORT1["F Port on switch face"]
        NSERV["Name Server FC-2"]
        ZONE["Zoning Engine FC-3"]
        BBMOD["BB Credit Manager FC-1"]
        EPORT["E Port for ISL Trunking"]
    end

    subgraph STORAGE["STORAGE ARRAY NODE FC-4"]
        FCIFACE["Front End FC Port N Port"]
        CTRL["Array Controller"]
        CACHE["Cache Memory"]
        DISK["Disks SSD HDD"]
        WWNS["WWN 50 00 00 0C 50 D3 8E 1F"]
    end

    APP --> SCSI --> HBA
    HBA -.- WWNH
    HBA --> SFP1 --> FIBER --> SFP2 --> FPORT1
    FPORT1 --> NSERV
    FPORT1 --> ZONE
    FPORT1 --> BBMOD
    NSERV <--> ZONE
    BBMOD <.- EPORT
    FPORT1 -.- EPORT
    EPORT --> FPORT1
    FPORT1 --> SFP2 --> FIBER --> SFP1 --> FCIFACE
    FCIFACE --> CTRL --> CACHE --> DISK
    FCIFACE -.- WWNS
```

### Diagram Interpretation Guide
- **Direction of arrows (top to bottom):** Data flow from server application → HBA → SFP → fiber → switch port → switch fabric (Name Server, Zoning, BB_Credit) → switch port → fiber → storage front-end → controller → cache → disk.
- **WWNs (dotted lines):** Logical identity tied to each end device.
- **E_Port (Trunking):** Provides switch-to-switch interconnection for fabric expansion.
- **Zoning & Name Server:** Logical services running inside the switch.

---

## 4.2 FC SAN Port Type Interaction Map

This second diagram clarifies the relationships between different FC port types and where they appear in the fabric.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph HOST["SERVER SIDE"]
        NPORT1["N Port on HBA"]
    end

    subgraph SW["FC SWITCH CORE"]
        FPORT["F Port connects to N Port"]
        GPORT["G Port auto negotiates"]
        EPORT1["E Port ISL to another switch"]
        TEPORT["TE Port Trunked E Port"]
        FLPORT["FL Port connects to FC-AL loop"]
    end

    subgraph LEGACY["LEGACY FC-AL"]
        NLPORT["NL Port on legacy device"]
        HUB["FC-AL Hub"]
    end

    subgraph STORAGE2["STORAGE SIDE"]
        NPORT2["N Port on storage controller"]
    end

    NPORT1 <-->|FCP SCSI Frames| FPORT
    FPORT <-->|login services| GPORT
    GPORT -.->|if link to another switch| EPORT1
    EPORT1 -.->|if multiple ISLs| TEPORT
    FLPORT <-->|arbitrated loop| HUB
    HUB <-->|NL Port traffic| NLPORT
    FPORT <-->|FCP frames| NPORT2
```

---

## 4.3 FC Topology Comparison — Visual Block Representation

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph P2P["FC TOPOLOGY 1 POINT TO POINT"]
        S1["Server HBA"] ---|direct cable| ARR1["Storage Array"]
    end

    subgraph AL["FC TOPOLOGY 2 FC-AL ARBITRATED LOOP"]
        S2["Server A"] --- H1["FC Hub"]
        S3["Server B"] --- H1
        S4["Tape Library"] --- H1
        ARR2["Storage"] --- H1
    end

    subgraph SF["FC TOPOLOGY 3 SWITCHED FABRIC"]
        S5["Server 1"] --- SW1["FC Switch Fabric"]
        S6["Server 2"] --- SW1
        S7["Server 3"] --- SW1
        ARR3["Storage 1"] --- SW1
        ARR4["Storage 2"] --- SW1
        TP["Tape Library"] --- SW1
        SW1 -.-|ISL| SW2["FC Switch 2 cascaded"]
    end
```

### Reading Guide
- **Point-to-Point:** Simplest. Two devices, one cable. No sharing, no scalability.
- **FC-AL (Hub-based):** Devices share bandwidth on a single arbitrated loop. **Only one pair communicates at a time.** A single failed device can break the loop unless bypassed.
- **Switched Fabric (FC-SW):** **The modern standard.** Concurrent full-duplex communication between multiple pairs. Switches can be cascaded via **ISL/TE_Ports**.

---

## 4.4 Component-to-Layer Mapping Diagram (FC Stack ↔ Physical Component)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L4["FC-4 UPPER LAYER MAPPING\nFCP SCSI | FC-NVMe | FC-IP"]
    L3["FC-3 COMMON SERVICES\nHunt Groups | Multicast | Striping"]
    L2["FC-2 FRAMING and FLOW CONTROL\nFrames | BB Credit | Name Server | Zoning"]
    L1["FC-1 ENCODE DECODE\n8b 10b or 64b 66b | Primitives"]
    L0["FC-0 PHYSICAL MEDIA\nFiber | Copper | SFP | Connectors"]

    L4 -.- L3 -.- L2 -.- L1 -.- L0

    HBA2["HBA Offloads FC-2 FC-1 FC-0"] -.-> L2
    SWC["FC Switch Implements FC-2 FC-3"] -.-> L2
    SFP2["SFP Transceiver Implements FC-0 FC-1"] -.-> L0
    STOR["Storage Array Front End FC-4 FCP"] -.-> L4
    ZS["Zoning Engine FC-3"] -.-> L3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Each FC SAN component **offloads or implements** specific FC layers. For example, an HBA offloads **FC-0, FC-1, and FC-2** from the host CPU. The FC switch implements **FC-2 (framing) and FC-3 (services like zoning)**. This is why HBAs are essential for FC performance.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define a Host Bus Adapter (HBA) and explain its role in an FC SAN. `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Host Bus Adapter (HBA)** is a specialized PCIe I/O card installed in a server that connects the server to the Fibre Channel SAN. Its role includes:

1. **Protocol offload** — Performs FC-0 (physical), FC-1 (encoding/decoding), and FC-2 (framing) functions in dedicated hardware, freeing the host CPU.
2. **WWN assignment** — Each HBA has a unique 64-bit World Wide Name used for FC addressing.
3. **Frame transmission/reception** — Converts host SCSI I/O requests into FC frames (FCP) and vice versa.
4. **Multipath support** — Dual-port HBAs enable redundant paths to the fabric.
5. **Virtualization support** — Modern HBAs support **NPIV (N_Port ID Virtualization)**, allowing a single HBA to present multiple virtual ports to VMs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Note:** A common mistake is to confuse an HBA with a NIC. An **HBA is for FC SANs**; a **NIC is for Ethernet/LANs**.

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between an FC Switch and an FC Director. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | FC Switch | FC Director |
|---|---|---|
| **Form factor** | Fixed 1U–4U, smaller | Modular chassis (8U–14U) |
| **Port count** | 8–96 ports | 64–512+ ports |
| **Redundancy** | Partial (PSU, fans) | **Full** (dual supervisors, fabrics, PSUs) |
| **Hot-swap** | Limited | All components hot-swappable |
| **Use case** | Mid-size SANs | Mission-critical enterprise core |
| **Cost** | Low to medium | Very high |

> A **Director** is essentially a **fully-redundant, modular, enterprise-class switch** designed for zero-downtime operation in core SAN deployments.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A: (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the major components of a Fibre Channel SAN with a neat block diagram. Highlight the role of WWN and BB_Credit in the fabric. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the three FC topologies — Point-to-Point, FC-AL, and Switched Fabric — with their advantages and limitations. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Components of FC SAN (7 marks)

A Fibre Channel SAN is built from the following key components, organized by the FC protocol layer:

**1. Physical Layer Components (FC-0):**
- **Optical fiber cables** — multi-mode (OM1–OM5) for short range, single-mode (OS1/OS2) for long range.
- **Copper cables** — twin-ax, used for very short runs (≤30m).
- **Connectors** — SC, LC, MTRJ.
- **Transceivers** — GBIC, SFP, SFP+, QSFP — convert between electrical and optical signals.

**2. FC Switch / Director (FC-1 to FC-3):**
- The **central routing device** of the fabric.
- Switches FC frames between N_Ports.
- Hosts the **Name Server** (analogous to DNS) that maps WWN ↔ FCID.
- Enforces **Zoning** policies.
- Manages **BB_Credit** for flow control.

**3. Host Bus Adapters (HBAs) (FC-0 to FC-2):**
- PCIe cards in servers that perform **FC protocol offload**.
- Each HBA has a **World Wide Name (WWN)** — a unique 64-bit address.

**4. Storage Arrays:**
- End devices with **front-end FC ports (N_Ports)**.
- Serve **LUNs** to hosts.

**5. SAN Management Software:**
- Tools like **Brocade SANnav** for configuration, zoning, monitoring, firmware management.

**Role of WWN (World Wide Name):**
- A **64-bit globally unique identifier** assigned by the manufacturer to every FC device.
- Two types: **WWNN (Node WWN)** identifies the device; **WWPN (Port WWN)** identifies a specific port.
- Used by the **Name Server** for fabric registration and discovery.
- Used in **WWN zoning** to control which devices can communicate.

**Role of BB_Credit (Buffer-to-Buffer Credit):**
- **Hop-by-hop flow control** mechanism in FC.
- A port can send only as many frames as the receiving port has **receive buffers** (credits) for.
- Each frame must be acknowledged by the receiver via an **R_RDY primitive** before the sender transmits the next.
- For long-distance links, **insufficient BB_Credit causes underutilization** of the link bandwidth.

---

#### Part (b) — Three FC Topologies (7 marks)

**1. Point-to-Point:**
- Two devices (e.g., a server and a storage array) connected by a single FC cable.
- **Advantages:** Simple, no configuration, no contention, dedicated bandwidth.
- **Limitations:** No scalability (only 2 devices), no sharing, no redundancy, inefficient for multi-server environments.

**2. Fibre Channel Arbitrated Loop (FC-AL):**
- Devices connected in a **logical loop** (daisy-chain), typically via a **hub**.
- Uses **NL_Ports** and **L_Ports**.
- Up to **126 devices** supported per loop.
- Only one pair of devices can communicate at a time (arbitrated).
- **Advantages:** Lower cost (no switch needed), simple cabling.
- **Limitations:** **Bandwidth is shared**; a single node failure (if not bypassed) can break the loop; no scalability; performance degrades with more devices.

**3. Switched Fabric (FC-SW):**
- Devices connect to FC switches via **N_Ports** and **F_Ports**.
- Full-duplex, **concurrent communication** between multiple pairs.
- **Advantages:** High scalability (hundreds of ports), high performance, supports zoning, supports multipath, supports VSANs and NPIV.
- **Limitations:** Higher cost (switches/directors), requires management and configuration.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often confuse **FC-AL** and **FC-SW** port types. Remember:
> - **FC-AL** uses **NL_Ports** and **FL_Ports**, with hubs.
> - **FC-SW** uses **N_Ports** and **F_Ports**, with switches.
> - A **G_Port** is a generic switch port that auto-negotiates as **F_Port** (if connected to a node) or **E_Port** (if connected to another switch).

---

### Question B: (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the FC protocol stack (FC-0 to FC-4) and identify which layer each major SAN component operates at. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Calculate the effective throughput of a 16GFC link, given: line rate = 14.025 Gbps, 64b/66b encoding, FC frame overhead = 36 bytes, inter-frame primitives = 6 bytes, max payload = 2112 bytes. Comment on the difference between 8b/10b and 64b/66b encoding efficiency. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — FC Protocol Stack (7 marks)

The FC protocol stack is defined by the **INCITS T11** committee and loosely maps to the OSI model:

| FC Layer | OSI Layer | Function | Components Implementing It |
|---|---|---|---|
| **FC-0** | Physical | Cables, connectors, transceivers, distances | Fiber, Copper, GBIC, SFP, SFP+, QSFP |
| **FC-1** | Data Link (lower) | 8b/10b or 64b/66b encoding, link control | Transceivers, HBA, Switch (partial) |
| **FC-2** | Data Link (upper) | Frames, Sequences, BB_Credit, Name Server | **FC Switch / Director (primary), HBA (offload)** |
| **FC-3** | Services | Hunt groups, multicast, striping | **FC Switch / Director (Zoning, Name Server)** |
| **FC-4** | Upper Layer Protocol Mapping | FCP (SCSI), FC-NVMe, FC-IP | **Server OS, Storage Array, HBA driver** |

**Component-to-Layer Mapping:**

- **HBA:** Implements FC-0, FC-1, FC-2 (offloads these from the host CPU).
- **FC Switch/Director:** Primarily FC-2 (frame switching) and FC-3 (Name Server, Zoning).
- **SFP/SFP+:** Implements FC-0 (physical) and FC-1 (encoding).
- **Storage Array:** Implements FC-4 (FCP or FC-NVMe) and FC-2 (front-end port).
- **SAN Management SW:** Operates above FC-4 (out-of-band management).

---

#### Part (b) — Throughput Calculation (7 marks)

**Given:**
- 16GFC line rate = **14.025 Gbps**
- 64b/66b encoding: every **64 bits** of user data → **66 bits** on the wire
- FC frame overhead: **36 bytes**
- Inter-frame primitives: **6 bytes**
- Max payload: **2112 bytes**

**Step 1: Apply 64b/66b encoding efficiency.**

$$ \eta_{64/66} = \frac{64}{66} \approx 0.9697 $$

**Step 2: Compute the user data rate after encoding.**

$$ \text{User Data Rate} = 14.025 \text{ Gbps} \times \frac{64}{66} $$

$$ \text{User Data Rate} = 14.025 \times 0.9697 \approx 13.6 \text{ Gbps} $$

**Step 3: Convert to GB/s.**

$$ \text{User Data Rate} = \frac{13.6 \text{ Gbps}}{8} = 1.7 \text{ GB/s} $$

**Step 4: Account for frame overhead.**

Total overhead per frame: $36 + 6 = 42$ bytes.
Payload: $2112$ bytes.

$$ \eta_{\text{frame}} = \frac{2112}{2112 + 42} = \frac{2112}{2154} \approx 0.9805 $$

**Step 5: Compute effective throughput.**

$$ \text{Effective Throughput} = 1.7 \text{ GB/s} \times 0.9805 \approx 1.667 \text{ GB/s} $$

**Result:** Effective throughput of 16GFC ≈ **1.67 GB/s**.

**Comment on Encoding Efficiency:**

| Encoding | Speed Range | Overhead Ratio | Encoding Efficiency |
|---|---|---|---|
| **8b/10b** | 1G to 8G FC | 2 bits per 8 bits (20%) | **80%** |
| **64b/66b** | 16G FC and above | 2 bits per 64 bits (~3%) | **~97%** |

> **16GFC uses 64b/66b** because it has **lower encoding overhead** (~3%) compared to **8b/10b** (20%). This is one of the key reasons **16GFC is more bandwidth-efficient** than 8GFC, in addition to the higher line rate.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing line rate and throughput** — Many students use "16 Gbps" as if it were usable data. Always account for encoding overhead.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert bits to bytes** — A throughput of "13.6 Gbps" is correct, but examiners ask for "GB/s" or "MB/s" — divide by 8.
> 3. **Mixing up 8b/10b and 64b/66b** — 8b/10b is used for speeds **up to 8GFC**; 64b/66b is used for **16GFC and above**. This is a common 2-mark trap question.
> 4. **Ignoring FC frame overhead** — Some students compute only the encoding overhead and forget the 42 bytes of frame/primitive overhead. This loses 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Confusing FC switch with FC-AL hub** — In design questions, students often recommend hubs in modern SANs. Always recommend **switches/directors** in production environments.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 2: FC SAN Components**

- **FC SAN** is a dedicated, high-speed network for block-level I/O, isolated from LAN traffic.
- **FC Protocol Stack:** FC-0 (physical) → FC-1 (encode/decode) → FC-2 (framing/flow) → FC-3 (services) → FC-4 (ULP mapping like FCP, FC-NVMe).
- **Key Components (8 total):**
  1. **Cables & Connectors** — fiber (MMF/SMF) and copper; SC, LC connectors.
  2. **Transceivers** — GBIC, SFP, SFP+, QSFP, QSFP28 — hot-swappable.
  3. **Hubs** — FC-AL, legacy, bandwidth-shared.
  4. **FC Switches** — fixed-port, F/E/G/N ports, Name Server, Zoning.
  5. **FC Directors** — modular, fully redundant, enterprise-grade.
  6. **HBAs** — PCIe cards, WWN, FC offload, dual-port for multipath, NPIV for virtualization.
  7. **Storage Arrays** — front-end FC ports, LUNs, cache, redundancy.
  8. **SAN Management Software** — Brocade SANnav, Cisco DCNM.
- **Port Types to Memorize:**
  - **N_Port** — node, switched fabric.
  - **NL_Port** — node, FC-AL loop.
  - **F_Port** — fabric, connects to N_Port.
  - **FL_Port** — fabric, connects to loop.
  - **E_Port** — expansion (ISL).
  - **TE_Port** — trunking E_Port (multiple ISLs).
  - **G_Port** — generic auto-negotiating.
  - **B_Port** — bridge.
  - **D_Port** — diagnostic.
- **Three Topologies:** Point-to-Point, FC-AL, Switched Fabric (FC-SW is dominant).
- **WWN (World Wide Name)** — 64-bit unique ID; WWNN for node, WWPN for port.
- **BB_Credit** — hop-by-hop flow control; long-distance links need extended credits.
- **Zoning:** Soft (Name Server, less secure) vs Hard (hardware-enforced, secure); types are Port, WWN, Mixed.
- **Throughput Math:** Always apply encoding overhead first (8b/10b = 80%, 64b/66b ≈ 97%), then convert bits to bytes (÷8), then apply frame overhead (~98%).
- **Distances:** MMF up to 150m @ 32G; SMF up to 10 km standard, 40 km with DWDM.
- **NPIV** — N_Port ID Virtualization — lets one HBA present as many virtual HBA ports, critical for VMFC (VM-to-FC) connectivity.
- **VSAN / Fabric** — logical partitioning of a physical FC fabric for multi-tenancy.
- **Modern Trend:** 32G/64G FC is the current production standard; 128GFC is emerging for AI/ML workloads; FC-NVMe is replacing FCP for low-latency flash arrays.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: SAN Topologies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECST867 - STORAGE SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** SAN Topologies # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **SAN (Storage Area Network) Topology** is the logical and physical arrangement of nodes (servers, storage devices, and switches) connected through a high-speed, dedicated network infrastructure designed specifically for block-level data transfer between computer systems and storage elements. In the context of **Fibre Channel (FC) SANs** — the dominant SAN architecture referenced in the KTU 2024 syllabus — three canonical topologies are recognized by the **ANSI T11 standards committee**: 1. **Point-to-Point (FC-P2P)** 2. **Arbitrated Loop (FC-AL)** 3. **Switched Fabric (FC-SW)** These topologies define how **N_Ports** (Node Ports on end devices) and **F_Ports** / **FL_Ports** (Fabric / Fabric-Loop Ports on switches) communicate using the FC-0, FC-1, and FC-2 layers of the Fibre Channel protocol stack. > [!NOTE] > **Key Terminology Anchors (KTU 2024 High-Frequency Vocabulary):** > - **N_Port** = End-device node port > - **F_Port** = Switch port in a fabric > - **L_Port** = Loop-capable port (used in FC-AL) > - **FL_Port** = Switch port that participates in a loop > - **E_Port** = Expansion port (switch-to-switch link) > - **G_Port** = Generic port (auto-configurable to F_Port or E_Port) --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **highway system** connecting cities (servers) and warehouses (storage arrays): | SAN Topology | Highway Analogy | Scale of Operation | |---|---|---| | **Point-to-Point** | A private two-lane road directly connecting one city to one warehouse | Smallest, most private | | **Arbitrated Loop** | A single circular one-way ring road where vehicles must "ask permission" to enter | Shared single lane, sequential access | | **Switched Fabric** | A multi-lane intelligent expressway with traffic signals, overpasses, and on-ramps | Large, parallel, full duplex | ### Plain-English Explanation Think of **Point-to-Point** as a direct cable (like a USB cable) — only **2 devices** can talk. Simple, fast, but doesn't scale. Think of **Arbitrated Loop (FC-AL)** as a circular daisy-chain (like a string of Christmas lights) where up to **126 devices** share a single logical channel. Only two devices can talk at a time because everyone shares the same bandwidth. They must "arbitrate" (negotiate) who gets the line. Think of **Switched Fabric (FC-SW)** as a smart telephone exchange. Each device has a **dedicated full-duplex channel** to the switch, and the switch routes traffic intelligently. Up to **~16 million** devices are addressable, and many conversations can occur **simultaneously** without contention. > [!TIP] > **Memory Trick:** The progression **P2P → AL → SW** mirrors the evolution of computer networking from RS-232 (direct) → Token Ring (shared loop) → Ethernet Switched (dedicated fabric). --- ## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants | Metric | Value | Significance | |---|---|---| | Maximum devices on FC-AL | **126** | Loop address limit | | Maximum devices on FC-SW | **16,777,216** (24-bit FCID) | Fabric address limit | | Maximum hops in fabric | **3 to 7** (vendor dependent) | E_Port-to-E_Port | | Fibre Channel speeds | **1 Gbps, 2 Gbps, 4 Gbps, 8 Gbps, 16 Gbps, 32 Gbps, 64 Gbps, 128 Gbps** | Line rate per direction | | Maximum FC-AL cable length (Copper) | **30 m** at 1 Gbps | Distance degrades with speed | | Maximum FC-AL cable length (Optical) | **10 km** at 1 Gbps | Single-mode fiber | # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Three SAN Topologies — Detailed Operational Analysis ### 2.1.1 Point-to-Point (FC-P2P) Topology **Operational Concept:** The simplest possible SAN configuration. Two devices are connected by a single dedicated link. **Why it exists:** Provides the lowest possible latency and the highest dedicated bandwidth between two endpoints. No protocol overhead for arbitration, addressing, or routing. **How it works:** - Each device has exactly **one N_Port** - Frames flow directly between the two N_Ports - No addressing beyond source/destination - No contention possible (only two devices) - Connection is "always up" — no login negotiation required for the link itself **Limitations:** - **Wasted bandwidth** when one device is idle - **Does not scale** — every new device needs its own dedicated port - In a data center with 1,000 servers and 1,000 storage arrays, you would need **1,000,000 cables** > [!NOTE] > Modern usage: **P2P** is now primarily used as the **building block** of Switched Fabric — every device-to-switch connection in a fabric is logically a P2P link at the physical layer. --- ### 2.1.2 Arbitrated Loop (FC-AL) Topology **Operational Concept:** A shared, ring-based topology where up to **126 devices** are connected in a unidirectional loop. Devices must **arbitrate** for the right to communicate. **Why it exists:** It was a cost-effective middle ground before switches became affordable. It allowed multiple devices to share infrastructure without requiring an intelligent switch. **How it works — Step-by-Step:** 1. Each device on the loop is assigned a unique **AL_PA (Arbitrated Loop Physical Address)** from 0 to 125 (126 usable addresses; 0x00 and 0xFF are reserved). 2. When Device A wants to talk to Device B, Device A sends an **ARB (Arbitrate) primitive signal** onto the loop. 3. The ARB signal propagates around the loop. If another device is already arbitrating, the lower AL_PA wins (priority based on address). 4. The winning device opens a **loop circuit** by replacing its ARB signal with an **OPN (Open) primitive** specifying the destination. 5. When the destination device receives the OPN, it accepts and the loop is "opened" between the two devices. 6. All other devices enter a **pass-through state** — frames flow through them transparently to complete the loop. 7. When communication ends, the originator sends a **CLS (Close) primitive** to release the loop. **Critical Constraints:** - **Only ONE pair** of devices can communicate at any given time - **Total loop bandwidth = single channel bandwidth** (e.g., 8 Gbps shared among all) - If **any** device fails, the entire loop fails (unless the device has an **L_Port bypass** circuit) - Adding/removing devices **disturbs the loop** (though hot-plug is supported) **Variants:** - **FC-AL** (original) — 1 Gbps, copper or optical - **FC-AL-2** — improved with dual-loop support - **FC-AL-2 with Dual-Loop** — two independent loops for redundancy (if one fails, the other survives) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Exam Pitfall:** Students often confuse the maximum number of devices. The **physical address space is 126** (addresses 0–125), NOT 127 and NOT 128. Address 0 is reserved for the **FL_Port** (if a switch is attached), and address 255 (0xFF) is reserved for broadcast. --- ### 2.1.3 Switched Fabric (FC-SW) Topology **Operational Concept:** A network of one or more Fibre Channel switches providing simultaneous, full-bandwidth, any-to-any connectivity through intelligent frame routing. **Why it exists:** Overcomes the scalability and bandwidth-sharing limitations of FC-AL by giving every device a **dedicated full-duplex channel** to the fabric. **How it works — Step-by-Step:** 1. Each end device connects to a switch via an **N_Port ↔ F_Port** link (logically a P2P connection). 2. When the device powers on, it performs **FLOGI (Fabric Login)** — the switch assigns a **24-bit FCID (Fibre Channel ID)**. 3. The FCID structure: `Domain_ID (8 bits) - Area_ID (8 bits) - Port_ID (8 bits)` → 16,777,216 possible addresses. 4. To communicate with another device, the source sends a frame with the destination's FCID. 5. The switch consults its **FSPF (Fabric Shortest Path First)** routing table and forwards the frame out the appropriate E_Port or F_Port. 6. Multiple frames can flow **simultaneously** across different switch ports — true full-duplex, non-blocking. 7. Switches interconnect via **E_Ports** to form a multi-switch fabric (cascaded fabric). **Critical Features:** - **Class of Service 1 (Circuit-Switched):** Dedicated connection, in-order delivery, acknowledged - **Class of Service 2 (Frame-Switched):** Connectionless, acknowledged - **Class of Service 3 (Datagram):** Connectionless, unacknowledged (most common for SCSI) - **Class of Service 4 (Fractional):** Bandwidth-provisioned virtual circuits - **Class F (Switch-to-Switch):** Used for fabric services - **Zoning:** Partitioning the fabric for security/isolation - **LUN Masking:** Restricting which LUNs a host can see (separate from zoning) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 PECST867 syllabus specifically asks students to understand the **comparative trade-offs** between these three topologies in terms of scalability, performance, fault tolerance, and cost. --- ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Parameter | Point-to-Point (FC-P2P) | Arbitrated Loop (FC-AL) | Switched Fabric (FC-SW) | |---|---|---|---| | **Maximum Devices** | **2** | **126** | **~16.7 million** (24-bit FCID) | | **Aggregate Bandwidth** | $B_{total} = 2 \times B_{link}$ (dedicated full-duplex per pair) | $B_{total} = B_{link}$ (shared) | $B_{total} = N \times B_{link}$ (N = number of ports) | | **Simultaneous Conversations** | **1** | **1** | **Up to N/2** (full-duplex ports) | | **Addressing** | None (hard-wired) | **AL_PA** (8-bit) | **FCID** (24-bit) | | **Failure Domain** | 1 link | Entire loop | Single link (isolated) | | **Cost** | Lowest | Moderate | Highest | | **Port Type** | N_Port only | L_Port (NL_Port for loop+switch) | N_Port ↔ F_Port / E_Port | | **Switch Required?** | No | No (optional FL_Port hub mode) | **Yes** | | **Topology Shape** | Line (2 nodes) | Ring (closed loop) | Star / Mesh (fabric) | | **Cable Hot-Plug** | Disruptive | Disruptive (but LIP supported) | **Non-disruptive** | ### 2.2.1 Key Bandwidth / Throughput Equations **Effective bandwidth of FC-AL under N active nodes:** $$B_{eff,AL} = \frac{B_{link}}{2} \times \frac{1}{N_{active}}$$ Where: - $B_{link}$ = raw link bandwidth (e.g., 8 Gbps) - $N_{active}$ = number of devices currently arbitrating - Division by 2 accounts for the **half-duplex** nature of the loop **Effective aggregate bandwidth of FC-SW fabric:** $$B_{eff,SW} = N_{ports} \times B_{link}$$ Where $N_{ports}$ is the number of active F_Port connections, assuming **non-blocking** switch architecture. **Frame transfer time (any topology):** $$T_{frame} = \frac{L_{frame} \times 8}{B_{link}} + T_{overhead}$$ Where: - $L_{frame}$ = frame payload size in bytes (max 2112 bytes in FC) - $T_{overhead}$ = encoding (8b/10b) + framing + latency > [!TIP] > **Engineering Utility:** These equations are used by SAN architects to size fabrics for OLTP workloads (high IOPS, low latency) versus bulk backup workloads (high throughput, latency-tolerant). The choice between FC-AL and FC-SW directly impacts the **TCO (Total Cost of Ownership)** of the storage infrastructure. --- ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility 1. **Enterprise Data Centers:** Modern data centers exclusively use **Switched Fabric** because of the high device count and concurrent workload demands. FC-AL is now considered legacy. 2. **Small/Mid-Size Business (SMB):** Direct-Attached Storage (DAS) or simple P2P FC connections may still appear in low-end SANs. 3. **High-Performance Computing (HPC):** FC-SW fabrics dominate; InfiniBand is an alternative. 4. **Fibre Channel over Ethernet (FCoE):** Encapsulates FC frames over 10/25/40/100 Gbps Ethernet — uses P2P and Fabric topologies. 5. **NVMe over Fabrics (NVMe-oF):** A newer evolution that may use FC or TCP/RoCE — same topology principles apply. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: FC-AL Address Space & Loop Capacity ### Step 1 — Raw Address Space The FC-AL standard allocates a single byte (8 bits) for the **AL_PA (Arbitrated Loop Physical Address)**. $$N_{addresses,raw} = 2^8 = 256$$ ### Step 2 — Subtract Reserved Addresses The standard reserves: - **0x00** for the **FL_Port** (fabric loop port, if a switch is attached) - **0xFF** (255) for the **broadcast** address $$N_{reserved} = 2$$ ### Step 3 — Compute Usable AL_PA Addresses $$N_{AL_PA,usable} = N_{addresses,raw} - N_{reserved} = 256 - 2 = 254$$ ### Step 4 — Apply the FC-AL-2 Masking Constraint In FC-AL-2, addresses are restricted to the lower 4 bits of the byte (the upper 4 bits must be zero for loop-only devices). This is the **"AL_PA valid bit"** constraint. $$N_{addresses,FCAL2} = 2^7 = 128 \text{ (when all upper bits must be zero)}$$ Re-applying the reserved subtraction: $$N_{AL_PA,final} = 128 - 2 = 126$$ > **Conclusion:** The maximum number of usable end-device addresses on a single FC-AL loop is **126**. This derivation explains **why** KTU textbooks uniformly cite the number 126, not 128 or 127. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: FC-SW Address Space (24-bit FCID) ### Step 1 — Structure of the FCID A Fibre Channel ID has three fields: $$FCID = \underbrace{D}_{Domain\_ID}\ \ \underbrace{A}_{Area\_ID}\ \ \underbrace{P}{Port\_ID}$$ Each field is **8 bits** wide: $$\vert D \vert = \vert A \vert = \vert P \vert = 8 \text{ bits}$$ ### Step 2 — Total Address Space $$N_{FCID,total} = 2^8 \times 2^8 \times 2^8 = 2^{24} = 16{,}777{,}216$$ ### Step 3 — Practical Fabric Limit In practice, a single fabric supports up to **239 domains** (Domain_IDs 1–239 are usable; 0 and 255 are reserved). With 256 areas per domain and 256 ports per area: $$N_{devices,practical} = 239 \times 256 \times 256 = 15{,}663{,}104$$ But the **theoretical** maximum cited in KTU exams is always **$2^{24} = 16{,}777{,}216$**. --- ## 3.3 Derivation: Frame Transfer Time for a 2 KB Block This is a **numerical example** commonly tested in KTU exams. **Given:** - $B_{link} = 8 \text{ Gbps}$ (Fibre Channel 8G) - $L_{payload} = 2048 \text{ bytes}$ (typical 2 KB disk block) - $L_{header} = 24 \text{ bytes}$ (FC frame header) - Encoding: 8b/10b (10 bits transmitted for every 8 bits of data) - Inter-frame gap + primitives overhead: $T_{overhead} = 0.5 \mu s$ ### Step 1 — Total Frame Size on the Wire $$L_{wire} = (L_{payload} + L_{header}) \times \frac{10}{8}$$ $$L_{wire} = (2048 + 24) \times 1.25 = 2072 \times 1.25 = 2590 \text{ bytes}$$ ### Step 2 — Convert to Bits $$L_{wire,bits} = 2590 \times 8 = 20{,}720 \text{ bits}$$ ### Step 3 — Compute Transfer Time (raw) $$T_{raw} = \frac{L_{wire,bits}}{B_{link}} = \frac{20{,}720}{8 \times 10^9}$$ $$T_{raw} = 2.59 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} = 2.59 \ \mu s$$ ### Step 4 — Add Overhead $$T_{total} = T_{raw} + T_{overhead} = 2.59 \ \mu s + 0.5 \ \mu s = 3.09 \ \mu s$$ ### Step 5 — Compute Throughput (useful payload only) $$Throughput = \frac{L_{payload} \times 8}{T_{total}} = \frac{2048 \times 8}{3.09 \times 10^{-6}}$$ $$Throughput = \frac{16{,}384}{3.09 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 5.30 \ \text{Gbps}$$ This is **66.3%** of the nominal 8 Gbps line rate, illustrating the **encoding and protocol overhead** of FC. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Decision Framework The following matrix maps each topology against the **KTU 2024 evaluation criteria** for storage system design: | Evaluation Criterion | Point-to-Point | Arbitrated Loop | Switched Fabric | Weight (KTU Marks) | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Scalability** | ★☆☆ (2 devices) | ★★☆ (126 devices) | ★★★ (16M devices) | 3 | | **Aggregate Throughput** | ★★☆ (dedicated, but 1 pair) | ★☆☆ (shared) | ★★★ (non-blocking) | 3 | | **Fault Tolerance** | ★☆☆ (single point) | ★☆☆ (loop-wide) | ★★★ (zoned, redundant) | 2 | | **Cost per Port** | ★★★ (cheapest) | ★★☆ (moderate) | ★☆☆ (most expensive) | 2 | | **Implementation Complexity** | ★★★ (trivial) | ★★☆ (moderate) | ★☆☆ (high) | 2 | | **Hot-Plug Support** | ★☆☆ | ★★☆ (LIP-based) | ★★★ (non-disruptive) | 1 | | **Distance Capability** | ★★☆ (limited by P2P) | ★★☆ (10 km optical) | ★★★ (10s of km) | 1 | | **Industry Adoption (2024)** | Legacy | Legacy | **Dominant** | 1 | > [!NOTE] > **How to read this matrix in an exam:** If a 14-mark question asks "Justify why Switched Fabric is the preferred SAN topology for enterprise data centers," use this matrix to construct a **3-point argument**: scalability + throughput + fault tolerance. --- ## 3.5 Worked Example: Topology Selection for a Given Workload **Problem Scenario (KTU 2019-style):** > "A small business has 8 servers and 2 storage arrays. The workload is light file serving with peak traffic of 2 Gbps. Recommend a SAN topology with justification." **Solution — Analytical Walkthrough:** **Step 1 — Determine device count.** $$N_{devices} = 8 + 2 = 10 \text{ devices}$$ **Step 2 — Check topology feasibility.** P2P: requires $10 \times 9 / 2 = 45$ cables → **infeasible** FC-AL: 10 ≤ 126 → **feasible** (one shared 8 Gbps loop) FC-SW: 10 → **feasible** (8-port switch minimum) **Step 3 — Check bandwidth adequacy.** Required: 2 Gbps peak FC-AL shared: 8 Gbps / 2 (half-duplex) = 4 Gbps max → **just adequate** FC-SW dedicated: 8 Gbps full-duplex per port → **more than adequate** **Step 4 — Cost-benefit decision.** For a small business, **FC-AL with dual-loop** is the most cost-effective. For an enterprise, **FC-SW** wins on future scalability. **Final Answer (KTU Model):** > "A Dual-Loop FC-AL topology is recommended. It supports the 10-device requirement, provides the 4 Gbps effective bandwidth needed for the 2 Gbps workload, and offers redundancy via the second loop. Cost is minimized by avoiding switch infrastructure." # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Point-to-Point (FC-P2P) Topology ```mermaid graph LR serverA[Server A

Examiners specifically check for this in the 14-mark questions. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, PECST867]` > **Q: Define SAN. List and briefly explain the three standard Fibre Channel SAN topologies.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **SAN Definition (1 mark):** A **Storage Area Network (SAN)** is a dedicated, high-speed network that provides block-level access to consolidated storage devices, allowing multiple servers to access multiple storage arrays as if they were locally attached. **Three FC Topologies (2 marks):** 1. **Point-to-Point (FC-P2P):** Direct connection between two N_Ports. Lowest latency but limited to 2 devices. 2. **Arbitrated Loop (FC-AL):** Up to 126 devices share a unidirectional loop; they arbitrate for access using AL_PAs; only one pair communicates at a time. 3. **Switched Fabric (FC-SW):** Intelligent switch fabric providing simultaneous full-duplex connections via dedicated F_Ports, with up to 16.7 million addressable devices using 24-bit FCIDs. --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, PECST867]` > **Q: What is an AL_PA? Why is the maximum number of devices in an FC-AL loop 126 and not 128 or 256?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **AL_PA Definition (1 mark):** **AL_PA (Arbitrated Loop Physical Address)** is a unique 8-bit identifier assigned to each device on an FC-AL loop, used during the arbitration process to determine which device wins access to the loop. **Derivation of 126 (2 marks):** - 8-bit address space gives $2^8 = 256$ raw addresses. - Address 0x00 is reserved for the **FL_Port** and 0xFF is reserved for **broadcast**, so $256 - 2 = 254$. - In FC-AL-2, only the lower 7 bits are valid for end devices: $2^7 = 128$. - Subtracting the 2 reserved addresses: $128 - 2 = \mathbf{126}$ usable AL_PAs. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) ### **Question A (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper PECST867]` > **Q: (a)** Explain in detail the **Arbitrated Loop (FC-AL) SAN topology** with a suitable diagram. Describe the **arbitration mechanism** using primitive signals (ARB, OPN, CLS) and explain how the loop is established between two communicating devices. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** Compare and contrast the three SAN topologies (**Point-to-Point, Arbitrated Loop, Switched Fabric**) in terms of scalability, bandwidth utilization, fault tolerance, and cost. Justify which topology is preferred in **modern enterprise data centers**. **(7 marks)** --- #### Part (a) Model Answer — Arbitrated Loop Detailed Explanation (7 Marks) **1. Definition & Physical Layout (2 marks):** FC-AL is a shared, unidirectional loop topology connecting up to **126 devices** in a ring. Each device has an **L_Port** (Loop Port) that supports both arbitrated and point-to-point operations. Devices are connected either through a **hub** (which logically creates the loop from a star physical layout) or directly in a physical ring. **2. Arbitration Mechanism — Step-by-Step (3 marks):** When Device A (AL_PA = 0x05) wishes to communicate with Device B (AL_PA = 0x10): | Step | Primitive | Action | |---|---|---| | 1 | **ARB(0x05)** | Device A transmits ARB primitive into the loop | | 2 | **Propagate** | ARB signal travels around the loop in one direction | | 3 | **Compare** | Any other arbitrating device compares its AL_PA; **lower wins** | | 4 | **Replace** | Winner replaces its ARB with **OPN(0x10)** — "Open to destination 0x10" | | 5 | **Open** | Destination accepts; loop is now opened between A and B | | 6 | **Data Transfer** | All other devices enter pass-through; data flows A→B | | 7 | **CLS** | When done, A sends CLS; loop is released for next arbitration | **3. Key Characteristics (2 marks):** - **Half-duplex, shared media** — only one pair active at a time - **Priority-based** arbitration via AL_PA comparison - **Single point of failure** — loop breaks if any device fails (unless L_Port bypass circuit activates) - **Dual-loop FC-AL-2** provides redundancy --- #### Part (b) Model Answer — Comparative Analysis (7 Marks) **Comparison Table (4 marks):** | Parameter | P2P | FC-AL | FC-SW | |---|---|---|---| | Max Devices | 2 | 126 | 16.7M | | Bandwidth | Dedicated, 1 pair | Shared, half-duplex | Dedicated, full-duplex, simultaneous | | Fault Tolerance | None (link only) | Loop-wide failure | Per-link isolation, redundant paths | | Cost | Lowest | Moderate | Highest | | Hot-Plug | Disruptive | LIP-based | Non-disruptive | | Addressing | None | AL_PA (8-bit) | FCID (24-bit) | **Justification for Modern Enterprise Choice (3 marks):** Modern enterprise data centers choose **Switched Fabric (FC-SW)** because: 1. **Scalability:** Modern data centers host thousands of servers and storage arrays — only FC-SW scales to this size. 2. **Concurrent Throughput:** Enterprise workloads (OLTP, virtualization, VDI) require multiple simultaneous high-bandwidth flows; FC-AL's shared bandwidth would be a bottleneck. 3. **High Availability:** Features like redundant E_Port links, failover routing via FSPF, and zoning make FC-SW suitable for mission-critical 24×7 operations. 4. **Ecosystem:** Modern arrays (e.g., NetApp, EMC, Pure Storage) and HBAs are designed primarily for FC-SW connectivity; FC-AL is a legacy technology. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Question A:** > - Students frequently **omit the primitive signals** (ARB, OPN, CLS) when explaining FC-AL arbitration. This loses **2-3 marks** as the primitive-level mechanism is a KTU 2024 syllabus highlight. > - In part (b), students often give a one-line "FC-SW is better" without the **3-point justification** (scalability, throughput, fault tolerance). Always provide structured reasoning. > - Forgetting to draw the **diagram in part (a)** costs **1-2 marks** because KTU 2024 marking scheme explicitly allocates marks for neat labeled diagrams. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper PECST867]` > **Q: (a)** With a suitable **block diagram**, explain the architecture of a **Fibre Channel Switched Fabric (FC-SW) SAN topology**. Describe the role of **N_Ports, F_Ports, E_Ports, and FCIDs** in the fabric. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** A financial services company has **500 servers** and **20 storage arrays** that must communicate with very high reliability and minimum latency. The IT team is considering three SAN topologies. **Recommend the most suitable topology with detailed justification**, including how **zoning and redundancy** are achieved. **(7 marks)** --- #### Part (a) Model Answer — Switched Fabric Architecture (7 Marks) **1. Architecture Overview (2 marks):** A Fibre Channel Switched Fabric SAN consists of one or more FC switches interconnected via **E_Ports** (Expansion Ports). End devices (servers, storage arrays) connect to switches via **N_Ports** ↔ **F_Ports**. The fabric provides: - **24-bit FCID** addressing (Domain.Area.Port) - **Cut-through or store-and-forward** switching - **Class of Service** support (Classes 1, 2, 3, 4, F) - **FSPF (Fabric Shortest Path First)** routing protocol between switches **2. Port Roles (3 marks):** | Port Type | Location | Function | |---|---|---| | **N_Port** | End device (HBA / storage controller) | Initiates / receives FC frames | | **F_Port** | Switch fabric port | Terminates N_Port links | | **E_Port** | Switch-to-switch link | Forms Inter-Switch Links (ISL) | | **G_Port** | Generic switch port | Auto-configures as F_Port or E_Port | | **FL_Port** | Switch port in loop mode | Connects FC-AL segments to fabric | | **NL_Port** | End-device port with loop + fabric support | Hybrid L_Port + N_Port | **3. FCID Structure (2 marks):** $$\underbrace{8 \text{ bits}}_{Domain\_ID} \cdot \underbrace{8 \text{ bits}}_{Area\_ID} \cdot \underbrace{8 \text{ bits}}{Port\_ID} = 24 \text{ bits total} = 16{,}777{,}216 \text{ addresses}$$ - **Domain_ID:** Identifies the switch (1-239 usable) - **Area_ID:** Group of ports within a switch (typically a blade) - **Port_ID:** Individual F_Port **Block Diagram:** (refer to Section 4.3 of these notes) --- #### Part (b) Model Answer — Recommendation for Financial Services (7 Marks) **Recommendation: Fibre Channel Switched Fabric (FC-SW) with Dual-Fabric Redundancy and Zoning.** **1. Justification — Device Count (2 marks):** - 500 servers + 20 arrays = **520 end devices** - FC-P2P: completely infeasible - FC-AL: $520 > 126$ → **infeasible** - FC-SW: 520 ≪ 16.7M → **fully supported** **2. Justification — Latency & Throughput (2 marks):** Financial workloads require: - **Microsecond-level latency** for trade execution - **High IOPS** (millions of I/Os per second) for OLTP databases - FC-SW provides **dedicated full-duplex bandwidth per port** with **cut-through switching** for sub-microsecond latency — essential for trading systems. **3. Redundancy Architecture (1.5 marks):** - **Dual Fabric:** Two completely independent FC-SW fabrics (Fabric A and Fabric B) - Every server has **dual HBAs**, one connected to each fabric - Every storage array exposes LUNs on **both fabrics** - If one fabric fails, the other continues serving I/O — **zero downtime** **4. Zoning for Security & Isolation (1.5 marks):** - **Zoning** partitions the fabric so that only authorized server-to-storage pairs can communicate - **WWN Zoning** (recommended for financial sector) uses World Wide Names — more secure than port-based zoning - Each trading server zone is isolated from development and admin zones - Combined with **LUN masking** at the storage array, this provides defense-in-depth security > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Question B:** > - **Common Mistake 1:** Students often recommend FC-AL for "cost reasons" without checking the device count constraint ($520 > 126$). This immediately fails the feasibility test and costs **2-3 marks**. > - **Common Mistake 2:** Students mention "redundancy" but do not specify **dual-fabric architecture** with **dual HBAs**. KTU 2024 specifically requires explanation of *how* redundancy is achieved. > - **Common Mistake 3:** Confusing **zoning** (fabric-level, done on the switch) with **LUN masking** (storage-level, done on the array). Examiners check this distinction carefully. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 — SAN Topologies: High-Density Revision Sheet** ### A. Core Topologies — One-Line Definitions - **Point-to-Point (FC-P2P):** 2 devices, direct link, lowest latency, non-scalable - **Arbitrated Loop (FC-AL):** 126 devices, shared unidirectional ring, half-duplex, arbitrated - **Switched Fabric (FC-SW):** 16.7M devices, dedicated full-duplex, intelligent routing ### B. Must-Memorize Numbers - **126** = max devices on FC-AL - **16,777,216** = max FCIDs on FC-SW (24-bit) - **239** = usable Domain_IDs per fabric - **8b/10b** = FC line encoding (causes 20% overhead) - **2112 bytes** = max FC frame payload ### C. Must-Memorize Port Types - **N_Port** = end device ↔ fabric - **F_Port** = fabric ↔ end device - **E_Port** = switch ↔ switch - **L_Port** = loop device - **NL_Port** = loop + fabric hybrid - **FL_Port** = fabric ↔ loop bridge - **G_Port** = auto-configurable ### D. Must-Memorize Primitive Signals (FC-AL) - **ARB** = Arbitrate (request access) - **OPN** = Open (establish loop circuit) - **CLS** = Close (release loop) - **LIP** = Loop Initialization Primitive ### E. Must-Memorize FCID Structure $$\underbrace{D}_{8\,bits} \cdot \underbrace{A}_{8\,bits} \cdot \underbrace{P}{8\,bits} = 24 \text{ bits} = 16{,}777{,}216$$ ### F. Common Exam Traps to Avoid - ❌ Saying FC-AL supports 128 devices (correct: **126**) - ❌ Confusing AL_PA with FCID - ❌ Saying FC-SW is "half-duplex" (it is **full-duplex**) - ❌ Forgetting the 8b/10b encoding overhead in throughput calculations - ❌ Recommending a topology without checking device count feasibility - ❌ Confusing **zoning** (switch-level) with **LUN masking** (array-level) ### G. Quick Decision Rule - **≤ 2 devices** → P2P - **3–126 devices, budget-constrained, low concurrency** → FC-AL - **> 126 devices OR high concurrency OR mission-critical** → **FC-SW** (always preferred in 2024) > [!TIP] > **Final Exam Strategy:** Always start any SAN topology question with a **labeled block diagram** (worth 1-2 marks by itself), then layer the explanation. The KTU 2024 marking scheme gives disproportionate weight to **neat diagrams with port-type labels** — make this your competitive advantage.

topics.title: iSCSI SAN - iSCSI Names
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST867 - STORAGE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** iSCSI SAN - iSCSI Names

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

An **iSCSI Name** is a worldwide unique, location-independent identifier assigned to an **iSCSI Node** (either an iSCSI Initiator or an iSCSI Target) to unambiguously identify it on an iSCSI Storage Area Network. It is defined in the **RFC 3720 / RFC 7143** standards and serves as the logical identity of the node, completely decoupled from its physical IP address, TCP port, or geographical location.

The two officially supported formats for iSCSI names are:
1. **IQN (iSCSI Qualified Name)** — the most widely used format
2. **EUI (Extended Unique Identifier)** — based on the IEEE EUI-64 format

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The iSCSI Name is exchanged during the **iSCSI Login Phase** as part of the Login Request / Login Response PDUs. It is the *first* identifying parameter negotiated before any SCSI command can be transported. Without a valid iSCSI Name, the iSCSI session cannot be established.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a multinational courier company that delivers packages:
- Every customer has a **unique, permanent account number** (e.g., `CUST-1985-2024-India-Mumbai-Rajesh`). This number never changes, even if the customer moves houses, changes cities, or switches phone numbers.
- The courier company uses this **account number** to look up *where* to deliver the package *now*, but the number itself never encodes the address.

In this analogy:
- The **Account Number** = **iSCSI Name** (logical, permanent identity)
- The **Current Delivery Address** = **IP Address** (physical, transient locator)
- The **Courier Company** = **iSCSI Network**
- The **Package** = **SCSI Command / Data Block**

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Students:** Just like an Aadhaar number in India identifies a person for life regardless of address, an iSCSI Name identifies a storage device for life regardless of network changes. This **independence of identity from location** is what makes iSCSI a robust SAN protocol.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Encoding

| Parameter | Value / Standard |
|---|---|
| IQN Maximum Length | **223 bytes** (UTF-8 encoded) |
| IQN Minimum Length | **16 bytes** |
| EUI Length | Exactly **20 bytes** (including `eui.` prefix) |
| Naming Authority Type | **"org"** for organizations, **"com"** for commercial, **"net"** for networks, etc. |
| Encoding Standard | **UTF-8** (RFC 3629) |
| Governing RFC | **RFC 3720**, updated by **RFC 7143** |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of an IQN (iSCSI Qualified Name)

An IQN follows a strict hierarchical grammar:

```
iqn.YYYY-MM.<reversed-domain>:<optional-unique-string>
```

**Component-by-Component Breakdown:**

- **`iqn.`** — Literal prefix (case-insensitive). Identifies this string as an IQN. Fixed.
- **`YYYY-MM`** — The year and month (4 digits, hyphen, 2 digits) when the **naming authority** was registered/established. This date is *not* the manufacturing date of the device. Example: `2003-08`.
- **`<reversed-domain>`** — The official domain name of the organization in **reversed** order. Example: `com.example` (if the company owns `example.com`).
- **`:`** — A literal colon separator.
- **`<optional-unique-string>`** — A colon-separated unique string chosen by the naming authority to identify the specific device. Can include model numbers, serial numbers, hostnames, etc.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Reverse the Domain?** Reversing the domain ensures global uniqueness using the existing domain name system. A company owning `example.com` cannot accidentally collide with `com.example` owned by someone else — they are explicitly distinct names.

## 2.2 Anatomy of an EUI Name

The EUI format is much simpler and is used when the organization owns an **IEEE OUI (Organizationally Unique Identifier)**:

```
eui.<64-bit-hexadecimal-identifier>
```

- **`eui.`** — Literal prefix (lowercase only).
- **`<64-bit-hexadecimal-identifier>`** — 16 hexadecimal characters representing the IEEE EUI-64 value, typically derived from the device's MAC address or a globally assigned IEEE identifier.

**Example:** `eui.02004567A425B9D4`

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The EUI form is **less commonly used** than IQN in practice. KTU examiners frequently contrast IQN vs. EUI, so memorize the difference.

## 2.3 Rules & Constraints (High-Yield for KTU)

1. **IQN strings** must not exceed **223 bytes** and must be at least **16 bytes** long.
2. The character set is **UTF-8**; however, the characters `,`, `"`, and `\` are reserved and must not appear.
3. Lowercase letters are **preferred**, but matching is case-insensitive.
4. The `iqn.` prefix is **case-insensitive**, but `eui.` is **case-sensitive** (must be lowercase).
5. The naming authority (date + reversed domain) must be a **registered domain** owned by the organization OR a domain assigned to the organization by a domain registrar.
6. The iSCSI Name is **persistent** — it survives reboots, IP changes, hardware migrations.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Example / Note |
|---|---|---|
| IQN Grammar | `iqn.YYYY-MM.reversed.domain:unique` | `iqn.2024-01.org.ktu.storage:array1.disk3` |
| EUI Grammar | `eui.HEX64` (16 hex chars) | `eui.3A5C6F1B2D4E7081` |
| Max IQN Length | **223 bytes** | UTF-8 encoded |
| Min IQN Length | **16 bytes** | Including `iqn.` prefix |
| Date Format | **YYYY-MM** (ISO 8601) | `2003-08`, `2024-01` |
| Reserved Chars in IQN | `, " \` | Must NOT appear |
| Case Sensitivity (IQN) | Case-INSENSITIVE matching | `IQN.2003-08...` ≡ `iqn.2003-08...` |
| Case Sensitivity (EUI) | Case-SENSITIVE prefix | Must be lowercase `eui.` |
| RFC Reference | **RFC 3720**, **RFC 7143** | Internet Standard |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** In real-world enterprise data centers, the iSCSI Name is stored in the **/etc/iscsi/initiatorname.iscsi** file (Linux) and the iSCSI Target's configuration database. SAN management software (e.g., NetApp OnCommand, Dell Unisphere) uses the IQN for zoning, LUN masking, and CHAP authentication. Mastering this concept directly maps to **system administrator interviews and SAN certifications**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: Constructing an IQN from Scratch

**Problem (KTU-Style):** A startup company **"Kerala Cloud Pvt. Ltd."** owns the domain `keralacloud.in`. They registered their company in **August 2023**. They have an iSCSI Target device with serial number `SN-7741-DISK-A` that needs an iSCSI Name. Construct a valid IQN.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1:** Begin with the literal prefix `iqn.`

$$ \text{Name} = \text{``iqn.''} $$

**Step 2:** Append the date when the naming authority (company) was established. The problem states **August 2023**, so the format is `YYYY-MM`:

$$ \text{Name} = \text{``iqn.2023-08.''} $$

**Step 3:** Reverse the official domain name. The company owns `keralacloud.in`, so reverse it:

$$ \text{``keralacloud.in''} \rightarrow \text{``in.keralacloud''} $$

Append this to the name:

$$ \text{Name} = \text{``iqn.2023-08.in.keralacloud''} $$

**Step 4:** Add the literal colon separator:

$$ \text{Name} = \text{``iqn.2023-08.in.keralacloud:''} $$

**Step 5:** Append a unique identifying string for the specific device. Using the serial number:

$$ \text{Name} = \text{``iqn.2023-08.in.keralacloud:SN-7741-DISK-A''} $$

**Step 6:** Validation — check against all KTU constraints:

- Starts with `iqn.` ✓
- Date format `2023-08` valid ✓
- Reversed domain present ✓
- Total length check: 
  $$ \text{Length} = 4 + 1 + 7 + 1 + 13 + 1 + 13 = 40 \text{ bytes} $$
  Since $16 \le 40 \le 223$ ✓
- No reserved characters (`,`, `"`, `\`) present ✓

**Final Valid IQN:**

$$ \boxed{\text{iqn.2023-08.in.keralacloud:SN-7741-DISK-A}} $$

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: IQN vs. EUI Comparison

The following table is a **high-yield analytical matrix** frequently expected in KTU 14-mark answers.

| Dimension | IQN (iSCSI Qualified Name) | EUI (Extended Unique Identifier) |
|---|---|---|
| **Prefix** | `iqn.` (case-insensitive) | `eui.` (case-sensitive, lowercase only) |
| **Length** | 16 to 223 bytes | Exactly 20 bytes (`eui.` + 16 hex) |
| **Uniqueness Basis** | Date + Reversed Domain + Unique String | IEEE-assigned 64-bit identifier |
| **Registration Required?** | No external registration; uses existing domain | Yes, requires IEEE OUI/EUI-64 assignment |
| **Readability** | Human-readable (recommended) | Machine-readable (hexadecimal) |
| **Practical Use** | Most common in software iSCSI (e.g., Linux open-iscsi) | Common in hardware iSCSI HBAs |
| **Governing Body** | IETF (RFC 3720) | IEEE |
| **Flexibility** | Highly flexible (free-form unique string) | Rigid (must be 16 hex digits) |
| **Discovery Friendly?** | Yes (descriptive) | No (cryptic) |

---

## 3.3 Validation Algorithm: Is the IQN Valid?

For a string $S$ to be a valid IQN, it must satisfy the Boolean conjunction:

$$
\text{Valid}(S) \;=\; \text{LengthOK}(S) \;\land\; \text{PrefixOK}(S) \;\land\; \text{DateOK}(S) \;\land\; \text{NoReserved}(S) \;\land\; \text{UTF8OK}(S)
$$

Where each sub-predicate is:

- $\text{LengthOK}(S)$: $16 \le \vert S \vert_{\text{bytes}} \le 223$
- $\text{PrefixOK}(S)$: $S$ starts with `iqn.` (case-insensitive)
- $\text{DateOK}(S)$: After the prefix, the next 7 characters match the regex `\d{4}-\d{2}`
- $\text{NoReserved}(S)$: $S$ contains no character from the set $\{$`,`$,$`"`$,$`\`$\}$
- $\text{UTF8OK}(S)$: $S$ is valid UTF-8 per RFC 3629

Only if **all five** predicates evaluate to **TRUE** is the IQN accepted by the iSCSI protocol stack.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example: Identifying a Mismatched IQN

**Problem:** Examine the following three candidate IQNs and identify which are valid, with justification:

1. `iqn.2024-12.com.example:disk1`
2. `IQN.24-12.com.example:disk1`
3. `iqn.2024-12.com.example:dis,k1`

**Solution:**

| Candidate | Verdict | Failing Predicate |
|---|---|---|
| #1 | **VALID** | All predicates satisfied |
| #2 | **INVALID** | `DateOK` fails: `24-12` does not match `\d{4}-\d{2}` (missing leading zeros) |
| #3 | **INVALID** | `NoReserved` fails: contains a comma `,` |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Anatomy of an IQN

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["iqn."]:::prefix --> B["YYYY-MM"]:::date
    B --> C["Reversed Domain"]:::domain
    C --> D[":"]:::separator
    D --> E["Unique Device String"]:::unique
    E --> F["VALID IQN"]:::result

    classDef prefix fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef date fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#4682B4,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef domain fill:#98FB98,stroke:#228B22,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef separator fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#DC143C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef unique fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#8B008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
    classDef result fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart: iSCSI Name Resolution During Login

```mermaid
graph TD
    Start["iSCSI Session Initiated"]:::start --> LoginReq["Initiator sends Login Request PDU"]:::pdu
    LoginReq --> ParseName["Parse iSCSI Name Field"]:::parse
    ParseName --> CheckFormat{"Is Name IQN or EUI?"}:::decision
    CheckFormat -->|IQN| ValidIQN{"Valid IQN Format?"}:::decision
    CheckFormat -->|EUI| ValidEUI{"Valid EUI Format?"}:::decision
    ValidIQN -->|No| Reject1["Send Login Reject - Invalid Name"]:::reject
    ValidEUI -->|No| Reject2["Send Login Reject - Invalid Name"]:::reject
    ValidIQN -->|Yes| Auth["Proceed to CHAP Authentication"]:::auth
    ValidEUI -->|Yes| Auth
    Auth -->|Success| SessionOpen["iSCSI Session Established"]:::success
    Auth -->|Failure| Reject3["Send Login Reject - Auth Failed"]:::reject

    classDef start fill:#4682B4,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
    classDef pdu fill:#FFA500,stroke:#8B4513,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef parse fill:#9370DB,stroke:#4B0082,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
    classDef decision fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef reject fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
    classDef auth fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef success fill:#32CD32,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFF
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: iSCSI Name in the SAN Ecosystem

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph INIT["INITIATOR SIDE"]
        I1["Host Server"]:::host
        I2["iSCSI Initiator Software / HBA"]:::initiator
        I3["IQN: iqn.2024-01.org.ktu.cs:host42"]:::iqn1
    end

    subgraph NET["IP NETWORK - TCP"]
        N1["IP Address: 192.168.10.50"]:::ip
        N2["TCP Port: 3260"]:::port
    end

    subgraph TARG["TARGET SIDE - STORAGE ARRAY"]
        T1["iSCSI Target Controller"]:::target
        T2["IQN: iqn.2023-08.com.dell:equallogic-disk7"]:::iqn2
        T3["LUN 0 - 10 TB Volume"]:::lun
    end

    I1 --> I2
    I2 --> I3
    I3 -. "Logical Identity" .-> N1
    N1 --> N2
    N2 --> T1
    T1 --> T2
    T2 --> T3

    classDef host fill:#4682B4,stroke:#000,color:#FFF
    classDef initiator fill:#5F9EA0,stroke:#000,color:#FFF
    classDef iqn1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000
    classDef ip fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef port fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef target fill:#8B0000,stroke:#FFF,color:#FFF
    classDef iqn2 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000
    classDef lun fill:#32CD32,stroke:#006400,color:#FFF
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Notice that the **IQN flows logically** from Initiator to Target *before* any data is exchanged. The IP address and port are merely the *transport* — the IQN is the *identity*. This is the core architectural insight KTU examiners test.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define iSCSI Name. List the two formats supported by the iSCSI protocol.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An **iSCSI Name** is a worldwide unique, location-independent identifier used to identify an iSCSI node (initiator or target) on an iSCSI SAN. It is defined in RFC 3720 and remains persistent irrespective of IP address changes. The two formats are:
1. **IQN (iSCSI Qualified Name)** — text-based, starts with `iqn.`
2. **EUI (Extended Unique Identifier)** — hexadecimal, starts with `eui.` (1 mark for definition, 1 mark each for the two formats)

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**What is the role of the `YYYY-MM` field in an iSCSI Qualified Name?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The `YYYY-MM` field (2 marks) represents the **year and month** when the **naming authority** (typically the organization owning the reversed domain) was registered or established (1 mark). It is not the manufacturing date of the device. This field ensures the global uniqueness of the IQN, since two different organizations that own the same domain name at different times will produce distinct IQNs.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question B-A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the structure of an iSCSI Qualified Name (IQN) with a suitable example. State the rules for IQN length and reserved characters. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Differentiate between IQN and EUI naming formats. Construct a valid IQN for a company named **"TechWave Solutions"** that owns the domain `techwave.org`, was registered in **March 2022**, and has a storage target with hostname `storage-prod-01`. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Structure of an IQN (7 marks):**

The IQN follows the syntax:
$$ \text{``iqn.YYYY-MM.reversed.domain:unique-string''} $$

**Components:**
- `iqn.` — Literal prefix identifying this as an IQN (case-insensitive) **(1 mark)**
- `YYYY-MM` — Year and month of naming authority registration **(1 mark)**
- `reversed.domain` — Official domain of the organization in reverse order **(1 mark)**
- `:` — Literal separator **(0.5 mark)**
- `unique-string` — Free-form identifier for the specific device **(1 mark)**

**Example:** `iqn.2023-11.com.netapp:sn-12345-lun0`

**Rules (2 marks):**
- **Length:** 16 to 223 bytes (UTF-8 encoded)
- **Reserved characters:** Must NOT contain `,`, `"`, or `\`
- **Encoding:** UTF-8 per RFC 3629
- The `iqn.` prefix is case-insensitive; matching is case-insensitive

---

**(b) IQN vs. EUI + Construction (7 marks):**

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Feature | IQN | EUI |
|---|---|---|
| Format | `iqn.YYYY-MM.domain:unique` | `eui.16-hex-chars` |
| Length | 16–223 bytes | Exactly 20 bytes |
| Basis | Date + Domain + String | IEEE EUI-64 |
| Readability | Human-readable | Cryptic |
| Case-sensitivity of prefix | Insensitive | Sensitive (lowercase) |

**Construction Steps (3 marks):**

1. Prefix: `iqn.`
2. Date of registration: `2022-03`
3. Reversed domain of `techwave.org` → `org.techwave`
4. Separator: `:`
5. Unique device string: `storage-prod-01`

**Final Answer:**
$$ \boxed{\text{iqn.2022-03.org.techwave:storage-prod-01}} $$

**Validation:**
- Length = 4+1+7+1+11+1+15 = **40 bytes** ✓ (within 16–223)
- No reserved characters ✓
- Valid `YYYY-MM` format ✓

---

### **Question B-B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the role of iSCSI Names during the iSCSI session login phase. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the IQN validation algorithm. Identify which of the following IQNs are invalid and state the reason: **(7 marks)**
- (i) `iqn.2003-08.com.example:disk1`
- (ii) `iqn.2024-1.com.example:disk1`  
- (iii) `iqn.2024-01.com.example:my"disk"`
- (iv) `eui.3A5C6F1B2D4E7081`

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Role in Login Phase (7 marks):**

During iSCSI session establishment, the iSCSI Name is the **first** parameter exchanged in the **Login Request / Login Response** PDUs over TCP port **3260**. The flow is:

1. **Initiator** opens a TCP connection to Target's IP:3260 **(1 mark)**
2. **Initiator** sends a Login Request PDU containing its IQN **(1 mark)**
3. **Target** parses and validates the IQN format **(1 mark)**
4. **Target** checks the IQN against its Access Control List (ACL) for authorization **(1 mark)**
5. If valid, both sides proceed to **CHAP authentication** using the IQN as the principal identity **(1 mark)**
6. Upon successful authentication, a full iSCSI session is established and SCSI I/O can begin **(1 mark)**
7. **Diagram** (1 mark) — Refer to the **Mermaid Flowchart in Section 4.2** for the architectural visualization.

**Significance:**
- The iSCSI Name acts as the **logical, stable identity** of the node, independent of its physical IP address.
- It enables **persistent zoning** and **LUN masking** policies in the SAN.

---

**(b) IQN Validation Algorithm + Validity Check (7 marks):**

**Algorithm (4 marks):** A candidate IQN is valid **if and only if** ALL the following hold:

$$ \text{Valid}(S) \iff \bigwedge_{i=1}^{5} P_i(S) $$

- $P_1$: $16 \le \text{byteLen}(S) \le 223$
- $P_2$: $S$ starts with `iqn.` (case-insensitive)
- $P_3$: After prefix, characters 5–11 match `\d{4}-\d{2}` (i.e., exact format `YYYY-MM`)
- $P_4$: $S$ contains no character from $\{$`,`$,$`"`$,$`\`$\}$
- $P_5$: $S$ is valid UTF-8

**Validity Check of Given IQNs (3 marks):**

| # | IQN | Valid? | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| (i) | `iqn.2003-08.com.example:disk1` | **VALID** | All predicates pass |
| (ii) | `iqn.2024-1.com.example:disk1` | **INVALID** | $P_3$ fails: month must be 2 digits (`01` not `1`) |
| (iii) | `iqn.2024-01.com.example:my"disk"` | **INVALID** | $P_4$ fails: contains reserved character `"` |
| (iv) | `eui.3A5C6F1B2D4E7081` | **VALID EUI** | EUI is a valid iSCSI Name format, length 20, lowercase `eui.` |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Date format error:** Writing `2024-1` instead of `2024-01`. The month MUST be zero-padded to 2 digits. **(-2 marks)**
> 2. **Forgetting to reverse the domain:** Writing `iqn.2023-08.com.example` instead of `iqn.2023-08.com.example` is acceptable (since `com.example` *is* a reversed form if they consider it as the authority), but writing `iqn.2023-08.example.com` is WRONG. **(-1 to -2 marks)**
> 3. **Confusing IQN with EUI length:** IQN is **16–223 bytes**, EUI is exactly **20 bytes**. Mixing these up loses a comparison-table mark. **(-1 mark)**
> 4. **Using reserved characters:** Forgetting that `,`, `"`, `\` are forbidden in IQN strings. **(-1 mark)**
> 5. **Not stating the RFC reference:** KTU 2024 scheme values standards-awareness. Always cite **RFC 3720 / RFC 7143**. **(-0.5 mark)**
> 6. **Skipping the block diagram** in login-phase questions — diagrams carry **1–2 marks** even if the text is correct.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- ⭐ **iSCSI Name** = unique, location-independent identifier for an iSCSI node.
- ⭐ Two formats: **IQN** (text, `iqn.YYYY-MM.domain:unique`) and **EUI** (hex, `eui.16hex`).
- ⭐ IQN length: **16–223 bytes**; EUI length: **exactly 20 bytes**.
- ⭐ The `YYYY-MM` is the registration date of the **naming authority**, NOT the device.
- ⭐ Domain must be **reversed** (e.g., `example.com` → `com.example`).
- ⭐ **Reserved characters** in IQN: comma (`,`), double-quote (`"`), backslash (`\`).
- ⭐ `iqn.` prefix is **case-insensitive**; `eui.` prefix must be **lowercase**.
- ⭐ Defined in **RFC 3720** (updated by **RFC 7143**); UTF-8 encoding per RFC 3629.
- ⭐ iSCSI Name is exchanged in the **Login Request / Login Response PDUs** during session establishment.
- ⭐ IQN is used for **zoning, LUN masking, and CHAP authentication** in enterprise SANs.
- ⭐ The IQN is **persistent** — it survives IP changes, hardware migrations, and reboots.
- ⭐ Default Linux initiator IQN location: `/etc/iscsi/initiatorname.iscsi`.
- ⭐ EUI names are typically derived from **IEEE OUI** assignments or device MAC addresses.
- ⭐ KTU favourite question: *"Construct a valid IQN for [company]..."* — practice the 5-step construction.
- ⭐ KTU favourite comparison: **IQN vs. EUI** — always include a comparison table in 14-mark answers.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sessions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECST867 - STORAGE SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Sessions ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **iSCSI Session**: An iSCSI session is a logical relationship established between an iSCSI initiator and an iSCSI target that persists across one or more TCP connections, enabling the ordered exchange of SCSI commands, data, and responses within a Storage Area Network (SAN). A **Session** in iSCSI is formally defined as the complete set of parameters and state information shared between an initiator and a target that uniquely identifies an ongoing communication relationship. Each session is identified by a tuple consisting of: - **ISID (Initiator Session ID)** — a **6-byte** identifier generated by the initiator - **Target Portal Group Tag (TPGT)** — identifies the target portal group - **TSIH (Target Session Identifying Handle)** — a **2-byte** value assigned by the target > [!NOTE] > **Core Distinction**: A *Session* is a logical construct, whereas a *Connection* is a physical TCP-level link. Multiple connections (up to a negotiated maximum) can belong to a single session for redundancy and load balancing. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!TIP] > **Real-World Analogy — The "Phone Call vs. Conversation" Model** > > Imagine a long business relationship between a customer and a company: > - The **Session** is the entire business relationship (e.g., "Account #4471") — it includes the customer's identity, agreed terms, account balance, and ongoing history. > - The **TCP Connection** is one specific phone call currently in progress to discuss a particular transaction. > - The customer (initiator) can hang up one phone line and call back on a different line, but the business relationship (session) continues seamlessly. > - The **Login Phase** is the authentication and negotiation of terms when the account is first opened. > - The **Logout Phase** is the formal closure of the account. > > This analogy makes it immediately clear why **multiple connections per session** are useful — they provide fault tolerance, similar to having backup communication channels in a critical business relationship. ### 1.3 Classification of Sessions Sessions are classified into two principal categories per the **RFC 3720 / RFC 7143** standards: | Session Type | Primary Purpose | Lifespan | |--------------|-----------------|----------| | **Normal Session** | Full-duplex SCSI I/O operations (reads, writes, reservations) | Long-lived, persistent | | **Discovery Session** | Query target portal information using `SendTargets` text command | Ephemeral, short-lived | ### 1.4 Standard Session Metrics > [!NOTE] > **Key Numerical Parameters (must be memorized for KTU exams):** > - **ISID size:** **48 bits (6 bytes)** > - **TSIH size:** **16 bits (2 bytes)** > - **CID size:** **16 bits (2 bytes)** > - **Reserved TSIH value `0x0000`:** Indicates a "session-less" discovery connection > - **Default MaxConnections:** **1** (negotiable upward) ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Structural Anatomy of an iSCSI Session A session consists of three operational phases that occur in strict sequential order: 1. **Login Phase (Security & Operational Negotiation)** - Sub-divided into two stages: - **Stage 1 — SecurityNegotiation**: Negotiation of authentication mechanisms (None, CHAP, SRP, Kerberos) - **Stage 2 — LoginOperationalNegotiation**: Negotiation of operational parameters (MaxConnections, MaxRecvDataSegmentLength, HeaderDigest, DataDigest, etc.) 2. **Full Feature Phase** - All SCSI commands, data, and responses are exchanged - Connection(s) are established and active 3. **Logout Phase** - Graceful session termination initiated by either side ### 2.2 Operational Parameter Negotiation (Key-Value Pairs) The login phase exchanges **key=value** text pairs in **transit (T)** or **final (F)** mode. Critical negotiated parameters include: - `MaxConnections` — maximum number of connections the session will support - `InitialR2T` — whether unsolicited R2T is allowed - `ImmediateData` — whether immediate data is permitted in a command PDU - `MaxBurstLength` — maximum data in a single unsolicited burst - `FirstBurstLength` — maximum immediate data size - `MaxRecvDataSegmentLength` — maximum data segment receiver will accept - `HeaderDigest` and `DataDigest` — CRC integrity algorithms (None, CRC32C) - `AuthMethod` — authentication protocol (None, CHAP, SRP) - `SessionType` — declares Normal or Discovery session ### 2.3 Session Identification Math > [!IMPORTANT] > **Session Identity Formula:** > > A session is uniquely identified across the iSCSI network by the **ordered tuple**: > $$\text{SessionID} \;=\; \langle \text{InitiatorName},\; \text{ISID},\; \text{TargetName},\; \text{TPGT},\; \text{TSIH} \rangle$$ > > For a discovery session, the tuple is: > $$\text{DiscoverySessionID} \;=\; \langle \text{InitiatorName},\;
