\text{ISID},\; 0x0000 \rangle$$ > > because TSIH is the reserved value $0\text{x}0000$. ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Symbol / Parameter | Meaning | Value / Range | Used In | |--------------------|---------|---------------|---------| | $ISID$ | Initiator Session ID | 48 bits | Session identification | | $TSIH$ | Target Session Handle | 16 bits (0x0000 reserved) | Session identification | | $CID$ | Connection ID | 16 bits (0 to 65,535) | Connection identification | | $CmdSN$ | Command Sequence Number | 32-bit unsigned | Ordering of commands | | $ExpCmdSN$ | Expected Command Sequence Number | 32-bit | Window tracking | | $MaxCmdSN$ | Maximum Command Sequence Number | 32-bit | Flow control upper bound | | $\vert \text{MaxCmdSN} - \text{ExpCmdSN} \vert$ | Outstanding command window size | — | Flow control credit | | $StatSN$ | Status Sequence Number | 32-bit | Ordering of status responses | | $TTT$ | Target Transfer Tag | 32-bit | Data transfer correlation | | $ITT$ | Initiator Task Tag | 32-bit | Command correlation | | $MaxRecvDataSegmentLength$ | Max data per PDU | Negotiated, typically **64 KB – 1 MB** | Data PDU sizing | | $MaxBurstLength$ | Max unsolicited data per SCSI write | Negotiated | Bursting | | $FirstBurstLength$ | Max immediate data in command PDU | Negotiated | Immediate data | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pitfall:** Do **not** confuse the three identifier types! *ISID* is per-session (initiator-side), *TSIH* is per-session (target-side), and *CID* is per-connection. Mixing these is a guaranteed mark-deduction in exam answers. ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility Sessions are foundational in: - **Enterprise SAN deployments** — providing redundant multi-path I/O between hosts and storage arrays - **Cloud block storage services** (AWS EBS, Azure Managed Disks) — abstract iSCSI/equivalent sessions for virtual machine disks - **Hyperconverged infrastructures** — using multi-connection sessions for load balancing across NICs - **Disaster recovery** — session reinstatement allows seamless failover after transient network failures ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Session Identification Uniqueness **Problem**: Show that the iSCSI session identifier tuple is globally unique across the iSCSI fabric, even when two initiators pick the same ISID. **Given**: - Two independent initiators $I_1$ and $I_2$ - Both randomly select the same $ISID = 0\text{x}A1B2C3D4E5F6$ - Both connect to the same target $T$ **Step 1 — Initiator Discrimination**: The *InitiatorName* field is an iSCSI-qualified name (IQN) of the form: $$IQN \;=\; \text{iqn.yyyy-mm.com.example:unique\_string}$$ Because $I_1 \neq I_2$ as strings, the session tuples become: $$\text{Session}_1 \;=\; \langle I_1,\; ISID,\; T,\; TPGT,\; TSIH_1 \rangle$$ $$\text{Session}_2 \;=\; \langle I_2,\; ISID,\; T,\; TPGT,\; TSIH_2 \rangle$$ Since the first component differs, the tuples are unique. **Step 2 — Target Discrimination**: The target further reinforces uniqueness by assigning a unique $TSIH$ to each incoming session request. The target must guarantee: $$TSIH_i \;\neq\; TSIH_j \quad \text{for all active sessions } i, j \text{ originating from } I_k$$ **Step 3 — Mathematical Statement of Uniqueness**: A session identity function $f: \text{Sessions} \to \mathbb{N}^{128}$ is **injective** if and only if: $$f(S_a) \;=\; f(S_b) \;\Longleftrightarrow\; a \;=\; b$$ Since the tuple components are concatenated as: $$f(S) \;=\; \text{hash}(\text{InitiatorName} \;\Vert\; ISID \;\Vert\; \text{TargetName} \;\Vert\; TPGT \;\Vert\; TSIH)$$ and each component is independently unique per its source entity, the injection property holds by construction. $\blacksquare$ ### 3.2 Derivation: Maximum Concurrent Outstanding Commands **Problem**: Compute the maximum number of commands that can be in flight at any moment for a given iSCSI session. **Given**: - $CmdSN$ is a **32-bit** unsigned counter - $ExpCmdSN$ is the next expected $CmdSN$ from the initiator - $MaxCmdSN$ is the highest $CmdSN$ the target will accept **Step 1 — Define the Outstanding Command Window**: $$W \;=\; (MaxCmdSN - ExpCmdSN + 1) \mod 2^{32}$$ **Step 2 — Bounding the Window**: The initiator must ensure at all times: $$W \;\leq\; MaxOutstandingR2T \;\leq\; \text{implementation limit}$$ **Step 3 — Numerical Example**: Suppose the initiator sends a command with $CmdSN = 100$. The target responds with status $StatSN = 50$ and updates its parameters: $$ExpCmdSN = 101, \quad MaxCmdSN = 250$$ The outstanding window becomes: $$W \;=\; (250 - 101 + 1) \mod 2^{32} \;=\; 150$$ So the initiator may now have up to **150 commands** outstanding before the target issues a window drop NOP-IN to push $MaxCmdSN$ forward. ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Session vs. Connection Parameters The following table maps each iSCSI login parameter to whether it is **session-wide** (negotiated once, applies to the whole session) or **connection-specific** (may be re-negotiated per connection): | Login Parameter | Scope | Negotiation Stage | Required/Optional | |-----------------|-------|-------------------|-------------------| | `MaxConnections` | Session | Login | Required | | `InitialR2T` | Session | Login | Required | | `ImmediateData` | Session | Login | Required | | `MaxBurstLength` | Session | Login | Required | | `FirstBurstLength` | Session | Login | Required | | `MaxRecvDataSegmentLength` | Connection | Login | Required | | `HeaderDigest` | Connection | Login | Optional | | `DataDigest` | Connection | Login | Optional | | `AuthMethod` | Connection | Security | Required | | `SessionType` | Session | Login | Required | | `TargetName` | Session | Login | Required | | `InitiatorName` | Session | Security/Login | Required | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Insight**: Session-wide parameters must remain consistent across **all** connections within the session. If an attempt is made to negotiate a different value on a second connection, the target **must reject** that connection per RFC 7143. ### 3.4 Derivation: Connection Failure Detection and Session Reinstatement **Problem**: Determine the maximum recovery time for a failed connection within an iSCSI session. **Given**: - TCP retransmission timeout: $RTO = 200$ ms (typical) - Maximum TCP retransmissions: $N_{max} = 5$ - iSCSI NOP-OUT polling interval: $T_{poll} = 5$ s **Step 1 — TCP Detection Time**: $$T_{TCP} \;=\; RTO \times N_{max} \;=\; 200 \text{ ms} \times 5 \;=\; 1.0 \text{ s}$$ **Step 2 — iSCSI Timeout Addition**: The iSCSI layer adds an additional detection delay bounded by the $MaxTime2Retain$ or $MaxTime2Wait$ parameters: $$T_{detect} \;=\; T_{TCP} + T_{poll} \;=\; 1.0 \text{ s} + 5.0 \text{ s} \;=\; 6.0 \text{ s}$$ **Step 3 — Total Recovery Time**: $$T_{recovery} \;=\; T_{detect} + T_{reinstate} \;\approx\; 6.0 \text{ s} + 2.0 \text{ s} \;=\; 8.0 \text{ s}$$ This is the time the session is effectively degraded before MPIO (Multi-Path I/O) or session reinstatement restores full operation. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: iSCSI Session Lifecycle State Machine ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Free Free --> SecurityNegotiation : Login Request (Stage 1) SecurityNegotiation --> LoginOperational : Security Accept SecurityOperational --> LoginOperational : T bit, CSG=0 LoginOperational --> FullFeature : Login Accept (CSG=3, NSG=3, T=1, F=1) LoginOperational --> LoginReject : Parameter Negotiation Failure SecurityNegotiation --> LoginReject : Auth Failure LoginReject --> Free : Connection Cleanup FullFeature --> FullFeature : SCSI Command / Data / Response FullFeature --> Logout : Logout Request Logout --> Free : Logout Response (clean close) Logout --> FullFeature : Connection Reinstatement FullFeature --> Free : TCP Connection Drop + Cleanup Free --> [*] ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Session Identification and Parameter Negotiation ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Initiator IQN: iqn.2024.com.kerala.host1] --> B[Generate ISID: 0xA1B2C3D4E5F6 48 bits] B --> C[Open TCP Connection to Target Portal 3260] C --> D[Login Request PDU CSG equals 0 Stage 1 Security] D --> E{Security Accept?} E -- No --> F[Login Reject Reason Auth Failed] E -- Yes --> G[Login Request PDU CSG equals 1 Stage 2 Operational Params] G --> H{Negotiation OK?} H -- No --> I[Login Reject Reason Protocol Error] H -- Yes --> J[Target Allocates TSIH 16 bits] J --> K[Login Accept PDU NSG equals 3 F bit equals 1 T bit equals 1] K --> L[Session Established Full Feature Phase] L --> M[Add Connection 2 with New CID 16 bits] M --> N[Multi Connection Session Active] L --> O[Logout Request PDU Reason 0 clean close] O --> P[Logout Response PDU clean] P --> Q[Session Terminated Return to Free] ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Architecture Diagram: Multi-Connection Session with MPIO ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph InitiatorHost A1[iSCSI Initiator Engine] A2[MPIO Layer] end subgraph SessionS1[Session 1 ISID 0xA1B2C3 ISID] C1[Connection CID 1 NIC 1] C2[Connection CID 2 NIC 2] C3[Connection CID 3 NIC 3] end subgraph TargetArray T1[iSCSI Target Portal Group TPGT 1] T2[SCSI LUN Engine] end A1 --> A2 A2 -- CID 1 --> C1 A2 -- CID 2 --> C2 A2 -- CID 3 --> C3 C1 --> T1 C2 --> T1 C3 --> T1 T1 --> T2 T1 -. "TSIH assigned 0x1234" .- A1 T1 -. "MaxConn equals 3 negotiated" .- A2 ``` ### 4.4 Mermaid Sequence Diagram: Login Phase Handshake ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant I as iSCSI Initiator participant T as iSCSI Target I->>T: TCP SYN to 3260 T-->>I: TCP SYN ACK I->>T: Login Request CSG=0 Stage=Security Note over I,T: AuthMethod=CHAP
InitiatorName=iqn.kerala.host1 T-->>I: Login Accept CSG=0 Security OK I->>T: Login Request CSG=1 Stage=Operational Note over I,T: MaxConnections=3
MaxBurstLength=1MB
ImmediateData=Yes T-->>I: Login Accept CSG=1 TSIH=0x1234 I->>T: Login Request CSG=3 NSG=3 F=1 Confirm T-->>I: Login Accept NSG=3 F=1 T=1 Note over I,T: Full Feature Phase Begins I->>T: SCSI READ (10) CmdSN=1 ITT=0x001 T-->>I: Data-IN PDU T-->>I: SCSI Response StatSN=1 ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] Define an iSCSI session. What is the difference between a session and a connection in iSCSI?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** An **iSCSI session** is a logical relationship between an initiator and a target that identifies a set of TCP connections exchanging SCSI commands, data, and responses. It is uniquely identified by the tuple of *InitiatorName*, *ISID*, *TargetName*, *TPGT*, and *TSIH*. A **connection** is a single TCP-level link between the initiator and the target that belongs to a session. The session is the *logical, persistent* relationship, while the connection is the *physical, transport-level* link. A session may contain one or more connections for redundancy, load balancing, and failover. (3 marks) --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024] List any six operational parameters that are negotiated during the iSCSI login phase.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The following operational parameters are negotiated during the Login Phase: 1. `MaxConnections` — maximum TCP connections per session 2. `InitialR2T` — whether the target may wait for R2T before data 3. `ImmediateData` — allows immediate data attachment to command PDU 4. `MaxBurstLength` — maximum unsolicited data bytes 5. `FirstBurstLength` — maximum immediate data bytes in a command 6. `MaxRecvDataSegmentLength` — maximum data segment in a Data-In PDU Any 6 of the above earn full marks. (3 marks) --- ### 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **Q.A. [KTU University Exam — Model Question]** **(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the **complete lifecycle of an iSCSI session**. Discuss the **Login Phase** in detail, including its two stages and the role of the **CSG (Current Stage)** and **NSG (Next Stage)** code bits. (7 marks) **(b)** Describe the **multi-connection session** concept in iSCSI. Explain how connection failure is detected and how **session reinstatement** recovers the session. (7 marks) --- **Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:** **1. Session Lifecycle Diagram (2 marks):** A session traverses these phases sequentially: **Free → Security Negotiation → Login Operational Negotiation → Full Feature Phase → Logout → Free** **2. Login Phase Detailed Explanation (5 marks):** The Login Phase is the first mandatory step and is performed using *Login Request* and *Login Response* PDUs. It has two distinct sub-stages identified by the 2-bit **CSG** field in the BHS: - **CSG = 0 — SecurityNegotiation Stage (Stage 1):** The initiator proposes authentication methods (e.g., `AuthMethod=CHAP,None`). The target replies with the acceptable method, and CHAP challenge/response exchanges occur. No operational parameters are exchanged yet. - **CSG = 1 — LoginOperationalNegotiation Stage (Stage 2):** After security is agreed, both sides negotiate operational parameters using the Transit (**T**) bit. Each key=value pair is sent with T=0 (intermediate) or T=1 (final). When T=1 and the target agrees, the target responds with `Login Accept`. The **NSG (Next Stage)** field indicates which stage the responder is transitioning to. To enter Full Feature Phase, the initiator sets NSG=3 and T=1, F=1 in the final Login Request; the target confirms with Login Accept (NSG=3, T=1, F=1). After Login Accept, the target allocates a unique **TSIH** and the session enters the **Full Feature Phase**, where SCSI I/O occurs. --- **Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:** **1. Multi-Connection Session (3 marks):** A multi-connection session contains **two or more TCP connections** between the same initiator–target pair, all sharing one *ISID* and *TSH*. Each connection has a unique *CID*. The number is bounded by the negotiated `MaxConnections` (default 1). Benefits: - **Load balancing** across NICs - **Failover** when one connection fails - **Aggregation** of bandwidth **2. Connection Failure Detection (2 marks):** Detection is done via: - TCP keepalive failures - Missed iSCSI NOP-Out/NOP-In heartbeats - Sequence number gaps in *StatSN* or *ExpCmdSN* **3. Session Reinstatement (2 marks):** When a connection drops, the initiator can: 1. Open a **new TCP connection** with a new CID 2. Send a **Login Request** with the **same ISID and TSIH**, but set the `CSG=0` *Reinstating* flag in the BHS 3. The target re-establishes session state and accepts the new connection 4. I/O resumes on the surviving or new connection This allows recovery **without** dropping the entire session. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **Q.B. [KTU University Exam — Model Question]** **(a)** Explain the **session identification parameters** in iSCSI: *ISID*, *TSIH*, and *CID*. State the size, the source (initiator or target), and the uniqueness scope of each. (7 marks) **(b)** Compare **Discovery Sessions** and **Normal Sessions** in iSCSI. Why is `TSIH=0x0000` reserved for discovery? Explain the `SendTargets` text command used during discovery. (7 marks) --- **Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:** | Parameter | Full Name | Size | Source | Uniqueness Scope | |-----------|-----------|------|--------|------------------| | **ISID** | Initiator Session ID | **48 bits (6 bytes)** | Initiator | Unique per *InitiatorName* + Target pair | | **TSIH** | Target Session Identifying Handle | **16 bits (2 bytes)** | Target | Unique per target, identifies session on target side | | **CID** | Connection ID | **16 bits (2 bytes)** | Initiator (proposed), Target (assigned) | Unique per session, identifies a specific TCP connection within the session | **Detailed Explanation:** - **ISID**: A 48-bit identifier chosen by the initiator, structured as *Type* (4 bits) + *Reserved* + *Qualifier* + *Session ID*. It must remain the same across all connections of a given session and must not be reused until the session is permanently closed. - **TSIH**: A 16-bit handle assigned by the target during Login. The target must guarantee that no two active sessions share the same TSIH. The value `0x0000` is reserved for session-less discovery connections. - **CID**: A 16-bit handle for a specific TCP connection. The initiator proposes a CID in the Login Request; the target confirms it. CIDs must be unique within a session, enabling the target to multiplex commands over multiple connections. (7 marks) --- **Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:** **1. Discovery Session (3 marks):** A **Discovery Session** is a special-purpose, session-less iSCSI session used **only** to query the target for the list of available target portals (IP addresses and TCP port numbers) that the initiator can connect to for normal I/O. It is established by: - The initiator opens a TCP connection - Sends a Login Request with `SessionType=Discovery` and TSIH=0x0000 - Target accepts; no SCSI I/O is permitted The initiator then issues the `SendTargets` text command: $$\text{Initiator} \rightarrow \text{Target}: \text{SendTargets=All}$$ $$\text{Target} \rightarrow \text{Initiator}: \text{TargetName=}\ldots,\; \text{TargetAddress=}\ldots$$ The target responds with the list of all *TargetName* and *TargetAddress* pairs it advertises. **2. Normal Session (2 marks):** A **Normal Session** is the operational session used for full SCSI I/O. The initiator negotiates `SessionType=Normal` and a non-zero TSIH is assigned. All SCSI commands (READ, WRITE, INQUIRY, etc.) flow within this session. **3. Why TSIH = 0x0000 is reserved (2 marks):** The RFC 7143 reserves `TSIH = 0x0000` to mean **"not yet assigned" or "session-less discovery"**. During a Discovery Session, the target has not yet created a persistent session record — the connection is purely transient for the `SendTargets` exchange. Using a reserved sentinel value prevents any collision between a normal persistent session and a discovery connection. (7 marks) --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in iSCSI Session Questions:** > 1. **Confusing ISID with CID** — ISID identifies the *session*; CID identifies a *connection within a session*. Examiners award 0 marks for swapped definitions. > 2. **Forgetting the TSIH = 0x0000 reservation** — when asked about discovery sessions, students often state any TSIH is valid. Always mention the reserved sentinel. > 3. **Missing the CSG/NSG stage transitions** — the Login Phase has two stages; failing to mention *SecurityNegotiation* and *LoginOperationalNegotiation* explicitly costs 1–2 marks. > 4. **No mention of `T` and `F` bits in Login PDUs** — these transit/final bits are mandatory in the negotiation flow. > 5. **Confusing session reinstatement with a fresh login** — reinstatement reuses the *same* ISID + TSIH; a fresh login generates a new ISID and creates a new session. > 6. **Wrong PDU names** — write *Login Request*, *Login Response*, *Logout Request*, *Logout Response* (not "Login Command" or "Logout Packet"). ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — iSCSI Sessions** - A **session** is a logical, persistent relationship; a **connection** is a single TCP link within it. - A session is uniquely identified by $\langle \text{InitiatorName}, ISID, \text{TargetName}, TPGT, TSIH \rangle$. - **ISID** = **48 bits**, chosen by the initiator. - **TSIH** = **16 bits**, assigned by the target; `0x0000` is reserved. - **CID** = **16 bits**, identifies a connection within a session. - Session lifecycle: **Free → Login (Security + Operational) → Full Feature Phase → Logout → Free**. - Login uses **CSG** (Current Stage) and **NSG** (Next Stage) code bits; the **T bit** marks final key-value transit; the **F bit** marks the final PDU of the login. - **Discovery Session**: `SessionType=Discovery`, `TSIH=0x0000`, used only for `SendTargets`. - **Normal Session**: `SessionType=Normal`, real SCSI I/O, non-zero TSIH. - Session-wide parameters (e.g., `MaxConnections`, `MaxBurstLength`) must be **identical across all connections** of a session. - **MaxConnections** default = 1; typically negotiated up to 3–4 for MPIO. - **Session reinstatement** reuses the same ISID + TSIH after a connection drop. - **Logout Reason codes**: 0 = clean close, 1 = remove connection for recovery. - Multi-connection sessions provide **load balancing, redundancy, and bandwidth aggregation**. - The 32-bit **CmdSN** window $\vert MaxCmdSN - ExpCmdSN \vert$ bounds the number of outstanding commands. - **iSCSI layer sits on top of TCP**; sessions are unaware of underlying TCP details except for connection drops.

topics.title: iSNS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECST867 - STORAGE SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** iSNS ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **iSNS (Internet Storage Name Service)** is a protocol and directory service standardized in **RFC 4171** by the IETF, designed to provide **centralized, automated discovery, naming, and management** of storage devices (both **iSCSI** and **Fibre Channel** nodes) within an IP-based **Storage Area Network (SAN)**. It functions as a "**directory server**" that allows iSCSI initiators to dynamically discover iSCSI targets without requiring static, manual IP/port configuration on every host. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECST867 – Module 2):** > iSNS is explicitly listed under *iSCSI and FC discovery mechanisms* alongside **SendTargets** and **SLP**. Examiners often frame it as a **"centralized discovery service"** question worth 7–14 marks. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a corporate office where every employee has a desk phone (an **iSCSI Initiator**), and the company has multiple printers, scanners, and meeting rooms (**iSCSI Targets** spread across a SAN). - **Without iSNS (Static SendTargets):** Every employee must memorize a list of phone numbers and manually type them into their device settings. If a printer moves to a new extension, every employee must be re-notified and re-configure their device. This is **static, error-prone, and unscalable**. - **With iSNS (Dynamic Discovery):** The company installs a **centralized corporate directory service** (the iSNS Server). Each device automatically **registers** its name, location, and capabilities. When an employee wants to print, their phone **queries the directory** and instantly receives an updated list of all available printers. If a printer is added, removed, or moved, the directory is updated, and all employees are **automatically notified**. The iSNS Server is essentially the **"Yellow Pages / DNS of the Storage Network"**. > [!NOTE] > **Key Term – Discovery Domain:** > A logical grouping mechanism inside the iSNS database that controls which initiators can "see" which targets. It implements **zoning** at the application/protocol layer rather than at the switch fabric layer. ### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Boundaries - **Default iSNS Server Port:** **TCP/UDP Port 3205** (registered with IANA). - **Protocol Standard:** **RFC 4171** (iSNS), **RFC 4172** (iSNS for iFCP). - **Maximum Node Name Length:** **255 bytes** (UTF-8 encoded). - **Maximum IQN Length:** **223 bytes** (RFC 3720/3721 iSCSI naming constraint). - **Discovery Domain Member Limit:** typically **1000+ members** per domain (vendor-dependent, e.g., NetApp, Cisco MDS). - **SCN (State Change Notification) Port:** Same as iSNS — **Port 3205**. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Concept Matrix ### 2.1 Why iSNS Exists — The "Why" Behind It iSCSI natively suffers from a discovery problem. While TCP/IP provides IP addressing, iSCSI targets are *logical entities* (LUNs behind a target portal). Hardcoding target IP+IQN+LUN on every initiator creates an **N×M configuration nightmare** in large SANs. iSNS solves this by introducing a **centralized, dynamic, event-driven directory**. ### 2.2 Core Architectural Components The iSNS framework comprises four logical entities: 1. **iSNS Server (iSNSP — iSNS Protocol Server):** A software daemon (e.g., `isnsd` on Linux, embedded in storage arrays like NetApp ONTAP, or in switches like Cisco MDS 9000) that maintains the authoritative **iSNS Database**. 2. **iSNS Client (iSNS Initiator/Target side):** Any node (initiator or target) that registers its attributes with the iSNS Server. 3. **iSNS Database:** A structured object store containing **Network Entities**, **Portal Groups**, **iSCSI Nodes**, and **Discovery Domains**. 4. **Discovery Domains & Members:** Logical access-control lists governing which initiators may discover which targets. ### 2.3 The Five Functional Operations of iSNS iSNS, as defined in **RFC 4171**, provides **five** core services: | # | Function | Description | iSNS Protocol Message | |---|----------|-------------|----------------------| | 1 | **Name Registration** | Clients register IQN, IP, Portal info | `DevAttrReg`, `DevAttrQry` | | 2 | **Discovery** | Initiators query for available targets | `DevAttrQry` | | 3 | **Discovery Domain & Access Control** | Zoning-like filtering | `DDReg`, `DDSReg` | | 4 | **State Change Notification (SCN)** | Server pushes updates to clients | `StateChangeNotify` | | 5 | **Open Authentication / Security** | ESP/SCTP-based secure comms | RFC 4171 §6 | ### 2.4 iSNS Protocol Stack (Layering) iSNS operates **at the application layer**, sitting atop TCP or SCTP. The stack from bottom to top is: $$ \text{Physical} \rightarrow \text{Data Link} \rightarrow \text{IP} \rightarrow \text{TCP/SCTP} \rightarrow \text{iSNS Protocol} \rightarrow \text{iSCSI / FC} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Exam Tip:** iSNS is **transport-agnostic to storage** — it can advertise both **iSCSI nodes** and **Fibre Channel nodes** (via iFCP gateways). This dual-protocol bridging is a frequently asked 7-mark question. ### 2.5 iSNS Object Data Model — The "How" The iSNS Database is composed of **Managed Objects**. Each object is identified by a **key-value attribute** pair. The primary object types are: | Object Type | Purpose | Example Attributes | |-------------|---------|-------------------| | **Network Entity (NE)** | Logical grouping (an array) | `NE-Name`, `NE-Type` | | **iSCSI Node** | Individual initiator/target | `iSCSI-Name` (IQN), `Node-Type` | | **Portal** | Network access point | `IP-Address`, `Port` | | **Portal Group (PG)** | Set of portals for one node | `PG-Tag`, `Portal List` | | **Discovery Domain (DD)** | Access-control container | `DD-Name`, `DD-Member list` | | **DD Set (DDS)** | Collection of DDs | `DDS-Name` | ### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Cloud Service Providers** (AWS, Azure) use iSNS-like centralized services to map millions of virtual volumes to compute instances without per-host configuration. - **Enterprise SANs** (Cisco MDS, Brocade, NetApp) embed iSNS servers in fabric switches to enable **multi-vendor interoperability**. - **Disaster Recovery**: iSNS SCN allows initiators to fail over to backup targets **automatically** within milliseconds of a primary target failure. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Process Flows & Analytical Matrix > [!IMPORTANT] > **Note on "Derivations":** iSNS is a **protocol and architecture** topic — not a physics/calculus derivation. The rigorous step-by-step "derivation" here is the **complete, exhaustive procedural walkthrough** of its three key operations: **Registration, Discovery, and SCN**. ### 3.1 Step-by-Step: Node Registration Procedure When a new iSCSI Initiator (e.g., a Linux host with `open-iscsi`) comes online, the following sequence executes: **Step 1 — TCP Connection Establishment** The iSNS Client (embedded in the iSCSI initiator software) opens a **TCP connection to the iSNS Server's Port 3205**. **Step 2 — Build Registration Request** The client constructs a `DevAttrReg` (Device Attribute Register) message containing: - Its **iSCSI Name (IQN)** in the format `iqn.YYYY-MM.reversed.domain:naming_authority` - Its **IP address and TCP port** - Its **Node Type** (Initiator) - Its **Network Entity** membership **Step 3 — Send and Wait for Acknowledgment** The client sends the `DevAttrReg` PDU (Protocol Data Unit) and awaits a `DevAttrRegRsp`. **Step 4 — Server-Side Processing** The iSNS Server: - Validates the IQN format and uniqueness. - Inserts/updates the **iSCSI Node object** in its database. - Adds the node to a default or specified **Discovery Domain**. - Sends back a `Success` (status code `0`) or an `Error` response (e.g., `Status: 4 — Invalid IQN`). **Step 5 — Heartbeat / Refresh** The client periodically re-registers (default **every 30 minutes** in most implementations) to keep its entry "alive" in the iSNS Database. ### 3.2 Step-by-Step: Target Discovery Procedure **Step 1 — Initiator Issues Query** The initiator sends a `DevAttrQry` message asking the server: *"Return all iSCSI Node objects where Node-Type = Target AND (Discovery-Domain contains me)."* **Step 2 — Server Filters by Discovery Domain** The server applies the **DD-Set membership** rules. Initiators in `DD-Prod` will NOT see targets in `DD-Test`. This is the zoning enforcement. **Step 3 — Server Returns Portal Groups** For each matching target, the server returns: - Target **IQN** - Target **IP address(es)** - Target **TCP port(s)** (default **3260**) - **Portal Group Tag** **Step 4 — Initiator Establishes iSCSI Session** With the resolved target portal information, the initiator performs a normal iSCSI **Login → Full Feature Phase** connection, completely unaware that iSNS was involved. ### 3.3 Step-by-Step: State Change Notification (SCN) Procedure This is iSNS's **event-driven notification** mechanism, similar to DNS NOTIFY. **Step 1 — Event Occurs** A target node goes offline (e.g., LUN unmapped, controller failover). **Step 2 — Target Informs iSNS Server** The target sends a `DevAttrReg` with an updated **entity status** flag, or the server detects the heartbeat timeout. **Step 3 — Server Initiates SCN** The iSNS Server sends a `StateChangeNotify` PDU to all iSNS Clients (initiators and other targets) that are members of the **affected Discovery Domain**. **Step 4 — Client Reacts** The initiator's iSNS client receives the SCN, marks the target as unavailable, and can: - Re-query the iSNS server for new targets. - Trigger an **MPIO (Multi-Path I/O) failover** to a backup path/target. ### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — iSNS vs Other Discovery Methods | Feature | Static SendTargets | SLP (Service Location Protocol) | **iSNS** | |---------|-------------------|--------------------------------|-----------| | Centralized Directory | ❌ No (config-file based) | ✅ Yes (DA/SA model) | ✅ Yes (dedicated server) | | Dynamic Updates | ❌ No | ⚠️ Manual SLP-DA | ✅ Yes (SCN) | | Supports iSCSI + FC | ❌ iSCSI only | ⚠️ iSCSI only | ✅ Both iSCSI & FC | | Discovery Domains / Zoning | ❌ No | ⚠️ Scopes (limited) | ✅ Full DD/DDS | | Authentication & Security | ❌ None | ⚠️ Basic | ✅ RFC 3723 + ESP | | Scalability | ❌ Poor (N×M config) | ⚠️ Moderate | ✅ Excellent | | KTU 2024 Relevance | ✅ Mentioned | ✅ Mentioned | ✅ **Heavily tested** | --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 iSNS Functional Architecture (Block-Level Flow) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Linux iSCSI Initiator
open-iscsi daemon] -->|DevAttrReg
TCP 3205| B[iSNS Server
isnsd process] C[Windows iSCSI Initiator
MS iSCSI Initiator] -->|DevAttrReg
TCP 3205| B B -->|StateChangeNotify
SCN| A B -->|StateChangeNotify
SCN| C D[iSCSI Target Array
NetApp ONTAP] -->|DevAttrReg
TCP 3205| B E[iFCP Gateway
FC-to-iSCSI bridge] -->|DevAttrReg
TCP 3205| B B -->|DevAttrQry
returns Portal Groups| A B -->|DevAttrQry
returns Portal Groups| C A -->|iSCSI Login
TCP 3260| D C -->|iSCSI Login
TCP 3260| D A -->|iSCSI Login
TCP 3260| E E -->|FCP Frames| F[Fibre Channel
Storage Array] ``` ### 4.2 iSNS Object Hierarchy in the Database ```mermaid flowchart TD Root[iSNS Database
RFC 4171 Managed Objects] Root --> NE1[Network Entity NE_Prod_Array] Root --> NE2[Network Entity NE_Dev_Array] Root --> DD1[Discovery Domain
DD_Production] Root --> DD2[Discovery Domain
DD_Development] Root --> DDS1[DD Set
DDS_All_Prod] NE1 --> N1[iSCSI Node
iqn.2000-01.com.synology:NAS1] NE1 --> N2[iSCSI Node
iqn.2000-01.com.synology:NAS1-backup] NE2 --> N3[iSCSI Node
iqn.1991-05.com.microsoft:dev-target] N1 --> PG1[Portal Group
192.168.10.50 3260] N1 --> PG2[Portal Group
192.168.10.51 3260] N2 --> PG3[Portal Group
10.0.0.50 3260] DD1 --> M1[Member: iSCSI Initiator HostA] DD1 --> M2[Member: iSCSI Node NAS1] DD2 --> M3[Member: iSCSI Initiator DevHost1] DD2 --> M4[Member: iSCSI Node dev-target] DDS1 --> S1[Contains: DD_Production] ``` ### 4.3 iSNS Registration & Discovery Sequence (Sequence Diagram) ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant I as iSCSI Initiator participant S as iSNS Server participant T as iSCSI Target I->>S: 1. TCP SYN (Port 3205) S-->>I: 2. TCP SYN-ACK I->>S: 3. DevAttrReg (Register my IQN + IP) S-->>I: 4. DevAttrRegRsp (Success) T->>S: 5. DevAttrReg (Register target IQN + Portal) S-->>T: 6. DevAttrRegRsp (Success) I->>S: 7. DevAttrQry (List targets in my DD) S-->>I: 8. DevAttrQryRsp (Return Portal Group 192.168.10.50 3260) I->>T: 9. iSCSI Login (TCP 3260) T-->>I: 10. iSCSI Login Accept Note over S,T: Later, target goes offline... S->>I: 11. StateChangeNotify (Target NAS1 DOWN) I->>I: 12. MPIO Failover triggered I->>S: 13. DevAttrQry (Re-discover) S-->>I: 14. DevAttrQryRsp (New target NAS1-backup) ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style]** *Define iSNS. Mention the IETF RFC that standardizes it and the default port number used.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **iSNS (Internet Storage Name Service)** is a centralized directory service protocol for SANs that enables dynamic discovery of iSCSI and Fibre Channel storage devices. It is standardized in **RFC 4171** and operates over **TCP/UDP Port 3205**. It eliminates the need for static SendTargets configuration on each initiator by maintaining a database of registered storage nodes and their portal groups. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]** *List any three advantages of iSNS over static SendTargets discovery in iSCSI SANs.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **Centralized Management:** A single iSNS server manages all targets, reducing per-host configuration overhead. 2. **Dynamic Discovery via SCN:** Initiators receive automatic `StateChangeNotify` messages when targets are added, removed, or fail, enabling fast MPIO failover. 3. **Discovery Domains (Zoning):** Provides software-defined access control (DD/DDS) without relying solely on FC switch zoning. 4. *(Bonus point)* Supports both iSCSI and Fibre Channel devices via iFCP gateways. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — iSNS Questions:** > Students frequently lose **2–3 marks** by: > - Confusing iSNS port (3205) with iSCSI target port (3260). > - Writing "iSNS is a discovery protocol for iSCSI only" — **DEDUCT 1 MARK** (it supports FC too). > - Failing to mention **SCN (State Change Notification)** — this is a **mandatory 3-mark component** in any 14-mark iSNS answer. > - Forgetting to state the **RFC number (4171)** — examiner deducts ½ to 1 mark. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 1** **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]** *(a)* Explain the architecture of iSNS with a neat block diagram. List its five functional services. **(7 marks)** *(b)* With a sequence diagram, describe the **Node Registration** and **State Change Notification (SCN)** procedures in iSNS. **(7 marks)** ##### Model Solution **(a) iSNS Architecture and Five Functional Services (7 marks):** The **iSNS architecture** consists of the following logical components: - **iSNS Server:** A directory service daemon (e.g., Linux `isnsd`, Cisco MDS embedded) that maintains the centralized iSNS Database over **Port 3205**. - **iSNS Clients:** iSCSI Initiators, iSCSI Targets, and iFCP gateways that register and query the server. - **iSNS Database:** Contains Network Entities, iSCSI Nodes, Portals, Portal Groups, and Discovery Domains. - **Discovery Domain (DD) & DD-Set (DDS):** Access-control containers that implement software zoning. The **five functional services** as defined in **RFC 4171** are: 1. **Name Registration** — Nodes register IQN, IP, and portal info. 2. **Name Discovery** — Initiators query for available targets. 3. **Discovery Domains & Access Control** — Zoning via DD/DDS. 4. **State Change Notification (SCN)** — Event-driven push updates. 5. **Open Authentication / Security** — Optional ESP/SCTP-secured transactions. *(Examiner note: A block diagram as shown in Section 4.1 will earn the full 7 marks.)* --- **(b) Registration and SCN Procedures (7 marks):** **Node Registration Steps:** 1. Client opens **TCP connection to Port 3205** of the iSNS Server. 2. Sends `DevAttrReg` PDU containing its **iSCSI Name (IQN)**, **IP address**, **TCP port**, and **Node Type**. 3. Server validates the IQN, stores the entry in the **iSCSI Node object** of the database, and assigns it to a **Discovery Domain**. 4. Server responds with `DevAttrRegRsp` (Status = 0 on success). 5. Client **periodically re-registers** (heartbeat) to maintain liveness. **State Change Notification (SCN) Steps:** 1. A **state event** occurs (e.g., target failure, new LUN, portal IP change). 2. The affected node (or the server's heartbeat watchdog) detects the change. 3. iSNS Server sends a **`StateChangeNotify` PDU** to all iSNS Clients that are members of the affected **Discovery Domain**. 4. Initiators receive the SCN, **update their local target list**, and may trigger: - **MPIO failover** to alternate paths/targets. - **Re-query** of the iSNS server for replacement targets. 5. This enables **sub-second failover** in enterprise SANs, far faster than TCP-level timeout-based detection. *(Sequence diagram from Section 4.3 will fetch the full 7 marks.)* --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 2** **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]** *(a)* Compare iSNS, SLP, and static SendTargets as iSCSI discovery mechanisms. Highlight why iSNS is preferred in large enterprise SANs. **(7 marks)** *(b)* Explain the **iSNS Object Data Model** in detail. What is the role of **Discovery Domains** in enforcing access control? **(7 marks)** ##### Model Solution **(a) Comparison of iSNS, SLP, and SendTargets (7 marks):** | Parameter | Static SendTargets | SLP | **iSNS** | |-----------|--------------------|----|----------| | Centralized Directory | No | Yes (SLP-DA) | **Yes (dedicated iSNS server)** | | Dynamic Discovery | No | Limited | **Yes (SCN push)** | | Protocol Support | iSCSI only | iSCSI only | **iSCSI + Fibre Channel** | | Access Control | None | Scopes | **Full DD/DDS zoning** | | Security | None | Basic | **RFC 3723 + ESP** | | Scalability | Poor | Moderate | **Excellent** | | Heartbeat / Liveness | No | No | **Yes (periodic reregistration)** | | Failover Speed | Slow (TCP timeout) | Slow | **Fast (sub-second SCN)** | **Why iSNS is preferred in large enterprise SANs:** - **Scalability:** A single server can serve thousands of initiators. - **Automation:** Zero-touch provisioning of new servers. - **Multi-vendor:** Bridges iSCSI and FC fabrics. - **Resilience:** SCN enables fast, automatic failover. - **Security:** Built-in authentication mechanisms. - **Management:** Reduces operational complexity in **datacenters with 100+ hosts**. --- **(b) iSNS Object Data Model & Discovery Domains (7 marks):** The **iSNS Object Data Model** is a hierarchical structure of **Managed Objects** stored in the iSNS Database. The key object types are: 1. **Network Entity (NE):** Logical grouping of related iSCSI nodes (e.g., a storage array). 2. **iSCSI Node:** A single iSCSI initiator or target, identified by its **IQN**. 3. **Portal:** An IP address + TCP port (e.g., `192.168.10.50:3260`) through which a node is reachable. 4. **Portal Group (PG):** A collection of portals belonging to one node, enabling **MPIO** at the iSNS level. 5. **Discovery Domain (DD):** A **named access-control container** that groups initiators and targets. 6. **Discovery Domain Set (DDS):** A collection of DDs to enable **hierarchical zoning** (e.g., `DDS_All_Production` containing `DD_DB`, `DD_App`, `DD_Web`). **Role of Discovery Domains:** - **Access Control / Zoning:** An initiator in `DD_Production` can ONLY see targets also registered in `DD_Production`. Targets in `DD_Development` are invisible — equivalent to FC switch zoning. - **Segmentation:** Logical separation of test, dev, and production environments. - **Security Boundary:** Prevents unauthorized cross-environment access. - **Scalable Administration:** Multiple DDs can be managed independently by different SAN administrators. - **DDS Nesting:** Allows complex topologies (e.g., a shared backup target visible to all production DDs). --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📌 > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist for iSNS (KTU 2024):** - ✅ **Full Form:** Internet Storage Name Service — standardized in **RFC 4171**. - ✅ **Port Number:** **TCP/UDP 3205** (NOT 3260 — that's iSCSI target login port). - ✅ **Purpose:** Centralized, dynamic discovery of iSCSI & FC storage nodes. - ✅ **Supports:** Both iSCSI and Fibre Channel (via iFCP gateways) — **not iSCSI-only**. - ✅ **Five Services:** Registration, Discovery, DD/DDS Zoning, SCN, Security. - ✅ **SCN = State Change Notification** — event-driven push; enables sub-second failover. - ✅ **Discovery Domain (DD):** Software-defined zoning; replaces/supplements FC switch zoning. - ✅ **iSNS Object Types:** Network Entity, iSCSI Node, Portal, Portal Group, DD, DDS. - ✅ **Default Heartbeat:** Re-registration every ~30 minutes (vendor-dependent). - ✅ **Comparison Traps:** Be ready to differentiate from **SLP** (Service Location Protocol) and static **SendTargets**. - ✅ **Real-world products:** NetApp ONTAP, Cisco MDS 9000, Brocade, Synology DSM, Windows Server iSNS Service. - ✅ **Exam Keywords to Use:** *Centralized, dynamic, RFC 4171, Port 3205, SCN, Discovery Domain, IQN registration.* > [!NOTE] > **Final KTU 2024 Exam Strategy:** Always draw a **labeled diagram** in any iSNS answer worth ≥7 marks. Examiners reward diagrams with **2–3 easy marks**. Pair the diagram with the **5 functional services** list for a guaranteed high-score answer. ---

topics.title: Network Attached Storage - NAS Protocols
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST867 - STORAGE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Network Attached Storage - NAS Protocols

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of NAS

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Network Attached Storage (NAS)** is a dedicated, high-performance, file-level data storage device that provides shared file-based data services to heterogeneous clients over a standard IP-based network (typically Ethernet), using industry-standard file access protocols such as **NFS**, **CIFS/SMB**, and **AFP**.

NAS is essentially a *self-contained file server* — a specialized computer (often a stripped-down, optimized appliance) whose sole purpose is to serve files to clients across a LAN/WAN using TCP/IP. It abstracts raw block storage behind a high-level file system and exports directories/shares to authorized clients.

### 1.2 Key Architectural Characteristics

| Property | Description |
|---|---|
| **Data Granularity** | File-level (not block-level) I/O |
| **Network Layer** | Layer 2/3 IP-Ethernet (Layer 7 file protocols) |
| **Access Pattern** | Concurrent multi-client, multi-protocol access |
| **Addressability** | Files and directories (not LUNs/sectors) |
| **Throughput Metric** | Typically **100 MB/s – 10 GB/s** (gigabit to 100 GbE) |
| **Latency** | Milliseconds (file I/O) vs microseconds (block I/O) |
| **Cost Band** | Low to Mid (consumer to enterprise NAS) |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Public Library" Analogy for NAS**
> Think of NAS as a **centralized public library** in a city. The library building (NAS head + storage) holds countless books (files) organized on shelves (file system). Citizens (clients) don't enter the library and grab individual pages (blocks); instead, they walk up to the librarian (NAS protocol engine), present their library card (authentication), and ask: *"Please lend me the book titled 'Storage Systems Notes'"* — using standardized rules (NFS, SMB). The librarian then hands over the **entire book** as a unit. Similarly, NAS hands over **entire files** to clients, not raw disk sectors.

### 1.4 Where NAS Fits in the Storage Hierarchy

NAS occupies the **mid-tier** of enterprise storage, sitting between direct-attached storage (DAS) and storage area networks (SAN):

- **DAS** → internal to a single server (no network)
- **NAS** → file-level over IP (this module)
- **SAN** → block-level over Fibre Channel / iSCSI

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid for KTU Exam:** A simple way to remember NAS = **"F**ile-**L**evel **O**ver **I**P" → **FLOI**.

### 1.5 The NAS Protocol Family

A *NAS Protocol* is a standardized set of communication rules that governs how a client requests, opens, reads, writes, locks, and closes files on a remote NAS server. The major protocols are:

1. **NFS (Network File System)** — UNIX/Linux world
2. **CIFS/SMB (Server Message Block)** — Windows world
3. **AFP (Apple Filing Protocol)** — legacy macOS
4. **FTP / FTPS / SFTP** — internet file transfer
5. **HTTP / WebDAV** — web-based file access
6. **iSCSI** — block-level extension (technically turns NAS into a unified gateway)
7. **NDMP (Network Data Management Protocol)** — backup-specific

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 NAS Internal Architecture — Component Breakdown

A NAS device is logically divided into **two main subsystems**:

#### A. NAS Head (Front-End)
- **CPU** — runs a slimmed-down OS (often Linux-based, FreeBSD, or proprietary)
- **Memory (RAM)** — caches metadata and file system lookups
- **NICs (Network Interface Cards)** — typically 1 GbE / 10 GbE / 25 GbE / 100 GbE
- **Protocol Engine** — daemon processes for NFS, SMB, etc.
- **File System Manager** — translates file requests to block-level operations

#### B. Storage Back-End
- One or more **disk enclosures** containing HDDs/SSDs
- **RAID controller** for redundancy and performance
- Increasingly, **hybrid tiers** (SSD cache + HDD capacity)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Performance Bottleneck Theorem:** The NAS throughput is constrained by:
> $$\text{Throughput}_{\text{NAS}} = \min\left(\text{NIC BW},\ \frac{\text{Storage IOPS}}{\text{IO per file op}},\ \text{Protocol overhead}\right)$$

### 2.2 NFS — Network File System (Deep Dive)

NFS was originally developed by **Sun Microsystems** (RFC 1094 in 1989, evolved through v2 → v3 → v4.2). It is the de-facto standard for UNIX/Linux file sharing.

#### NFS Versions Comparison

| Version | Year | Transport | Key Enhancements |
|---|---|---|---|
| **NFSv2** | 1989 | UDP | 32-bit file sizes, 8 KB max transfer |
| **NFSv3** | 1995 | TCP/UDP | 64-bit offsets, async writes, larger files |
| **NFSv4** | 2003 | TCP | Stateful, Kerberos security, compound ops, locking (no separate NLM) |
| **NFSv4.1** | 2010 | TCP | **pNFS** (parallel NFS), session trunking |
| **NFSv4.2** | 2016 | TCP | Sparse files, server-side copy, application I/O hints |

#### NFS Operation Sequence (Read Example)

1. **mount** (or pseudo-filesystem in v4) → client obtains file handle
2. **LOOKUP** → resolve filename to file handle
3. **OPEN** → acquire state, negotiate delegation
4. **READ** → request file data over TCP
5. **CLOSE** → release locks and state
6. **umount** → release mount point

> [!NOTE]
> NFS is **stateless** in v2/v3 — the server holds no client session info (crash recovery is simple). NFSv4 introduced **statefulness** for better locking semantics.

### 2.3 SMB/CIFS — Server Message Block

Originally created by IBM, refined by Microsoft. The current version is **SMB 3.1.1** (Windows 10/Server 2016+).

| Feature | Description |
|---|---|
| **Transport** | TCP (port **445**), NetBIOS legacy (137/138/139) |
| **Security** | Kerberos, NTLM v2, AES-128-GCM (SMB 3.0+) |
| **Authentication** | Active Directory integrated |
| **Encryption** | End-to-end (SMB 3.0+) |
| **Multichannel** | Aggregate multiple NICs (SMB 3.0+) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CIFS = SMB 1.0 dialect.** Modern systems use SMB 2.0/3.0. KTU exam question "Differentiate CIFS and SMB" → CIFS is the *older common dialect*, SMB is the *broader protocol family*.

### 2.4 AFP — Apple Filing Protocol

Developed by Apple for macOS. Now deprecated in favor of SMB since macOS 10.9 (Mavericks), but still discussed in syllabus. Default port **548** (TCP).

### 2.5 FTP / HTTP / WebDAV

- **FTP** (port 21 control, 20 data) — stateful, separate control & data channels
- **HTTP/HTTPS** (port 80/443) — used by NAS web UIs (Synology DSM, QNAP QTS)
- **WebDAV** — HTTP extension enabling collaborative file editing over the web

### 2.6 iSCSI — The NAS/SAN Bridge

Although iSCSI is **block-level**, many enterprise NAS devices (called **Unified Storage** or **NAS Gateways**) act as iSCSI *targets*, blurring the line between NAS and SAN.

### 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table contains the core formulas needed for KTU numerical problems on NAS performance.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **File Read Time** | $T_{\text{read}} = \dfrac{\text{File Size}}{B_{\text{eff}}}$ | $B_{\text{eff}}$ = effective bandwidth | seconds |
| 2 | **Effective NAS Bandwidth** | $B_{\text{eff}} = B_{\text{link}} \times (1 - O_p)$ | $O_p$ = protocol overhead ratio | MB/s |
| 3 | **Total NAS Throughput** | $T_{\text{agg}} = N_{\text{clients}} \times \dfrac{S_{\text{file}}}{t_{\text{resp}}}$ | $N$ = number of clients | MB/s |
| 4 | **RAID Reliability (MTBF)** | $\text{MTBF}_{\text{array}} = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}_{\text{disk}}}{N_{\text{disks}} \times \lambda_{\text{rebuild}}}$ | depends on redundancy level | hours |
| 5 | **Storage Capacity** | $C_{\text{usable}} = (N \times C_{\text{disk}}) \times \eta_{\text{RAID}}$ | $\eta$ = RAID efficiency | TB |
| 6 | **NAS Latency** | $L = L_{\text{net}} + L_{\text{proc}} + L_{\text{disk}}$ | sum of three delays | ms |
| 7 | **RAID 5 Efficiency** | $\eta_{\text{RAID5}} = \dfrac{N-1}{N}$ | $N$ = total disks | dimensionless |
| 8 | **RAID 6 Efficiency** | $\eta_{\text{RAID6}} = \dfrac{N-2}{N}$ | double parity | dimensionless |
| 9 | **RAID 1/10 Efficiency** | $\eta_{\text{RAID1}} = \dfrac{1}{2}$ | mirroring | dimensionless |
| 10 | **Mean Time To Data Loss (MTDL)** | $\text{MTDL} \approx \dfrac{\text{MTBF}^2}{N \times (N-1) \times M_{\text{rebuild}}^2}$ | $M$ = MTTR | hours |

> **Boundary Conditions / Tips:**
> - Always express file sizes in consistent units (**KB = 1024 bytes** in storage, or 1000 in marketing — KTU uses **1024**).
> - $B_{\text{link}}$ is **raw line rate**; $B_{\text{eff}}$ accounts for **TCP + protocol** overhead (~10–15%).
> - For 1 GbE: $B_{\text{link}} = 125$ MB/s (theoretical), practical ≈ **110–115 MB/s**.

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

NAS protocols underpin virtually every modern IT infrastructure:

- **Enterprise file shares** (Windows SMB + AD)
- **HPC cluster home directories** (NFS over RDMA)
- **Media & entertainment** (large-file NFS, SMB Multichannel)
- **Backup targets** (NDMP + CIFS)
- **Container/Kubernetes persistent volumes** (CSI drivers using NFS/iSCSI)
- **Home/SOHO** (Synology, QNAP over SMB)

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Effective File Transfer Time Over NAS

**Problem:** A 4 GB video file is stored on a NAS connected via 1 GbE Ethernet. SMB protocol adds ~12% protocol overhead. Calculate the minimum transfer time.

**Given:**
- File size $S = 4 \text{ GB} = 4 \times 1024 \text{ MB} = 4096 \text{ MB}$
- Link bandwidth $B_{\text{link}} = 1 \text{ GbE} = 1000 \text{ Mb/s} = 125 \text{ MB/s}$
- Protocol overhead $O_p = 0.12$

**Step 1:** Compute effective payload bandwidth.

$$
B_{\text{eff}} = B_{\text{link}} \times (1 - O_p) = 125 \times (1 - 0.12) = 125 \times 0.88 = 110 \text{ MB/s}
$$

*Logic:* Of every 100 bytes transmitted, only 88 carry application data; the remaining 12 are SMB headers, TCP/IP headers, ACKs, and retransmission padding.

**Step 2:** Compute transfer time.

$$
T_{\text{read}} = \frac{S}{B_{\text{eff}}} = \frac{4096 \text{ MB}}{110 \text{ MB/s}} \approx 37.24 \text{ seconds}
$$

**Step 3:** Add TCP+IP overhead explicitly (optional refinement).

$$
T_{\text{read, refined}} = \frac{S}{B_{\text{link}} \times (1 - O_p - O_{\text{TCP/IP}})} \approx \frac{4096}{125 \times 0.82} \approx 39.96 \text{ s}
$$

*Logic:* TCP/IP adds ~6% additional framing (headers, ACKs, windowing).

**Result:** **$T_{\text{read}} \approx 37.2$ seconds (basic) or 40 seconds (refined).**

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — RAID Usable Capacity for a NAS Filer

**Problem:** A 12-bay NAS uses 4 TB 7200 RPM drives in RAID 6. Calculate the usable capacity.

**Given:**
- Number of disks $N = 12$
- Capacity per disk $C = 4 \text{ TB}$
- RAID level: 6 (double parity)

**Step 1:** Apply RAID 6 efficiency formula.

$$
\eta_{\text{RAID6}} = \frac{N - 2}{N} = \frac{12 - 2}{12} = \frac{10}{12} = 0.8333
$$

*Logic:* Two disks are reserved for parity (P and Q) stripes, distributed across all 12 drives.

**Step 2:** Compute raw capacity.

$$
C_{\text{raw}} = N \times C = 12 \times 4 = 48 \text{ TB}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute usable capacity.

$$
C_{\text{usable}} = C_{\text{raw}} \times \eta_{\text{RAID6}} = 48 \times 0.8333 = 40 \text{ TB}
$$

**Step 4:** Verify by direct subtraction.

$$
C_{\text{usable}} = (N - 2) \times C = 10 \times 4 = 40 \text{ TB} \quad \checkmark
$$

**Result:** **Usable capacity = 40 TB.**

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Aggregate Throughput for Multiple NAS Clients

**Problem:** 50 office users simultaneously read a 200 MB presentation file from a NAS. Each read takes 4 s at the disk. The NAS uses RAID 5 over 6 disks. Compute aggregate disk throughput required and verify if a 1 GbE link is sufficient.

**Step 1:** Compute aggregate demand.

$$
T_{\text{agg}} = N_{\text{clients}} \times \frac{S_{\text{file}}}{t_{\text{resp}}} = 50 \times \frac{200 \text{ MB}}{4 \text{ s}} = 50 \times 50 = 2500 \text{ MB/s} = 2.5 \text{ GB/s}
$$

*Logic:* Each of 50 users causes the disk subsystem to deliver 50 MB/s; total is 2500 MB/s.

**Step 2:** Compare to 1 GbE link.

$$
B_{\text{1GbE,eff}} = 125 \times 0.88 = 110 \text{ MB/s}
$$

*Logic:* Even with ideal protocol, 1 GbE can deliver only 110 MB/s — far below the 2500 MB/s disk output.

**Step 3:** Conclusion.

The 1 GbE link is **grossly insufficient**; the network becomes the bottleneck. Required upgrade: **10 GbE** (≈ 1100 MB/s effective) at minimum, or **25 GbE** for headroom.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world impact:** This is why video editing studios and VFX houses use **25/50/100 GbE NAS** — even modest client counts saturate 1 GbE quickly.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Protocol Comparison

| Feature | NFSv4.2 | SMB 3.1.1 | AFP | FTP | iSCSI |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **OS Origin** | UNIX | Windows | macOS | UNIX | IETF |
| **Default Port** | 2049 | 445 | 548 | 21, 20 | 3260 |
| **I/O Granularity** | File | File | File | File | **Block** |
| **Auth Method** | Kerberos | Kerberos/NTLM | Kerberos | Username/PWD | CHAP |
| **Encryption** | Yes (krb5p) | Yes (AES-GCM) | Yes | Optional (FTPS) | Yes (IPsec) |
| **Multichannel** | pNFS | Yes (SMB3) | No | No | MPIO |
| **Stateful?** | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| **Best Use Case** | Linux/Unix | Windows | Legacy Mac | Internet | Block SAN |

---

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level NAS Architecture Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    clientA[Client A Linux NFS] --> sw[Network Switch L2 L3 IP]
    clientB[Client B Windows SMB] --> sw
    clientC[Client C Mac AFP] --> sw
    sw --> nashead[NAS Head]
    nashead --> cpu[Multi-core CPU]
    nashead --> ram[RAM Cache for Metadata]
    nashead --> nic1[10 GbE NIC 1]
    nashead --> nic2[10 GbE NIC 2]
    nashead --> proto[Protocol Engine NFS SMB AFP]
    nashead --> fsman[File System Manager e.g. ext4 ZFS Btrfs]
    fsman --> raidctl[RAID Controller]
    raidctl --> d1[HDD Disk 1]
    raidctl --> d2[HDD Disk 2]
    raidctl --> d3[HDD Disk 3]
    raidctl --> d4[SSD Cache Tier]
```

### 4.2 NFS Mount & File Access Sequence

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant C as NFS Client
    participant S as NFS Server NAS

    C->>S: 1. MOUNT request port 2049
    S-->>C: 2. MOUNT response file handle
    C->>S: 3. LOOKUP file path
    S-->>C: 4. File handle for target file
    C->>S: 5. OPEN state share reservation
    S-->>C: 6. OPEN confirmation state ID
    C->>S: 7. READ offset 0 length 8192
    S-->>C: 8. Data payload 8 KB
    C->>S: 9. CLOSE release state
    S-->>C: 10. CLOSE ack
    Note over C,S: NFSv4 stateful session ends after CLOSE
```

### 4.3 NAS Protocol Stack vs SAN Block Stack

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph NAS_STACK[NAS Protocol Stack]
        nApp[Application MS Word gedit] --> nFS[File System NFS SMB AFP]
        nFS --> nTCP[TCP Port 2049 445]
        nTCP --> nIP[IP]
        nIP --> nEth[Ethernet]
    end

    subgraph SAN_STACK[SAN Block Protocol Stack]
        sApp[Application Database VM] --> sFS[File System ext4 NTFS]
        sFS --> sBLK[Block Layer SCSI commands]
        sBLK --> sFC[FC or iSCSI Port 3260]
        sFC --> sIP[IP for iSCSI or FC frame]
        sIP --> sEth[Ethernet or Fibre Channel]
    end

    Note1[NAS operates at Layer 7 file semantics] -.-> nFS
    Note2[SAN operates at Layer 4-5 block semantics] -.-> sBLK
```

### 4.4 NAS File Access Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User clicks file in Explorer] --> B[Client SMB redirector]
    B --> C{File cached locally?}
    C -->|Yes| D[Read from local cache]
    C -->|No| E[SMB Negotiate Protocol Dialect]
    E --> F[SMB Session Setup Auth]
    F --> G[SMB Tree Connect to Share]
    G --> H[SMB Open File]
    H --> I[NAS File System Manager Lookup]
    I --> J[RAID controller reads stripe]
    J --> K[Data returns to client over TCP]
    K --> L[User sees file content]
```

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Set A]**

**Q1. Define Network Attached Storage. List any two file access protocols used in NAS.**
*(CO2, Remember Level, 3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
NAS is a dedicated, file-level data storage device connected to a network, providing data access to heterogeneous clients using TCP/IP. (2 marks)
Two NAS protocols: **NFS** (Network File System — UNIX/Linux) and **SMB/CIFS** (Server Message Block — Windows). (1 mark)

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q2. Differentiate between file-level and block-level storage access. Give one example each.**
*(CO2, Understand Level, 3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | File-Level (NAS) | Block-Level (SAN) |
|---|---|---|
| Granularity | Entire files | Individual disk blocks/sectors |
| Protocol | NFS, SMB | iSCSI, Fibre Channel |
| Server loads | File system on NAS | File system on client |
| Example | Synology NAS | EMC VMAX SAN |

(3 marks for clear table + example)

---

### 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**Q3(a)** Explain the architecture of a Network Attached Storage system with a neat block diagram. Describe the role of the NAS head and storage back-end. *(7 marks — CO2, Understand)*

**Model Answer:**

The NAS architecture consists of two major subsystems: the **NAS head** (front-end) and the **storage back-end**.

**NAS Head components:**

- **CPU**: A multi-core processor runs the NAS operating system (typically Linux/Unix-based) and protocol daemons. Modern NAS CPUs range from ARM Cortex to Intel Xeon.
- **Memory (RAM)**: Caches file system metadata, directory entries, and frequently accessed data. Typical sizes: 4 GB (entry-level) to 512 GB (enterprise).
- **Network Interface Cards (NICs)**: Provide connectivity — 1 GbE, 10 GbE, 25 GbE, or 100 GbE. Multiple NICs enable link aggregation (LACP) and failover.
- **Protocol Engine**: Implements the file-serving protocols — NFS, SMB/CIFS, AFP, FTP, HTTP. Each protocol runs as a daemon listening on its well-known port (NFS: 2049, SMB: 445, AFP: 548).
- **File System Manager**: Translates high-level file requests (open, read, write) into low-level block operations on the underlying disks. Examples: ZFS, Btrfs, ext4, NTFS.

**Storage Back-End components:**

- **Disk Enclosures**: Houses multiple HDDs/SSDs in bays (4-bay SOHO to 60+ bay enterprise).
- **RAID Controller**: Provides redundancy (RAID 0/1/5/6/10) and performance improvement.
- **Hybrid Tiering**: Combines SSD cache with HDD capacity for cost-performance optimization.

**Role of NAS Head:** Manages client authentication, file system operations, protocol translation, and metadata management. It is the "brain" of the NAS.

**Role of Storage Back-End:** Provides persistent, redundant storage. It is the "warehouse" that physically holds the data.

*(7 marks: 3 for diagram, 4 for explanation)*

---

**Q3(b)** Compare NFS, SMB/CIFS, and AFP protocols in terms of origin OS, default port, authentication, and typical use case. *(7 marks — CO2, Analyze)*

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | **NFS (v4.2)** | **SMB/CIFS (3.1.1)** | **AFP** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Origin OS** | Sun Microsystems / UNIX | Microsoft / Windows | Apple / macOS |
| **Default Port** | 2049/TCP | 445/TCP | 548/TCP |
| **Authentication** | Kerberos (RPCSEC_GSS) | Kerberos, NTLM v2 | Kerberos |
| **Encryption** | krb5p (per-message AES) | AES-128-GCM (SMB 3.0+) | SSL/TLS |
| **Statefulness** | Stateful (NFSv4+) | Stateful | Stateful |
| **Unicode Support** | Limited (UTF-8) | Full UTF-16 | Full |
| **Resource Forking** | No | Limited (Alt Streams) | Yes (native) |
| **Performance** | High (pNFS parallelism) | High (Multichannel) | Moderate |
| **Typical Use** | Linux/Unix file sharing, HPC | Windows enterprise shares | Legacy Mac (pre-10.9) |
| **Status (2024)** | Active, evolving | Active, dominant in enterprise | Deprecated by Apple |

**Key Observations:**

1. **NFS** is the standard for UNIX/Linux environments and HPC; pNFS enables parallel I/O across multiple storage servers.
2. **SMB 3.0+** introduced end-to-end encryption and multichannel, making it competitive with NFS even in mixed environments.
3. **AFP** is now deprecated in favor of SMB — Apple officially recommended SMB as default in macOS 10.9 (2013) and removed AFP support from newer OS versions.

**Conclusion:** For modern KTU 2024 exam context, **NFS** (Linux/Unix) and **SMB** (Windows) are the two most relevant protocols; AFP is mainly of historical interest.

*(7 marks: 4 for table, 3 for key observations)*

---

### 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**Q4(a)** What is NAS? Explain the working of an NFS file access operation with a sequence diagram. *(7 marks — CO2, Understand)*

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Network Attached Storage (NAS) is a dedicated file storage device that provides file-level data access to multiple clients over an IP network, using standardized protocols such as NFS, SMB, or AFP.

**Working of NFS File Access (Read Operation):**

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant C as NFS Client
    participant S as NFS Server
    C->>S: 1. MOUNT (request access)
    S-->>C: 2. File Handle (root)
    C->>S: 3. LOOKUP /home/user/file.txt
    S-->>C: 4. File Handle for file.txt
    C->>S: 5. OPEN (state, share)
    S-->>C: 6. State ID, delegation
    C->>S: 7. READ (offset 0, count 8192)
    S-->>C: 8. 8 KB of data
    C->>S: 9. CLOSE
    S-->>C: 10. ACK
```

**Step-by-step Explanation:**

1. **MOUNT** — The client establishes the initial mount; the server returns a file handle (an opaque token) representing the root of the exported file system.
2. **LOOKUP** — The client traverses the directory path; the server returns a file handle for the target file.
3. **OPEN** (NFSv4) — The client opens the file and acquires a state ID used to track locks and delegation.
4. **READ** — The client issues a READ RPC, specifying offset and byte count; the server returns the data over TCP.
5. **CLOSE** — The client releases the state, ending the file access session.
6. **umount** — The client tears down the mount point (not shown).

**NFSv4 Innovation:** All operations use a **single TCP connection** (stateful), unlike v3 which could use multiple UDP/TCP connections — greatly simplifying firewall traversal.

*(7 marks: 2 for definition, 3 for diagram, 2 for explanation)*

---

**Q4(b)** A 24-bay enterprise NAS uses 8 TB enterprise SAS drives in a RAID 6 configuration. Calculate:
   (i) The usable storage capacity.
   (ii) The effective file transfer time for a 10 GB file over 10 GbE with 10% protocol overhead.
   *(7 marks — CO2, Apply)*

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**
- $N = 24$ disks
- $C = 8$ TB per disk
- RAID level = 6
- File size $S = 10$ GB
- Network = 10 GbE
- Protocol overhead $O_p = 0.10$

**Part (i) — Usable Capacity:**

RAID 6 reserves 2 disks worth of space for parity (P and Q). Therefore:

$$
\eta_{\text{RAID6}} = \frac{N - 2}{N} = \frac{24 - 2}{24} = \frac{22}{24} = 0.9167
$$

$$
C_{\text{raw}} = N \times C = 24 \times 8 = 192 \text{ TB}
$$

$$
C_{\text{usable}} = C_{\text{raw}} \times \eta_{\text{RAID6}} = 192 \times 0.9167 = 176 \text{ TB}
$$

**Verification by direct subtraction:**

$$
C_{\text{usable}} = (N - 2) \times C = 22 \times 8 = 176 \text{ TB} \quad \checkmark
$$

**Answer (i): Usable capacity = 176 TB.**

**Part (ii) — File Transfer Time:**

Raw 10 GbE bandwidth:

$$
B_{\text{link}} = 10 \text{ Gb/s} = 10000 \text{ Mb/s} = 1250 \text{ MB/s}
$$

Effective bandwidth with protocol overhead:

$$
B_{\text{eff}} = B_{\text{link}} \times (1 - O_p) = 1250 \times (1 - 0.10) = 1250 \times 0.90 = 1125 \text{ MB/s}
$$

Convert file size to MB:

$$
S = 10 \text{ GB} = 10 \times 1024 \text{ MB} = 10240 \text{ MB}
$$

Transfer time:

$$
T_{\text{read}} = \frac{S}{B_{\text{eff}}} = \frac{10240 \text{ MB}}{1125 \text{ MB/s}} \approx 9.10 \text{ seconds}
$$

**Answer (ii): Transfer time ≈ 9.1 seconds.**

**Summary Table for Solution:**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Raw Capacity | 192 TB |
| Usable Capacity (RAID 6) | **176 TB** |
| Link Bandwidth | 1250 MB/s |
| Effective Bandwidth | 1125 MB/s |
| File Transfer Time | **9.1 s** |

*(7 marks: 2 for part (i), 3 for part (ii) calculation, 2 for summary table)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Student Mistakes:**
> 1. **Unit conversion error:** Forgetting to convert 10 Gb to 1250 MB/s. Always: 1 Gb = 1000 Mb = 125 MB (decimal) OR 1024 Mb = 128 MB (binary). KTU uses **decimal** in networking.
> 2. **File size unit confusion:** 1 GB = 1024 MB in storage (binary) but 1000 MB in networking (decimal). Check the problem context.
> 3. **RAID efficiency formula misapplication:** Some students use $(N-1)/N$ for RAID 6 — that is **wrong**. RAID 6 needs 2 parity disks → $(N-2)/N$.
> 4. **Confusing CIFS with SMB:** CIFS = SMB 1.0. Do not claim they are separate unrelated protocols.
> 5. **Forgetting port numbers in answers:** Examiner expects **port 2049 (NFS)**, **port 445 (SMB)**, **port 548 (AFP)** for full marks.
> 6. **Missing protocol overhead:** In numerical problems, students often compute $T = S/B_{\text{link}}$ without deducting overhead — leading to an overestimate of speed.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Scheme Module 2**

- ✅ **NAS** = file-level storage over **IP/Ethernet**, dedicated appliance, multi-client.
- ✅ **Core NAS protocols** = **NFS, SMB/CIFS, AFP** (legacy), **FTP, HTTP/WebDAV, iSCSI** (unified).
- ✅ **NFS port = 2049/TCP**, **SMB port = 445/TCP**, **AFP port = 548/TCP**, **iSCSI port = 3260/TCP**.
- ✅ **NFSv4** is **stateful**; NFSv2/v3 are **stateless**.
- ✅ **SMB 3.0+** has multichannel, end-to-end encryption, and **SMB Multichannel** for link aggregation.
- ✅ **AFP** is **deprecated** by Apple since macOS 10.9; SMB is now default.
- ✅ **NAS head** = CPU + RAM + NICs + Protocol Engine + File System Manager.
- ✅ **RAID 5 efficiency** = $(N-1)/N$; **RAID 6 efficiency** = $(N-2)/N$.
- ✅ **1 GbE raw bandwidth** = 125 MB/s; **10 GbE** = 1250 MB/s.
- ✅ **Effective bandwidth** = $B_{\text{link}} \times (1 - O_p)$ where $O_p$ = protocol overhead.
- ✅ **NAS is file-level (Layer 7); SAN is block-level (Layer 4-5)** — key distinction for exam.
- ✅ **pNFS** = parallel NFS enabling direct client-to-storage I/O bypassing head bottleneck.
- ✅ **NDMP** = protocol dedicated to NAS backup (do not confuse with file serving protocols).
- ✅ **Unified Storage** = NAS device that also serves block (iSCSI) → bridges NAS and SAN.
- ✅ **CIFS = SMB 1.0** (older dialect, not a separate protocol).
- ✅ **NFSv4 uses a single TCP connection**; NFSv3 used multiple UDP/TCP.
- ✅ **Kerberos authentication** is standard for both NFSv4 and modern SMB.
- ✅ **Most common KTU exam topics**: Protocol comparison tables, NAS architecture diagrams, RAID capacity, throughput calculations.
- ✅ **Always show unit conversions and formula substitutions** in numericals — partial marks are awarded for steps.

> **End of Module 2 — Network Attached Storage Protocols Notes**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a NAS Array?

A **Network Attached Storage (NAS) Array** is a dedicated, high-performance file-level data storage system that consolidates and shares data across an Ethernet-based IP network. A *NAS Array* specifically refers to the pairing of a **NAS head** (the file-serving controller) with a **redundant array of independent disks (RAID)** that provides the underlying physical storage media. Unlike direct-attached storage (DAS) which serves a single host, or a storage area network (SAN) which provides block-level access, a NAS Array exports files using industry-standard protocols such as **NFS (Network File System)** for UNIX/Linux and **CIFS/SMB (Common Internet File System / Server Message Block)** for Windows environments.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A NAS Array is a hybrid storage architecture combining file-serving intelligence (NAS) with RAID-protected physical disks (Array) to deliver shared, fault-tolerant, network-accessible storage.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **centralized library building** in a city:

- The **NAS Head** is the *librarian and the card-catalogue system* — it knows where every book (file) is located, who is allowed to borrow it, and how to check it in/out (file serving).
- The **Storage Array (RAID)** is the *basement archive room* filled with reinforced, fireproof filing cabinets arranged in a fault-tolerant pattern (RAID). Even if a few cabinets break, the data is preserved.
- The **Ethernet Network** is the *city's road system* — it allows authorized citizens (clients) to drive up and request files from the library.
- The **File Sharing Protocol (NFS/CIFS)** is the *library's borrowing card rulebook* — it standardizes how clients ask for and receive files.

So when a client wants a file, the request travels over the network, the librarian (NAS head) looks it up in the archive (RAID array), and delivers it back as a complete file — never exposing the raw disk layout.

### 1.3 Key Physical/Performance Constants

| Metric | Standard Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Standard disk capacity reference | **146 GB**, **300 GB**, **600 GB**, **1 TB**, **2 TB**, **4 TB** | GB / TB |
| Standard rotational speed | **7200**, **10000**, **15000** | RPM |
| Typical Ethernet link speed | **1 Gbps**, **10 Gbps**, **25 Gbps**, **100 Gbps** | Gigabits per second |
| Standard NAS head RAM | **8 GB – 256 GB** | GB |
| Default NAS block size (NFS) | **8192 bytes (8 KB)** | Bytes |
| Default CIFS packet size | **32 KB – 64 KB** | Kilobytes |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** Questions on NAS Arrays often hinge on the formula-driven difference between *physical capacity* (raw disk sum) and *usable capacity* (after RAID overhead). Mastering the constants above is essential for numerical problems.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Components of a NAS Array

A NAS Array is composed of four logical layers, each with specific responsibilities:

1. **Client Layer** — End-users and applications that generate file I/O requests over **TCP/IP**. The clients do not see raw LUNs (Logical Unit Numbers); they only see shared folders/exports.

2. **Network Layer** — A switched **Ethernet** fabric (Layer 2) interconnected with routers (Layer 3). Key design rules:
   - **Dedicated VLAN** for storage traffic to isolate it from user data.
   - **Jumbo Frames** (MTU **9000 bytes**) enabled end-to-end to reduce CPU overhead and improve throughput.
   - **Flow control** via IEEE 802.3x and **priority flow control** (PFC) for lossless Ethernet (especially for iSCSI or FCoE, but also relevant in modern NAS).

3. **NAS Head / File Server Layer** — The intelligence of the system. It runs an optimized file system (e.g., **WAFL** in NetApp, **OneFS** in Isilon/Dell EMC, **ONTAP**, or open-source **ZFS**, **btrfs**). Responsibilities:
   - Manages metadata (file names, permissions, locations, timestamps).
   - Performs file-level operations: `open`, `read`, `write`, `close`.
   - Translates file requests into block-level reads/writes against the array.
   - Enforces **access control lists (ACLs)** and **quota policies**.

4. **Storage Array / RAID Layer** — The physical disks organized in RAID sets. Provides:
   - **Data protection** (parity or mirroring).
   - **Performance** (striping for parallelism).
   - **Capacity aggregation**.

## 2.2 NAS Array Operational Flow (Step-by-Step Logic)

- **Step 1 — Client Request:** A user application issues a file request (e.g., `read file report.docx`). The request is wrapped in NFS or CIFS protocol headers and sent over TCP/IP to the NAS head's IP address.
- **Step 2 — File System Lookup:** The NAS head parses the request, validates user credentials (via Kerberos, LDAP, or local ACLs), and consults its in-memory metadata cache. If the metadata is not cached, it fetches it from the metadata volumes on the array.
- **Step 3 — Block Translation:** The file system converts the file's logical offset (byte position) into the corresponding **logical block addresses (LBAs)** on the physical disks.
- **Step 4 — RAID Operation:** The RAID controller retrieves the relevant data blocks. For RAID 5/6, it reads data stripes and verifies/strips parity as needed.
- **Step 5 — Data Assembly:** The NAS head assembles the file from the retrieved blocks, possibly reassembling small block reads into the requested file.
- **Step 6 — Response Delivery:** The complete file (or chunk) is sent back to the client over the network.

> [!NOTE]
> **The 'Why' Behind the Design:** File-level serving is CPU-intensive but network-efficient for small/medium files. Block-level serving (SAN) is more efficient for large, sequential workloads like databases. NAS Arrays are optimized for the *former* — sharing documents, media, backups, virtualization home directories.

## 2.3 RAID Levels — The Heart of the Array

A NAS Array *requires* a RAID layer. Each RAID level offers a unique trade-off between **capacity efficiency**, **performance**, and **fault tolerance**.

| RAID Level | Min. Disks | Parity/Mirror Strategy | Fault Tolerance | Read Perf. | Write Perf. | Use Case in NAS |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **RAID 0** | 2 | Striping, no parity | **0 disks** | High | High | Temp scratch, no protection needed |
| **RAID 1** | 2 | Full mirroring | **1 disk per pair** | Good | Medium | Critical OS volumes, small NAS |
| **RAID 5** | 3 | Single parity, rotating | **1 disk** | Good | Medium (write penalty) | General file shares, backups |
| **RAID 6** | 4 | Double parity, rotating | **2 disks** | Good | Low | Large-capacity NAS, archival |
| **RAID 10 (1+0)** | 4 | Stripe of mirrors | **1 disk per mirrored pair** | High | High | High-IOPS NAS, databases, VMs |
| **RAID-DP** (NetApp) | 3 | Two-diamond parity | **2 disks** | Good | Medium | Enterprise NAS (WAFL-based) |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas — they appear in 80% of numerical NAS Array problems.**

| Formula Description | Equation | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|
| **Usable Capacity — RAID 0** | $C_{usable} = n \times D_{min}$ | $n$ = number of disks, $D_{min}$ = smallest disk (GB) |
| **Usable Capacity — RAID 1** | $C_{usable} = \left(\dfrac{n}{2}\right) \times D_{min}$ | Assumes pairs; $n$ must be even |
| **Usable Capacity — RAID 5** | $C_{usable} = (n - 1) \times D_{min}$ | Single parity disk equivalent |
| **Usable Capacity — RAID 6** | $C_{usable} = (n - 2) \times D_{min}$ | Double parity |
| **Usable Capacity — RAID 10** | $C_{usable} = \left(\dfrac{n}{2}\right) \times D_{min}$ | $n$ must be even |
| **Usable Capacity — RAID-DP** | $C_{usable} = (n - 2) \times D_{min}$ | NetApp proprietary |
| **Total Raw Capacity** | $C_{raw} = n \times D_{min}$ | Sum of all disks (GB/TB) |
| **Capacity Overhead / Penalty** | $C_{overhead} = C_{raw} - C_{usable}$ | The "lost" capacity to protection |
| **Read Throughput (Sequential)** | $T_{read} = n_{data\_disks} \times T_{disk}$ | $T_{disk}$ = single-disk sequential throughput (MB/s) |
| **Write Throughput (RAID 5)** | $T_{write}^{R5} = \dfrac{n \times T_{disk}}{4}$ | Read-modify-write penalty (worst case small writes) |
| **Mean Time to Data Loss (MTDL)** | $\text{MTDL} \approx \dfrac{\text{MTBF}_{disk}^{2}}{n \times (n-1) \times \text{MTTR}}$ | For RAID 5/6 rebuild reliability |
| **NAS Effective Bandwidth** | $B_{eff} = \min(B_{network},\; B_{array})$ | Bottleneck rule — the slowest link limits throughput |
| **NFS Mount Point** | $/path/to/export$ | Standard Linux syntax |

> **Real-World Utility:** These formulas are used by storage architects when *sizing* a NAS Array. For example, a hospital needing **50 TB** of usable storage with RAID 6 using **4 TB** disks must order: $n = (50/4) + 2 = 14.5 \Rightarrow 15$ disks (round up for spares as well).
