# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation 1: Usable Capacity of a NAS Array with RAID 5

**Problem Statement (KTU-typical):** A NAS Array is configured with **8 disks**, each of capacity **2 TB**, in a RAID 5 configuration. Calculate the (a) raw capacity, (b) usable capacity, and (c) percentage of capacity overhead lost to parity.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Given:**
- $n = 8$ (number of disks)
- $D_{min} = 2$ TB (smallest disk capacity; all are equal)
- RAID Level = 5 (single distributed parity)

**Step (a) — Raw Capacity:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{raw} &= n \times D_{min} \\
        &= 8 \times 2 \text{ TB} \\
        &= 16 \text{ TB}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Conversion logic:* Raw capacity is the simple sum of every physical disk in the array, regardless of RAID configuration. It represents the total physical storage installed.

**Step (b) — Usable Capacity:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{usable} &= (n - 1) \times D_{min} \\
           &= (8 - 1) \times 2 \text{ TB} \\
           &= 7 \times 2 \text{ TB} \\
           &= 14 \text{ TB}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Conversion logic:* In RAID 5, the equivalent of one full disk's worth of space is consumed by distributed parity. Parity is striped across all disks (not stored on one dedicated disk), but its total *space* is exactly one disk's worth.

**Step (c) — Capacity Overhead Percentage:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{overhead} &= C_{raw} - C_{usable} \\
             &= 16 \text{ TB} - 14 \text{ TB} \\
             &= 2 \text{ TB}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%\;\text{Overhead} &= \left(\dfrac{C_{overhead}}{C_{raw}}\right) \times 100 \\
                    &= \left(\dfrac{2}{16}\right) \times 100 \\
                    &= 12.5\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Final Answer:** Raw = **16 TB**, Usable = **14 TB**, Overhead = **2 TB (12.5 %)**.

---

## 3.2 Worked Derivation 2: Usable Capacity with RAID 6 and Hot Spare

**Problem Statement:** A NAS Array has **10 disks of 4 TB each** in RAID 6, with **1 dedicated hot spare**. Calculate the total raw, usable, and *practically available* capacity. Also determine the fault tolerance.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Given:**
- Total disks installed $n_{total} = 10$
- Hot spares $n_{spare} = 1$
- Data-carrying disks $n = n_{total} - n_{spare} = 9$
- $D_{min} = 4$ TB
- RAID Level = 6 (double parity)

**Step 1 — Total Raw Capacity (all disks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{raw}^{total} &= n_{total} \times D_{min} \\
                &= 10 \times 4 \text{ TB} \\
                &= 40 \text{ TB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Usable Capacity (excluding hot spare, since it carries no data):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{usable} &= (n - 2) \times D_{min} \\
           &= (9 - 2) \times 4 \text{ TB} \\
           &= 7 \times 4 \text{ TB} \\
           &= 28 \text{ TB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Fault Tolerance:**

In RAID 6, the system can tolerate **2 simultaneous disk failures** *and* additionally survive the failure of the **hot spare** during rebuild — a total of **3 disk failures** before data loss.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Fault Tolerance (data disks)} &= 2 \\
\text{Fault Tolerance (with hot spare)} &= 3
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Final Answer:** Raw = **40 TB**, Usable = **28 TB**, Fault Tolerance = **2 disks (or 3 with spare)**.

---

## 3.3 Worked Derivation 3: NAS Network Bandwidth Bottleneck

**Problem Statement:** A NAS Array is connected via a **single 10 Gbps Ethernet link**. The underlying RAID 5 array comprises **8 SSDs**, each capable of **500 MB/s** sequential read. Calculate (a) the array's theoretical read throughput and (b) the effective NAS throughput, identifying the bottleneck.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Given:**
- $n = 8$ SSDs
- $T_{disk} = 500$ MB/s per disk
- RAID 5: $n_{data} = n - 1 = 7$ data disks
- $B_{network} = 10$ Gbps

**Step (a) — Array Read Throughput (RAID 5 read = all data disks can read in parallel, parity ignored):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{array} &= n_{data} \times T_{disk} \\
          &= 7 \times 500 \text{ MB/s} \\
          &= 3500 \text{ MB/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (b) — Convert Network Bandwidth to MB/s:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_{network}^{MB/s} &= 10 \text{ Gbps} \times \dfrac{1000 \text{ Mb/s}}{1 \text{ Gbps}} \times \dfrac{1 \text{ Byte}}{8 \text{ bits}} \\
                  &= \dfrac{10 \times 1000}{8} \text{ MB/s} \\
                  &= 1250 \text{ MB/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (c) — Effective NAS Throughput (Bottleneck Rule):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_{eff} &= \min(T_{array},\; B_{network}) \\
        &= \min(3500,\; 1250) \text{ MB/s} \\
        &= 1250 \text{ MB/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Final Answer:** Array = **3500 MB/s**, Network = **1250 MB/s**, **Network is the bottleneck**. To fully utilize the array, you would need $3500 / 1250 \approx 3$ additional 10 GbE links in a **LACP (Link Aggregation Control Protocol)** bundle, i.e., a **4 × 10 GbE** trunk.

---

## 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix — Choosing RAID for NAS

| Workload Type | Recommended RAID | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| Office file shares (mixed) | **RAID 5 or RAID 6** | Balance of capacity and protection |
| Video streaming / media | **RAID 6** | Large sequential reads, two-disk safety for big capacity |
| VM datastore on NAS (iSCSI) | **RAID 10** | Random I/O heavy, low latency, high IOPS |
| Email archive (write-once, read-many) | **RAID 5** | Sequential large writes, single parity sufficient |
| Backup target NAS | **RAID 6** | Capacity efficiency + double parity (rebuild risk on large disks) |
| Database files on NAS | **RAID 10** | Mirroring + striping for low-latency, high-IOPS random I/O |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Pattern]**

### Question 1: Define NAS Array. List any two file-sharing protocols used in NAS systems.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **NAS Array** is a dedicated file-level storage system that combines a NAS head (file server) with a RAID-based storage array, providing shared, fault-tolerant storage to multiple clients over an Ethernet network using TCP/IP. **(2 marks)**

Two file-sharing protocols used in NAS:
1. **NFS (Network File System)** — used primarily in UNIX/Linux environments. **(0.5 marks)**
2. **CIFS / SMB (Server Message Block)** — used primarily in Windows environments. **(0.5 marks)**

---

### Question 2: State the usable capacity formula for RAID 5 with `n` disks of equal size `D`.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The usable capacity of a RAID 5 array is:

$$
C_{usable} = (n - 1) \times D
$$

where $n$ is the number of disks and $D$ is the capacity of one disk. **(2 marks for formula, 1 mark for variable definition)**

**Justification:** In RAID 5, the equivalent of one disk's worth of space is used for distributed parity. **(included in the 2 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Question — Choice A (14 Marks Total)

> **[KTU University Exam – December 2023 Pattern, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

### Question A(a): [7 Marks]

A NAS Array is built using **10 disks of 1.8 TB each**, configured as **RAID 6** with **1 hot spare**. Calculate:
1. Total raw capacity
2. Usable capacity
3. Number of disk failures the array can tolerate
4. Percentage of capacity used for protection

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Total disks = 10
- Hot spares = 1 → Data disks $n = 9$
- Disk size $D = 1.8$ TB
- RAID 6 → two parity equivalents

**Step 1: Total Raw Capacity:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{raw} &= n_{total} \times D \\
        &= 10 \times 1.8 \text{ TB} \\
        &= 18 \text{ TB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Usable Capacity (RAID 6 formula):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{usable} &= (n - 2) \times D \\
           &= (9 - 2) \times 1.8 \text{ TB} \\
           &= 7 \times 1.8 \text{ TB} \\
           &= 12.6 \text{ TB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Fault Tolerance:**
- RAID 6 → can tolerate **2 simultaneous disk failures** among the 9 data disks. **(2 marks for stating)**
- With 1 hot spare that auto-rebuilds, the system can also survive a **third failure** that occurs during rebuild. **(1 mark for nuance)**

**Step 4: Percentage Used for Protection:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{overhead} &= C_{raw} - C_{usable} \\
             &= 18 - 12.6 = 5.4 \text{ TB}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%\;\text{Overhead} &= \left(\dfrac{5.4}{18}\right) \times 100 \\
                    &= 30\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Final Answer:** Raw = **18 TB**, Usable = **12.6 TB**, Tolerable failures = **2 (or 3 with spare)**, Protection overhead = **30 %**.

---

### Question A(b): [7 Marks]

Explain the architecture of a NAS Array with a neat block diagram. Compare NFS and CIFS protocols in terms of OS support, transport, and authentication.

**Model Solution Outline:**

**Architecture Explanation (4 marks):**
A NAS Array has three layers:
- **Network Layer:** Switched Ethernet, typically 1/10/25 GbE, with dedicated VLANs and jumbo frames.
- **NAS Head:** Optimized file system (WAFL, ZFS, OneFS) that handles metadata, ACLs, and file protocol processing.
- **Storage Array:** RAID-protected disks providing the underlying physical storage.

A standard block diagram (see SECTION 4.1 of these notes) should be drawn showing: Clients → Ethernet Switch → NAS Head (with file system) → RAID Controller → Disks.

**NFS vs CIFS Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Feature | NFS | CIFS / SMB |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary OS** | UNIX / Linux | Windows |
| **Default Transport** | TCP (also UDP in older versions) | TCP (port 445) |
| **Authentication** | UNIX UIDs/GIDs, Kerberos | NT Hash, Kerberos, NTLM |
| **File Locking** | Advisory (client-cooperative) | Mandatory (opportunistic + leased) |
| **Typical Use** | UNIX home dirs, VM farms | Windows shares, Office docs |

> **Closing Sentence:** NFS and CIFS are functionally similar (file sharing) but differ in platform affinity and locking semantics.

---

## 5.3 Part B Question — Choice B (14 Marks Total — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Sample Pattern, 2024 Scheme]**

### Question B(a): [7 Marks]

A media company requires a **NAS Array** with **at least 20 TB of usable storage** to store 4K video footage. Each disk is **4 TB**. The system must survive at least **2 simultaneous disk failures** during heavy rebuild periods. Recommend a suitable RAID level and calculate the minimum number of disks required. Also include 1 hot spare.

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Choose RAID Level:**

The requirement of surviving **2 simultaneous failures** rules out RAID 0, 1, 5, and 10. The candidate is **RAID 6** (or RAID-DP). **(1 mark for justification)**

**Step 2: Apply Usable Capacity Formula for RAID 6:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{usable} &= (n - 2) \times D \geq 20 \text{ TB}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where $n$ is the number of data disks (excluding hot spares) and $D = 4$ TB.

$$
\begin{aligned}
n - 2 &\geq \dfrac{20}{4} \\
n - 2 &\geq 5 \\
n &\geq 7
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Add Hot Spare:**

$$
n_{total} = 7 + 1 = 8 \text{ disks}
$$

**Step 4: Verify Capacity:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{usable} &= (7 - 2) \times 4 = 5 \times 4 = 20 \text{ TB} \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: State Recommendation:**

> **Recommended:** RAID 6 with **8 disks total** (7 data + 1 hot spare), each **4 TB**, providing **20 TB usable** with tolerance for **2 simultaneous failures** plus an additional disk during rebuild.

---

### Question B(b): [7 Marks]

A NAS Array is connected to the network via **two 10 GbE links** aggregated using **LACP**. The RAID 5 array has **6 SSDs**, each delivering **400 MB/s** sequential read. Compute:
1. Array read throughput
2. Aggregate network throughput
3. Effective NAS throughput
4. Identify the bottleneck

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Array Read Throughput (RAID 5: parity disk not read for data reads):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{array} &= n_{data} \times T_{disk} \\
          &= 5 \times 400 \text{ MB/s} \\
          &= 2000 \text{ MB/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Aggregate Network Throughput (2 × 10 GbE with LACP):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_{network} &= 2 \times 10 \text{ Gbps} \times \dfrac{1000}{8} \text{ MB/s per Gbps} \\
            &= 2 \times 1250 \text{ MB/s} \\
            &= 2500 \text{ MB/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Effective NAS Throughput (Bottleneck Rule):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_{eff} &= \min(T_{array},\; B_{network}) \\
        &= \min(2000,\; 2500) \text{ MB/s} \\
        &= 2000 \text{ MB/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Bottleneck Identification:**

> **The array (2000 MB/s) is the bottleneck.** The network has spare capacity of $2500 - 2000 = 500$ MB/s. To eliminate the bottleneck, one could add more SSDs to the array or use larger-capacity faster disks.

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in NAS Array Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the hot spare in capacity calculations** — Students often include the hot spare in the data-disk count, which is *wrong*. Hot spares carry no data and must be excluded from $n$ when applying RAID formulas. **Always subtract spares first.**
> 2. **Using `n` for RAID 1 incorrectly** — RAID 1 requires *paired* disks. If you have 7 disks in RAID 1, you have only 3 mirrored pairs (with 1 disk unused or as a hot spare), giving $C_{usable} = 3 \times D$, not $3.5 \times D$.
> 3. **Converting Gbps to MB/s incorrectly** — Use the factor $\frac{1000}{8} = 125$ MB/s per Gbps, *not* $\frac{1024}{8}$. KTU expects SI units (decimal).
> 4. **Confusing "raw" with "usable"** — Examiners deduct marks if a question explicitly asks for *usable* capacity and the student gives the *raw* total.
> 5. **Omitting units (TB, MB/s)** — Always write units in your final numerical answer. A correct number without units gets partial credit only.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist for NAS Arrays:**

- **Definition:** A NAS Array = NAS Head + RAID Array, providing file-level shared storage over Ethernet.
- **Two Key Protocols:** NFS (UNIX/Linux, TCP/UDP) and CIFS/SMB (Windows, TCP/445).
- **NAS Head Functions:** Metadata management, file serving, ACL enforcement, protocol translation.
- **Network Best Practices:** Dedicated VLAN, jumbo frames (MTU 9000), LACP for link aggregation.
- **RAID 0:** $C = n \times D$ — no protection.
- **RAID 1:** $C = (n/2) \times D$ — full mirroring, $n$ must be even.
- **RAID 5:** $C = (n-1) \times D$ — single distributed parity, 1-disk fault tolerance.
- **RAID 6:** $C = (n-2) \times D$ — double parity, 2-disk fault tolerance, best for large NAS.
- **RAID 10:** $C = (n/2) \times D$ — best performance, $n$ must be even, 1-disk tolerance per pair.
- **Hot Spare Rule:** Always subtract hot spares from $n$ before applying RAID capacity formulas.
- **Bottleneck Rule:** $B_{eff} = \min(B_{network}, B_{array})$ — the slower link limits throughput.
- **Gbps to MB/s Conversion:** Multiply Gbps by $125$ to get MB/s (SI).
- **Fault Tolerance Order:** RAID 0 (0) < RAID 1/10 (1) < RAID 5 (1) < RAID 6 / RAID-DP (2).
- **Write Penalty (RAID 5):** A small write requires 4 I/O operations (read old data, read old parity, write new data, write new parity).
- **Use RAID 10 for:** VM datastores, databases, transactional workloads.
- **Use RAID 6 for:** Backup targets, archives, large-capacity media storage.
- **Use RAID 5 for:** General file shares with moderate capacity (< 10 TB raw).
- **LACP:** Combine multiple physical links into one logical link for redundancy and bandwidth.
- **MTDL Formula:** $\text{MTDL} \approx \text{MTBF}_{disk}^{2} / (n(n-1) \cdot \text{MTTR})$ — decreases as $n$ grows.
- **NSPOF (No Single Point of Failure):** Achieved in NAS Arrays through dual NAS heads, redundant RAID controllers, dual power supplies, and LACP network links.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: NAS Performance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST867 - STORAGE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** NAS Performance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**NAS Performance** refers to the quantitative and qualitative measurement, analysis, and optimization of how efficiently a **Network Attached Storage (NAS)** system delivers data to clients over a network. It encompasses the speed, reliability, and scalability of file-serving operations (read/write/metadata) across standardized protocols such as **NFS (Network File System)** and **CIFS/SMB (Common Internet File System / Server Message Block)**.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme outcome-based framework, NAS performance is evaluated across four canonical metrics:

1. **Throughput** – Data delivered per unit time (MB/s or GB/s).
2. **IOPS (Input/Output Operations Per Second)** – Number of file operations completed per second.
3. **Latency / Response Time** – Time delay between a client request and the NAS response (milliseconds).
4. **Utilization** – Percentage of system resources (CPU, memory, NIC, disk spindle) actively engaged.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The university examiner frequently tests the difference between **block-level performance (SAN/DAS)** and **file-level performance (NAS)**. Remember — NAS operates at the **file level** through the OS file system, while SAN operates at the **block level** below the file system. This architectural difference dictates the entire performance envelope.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

Imagine a **centralized library** in your college:

- The **NAS device** is the librarian + the bookshelves.
- The **clients** are students requesting books.
- The **network (LAN/Ethernet)** is the corridor through which the student walks to the librarian.
- **NFS/CIFS** is the rulebook governing how requests are written on paper and how books are handed over.

Now, **NAS performance** is the time it takes from the moment a student says *"I want that book"* to the moment they hold it. This depends on:

- How fast the librarian thinks (NAS **CPU/memory**)
- How fast the librarian walks down the aisle (NAS **disk speed**)
- How wide the corridor is (Network **bandwidth**)
- Whether the librarian has a quick-recall desk for popular books (NAS **cache**)
- How long the request slip takes to travel (Protocol **overhead**)

> [!NOTE]
> If any single component — corridor, librarian, or shelf — is too slow, the entire request is delayed. This is the **"weakest link" principle** of NAS performance tuning.

## 1.3 Key Performance Metrics (Standard Units)

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Unit | Typical NAS Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| Throughput | $T$ | MB/s, GB/s | 100 MB/s – 10 GB/s |
| IOPS | $\text{IOP}$ | ops/sec | 1,000 – 100,000+ |
| Latency | $L$ | ms (milliseconds) | 1 ms – 50 ms |
| Response Time | $R_t$ | ms | 1 ms – 100 ms |
| Bandwidth | $BW$ | Mbps, Gbps | 1 Gbps – 100 Gbps |
| Queue Depth | $Q_d$ | integer | 1 – 256 |
| CPU Utilization | $U_{cpu}$ | % | 0 % – 100 % |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Tip:** Throughput and IOPS are **not** the same. A NAS optimized for **large sequential file transfers** (video editing) may score high on throughput but low on IOPS. A NAS optimized for **small random I/O** (database, mail server) scores high on IOPS but lower on throughput. Always identify the **workload profile** before benchmarking.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The NAS Performance Stack (Layered Architecture)

NAS performance is the **aggregate result** of delays accumulated at every architectural layer. The KTU 2024 examiner expects you to enumerate these layers explicitly:

1. **Application Layer** — File open/close, data read/write requests issued by client software.
2. **File System Layer (Client-side)** — Local OS buffers, page cache, and VFS translates the request into NFS/CIFS calls.
3. **Protocol Layer (NFS / CIFS)** — Packetization, RPC marshalling, TCP/IP encapsulation. Adds **protocol overhead**.
4. **Network Layer (Ethernet / TCP/IP)** — Bandwidth limitation, latency, packet loss, retransmission.
5. **NAS Head (Controller) Layer** — NAS CPU + memory handles authentication, protocol termination, file system metadata lookup.
6. **Storage Layer (Disks / RAID / SSDs)** — Physical read/write operations, seek time, rotational latency, controller cache.

> [!NOTE]
> **End-to-end latency** = Σ (delay at each layer). Optimizing only one layer (e.g., buying faster disks) without addressing another (e.g., congested 1 Gbps NIC) yields **diminishing returns** — this is the **system bottleneck principle**.

## 2.2 Fundamental Performance Formulas

### 2.2.1 Throughput

$$T = \frac{D}{t}$$

Where $D$ is the total data transferred (in MB) and $t$ is the elapsed time (in seconds). For streaming workloads, throughput dominates.

### 2.2.2 IOPS Calculation

$$\text{IOPS} = \frac{1}{T_{s} + T_{r} + T_{t}}$$

Where:
- $T_{s}$ = average seek time (ms)
- $T_{r}$ = average rotational latency (ms)
- $T_{t}$ = transfer time per I/O (ms)

For a **7,200 RPM** disk: $T_{r} = \dfrac{60{,}000 \text{ ms}}{7{,}200} \cdot 0.5 \approx 4.17 \text{ ms}$.

### 2.2.3 Latency vs. Response Time

$$R_t = L_{queue} + L_{service} + L_{network}$$

Where $L_{queue}$ is queueing delay, $L_{service}$ is service time, and $L_{network}$ is round-trip network delay.

### 2.2.4 Little's Law (Queueing Theory)

$$Q_{avg} = \lambda \cdot R_t$$

Where $Q_{avg}$ is the average number of requests in the system, $\lambda$ is the arrival rate (req/s), and $R_t$ is the average response time. This is the **gold-standard relationship** for sizing NAS controllers.

### 2.2.5 Effective Network Throughput

$$T_{eff} = \frac{P_{size} \cdot 8}{RTT} \cdot \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{p}}$$

Where $P_{size}$ is the packet size, $RTT$ is round-trip time, and $p$ is packet loss probability. This TCP-window-aware formula shows why **packet loss devastates NAS throughput** over WANs.

### 2.2.6 Protocol Overhead (NFS/CIFS)

$$T_{useful} = T_{wire} \cdot \frac{D_{payload}}{D_{payload} + D_{header}}$$

Typical NFS over TCP/IP adds **~80–120 bytes** of header per RPC call; small file workloads are highly sensitive to this overhead.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Application / When to Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T = D / t$ | Throughput for sequential workloads |
| 2 | $\text{IOPS} = 1/(T_s + T_r + T_t)$ | Disk-level IOPS estimation |
| 3 | $R_t = L_q + L_s + L_{net}$ | Total response time decomposition |
| 4 | $Q_{avg} = \lambda \cdot R_t$ | Little's Law — controller sizing |
| 5 | $T_{eff} = (P \cdot 8 / RTT) \cdot 1/\sqrt{p}$ | TCP throughput with loss |
| 6 | $T_{useful} = T_{wire} \cdot (D_p / (D_p + D_h))$ | Protocol overhead ratio |
| 7 | $U = (\text{Busy time}) / (\text{Total time})$ | Resource utilization check |
| 8 | $\text{Speedup} = T_{old} / T_{new}$ | Quantify tuning benefit |
| 9 | $\text{RAID penalty} = R + W$ | RAID-1 write = 2 I/Os, RAID-5 write = 4 I/Os |
| 10 | $\text{Total I/O} = \text{Read} + k \cdot \text{Write}$ | Write penalty-aware IOPS planning |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield:** **Little's Law** is a favourite 7-mark question. State the law, define all three variables ($\lambda$, $R_t$, $Q_{avg}$), and apply it to a **NAS sizing scenario** (e.g., "If a NAS must serve 5,000 requests/sec and the average response time is 4 ms, how many requests are in-flight?").

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

NAS performance engineering is mission-critical in:

- **Media & Entertainment** — 4K/8K video editing requires sustained **multi-GB/s throughput**.
- **Healthcare (PACS)** — Medical imaging archives need **low-latency random read** of large DICOM files.
- **E-Commerce / OLTP** — Transactional databases demand **high IOPS + consistent sub-10 ms latency**.
- **AI/ML Data Lakes** — Training pipelines require **parallel read bandwidth** from thousands of small files.
- **Backup & Archival** — Long sequential writes dominate; latency is irrelevant, throughput is everything.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Maximum Theoretical IOPS of a Single HDD

> **Problem:** A 7,200 RPM SATA disk has an average seek time of 8 ms and a transfer rate of 100 MB/s. If every I/O is a random 4 KB read, calculate the maximum IOPS and the corresponding throughput.

### Step 1: Calculate Average Rotational Latency

For a disk rotating at 7,200 RPM, one full revolution takes:

$$T_{rev} = \frac{60{,}000 \text{ ms}}{7{,}200} = 8.33 \text{ ms}$$

The average rotational latency is **half** of one revolution (head is equally likely to be at any angular position):

$$T_{r} = \frac{T_{rev}}{2} = \frac{8.33}{2} \approx 4.17 \text{ ms}$$

### Step 2: Calculate Transfer Time for 4 KB

$$T_{t} = \frac{\text{Block size}}{\text{Transfer rate}} = \frac{4 \text{ KB}}{100 \text{ MB/s}} = \frac{4 \times 1024 \text{ Bytes}}{100 \times 10^{6} \text{ Bytes/s}}$$

$$T_{t} = \frac{4096}{10^{8}} \text{ s} = 4.096 \times 10^{-5} \text{ s} = 0.041 \text{ ms}$$

### Step 3: Sum All Components of Service Time

$$T_{service} = T_{s} + T_{r} + T_{t} = 8 + 4.17 + 0.041 = 12.211 \text{ ms}$$

### Step 4: Compute IOPS

$$\text{IOPS} = \frac{1}{T_{service}} = \frac{1}{0.012211 \text{ s}} \approx 81.9 \text{ IOPS}$$

### Step 5: Compute Throughput

$$T = \text{IOPS} \times \text{Block size} = 81.9 \times 4 \text{ KB} \approx 327.6 \text{ KB/s}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A single 7.2K HDD can only serve ~80 random 4 KB IOPS. This is why **enterprise NAS systems use hundreds of disks in parallel** or migrate to SSDs/flash to scale IOPS.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Applying Little's Law to NAS Sizing

> **Problem:** A NAS head is observed to have an average of 80 requests in its service queue. The average arrival rate of file requests is 4,000 req/s. Calculate the average response time. If the system administrator wishes to reduce response time to 5 ms, how many requests will be in the system at the new steady state?

### Step 1: Recall Little's Law

$$Q_{avg} = \lambda \cdot R_t$$

### Step 2: Solve for Response Time

$$R_t = \frac{Q_{avg}}{\lambda} = \frac{80}{4{,}000} = 0.02 \text{ s} = 20 \text{ ms}$$

### Step 3: Recompute for Target Response Time

At the same arrival rate ($\lambda = 4{,}000$ req/s) and new response time $R_t = 5$ ms $= 0.005$ s:

$$Q_{avg}^{new} = \lambda \cdot R_t^{new} = 4{,}000 \times 0.005 = 20 \text{ requests}$$

### Step 4: Engineering Interpretation

The administrator has reduced the in-flight queue by **75%** (80 → 20), which corresponds to faster CPU/memory processing or better caching — both of which shrink $L_{service}$ in the response time equation.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: NAS Write Penalty in RAID-5

> **Problem:** A NAS uses RAID-5 with 8 disks. Calculate the effective IOPS for 100% random write workload, given that each disk can do 180 IOPS. Also calculate the IOPS for 100% random read.

### Step 1: Recall RAID-5 Write Penalty

A RAID-5 write of a single block requires:
- 1 read of the old data
- 1 read of the old parity
- 1 write of the new data
- 1 write of the new parity

Total = **4 I/Os per logical write**.

### Step 2: Disk-Level IOPS Budget

Total available IOPS across 8 disks:

$$\text{IOPS}_{total} = 8 \times 180 = 1{,}440 \text{ disk-IOPS}$$

### Step 3: Effective Write IOPS

Each logical write consumes 4 disk I/Os, so:

$$\text{IOPS}_{write}^{eff} = \frac{\text{IOPS}_{total}}{4} = \frac{1{,}440}{4} = 360 \text{ IOPS}$$

### Step 4: Effective Read IOPS

RAID-5 reads do **not** need parity access, so:

$$\text{IOPS}_{read}^{eff} = 1{,}440 \text{ IOPS}$$

### Step 5: Mixed Workload (e.g., 70% Read, 30% Write)

$$\text{IOPS}_{mixed} = 0.7 \times 1{,}440 + 0.3 \times 360 = 1{,}008 + 108 = 1{,}116 \text{ IOPS}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often forget the **write penalty multiplier** when computing RAID capacity. For RAID-5, the penalty is 4. For RAID-6, it is **6** (two parity writes). For RAID-1/10, it is **2**. Always memorize these.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: NAS vs. SAN Performance Profile

| Performance Aspect | NAS (File-Level) | SAN (Block-Level) |
|---|---|---|
| Protocol overhead | Higher (NFS/CIFS + TCP/IP) | Lower (iSCSI/FC encapsulated) |
| Typical latency | 5 – 50 ms | 1 – 10 ms |
| Throughput ceiling | Limited by file system + NIC | Limited by HBA + storage array |
| IOPS ceiling | Moderate (file system bottleneck) | Very high (direct block access) |
| Concurrent clients | Hundreds to thousands | Tens to hundreds |
| Caching granularity | File-level cache | Block-level cache |
| Best workload | File sharing, home directories, media | Databases, VMs, mission-critical OLTP |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 NAS Performance Data Flow (Layered Bottleneck Identification)

The following Mermaid block renders the **block-level functional architecture** of a NAS request, identifying which layer contributes what delay. This is the **canonical diagram** KTU expects for 7-mark "Explain NAS performance factors" questions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Client Application] --> B[Client OS File System Cache]
    B --> C[NFS or CIFS Protocol Stack]
    C --> D[TCP IP Network]
    D --> E[NAS Head NIC]
    E --> F[NAS Head CPU and Memory]
    F --> G[NAS File System Metadata Lookup]
    G --> H[Storage Controller Cache]
    H --> I[RAID Engine]
    I --> J[Physical Disks or SSDs]
    J --> I
    I --> H
    H --> G
    G --> F
    F --> E
    E --> D
    D --> C
    C --> B
    B --> A
    K[Aggregate End to End Latency] -. monitors .- A
    K -. monitors .- B
    K -. monitors .- C
    K -. monitors .- D
    K -. monitors .- E
    K -. monitors .- F
    K -. monitors .- G
    K -. monitors .- H
    K -. monitors .- I
    K -. monitors .- J
```

**Reading the diagram:** The leftward arrows on rows B–A represent the **response path** back to the client. The dashed arrows from `K` indicate the **monitoring points** where performance metrics are collected by a NAS performance analyzer (e.g., `nfsstat`, `iostat`, `netstat`).

---

## 4.2 NAS Performance Tuning Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start NAS Performance Audit] --> B{Is latency high?}
    B -- Yes --> C{CPU Utilization gt 80 percent}
    B -- No --> D{Is throughput low}
    C -- Yes --> E[Upgrade NAS Head CPU and RAM]
    C -- No --> F{Network Utilization gt 80 percent}
    F -- Yes --> G[Upgrade NIC to 10 Gbps or add link aggregation]
    F -- No --> H{Disk Queue Depth gt 10}
    H -- Yes --> I[Add spindles or migrate to SSD tier]
    H -- No --> J[Profile Application Workload]
    D --> K{Block size small and random}
    K -- Yes --> I
    K -- No --> L{Workload is sequential large file}
    L --> Yes --> M[Enable Read Ahead Cache and Jumbo Frames]
    L --> No --> J
    J --> N[Apply Protocol Tuning NFS rsize wsize CIFS Buffer Size]
```

---

## 4.3 NAS Performance Benchmarking Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Define Workload Profile] --> B[Select Benchmark Tool]
    B --> C[IOzone or dd for throughput]
    B --> D[fio or SPECsfs for IOPS]
    B --> E[Netperf or iperf for network]
    C --> F[Run Baseline Test]
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G[Collect Metrics]
    G --> H[Identify Bottleneck]
    H --> I[Apply Tuning Fix]
    I --> J[Re run Test]
    J --> K{Performance Improved}
    K -- Yes --> L[Document Configuration]
    K -- No --> H
```

---

## 4.4 Component-Level Latency Contribution (Textual Block Diagram)

| Layer | Component | Typical Latency Contribution | Optimisation Lever |
|---|---|---|---|
| L1 | Application | 0.1 – 1 ms | Application-side buffering |
| L2 | Client Cache | 0.01 – 0.1 ms (hit) / 5 ms (miss) | Increase client RAM |
| L3 | NFS / CIFS | 0.5 – 2 ms | Increase `rsize`/`wsize` |
| L4 | TCP/IP Network | 0.1 – 10 ms (LAN) | Upgrade to 10/25/100 GbE |
| L5 | NAS Head CPU | 0.5 – 3 ms | Faster CPU, offload crypto |
| L6 | File System | 0.2 – 2 ms | Defragment, switch to WAFL/ZFS |
| L7 | Storage Cache | 0.05 – 0.5 ms (hit) | Expand controller cache |
| L8 | RAID + Disks | 1 – 15 ms | SSD tier, more spindles |

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

### Q1. Differentiate between IOPS and Throughput in the context of NAS performance. *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | IOPS | Throughput |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Number of I/O operations completed per second | Volume of data transferred per second |
| Unit | Operations/second | MB/s or GB/s |
| Workload Fit | Small random I/O (databases, mail) | Large sequential I/O (video, backup) |
| Disk Sensitivity | High (seeks dominate) | Low (transfer rate dominates) |
| SSD vs HDD | SSDs achieve 10× – 100× higher IOPS | Throughput gap is narrower (2× – 5×) |

A NAS workload biased toward many small files benefits from higher IOPS, while a workload biased toward large file streaming benefits from higher throughput. **Both metrics must be measured**; they are not interchangeable. *(3 marks — definition 1, unit 0.5, workload mapping 1, distinction 0.5)*

---

### Q2. What is the role of caching in improving NAS performance? *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

Caching stores frequently or recently accessed data in a **faster memory tier** (RAM or SSD) close to the NAS CPU, bypassing the slow disk layer.

1. **Read Cache** — serves repeat reads from RAM/SSD in **<1 ms** instead of 5–15 ms from disk.
2. **Write Cache** — coalesces multiple small writes and flushes them to disk asynchronously, reducing write latency.
3. **Metadata Cache** — caches inode/directory lookups, accelerating file open/create operations.
4. **Net Effect** — caching typically delivers **3× – 10× latency reduction** and **2× – 5× throughput improvement** for cache-friendly workloads.

*(3 marks — definition 1, types 1, numerical impact 1)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 2]**

### Question A — 14 Marks

**(a)** Explain the **key performance metrics** used to evaluate a NAS system. For each metric, state its unit and the type of workload it best characterizes. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A NAS uses **RAID-5 across 6 disks**, each capable of **150 IOPS**. Calculate the effective IOPS for:
   (i) 100% random read workload,
   (ii) 100% random write workload,
   (iii) A mixed workload of **60% reads and 40% writes**. *(7 marks)*

---

### Model Answer — Question A

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

The four canonical NAS performance metrics are:

**1. Throughput (Bandwidth achieved)**
- **Unit:** MB/s, GB/s, Mbps, Gbps
- **Best for:** Large sequential workloads — video editing, backup, scientific data.
- **Formula:** $T = D/t$.
- **Measured by:** `dd`, `IOzone`, `iperf`.

**2. IOPS (I/O Operations Per Second)**
- **Unit:** operations per second
- **Best for:** Small random workloads — OLTP databases, email, virtualization.
- **Formula:** $\text{IOPS} = 1/(T_s + T_r + T_t)$.
- **Measured by:** `fio`, `SPECsfs`.

**3. Latency / Response Time**
- **Unit:** milliseconds (ms)
- **Best for:** Interactive workloads — file open, database query.
- **Formula:** $R_t = L_{queue} + L_{service} + L_{network}$.
- **Measured by:** `nfsstat`, `iostat -x`.

**4. Utilization**
- **Unit:** Percentage (%)
- **Best for:** Capacity planning, bottleneck detection.
- **Formula:** $U = \text{Busy time} / \text{Total time}$.
- **Measured by:** OS-level performance monitors (top, sar, perfmon).

**Additional metrics for NAS:**
- **Queue Depth** — number of outstanding I/O requests.
- **File Open Rate** — files opened per second (CIFS/NFS metadata load).
- **Cache Hit Ratio** — percentage of requests served from cache.

*(Allocation: 4 metrics × 1.25 marks = 5; workload mapping 1; tools 1)*

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:** 6 disks × 150 IOPS each, RAID-5 configuration.

**(i) 100% Random Read**

RAID-5 reads require **1 disk I/O** per logical read. Effective IOPS:

$$\text{IOPS}_{read} = 6 \times 150 = 900 \text{ IOPS}$$

**(ii) 100% Random Write**

RAID-5 writes have a **write penalty of 4** (old data read + old parity read + new data write + new parity write). Effective IOPS:

$$\text{IOPS}_{write} = \frac{6 \times 150}{4} = \frac{900}{4} = 225 \text{ IOPS}$$

**(iii) Mixed: 60% Read, 40% Write**

$$\text{IOPS}_{mixed} = 0.6 \times 900 + 0.4 \times 225 = 540 + 90 = 630 \text{ IOPS}$$

**Interpretation:** The RAID-5 write penalty causes a **4× reduction** in write IOPS compared to read IOPS. For write-heavy NAS workloads, **RAID-10** (penalty = 2) or SSD tiering is preferred. *(7 marks: 2 + 2 + 3)*

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**(a)** With the help of a **block diagram**, describe the **NAS performance stack** and explain how latency accumulates at each layer. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Apply **Little's Law** to solve the following: A NAS file server observes an average of **120 concurrent requests** in its service system. The average arrival rate of client requests is **6,000 req/s**.
   (i) Calculate the average response time per request.
   (ii) If the administrator upgrades the NAS CPU and reduces response time to **10 ms**, how many requests will now be in-flight?
   (iii) Comment on the engineering significance of Little's Law for NAS capacity planning. *(7 marks)*

---

### Model Answer — Question B

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

A NAS request traverses **six logical layers**; each contributes to end-to-end latency.

**Block Diagram (textual):**

| Stage | Layer | Function | Typical Latency |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Client Application | Issues file I/O request | 0.1 – 1 ms |
| 2 | Client File System + Protocol Stack | NFS/CIFS packetization | 0.5 – 2 ms |
| 3 | Network (TCP/IP) | Wire transmission, retransmission | 0.1 – 10 ms |
| 4 | NAS Head (CPU + RAM) | Authentication, RPC, FS lookup | 0.5 – 3 ms |
| 5 | Storage Controller Cache | Cache hit/miss decision | 0.05 – 0.5 ms |
| 6 | Disks / RAID Group | Physical read/write | 1 – 15 ms |

**Cumulative Latency Equation:**

$$R_t^{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{6} L_i = L_{app} + L_{proto} + L_{net} + L_{cpu} + L_{cache} + L_{disk}$$

**Why latency accumulates:** Each layer's delay is **serial and blocking** — a request cannot proceed to the next layer until the current one completes. The slowest layer (typically the disk or the network) becomes the **bottleneck** that determines overall throughput. Optimisation must target the **dominant layer** identified via monitoring.

*(7 marks: layers 4, diagram 1, equation 1, bottleneck explanation 1)*

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:** $Q_{avg} = 120$, $\lambda = 6{,}000$ req/s.

**(i) Average Response Time**

By Little's Law: $Q_{avg} = \lambda \cdot R_t$

$$R_t = \frac{Q_{avg}}{\lambda} = \frac{120}{6{,}000} = 0.02 \text{ s} = 20 \text{ ms}$$

**(ii) In-flight Requests at 10 ms Response Time**

With $\lambda$ unchanged at 6,000 req/s and new $R_t = 10$ ms $= 0.01$ s:

$$Q_{avg}^{new} = \lambda \cdot R_t^{new} = 6{,}000 \times 0.01 = 60 \text{ requests}$$

**(iii) Engineering Significance**

Little's Law provides a **fundamental relationship** between three operational variables — arrival rate, in-flight requests, and response time — that is independent of the internal queueing distribution. For NAS capacity planners, it enables:

1. **Sizing the NAS controller** — by fixing a target response time (e.g., 5 ms) and known arrival rate, one computes the required concurrency to provision CPU/memory.
2. **Predicting saturation** — if $R_t$ grows linearly with $\lambda$, the system is approaching its capacity ceiling.
3. **Justifying hardware upgrades** — measuring $Q_{avg}$ before/after an upgrade quantifies the improvement (here, 120 → 60 = 50% reduction).
4. **SLA validation** — knowing $\lambda$ and monitoring $Q_{avg}$ in real time, operators can compute live $R_t$ and ensure it stays below SLA thresholds.

*(7 marks: part i 2, part ii 2, part iii 3)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark Deductions in NAS Performance Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing IOPS with Throughput** — IOPS measures *operations per second*, throughput measures *bytes per second*. Mixing units is an instant **−1 to −2 marks**.
> 2. **Forgetting RAID write penalty** — Using a single 150 IOPS figure for RAID-5 writes (instead of dividing by 4) loses **2 marks** in numerical questions.
> 3. **Skipping units** — KTU examiners deduct 0.5 marks per missing unit (MB/s, ms, req/s).
> 4. **Omitting the workload assumption** — IOPS/throughput numbers must always be qualified as *random 4 KB* or *sequential 1 MB*. A bare "900 IOPS" is incomplete.
> 5. **Mishandling Little's Law** — Units must be consistent: if $\lambda$ is in **req/s** and $R_t$ is in **s**, then $Q_{avg}$ is dimensionless (requests). Converting to ms incorrectly is a frequent error.
> 6. **No mention of protocol overhead** — When discussing NAS bottlenecks, omitting NFS/CIFS overhead and TCP/IP packetization is considered an **incomplete answer** for 7-mark questions.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Quick-Revision Checklist — NAS Performance**

- ☐ NAS performance is evaluated using **four core metrics**: Throughput, IOPS, Latency, Utilization.
- ☐ **Throughput** = $D/t$ (MB/s) — dominant for **large sequential** workloads.
- ☐ **IOPS** = $1/(T_s + T_r + T_t)$ — dominant for **small random** workloads.
- ☐ **Response Time** $R_t = L_{queue} + L_{service} + L_{network}$ — decomposes total delay into three components.
- ☐ **Little's Law** $Q_{avg} = \lambda \cdot R_t$ — the master equation for NAS capacity planning.
- ☐ **RAID-5 write penalty = 4**, **RAID-6 = 6**, **RAID-1/10 = 2** — must be applied in every IOPS calculation.
- ☐ **Effective TCP throughput** drops with the square root of packet loss — avoid congested networks.
- ☐ **Caching** typically gives 3×–10× latency reduction — use read cache for hot files, write cache for bursty writes.
- ☐ **Network upgrades** (1 → 10 → 25 → 100 GbE) and **SSD tiering** are the two highest-impact NAS performance levers.
- ☐ **Protocol overhead** (NFS/CIFS + TCP/IP) adds 80–120 bytes per request — small files suffer most.
- ☐ Always specify the **workload profile** (block size, read/write ratio, sequential/random) before quoting IOPS/throughput numbers.
- ☐ NAS operates at the **file level** through the OS file system; SAN operates at the **block level** — a foundational distinction for every KTU exam answer.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Object Storage - Objects and Object IDs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECST867 - STORAGE SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Object Storage - Objects and Object IDs # 🧱 Module 2 — Object Storage: Objects and Object IDs ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 What is Object Storage? > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Aligned):** > **Object Storage** is a flat-addressing, distributed data storage architecture that manages data as discrete, self-describing units called **objects**. Unlike file systems (hierarchical) or block storage (raw sectors), object storage stores each piece of data together with its **metadata**, a **globally unique identifier (Object ID)**, and access policies inside a single container called an *object*. Data is retrieved purely via **HTTP-based REST APIs** (e.g., `GET`, `PUT`, `DELETE`) using the object ID — there is no directory tree, no block-level addressing, and no path-based lookup. The three primitive entities in any Object Storage System (OSS) are: | Entity | Role | |---|---| | **Object** | The actual data payload + user-defined metadata + system metadata | | **Object ID (OID / Key)** | A globally unique, opaque, flat identifier (typically a 128-bit or 160-bit hash) used to address the object | | **Bucket / Container** | A logical namespace that groups objects and holds bucket-level policies (ACL, lifecycle, versioning) | ### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Smart Parcel" Model > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy: The Smart Postal Locker** > Imagine a massive, infinitely large warehouse with **billions of identical metal lockers**, each with a unique **barcode number** printed on the door. There are **no aisles, no floor numbers, no section signs** — just lockers and barcodes. > > 1. You walk up to a clerk and hand over a **sealed parcel** (your file). The clerk prints a **barcode label** (Object ID), writes your **return address on the parcel itself** (metadata), and tosses the parcel into *some* locker that happens to be free. The clerk does **not** tell you the locker number — only the barcode. > 2. The next day, you return and say, *"Please give me parcel with barcode 47A9-F2..."* The clerk scans the barcode, the system tells the *robot* which physical locker holds it, the robot retrieves it, and the clerk hands it back. > 3. The barcode is **the only address**. The locker's physical position is irrelevant and may change (the robot may relocate parcels to free up space). > > **Mapping the analogy to storage terms:** > - Parcel → **Object** (data + metadata packaged together) > - Barcode → **Object ID** (flat, opaque, globally unique) > - Warehouse → **Storage Cluster / OSD pool** > - Clerk + Robot → **Metadata Server (MDS) + Object Storage Device (OSD)** > - Locker number (hidden) → **Physical placement / Logical block address** (internal to OSD) > - "No aisles" → **Flat namespace** (no directories, no paths) This is *exactly* how Amazon S3, OpenStack Swift, MinIO, and Ceph RADOS Object Gateway work. The Object ID is the *only* way to reach the object. ### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Reference Constants > [!NOTE] > **Key Engineering Constants for Object IDs:** > - **Object ID size:** 128 bits (UUID v4) or 160 bits (SHA-1) or 256 bits (SHA-256) — depending on the storage implementation > - **Maximum single object size (S3 standard):** **5 TB** (5,497,558,138,880 bytes) > - **Maximum object name length:** **1024 bytes** (UTF-8 encoded) > - **Flat namespace depth:** **Zero** — no nesting inside a bucket, only the **flat key** is used > - **Typical HTTP status for successful `PUT`:** **`201 Created`** > - **Typical HTTP status for successful `GET`:** **`200 OK`** > - **SLA durability (e.g., Amazon S3 Standard):** **99.999999999 %** (commonly called *eleven nines of durability*) — i.e., expected loss of **1 object every 10,000,000,000 objects** over a year ### 1.4 Why Flat Addressing Wins at Petabyte Scale | Property | File System (NFS/CIFS) | Block Storage (iSCSI/FC) | Object Storage | |---|---|---|---| | Namespace | Hierarchical (tree) | None — raw LUNs | **Flat** (bucket + key) | | Metadata | Limited, file-system defined | None | **Rich, user-defined + system** | | Access Protocol | NFS/SMB mount | SCSI over network | **RESTful HTTP/S (PUT/GET/DELETE)** | | Scalability ceiling | ~10⁹ files (metadata bottleneck) | ~1000s of LUNs | **~10¹⁵ objects per cluster** | | Typical use case | User home dirs, OS mounts | Databases, VM disks | Backups, archives, big data, media | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Anatomy of an Object — The Three-Tier Payload Every object stored in an OSS is a **composite, self-describing blob**. It contains three distinct logical layers, bundled together and atomically written or read: ### Tier 1 — The Data Payload (BLOB) - The raw, opaque user bytes (a photo, a video frame, a log archive, a JSON document, a binary executable). - The OSS **does not interpret, parse, or index** this data. It is treated as an opaque byte stream. - Streaming uploads use **chunked transfer encoding** (HTTP `Transfer-Encoding: chunked`) so that the producer need not know the total size in advance. ### Tier 2 — User-Defined Metadata - Key–value pairs supplied by the client at the time of `PUT`, e.g., `x-amz-meta-author: "George"`, `Content-Type: image/jpeg`. - Immutable in most systems once the object is written (a `PUT` with the same key creates a *new version*, not an in-place edit, unless versioning is disabled and overwrites are allowed). - Examples: `author`, `department`, `retention-policy`, `sha256-checksum`, `camera-model`, `geo-coordinates`. ### Tier 3 — System-Generated Metadata (Managed by OSS) - Created and maintained *automatically* by the storage cluster. - Includes: `ETag` (often an MD5 or SHA of the payload), `Last-Modified` timestamp, `Content-Length`, `Storage-Class` (e.g., *Standard*, *Infrequent Access*, *Glacier*), `VersionId`, `Server-Side-Encryption` status, internal placement hints (OSD node, disk, erasure-coding stripe). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why this matters for the KTU exam:** When asked *"Why is object storage called 'self-describing'?"*, the answer is: *every object carries its own metadata, so the system does not need a separate inode table or MFT to interpret it — the object is sufficient unto itself.* ## 2.2 Anatomy of an Object ID (OID) ### 2.2.1 Formal Definition > [!NOTE] > **Object ID (OID)** — A **flat, opaque, globally unique** binary string assigned by the storage system at `PUT` time. It serves as the *only* addressing primitive and is the input key for the distributed hash table (DHT) that maps the OID to a physical Object Storage Device (OSD). ### 2.2.2 Properties an OID **must** satisfy - **Global Uniqueness:** No two objects in the entire cluster (and ideally in the entire universe, to allow federation) may share the same OID. - **Opacity:** The OID is a *meaningless binary blob* to the client. Clients do not parse it; they treat it as an opaque token. - **Immutability (in most designs):** Once an object is written, its OID never changes — even when the object is moved, replicated, or re-erasure-coded. - **Determinism (optional but common):** Many systems compute the OID as `H(bucket, key, versionId)` so that the same logical key always hashes to the same OID, allowing **content-addressable retrieval** and **deduplication**. ### 2.2.3 OID Generation Strategies | Strategy | Formula | Properties | Used By | |---|---|---|---| | **UUID v4** | 122 random bits + 6 version bits | Globally unique, non-deterministic, no dedup | Swift, MinIO | | **SHA-1 / SHA-256 of content** | `OID = SHA-1(payload)` | Deterministic, enables **deduplication**, breaks if two users upload identical files from different accounts (must include namespace salt) | S3 (Etag hint), Content-Addressable Storage (CAS) | | **Composite Hash** | `OID = SHA-1(tenant-id ‖ bucket ‖ key ‖ versionId)` | Deterministic *within* a tenant, allows dedup *within* a namespace, unique globally | OpenStack Swift (uses *ring* hashing) | | **Internal flat index** | Server-generated monotone counter + prefix | Sequential, easy to debug, NOT scalable across federated clusters | Some proprietary systems | ### 2.2.4 SHA Family Quick Reference | Algorithm | Output size | Block size | Rounds | Status (2024) | |---|---|---|---|---| | **MD5** | **128 bits** | 512 bits | 4 | **Broken** (collisions found) — still used for ETag in S3 only for non-multipart objects | | **SHA-1** | **160 bits** | 512 bits | 80 | Deprecated; collision attack demonstrated (SHAttered, 2017) | | **SHA-256** | **256 bits** | 512 bits | 64 | **Recommended** for new systems | ## 2.3 The Object PUT Workflow (Why OIDs Are Flat) The lifecycle of a `PUT` request makes the design rationale clear: 1. **Client** constructs an HTTP `PUT /bucket/myphoto.jpg HTTP/1.1` with the body and headers. 2. **Authentication layer** validates HMAC-SHA256 signature (e.g., AWS Signature V4). 3. **Front-end / proxy node** receives the request, computes (or accepts) the **OID**, and looks up the **placement map**. 4. **Placement algorithm** (consistent hashing) maps `OID → OSD node` and selects an **erasure-coded stripe** or **replica set** across distinct failure domains. 5. **Data is streamed** to the OSDs; each OSD writes to a local journal, then flushes to the B-tree / LSM-tree index keyed by OID. 6. **Metadata service** records `(bucket, key) → OID` plus system metadata in its own DHT (this is the *only* hierarchical index — and it is logically **separate** from the data placement). 7. **Acknowledgement** with `ETag` is returned to the client. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical insight:** The **bucket+key** namespace is hierarchical *to the user*, but the system internally converts it to a flat OID before any data placement occurs. The directory tree is a *user-facing illusion* maintained only by the metadata service. ## 2.4 KTU Formula / Concept Cheat Sheet | Symbol / Term | Definition | Units / Bounds | Notes | |---|---|---|---| | $H(\cdot)$ | Cryptographic hash function (SHA-256) | — | One-way, collision-resistant | | $N$ | Total number of objects in cluster | dimensionless | Up to $\sim 10^{15}$ | | $b$ | Bits in OID | bits | 128 (UUID) or 160 (SHA-1) or 256 (SHA-256) | | $N_{max}$ | Theoretical maximum objects from $b$-bit OID | $2^{b}$ | $2^{128} \approx 3.4 \times 10^{38}$ for UUID | | $P_{collision}$ | Birthday-attack collision probability | $0 \leq P \leq 1$ | $\approx 1.18 \sqrt{N / 2^{b}}$ for $N \ll 2^{b}$ | | $D$ | Durability SLA | nines of 9s | S3 Standard = **11 nines** | | $S_{obj}$ | Object size | bytes | Max 5 TB (S3) | | $R$ | Replication factor (replicas) | integer $\geq 1$ | Common: 3 | | $k, m$ | Erasure-coding parameters | integers | $k$ data, $m$ parity shards; tolerance $= m$ | ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Cloud backup & archive:** Veeam, Rubrik, and Commvault dump deduplicated, content-hashed objects to S3-compatible stores. - **Big data & data lakes:** Hadoop, Spark, and Presto query S3 directly via `s3a://` or `s3://` URI schemes — the OID-based addressing is essential for terabyte-to-petabyte scale-out. - **Static web hosting & CDN origins:** Each image, CSS, JS file is a *separate object* addressable by URL path that maps to an OID. - **Genomics, medical imaging, scientific data:** Petabyte-scale repositories (e.g., 1000 Genomes, NOAA archives) use object stores because flat OIDs allow near-infinite horizontal scaling without inode exhaustion. - **Container registries:** Docker Registry v2 protocol stores each image *layer* as a single object with `sha256:` as its OID — a perfect example of content-addressable object storage. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation — Generating a SHA-256 Object ID from a User Key The most common OID-generation pipeline used in modern object stores is: $$ \text{OID} = \text{SHA256}(\text{tenant\_id} \;\Vert\; \text{bucket} \;\Vert\; \text{key} \;\Vert\; \text{version\_id}) $$ We will derive the *expected uniqueness probability* for a system using this scheme and compare it to the trivial UUID approach. ### 3.1.1 Birthday Bound Derivation for OID Collisions Let $b$ be the number of output bits of the hash, and $N$ be the number of objects already stored. After a new object is inserted, the probability that **at least one** collision exists with any previous object is bounded by the **birthday paradox approximation**. We want to find $P_{\text{collision}}$ given $N$ and $b$. **Step 1 — Number of possible OID values:** $$ M \;=\; 2^{b} $$ For SHA-256: $M = 2^{256} \approx 1.158 \times 10^{77}$. **Step 2 — Probability of *no* collision when storing $N$ objects (assuming uniform independent random hash outputs):** The classic result from probability theory is: $$ P_{\text{no collision}} \;\approx\; \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{N(N-1)}{2M}\right) $$ This comes from Taylor-expanding the exact product $\prod_{i=1}^{N-1}\left(1 - \dfrac{i}{M}\right) \approx e^{-\sum i / M}$ when each $i \ll M$. **Step 3 — Probability of *at least one* collision:** $$ P_{\text{collision}} \;=\; 1 - P_{\text{no collision}} \;\approx\; 1 - \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{N(N-1)}{2M}\right) $$ **Step 4 — Linearisation for $N \ll \sqrt{M}$ (the standard operational regime):** Using the small-argument expansion $1 - e^{-x} \approx x$ for $x \ll 1$: $$ P_{\text{collision}} \;\approx\; \dfrac{N(N-1)}{2M} \;\approx\; \dfrac{N^{2}}{2 \cdot 2^{b}} $$ **Step 5 — Birthday bound (the threshold at which collision becomes likely, $P \approx 0.5$):** Setting $P_{\text{collision}} = 0.5$ and solving for $N$: $$ \dfrac{N^{2}}{2 \cdot 2^{b}} = 0.5 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; N^{2} = 2^{b} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; N_{\text{birthday}} = 2^{b/2} $$ **Step 6 — Concrete numbers for SHA-256 (b = 256):** $$ N_{\text{birthday}} \;=\; 2^{256/2} \;=\; 2^{128} \;\approx\; 3.4 \times 10^{38} $$ **Step 7 — Operational margin for the universe-scale object count:** If the entire cluster ever holds $N = 10^{15}$ objects (a trillion times more than today's largest deployments): $$ P_{\text{collision}} \;\approx\; \dfrac{(10^{15})^{2}}{2 \cdot 2^{256}} \;=\; \dfrac{10^{30}}{2 \cdot 1.158 \times 10^{77}} \;\approx\; 4.3 \times 10^{-48} $$ This is astronomically small — meaning **256-bit OIDs are over-engineered** for any practical cluster. This is why most production systems use **128-bit UUIDs** ($N_{\text{birthday}} = 2^{64} \approx 1.8 \times 10^{19}$, still vastly larger than any real workload). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Exam Tip:** The take-away is that for $b \geq 128$ bits, collisions are *practically impossible*. The hash width is chosen for cryptographic strength, not for collision math. ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparing OID Schemes Against KTU-Mandated Criteria | Criterion (Weight) | UUID v4 | SHA-1 of content | Composite SHA-256 | Counter-based | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Uniqueness** (high) | ★★★★★ (122 random bits) | ★★★★ (depends on input) | ★★★★★ (256 bits) | ★★★ (limited cluster scope) | | **Deduplication support** (high) | ✗ (random) | ★★★★★ (same content → same OID) | ★★★★★ (if namespace salted consistently) | ✗ | | **Predictable placement** (medium) | ★★★ (hashed by placement DHT) | ★★★★★ (content-known) | ★★★★★ | ★★★★★ | | **Privacy / leak resistance** (medium) | ★★★★★ (random) | ✗ (OID reveals content hash) | ★★★ (reveals tenant) | ★★★★★ | | **Cross-cluster federation** (high) | ★★★★★ | ★★★★ (salt must be global) | ★★★★ (salt must be global) | ★ | | **Compute cost per PUT** (low) | ★★★★★ (negligible) | ★★★ (must hash payload) | ★★★ | ★★★★★ | | **Recommended for KTU exam answer** | Yes (modern default) | Yes (content-addressable) | Yes (best of both worlds) | No (legacy / proprietary) | ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Mapping an OID to a Physical Node (Consistent Hashing) Modern object stores (Ceph, DynamoDB-style) place data using a **consistent-hash ring**. Given: - A 256-bit OID: $\text{OID} = 0x9F2A\ldots$ (hex, 64 hex chars) - $K = 8$ storage nodes in the cluster - Each OID and each node is hashed onto a 32-bit ring: $H : \{0,1\}^{*} \rightarrow [0, 2^{32})$ The placement rule is: **walk clockwise from the OID's hash to the next node's hash**. **Step 1 — Hash the OID (using, say, the first 32 bits of SHA-1):** $$ h_{\text{oid}} \;=\; \text{SHA1}(\text{OID})[0..32) \;=\; 0x1A2B3C4D \;=\; 439,041,101 $$ **Step 2 — Hash the node names:** | Node | $h_{\text{node}}$ (decimal) | |---|---| | $N_1$ | $150{,}000{,}000$ | | $N_2$ | $420{,}000{,}000$ | | $N_3$ | $600{,}000{,}000$ | | $N_4$ | $900{,}000{,}000$ | | $N_5$ | $1{,}100{,}000{,}000$ | | $N_6$ | $1{,}500{,}000{,}000$ | | $N_7$ | $2{,}700{,}000{,}000$ | | $N_8$ | $3{,}800{,}000{,}000$ | **Step 3 — Walk clockwise from $h_{\text{oid}} = 439{,}041{,}101$:** The next node hash above $439{,}041{,}101$ is $N_3$ at $600{,}000{,}000$. $$ \text{Placement} :\; \text{OID} \;\mapsto\; N_3 $$ **Step 4 — Replica placement (factor $R = 3$):** Continue clockwise to the next two distinct nodes: $N_4$ and $N_5$. $$ \text{Replicas of OID} = \{N_3,\; N_4,\; N_5\} $$ **Step 5 — Failure-domain isolation:** Each node should be in a distinct **rack, power zone, and availability zone**. $N_3, N_4, N_5$ are checked to lie in different failure domains; if any two share a domain, the replica set is rejected and a re-placement is forced. > [!NOTE] > **Key engineering principle:** A *single* OID lookup on the consistent-hash ring yields **all** replica locations in $O(\log K)$ using a skip-list or B-tree — no name service round-trip is needed for placement, only for ACL/auth. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of an Object Storage System The diagram below shows the full pipeline of a `PUT` (write) and a `GET` (read) operation, with the OID as the central data structure that flows through every component. ```mermaid flowchart TB clientApp[Client Application]:::client authSvc[Authentication Service
HMAC SHA-256 SigV4]:::auth proxy[Front-end Proxy
HTTP REST Endpoint]:::proxy oidrng[OID Generator
SHA-256 or UUIDv4]:::oid mdIdx[Metadata Index
Bucket x Key to OID]:::meta ring[Consistent Hash Ring
OID maps to OSDs]:::ring osd1[OSD Node N3
Disk A Disk B]:::osd osd2[OSD Node N4
Disk A Disk B]:::osd osd3[OSD Node N5
Disk A Disk B]:::osd monitor[Cluster Monitor
Heartbeat and Failure Detection]:::monitor clientApp -- "PUT /bucket/key HTTP/1.1
Headers: Content-Type, x-amz-meta-*" --> authSvc authSvc -- "Validated Request" --> proxy proxy -- "Compute OID" --> oidrng oidrng -- "OID returned" --> proxy proxy -- "Stream payload" --> osd1 proxy -- "Replicate stream" --> osd2 proxy -- "Replicate stream" --> osd3 proxy -- "Record bucket+key to OID" --> mdIdx osd1 -. "Heartbeat" .-> monitor osd2 -. "Heartbeat" .-> monitor osd3 -. "Heartbeat" .-> monitor monitor -. "Membership updates" .-> ring ring -. "Placement hints" .-> proxy clientApp2[Client Application]:::client proxy2[Front-end Proxy]:::proxy mdIdx2[Metadata Index]:::meta ring2[Consistent Hash Ring]:::ring osdRead[OSD Node N3
Read OID at offset 0]:::osd clientApp2 -- "GET /bucket/key HTTP/1.1" --> authSvc authSvc --> proxy2 proxy2 -- "Lookup OID by bucket+key" --> mdIdx2 mdIdx2 -- "OID returned" --> proxy2 proxy2 -- "Hash OID on ring" --> ring2 ring2 -- "Primary OSD is N3" --> proxy2 proxy2 -- "Read request" --> osdRead osdRead -- "Object bytes + ETag" --> clientApp2 classDef client fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0,color:#0D47A1 classDef auth fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,color:#BF360C classDef proxy fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#4A148C,color:#311B92 classDef oid fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#1B5E20 classDef meta fill:#FCE4EC,stroke:#880E4F,color:#880E4F classDef ring fill:#E0F7FA,stroke:#006064,color:#006064 classDef osd fill:#FFF8E1,stroke:#F57F17,color:#E65100 classDef monitor fill:#ECEFF1,stroke:#37474F,color:#263238 ``` ## 4.2 Object Internal Structure — Layered View ```mermaid flowchart TB object[OBJECT]:::outer data[DATA PAYLOAD
Opaque byte stream
Max 5 TB]:::data userMeta[USER METADATA
Key=Value pairs
x-amz-meta-author etc.]:::user sysMeta[SYSTEM METADATA
ETag, Last-Modified,
Content-Length, Storage-Class,
Server-Side-Encryption]:::sys oid[OBJECT ID
Flat, Opaque, 128-256 bits
Example: 9F2A-7B1C-...-E5D2]:::oidlayer object --> data object --> userMeta object --> sysMeta object --> oid classDef outer fill:#FAFAFA,stroke:#212121,color:#000000,stroke-width:2px classDef data fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#B71C1C,color:#B71C1C classDef user fill:#E8EAF6,stroke:#1A237E,color:#1A237E classDef sys fill:#E0F2F1,stroke:#004D40,color:#004D40 classDef oidlayer fill:#FFFDE7,stroke:#F57F17,color:#E65100,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.3 OID-to-OSD Placement via Consistent Hashing ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph ring["Consistent Hash Ring (32-bit space)"] direction LR p1[0]:::tick p2[500M]:::tick p3[1B]:::tick p4[1.5B]:::tick p5[2B]:::tick p6[2.5B]:::tick p7[3B]:::tick p8[3.5B]:::tick p9[4B-1]:::tick end n1((Node N1
hash=150M)):::node n2((Node N2
hash=420M)):::node n3((Node N3
hash=600M)):::nodePri n4((Node N4
hash=900M)):::node n5((Node N5
hash=1.1B)):::nodeRep n6((Node N6
hash=1.5B)):::nodeRep n7((Node N7
hash=2.7B)):::node n8((Node N8
hash=3.8B)):::node oidLoc((OID
hash=439M)):::oidNode oidLoc -. "Walk clockwise" .-> n3 n3 -. "Replica 1" .-> n4 n4 -. "Replica 2" .-> n5 classDef tick fill:#FFFFFF,stroke:#9E9E9E,color:#616161 classDef node fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#0D47A1,color:#0D47A1 classDef nodePri fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#1B5E20,stroke-width:3px classDef nodeRep fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17,color:#E65100 classDef oidNode fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,color:#B71C1C,stroke-width:3px ``` ## 4.4 Logical Flow — OID Lifecycle Across PUT and GET ```mermaid sequenceDiagram autonumber participant C as Client participant A as Auth Layer participant P as Proxy participant G as OID Generator participant M as Metadata Index participant R as Hash Ring participant S as OSD Pool C->>A: PUT /bucket/photo.jpg
+ payload + headers A->>P: Validated request P->>G: Compute OID = SHA256(tenant, bucket, key, version) G-->>P: OID = 9F2A7B1C... P->>R: Lookup OID on ring R-->>P: Primary=N3, Replicas=N4, N5 P->>S: Stream payload to N3 S->>S: Write to local journal, then B-tree P->>M: Persist (bucket, key) -> OID mapping S-->>P: ACK + ETag P-->>C: HTTP 201 Created, ETag Note over C,S: ------- Later: GET ------- C->>A: GET /bucket/photo.jpg A->>P: Validated request P->>M: Lookup (bucket, key) M-->>P: OID = 9F2A7B1C... P->>R: Hash OID R-->>P: Primary=N3 P->>S: GET OID from N3 S-->>P: Object bytes + metadata P-->>C: HTTP 200 OK, payload, ETag ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** *Define an object in an object storage system. List its three logical components and explain why this design is called "self-describing".* **Model Answer (3 marks):** An **object** is the fundamental unit of storage in an object storage system — a self-contained, opaque container holding (i) the **data payload** (the raw user bytes), (ii) **user-defined metadata** (key-value attributes supplied by the client such as author, content-type, custom tags), and (iii) **system-generated metadata** (ETag, last-modified timestamp, storage class, encryption status). The design is called **"self-describing"** because *all* information required to interpret, route, and manage the data travels *with* the data itself — there is no external inode table, file allocation table, or directory hierarchy that the storage server must consult separately. The object is sufficient unto itself. **[3 marks]** ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024 Model]** *What is an Object ID (OID)? State any four properties that a good OID must satisfy.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** An **Object ID (OID)** is a flat, opaque, globally unique binary string assigned to every object at the time of writing. It is the *only* addressing primitive used by the storage system to locate, retrieve, and manage the object. Four required properties: (1) **Global Uniqueness** — no two objects anywhere may share the same OID; (2) **Opacity** — the OID is a meaningless binary blob to the client; (3) **Immutability** — once assigned, the OID never changes for the lifetime of the object; (4) **Determinism (optional but desirable)** — the same logical content must map to the same OID to enable **deduplication** and **content-addressable retrieval**. **[3 marks]** --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) — *Object Anatomy & Metadata Model* **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 2]** **(a)** With a neat diagram, describe the **internal structure of an object** in an object storage system. Differentiate between *user-defined metadata* and *system-generated metadata*, giving **two examples of each**. **[7 marks]** **(b)** Explain in detail the **process of storing (PUT) and retrieving (GET) an object** in a typical object storage system. Highlight the role of the **Object ID** and the **metadata index** in both operations. **[7 marks]** #### Model Solution **(a) Object Internal Structure — 7 marks** | Layer | Content | Examples | Marks | |---|---|---|---| | Data payload | Opaque user bytes (max 5 TB in S3) | A 4 MB JPEG photo, a 200 GB log archive | 2 | | User metadata | Key-value pairs set by client at `PUT` | `x-amz-meta-author: "Anil"`, `x-amz-meta-department: "Finance"`, `Content-Type: video/mp4` | 2.5 | | System metadata | Auto-generated by OSS | `ETag: "d41d8cd98f00b204e9800998ecf8427e"`, `Last-Modified: 2024-09-15T10:30:00Z`, `x-amz-storage-class: STANDARD_IA`, `x-amz-server-side-encryption: AES256` | 2.5 | The diagram from **Section 4.2** above should be reproduced and labeled. Marks are awarded for correctly identifying the three layers and giving valid examples. **(b) PUT and GET Workflow — 7 marks** *PUT (write) flow — 3.5 marks:* 1. Client sends `PUT /bucket/object-key` with body and headers (content-type, user-metadata, ACL). 2. Authentication layer validates HMAC-SHA256 signature. 3. Proxy **computes the OID** (e.g., `OID = SHA-256(tenant, bucket, key, versionId)`) and consults the consistent-hash ring to determine the **primary OSD and replicas**. 4. The data payload is **streamed** to the primary OSD, which writes to its local **journal** and then to the **B-tree index** keyed by OID. 5. Replicas receive the stream in parallel. 6. The **metadata index** records the mapping `(bucket, key) → OID` plus system metadata. The system returns `HTTP 201 Created` with the `ETag`. *GET (read) flow — 3.5 marks:* 1. Client sends `GET /bucket/object-key`. 2. Auth validates the request. 3. Proxy **looks up the OID** in the metadata index using `(bucket, key)`. 4. Proxy **hashes the OID** on the consistent-hash ring to find the primary OSD. 5. Proxy issues an internal `READ OID` RPC to the primary OSD, which locates the offset via its local B-tree and streams the bytes back. 6. Proxy returns `HTTP 200 OK` with the payload, content-type, ETag, and system metadata to the client. **Role of the OID — emphasised throughout.** The OID is the *only* identifier the data plane understands; all other addresses (bucket, key, version) are converted into an OID at the metadata layer before any data movement. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — *Object IDs, Hashing, and Placement* **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 2, Alternative Choice]** **(a)** Explain **four different strategies** for generating Object IDs in an object storage system. Compare them on the basis of **uniqueness, deduplication support, and privacy**. **[7 marks]** **(b)** An object store uses **SHA-256 (b = 256 bits)** to generate OIDs. The cluster currently holds $N = 10^{12}$ objects. **(i)** Compute the probability of an OID collision using the birthday-bound formula. **(ii)** If the cluster grows to $N = 10^{15}$ objects, recompute the collision probability. **(iii)** Comment on whether SHA-256 is *over-engineered* for this use case. **[7 marks]** #### Model Solution **(a) Four OID Generation Strategies — 7 marks** | # | Strategy | Formula | Uniqueness | Dedup | Privacy | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **UUID v4** | 122 random bits | ★★★★★ (cryptographic RNG) | ✗ | ★★★★★ | | 2 | **SHA-1 of content** | `OID = SHA-1(payload)` | ★★★★ (depends on input) | ★★★★★ | ✗ (leaks content) | | 3 | **Composite SHA-256** | `OID = SHA-256(tenant ‖ bucket ‖ key ‖ ver)` | ★★★★★ | ★★★★★ (within tenant) | ★★★ | | 4 | **Monotone counter** | `OID = prefix ++ atomic++_counter` | ★★★ (cluster-scoped) | ✗ | ★★★★★ | *Key insights to include for full marks:* - UUID v4 is the simplest, most privacy-preserving, and federation-friendly, but **deduplication is impossible** because two identical files get different OIDs. - Content hashing enables **massive storage savings** via dedup but **leaks information** (an attacker who knows the OID can verify whether a specific file exists in the cluster). - Composite hashing is the **production-grade compromise** used by Swift, Ceph, and most CDNs. - Counters are fast and debuggable but cannot federate across clusters without coordination. **(b) Collision Probability Calculation — 7 marks** *Formula reminder:* $$ P_{\text{collision}} \;\approx\; \dfrac{N^{2}}{2 \cdot 2^{b}} \quad \text{for } N \ll 2^{b/2} $$ **(i) For $N = 10^{12}$:** $$ P_1 \;=\; \dfrac{(10^{12})^{2}}{2 \cdot 2^{256}} \;=\; \dfrac{10^{24}}{2 \cdot 1.158 \times 10^{77}} \;\approx\; 4.32 \times 10^{-54} $$ **(ii) For $N = 10^{15}$:** $$ P_2 \;=\; \dfrac{(10^{15})^{2}}{2 \cdot 2^{256}} \;=\; \dfrac{10^{30}}{2 \cdot 1.158 \times 10^{77}} \;\approx\; 4.32 \times 10^{-48} $$ **(iii) Comment (3 marks):** Both probabilities are **astronomically smaller than the probability of being struck by lightning ($10^{-7}$) or of a hardware bit-flip on a single disk read ($10^{-15}$)**. The birthday-bound threshold is reached only at $N_{\text{bd}} = 2^{128} \approx 3.4 \times 10^{38}$ — a number so large that the entire visible universe contains fewer atoms ($\sim 10^{80}$). Therefore **SHA-256 is indeed over-engineered** for OID generation; 128-bit UUIDs would provide equally negligible collision risk with **half the storage overhead** per object. The reason SHA-256 (or SHA-1) is still preferred in many systems is **content-addressable deduplication** and **cryptographic integrity verification**, not collision math. --- > [!WARNING] > **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls** > 1. **Do not confuse Object ID with Block Address.** Many students write "OID is the address of the block on the disk." Wrong. The OID is a *logical, content-independent (in UUID case) token*; the *physical* block address is an internal OSD-local detail that changes when the object is migrated. **Lose 2 marks if you conflate them.** > 2. **Do not claim object storage has a directory tree.** The "bucket/key" path is a *user-facing string*; internally, the system converts it to a flat OID. Saying "objects are stored in folders" loses marks. **Lose 1 mark.** > 3. **For collision calculations, always state the birthday-bound formula explicitly.** Writing just the final number without the formula $\frac{N^2}{2 \cdot 2^b}$ loses 1 mark. > 4. **Always mention both uniqueness and determinism** when defining an OID. Skipping either loses 0.5–1 mark. > 5. **In the PUT/GET flow, do not skip the role of the metadata index.** The metadata service is the *only* place that maintains the `(bucket, key) → OID` mapping. Omitting it costs 1.5 marks in part (b) of Question A. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - ✅ **Object Storage** is a **flat-addressing, REST-API-based, horizontally scalable** storage architecture. - ✅ An **object = data + user metadata + system metadata + Object ID**, all bundled into one atomic unit. - ✅ Objects are stored in **buckets (containers)**; the *key* inside a bucket is a flat string, not a path. - ✅ The **Object ID (OID)** is **flat, opaque, globally unique, immutable** — typically 128 bits (UUID) or 160/256 bits (SHA family). - ✅ OIDs are computed by **hashing tenant + bucket + key + version** (composite) or by **random generation** (UUID) or by **hashing the payload** (content-addressable). - ✅ **Object size limits:** up to **5 TB** per object in S3-compatible systems. - ✅ **Durability SLA:** S3 Standard offers **11 nines (99.999999999 %)** durability via cross-AZ replication and erasure coding. - ✅ Access protocol: **HTTP/S REST** with verbs `PUT`, `GET`, `DELETE`, `HEAD`, and `LIST`. - ✅ **Placement** is done via **consistent hashing** of the OID onto a ring of OSDs; the next $R$ distinct nodes clockwise hold the replicas. - ✅ **Birthday-bound collision probability** for a $b$-bit OID with $N$ objects: $P \approx \dfrac{N^2}{2 \cdot 2^{b}}$. - ✅ For **$b = 256$ (SHA-256)**, the collision probability for $N = 10^{15}$ objects is $\approx 4.3 \times 10^{-48}$ — *practically zero*. - ✅ **Content-addressable storage (CAS)** uses `OID = H(payload)` — enables dedup but leaks information about stored content. - ✅ **UUID v4** is preferred when **privacy** and **federation** matter more than dedup. - ✅ **Self-describing** means the object carries everything needed to interpret it — no external inode/MFT is required. - ✅ The **metadata index** is the *only* hierarchical component and is logically *separate* from the data placement layer. - ✅ **Erasure coding** (e.g., Reed-Solomon with $k$ data + $m$ parity shards) is often preferred over pure replication for cost-efficient durability. - ✅ Real-world examples to cite: **Amazon S3, OpenStack Swift, Ceph RGW, MinIO, Azure Blob Storage, Google Cloud Storage**. - ✅ Common exam trap: do NOT call the bucket+key path a "folder hierarchy" — it is a flat namespace presented as a string. - ✅ Common exam trap: OID ≠ physical disk address. OID is logical; physical addresses are OSD-internal. - ✅ **MD5 is broken** for cryptographic collision resistance but is still used by S3's `ETag` (single-part objects only). - ✅ **SHA-1 is deprecated** since the SHAttered attack (Feb 2017) — use SHA-256 or SHA-3 for new designs.

topics.title: Metadata
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST867 - STORAGE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Metadata

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Metadata in Storage Systems

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of computer storage systems, **Metadata** is structured descriptive information that defines, describes, locates, and facilitates the retrieval, management, and administration of actual user data (often called the *payload* or *data payload*). Metadata does not contain the primary content of the information resource; rather, it answers the questions *who, what, when, where, why,* and *how* about the data.

As per the **SNIA (Storage Networking Industry Association)** dictionary and KTU 2024 syllabus terminology, metadata in storage is classified as:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Metadata = "Data about Data"**
> In storage architecture, metadata is the side information stored separately (or alongside) the actual data blocks. It is required by the file system, volume manager, or object store to correctly locate, protect, secure, and reconstruct user data.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a massive **library** with millions of books (the *data*) stacked in random shelves. Without a **card catalog** (the *metadata*), no one could find a book efficiently.

| Library Element | Storage System Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Book Title, Author, ISBN | File name, Owner, Inode number |
| Shelf number, Row, Position | Logical Block Address (LBA), Disk cylinder/sector |
| Dewey Decimal Code | File path, Directory hierarchy |
| Issue date, Due date | Creation timestamp, Modification timestamp |
| Borrower record, Permissions | Access Control List (ACL), POSIX permissions |

Every time you search "Find me the SQL programming book by Joe," you don't scan every shelf — you consult the catalog. Similarly, when an OS opens a file, it consults the **metadata structures (inodes, MFT entries, object headers)** to find the file's blocks on disk without reading the entire disk.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Metadata is typically **far smaller** than the actual data it describes, but it is **far more frequently accessed**. Losing metadata is catastrophic — even if the raw data is intact on disk, it becomes unreachable garbage.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following standard metrics are used while sizing metadata in enterprise storage:

- **4 KB** — typical ext4/XFS inode size for small files
- **128 bytes** — minimum size of a UNIX inode (historically)
- **1 KB / 4 KB** — default MFT (Master File Table) entry size in NTFS
- **1024 B / 1 MiB** — typical directory entry (dirent) record size
- **16 bytes** — common UUID size in object storage
- **64-bit** — standard LBA address width in modern SATA/SAS/NVMe drives

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
