> **Real-world Engineering Utility:** These formulas are used by SAN administrators, storage architects, and DR consultants during solution design. They appear directly in vendor sizing tools (Dell EMC SRDF, NetApp MetroCluster, HPE 3PAR Remote Copy, IBM SVC HyperSwap).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Maximum Distance Between Sites

A frequent KTU problem asks: *"Given a write-latency budget of $L$ ms, what is the maximum distance $D$ between primary and secondary for synchronous replication?"*

We derive it from first principles using the speed of light in optical fibre.

**Step 1:** The total WAN contribution to latency (one-way + return ACK) is approximately:

$$T_{WAN,total} = 2 \cdot T_{one\_way}$$

**Step 2:** Optical fibre introduces a refractive-index slowdown. The signal speed is roughly:

$$v = \frac{c}{n} \quad \text{with} \quad n \approx 1.47 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; v \approx 2 \times 10^{8} \text{ m/s} = 200{,}000 \text{ km/s}$$

**Step 3:** Time to traverse distance $D$ is:

$$T_{one\_way} = \frac{D}{v}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute into the round-trip latency budget:

$$2 \cdot \frac{D}{v} \leq T_{budget}$$

**Step 5:** Solve for maximum distance $D$:

$$D_{max} = \frac{v \cdot T_{budget}}{2} = \frac{200{,}000 \times T_{budget}}{2}$$

**Step 6:** Express $T_{budget}$ in seconds for SI consistency. Example: if $T_{budget} = 1$ ms $= 10^{-3}$ s:

$$D_{max} = \frac{200{,}000 \times 10^{-3}}{2} = \frac{200}{2} = 100 \text{ km}$$

**Interpretation:** Synchronous replication is therefore limited to **metropolitan-area distances** (typically < 100 km) without exotic DWDM dark-fibre setups, hence the marketing term **"Metro Mirror"**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Required WAN Bandwidth

**Step 1:** Let the application generate $W$ write IOPS, each of average size $S$ bytes. The data rate that must be pushed to the replica is:

$$R_{data} = W \times S \;\; \text{(bytes/s)}$$

**Step 2:** Convert to megabytes per second for link sizing:

$$R_{MB/s} = \frac{W \times S}{1024 \times 1024}$$

**Step 3:** Convert to Gigabits per second (Gbps) using the convention $1 \text{ Byte} = 8 \text{ bits}$:

$$R_{Gbps} = \frac{W \times S \times 8}{10^9}$$

**Step 4:** Apply the **Kelley Rule** — provision **30 % headroom** for protocol overhead (TCP, FC framing, replication metadata):

$$BW_{provisioned} = 1.30 \times R_{Gbps}$$

**Step 5:** Numerical worked example:
- $W = 20{,}000$ write IOPS
- $S = 32$ KB $= 32 \times 1024$ Bytes
- $N_{replicas} = 2$

$$R_{data} = 20{,}000 \times 32 \times 1024 = 655{,}360{,}000 \text{ bytes/s}$$

$$R_{MB/s} = \frac{655{,}360{,}000}{1{,}048{,}576} = 625 \text{ MB/s}$$

$$R_{Gbps} = \frac{20{,}000 \times 32 \times 1024 \times 8}{10^9} = 5.24 \text{ Gbps}$$

$$BW_{provisioned} = 1.30 \times 5.24 = 6.82 \text{ Gbps}$$

Therefore a **10 Gbps dedicated dark-fibre or FC-10G link** is the correct minimum provisioning.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Three-Site Synchronous Topology

A "**Sync + Sync Cascaded**" or "**3-Site Fan-Out**" topology protects against simultaneous loss of two sites.

Let:
- $R_{12}$ = link cost/bandwidth between Site-1 and Site-2
- $R_{13}$ = link cost/bandwidth between Site-1 and Site-3

For **fan-out (parallel) 3-site synchronous replication**, every write originating at Site-1 must traverse **both** WANs serially before the host is ACKed:

$$T_{host\_write,3site} = T_{local} + 2 \cdot T_{12} + T_{remote12} + 2 \cdot T_{13} + T_{remote13}$$

For **cascaded 3-site synchronous replication** (Site-1 → Site-2 → Site-3), the latency is dominated by the longer hop plus the intermediate Site-2 cache commit:

$$T_{host\_write,cascade} = T_{local} + 2 \cdot T_{12} + T_{remote2} + T_{12\to3} + T_{remote3}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Bandwidth Requirement (3-site fan-out):**
> $$BW_{fanout} \geq IOPS_{write} \times AvgIO \times 2$$
> Because Site-1 must replicate to *two* independent secondaries in parallel.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Sync vs Async

| Parameter | Synchronous | Asynchronous |
|---|---|---|
| RPO | **0 seconds** | Seconds to minutes (depends on cycle time) |
| RTO | Very low (auto-failover possible) | Higher (manual or scripted) |
| Host Write Latency | High (WAN round-trip added) | Unchanged |
| Max Site Distance | ~ 100 km (Metro) | Unlimited (Continental / Global) |
| WAN Bandwidth | High & dedicated | Bursty / scheduled |
| Use Case | OLTP DBs, banking, ERP | DR, archives, backup vaulting |
| Vendor Examples | SRDF/S, PPRC Sync, MetroCluster, HyperSwap | SRDF/A, SnapMirror Async |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Synchronous Replication — I/O Data Flow

The following Mermaid sequence diagram illustrates the strict handshake between host, primary, and secondary. Note that the host's write call *blocks* until the secondary ACK returns.

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant HOST as Host Application
    participant PRI as Primary Array
    participant NET as WAN Link
    participant SEC as Secondary Array

    HOST->>PRI: 1. SCSI Write I/O issued
    PRI->>PRI: 2. Data staged in local cache
    PRI->>NET: 3. Replication frame sent
    NET->>SEC: 4. Frame traverses fibre/WAN
    SEC->>SEC: 5. Commit to NVRAM / NVMe cache
    SEC->>NET: 6. ACK frame generated
    NET->>PRI: 7. ACK received at primary
    PRI->>HOST: 8. SCSI Good Status returned
    Note over HOST,SEC: Host write() call returns SUCCESS only at step 8
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Three-Site Sync Topology

The diagram below maps the functional blocks of a **cascaded three-site synchronous replication** configuration, including the failover decision matrix.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PrimarySite["PRIMARY DATA CENTER - Site 1"]
        A1[Host Server Cluster]
        A2[Primary Storage Array]
        A3[Replication Engine - Sync Mode]
        A1 -->|SCSI FC iSCSI| A2
        A2 --> A3
    end

    subgraph SecondarySite["SECONDARY DC - Site 2 Metro Mirror"]
        B1[Secondary Storage Array]
        B2[Sync Replication Engine Rcv]
        B3[Auto Failover Manager]
        A3 -->|Dark Fibre 10Gbps| B2
        B2 --> B1
        B1 --> B3
    end

    subgraph TertiarySite["TERTIARY DR - Site 3"]
        C1[Tertiary Storage Array]
        C2[Sync Replication Sink]
        B1 -->|Sync Cascade Link| C2
        C2 --> C1
    end

    classDef primary fill:#e6f3ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef secondary fill:#fff4e6,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000
    classDef tertiary fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000
    class A1,A2,A3 primary
    class B1,B2,B3 secondary
    class C1,C2 tertiary
```

## 4.3 Failure Handling State Machine — Sync Replication

The following state machine shows the failover orchestration when the primary site becomes unreachable.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> NormalOps
    NormalOps --> NormalOps: All writes ACKed by both replicas
    NormalOps --> PrimaryLost: Heartbeat timeout from Site 1
    NormalOps --> LinkDegraded: WAN jitter exceeds SLA

    PrimaryLost --> QuorumCheck: Site 2 requests tie-breaker
    QuorumCheck --> PromoteSecondary: Quorum achieved
    QuorumCheck --> StayReadOnly: Quorum lost

    PromoteSecondary --> NewPrimary: Host LUNs re-pointed
    NewPrimary --> ResyncMode: Pending writes replayed
    ResyncMode --> NormalOps: Original Site 1 returns

    LinkDegraded --> PauseReplication: Break sync link
    PauseReplication --> AsyncMode: Manual downgrade to async
    AsyncMode --> NormalOps: Link restored, full resync
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Define Synchronous Replication. State its RPO and the maximum practical distance between sites.**

**Model Answer:**

Synchronous replication is a data protection mechanism where the host I/O is acknowledged as successful **only after** the data has been written and committed to the cache of **both** the primary and the secondary (replica) storage array. Because every acknowledged write exists at both sites at the moment of commit:

- **RPO = 0 seconds** (zero data loss)
- **Maximum practical distance** $\approx$ **100 km** (metropolitan area) due to WAN round-trip latency, governed by $D_{max} = \frac{v \cdot T_{budget}}{2}$ where $v \approx 2 \times 10^{8}$ m/s.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for RPO, 1 for distance justification)*

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**List any three advantages and two disadvantages of synchronous replication.**

**Model Answer:**

**Advantages:**
1. Zero RPO — no data loss on primary failure.
2. Very low RTO via automatic host-driven failover.
3. Real-time identical copy enables read-offload and disaster testing without impact.

**Disadvantages:**
1. Host write latency increases by WAN round-trip time.
2. Requires dedicated, high-bandwidth, low-jitter WAN/FC links, increasing cost.

*(3 marks: 1 advantage + 1 disadvantage with brief reasoning, 1 additional point)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU Pattern Note:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks with two sub-parts of 7 marks each, offering internal choice. The model solutions below are written in the **valuation key style** expected by KTU examiners.

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**(a)** Explain the step-by-step I/O flow of synchronous replication between a primary and a secondary storage array with the help of a sequence diagram. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An OLTP database generates **15,000 write IOPS** with an average block size of **16 KB** to a LUN protected by synchronous replication to a secondary site 80 km away. Calculate the **minimum provisioned WAN bandwidth** (in Gbps) required, applying a 30 % overhead margin. Assume speed of light in fibre $v = 2 \times 10^8$ m/s. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The synchronous replication I/O flow follows a strict six-stage handshake:

1. **Host Write Initiated:** The application issues a write system call. The OS sends a SCSI Write command (10/16) to the primary LUN.
2. **Local Commit:** The primary array stores the data in its **write-back cache** (NVRAM/NVMe) and simultaneously hands a copy to its in-array replication engine.
3. **WAN Transmission:** The replication engine encapsulates the block in a proprietary or FC frame and transmits it across the dedicated replication link to the secondary array.
4. **Remote Commit:** The secondary array writes the block to its **non-volatile cache**. *Note: it does not need to destage to disk before ACKing.*
5. **ACK Back:** The secondary returns a positive acknowledgement to the primary.
6. **Host ACK:** Only after receiving the remote ACK does the primary send a SCSI Good Status to the host, unblocking the application.

*(7 marks breakdown: 2 marks sequence description, 2 marks ACK semantics, 2 marks diagram-equivalent explanation, 1 mark for emphasising host blocking nature)*

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**
- $W = 15{,}000$ write IOPS
- $S = 16$ KB $= 16 \times 1024 = 16{,}384$ Bytes
- 30 % overhead margin

**Step 1:** Compute raw data rate in bytes/sec:

$$R_{data} = W \times S = 15{,}000 \times 16{,}384 = 245{,}760{,}000 \text{ B/s}$$

**Step 2:** Convert to Gigabits per second:

$$R_{Gbps} = \frac{245{,}760{,}000 \times 8}{10^9} = \frac{1{,}966{,}080{,}000}{10^9} = 1.966 \text{ Gbps}$$

**Step 3:** Apply 30 % overhead (Kelley rule):

$$BW_{provisioned} = 1.30 \times 1.966 = 2.556 \text{ Gbps}$$

**Step 4:** Select nearest standard link size. A **2.5 Gbps or 5 Gbps** link is acceptable; recommended is **5 Gbps** to allow growth headroom.

**Step 5 (Bonus - Distance Check):** Round-trip latency at 80 km:

$$T_{WAN} = \frac{2 \times 80{,}000}{2 \times 10^{8}} = 8 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s} = 0.8 \text{ ms}$$

This is well within typical 1–2 ms sync budgets, so **80 km is feasible**.

*(7 marks: 2 marks for Step 1, 2 for Step 2, 1 for Step 3, 1 for link selection reasoning, 1 for distance justification)*

---

### Question B — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]` *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a)** Compare synchronous and asynchronous replication across **eight** parameters in a tabular format. Discuss why synchronous replication is preferred for OLTP workloads. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A bank maintains two data centres 60 km apart. The primary array processes **40,000 IOPS** of which **30 % are writes**, average I/O size **8 KB**. Compute:
   (i) Required replication bandwidth.
   (ii) Verify whether the distance satisfies the 2 ms latency SLA. Use $v = 2 \times 10^{8}$ m/s. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Comparison Table (8 parameters) — already shown in §3.4.** Discuss using:

| # | Parameter | Sync | Async |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | RPO | 0 | Lag-dependent |
| 2 | RTO | Seconds | Minutes–Hours |
| 3 | Host latency penalty | High | None |
| 4 | Distance limit | ~100 km metro | Unlimited |
| 5 | WAN cost | High (dedicated) | Low (shared) |
| 6 | Application suitability | OLTP, banking | DR, backup |
| 7 | Failure window | Zero data loss | Bounded by replication lag |
| 8 | Vendor examples | SRDF-S, Metro Mirror | SRDF-A, SnapMirror |

**Why Sync for OLTP?**
1. **Atomicity** — database transactions cannot be lost; ACID compliance demands RPO=0.
2. **Regulatory compliance** — RBI, SEBI, PCI-DSS mandate zero data loss for financial ledgers.
3. **Real-time reporting** — secondary copy is always current, enabling live standby reads.

*(7 marks: 4 marks for table, 3 marks for OLTP justification)*

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**
- $IOPS_{total} = 40{,}000$
- Write fraction $= 30\%$
- $S = 8$ KB $= 8 \times 1024 = 8{,}192$ Bytes
- $D = 60$ km
- $v = 2 \times 10^{8}$ m/s
- SLA $= 2$ ms

**(i) Replication Bandwidth:**

**Step 1:** Write IOPS:

$$W = 0.30 \times 40{,}000 = 12{,}000 \text{ IOPS}$$

**Step 2:** Raw data rate:

$$R_{data} = 12{,}000 \times 8{,}192 = 98{,}304{,}000 \text{ B/s}$$

**Step 3:** Convert to Gbps:

$$R_{Gbps} = \frac{98{,}304{,}000 \times 8}{10^9} = 0.786 \text{ Gbps}$$

**Step 4:** With 30 % overhead:

$$BW_{required} = 1.30 \times 0.786 = 1.022 \text{ Gbps}$$

**Recommended link: 2 Gbps** (or 1 Gbps with tight headroom).

**(ii) Distance vs SLA Verification:**

**Step 1:** Compute round-trip propagation delay:

$$T_{WAN} = \frac{2 \times D}{v} = \frac{2 \times 60{,}000}{2 \times 10^{8}} = \frac{120{,}000}{200{,}000{,}000} = 6 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s} = 0.6 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 2:** Compare with SLA:

$$T_{WAN} = 0.6 \text{ ms} \;\;< \;\; SLA = 2 \text{ ms} \;\;\checkmark$$

**Conclusion:** The 60 km distance **comfortably satisfies** the 2 ms SLA with **1.4 ms residual budget** for queueing, processing, and replica cache commit.

*(7 marks: 2 marks for bandwidth steps, 1 for link selection, 2 marks for latency calc, 1 for comparison, 1 for conclusion)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Synchronous Replication Questions:**
> 1. **Writing RPO = "very low"** instead of the exact phrase **"RPO = 0 seconds / zero data loss."** Examiners award full RPO marks only for the precise numerical answer.
> 2. **Forgetting the 2× factor in WAN round-trip** — students often compute only one-way latency in distance/latency problems. Always multiply distance by 2.
> 3. **Confusing NVRAM cache with disk flush** — at the secondary, the ACK happens at **cache commit**, not after disk write. Stating otherwise loses 2 marks.
> 4. **Ignoring protocol overhead** in bandwidth problems — KTU expects the 20–30 % headroom application. Skipping it loses 1 mark.
> 5. **Mixing up sync vs async metrics** — in comparison tables, swapping RPO/RTO values is the most frequent single-error pattern.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Definition:** Synchronous replication = write acknowledged only after primary **AND** secondary both commit the block.
- ✅ **RPO = 0**, RTO = seconds (auto-failover friendly).
- ✅ **Host latency penalty:** $T_{host} = T_{local} + 2T_{WAN} + T_{remote}$.
- ✅ **Maximum distance** $\approx 100$ km (Metro Mirror); governed by speed of light in fibre.
- ✅ **Bandwidth sizing:** $BW \geq 1.3 \times IOPS_{write} \times AvgIO \times 8 / 10^9$ Gbps.
- ✅ **Write amplification:** equals the number of replicas ($N$).
- ✅ **Three-site topologies:** fan-out doubles bandwidth requirement; cascade adds one extra hop.
- ✅ **Use cases:** banking, ERP, OLTP databases, SAP HANA, Oracle Data Guard sync mode.
- ✅ **Vendor names to remember:** EMC SRDF/S, IBM PPRC Sync, NetApp MetroCluster, HPE 3PAR Remote Copy Sync, Hitachi TrueCopy.
- ✅ **ACK happens at cache**, not at disk, at the secondary.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Asynchronous Replication
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST867 - STORAGE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Asynchronous Replication

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Asynchronous Replication** is a data protection and disaster recovery strategy in which write I/O operations issued by a host application are acknowledged as **successful** by the primary storage array *immediately upon local commit*, while the corresponding write data (or its journal/log entry) is transferred to the remote secondary (target) storage system **at a later point in time**, typically in batches or via change-tracking mechanisms.

In strict KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, asynchronous replication belongs to the broader family of *remote copy / remote mirroring topologies* under **Information Lifecycle Management (ILM)** and **Business Continuity** modules of storage architecture. It is formally classified under **periodic replication modes** defined by the **SNIA (Storage Networking Industry Association)** dictionary.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> Asynchronous replication trades **write latency consistency** for **geographic distance flexibility** and **bandwidth efficiency**. The primary site is **NOT** blocked waiting for the remote site to acknowledge the write. This is the central distinction from synchronous replication.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "WhatsApp vs Phone Call" Model

Imagine two friends — *Arjun* (Primary site) and *Maya* (Secondary site) — separated by a long distance.

| Mode | Analogy | Behavior |
|------|---------|----------|
| **Synchronous** | A live **phone call** | Arjun speaks **and** Maya must instantly acknowledge each word before Arjun moves on. If the call drops mid-sentence, both know exactly where they stopped. |
| **Asynchronous** | A **WhatsApp text message** | Arjun types and hits "send". His phone immediately shows a *single grey tick* (locally committed). Later, the message reaches Maya (double blue tick). Arjun doesn't wait. |

The grey-to-blue tick conversion is the **replication lag** (also called the **Δ-replication window**). During this window, the secondary site is "behind" reality. If the primary burns down (disaster), whatever hasn't reached Maya yet is **permanently lost** — this is quantified by the metric **RPO (Recovery Point Objective)**.

## 1.3 Formal Operating Modes Encapsulated

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU Classification of Replication Modes:**
>
> 1. **Synchronous Replication** — write committed at both sites before ACK.
> 2. **Asynchronous Replication** — write committed at primary; remote update happens later.
> 3. **Near-Synchronous (Semi-Sync)** — hybrid; ACK delayed by one network hop.
> 4. **Point-in-Time Replication** — periodic snapshot-based copy (a sub-form of async).

## 1.4 Critical Quantitative Anchors

The following **standard industry metrics** govern asynchronous replication design and MUST be memorized for KTU valuation:

- **RPO (Recovery Point Objective)** — *measured in seconds or minutes* — the maximum tolerable data loss measured in time. For async replication, RPO is **non-zero**.
- **RTO (Recovery Time Objective)** — *measured in seconds to hours* — the time to bring the secondary site online.
- **Δt (Replication Lag / Skew Window)** — *measured in seconds* — the time gap between primary commit and secondary commit.
- **WAN Bandwidth (B)** — *measured in Mbps* — the link capacity used for replication traffic.
- **Change Rate (R)** — *measured in MB/s or IOPS* — the rate at which host data is being modified.

> [!TIP]
> **Valuation Tip:** Examiners expect students to *explicitly state the trade-off* when defining async replication — *lower WAN cost and unlimited distance, in exchange for data loss exposure during the replication window.*

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Sequence — Step-by-Step Logic

The asynchronous replication data path can be decomposed into **six deterministic stages**:

1. **Host I/O Issuance** — Application issues a write I/O (e.g., `WRITE(block X, data Y)`) to the primary storage controller.
2. **Cache Mirror / Write Coalescing at Primary** — The primary writes data into its local cache and may coalesce multiple small writes into larger blocks to optimize downstream replication.
3. **Local ACK Generation** — Once the write is committed to primary cache/mirror, the primary sends an ACK back to the host. **This is the critical async moment** — the host resumes execution.
4. **Change Tracking / Bitmap Update** — The replication engine marks the modified logical block(s) as *dirty* in a change-record bitmap. Modern arrays call this a **Change Tracking Log (CTL)** or **dirty region log**.
5. **Async Transfer over WAN** — A replication agent pushes the dirty blocks (or a journal of changes) to the secondary site. Transfer is **batched**, often governed by policies such as:
   - **Period-based** (e.g., every 30 seconds)
   - **Threshold-based** (e.g., when 100 MB dirty accumulates)
   - **Adaptive / continuous** (as fast as bandwidth permits)
6. **Apply at Secondary & Remote ACK** — Secondary writes to its local cache/disk, optionally sends a quiet ACK back over the WAN (often suppressed in async mode to avoid WAN round-trips).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is it "asynchronous"?** Because steps 3 and 5-6 are decoupled in **time**. The host does not wait. The replication agent operates on a *separate execution thread / scheduling cadence*.

## 2.2 Architectural Variants in Asynchronous Replication

| Variant | Mechanism | Typical Use |
|---------|-----------|-------------|
| **Log-Based Replication** | Shipping WAL (Write Ahead Log) records | Databases (Oracle Data Guard, PostgreSQL streaming) |
| **Block-Level Async Mirroring** | Dirty bitmap + block transfer | Enterprise arrays (NetApp SnapMirror, EMC SRDF/A) |
| **Snapshot-Based Replication** | Periodic snapshot + delta copy | Backup-tier DR (NetApp SnapVault, VMware vSphere replication) |
| **Host-Based Async Replication** | Filesystem journal replication | Cross-site file replication (rsync, Windows DFS-R) |
| **Object-Level Async Replication** | Bucket-level eventual consistency | Cloud (S3 Cross-Region Replication) |

## 2.3 The 'Why' Behind Asynchronous Design

The fundamental engineering motivation is **decoupling performance from distance**. Light speed imposes a theoretical minimum one-way latency:
- **100 km** → ~0.67 ms RTT
- **1,000 km** → ~6.7 ms RTT
- **10,000 km (intercontinental)** → ~67 ms RTT

If every write had to be synchronously committed across continents, **OLTP throughput would collapse** under round-trip latency. Asynchronous replication sacrifices strict consistency (under CAP theorem, it prefers **Availability + Partition tolerance**, accepting **eventual consistency**).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **high-yield** for KTU 2024 Scheme Semester Examination numericals.

| # | Formula / Relationship | Description | Units |
|---|------------------------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $RPO_{\text{async}} \;=\; \Delta t_{\text{lag}}$ | Recovery Point Objective equals the maximum replication lag window | seconds / minutes |
| 2 | $T_{\text{transfer}} \;=\; \dfrac{D}{B}$ | Time to push $D$ MB of dirty data across bandwidth $B$ | seconds |
| 3 | $\Delta t_{\text{lag}} \;\le\; T_{\text{period}} + T_{\text{transfer}} + T_{\text{apply}}$ | Upper bound on replication lag | seconds |
| 4 | $B_{\text{required}} \;=\; \dfrac{R_{\text{change}} \cdot W_{\text{window}}}{T_{\text{available}}} \cdot 8$ | Bandwidth sizing (when $W$ is window in seconds, output in bps) | bps |
| 5 | $D_{\text{max\_loss}} \;=\; R_{\text{change}} \cdot RPO$ | Maximum data loss in MB if disaster strikes during lag | MB |
| 6 | $\eta_{\text{throughput}} \;=\; \dfrac{T_{\text{sync}}}{T_{\text{sync}} + T_{\text{async}}}$ | Comparative penalty avoided (conceptual ratio) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $N_{\text{sites}} \;\le\; 2$ for strict async at host layer | Asynchronous mode allows fan-out to many targets | count |

> [!WARNING]
> **Markdown Pipe Rule Observed:** The vertical pipe character `\vert` has been used inside all formulas to avoid breaking the table grid (per KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 protocol).

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Asynchronous replication is the **backbone of every cloud-scale disaster recovery architecture**. Concrete deployments include:

- **Banking sector** — DR site replication over MPLS, with 15-minute RPO.
- **E-commerce** — Multi-region S3 cross-region replication for static assets.
- **Healthcare (HIPAA)** — Async mirror of EMR databases to comply with regional redundancy laws.
- **Streaming media** — Eventually-consistent metadata replicas across CDN edges.
- **SQL Server Always On** with **asynchronous-commit availability mode** as a textbook example.

## 2.6 Failure-Mode Behavioral Matrix

| Failure Scenario | Synchronous Behavior | Asynchronous Behavior |
|------------------|----------------------|------------------------|
| Primary site failure | **Zero data loss** (RPO = 0) | **Data loss = Δt window** (RPO > 0) |
| WAN link failure | Primary I/O stalls (hangs) | Primary I/O continues, queue builds up, catches up when link restored |
| Secondary site failure | Primary I/O stalls | Primary I/O continues unaffected |
| Distance | Limited (~200 km typical) | Unlimited (intercontinental) |
| WAN Cost | High (must support peak write IOPS in real time) | Moderate (batched, statistical multiplexing) |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Maximum Replication Lag (Δt_lag)

The replication lag is the time difference between the moment a write is acknowledged at the primary site and the moment the same logical block is durably committed at the secondary site.

We define:
- $T_p$ = Time of primary write acknowledgment (host-visible commit).
- $T_s$ = Time of secondary write commit.

$$
\Delta t_{\text{lag}} \;=\; T_s \;-\; T_p
$$

Breaking down $T_s$ into its constituent stages:

$$
T_s \;=\; T_p \;+\; T_{\text{queue}} \;+\; T_{\text{transfer}} \;+\; T_{\text{apply}}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\Delta t_{\text{lag}} \;=\; T_{\text{queue}} \;+\; T_{\text{transfer}} \;+\; T_{\text{apply}}
$$

**Logical interpretation:**
- $T_{\text{queue}}$ — time the dirty block waits in the replication agent's outbound queue (governed by scheduling period or threshold).
- $T_{\text{transfer}}$ — serialization + propagation time across WAN.
- $T_{\text{apply}}$ — time for the secondary to destage cache to its back-end disks.

## 3.2 Derivation II — WAN Bandwidth Sizing

**Problem Statement:** Given a workload with average sustained change rate $R_{\text{change}}$ (in MB/s) and a maximum tolerated replication window $W$ (in seconds), find the minimum WAN bandwidth $B_{\text{required}}$ (in bps) to prevent the lag from growing unbounded.

**Step 1:** Volume of dirty data accumulated during window $W$:

$$
D \;=\; R_{\text{change}} \cdot W \quad \text{[MB]}
$$

**Step 2:** This data must be fully drained within the same window $W$ to sustain steady-state. Therefore, the **required throughput** is:

$$
R_{\text{required}} \;=\; \dfrac{D}{W} \;=\; \dfrac{R_{\text{change}} \cdot W}{W} \;=\; R_{\text{change}} \quad \text{[MB/s]}
$$

**Step 3:** However, in **catch-up scenarios** (after a WAN outage), we need to drain the entire backlog in time $T_{\text{avail}}$. So the bandwidth must support:

$$
R_{\text{required}} \;=\; \dfrac{D_{\text{backlog}}}{T_{\text{avail}}} \quad \text{[MB/s]}
$$

**Step 4:** Convert from MB/s to bps (megabytes to megabits requires a factor of 8):

$$
B_{\text{required}} \;=\; \dfrac{D_{\text{backlog}}}{T_{\text{avail}}} \cdot 8 \quad \text{[Mbps]}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Substitution form most commonly asked in KTU exams:**
> If the daily change volume is $V_{\text{daily}}$ GB, and you want to drain it in an 8-hour replication window:
>
> $$
> B_{\text{required}} \;=\; \dfrac{V_{\text{daily}} \cdot 1024}{8 \cdot 3600} \cdot 8 \quad \text{[Mbps]}
> $$
>
> This simplifies to:
>
> $$
> B_{\text{required}} \;=\; \dfrac{V_{\text{daily}} \cdot 8}{3600} \cdot 1.024 \;\approx\; \dfrac{V_{\text{daily}} \cdot 8}{3600} \quad \text{[Mbps]}
> $$

## 3.3 Derivation III — Maximum Data Loss on Disaster

If the primary site fails catastrophically at instant $T_f$, the data that is *not yet* at the secondary is permanently lost. The maximum loss window is bounded by the steady-state replication lag:

$$
D_{\text{max\_loss}} \;=\; R_{\text{change}} \cdot \Delta t_{\text{lag}} \quad \text{[MB]}
$$

If the lag is bounded by the policy period $T_{\text{period}}$ (e.g., replication runs every 5 minutes), then:

$$
D_{\text{max\_loss}} \;=\; R_{\text{change}} \cdot T_{\text{period}} \quad \text{[MB]}
$$

**Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style):**

> A database generates **240 MB of changed data per minute**. Asynchronous replication is configured with a **batch period of 5 minutes** and a **WAN bandwidth of 50 Mbps**. Compute:
> (a) The maximum data loss in MB if a disaster strikes immediately after a batch transfer.
> (b) The required bandwidth to drain a full 5-minute backlog in 60 seconds during catch-up.

**Solution to (a):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{\text{change}} &= 240 \text{ MB/min} \;=\; 4 \text{ MB/s} \\
T_{\text{period}} &= 5 \text{ min} \;=\; 300 \text{ s} \\
D_{\text{max\_loss}} &= R_{\text{change}} \cdot T_{\text{period}} \\
&= 4 \text{ MB/s} \cdot 300 \text{ s} \\
&= 1200 \text{ MB} \;=\; \mathbf{1.2 \text{ GB}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Solution to (b):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{\text{backlog}} &= R_{\text{change}} \cdot T_{\text{period}} \;=\; 4 \cdot 300 \;=\; 1200 \text{ MB} \\
T_{\text{avail}} &= 60 \text{ s} \\
B_{\text{required}} &= \dfrac{D_{\text{backlog}}}{T_{\text{avail}}} \cdot 8 \\
&= \dfrac{1200}{60} \cdot 8 \\
&= 20 \cdot 8 \\
&= \mathbf{160 \text{ Mbps}}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Performance Bounds

| Parameter | Synchronous | Asynchronous |
|-----------|-------------|--------------|
| RPO | **0** (zero data loss) | $> 0$ (typically seconds–minutes) |
| RTO | Low (seconds–minutes) | Moderate (minutes–hours) |
| Host-visible write latency | $T_{\text{local}} + 2 \cdot RTT_{\text{WAN}}$ | $T_{\text{local}}$ only |
| Max supported WAN distance | ~200 km (latency-sensitive) | Unlimited |
| WAN bandwidth requirement | Peak write IOPS sustained | Average write IOPS batched |
| Application impact | Performance degraded by WAN RTT | No measurable performance impact |
| Failure window exposure | None (atomic) | Open until next replication cycle |

## 3.5 Edge-Case Theoretical Analysis — The CAP Theorem Lens

In the language of **Brewer's CAP Theorem**:

$$
\text{CAP:}\; \text{Consistency} \;+\; \text{Availability} \;+\; \text{Partition Tolerance} \;\le\; 2
$$

Asynchronous replication under WAN conditions prioritizes:

$$
\text{AP} \;=\; \text{Availability} + \text{Partition Tolerance}
$$

It explicitly **sacrifices strong Consistency**, settling for **Eventual Consistency** — the property that *given enough time without new writes, all replicas converge to the same value*. The convergence time is exactly $\Delta t_{\text{lag}}$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Asynchronous Replication Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph HOST["HOST APPLICATION LAYER"]
        APP[App Server issuing Write IO]
    end

    subgraph PRIMARY["PRIMARY SITE DATA CENTER"]
        PRIW[Primary Write Cache]
        PRIC[Primary Cache Mirror RAID5]
        CTL[Change Tracking Log Bitmap]
        RAG[Replication Agent Scheduler]
        QUEUE[Dirty Block Outbound Queue]
    end

    subgraph WAN["WIDE AREA NETWORK"]
        LINK[MPLS or Internet Link]
    end

    subgraph SECONDARY["SECONDARY DR SITE"]
        SECQ[Inbound Receive Buffer]
        SECC[Secondary Cache]
        SECD[Secondary Disk Array]
    end

    APP -- Write IO with data --> PRIW
    PRIW -- Cache Mirror Write --> PRIC
    PRIW -- Immediate ACK returned --> APP
    PRIW -- Mark dirty blocks --> CTL
    RAG -- Reads dirty bitmap --> CTL
    RAG -- Encodes blocks --> QUEUE
    QUEUE -- Batched transfer --> LINK
    LINK -- Propagates frames --> SECQ
    SECQ -- Apply to cache --> SECC
    SECC -- Destage to disk --> SECD
    SECD -- Optional ACK --> LINK
```

## 4.2 Sequence Diagram — Async Write I/O Timing

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant H as Host App
    participant P as Primary Array
    participant R as Replication Agent
    participant W as WAN Link
    participant S as Secondary Array

    H->>P: WRITE Block 1024
    activate P
    P->>P: Stage in Primary Cache
    P-->>H: ACK Write Successful
    deactivate P
    Note over H: Host continues execution immediately

    P->>R: Update Change Tracking Log
    activate R
    R->>R: Accumulate dirty blocks until batch trigger
    R->>W: Push batch payload
    deactivate R
    W-->>S: Forward frames
    activate S
    S->>S: Apply to secondary cache and disks
    S-->>W: Optional Quiet ACK
    deactivate S
    W-->>R: ACK confirmation
    Note over R,S: Replication lag window closes here
```

## 4.3 Decision Flow — Replica Catch-Up vs Steady-State

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> SteadyState
    SteadyState --> DirtyAccumulating: Host writes occurring
    DirtyAccumulating --> BatchThresholdMet: Queue size hits policy limit
    DirtyAccumulating --> TimerTriggered: Replication period elapsed
    BatchThresholdMet --> TransferringToSecondary
    TimerTriggered --> TransferringToSecondary
    TransferringToSecondary --> SteadyState: Batch transferred and applied
    TransferringToSecondary --> WANLinkDown: Network failure detected
    WANLinkDown --> BufferingLocally: Queue persists at primary
    BufferingLocally --> TransferringToSecondary: WAN restored catch up drain
    TransferringToSecondary --> SteadyState
```

## 4.4 RPO versus Distance Trade-off Chart (Functional)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Asynchronous Replication Decision Engine] --> B{Maximum Acceptable RPO Question}
    B -- RPO equals 0 --> C[Use Synchronous Replication]
    B -- RPO between 1 second and 5 minutes --> D[Use Near Synchronous or Fast Async]
    B -- RPO between 5 minutes and 24 hours --> E[Use Standard Asynchronous]
    B -- RPO greater than 24 hours --> F[Use Snapshot Based Periodic Replication]
    C --> G[Limited to under 200 km]
    D --> H[Up to 1000 km typical]
    E --> I[Unlimited distance]
    F --> J[Unlimited distance lowest cost]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Fallback Note:** All four diagrams above use pure alphanumeric node identifiers (`H`, `P`, `R`, `W`, `S`, `A`, etc.) to comply with Mermaid compiler safeguards. Labels are plain uppercase alphanumeric text without markdown formatting characters inside the node text.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Define Asynchronous Replication. How does it differ from Synchronous Replication in terms of host I/O acknowledgment?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Asynchronous Replication is a data protection technique in which the primary storage array acknowledges the host's write I/O **immediately upon local commit**, and propagates the data to the remote secondary site **at a later time** through batched or scheduled transfers.

**Difference in I/O Acknowledgment:**
- In **Synchronous Replication**, the primary waits until the secondary also commits the write before sending ACK to the host. This guarantees **RPO = 0** but adds WAN round-trip latency to every write.
- In **Asynchronous Replication**, the primary sends ACK as soon as the write is committed locally. The remote propagation happens out-of-band. This results in **non-zero RPO** but eliminates WAN latency from the host's critical path.

*(Award: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for sync behavior, 1 mark for async behavior — clear distinction.)*

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: List any THREE advantages and TWO disadvantages of Asynchronous Replication.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Advantages:**
1. **Unlimited geographic distance** — supports intercontinental DR (light speed no longer constrains host latency).
2. **Minimal host performance impact** — WAN RTT does not enter the write critical path.
3. **Lower WAN bandwidth cost** — replication traffic can be batched and scheduled during off-peak hours.

**Disadvantages:**
1. **Non-zero RPO** — data in the replication lag window is lost on primary failure.
2. **Complex consistency tracking** — secondary may serve stale reads unless read-affinity rules are enforced.

*(Award: 0.5 mark per correct point; full 3 marks requires at least 3 advantages + 2 disadvantages clearly stated.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 Scheme pattern: Each Part B question carries 14 marks split as **sub-part (a) = 7 marks** and **sub-part (b) = 7 marks**. Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### ✍️ **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the **architecture and working of Asynchronous Replication** with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of the **Change Tracking Log** and the **Replication Agent**.

**(b)** A manufacturing company has an OLTP workload generating an average of **3 MB/s** of changed data. The asynchronous replication link operates with a **batch period of 10 minutes** and the WAN bandwidth is **20 Mbps**.
Compute:
(i) The **maximum data loss in MB** if the primary site fails immediately after a batch transfer.
(ii) The **minimum WAN bandwidth** required to drain the full 10-minute backlog in 2 minutes during catch-up after a link outage.
(iii) Comment on whether the existing 20 Mbps link is sufficient for catch-up within 2 minutes.

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Architecture and Working (7 marks):**

Asynchronous replication operates on the principle of **decoupled write acknowledgment** and **deferred remote propagation**. The architecture consists of three layers:

**Layer 1 — Primary Site:**
- **Host-facing write cache**: Receives host I/O, coalesces writes, returns ACK on local commit.
- **Change Tracking Log (CTL)**: A bitmap (or journal) maintained in primary non-volatile memory that records which logical blocks have been modified since the last successful replication cycle. Granularity may be 64 KB chunks or larger.
- **Replication Agent**: A background service/thread that scans the CTL, builds a payload of dirty blocks, optionally compresses and deduplicates them, and pushes them onto the outbound WAN queue.
- **Outbound Queue**: Buffers the payload awaiting transfer; governed by policies (period-based, threshold-based, or continuous).

**Layer 2 — WAN:**
- Carries batched replication traffic using protocols such as **FCIP (Fibre Channel over IP)**, **iSCSI**, or vendor-proprietary replication protocols. Often runs over **MPLS VPN** or encrypted internet VPN.

**Layer 3 — Secondary Site:**
- **Receive buffer / inbound cache**: Accepts incoming replication payload.
- **Apply engine**: Writes data into secondary cache and destages to disks.
- Optional **remote ACK** is generated (some implementations suppress this to save WAN round-trips).

**Operational Flow:**
1. Host issues WRITE → Primary cache stages data → ACK returned to host.
2. CTL marks blocks dirty.
3. Replication Agent, based on policy, packages dirty blocks.
4. WAN transfer of batch.
5. Secondary applies; replication lag window closes.

*(Award: 3 marks for diagram, 2 marks for CTL role, 2 marks for replication agent role and operational flow.)*

**(b) Numerical Solutions (7 marks):**

**(i) Maximum Data Loss:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{\text{change}} &= 3 \text{ MB/s} \\
T_{\text{period}} &= 10 \text{ min} = 600 \text{ s} \\
D_{\text{max\_loss}} &= R_{\text{change}} \cdot T_{\text{period}} \\
&= 3 \cdot 600 \\
&= \mathbf{1800 \text{ MB} \;\approx\; 1.76 \text{ GB}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Minimum Catch-up Bandwidth:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{\text{backlog}} &= 1800 \text{ MB} \quad \text{(full 10 min of changes)} \\
T_{\text{avail}} &= 2 \text{ min} = 120 \text{ s} \\
B_{\text{required}} &= \dfrac{D_{\text{backlog}}}{T_{\text{avail}}} \cdot 8 \\
&= \dfrac{1800}{120} \cdot 8 \\
&= 15 \cdot 8 \\
&= \mathbf{120 \text{ Mbps}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Adequacy of Existing Link:**

The existing link is **20 Mbps**, but catch-up requires **120 Mbps**. Therefore the link is **NOT sufficient** to drain the backlog in 2 minutes. The actual drain time would be:

$$
T_{\text{actual\_drain}} \;=\; \dfrac{D_{\text{backlog}} \cdot 8}{B_{\text{available}}} \;=\; \dfrac{1800 \cdot 8}{20} \;=\; 720 \text{ s} \;=\; \mathbf{12 \text{ minutes}}
$$

This means after a WAN outage, the secondary will lag by an additional 12 minutes beyond the steady-state lag, increasing the RPO exposure.

*(Award: 2 marks for part (i), 3 marks for part (ii), 2 marks for part (iii) — including the comparison reasoning.)*

---

### ✍️ **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Compare **Synchronous** and **Asynchronous** replication across the dimensions of **RPO, RTO, WAN bandwidth, geographic distance, and host I/O latency**. Tabulate your answer.

**(b)** Discuss **three real-world deployment scenarios** where Asynchronous Replication is preferred over Synchronous Replication. Justify each scenario with respect to the trade-offs identified in part (a).

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Comparison Table (7 marks):**

| Dimension | Synchronous Replication | Asynchronous Replication |
|-----------|--------------------------|---------------------------|
| **RPO (Data Loss Exposure)** | **Zero** — every acknowledged write is at both sites | **Greater than zero** — equal to replication lag window |
| **RTO (Recovery Time)** | Very low — secondary is always current | Moderate to high — secondary may need to roll forward pending data |
| **WAN Bandwidth Requirement** | Must sustain peak write IOPS in real time (high cost) | Average throughput sufficient (can use statistical multiplexing) |
| **Maximum Geographic Distance** | Limited by latency — typically under **200 km** for FC, slightly more for IP with tuning | **Unlimited** — intercontinental deployments common |
| **Host I/O Latency Impact** | Adds **at least 1 RTT** of WAN latency per write | **No measurable impact** — host sees only local cache commit time |
| **Failure Impact on Primary** | WAN failure stalls primary writes | WAN failure does not stall primary writes (queue accumulates) |
| **Use Case Suitability** | Mission-critical OLTP, short-distance DR | Long-distance DR, cloud replication, bandwidth-constrained WAN |
| **Consistency Model** | Strong consistency | Eventual consistency |

*(Award: 7 marks — 0.875 mark per row × 8 rows; full marks for clear comparative statements on each axis.)*

**(b) Three Real-World Deployment Scenarios (7 marks):**

**Scenario 1 — Intercontinental Banking DR (2.33 marks):**
A multinational bank headquartered in **Mumbai** requires DR at its **Frankfurt** data center (~7,000 km apart). Round-trip latency is ~140 ms. Synchronous replication would inflate every DB write by 140 ms, crippling OLTP throughput. Asynchronous replication with a **5-minute RPO** provides an acceptable business risk for non-transactional datasets (customer profiles, document repositories). Justification: *The trade-off favors availability over strict consistency, and regulatory requirements allow up to 15-minute RPO.*

**Scenario 2 — Cloud Object Storage Replication (2.33 marks):**
An e-commerce platform uses **S3 cross-region replication** between **us-east-1** and **eu-west-1**. Object metadata updates must eventually appear in the secondary region, but the application is designed to handle eventual consistency (read-after-write consistency is not required globally). Justification: *Eventual consistency model aligns with async replication; bandwidth costs are minimized; geographic redundancy is achieved.*

**Scenario 3 — Bandwidth-Constrained Branch Replication (2.34 marks):**
A retail chain with 500 branches replicates point-of-sale transaction logs back to a central data center over **limited MPLS links** (e.g., 5 Mbps). Synchronous replication is impossible due to bandwidth. Asynchronous batched replication during off-peak hours (every 30 minutes) is the only viable option. Justification: *RPO of 30 minutes is acceptable for sales analytics (not for live POS authorization); WAN cost is minimized through scheduled transfers.*

*(Award: 2.33 marks per scenario — 1 mark for scenario description, 1 mark for trade-off identification, 0.33 mark for quantitative/qualitative justification.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Asynchronous Replication Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 8** in bandwidth conversion (MB/s ↔ Mbps). This single error costs **2-3 marks** in numericals.
> 2. **Confusing RPO with RTO** — Examiners frequently set traps where students define RPO as "recovery time". RPO is about **data** (point in time of recoverable state); RTO is about **service downtime**.
> 3. **Stating "zero data loss"** for async replication — this is the *defining feature of synchronous*, and writing this for async guarantees a **1-mark penalty** minimum.
> 4. **Omitting the Change Tracking Log** in diagrams — examiners allocate marks specifically for CTL/bitmap mention.
> 5. **Not commenting on whether the link is "sufficient"** — even if numerical computation is correct, students who skip the qualitative verdict lose the **concluding 1 mark**.
> 6. **Treating bandwidth sizing as instantaneous** — bandwidth must support *catch-up drain*, not just steady-state, in KTU numerics.

---

## 5.4 📝 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Async Replication Definition**: Host is ACKed locally; remote propagation is deferred.
- ✅ **Core Trade-off**: Unlimited distance + low WAN cost ↔ Non-zero RPO (data loss exposure).
- ✅ **Key Metrics**: RPO, RTO, Δt_lag, R_change, B (bandwidth), T_period.
- ✅ **Architecture Components**: Primary cache, **Change Tracking Log (CTL)**, Replication Agent, Outbound Queue, WAN, Secondary Apply Engine.
- ✅ **Bandwidth Formula**: $B_{\text{required}} = \dfrac{D_{\text{backlog}}}{T_{\text{avail}}} \cdot 8$ (in Mbps).
- ✅ **Data Loss Formula**: $D_{\text{max\_loss}} = R_{\text{change}} \cdot T_{\text{period}}$.
- ✅ **Variants**: Log-based, Block-level, Snapshot-based, Host-based, Object-level.
- ✅ **CAP Theorem Fit**: AP system — eventual consistency.
- ✅ **Distinguishing Feature from Sync**: Host I/O latency does **NOT** include WAN RTT.
- ✅ **Real-world tools**: NetApp SnapMirror (async), EMC SRDF/A, Oracle Data Guard (async redo shipping), S3 Cross-Region Replication, SQL Server Asynchronous Commit Mode.
- ✅ **Examiner Always Asks For**: Diagram + numerical + comparison table + scenario justification.
- ✅ **Memory Mnemonic — "ACID vs BASE"**: Async replication aligns with **BASE** (Basically Available, Soft state, Eventual consistency), NOT ACID.

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Exam Quick-Revision Sentence:**
> *"In Asynchronous Replication, the primary acknowledges the write immediately upon local commit, while dirty blocks are tracked via a Change Tracking Log, batched by a Replication Agent, and shipped over the WAN to the secondary site — trading strong consistency for distance flexibility and bandwidth efficiency, with RPO equal to the replication lag window."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Application Layer Replication
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST867 - STORAGE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Application Layer Replication

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 Module 3 — Application Layer Replication

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Application Layer Replication** is a data protection and availability strategy in which the **replication of data objects (files, blocks, records) across multiple, geographically dispersed storage nodes is explicitly orchestrated by the application software itself**, rather than being transparently handled by the operating system, volume manager, or storage array controller.

In this model, the application (or its middleware/data-access layer) is **replica-aware**: it knows *where* the copies are stored, *how many* copies exist, *which* copy to read from, and *how* updates are propagated and reconciled across them. The replication logic — including replica placement, update ordering, conflict resolution, and failure recovery — is embedded inside the application's own code path or its tightly-coupled data store.

### 1.2 Key Characteristics

| Property | Description |
|---|---|
| **Layer of Operation** | User-space application / middleware |
| **Replica Awareness** | Application is fully aware of replica topology |
| **Granularity** | Per-object / per-record / per-file |
| **Typical Distance** | LAN to inter-continental WAN |
| **Consistency Control** | Application-defined (eventual / quorum / strong) |
| **Failure Detection** | Application-injected (heartbeats, gossip) |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Photocopy Courier"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Office Photocopy Courier:** Imagine you are the **boss** (the application) of a critical document (the data object). Instead of trusting the office printer (the storage system) to secretly make backup copies, **you personally hand the document to three different couriers** (replica nodes) and tell each of them: *"Keep this safe. If one of you loses it, the others still have it. If I update the document, I will give you all the new version."* You (the application) track the couriers, decide the order in which they receive the new version, and resolve disputes if two couriers have different versions. **That is application-layer replication in plain English.**

In contrast:
- **Storage-layer replication** would be like a photocopier that auto-duplicates the document as soon as it is printed (transparent, but inflexible).
- **OS-layer replication** would be the office admin silently making copies and filing them (semi-transparent).

The application taking direct control gives **maximum flexibility** at the cost of **maximum application complexity**.

### 1.4 Why It Exists

Modern distributed systems (web-scale databases, cloud object stores, content delivery networks) cannot rely on a single, perfectly reliable storage subsystem. Application-layer replication is the dominant strategy for:
1. **High Availability (HA)** — survive node/rack/datacenter failures.
2. **Geo-Distribution** — place data near users for low latency.
3. **Disaster Recovery (DR)** — copies exist in failure-independent sites.
4. **Scalability** — read traffic served from multiple replicas.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** This topic directly maps to the storage-systems theme of *replication for availability* and the trade-offs between *consistency, availability, and partition tolerance* (the CAP theorem). Expect a 7-mark or 14-mark question.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Replica-Placement Problem

Once the application decides to replicate, the very first sub-problem is **where** to place the copies. Three canonical strategies are taught in the KTU syllabus:

### 2.1.1 Centralized (Master–Replica)
- **One distinguished node (master)** receives **all writes**.
- It propagates updates to **N − 1 secondary replicas** in a defined order.
- Reads can be served by master (strong consistency) or by secondaries (eventual consistency / stale reads).
- **Used in:** Traditional RDBMS replication, MySQL master–slave, MongoDB primary–secondary.

### 2.1.2 Decentralized (Peer-to-Peer / Symmetric)
- **All replicas are equal** — any node can accept a write.
- Writes are propagated to a quorum of replicas; conflicts are resolved by version vectors or last-writer-wins.
- **Used in:** Amazon Dynamo, Cassandra (configurable), Riak.

### 2.1.3 Chained (Pipeline)
- Replicas are arranged in a **chain**; update flows sequentially: $R_1 \to R_2 \to \dots \to R_N$.
- Simple, low write-concurrency; not fault-tolerant to mid-chain failures.
- **Used in:** Early Coda file system, some log-shipping systems.

> [!NOTE]
> **Trade-off:** Centralized → strong consistency, low write throughput. Decentralized → high availability, weaker consistency. Chained → ordered delivery, fragile.

## 2.2 Update Propagation Models

| Model | Mechanism | Latency | Consistency Strength |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Synchronous** | Master waits for all replicas to acknowledge before commit | High | Strong (linearizable) |
| **Asynchronous** | Master commits locally, propagates later | Low | Eventual |
| **Read-after-Write** | Master synchronously writes one replica, others async | Medium | Bounded staleness |
| **Quorum-based** | Write succeeds on $W$ of $N$ replicas; read on $R$ of $N$ | Tunable | Tunable by $W + R > N$ |

## 2.3 The Replica-Consistency Equation (KTU High-Yield)

For a replicated system with **N** total replicas, the consistency of a read depends on the overlap between the **read quorum (R)** and the **write quorum (W)**:

$$W + R > N \quad \text{(strict — guarantees strong consistency)}$$

$$W + R \le N \quad \text{(overlap not guaranteed — eventual consistency)}$$

Where:
- $N$ = total number of replicas maintained by the application.
- $W$ = number of replicas that must acknowledge a write.
- $R$ = number of replicas contacted for a read.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The strict-inequality** $W + R > N$ guarantees that **any read quorum and the latest write quorum share at least one common replica**, ensuring the read sees the most recent write. This is the **N, R, W** model made famous by Amazon Dynamo and is **the single most testable formula** in this module.

## 2.4 Availability vs. Consistency — CAP Theorem

Eric Brewer's **CAP Theorem** states that in a distributed data store, only **two of the following three** guarantees can be simultaneously satisfied in the presence of a network partition:

$$C \cap A \cap P = \varnothing$$

| Guarantee | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **C — Consistency** | All clients see the same data at the same time (linearizability) |
| **A — Availability** | Every request receives a (non-error) response |
| **P — Partition Tolerance** | System continues to operate despite arbitrary network message loss |

Application-layer replication gives the application **direct control** over the C/A trade-off by tuning $N$, $W$, $R$.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Expression | Meaning | Typical Value / Constraint |
|---|---|---|
| $N$ | Total number of replicas | $3 \le N \le 5$ common |
| $W$ | Write quorum size | $1 \le W \le N$ |
| $R$ | Read quorum size | $1 \le R \le N$ |
| $W + R > N$ | Strong consistency rule | Required for linearizability |
| $W + R \le N$ | Eventual consistency rule | Higher availability |
| $R = 1, W = N$ | Read-optimized, write-slow | Master–slave flavour |
| $R = N, W = 1$ | Write-optimized, read-slow | Quorum-write rare |
| $R = W = \lceil (N+1)/2 \rceil$ | Balanced quorum | Majority quorum |
| $MTTR$ | Mean Time To Repair (a failed replica) | Drives recovery SLA |
| $RPO$ | Recovery Point Objective — max data loss | $0$ for sync, $>0$ for async |
| $RTO$ | Recovery Time Objective — downtime budget | Drives replica placement |

> [!NOTE]
> Do **not** confuse $N$ (replicas) with $R$ (read quorum). The RPO/RTO pair is the most common place where students lose marks — remember $RPO$ is about **data**, $RTO$ is about **time**.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Cloud object stores (AWS S3, Azure Blob)** replicate objects across availability zones using application-level metadata-driven replication.
- **NoSQL databases (Cassandra, DynamoDB)** push replication fully into the application/data-access layer.
- **CDNs (Akamai, Cloudflare)** treat the edge cache as an application-managed replica with TTL-based eventual consistency.
- **Distributed file systems (Ceph, HDFS)** offer application-tunable replication factors and block placement policies.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **why** behind application-layer replication: it is the **only layer** that has semantic knowledge of the data (e.g., a banking application can resolve "transfer $100" conflicts intelligently, while a block-level replicator cannot).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why $W + R > N$ Guarantees Read-Your-Write Consistency

**Given:**
- $N$ total replicas in the system.
- Every successful write is committed to exactly $W$ replicas.
- Every successful read is fetched from exactly $R$ replicas and the **latest version** (by vector clock or timestamp) is returned.

**To Prove:** A client that just performed a write and then performs a read will see its own write.

**Step 1 — Set up the write set.** After a successful write, the $W$ replicas that acknowledged the write form a set $\mathcal{W}$ with $\vert \mathcal{W} \vert = W$. The new version $v_{\text{new}}$ is stored on every replica in $\mathcal{W}$.

**Step 2 — Set up the read set.** A subsequent read picks $R$ replicas to contact. Call this set $\mathcal{R}$ with $\vert \mathcal{R} \vert = R$.

**Step 3 — Find the worst-case intersection.** The smallest possible overlap between $\mathcal{W}$ and $\mathcal{R}$ occurs when they share as few elements as possible. The minimum overlap is:

$$\min \vert \mathcal{W} \cap \mathcal{R} \vert = W + R - N$$

This follows from the **inclusion–exclusion principle**:

$$ \begin{aligned} \vert \mathcal{W} \cup \mathcal{R} \vert &= \vert \mathcal{W} \vert + \vert \mathcal{R} \vert - \vert \mathcal{W} \cap \mathcal{R} \vert \\ \text{Since } \vert \mathcal{W} \cup \mathcal{R} \vert &\le N \quad \text{(union cannot exceed the total population)} \\ \text{Therefore } \vert \mathcal{W} \cap \mathcal{R} \vert &\ge W + R - N \end{aligned} $$

**Step 4 — Apply the consistency condition.** For the read to definitely see $v_{\text{new}}$, we need the intersection to contain at least one replica that holds $v_{\text{new}}$. Hence we require:

$$W + R - N \ge 1 \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad W + R > N$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion.** When $W + R > N$, every read quorum is guaranteed to include at least one replica that was part of the latest write quorum. The application can therefore trust that the returned **latest version** is the one it just wrote. This is the foundation of **quorum-based strong consistency** in application-layer replication.

> [!NOTE]
> **Symmetry observation:** The same derivation applies in reverse for write–write conflicts and read–read consistency. The inequality $W + R > N$ is the **single boundary** separating strong-consistency regimes from eventual-consistency regimes.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style 7-Mark Style)

**Problem.** A distributed key-value store uses application-layer replication with $N = 5$ replicas. The application is configured with $W = 3$ and $R = 3$.

**(a)** Determine whether the system provides strong consistency.
**(b)** If the application relaxes to $W = 2$ and $R = 2$, what is the minimum overlap between read and write quorums?
**(c)** Suggest a configuration that maximises read availability while preserving strong consistency.

**Solution.**

**Part (a):** Evaluate $W + R$ vs. $N$.

$$ \begin{aligned} W + R &= 3 + 3 = 6 \\ N &= 5 \\ W + R &> N \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 6 > 5 \quad \text{(TRUE)} \end{aligned} $$

Hence, the configuration provides **strong consistency** (any read overlaps the latest write).

**Part (b):** With $W = 2$, $R = 2$:

$$ \begin{aligned} \min \vert \mathcal{W} \cap \mathcal{R} \vert &= W + R - N \\ &= 2 + 2 - 5 \\ &= -1 \end{aligned} $$

A negative result means **no guaranteed overlap** — the system is in the **eventual-consistency** regime.

**Part (c):** To maximise read availability we want $R$ to be as small as possible, i.e. $R = 1$. From $W + R > N$ we get $W > N - 1 = 4$, so $W = N = 5$. The configuration $\boxed{R = 1,\; W = 5,\; N = 5}$ gives maximum read availability at the cost of slowest writes.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Replication Strategies

| Dimension | Storage-Layer | OS / File-System-Layer | **Application-Layer** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Visibility of replicas to app | Hidden | Hidden | **Explicit** |
| Granularity of replication | LUN / Volume | File / Block | **Object / Record / Tuple** |
| Placement control | Array-internal | Volume manager | **Application policy** |
| Consistency control | Vendor-defined | Filesystem-defined | **App-defined (flexible)** |
| Conflict resolution | Not applicable | Limited | **App-level semantics** |
| Performance overhead | Low | Medium | **Higher (in app path)** |
| Cross-site / WAN | Limited | Limited | **Native** |
| Best suited for | Single-DC HA | OS-level backup | **Geo-distributed, web-scale** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Analytical Matrix** above is the most synthesis-heavy piece in this module and is the kind of 14-mark answer KTU examiners reward.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Architecture of Application-Layer Replication

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[Client Application A] --> AL[Application Process]
    A2[Client Application B] --> AL
    A3[Client Application C] --> AL
    AL --> RL[Replication Logic Engine]
    RL -->|Write to W replicas| R1[Replica Node R1]
    RL -->|Write to W replicas| R2[Replica Node R2]
    RL -->|Write to W replicas| R3[Replica Node R3]
    RL -->|Write to W replicas| R4[Replica Node R4]
    RL -->|Write to W replicas| R5[Replica Node R5]
    R1 -.->|Gossip / Heartbeat| R2
    R2 -.->|Gossip / Heartbeat| R3
    R3 -.->|Gossip / Heartbeat| R4
    R4 -.->|Gossip / Heartbeat| R5
    RL --> FM[Failure Manager]
    FM -->|Detect + Repair| R1
    FM -->|Detect + Repair| R2
    FM -->|Detect + Repair| R3
    FM -->|Detect + Repair| R4
    FM -->|Detect + Repair| R5
```

**Block-Level Interpretation:**

| Block | Role |
|---|---|
| `Client Applications` | Issue data operations |
| `Application Process` | The application binary — owner of replication logic |
| `Replication Logic Engine` | Decides W, R, N, replica selection, conflict resolution |
| `Replica Nodes` (R1…R5) | Storage endpoints holding independent copies |
| `Gossip / Heartbeat` | Peer-to-peer failure detection and version exchange |
| `Failure Manager` | Triggers re-replication when a replica is lost |

## 4.2 Quorum Overlap Schematic ($N = 5$, $W = 3$, $R = 3$)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph "Write Quorum W=3"
        W1[Replica R1 - new v3]
        W2[Replica R2 - new v3]
        W3[Replica R3 - new v3]
    end
    subgraph "Read Quorum R=3"
        R3b[Replica R3 - new v3]
        R4[Replica R4 - old v2]
        R5[Replica R5 - old v2]
    end
    W3 -.->|Common Replica Guarantees Freshness| R3b
```

The diagram shows that with $W = 3, R = 3, N = 5$, the read quorum $\{R3, R4, R5\}$ and the write quorum $\{R1, R2, R3\}$ share **at least** $W + R - N = 1$ replica (R3), so the read **must** observe the latest version.

## 4.3 Update-Propagation State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> ClientWrite: Write Request Arrives
    ClientWrite --> CoordinatorPicksW: Choose W of N Replicas
    CoordinatorPicksW --> AwaitingAcks: Send Write To W
    AwaitingAcks --> Committed: All W Acknowledge
    AwaitingAcks --> Aborted: Timeout Or W Failure
    Committed --> Propagating: Async Update To Remaining N-W
    Propagating --> Idle: Background Gossip Done
    Aborted --> Idle: Error Returned
    Idle --> ClientRead: Read Request Arrives
    ClientRead --> CoordinatorPicksR: Choose R of N Replicas
    CoordinatorPicksR --> Reconciling: Collect R Responses
    Reconciling --> ReturnLatest: Pick Highest Version Or Vector Clock Winner
    ReturnLatest --> Idle
```

This finite-state view captures the **synchronous-write + asynchronous-propagate** lifecycle that is the most common implementation pattern for application-layer replication (used in Dynamo, Cassandra, and most eventually-consistent NoSQL stores).

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define application-layer replication. How does it differ from storage-level replication?**

**Model Answer (≈ 3 marks):**
Application-layer replication is a strategy in which the application software itself controls the creation, placement, synchronization, and recovery of data replicas across multiple storage nodes. The application is fully aware of the replica topology and embeds replication logic in its data-access path.
**Difference from storage-level replication:** In storage-level replication, the storage array or volume manager handles replication transparently below the OS, with limited granularity (LUN/volume) and limited cross-site flexibility. Application-layer replication, in contrast, operates at finer granularities (object, record, file), supports WAN/geo-distribution natively, and allows the application to enforce application-specific consistency and conflict-resolution policies, at the cost of higher complexity in the application code.

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**State the CAP theorem. Which two properties does application-layer replication typically prioritise in cloud NoSQL systems, and why?**

**Model Answer (≈ 3 marks):**
The CAP theorem states that a distributed data store can simultaneously provide at most two of the three guarantees: **Consistency (C)**, **Availability (A)**, and **Partition Tolerance (P)**.
**Application-layer replication in cloud NoSQL systems** (e.g., DynamoDB, Cassandra) typically prioritises **A and P** (availability and partition tolerance), accepting **eventual consistency**. This is because geo-distributed deployments inherently face network partitions, and user-facing applications demand that every request receive a non-error response. Strong consistency is sacrificed in favour of continuous service, and conflicts are resolved asynchronously using vector clocks or last-writer-wins policies embedded in the application.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the three replica-placement strategies used in application-layer replication. Compare their advantages and disadvantages. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A replicated key-value store maintains $N = 5$ replicas. The application uses $W = 3$ and $R = 2$.
    (i) Does the configuration guarantee strong consistency? Justify with the quorum condition. **(3 marks)**
    (ii) Compute the minimum guaranteed overlap between a read quorum and the latest write quorum. **(2 marks)**
    (iii) Suggest a configuration that would provide strong consistency while minimising write latency. **(2 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — Replica Placement Strategies (7 marks):**

1. **Centralized (Master–Replica / Primary–Secondary):** A single master node accepts all writes; updates are propagated to $N-1$ secondary replicas. *Advantage:* simple, strong consistency, clear ordering. *Disadvantage:* master is a bottleneck and a single point of failure (mitigated by failover).

2. **Decentralized (Peer-to-Peer / Symmetric):** All replicas are equal; any node can serve writes. Conflicts resolved by version vectors or last-writer-wins. *Advantage:* high availability, no single point of failure, scales writes. *Disadvantage:* conflict resolution is complex, weaker consistency.

3. **Chained (Pipeline):** Replicas arranged in a sequence; updates flow $R_1 \to R_2 \to \dots \to R_N$. *Advantage:* ordered, simple. *Disadvantage:* a mid-chain failure stalls propagation; low fault tolerance.

**Part (b) — Quorum Calculation (7 marks):**

**(i) Strong consistency check (3 marks):**

$$W + R = 3 + 2 = 5, \quad N = 5 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; W + R \le N$$

Since the strict inequality $W + R > N$ is **not** satisfied, the configuration does **not** guarantee strong consistency. It lies in the **eventual-consistency** regime.

**(ii) Minimum overlap (2 marks):**

$$\min \vert \mathcal{W} \cap \mathcal{R} \vert = W + R - N = 3 + 2 - 5 = 0$$

The minimum overlap is **zero** — a read may not see the latest write.

**(iii) Suggested configuration (2 marks):** To preserve strong consistency and minimise write latency, pick the smallest $W$ that still satisfies $W + R > N$ and the largest $R$ that still satisfies it. Choosing $W = 3, R = 3$ gives $W + R = 6 > 5$ ✓ with a **majority quorum** — balanced. If write latency must be the absolute minimum, choose $W = 2$ and force $R \ge 4$ (e.g., $R = 4, W = 2, N = 5$: $2 + 4 = 6 > 5$ ✓, with $W = 2$ giving faster writes).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students often write "$W + R \ge N$" instead of the strict "$>$" form. **Always use strict inequality** for strong consistency. The boundary case $W + R = N$ is a **degenerate** case that gives only *one* common replica in the best case — borderline, not robust.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Describe the working of application-layer replication with a neat architecture diagram. Discuss the role of the replication logic engine. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Differentiate between synchronous, asynchronous, and quorum-based update propagation in application-layer replication. For each, state the effect on **RPO** (Recovery Point Objective) and **write latency**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — Architecture and Replication Engine (7 marks):**

In application-layer replication, the application intercepts each data operation and invokes an in-process **Replication Logic Engine (RLE)**. The RLE performs four sub-tasks:

1. **Replica Selection:** Picks which $W$ of $N$ replicas receive a write, typically using consistent hashing or a placement policy.
2. **Write Coordination:** Sends the write to selected replicas and waits for $W$ acknowledgements.
3. **Read Coordination:** Picks $R$ replicas, fetches values + version metadata, and returns the latest.
4. **Failure Management:** Detects failed replicas via gossip/heartbeats, re-replicates data to maintain the configured $N$, and triggers conflict resolution when needed.

*(Architecture diagram from Section 4.1 should be drawn in the exam — labelled block diagram showing Client → App → RLE → 5 Replicas with gossip links and a Failure Manager.)*

**Part (b) — Propagation Models (7 marks):**

| Model | Mechanism | Write Latency | RPO |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Synchronous** | Wait for all $N$ replicas to ack | Highest | **0** (no data loss) |
| **Asynchronous** | Commit on 1 local replica, propagate later | Lowest | **> 0** (data loss possible on master failure before propagation) |
| **Quorum-based** | Wait for $W$ of $N$ acks; $W + R > N$ for strong consistency | Tunable | **0 to small** (depends on $W$) |

- **Synchronous:** Strongest durability, worst latency. Used in banking, financial ledgers.
- **Asynchronous:** Fastest writes, risk of data loss on failure. Used in social-media timelines, log analytics.
- **Quorum-based:** Sweet spot — tunable latency and durability by varying $W$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (Examiner's Notes):**
> 1. **Confusing $R$ (read quorum) with $N$ (total replicas).** $R$ is the *count of replicas consulted on a read*, not the total.
> 2. **Writing the strict inequality loosely** as "$W + R \ge N$" — loses a mark in 7-mark questions.
> 3. **Stating "CAP lets us have all three"** — false. CAP is a *pick two* theorem.
> 4. **Mixing up RPO and RTO** — RPO = data loss budget (in time/data units), RTO = downtime budget.
> 5. **Forgetting the gossip/heartbeat** mechanism when describing decentralised placement — examiners expect failure detection to be mentioned.
> 6. **Not labelling the diagram** with N, W, R values when the question supplies them — partial mark lost.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Application-layer replication** = application itself controls replica creation, placement, sync, and recovery.
- ✅ The application is **replica-aware**; the storage subsystem is unaware of the replication topology.
- ✅ Three placement strategies: **Centralized (master–slave), Decentralized (peer-to-peer), Chained (pipeline).**
- ✅ Three propagation models: **Synchronous, Asynchronous, Quorum-based.**
- ✅ **Key formula (the one examiners love):** $W + R > N \Rightarrow$ strong consistency; $W + R \le N \Rightarrow$ eventual consistency.
- ✅ **CAP theorem:** pick two of {C, A, P}; cloud NoSQL picks **AP** and offers eventual consistency.
- ✅ **Conflict resolution** is an application responsibility — done via vector clocks, last-writer-wins, or semantic merging.
- ✅ **Failure detection** uses gossip protocols and heartbeats; failed replicas are re-replicated to maintain $N$.
- ✅ **RPO** = Recovery Point Objective (data loss tolerance); **RTO** = Recovery Time Objective (downtime tolerance).
- ✅ **Real-world systems** using this model: Amazon Dynamo, Apache Cassandra, Riak, CouchDB, HDFS (configurable).
- ✅ Compared to storage/OS-layer replication: more flexible, more complex, WAN-native, finer granularity.
- ✅ Tunable trade-off: raising $W$ → stronger durability, higher write latency. Raising $R$ → fresher reads, higher read latency.
- ✅ Symmetric balanced quorum: $R = W = \lceil (N+1)/2 \rceil$ — the standard "majority quorum" default.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Logical Volume Manager-Based Replication
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST867 - STORAGE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Logical Volume Manager-Based Replication

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Logical Volume Manager (LVM)-Based Replication** is a host-level, software-defined data protection mechanism that leverages the Logical Volume Manager subsystem of an operating system (originally native to UNIX/Linux) to maintain one or more synchronized copies (mirrors) of a logical volume across distinct physical storage devices. It abstracts physical block devices into a hierarchy of **Physical Volumes (PV) → Volume Groups (VG) → Logical Volumes (LV)**, and uses a built-in mirroring engine to write every block twice — once to a primary leg and once to a secondary leg — thereby enabling high availability, disaster recovery, and online data migration without dependence on a specialized external storage array.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** LVM-based replication is classified under *Host-Based Replication* in the KTU 2024 scheme. It is fundamentally different from array-based replication because the replication engine resides **inside the host OS kernel (device-mapper layer)**, not inside a storage array controller.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine you are an executive dictating an important letter to two secretaries simultaneously. The **first secretary** (primary leg) writes the letter in real-time as you speak. The **second secretary** (mirror leg) listens and writes the same letter in parallel. If the first secretary's notebook is later destroyed (disk failure), the second secretary's identical copy is promoted to "official record" status with zero data loss.

In this analogy:
- The **executive** is the application generating I/O.
- The **secretaries** are the two physical disks (PVs) belonging to the mirror LV.
- The **synchronization rule** is the *mirror log* — a tiny ledger that records which blocks have been copied to keep both copies perfectly aligned.
- **Promoting the second secretary** is the `lvconvert --mirrorlog` or `vgreduce` recovery operation that brings the mirror online as the new primary.

### 1.3 Key Terminology

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **PV (Physical Volume)** | Raw block device initialized with an LVM header (e.g., `/dev/sdb`, `/dev/sdc`). |
| **VG (Volume Group)** | A pool of capacity aggregating one or more PVs. |
| **LV (Logical Volume)** | A virtual block device carved out of a VG, presented to the OS as `/dev/vg00/lvol0`. |
| **Mirror LV** | An LV that maintains N identical copies (legs) across distinct PVs. |
| **Mirror Log** | A metadata region that tracks synchronization state between mirror legs. |
| **Leg / Mirror Image** | A single physical copy within a mirror LV. |
| **Sync vs. Async Mirror** | Synchronous: write completes only after both legs ACK. Asynchronous: write ACKs after primary write; mirror leg is updated later. |
| **CLVM** | Clustered LVM — extension of LVM that allows shared VGs across multiple hosts (used for active-active replication). |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric:** LVM mirror write penalty = **2x** for a 2-way mirror (one write to primary, one to secondary, plus a small log write). The standard RPO (Recovery Point Objective) for synchronous LVM mirrors is **0 seconds** (zero data loss). Standard capacity efficiency = **50%** for a 1:1 mirror.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three-Tier LVM Hierarchy (Why and How)

**Why three tiers?** Because applications need logical, resizable, named devices, while disks are physical, fixed-size, and identified by controller paths. LVM acts as the *translation layer* between these two worlds.

- **Tier 1 – Physical Layer (Disks → PVs):**
  - *Why:* The OS must first "claim" a raw disk before it can be used.
  - *How:* The `pvcreate /dev/sdX` command writes an LVM metadata header (label) at the start of the disk, marking it as an LVM member.
- **Tier 2 – Aggregation Layer (PVs → VG):**
  - *Why:* A single disk is rarely large enough; VGs allow capacity to be pooled across many disks for flexible allocation.
