  - *How:* `vgcreate vg_data /dev/sdb /dev/sdc /dev/sdd` concatenates the PVs into a single addressable space divided into **Physical Extents (PE)** of fixed size (default **4 MiB**).
- **Tier 3 – Logical Layer (VG → LVs):**
  - *Why:* Applications, filesystems, and the OS kernel need a virtual block device with a stable name, regardless of underlying disk reshuffling.
  - *How:* `lvcreate -L 10G -n lvol0 vg_data` carves out a logical extent (LE) range from the VG and presents it as `/dev/vg_data/lvol0`.

### 2.2 How LVM-Based Replication Works (Operational Walkthrough)

1. **Mirror LV Creation:** `lvcreate -L 20G -m1 -n mirror_lv vg_data` allocates **two legs** (`-m1`) of 20 GiB each from distinct PVs inside the VG.
2. **Mirror Log Allocation:** A mirror log is created on a separate PV to track in-flight writes and dirty regions. If the host crashes mid-write, the log indicates which leg holds the most recent committed data.
3. **Write Path:** When the application issues a `write()` syscall, the device-mapper mirror target duplicates the I/O:
   - Writes to **primary leg** (the leg whose PV holds the *highest* device priority).
   - Writes to **secondary leg**.
   - Updates the **mirror log** entry for that Logical Extent (LE).
4. **Read Path:** By default, reads are served from the primary leg only (no load balancing across mirror legs in classic LVM). Some implementations allow round-robin read distribution.
5. **Failure Detection:** If a write to a leg fails, that leg is marked as **"missing"** and I/O continues on the surviving leg. The kernel emits a `dmeventd` notification to system administrators.
6. **Recovery:** A new PV is added via `vgextend`, and `lvconvert --repair vg_data/mirror_lv` rebuilds the failed leg from the surviving one, restoring redundancy.
7. **Promotion (Split-Mirror):** `lvconvert --splitmirrors 1 -n backup_lv vg_data/mirror_lv` detaches one leg as an independent LV — useful for creating point-in-time backups or test clones.

### 2.3 Mirror Log Types — The Heart of Synchronization

| Log Type | Storage Location | Performance | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Disk Log** | Dedicated PV / separate LUN | Highest durability; small extra write | Production servers where log survival is critical |
| **Core Log** | In-memory only (RAM) | Fastest; lost on reboot | Single-host setups; risk of resync on reboot |
| **Mirrored Log** | Mirror of disk logs (in newer LVM2) | Highest availability | Clustered environments and high-end deployments |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Key Point:** A *core log* survives only until system reboot. After a crash, LVM must perform a **full re-synchronization** of the mirror because the in-RAM dirty-region map is gone. A *disk log* allows LVM to know exactly which regions are consistent, enabling a **partial (delta) resync** — much faster.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Metric | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $WP_{mirror} = 2$ | Write penalty for a 2-way synchronous mirror | dimensionless |
| 2 | $CE_{mirror} = \dfrac{1}{N}$ | Capacity efficiency for an $N$-way mirror | fraction |
| 3 | $C_{effective} = \min(PV_1, PV_2, \ldots, PV_N)$ | Usable mirror capacity (limited by smallest leg) | GiB |
| 4 | $T_{write\_total} = T_{primary} + T_{secondary} + T_{log}$ | End-to-end write latency for synchronous mirror | ms |
| 5 | $RPO_{sync} = 0$ | Recovery Point Objective for synchronous replication | seconds |
| 6 | $RPO_{async} = f(RTT,\; flush\_interval)$ | RPO for async depends on network and flush cadence | seconds |
| 7 | $RTO = T_{detect} + T_{promote} + T_{redirect}$ | Recovery Time Objective | seconds |
| 8 | $T_{resync} = \dfrac{C_{mirror}}{R_{disk}}$ | Time to rebuild a failed leg (linear model) | seconds |
| 9 | $IOPS_{write\_eff} = \dfrac{IOPS_{underlying}}{WP}$ | Effective write IOPS under mirror | IOPS |
| 10 | $PE_{size} \in \{4, \; 8, \; 16, \; 32, \; 64\}$ MiB | Standard LVM Physical Extent sizes | MiB |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas above are dimensionally and logically verified.** $CE_{mirror} = 1/N$ because an $N$-way mirror stores $N$ copies of the same data, so only $1/N$ of the raw capacity is unique user data.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Linux Server Farms:** Standard mechanism for root-disk redundancy in RHEL/CentOS/Ubuntu servers.
- **Database High Availability:** Oracle RAC, PostgreSQL streaming replication often rides on top of a mirrored LV.
- **Disaster Recovery:** When combined with **DRBD** (Distributed Replicated Block Device), LVM mirrors extend across geographically separated data centers.
- **Cloud Block Storage:** AWS EBS, Azure Managed Disks, and Google Persistent Disk use LVM-like logical abstraction in their backend.
- **SAN-Free Replication:** Cost-effective for SMBs that cannot afford a dedicated storage array with native replication licenses.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Effective Write IOPS for a 2-Way Synchronous Mirror

We derive the effective write throughput ceiling of a 2-way LVM mirror so the student can size storage systems correctly.

**Step 1: Define the underlying disk capability.**
Let a single underlying physical disk (PV leg) support a maximum write rate of $IOPS_{disk}$ IOPS.

**Step 2: Recognize that every host write generates two physical writes.**
A single logical write from the application must be duplicated — one to the primary leg and one to the secondary leg. The mirror log is a small sequential write that we initially approximate as negligible.

$$IOPS_{primary} + IOPS_{secondary} = 2 \cdot IOPS_{disk\_demand}$$

**Step 3: Apply the write penalty factor.**
The write penalty for a 2-way mirror is defined as the ratio of physical I/Os to logical I/Os.

$$WP_{mirror} = \dfrac{\text{Physical Writes}}{\text{Logical Writes}} = 2$$

**Step 4: Solve for effective logical IOPS.**
Rearranging for the effective write IOPS that the application observes:

$$IOPS_{logical\_write} = \dfrac{IOPS_{physical\_write}}{WP_{mirror}} = \dfrac{IOPS_{disk}}{2}$$

**Step 5: Account for mirror log overhead (refined model).**
In a disk-logged mirror, every logical write also incurs a small metadata write to the log device. Including the log, the refined write penalty becomes:

$$WP_{mirror}^{refined} = 2 + \epsilon_{log}$$

where $\epsilon_{log}$ is a small fractional overhead (typically $0.05$ to $0.15$ for disk-logged mirrors). Therefore:

$$IOPS_{logical\_write}^{refined} = \dfrac{IOPS_{disk}}{2 + \epsilon_{log}}$$

**Conclusion:** A 2-way mirror **halves the effective write IOPS** of a single disk, while preserving **full read IOPS** (since reads can still be served from the primary leg at full speed).

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Mirror Resynchronization Time After Disk Failure

When a mirror leg fails, LVM must rebuild a new leg from the surviving copy.

**Step 1: Define the mirror capacity.**
Let $C_{mirror}$ = total logical capacity of the mirror LV (in GiB).

**Step 2: Define the rebuild bandwidth.**
Let $R_{disk}$ = sequential rebuild throughput of the destination disk (in MiB/s). LVM typically reserves a portion of disk bandwidth for foreground I/O, so the rebuild runs at a throttled rate.

**Step 3: Write the resync time formula.**

$$T_{resync} = \dfrac{C_{mirror} \cdot 1024}{R_{disk}}$$

The factor of 1024 converts GiB to MiB.

**Step 4: Account for dirty-region-only resync (disk-log scenario).**
With a persistent disk log, only dirty regions need to be re-copied, not the full mirror. Let $D$ be the dirty fraction ($0 < D \leq 1$).

$$T_{resync}^{delta} = D \cdot \dfrac{C_{mirror} \cdot 1024}{R_{disk}}$$

For a **core log**, $D = 1$ (full resync required). For a **disk log**, $D$ can be as low as $0.01$ after a brief outage.

**Conclusion:** A persistent mirror log can reduce rebuild time by **two orders of magnitude** in outage-recovery scenarios — a critical KTU high-yield point.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Recovery Time Objective (RTO) Decomposition

The RTO quantifies how quickly a service can be restored after a primary leg failure.

**Step 1: Detection time.**
LVM's `dmeventd` daemon polls mirror health at an interval of $\Delta t_{poll}$. The expected detection time is half the polling interval on average.

$$T_{detect} = \dfrac{\Delta t_{poll}}{2}$$

**Step 2: Promotion time.**
The kernel must reconfigure the device-mapper table to mark the surviving leg as the new primary. This is essentially a metadata-only operation.

$$T_{promote} \approx 1 \text{ to } 5 \text{ seconds}$$

**Step 3: I/O redirection time.**
Applications or volume managers must remount or redirect I/O paths. Filesystems may need a quick journal replay.

$$T_{redirect} = T_{fs\_replay} + T_{mount}$$

**Step 4: Compose the total RTO.**

$$RTO = \dfrac{\Delta t_{poll}}{2} + T_{promote} + T_{fs\_replay} + T_{mount}$$

For a default LVM polling interval of **10 seconds**, typical RTO is in the range of **6 to 15 seconds** for synchronous local mirrors.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: LVM-Based vs. Array-Based Replication

| Parameter | LVM-Based Replication | Array-Based Replication |
|---|---|---|
| **Location of Engine** | Host OS kernel (device-mapper) | Storage array controller |
| **OS Dependency** | Tied to a specific OS / kernel | OS-agnostic (LUN-level) |
| **Performance Overhead** | Consumes host CPU and memory | Offloaded to array |
| **Cost** | Free (bundled with OS) | Requires licensed replication feature |
| **Distance Replication** | Limited (needs DRBD for remote) | Native Fibre Channel / IP support |
| **Granularity** | Per-LV (filesystem-aware) | Per-LUN (block-level) |
| **RPO Capability** | Near-zero (sync) | Zero (sync) to minutes (async) |
| **Best Suited For** | SMBs, dev/test, single-host HA | Enterprise, multi-site DR |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: LVM-Based Replication Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    APP[Application Layer - File System, DB] --> LV[Logical Volume - mirror_lv]
    LV --> DMT[Device Mapper Mirror Target - dm-mirror]
    DMT --> LEG1[Mirror Leg 1 - Primary PV - /dev/sdb]
    DMT --> LEG2[Mirror Leg 2 - Secondary PV - /dev/sdc]
    DMT --> LOG[Mirror Log - Disk Log - /dev/sdd]
    DMT --> KERN[Kernel dmeventd - Health Monitor]
    KERN -.-> DMT
    LEG1 --> PHYS1[Physical Disk 1 - HDD or SSD]
    LEG2 --> PHYS2[Physical Disk 2 - HDD or SSD]
    LOG --> PHYS3[Physical Disk 3 - Dedicated Log Device]
```

**Interpretation of the Diagram:**
- The **application** issues standard block I/O to the logical volume.
- The **device-mapper mirror target** intercepts every write and fans it out to both legs.
- The **mirror log** is updated on each write so the kernel knows which leg is in sync.
- The **dmeventd daemon** monitors leg health and triggers recovery on failure.
- Each leg resides on a **physically separate disk**, satisfying the independence requirement of RAID-1 / mirror configurations.

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Sequence Diagram: Write I/O Path in Synchronous Mirror

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant App as Application
    participant FS as File System
    participant DM as DM-Mirror Target
    participant Leg1 as Primary Leg PV
    participant Log as Mirror Log
    participant Leg2 as Secondary Leg PV
    participant Mon as dmeventd

    App->>FS: write data block
    FS->>DM: submit bio request
    DM->>Leg1: write block to primary
    Leg1-->>DM: ack primary write
    DM->>Log: update dirty region entry
    Log-->>DM: ack log write
    DM->>Leg2: write block to secondary
    Leg2-->>DM: ack secondary write
    DM-->>FS: complete with status OK
    DM-->>Mon: notify successful mirror
    Mon->>Mon: schedule next health poll
```

**Interpretation:** The synchronous mirror enforces a strict ordering — the write is reported as complete to the application only after **both** legs and the mirror log have acknowledged. This guarantees $RPO = 0$ at the cost of latency equal to the slowest leg plus the log write.

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Flowchart: Failure and Recovery Workflow

```mermaid
graph TD
    NORMAL[Normal Mirror Operation] --> CHECK{dmeventd Poll}
    CHECK --> LEG1FAIL[Leg 1 Write Failure Detected]
    LEG1FAIL --> MARK[Mark Leg 1 as Missing]
    MARK --> CONTINUE[Continue I/O on Leg 2 Only]
    CONTINUE --> NOTIFY[Notify Admin via Syslog]
    NOTIFY --> ADMIN[Admin Adds New PV via vgextend]
    ADMIN --> REPAIR[Run lvconvert --repair]
    REPAIR --> RESYNC[Delta Resync from Log]
    RESYNC --> RESTORED[Mirror Fully Redundant Restored]
```

**Interpretation:** LVM degrades gracefully — the volume remains online (degraded mode) even after a leg failure, and full redundancy is restored once a replacement disk is introduced and a resync is triggered.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style]**
Define **Logical Volume Manager (LVM)-based replication**. List its three primary components.

**Model Answer:**

LVM-based replication is a host-level data protection technique that uses the Logical Volume Manager subsystem of an operating system to maintain synchronized copies (mirrors) of logical volumes across physically distinct storage devices.

The three primary components are:
1. **Physical Volume (PV)** — the underlying block device initialized with LVM metadata.
2. **Volume Group (VG)** — a pool of storage capacity formed by aggregating one or more PVs.
3. **Logical Volume (LV)** — a virtual block device carved out of a VG; the unit that is mirrored.

*(3 marks — 1 for definition, 2 for components)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**
Differentiate between a **disk mirror log** and a **core mirror log** in LVM.

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Disk Log | Core Log |
|---|---|---|
| **Location** | Stored on a dedicated disk / LUN | Stored in host RAM only |
| **Persistence** | Survives reboot and crash | Lost on reboot/crash |
| **Resync Type** | Partial (delta) resync | Full resync required |
| **Performance** | Slightly slower (extra disk write) | Slightly faster (in-memory) |
| **Use Case** | Production environments | Single-host dev / non-critical use |

*(3 marks — 1 for definition, 2 for differentiation)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q.A.(a)** [7 Marks] — Explain the **architecture of LVM-based replication** with a neat block diagram. Describe the role of each component.

**Model Solution:**

The architecture of LVM-based replication consists of the following layers:

1. **Application Layer:** Issues standard block-level I/O operations (read/write) to a logical volume.
2. **Logical Volume Layer:** A virtual block device (e.g., `/dev/vg_data/mirror_lv`) that aggregates two or more physical copies.
3. **Device-Mapper Mirror Target:** A kernel module (`dm-mirror`) that intercepts every I/O and duplicates it to the mirror legs.
4. **Mirror Legs (Primary and Secondary):** Physical storage copies that reside on distinct PVs.
5. **Mirror Log:** A small metadata region that records dirty regions of the mirror.
6. **Health Monitor (`dmeventd`):** A user-space daemon that polls leg health and initiates recovery on failure.

Each component is essential:
- The **device-mapper target** ensures write duplication.
- The **mirror log** ensures crash-consistent recovery.
- The **dmeventd daemon** ensures automatic failover and resync.

*(Refer to Section 4.1 diagram for the architecture visualization.)*

---

**Q.A.(b)** [7 Marks] — Discuss the **mirror recovery process** in LVM. What happens when one leg fails?

**Model Solution:**

When a leg fails in an LVM mirror, the following sequence occurs:

1. **Detection:** The `dmeventd` daemon detects the failure during its periodic poll (default 10 seconds) and marks the failed leg as *missing*.
2. **Degraded Mode:** The mirror continues to serve I/O using the surviving leg. The volume is online but in a *degraded* state — no redundancy exists.
3. **Notification:** A syslog entry and an email (if configured) is sent to the administrator.
4. **Replacement:** The administrator physically replaces the failed disk and adds it to the VG using `vgextend vg_data /dev/new_disk`.
5. **Repair Command:** `lvconvert --repair vg_data/mirror_lv` is executed, which tells LVM to rebuild the missing leg from the surviving one.
6. **Resynchronization:** LVM reads the mirror log to determine dirty regions. With a disk log, only the dirty regions are resynchronized (delta resync). With a core log, the entire leg is rebuilt.
7. **Full Redundancy:** Once resync completes, the mirror is fully redundant again, and normal operation resumes.

**Performance Impact During Resync:** The rebuild process consumes disk bandwidth and may degrade foreground I/O performance by 10–30%, depending on the rebuild throttle setting (`--readahead`, `activation/threshold` in `lvm.conf`).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**Q.B.(a)** [7 Marks] — Explain the **step-by-step procedure to create a mirrored logical volume in Linux** using LVM.

**Model Solution:**

1. **Initialize Physical Volumes:**
   `pvcreate /dev/sdb /dev/sdc /dev/sdd`
   This writes the LVM metadata header to each disk.

2. **Create a Volume Group:**
   `vgcreate vg_data /dev/sdb /dev/sdc /dev/sdd`
   This aggregates the three PVs into a single addressable pool.

3. **Create a Mirrored Logical Volume:**
   `lvcreate -L 20G -m1 -n mirror_lv --mirrorlog disk vg_data`
   - `-L 20G` sets the logical size to 20 GiB.
   - `-m1` specifies a 2-way mirror (one primary + one secondary).
   - `--mirrorlog disk` allocates a persistent disk log on a separate PV.

4. **Create a Filesystem:**
   `mkfs.xfs /dev/vg_data/mirror_lv`
   Formats the mirrored LV with the XFS filesystem.

5. **Mount the Volume:**
   `mount /dev/vg_data/mirror_lv /mnt/data`
   The volume is now accessible and protected by the mirror.

6. **Verify the Mirror Status:**
   `lvs -a -o +devices vg_data`
   Displays the mirror legs, log, and synchronization state.

7. **Simulate Failure (Optional):**
   `lvconvert --repair vg_data/mirror_lv` after manually failing one leg to test the recovery workflow.

---

**Q.B.(b)** [7 Marks] — Compare **LVM-based replication** with **array-based replication**. State two advantages and two disadvantages of each.

**Model Solution:**

| Aspect | LVM-Based Replication | Array-Based Replication |
|---|---|---|
| **Engine Location** | Host OS kernel | Storage array controller |
| **Cost** | Free (OS-bundled) | Licensed feature per array |
| **CPU Overhead** | Consumes host CPU | Offloaded to array |
| **Distance Support** | Local only (needs DRBD for remote) | Native WAN / FC extension |
| **Granularity** | Per-LV | Per-LUN |
| **Performance** | Limited by host resources | Dedicated array hardware |

**Two Advantages of LVM-Based Replication:**
1. **Zero licensing cost** — included in the operating system.
2. **Fine-grained control** — mirror individual LVs rather than entire LUNs.

**Two Disadvantages of LVM-Based Replication:**
1. **Consumes host CPU and RAM** — competes with application workloads.
2. **Limited to local disks** — requires DRBD or similar for remote replication.

**Two Advantages of Array-Based Replication:**
1. **Offloads processing** — does not impact host performance.
2. **Supports long-distance replication** natively over Fibre Channel or IP.

**Two Disadvantages of Array-Based Replication:**
1. **Expensive licensing** — replication features are premium-tier.
2. **Vendor lock-in** — tied to a specific array vendor.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students frequently **omit the mirror log discussion** and lose 2–3 marks. Always mention the *type* of log and its impact on resync time.
> - Confusing **RPO and RTO** is a common error. Remember: RPO = *data loss tolerance*; RTO = *downtime tolerance*.
> - Do **not** state that LVM mirrors are "the same as RAID-1." RAID-1 is implemented in the **array** hardware; LVM mirrors are implemented in the **host** kernel. This distinction is worth 2 marks.
> - Avoid writing "LVM is used for backup" — LVM mirroring is for **availability and replication**, not for backup. Backup is a separate, complementary process.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **LVM-Based Replication** is a **host-based, software-defined** replication mechanism that operates at the logical-volume layer.
- The **three-tier architecture** is **PV → VG → LV**; mirroring is applied at the LV layer.
- A **2-way mirror** halves the effective write IOPS and reduces usable capacity to **50%** of the raw pool.
- **Mirror logs are mandatory** for crash-consistent recovery. Disk logs enable delta resync; core logs force full resync.
- The **write penalty** is $2$ (plus a small log overhead), and the **read IOPS** is unaffected in classic LVM.
- **RPO for synchronous LVM mirrors = 0**; **RTO ≈ 5–15 seconds** for local mirrors.
- **CLVM (Clustered LVM)** extends replication to multi-host clusters.
- **DRBD** is the standard way to extend LVM mirroring to **remote / geo-distributed** sites.
- **LVM mirrors ≠ RAID-1 arrays**: the former runs in the host kernel; the latter runs in the array controller.
- **Key commands to remember for lab exams:** `pvcreate`, `vgcreate`, `lvcreate -m1`, `lvs`, `lvconvert --repair`, `vgextend`.
- **Standard PE size = 4 MiB** (default in most Linux distributions).
- **LVM-based replication is best suited for** single-host HA, SAN-free environments, dev/test, and as a building block for DRBD-based geo-replication.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Backup Methods - Hot Backups
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST867 - STORAGE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Backup Methods - Hot Backups

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3 — Backup Methods: Hot Backups

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hot Backup (Online Backup)** is a backup methodology in which a copy of live, actively-running data is captured while the production system, application, or database continues to serve read/write requests without service interruption. It is the de-facto standard for mission-critical **24×7** enterprise storage environments where downtime (even minutes) results in unacceptable revenue loss or SLA violation.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, a **Hot Backup** is defined under three simultaneous conditions:

1. The **primary system remains ONLINE** and accepts user I/O during the entire backup window.
2. The backup operation operates on a **consistent point-in-time image** of the data, guaranteed by mechanisms such as **write-ahead logging (WAL)**, **copy-on-write snapshots**, or **redirect-on-write snapshots**.
3. The backup must satisfy the **ACID (Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability)** invariants of the underlying transactional system.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Live CCTV Recording" Metaphor

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Box — Real-World Analogy**
>
> Imagine a busy restaurant kitchen during dinner rush. A **cold backup** would be closing the entire restaurant, dimming the lights, and photographing the kitchen inventory. A **warm backup** would be closing only half the kitchen. A **HOT BACKUP** is like installing a high-speed overhead camera that records the contents of every shelf, pot, and fridge **in real time** while the chefs continue cooking and serving customers. The camera (backup engine) must be smart enough to:
> - Capture a *consistent* frame even if a chef moves a pan mid-snapshot.
> - Tag each frame with a precise timestamp.
> - Not slow down the chefs (no performance degradation).
> - Allow the recording to be replayed exactly even if the kitchen is later destroyed (disaster recovery).

This is precisely what hot backup engines do: they interleave with the live I/O stream, capture state, and guarantee a recoverable consistent image.

### 1.3 Hot Backup vs. Cold Backup vs. Warm Backup — Comparative Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight** — This comparison is a frequently tested **2-mark** question in KTU ESE Module 3.

| Parameter | **Cold Backup** | **Warm Backup** | **Hot Backup (Online)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| System State | Powered OFF / Offline | Partially online (read-only mode) | Fully ONLINE (read/write) |
| Service Availability | **0%** during backup | **~50–80%** | **100%** |
| Data Consistency | Easy (filesystem quiesced) | Moderate | Requires **logs/snapshots** |
| Performance Impact | None | Low–Moderate | Moderate–High (I/O contention) |
| Backup Window | Large | Medium | Zero (continuous) |
| Recovery Time Objective (RTO) | High | Medium | **Low** |
| Recovery Point Objective (RPO) | Last successful backup | Near real-time | **Near-zero** |
| Typical Use Case | Dev/test systems | Reporting DB replicas | **OLTP, Banking, E-commerce** |
| Cost | Low | Medium | High |

### 1.4 Core Metrics Highlighted in KTU Syllabus

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following **bold metrics** are the standard engineering quantities used to evaluate hot backup strategies:
>
> - **RPO (Recovery Point Objective)** — maximum tolerable data loss, measured in **seconds**.
> - **RTO (Recovery Time Objective)** — maximum tolerable downtime, measured in **seconds/minutes**.
> - **MTTR (Mean Time To Recover)** — average time to restore service.
> - **Backup Throughput** — measured in **MB/s** or **GB/hour**.
> - **Log Shipping Latency** — typically **< 5 seconds** for synchronous replication.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Architecture of a Hot Backup

A hot backup is **not a single operation** — it is a coordinated pipeline of five phases. The following structured breakdown represents the *canonical* enterprise hot backup workflow:

### Phase 1 — Snapshot Initiation
- Backup agent signals the storage controller / volume manager to **freeze the data view**.
- This does **not** halt I/O; instead, it triggers either:
  - **Copy-on-Write (CoW)** — original blocks preserved; new writes go to a diff area.
  - **Redirect-on-Write (RoW)** — pointer redirection; metadata-only operation.

### Phase 2 — Data Transfer
- A **backup server / media server** streams blocks from the snapshot to:
  - **D2D** (Disk-to-Disk) — local or remote disk array.
  - **D2T** (Disk-to-Tape) — for long-term archival.
  - **D2D2T** (Disk-to-Disk-to-Tape) — hybrid.
- Transfer uses protocols: **iSCSI**, **FC (Fibre Channel)**, **NFS**, **CIFS/SMB**, or **NDMP (Network Data Management Protocol)**.

### Phase 3 — Log Capture (Transactionally Consistent)
- The database or application **transaction log** (e.g., **WAL** in PostgreSQL, **redo log** in Oracle, **binlog** in MySQL) is continuously streamed.
- This ensures that any writes committed *after* the snapshot start can be **replayed** during recovery.

### Phase 4 — Consistency Verification
- Checksum validation (e.g., **CRC32, SHA-256**) on every transferred block.
- Optional **application-level consistency check** (e.g., DBMS `DBCC`, `pg_verifybackup`).

### Phase 5 — Catalog Update & Housekeeping
- Backup metadata (timestamp, size, checksum, retention policy) is written to the **backup catalog**.
- Old backups are pruned per the **Grandfather-Father-Son (GFS)** retention scheme or **3-2-1 rule**.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Hot Backup Mechanisms

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical "Why" Analysis**
>
> - **Why CoW / RoW snapshots?** — Because stopping live I/O on a petabyte database is impossible; the snapshot must be atomic at the block level.
> - **Why transaction log shipping?** — Because the snapshot is only *eventually* consistent; logs fill the gap to achieve a *point-in-time consistent* state.
> - **Why checksum verification?** — Silent data corruption (bit rot) is a real phenomenon; checksums detect it with probability **1 − 2⁻ⁿ** for an n-bit hash.

## 2.3 The "How" — Synchronous vs. Asynchronous Hot Backup

| Mode | Mechanism | Latency | Data Loss Risk | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Synchronous** | Log shipped **before** transaction commit acknowledged | High (~ms) | **Zero RPO** | Banking, stock trading |
| **Asynchronous** | Log shipped **after** commit | Low | RPO > 0 (seconds–minutes) | Email, CRM |
| **Near-synchronous** | Log shipped within bounded window | Medium | RPO ≈ bounded window | Mid-tier OLTP |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formulas for Hot Backup Calculations**

| # | Formula / Concept | Description | Unit / Boundary |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{Backup Window} = \dfrac{\text{Data Size (GB)}}{\text{Throughput (GB/hr)}}$ | Time required to complete a single backup | hours |
| 2 | $\text{RPO} \le \text{Log Shipping Interval}$ | Maximum data loss bounded by log cadence | seconds |
| 3 | $\text{RTO} = T_{\text{detect}} + T_{\text{restore}} + T_{\text{verify}}$ | Total downtime budget | seconds |
| 4 | $\text{Required Throughput} = \dfrac{\text{Daily Change Rate (\%)}}{\text{Backup Window (hr)}}$ | Minimum backup pipe capacity | GB/hr |
| 5 | $\text{Storage Efficiency} = \dfrac{\text{Logical Data}}{\text{Physical Allocated}} \le 1$ | Deduplication/compression ratio | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\text{CoW Overhead} = \dfrac{\text{Write I/O during snapshot}}{\text{Total I/O}}$ | Performance cost | 0 < x < 1 |
| 7 | $\text{SLA Compliance} = \dfrac{\text{Successful Backups}}{\text{Total Scheduled Backups}} \times 100\%$ | Reliability metric | % (target ≥ 99.9%) |
| 8 | $\text{3-2-1 Rule} = 3 \text{ copies}, 2 \text{ media}, 1 \text{ offsite}$ | Data protection invariant | rule |
| 9 | $\text{MTTDL} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda_{\text{disk failure}} \times \text{Active Disks}}$ | Mean Time To Data Loss | hours |
| 10 | $\text{Sync Replication Overhead} \approx 2 \times RTT_{\text{network}}$ | Round-trip latency cost | ms |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Application Callout**
> - **Banking**: Hot backup ensures ATM/POS networks never stop.
> - **Cloud (AWS, Azure, GCP)**: All managed databases (RDS, Azure SQL) use hot backup under the hood via storage-level snapshots + log streaming.
> - **VMware vSphere**: Uses **Changed Block Tracking (CBT)** to perform incremental hot backups — a direct application of the CoW principle.
> - **Container Orchestration (Kubernetes)**: CSI drivers perform hot snapshots of PVCs using the same architecture.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough

## 3.1 Problem 1 — Backup Window Calculation (Worked Example)

> **Problem:** A production database has a logical size of **4 TB**. The daily change rate is **8%**. The network link between primary site and backup site has a throughput of **200 MB/s**. Compute:
> (a) The total bytes to be backed up daily (full + incremental).
> (b) The backup window in hours.
> (c) Whether this satisfies an SLA requiring backup completion within **6 hours**.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Compute the daily changed data volume.**

The full backup is performed once weekly (assumed), and the daily backup is incremental (only changed blocks). For the *daily incremental*:

$$
\text{Daily Change Volume} = 4\ \text{TB} \times 0.08 = 0.32\ \text{TB}
$$

Converting to MB:
$$
0.32\ \text{TB} \times 1024\ \frac{\text{GB}}{\text{TB}} \times 1024\ \frac{\text{MB}}{\text{GB}} = 335{,}544.32\ \text{MB}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the backup window using the throughput formula.**

$$
\text{Backup Window (s)} = \dfrac{335{,}544.32\ \text{MB}}{200\ \text{MB/s}} = 1677.72\ \text{s}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to hours.**

$$
\text{Backup Window (hr)} = \dfrac{1677.72}{3600} = 0.466\ \text{hr} \approx 28\ \text{min}
$$

**Step 4 — Compare with SLA.**

$$
0.466\ \text{hr} \ll 6\ \text{hr} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{SLA SATISFIED with } 92\%\ \text{margin}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** A 28-minute hot backup window means we can comfortably schedule **multiple daily hot backups**, achieving an RPO of less than 1 hour, which is excellent for most enterprise OLTP workloads.

---

## 3.2 Problem 2 — RPO / RTO Trade-off Analysis

> **Problem:** An e-commerce platform uses **asynchronous log shipping** to a standby site. The log shipping interval is **30 seconds**. Network RTT is **10 ms**. The standby must be activated manually, taking **3 minutes** for DNS failover. Failure detection (via heartbeat) takes **15 seconds**. Compute RPO and RTO.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Compute RPO.**

For asynchronous log shipping:
$$
\text{RPO} \approx \text{Log Shipping Interval} = 30\ \text{s}
$$

This is the *worst-case* data loss window — at most 30 seconds of committed transactions could be lost.

**Step 2 — Compute RTO.**

$$
\text{RTO} = T_{\text{detect}} + T_{\text{failover}} + T_{\text{verify}}
$$

$$
\text{RTO} = 15\ \text{s} + 180\ \text{s} + T_{\text{verify}}
$$

Assuming verification of standby consistency takes **45 seconds**:
$$
\text{RTO} = 15 + 180 + 45 = 240\ \text{s} = 4\ \text{minutes}
$$

**Step 3 — Comparison with SLA.**

If the SLA mandates **RPO ≤ 60 s** and **RTO ≤ 5 min**, then:

- $\text{RPO} = 30\ \text{s} \le 60\ \text{s}$  ✅
- $\text{RTO} = 240\ \text{s} \le 300\ \text{s}$ ✅

Both are satisfied.

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical Takeaway:** Reducing RPO further requires switching to **synchronous** replication, which increases write latency by approximately **$2 \times \text{RTT}$** = 20 ms in this case.

---

## 3.3 Problem 3 — CoW Snapshot Overhead Estimation

> **Problem:** A storage volume has a steady-state write rate of **500 IOPS** during business hours. A hot snapshot is triggered at 10:00 AM. For the next 15 minutes, 30% of incoming writes trigger CoW metadata operations. Compute the additional I/O burden on the storage array.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the affected IOPS.**

$$
\text{CoW IOPS} = 500 \times 0.30 = 150\ \text{IOPS}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute total I/O burden on the array.**

Each CoW operation involves:
- 1 read of the original block (to preserve it)
- 1 write of the original block to the snapshot area
- 1 write of the new block to the live volume

Therefore, each logical CoW write becomes **3 physical I/Os**:

$$
\text{Additional Physical IOPS} = 150 \times 3 = 450\ \text{IOPS}
$$

**Step 3 — Total IOPS experienced by the array.**

$$
\text{Total} = 500 + 450 = 950\ \text{IOPS}
$$

**Step 4 — Percentage overhead.**

$$
\text{Overhead} = \dfrac{450}{950} \times 100\% \approx 47.4\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Decision Point:** A 47% I/O overhead is significant. In latency-sensitive workloads, **Redirect-on-Write (RoW)** snapshots are preferred because they avoid the read-before-write penalty.

---

## 3.4 Comprehensive Step-by-Step Recovery Walkthrough (Post-Disaster)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** Recovery is the *real* test of a hot backup. A perfect backup with a broken recovery is useless.

**Step 1 — Identify the failure scope** (full site, single VM, corrupt file).
**Step 2 — Locate the most recent successful hot backup** in the catalog.
**Step 3 — Restore the snapshot to a staging volume.**
**Step 4 — Apply transaction logs** from the backup start time forward, in order.
**Step 5 — Roll forward** to the desired point-in-time (PITR).
**Step 6 — Verify consistency** using application-level checks (`dbcc checkdb`, `pg_dump --verify`).
**Step 7 — Promote the staging volume** to production (atomic pointer swap).
**Step 8 — Redirect clients** (DNS, load balancer, virtual IP).
**Step 9 — Monitor for replication lag** if a new standby must be re-established.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hot Backup End-to-End Architecture (Mermaid Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Production Application] --> B[Primary Storage Volume LUN]
    B --> C{Snapshot Engine}
    C -->|CoW or RoW| D[Point-in-Time Snapshot]
    D --> E[Backup Media Server]
    B --> F[Transaction Log Streamer WAL]
    F --> G[Log Staging Area]
    G --> E
    E --> H{Destination Tier}
    H -->|D2D| I[Disk Backup Array]
    H -->|D2T| J[Tape Library]
    H -->|D2D2T| K[Disk then Tape]
    E --> L[Backup Catalog Database]
    L --> M[Verification Service CRC SHA256]
    M --> N[Recovery Ready Backup Set]
    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000
    style D fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000
    style N fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **yellow node** is the live production workload.
- The **green node** is the consistent snapshot point.
- The **red node** is the recovery-ready output.

## 4.2 Hot Backup Operational State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> SnapshotInitiate: Schedule Trigger
    SnapshotInitiate --> CapturingImage: Freeze View
    CapturingImage --> StreamingBlocks: Transfer to Media Server
    StreamingBlocks --> LogCapture: Continuous WAL Shipping
    LogCapture --> Verification: Checksum Compute
    Verification --> CatalogUpdate: Metadata Persist
    CatalogUpdate --> Idle: Success
    Verification --> RetryQueue: Checksum Mismatch
    RetryQueue --> StreamingBlocks: Resume Transfer
    CatalogUpdate --> [*]: Backup Complete
```

## 4.3 Decision Tree — Choosing Backup Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Can system tolerate downtime?}
    Q1 -->|Yes No| Q2{Critical data loss risk?}
    Q1 -->|Yes Yes| Z1[Use Cold Backup]
    Q2 -->|High| Z2[Use Hot Backup Synchronous]
    Q2 -->|Medium| Z3[Use Hot Backup Asynchronous]
    Q2 -->|Low| Z4[Use Warm Backup]
    style Z1 fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#000
    style Z2 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style Z3 fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000
    style Z4 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000
```

## 4.4 3-2-1 Backup Topology (Industry Standard)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Primary Site Live Data] --> C1[Copy 1 Local Disk]
    P --> C2[Copy 2 Local Disk]
    P --> C3[Copy 3 Offsite Cloud]
    C1 --> R[Recovery Engine]
    C2 --> R
    C3 --> R
    style P fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000
    style C3 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000
    style R fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question Style]**

### **Q1. Define Hot Backup. List two advantages and one disadvantage.**
**[3 Marks | CO3 / Bloom Level: Remember]**

**Model Answer:**

A **Hot Backup** is a backup operation performed while the production system remains online and continues to serve user requests. The backup is achieved by creating a point-in-time snapshot (CoW or RoW) and streaming transaction logs to ensure recoverability.

**Advantages:**
1. **Zero downtime** — production services remain available 24×7.
2. **Low RPO/RTO** — near-real-time recovery point and fast restoration.

**Disadvantage:**
1. **Higher cost and complexity** — requires snapshot engines, log shipping, and dedicated backup infrastructure.

---

### **Q2. Differentiate between Copy-on-Write and Redirect-on-Write snapshot mechanisms.**
**[3 Marks | CO3 / Bloom Level: Understand]**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Copy-on-Write (CoW) | Redirect-on-Write (RoW) |
|---|---|---|
| On first write to a snapped block | Original block is **copied** to snapshot area; new write goes to live volume | Block pointer is **redirected** to a new location; metadata only |
| I/O Pattern | Read + Write + Write (3 I/Os) | Write only (1 I/O) |
| Latency Impact | Higher (read-before-write penalty) | Lower |
| Snapshot Read Performance | Slower (reads from snapshot area) | Faster (pointer follow) |
| Example | VMware VMFS, ZFS (traditional) | NetApp WAFL, Oracle ASM |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice Pattern:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the **architecture of a hot backup system** for a relational database. Include the role of **transaction logs**, **point-in-time snapshots**, and **the backup catalog**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A company has **6 TB of data**, a **daily change rate of 12%**, and a backup link of **150 MB/s**. Calculate the daily backup window. Comment on whether a **4-hour backup SLA** is achievable. If not, recommend two engineering solutions.

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) Architecture**

A hot backup architecture for an RDBMS has six layers:

1. **Production Database Engine** — serves OLTP transactions continuously.
2. **Storage Layer** — provides LUN/volume abstraction; supports snapshot APIs.
3. **Snapshot Engine** — creates CoW/RoW point-in-time images without quiescing I/O.
4. **Log Shipping Module** — streams the Write-Ahead Log (WAL) to a staging area with timestamps.
5. **Backup Media Server** — reads from snapshot + log area, applies deduplication/compression, and writes to the backup target (D2D/D2T).
6. **Catalog & Verification Service** — stores metadata (start time, end time, size, checksum) and validates the backup set.

**Workflow:**
- T0: Snapshot initiated → freeze view.
- T0 → Tn: All committed transactions logged.
- Tn: Snapshot frozen; copy begins.
- Tn+1: Incremental logs continue to ship.
- Recovery: Restore snapshot + apply logs from T0 to failure point = PITR (Point-in-Time Recovery).

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) Calculation**

**Step 1 — Daily changed data:**
$$
\text{Daily Change} = 6\ \text{TB} \times 0.12 = 0.72\ \text{TB} = 720{,}000\ \text{MB}
$$

**Step 2 — Backup window:**
$$
\text{Window} = \dfrac{720{,}000}{150} = 4800\ \text{s} = 1.33\ \text{hours} \approx 80\ \text{minutes}
$$

**Step 3 — SLA comparison:**
$$
1.33\ \text{hr} \ll 4\ \text{hr} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{SLA ACHIEVABLE with} \approx 67\%\ \text{headroom}
$$

**Engineering Recommendations (in case SLA tightened):**
1. **Source-side deduplication** to reduce bytes transferred by 30–50%.
2. **Multi-stream parallelism** with 4 parallel backup streams to use aggregate bandwidth.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning** — Students frequently:
> - Forget to convert TB to MB (use $1024^2$ conversion, not $1000^2$).
> - Confuse **full backup size** with **incremental backup size** in the formula.
> - Ignore **network protocol overhead** (~5–10% for iSCSI/NFS headers).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **3-2-1 backup rule** and explain how it is implemented in a hot backup architecture. Mention the role of **offsite replication** and **immutability**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare **synchronous** and **asynchronous** hot replication. With a neat diagram, show how **RPO differs** in each case. State one real-world scenario where each is preferred.

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) 3-2-1 Rule**

The **3-2-1 rule** mandates:
- **3** copies of data (1 primary + 2 backups).
- **2** different media types (e.g., disk + tape).
- **1** copy offsite (geographically separate).

**Implementation in Hot Backup:**
1. **Copy 1** — Primary site live data (production).
2. **Copy 2** — Local backup array (D2D) — captured via hot snapshot.
3. **Copy 3** — Offsite cloud or DR site — replicated synchronously/asynchronously.

**Offsite Replication** ensures survival against site-wide disasters (earthquake, fire, ransomware). **Immutability** (e.g., S3 Object Lock, WORM storage) prevents backup tampering by ransomware — a critical modern concern.

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) Sync vs. Async**

| Parameter | Synchronous | Asynchronous |
|---|---|---|
| Acknowledge commit | After remote write confirmed | Immediately after local write |
| RPO | **0 bytes** | Bounded by log shipping lag |
| Write Latency | High (+2×RTT) | Low |
| Bandwidth | High | Low |
| Failure Impact | Halt on remote failure | Continue on local failure |

**RPO Diagram (conceptual):**
```
Synchronous:   |====W====|  RPO = 0
               T0       T1

Asynchronous: |==W==|........lost?........|  RPO = (T_ship - T_commit)
               T0                            T1
```

**Scenarios:**
- **Synchronous** → Stock exchange order book, nuclear plant SCADA (zero data loss mandatory).
- **Asynchronous** → Social media feeds, email archives (low latency, occasional loss tolerable).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning** — Common mistakes:
> - Drawing the diagram with arrows pointing the wrong way for log flow.
> - Confusing RPO with RTO in the explanation.
> - Omitting the **2×RTT** cost in the synchronous case (a very common KTU valuation trap).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Hot Backups (Module 3)**

- ✅ **Hot Backup = Online Backup** = system active during backup.
- ✅ Built on three pillars: **Snapshot + Log Shipping + Verification**.
- ✅ **CoW** = read-before-write, high consistency, slower.
- ✅ **RoW** = pointer redirect, faster, metadata-heavy.
- ✅ **Synchronous replication** → **RPO = 0**; cost = **2×RTT latency**.
- ✅ **Asynchronous replication** → **RPO > 0**; cheaper, faster.
- ✅ **RPO** = data loss budget; **RTO** = downtime budget.
- ✅ **Backup Window** $= \dfrac{\text{Data Size}}{\text{Throughput}}$.
- ✅ **3-2-1 Rule** = 3 copies, 2 media, 1 offsite — minimum data protection.
- ✅ **Immutability** is essential against ransomware in modern hot backup.
- ✅ **Verification** uses CRC32 / SHA-256 checksums.
- ✅ **NDMP protocol** is standard for enterprise NAS hot backups.
- ✅ **VMware CBT** (Changed Block Tracking) enables efficient hot incremental backups.
- ✅ **Cloud-managed hot backups** (AWS RDS, Azure SQL) all use snapshot + log architecture under the hood.
- ✅ Conversion constant: **1 TB = $1024^2$ MB = 1,048,576 MB** (use $1024$, not $1000$).

---

> **End of Module 3 — Hot Backups | KTU 2024 Scheme | PECST867**
