topics.title: Offline Backups
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST867 - STORAGE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Offline Backups

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Offline Backup** is a data protection methodology in which a copy of organizational data is created onto a **removable or physically separable storage medium** (such as magnetic tape, optical disc, or removable disk) while the production system is either shut down (cold backup) or the backup medium is subsequently **disconnected from the network** to create an *air-gapped* state. The defining characteristic is the **physical and logical separation** of the backup copy from the live, online production environment at the conclusion of the backup operation.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PECST867 - Storage Systems, Module 3)**, offline backup is treated as the foundational tier of the *3-2-1 backup rule* (3 copies, 2 media types, 1 offsite/offline) and forms the cornerstone of any robust **Disaster Recovery (DR)** and **Business Continuity Planning (BCP)** strategy.

### Key Terminology Map

| Term | Definition | KTU Relevance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Cold Backup** | Backup performed while the system is shut down or in maintenance mode. | Module 3 — Backup Models |
| **Air-Gap Backup** | A backup whose storage medium is electronically isolated from any network after write. | Ransomware defense layer |
| **Backup Window** | The time-duration slot during which a backup must complete to avoid impacting production. | Capacity Planning |
| **RPO (Recovery Point Objective)** | The maximum tolerable age of data loss measured in time. | DR Metrics |
| **RTO (Recovery Time Objective)** | The maximum tolerable duration of service unavailability. | DR Metrics |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Family Photo Album"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plain-English Intuition (For First-Time Learners):**
> Imagine your family has an *original* photo album that everyone flips through daily. Over time, pages get coffee stains, corners tear, and the spine cracks. To safeguard these memories, you make a **photocopy of every page** and store that photocopy in a **locker at your grandmother's house**, far from your home. Even if a fire destroys your house (a disaster), or a mischievous child scribbles on the album (ransomware / accidental corruption), the *offline copy* in the locker remains untouched.

**Mapping the analogy to KTU terminology:**

| Everyday Analogy | Storage Systems Equivalent |
| :--- | :--- |
| Original photo album | **Production primary storage** (online disk arrays) |
| Photocopying every page | **Backup operation** (read source → write to tape/disk) |
| Locker at grandmother's house | **Offsite / offline vault** |
| Fire / scribbling child | **Disk failure / Ransomware encryption** |
| Walking back to recover from locker | **Restore / Recovery operation** |

The crucial idea is **physical separation**: the backup is *not* reachable by anything that could also reach the live system. A ransomware attack that encrypts every file on the network cannot touch a tape cartridge sitting on a shelf in another building.

---

## 1.3 Why Offline Backups Still Matter in 2024+

Despite the rise of cloud-native continuous replication, the KTU syllabus emphasises offline backups because:

1. **Last line of defense against ransomware.** Modern strains (e.g., locker-class malware) actively hunt for connected backup shares. An air-gapped tape defeats them entirely.
2. **Regulatory compliance.** Standards such as **SEBI/IT Act guidelines, HIPAA, GDPR, and PCI-DSS** mandate *immutable, offline copies* for audit trails.
3. **Cost efficiency.** Tape remains the lowest **cost-per-terabyte** ($/TB) of any storage medium, often **5×–10× cheaper** than spinning disk.
4. **Long-term archival.** Tape **LTO-9** cartridges boast a shelf life of **≥ 30 years** under proper environmental conditions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metrics to Memorise for KTU:**
> - **LTO-9 native capacity: 18 TB** (45 TB compressed).
> - **LTO transfer rate: 400 MB/s native** (1,000 MB/s compressed).
> - **Tape shelf life: 30 years** (stored at 16–25 °C, 20–50 % RH).
> - **3-2-1 Rule**: 3 copies, 2 different media, 1 offsite/offline.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Offline Backup Methods

Offline backups are not a single monolithic technique. The KTU 2024 module distinguishes four principal variants:

### A. Cold Backup (True Offline)
- Performed with the application/database **shut down** or quiesced.
- Guarantees **full data consistency** because no transactions are in-flight.
- Downtime cost = the entire backup window.
- **Used for:** databases, mail servers, ERP systems.

### B. Warm Backup (Hybrid)
- The system stays online in *read-only* or *quiesced* mode.
- Filesystem snapshots are taken first (online), then *exported* to offline media.
- Compromise between consistency and availability.

### C. Offsite Tape Vaulting
- After the backup finishes, tapes are **physically transported** to a secure offsite vault (e.g., Iron Mountain).
- The transit window is the only period of vulnerability.

### D. Air-Gapped Removable Disk
- Modern incarnation: USB HDDs or RDX cartridges inserted only during the backup window.
- Ejected (logically unmounted AND physically removed) when backup completes.

---

## 2.2 Operational Workflow — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Pre-Backup Verification** → confirm tape cartridge health via *write-test* or *tape-read diagnostic*.
2. **Application Quiesce** → flush buffers, lock tables, suspend I/O.
3. **Mount Medium** → load tape into robotic library or insert RDX cartridge.
4. **Data Read** → backup server streams data blocks from primary storage over SAN/NAS.
5. **Data Write** → backup software (e.g., NetBackup, Veeam, Commvault) writes sequentially to tape.
6. **Verification Pass** → optional read-back to compute checksums (CRC-32C, SHA-256).
7. **Dismount & Label** → eject media, apply bar-code / RFID label with timestamp.
8. **Catalog Update** → metadata recorded in the backup software's *catalog database*.
9. **Transport to Vault** → if offsite policy applies, courier or robot retrieves cartridge.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" behind Step 5 (Sequential Write):**
> Tape drives are *sequential-access* devices. Random I/O is hundreds of times slower than sequential streaming. Hence the backup window is dominated by the **sustained transfer rate**, not seek latency. If the source cannot sustain the feed rate, the drive performs **back-hitching** (stopping and repositioning), which degrades both throughput and tape life.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are routinely tested in KTU Numerical/Analytical questions.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Backup Window ($T_{bw}$)** | $T_{bw} = \dfrac{D_{eff}}{R_{seq}}$ | seconds (s) | $D_{eff}$ = effective data to back up, $R_{seq}$ = sustained transfer rate |
| 2 | **Effective Data After Compression** | $D_{eff} = \dfrac{D_{raw}}{C_r}$ | bytes | $C_r \geq 1$ is the compression ratio |
| 3 | **Number of Tapes Required** | $N_t = \left\lceil \dfrac{D_{eff}}{C_{tape}} \right\rceil$ | integer | $C_{tape}$ = native cartridge capacity |
| 4 | **Cost-per-Terabyte ($/TB)** | $\$/\text{TB} = \dfrac{C_{media}}{C_{tape}}$ | USD / TB | Used to compare media economically |
| 5 | **RPO Estimation** | $RPO = T_{bw} + T_{schedule}$ | time | Worst-case data loss window |
| 6 | **RTO Lower Bound** | $RTO \geq T_{retrieve} + T_{restore}$ | time | $T_{retrieve}$ = vault retrieval time |
| 7 | **Throughput Efficiency ($\eta$)** | $\eta = \dfrac{\bar{R}_{actual}}{R_{native}}$ | dimensionless | Accounts for back-hitching, verify-passes |
| 8 | **Tape Usage Ratio (TUR)** | $TUR = \dfrac{\text{Compressed data}}{\text{Native capacity}}$ | dimensionless | Target $\leq 1$ for healthy backup |
| 9 | **Media Shelf Life (Arrhenius)** | $t_{life} = t_{ref} \cdot 2^{\frac{T_{ref}-T_{storage}}{10}}$ | years | "Rule of thumb" — every 10 °C drop doubles life |
| 10 | **3-2-1 Storage Requirement** | $C_{total} \geq 3 \cdot D_{prod}$ | bytes | Three full copies of production data |

> **Units Table (commonly confused):**
> - $1 \text{ TB} = 10^{12}$ bytes (decimal — used by tape vendors)
> - $1 \text{ TiB} = 2^{40}$ bytes (binary — used by OS)
> - $1 \text{ MB/s} = 10^{6}$ bytes/s (decimal throughput)

---

## 2.4 Engineering Utility & Real-World Application

| Industry | Offline Backup Use-Case |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Banking & Finance** | End-of-day tape archival for SEBI/RBI audit compliance. |
| **Healthcare (PACS)** | Long-term DICOM image archival on LTO tape (30-year retention). |
| **Cinema & Media** | Master-copy archival of raw 8K footage; tape chosen for $/TB. |
| **Government / Defence** | Air-gapped backup for classified networks (no internet/USB allowed). |
| **Research (CERN, ISRO)** | Petabyte-scale scientific data archival with periodic tape migration. |

---

## 2.5 Comparison Matrix: Offline vs Online vs Nearline

| Parameter | **Offline (Tape)** | **Online (Disk)** | **Nearline (Disk + Cache)** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Access Latency | **Minutes** (mount + load) | **< 1 ms** | **Seconds** |
| Cost / TB | **Lowest** | **Highest** | **Medium** |
| RPO Achievable | **Hours – Days** | **Seconds** | **Minutes** |
| Ransomware Safe? | **Yes (air-gap)** | **No** | **No** |
| Power Required at Rest | **None** | **Yes (24×7)** | **Yes** |
| Best For | Archival, DR | Production I/O | Recent backups |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Analysis

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Backup Window from First Principles

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **favourite KTU numerical question** (often 3–5 marks).

**Given:**
- Production database raw size: $D_{raw} = 8 \text{ TB}$ (decimal).
- Compression ratio achievable by backup software: $C_r = 2.5$ (typical for text/log files).
- LTO-9 drive native transfer rate: $R_{native} = 400 \text{ MB/s}$.
- Sustained throughput efficiency (after back-hitching & verify): $\eta = 0.70$.

**To Find:** Minimum backup window $T_{bw}$.

---

### Step 1 — Effective (Compressed) Data Size

The backup software compresses the raw data, so the *bytes that must actually be written* to tape are smaller.

$$
D_{eff} = \frac{D_{raw}}{C_r}
$$

Substituting values:

$$
D_{eff} = \frac{8 \text{ TB}}{2.5}
$$

$$
D_{eff} = 3.2 \text{ TB}
$$

Convert to bytes (decimal SI definition, as tape vendors use it):

$$
D_{eff} = 3.2 \times 10^{12} \text{ bytes}
$$

---

### Step 2 — Effective Transfer Rate

The drive nominally streams at $R_{native}$, but real-world back-hitching and verify passes reduce throughput.

$$
R_{actual} = \eta \cdot R_{native}
$$

$$
R_{actual} = 0.70 \times 400 \text{ MB/s}
$$

$$
R_{actual} = 280 \text{ MB/s} = 280 \times 10^{6} \text{ bytes/s}
$$

---

### Step 3 — Backup Window Calculation

Backup window is simply *bytes written* divided by *bytes written per second*:

$$
T_{bw} = \frac{D_{eff}}{R_{actual}}
$$

$$
T_{bw} = \frac{3.2 \times 10^{12}}{280 \times 10^{6}} \text{ seconds}
$$

$$
T_{bw} = \frac{3200}{280} \times 10^{3} \text{ seconds}
$$

$$
T_{bw} \approx 11.43 \times 10^{3} \text{ s}
$$

$$
T_{bw} \approx 11{,}429 \text{ seconds}
$$

Convert to hours:

$$
T_{bw} \approx \frac{11{,}429}{3600} \text{ h} \approx 3.17 \text{ hours}
$$

---

### Step 4 — Number of LTO-9 Cartridges Required

LTO-9 native capacity = $C_{tape} = 18 \text{ TB}$.

$$
N_t = \left\lceil \frac{D_{eff}}{C_{tape}} \right\rceil
$$

$$
N_t = \left\lceil \frac{3.2}{18} \right\rceil = \lceil 0.178 \rceil = 1 \text{ cartridge}
$$

So **one** LTO-9 tape suffices for the compressed data.

---

### Final Answer Summary

$$
\boxed{\;T_{bw} \approx 3.17 \text{ hours}, \quad N_t = 1 \text{ LTO-9 cartridge}\;}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Tape Shelf Life via the Arrhenius "10 °C Rule"

**Problem Statement:** A backup tape is rated for **15 years** at a reference storage temperature of $T_{ref} = 25 \text{ °C}$. The actual vault is maintained at $T_{storage} = 15 \text{ °C}$. Estimate the new shelf life.

**Rule (Arrhenius approximation for magnetic media):**

$$
t_{life} = t_{ref} \cdot 2^{\frac{T_{ref} - T_{storage}}{10}}
$$

### Step 1 — Compute the Temperature Delta

$$
\Delta T = T_{ref} - T_{storage} = 25 - 15 = 10 \text{ °C}
$$

### Step 2 — Plug into the Formula

$$
t_{life} = 15 \cdot 2^{\frac{10}{10}} = 15 \cdot 2^{1}
$$

$$
t_{life} = 30 \text{ years}
$$

### Interpretation

A 10 °C drop in storage temperature **doubles** the archival life of the tape. This is why climate-controlled vaults (16–25 °C) are mandated in **ISO 18923 / ECMA-315** standards.

$$
\boxed{\;t_{life} \approx 30 \text{ years at 15 °C}\;}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — RPO and RTO Bound Analysis

A company performs a **full offline backup every Sunday at 02:00**, and the backup window from §3.1 is **3.17 hours**. On Wednesday at 11:00 AM, the primary site is destroyed.

**Recovery Point Objective (RPO) — worst-case data loss:**

$$
RPO = T_{bw} + T_{schedule}
$$

The schedule interval (Sunday → Wednesday) is **3 days = 72 h**.

$$
RPO = 3.17 + 72 \approx 75.17 \text{ hours}
$$

All transactions between **Sunday 05:11 AM** (end of backup) and **Wednesday 11:00 AM** are lost.

**Recovery Time Objective (RTO) — restore duration:**

Assume tape vault retrieval $T_{retrieve} = 1$ hour and restoration rate $R_{restore} = 280 \text{ MB/s}$.

$$
T_{restore} = \frac{D_{eff}}{R_{restore}} = \frac{3.2 \times 10^{12}}{280 \times 10^{6}} \approx 3.17 \text{ h}
$$

$$
RTO = T_{retrieve} + T_{restore} = 1 + 3.17 = 4.17 \text{ hours}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;RPO \approx 75.17 \text{ h}, \quad RTO \approx 4.17 \text{ h}\;}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** If the business requirement states $RPO \leq 1 \text{ hour}$, the offline-only scheme fails. The solution is to **augment** offline backup with **online incremental replication**, forming a *hybrid* backup architecture (still a Module 3 concept, but covered in detail in Module 4).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Offline Backup Workflow

The following **Mermaid block diagram** traces the complete lifecycle of an offline backup, from pre-flight checks through vault archival.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Backup Job Trigger] --> B[Pre-Flight Verify Tape Health]
    B --> C{Read/Write Diagnostic Pass?}
    C -->|No| D[Mark Tape BAD - Replace]
    D --> B
    C -->|Yes| E[Quiesce Application / DB]
    E --> F[Suspend User I/O]
    F --> G[Mount Tape in Drive]
    G --> H[Backup Server Streams Data]
    H --> I[Sequential Write to Tape]
    I --> J{Verify Pass Required?}
    J -->|Yes| K[Read-Back and Checksum]
    K --> L{Verify OK?}
    L -->|No| M[Mark Tape FAIL - Restart]
    M --> H
    L -->|Yes| N[Dismount and Eject Tape]
    J -->|No| N
    N --> O[Apply Barcode and Label]
    O --> P[Update Backup Catalog DB]
    P --> Q{Offsite Policy?}
    Q -->|Yes| R[Crypto-Erase Old Tape]
    R --> S[Robot Arm or Courier to Vault]
    S --> T[End: Tape Archived Offline]
    Q -->|No| T
```

---

## 4.2 Architecture Block Diagram — Production ↔ Backup ↔ Vault

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PROD[Production Site]
        P1[App Server 1] --> SAN[Primary SAN Storage]
        P2[App Server 2] --> SAN
        P3[DB Server] --> SAN
    end

    subgraph BKP[Backup Infrastructure]
        BKSVR[Backup Server - NetBackup/Veeam]
        LIB[Tape Library - LTO-9]
        BKSVR <--> LIB
    end

    subgraph VAULT[Offline Vault - Offsite]
        V1[Rack 1 - Weekly Tapes]
        V2[Rack 2 - Monthly Tapes]
        V3[Rack 3 - Yearly Tapes]
    end

    SAN ==Fiber Channel / iSCSI==> BKSVR
    LIB ==Physical Transport==> V1
    LIB ==Physical Transport==> V2
    LIB ==Physical Transport==> V3

    classDef prod fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000
    classDef bkp fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107,color:#000
    classDef vault fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,color:#000
    class P1,P2,P3,SAN prod
    class BKSVR,LIB bkp
    class V1,V2,V3 vault
```

---

## 4.3 Decision Flowchart — When to Choose Offline vs Online

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[New Backup Requirement] --> Q1{Data criticality level?}
    Q1 -->|Low - test/dev| Online[Online Disk Backup]
    Q1 -->|Medium| Q2{Need restore < 1 min?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| Online
    Q2 -->|No| Q3{Compliance mandates offline copy?}
    Q3 -->|No| Hybrid[Hybrid - Online + Weekly Offline]
    Q3 -->|Yes| Offline[Offline Tape Backup]
    Q1 -->|High - production DB| Q4{RPO < 15 min?}
    Q4 -->|Yes| OnlineRepl[Online Continuous Replication + Offline Daily]
    Q4 -->|No| Offline
    Q4 -->|RPO 1 to 24 h| Hybrid2[Online Incremental + Offline Weekly Full]
```

---

## 4.4 Data Flow Sequence — Write/Read on LTO Tape

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant APP as Application
    participant BSVR as Backup Server
    participant LIB as Tape Library
    participant TAPE as LTO-9 Cartridge

    APP->>BSVR: Initiate backup job (policy = FULL)
    BSVR->>LIB: Load cartridge slot 7 into drive 1
    LIB-->>BSVR: Drive ready, BOT marker read
    BSVR->>APP: Quiesce request
    APP-->>BSVR: I/O frozen at checkpoint
    loop Data Blocks
        BSVR->>LIB: Write 1 MB record
        LIB->>TAPE: Magnetic write via head
    end
    BSVR->>LIB: Verify (read-back 10 % sample)
    LIB-->>BSVR: CRC-32C checksum OK
    BSVR->>LIB: Unload drive, return to slot
    BSVR->>APP: Resume I/O
    Note over LIB,TAPE: Tape now air-gapped until next job
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this diagram matters for KTU:**
> Examiners frequently ask students to *"draw the offline backup architecture"* or *"explain the role of the catalog database"*. Memorising the four actors above (App, Backup Server, Library, Cartridge) is sufficient to sketch any variant asked in the ESE.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between offline backup and online backup. State any two scenarios where offline backup is preferred.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Offline Backup | Online Backup |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| System state | Application shut down / quiesced | Application running |
| Media connection | Removed/disconnected after backup | Always connected |
| Data consistency | Guaranteed (atomic snapshot) | Depends on snapshot technology |
| Recovery latency | High (minutes to hours) | Low (seconds) |
| Cost per TB | Low (tape) | High (disk) |
| Use case | Archival, DR, compliance | Operational recovery |

**Two scenarios (1 mark each):**
1. **Ransomware-resilient archival** — An air-gapped tape cannot be encrypted by malware that only traverses the network.
2. **Regulatory compliance** — SEBI, RBI, HIPAA mandates immutable offline copies retained for 7–10 years.
3. *(Bonus)* Cost-sensitive archival of large scientific datasets.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Explain the 3-2-1 backup rule with reference to offline backups.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **3-2-1 rule** is an industry-standard data-protection guideline mandating:
- **3** copies of every important dataset (1 production + 2 backups).
- **2** different types of storage media (e.g., disk + tape), so a vulnerability in one medium cannot destroy all copies.
- **1** copy stored **offsite and offline**, immune to local disasters (fire, flood, ransomware).

In the KTU context, the "1 offline copy" is most commonly implemented via **LTO tape vaulting** or **RDX cartridges** held in a geographically separate, climate-controlled vault. This rule is the foundation of every modern DR plan and is **frequently asked as a 3-mark question**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> As per the **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern**, Module-3 questions carry 14 marks with an internal choice. Two fully solved alternatives are provided below.

---

### Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe in detail the **operational procedure of an offline tape backup** for a 5 TB enterprise database server. Include pre-backup checks, application quiesce, write process, verification, and vault archival.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Calculate the **backup window, number of LTO-9 cartridges, and RPO** for the following scenario:
- Raw database size: $D_{raw} = 12 \text{ TB}$
- Compression ratio: $C_r = 2.0$
- Drive native rate: $R_{native} = 400 \text{ MB/s}$
- Efficiency: $\eta = 0.65$
- Backup schedule: weekly full, taken every Sunday 01:00
- Disaster occurred on Thursday 09:00

---

#### Model Solution to A(a) — Operational Procedure [7 marks]

1. **Pre-Backup Health Check (1 mark):**
   - Inspect tape cartridge for physical damage; run library *cleaning cycle* if the head-cleaning counter exceeds threshold.
   - Execute a *write-read diagnostic* on the target cartridge. If CRC mismatch > 0, mark cartridge as BAD and select next one from inventory.

2. **Application Quiesce (1 mark):**
   - Issue `ALTER SYSTEM QUIESCE RESTRICTED` (Oracle) or `dbcc quiesce` (SQL Server) to freeze user transactions.
   - Flush OS page cache to disk (`sync; echo 3 > /proc/sys/vm/drop_caches` on Linux).
   - Lock filesystems to read-only at the OS layer.

3. **Mount and Stream (1.5 marks):**
   - Robotic library picks cartridge from slot N, loads into drive D.
   - Backup server opens a *data pipe* from SAN LUNs to tape device using a protocol such as **NDMP (Network Data Management Protocol)** to offload data from the SAN fabric.
   - Data is read in 1 MB blocks, optionally compressed in-line, and written sequentially.

4. **Verification Pass (1 mark):**
   - Re-read the last written record and compare SHA-256 checksum with the in-memory value.
   - Some enterprises perform a **full read-back verification** on a separate drive — extremely safe but doubles the backup window.

5. **Dismount and Label (0.5 mark):**
   - Write tape mark (filemark), rewind to BOT (Beginning of Tape), and eject.
   - Apply a barcode label: `SITE-<date>-<jobid>-<seq>`.

6. **Catalog and Vault (1.5 marks):**
   - Update backup catalog with: media ID, start/end time, byte count, checksum, retention class.
   - If retention expired on the previous weekly tape, trigger *crypto-erase* (LTO-9 supports *crypto-erase* by destroying the data-encryption key in < 1 second).
   - Move tape to offsite vault via secure courier; log chain-of-custody.

7. **Application Resume (0.5 mark):**
   - Release the quiesce; allow user transactions to resume.

---

#### Model Solution to A(b) — Numerical [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Effective Data (1 mark):**

$$
D_{eff} = \frac{D_{raw}}{C_r} = \frac{12 \text{ TB}}{2.0} = 6 \text{ TB}
$$

Convert: $D_{eff} = 6 \times 10^{12}$ bytes.

**Step 2 — Effective Transfer Rate (1 mark):**

$$
R_{actual} = \eta \cdot R_{native} = 0.65 \times 400 \text{ MB/s} = 260 \text{ MB/s} = 260 \times 10^{6} \text{ bytes/s}
$$

**Step 3 — Backup Window (2 marks):**

$$
T_{bw} = \frac{D_{eff}}{R_{actual}} = \frac{6 \times 10^{12}}{260 \times 10^{6}}
$$

$$
T_{bw} = 23{,}076.9 \text{ s} \approx 6.41 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 4 — Number of LTO-9 Cartridges (1 mark):**

LTO-9 native capacity = 18 TB.

$$
N_t = \left\lceil \frac{6}{18} \right\rceil = 1 \text{ cartridge}
$$

**Step 5 — RPO Calculation (2 marks):**

Schedule interval = Sunday 01:00 → Thursday 09:00.

Days elapsed = 3 full days + 8 hours = **80 hours**.

Backup completion time = Sunday 01:00 + 6.41 h ≈ Sunday 07:25.

$$
RPO = T_{bw} + T_{schedule} = 6.41 + 80 = 86.41 \text{ hours}
$$

**Final Answer:**

$$
\boxed{\;T_{bw} \approx 6.41 \text{ h}, \quad N_t = 1 \text{ cartridge}, \quad RPO \approx 86.41 \text{ h}\;}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part B - 14 mark questions):**
> Students frequently lose **2–3 marks** because they:
> 1. Forget to **convert TB → bytes** using the decimal SI multiplier ($10^{12}$), not the binary $2^{40}$. Tape vendors always quote decimal TB.
> 2. Apply compression ratio in the wrong direction (writing $D_{raw} \times C_r$ instead of $D_{raw} / C_r$).
> 3. Omit the **verify-pass overhead** from the throughput (i.e., assume $\eta = 1.0$).
> 4. Define RPO as *only* the schedule interval, missing the backup window. Always: $RPO = T_{bw} + T_{schedule}$.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (ALTERNATIVE CHOICE) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **concept of an air-gapped backup** and discuss how it provides defense against modern ransomware attacks. Mention at least two technical mechanisms to achieve air-gapping.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A hospital PACS system generates **2 TB of medical images per day**. Images are retained for **10 years**. Compute:
- Total storage required after 10 years (raw, uncompressed).
- Tape cost using LTO-9 at ₹ 4,500 per 18 TB cartridge, assuming 1.5× compression.
- Whether an **online disk-only** strategy (₹ 12 per GB-month) is cheaper or costlier than tape for the 10-year horizon.

---

#### Model Solution to B(a) — Air-Gap Concept [7 marks]

**Definition (1 mark):**
An **air-gapped backup** is a backup copy that has *no electronic path* to the production network. Even if every online system is compromised, the air-gapped copy remains unreachable.

**Ransomware Threat Model (2 marks):**
Modern strains (LockBit, BlackCat, Conti) explicitly search for:
- SMB/NFS shares labelled "backup".
- Veeam/Commvault repositories.
- Cloud sync folders (OneDrive, Dropbox).

They also use **double-extortion**: encrypting data *and* exfiltrating it for public leak.

**Defense Mechanism — Air Gap (2 marks):**
Because the medium is electronically isolated, malware cannot reach it through any IP-based vector. Recovery is possible by simply *re-inserting* the medium into a clean machine.

**Two Technical Mechanisms to Achieve Air-Gap (2 marks):**

1. **Tape-based air gap:** Tapes sit in a robotic library but are ejected to a *secure vault* (different building) after each backup. Network path is broken at the physical layer.
2. **Removable disk air gap:** RDX cartridges or USB HDDs are inserted only during the backup window and then physically removed. Linux `udisksctl power-off` ensures the USB controller is also electrically dormant.
3. *(Bonus — software-defined air gap):* Write-Once-Read-Many (WORM) storage or object-lock on S3 buckets prevents *deletion* for a defined retention period.

---

#### Model Solution to B(b) — Hospital PACS Cost Analysis [7 marks]

**Given:**
- $D_{day} = 2 \text{ TB/day}$
- Retention $T = 10 \text{ years} = 3650 \text{ days}$
- Compression $C_r = 1.5$
- Tape cost = ₹ 4,500 per 18 TB cartridge
- Disk cost = ₹ 12 per GB-month

**Step 1 — Total Raw Data (1.5 marks):**

$$
D_{total} = D_{day} \times T = 2 \times 3650 = 7300 \text{ TB} = 7.3 \text{ PB}
$$

**Step 2 — Compressed Data (1 mark):**

$$
D_{compressed} = \frac{7300}{1.5} \approx 4867 \text{ TB}
$$

**Step 3 — Tape Cost (2 marks):**

Number of LTO-9 cartridges:

$$
N_t = \left\lceil \frac{4867}{18} \right\rceil = \lceil 270.4 \rceil = 271 \text{ cartridges}
$$

$$
\text{Tape Cost} = 271 \times 4500 = \text{₹ } 12{,}19{,}500 \approx \text{₹ } 12.2 \text{ lakh (one-time)}
$$

Plus annual vaulting cost (assume ₹ 50,000/year × 10 = ₹ 5 lakh).

$$
\text{Total Tape TCO}_{10yr} \approx \text{₹ } 17.2 \text{ lakh}
$$

**Step 4 — Disk Cost (2 marks):**

Assume average disk occupancy over 10 years = half of final capacity = 3.65 PB ≈ 3,650,000 GB.

Disk-months (assuming data grows linearly):

$$
\text{Avg occupancy} = \frac{7300 \text{ TB}}{2} = 3650 \text{ TB} = 3.65 \text{ PB}
$$

For 10 years (120 months):

$$
\text{Disk Cost} = 3.65 \times 10^{6} \text{ GB} \times 120 \text{ months} \times \text{₹ } 12 / \text{GB-month}
$$

$$
\text{Disk Cost} = 3.65 \times 10^{6} \times 120 \times 12 = \text{₹ } 525.6 \text{ crore}
$$

(Even using a more conservative averaging, disk cost is orders of magnitude higher.)

**Step 5 — Conclusion (0.5 mark):**

$$
\boxed{\;\text{Tape is approx. } 3000\times \text{ cheaper than disk for 10-year archival.}\;}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part B - 14 mark questions):**
> For cost-comparison questions, students frequently:
> 1. Forget to apply **compression to disk cost** too (if applicable) — here compression applies only to tape.
> 2. Compute disk cost using *final capacity only* instead of **time-averaged occupancy**.
> 3. Miss including **recurring operational costs** (power, vault rent, courier) for tape.
> 4. Forget to convert TB ↔ GB using the correct multiplier ($1 \text{ TB} = 1000 \text{ GB}$ for billing).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Read this the night before the exam.**

- ✅ **Offline backup** = backup whose medium is *physically disconnected* from production after write.
- ✅ **Cold backup** requires system shutdown; **warm backup** uses quiesce/snapshot.
- ✅ The **3-2-1 rule**: 3 copies, 2 media types, 1 offsite/offline.
- ✅ **Air-gapping** is the only ransomware-proof backup strategy at the physical layer.
- ✅ **LTO-9 specs:** 18 TB native, 45 TB compressed, 400 MB/s native, 30-year shelf life.
- ✅ **Backup window** formula: $T_{bw} = \dfrac{D_{raw}/C_r}{\eta \cdot R_{native}}$
- ✅ **Tape cartridges** $N_t = \left\lceil \dfrac{D_{eff}}{C_{tape}} \right\rceil$
- ✅ **RPO** = $T_{bw}$ + schedule interval (worst-case data loss window).
- ✅ **RTO** = retrieval time + restore time (must meet business SLA).
- ✅ **Arrhenius rule:** every 10 °C drop doubles magnetic media shelf life.
- ✅ Use **NDMP protocol** to offload backup traffic from the LAN.
- ✅ **Crypto-erase** destroys the data-encryption key on LTO-9 in < 1 s — used for fast, secure tape retirement.
- ✅ Always **verify** with checksum (CRC-32C, SHA-256) — never trust a backup you haven't tested.
- ✅ **Catalog database** is the *index* of all backup jobs; losing it = losing all knowledge of backup contents.
- ✅ Tape uses **sequential access** → back-hitching on slow source streams degrades throughput.
- ✅ Online + offline = **hybrid** is the modern best-practice architecture (Module 4 builds on this).

---

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Offline Backups | KTU 2024 Scheme**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: LAN-Based Backups
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST867 - STORAGE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** LAN-Based Backups

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **LAN-Based Backup** is a centralized data protection architecture in which backup data from application hosts (clients) is transported **over the existing Local Area Network (LAN)** infrastructure to a centralized **backup server** (also called a *media server* or *backup master*), which then writes the data to backup media (tape library, VTL, or disk-based target). The backup traffic shares the same Ethernet/IP network fabric used for normal client–server communication.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, this falls under the **traditional/native backup topology** classification in storage system design, characterized by:

- **Backup Initiator** — the application host whose data is being protected.
- **Backup Server (Media Server)** — orchestrates the backup job, manages the backup device, and acts as the data mover.
- **LAN Fabric** — Ethernet/IP network (typically 1 Gbps / 10 Gbps) shared between production traffic and backup traffic.
- **Backup Target** — tape autoloader, tape library, Virtual Tape Library (VTL), or disk-based backup appliance.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy — "The Office Courier Service"**
>
> Imagine a large office building where every department generates important paper documents. Instead of building a **separate private pneumatic tube system** (this would be a *SAN-based backup*) to send documents directly to the archive room, the office simply uses the **existing internal mailroom corridor** (the LAN). Every morning, a courier (the *backup agent*) picks up the documents from each department, walks them down the **same hallway** the employees use, and delivers them to the central archive (the *tape library*).
>
> The corridor gets congested at peak hours — exactly the trade-off you face in real LAN-based backups. The hallway is *cheap* (no new infrastructure), but during the **backup window**, it becomes a bottleneck.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following standard metrics govern LAN-based backup performance:

- **LAN Throughput:** 1 Gbps (≈ **125 MB/s**), 10 Gbps (≈ **1250 MB/s**), 25/40/100 Gbps for modern data centers.
- **Backup Window:** The pre-defined time period (typically nights/weekends) during which backups must complete.
- **Protocol Overhead:** TCP/IP adds roughly **3–5%** overhead due to headers, ACKs, and retransmissions.
- **MTU (Maximum Transmission Unit):** Standard Ethernet MTU = **1500 bytes**; Jumbo Frames = **9000 bytes**.

### 1.4 Syllabus Highlight Callouts

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Module 3 — Key Focus Areas**
> - Identify why LAN is the *cheapest* but *slowest* backup topology.
> - Contrast **server-based** vs. **serverless** LAN backup variants.
> - Compute backup window feasibility using throughput equations.
> - Recognize the role of **deduplication** and **compression** in reducing LAN burden.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown

LAN-based backup operation can be broken down into **six structured logic steps**:

- **Step 1 — Agent Initialization**
  *A backup agent* (software module, e.g., NetBackup client, TSM BA) installed on the application host registers with the central backup server over the LAN using TCP port ranges (commonly **13782, 13720** for Veritas, **1500** for TSM).

- **Step 2 — Job Scheduling & Policy Lookup**
  The backup server consults its policy database to determine *what* to back up, *when*, and *where to*. This metadata exchange consumes negligible bandwidth but is essential for orchestration.

- **Step 3 — Data Read (at the Client)**
  The agent reads the file system blocks or raw LUN sectors from local or attached storage. This is **I/O bound** at the client side.

- **Step 4 — Data Transmission over LAN**
  The data is encapsulated into TCP/IP packets and sent across the LAN. **This is the bottleneck step** because the LAN must simultaneously carry production traffic.

- **Step 5 — Data Reception & Write (at the Backup Server)**
  The media server receives the TCP stream, optionally applies **client-side or server-side deduplication** and **compression**, then writes to tape or backup-to-disk (B2D) target.

- **Step 6 — Catalog Update & Verification**
  The backup server updates its catalog/index database and may perform checksum verification.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" Behind Each Step**
> Steps 3 and 4 are the two **competing bottlenecks**. If client disk I/O is slow, no amount of LAN bandwidth helps. If LAN is saturated by production traffic, even a fast disk cannot stream data. The backup window must accommodate the **slower of the two**.

### 2.2 Backup Architecture Variants under LAN-Based Model

| Variant | Description | Data Path |
|---|---|---|
| **Server-Based Backup** | Backup server acts as the data mover; data flows Client → LAN → Backup Server → Tape | Two LAN hops (read + write) |
| **Serverless Backup (LAN-Free variant bridge)** | Application host writes directly to a shared SCSI/Fibre Channel tape device visible on the LAN via gateway; the backup server only controls the job | Client → Tape (LAN used for control only) |
| **NDMP-Based Backup** | Network Data Management Protocol (port **10000**) lets a NAS filer stream data directly to a tape device, bypassing the application server's CPU | NAS → LAN → Tape (no app server CPU) |

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas are mathematically verifiable and aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme numerical problems.**

$$
T_{\text{backup}} \;=\; \frac{D_{\text{logical}}}{R_{\text{eff}}} \;\times\; k
$$

where:

- $T_{\text{backup}}$ = total backup time in seconds
- $D_{\text{logical}}$ = logical (un-deduplicated) data size in **MB**
- $R_{\text{eff}}$ = effective throughput in **MB/s** (minimum of LAN throughput, server write speed, and client read speed)
- $k$ = overhead factor (typically **1.05 to 1.20** to account for metadata, catalog, network ACKs)

**Compression ratio:**

$$
C_{\text{ratio}} \;=\; \frac{D_{\text{original}}}{D_{\text{compressed}}}
$$

**Deduplication ratio:**

$$
D_{\text{ratio}} \;=\; \frac{D_{\text{logical}}}{D_{\text{physical}}}
$$

**Effective data on LAN (after dedup + compression):**

$$
D_{\text{LAN}} \;=\; \frac{D_{\text{logical}}}{C_{\text{ratio}} \cdot D_{\text{ratio}}}
$$

**Network Utilization during backup window:**

$$
U_{\text{net}} \;=\; \left(\frac{R_{\text{backup}} + R_{\text{prod}}}{R_{\text{LAN}}}\right) \times 100\%
$$

**Required LAN bandwidth:**

$$
R_{\text{req}} \;=\; \frac{D_{\text{LAN}}}{T_{\text{window}}}
$$

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Small-to-Medium Enterprises (SMEs):** LAN-based backup is the **default** choice because no specialized SAN infrastructure is required. Cost-of-ownership is low.
- **Branch Offices:** Centralized LAN backup to a corporate data center over WAN-optimized links.
- **Legacy Systems:** Environments lacking Fibre Channel SANs rely on LAN-based backup.
- **Cloud Gateways:** Modern Veeam, Commvault, and Rubrik deployments often begin as LAN-based and evolve to LAN-free or SAN-based as data grows.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Derivation 1 — Backup Window Feasibility

> **Problem Statement (KTU Numerical Pattern):**
> A company has **3 TB** of logical data to back up nightly. The LAN is **1 Gbps Ethernet**. The backup server can write to tape at **80 MB/s**. The client can read from disk at **120 MB/s**. The data has a deduplication ratio of **4:1** and compression ratio of **1.5:1**. The protocol overhead factor is **k = 1.10**. Determine whether the backup completes within a **6-hour** backup window.

**Step 1 — Identify the Effective Throughput**

The effective throughput is the **minimum** of three bottlenecks:

$$
R_{\text{LAN}} \;=\; \frac{1\,\text{Gbps}}{8} \;\approx\; 125\;\text{MB/s}
$$

$$
R_{\text{server write}} \;=\; 80\;\text{MB/s}
$$

$$
R_{\text{client read}} \;=\; 120\;\text{MB/s}
$$

$$
R_{\text{eff}} \;=\; \min(125,\; 80,\; 120) \;=\; 80\;\text{MB/s}
$$

**Why?** The backup server's tape write speed of 80 MB/s is the slowest link, so it caps the entire pipeline. Even if LAN and disk are faster, the data cannot leave the server faster than 80 MB/s.

**Step 2 — Compute Effective Data on LAN**

$$
D_{\text{logical}} \;=\; 3\,\text{TB} \;=\; 3 \times 1024 \times 1024\;\text{MB} \;=\; 3{,}145{,}728\;\text{MB}
$$

$$
D_{\text{LAN}} \;=\; \frac{D_{\text{logical}}}{C_{\text{ratio}} \cdot D_{\text{ratio}}} \;=\; \frac{3{,}145{,}728}{1.5 \times 4} \;\text{MB}
$$

$$
D_{\text{LAN}} \;=\; \frac{3{,}145{,}728}{6.0} \;=\; 524{,}288\;\text{MB}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Overhead Factor**

$$
D_{\text{total}} \;=\; D_{\text{LAN}} \times k \;=\; 524{,}288 \times 1.10
$$

$$
D_{\text{total}} \;=\; 576{,}716.8\;\text{MB}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Backup Time**

$$
T_{\text{backup}} \;=\; \frac{D_{\text{total}}}{R_{\text{eff}}} \;=\; \frac{576{,}716.8}{80}
$$

$$
T_{\text{backup}} \;=\; 7{,}208.96\;\text{seconds}
$$

Converting to hours:

$$
T_{\text{backup}} \;=\; \frac{7{,}208.96}{3600} \;\approx\; 2.00\;\text{hours}
$$

**Step 5 — Compare with Backup Window**

$$
T_{\text{backup}} \;=\; 2.00\,\text{hours} \;\le\; T_{\text{window}} \;=\; 6.00\,\text{hours}
$$

**Conclusion:** Backup **completes successfully** with a comfortable margin of **4 hours** to spare.

---

### 3.2 Worked Derivation 2 — Network Utilization Analysis

> **Problem Statement:**
> During the backup window, the LAN (1 Gbps) carries **60 MB/s** of production traffic in addition to the 80 MB/s backup stream. Calculate the network utilization.

**Step 1 — Sum all traffic**

$$
R_{\text{total}} \;=\; R_{\text{backup}} + R_{\text{prod}} \;=\; 80 + 60
$$

$$
R_{\text{total}} \;=\; 140\;\text{MB/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert LAN capacity to MB/s**

$$
R_{\text{LAN}} \;=\; \frac{1000\,\text{Mbps}}{8} \;=\; 125\;\text{MB/s}
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate Utilization**

$$
U_{\text{net}} \;=\; \left(\frac{140}{125}\right) \times 100\%
$$

$$
U_{\text{net}} \;=\; 112\%
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Result > 100% means the LAN is OVERSATURATED.** This is a classic failure scenario — the backup cannot complete because the network is congested. The remedy is to upgrade to **10 Gbps** LAN, schedule backup during a lower-traffic window, or migrate to a **SAN-based / LAN-free** architecture.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Backup Topologies

| Parameter | LAN-Based | LAN-Free (Serverless) | SAN-Based |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Network Used** | Production LAN | Storage SAN (FC) | Dedicated backup SAN |
| **Impact on Production** | **High** | Low | **Negligible** |
| **Infrastructure Cost** | **Low** | Medium | High |
| **Backup Throughput** | 80–125 MB/s | 200–800 MB/s | 400–1600 MB/s |
| **Scalability** | Limited by LAN BW | High | Very High |
| **Suitable For** | SMEs, branch offices | Mid-size enterprises | Large data centers |
| **Complexity** | **Low** | Medium | High |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All node IDs in the following diagrams are purely **alphanumeric** (prefixed with letters). No reserved Mermaid keywords (`end`, `graph`, `subgraph`, `style`) are used as node IDs. All labels are clean uppercase text without markdown formatting symbols.

### 4.2 LAN-Based Backup Architecture — Block-Level Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[APP SERVER 1] --> N1
    A2[APP SERVER 2] --> N1
    A3[APP SERVER 3] --> N1
    N1[LAN SWITCH 1 Gbps] --> B1
    B1[BACKUP SERVER MEDIA SERVER] --> T1
    B1 --> T2
    T1[TAPE LIBRARY LTO]
    T2[DISK BASED VTL]
    CAT[CATALOG DATABASE] --- B1
    SCHED[BACKUP SCHEDULER] --- B1
```

### 4.3 Data Flow Sequence — LAN-Based Backup Job

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[STEP 01 AGENT REGISTRATION] --> P2[STEP 02 POLICY LOOKUP]
    P2 --> P3[STEP 03 CLIENT READS DATA]
    P3 --> P4[STEP 04 DATA OVER LAN TCP IP]
    P4 --> P5[STEP 05 DEDUP AND COMPRESS]
    P5 --> P6[STEP 06 WRITE TO TAPE]
    P6 --> P7[STEP 07 CATALOG UPDATE]
    P7 --> P8[STEP 08 VERIFY CHECKSUM]
```

### 4.4 Decision Matrix — When to Choose LAN-Based

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{DATA VOLUME UNDER 5 TB} -->|YES| Q2{LAN BANDWIDTH SUFFICIENT}
    Q1 -->|NO| Q3[CONSIDER LAN FREE OR SAN BASED]
    Q2 -->|YES| R1[CHOOSE LAN BASED BACKUP]
    Q2 -->|NO| Q4[UPGRADE LAN OR USE LAN FREE]
    Q3 --> R2[RECOMMEND SAN BASED]
    Q4 --> R3[RECOMMEND LAN FREE]
```

### 4.5 LAN Saturation Scenario — Visual Bottleneck

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PROD[PRODUCTION TRAFFIC 60 MBps] --> SW[LAN SWITCH 1 Gbps]
    BACK[BACKUP TRAFFIC 80 MBps] --> SW
    SW --> CONG[CONGESTED LINK 140 MBps OVER 125 MBps CAPACITY]
    CONG --> X[BACKUP FAILURE]
    style CONG fill:#ffcccb
    style X fill:#ff9999
```

> [!NOTE]
> The red-tinted nodes highlight the **bottleneck point** where the combined traffic exceeds the LAN capacity, leading to backup job failure or severe slowdown.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Pattern]**

Define **LAN-based backup**. List any **two advantages** and **two disadvantages** of using a LAN as the data path for backups.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
LAN-based backup is a backup topology in which backup data is transferred from application hosts to a centralized backup server over the existing **Local Area Network (LAN)** infrastructure. The backup server then writes the data to a tape library, VTL, or disk-based backup target.

**Two Advantages (1 mark each):**

- **Low Infrastructure Cost:** No additional SAN fabric, HBAs, or dedicated switches are required. Existing Ethernet cabling and switches are reused.
- **Simple Management:** Backup jobs are orchestrated from a single media server, making policy administration straightforward. Suitable for small and medium enterprises.

**Two Disadvantages (1 mark for any two):**

- **Network Congestion:** Backup traffic competes with production traffic, potentially saturating the LAN and degrading application performance.
- **Limited Scalability:** As data volume grows, the LAN becomes a bottleneck. 1 Gbps Ethernet caps throughput at approximately 125 MB/s, which may be insufficient for multi-TB environments.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Jun 2024, Model Question Pattern]**

Differentiate between **server-based** and **serverless (LAN-free) backup** with respect to the role of the backup server.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Server-Based Backup | Serverless (LAN-Free) Backup |
|---|---|---|
| **Role of Backup Server** | Backup server is the **data mover**; it reads data from the client, transfers it across the LAN, and writes to the backup device | Backup server acts only as a **job coordinator**; it sends SCSI/FC commands but the **client writes data directly** to the shared tape device |
| **Data Path** | Client → LAN → Backup Server → Tape | Client → SAN/Shared Bus → Tape (LAN used only for control signals) |
| **LAN Usage** | **Heavy** (full data stream) | **Minimal** (only metadata/control) |
| **Server CPU Load** | **High** (server processes all data) | **Low** (server is bypassed for data) |

(Any two distinguishing points: 1.5 marks each)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout**
> - In 14-mark questions, students frequently **lose 2–3 marks** by:
>   - Skipping the **numerical unit conversions** (e.g., confusing bits vs. bytes, GB vs. GiB).
>   - Failing to identify the **minimum** of the three throughputs (LAN, client read, server write) — always state explicitly which is the bottleneck.
>   - Not applying the **overhead factor k** in backup-time calculations.
>   - Omitting the **deduplication AND compression** steps separately rather than combining them in a single calculation.
>   - Forgetting to **state assumptions** in derivation problems.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Set 1**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the **functional architecture of a LAN-based backup system** with a neat block diagram. Describe the role of each component in the data path. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A LAN-based backup system consists of the following functional blocks:

1. **Application Host (Client)** — contains a backup agent software that reads file system data or raw LUN data for protection. *(1 mark)*
2. **LAN Infrastructure (Ethernet Switch + Cabling)** — provides the shared IP network that carries both production and backup traffic. Typical speeds: 1 Gbps, 10 Gbps. *(1 mark)*
3. **Backup Server (Media Server)** — central orchestrator. Receives data from clients, applies optional deduplication/compression, and writes to backup media. Maintains the catalog database. *(2 marks)*
4. **Backup Target (Tape Library / VTL / B2D)** — stores the protected data. May be SCSI, FC, SAS, or iSCSI attached to the backup server. *(1 mark)*
5. **Catalog & Policy Database** — stores metadata about backed-up files, retention policies, schedules. *(1 mark)*
6. **Scheduler** — triggers backup jobs at predefined times. *(0.5 mark)*
7. **Verification Module** — performs checksum/hash validation post-backup. *(0.5 mark)*

**Data Path Summary:** Application Host → Agent → LAN Switch → Backup Server (dedup/compress) → Tape/VTL → Catalog Update.

---

**(b)** A data center has **5 TB** of logical data. The **deduplication ratio is 6:1** and the **compression ratio is 1.8:1**. The LAN operates at **10 Gbps**. The backup server writes to a tape library at **150 MB/s**, and the client reads from disk at **200 MB/s**. The protocol overhead factor is **k = 1.15**. Calculate:

  (i) The effective data transmitted over the LAN. **(3 marks)**
  (ii) The effective throughput. **(1 mark)**
  (iii) The total backup time in hours. **(3 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert logical data to MB**

$$
D_{\text{logical}} \;=\; 5\,\text{TB} \;=\; 5 \times 1024 \times 1024 \;\text{MB} \;=\; 5{,}242{,}880\;\text{MB}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate data on LAN after dedup + compression**

$$
D_{\text{LAN}} \;=\; \frac{D_{\text{logical}}}{D_{\text{ratio}} \times C_{\text{ratio}}} \;=\; \frac{5{,}242{,}880}{6 \times 1.8}
$$

$$
D_{\text{LAN}} \;=\; \frac{5{,}242{,}880}{10.8} \;=\; 485{,}452\;\text{MB}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply overhead factor**

$$
D_{\text{total}} \;=\; D_{\text{LAN}} \times k \;=\; 485{,}452 \times 1.15
$$

$$
D_{\text{total}} \;=\; 558{,}270\;\text{MB}
$$

**Step 4 — Determine Effective Throughput**

$$
R_{\text{LAN}} \;=\; \frac{10\,\text{Gbps}}{8} \;=\; 1250\;\text{MB/s}
$$

$$
R_{\text{eff}} \;=\; \min(1250,\; 150,\; 200) \;=\; 150\;\text{MB/s}
$$

The **tape write speed of 150 MB/s** is the bottleneck.

**Step 5 — Calculate Backup Time**

$$
T_{\text{backup}} \;=\; \frac{558{,}270}{150} \;=\; 3{,}721.8\;\text{seconds}
$$

$$
T_{\text{backup}} \;=\; \frac{3{,}721.8}{3600} \;\approx\; 1.034\;\text{hours}
$$

**Final Answer:** The total backup time is approximately **1.03 hours (≈ 62 minutes)**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Set 2 (Alternative Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024 Pattern, Adapted]**

**(a)** Compare and contrast **LAN-based backup** with **SAN-based backup** under the following heads: **(7 marks)**
   - (i) Network used for data transfer
   - (ii) Impact on production traffic
   - (iii) Cost of implementation
   - (iv) Typical throughput
   - (v) Scalability
   - (vi) Use case suitability
   - (vii) Complexity

**Model Answer (Tabular Form):**

| Head | LAN-Based Backup | SAN-Based Backup |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Network | Production LAN (Ethernet/IP) | Dedicated FC SAN fabric |
| (ii) Production Impact | High (contention) | Negligible (isolated fabric) |
| (iii) Cost | Low (reuses LAN) | High (HBAs, FC switches, cables) |
| (iv) Throughput | 80–125 MB/s (1 Gbps) | 400–1600 MB/s (4/8 Gbps FC) |
| (v) Scalability | Limited by LAN BW | Highly scalable |
| (vi) Suitable For | SMEs, small data sets | Enterprises, large data centers |
| (vii) Complexity | Low | High |

*(1 mark per correct row × 7 rows, but with internal scaling — examiners typically award 1 mark per head covering 2–3 lines of explanation, total 7 marks.)*

---

**(b)** A backup system uses a **1 Gbps LAN**. During a backup window, the **production traffic is 40 MB/s** and the **backup stream is 90 MB/s**. The deduplication ratio is **3:1** and compression ratio is **1.3:1**. Determine: **(7 marks)**

  (i) The LAN utilization percentage.
  (ii) Whether the backup will fail or succeed.
  (iii) If it fails, suggest **two remedies**.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute total traffic on LAN**

$$
R_{\text{total}} \;=\; R_{\text{prod}} + R_{\text{backup}} \;=\; 40 + 90 \;=\; 130\;\text{MB/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert LAN capacity**

$$
R_{\text{LAN}} \;=\; \frac{1000}{8} \;=\; 125\;\text{MB/s}
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate Utilization**

$$
U_{\text{net}} \;=\; \left(\frac{130}{125}\right) \times 100\% \;=\; 104\%
$$

**Step 4 — Verdict**

$$
U_{\text{net}} \;=\; 104\% \;>\; 100\%
$$

Since utilization **exceeds 100%**, the LAN is **oversaturated** and the backup **will experience severe degradation or failure**. *(3 marks for steps 1–4)*

**Step 5 — Two Remedies** *(2 marks)*

- **Remedy 1:** Upgrade the LAN from **1 Gbps to 10 Gbps**. This increases capacity tenfold, dropping utilization to ≈10.4% even with the same traffic.
- **Remedy 2:** Implement **client-side deduplication** to reduce backup traffic. With a 3:1 dedup and 1.3:1 compression:

$$
D_{\text{LAN,reduced}} \;=\; \frac{90}{3 \times 1.3} \;\approx\; 23.08\;\text{MB/s}
$$

New total = 40 + 23.08 ≈ 63 MB/s, well within 125 MB/s capacity.

*(Any other valid remedy: schedule backup during low-traffic window, migrate to SAN-based backup, throttle backup stream during business hours — 1 mark each.)*

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — LAN-Based Backups**

- **Definition:** LAN-based backup sends data over the production Ethernet LAN from clients to a central backup server, which writes to tape/VTL/B2D. *(High-yield 1-liner)*

- **Core Architecture Components:** Application host (with agent) + LAN switch + Backup/Media server + Backup target (tape/VTL) + Catalog database. *(Frequently asked in 3-mark questions)*

- **Bottleneck Identification Rule:** Always take the **MINIMUM** of (LAN throughput, client disk read, server write). The slowest component dictates the entire backup duration.

- **Unit Conversion Traps:**
  * $1\,\text{Gbps} = 125\,\text{MB/s}$ (divide by 8)
  * $1\,\text{TB} = 1{,}048{,}576\,\text{MB}$ (use binary, not decimal, for KTU problems unless stated otherwise)
  * $1\,\text{hour} = 3{,}600\,\text{seconds}$

- **Key Formulas (must memorize):**
  * $T_{\text{backup}} = \frac{D_{\text{logical}}}{R_{\text{eff}}} \times k$
  * $D_{\text{LAN}} = \frac{D_{\text{logical}}}{C_{\text{ratio}} \times D_{\text{ratio}}}$
  * $U_{\text{net}} = \left(\frac{R_{\text{backup}} + R_{\text{prod}}}{R_{\text{LAN}}}\right) \times 100\%$

- **Variants:** Server-based (data mover model) vs. Serverless (LAN-free, client writes directly to shared tape) vs. NDMP-based (NAS filer streams directly to tape).

- **Critical Pitfall:** If $U_{\text{net}} > 100\%$, the backup **fails** — always check utilization before declaring success.

- **Advantages:** Low cost, simple architecture, easy to deploy, suitable for SMEs.

- **Disadvantages:** LAN contention, limited scalability, performance impact on production traffic, single point of failure (central backup server).

- **When to Migrate:** If data volume exceeds ~5 TB, or backup window consistently overruns, switch to **LAN-free** or **SAN-based** backup.

- **Examiner's Favorite Questions:** (1) Backup window feasibility numerical, (2) Bottleneck identification, (3) LAN-based vs. SAN-based comparison table, (4) Role of dedup and compression in reducing LAN load.

- **Related KTU 2024 Module 3 Topics:** LAN-free backup, Serverless backup, NDMP, Backup topologies taxonomy, Deduplication technologies.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: LAN-Free Backups (SAN-Based)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST867 - STORAGE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** LAN-Free Backups (SAN-Based)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 3 — LAN-Free Backups (SAN-Based)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Aligned with EMC Information Storage & Management – Module 3):**
> **LAN-Free Backup** is a data protection strategy in which backup data travels over a **Storage Area Network (SAN)** — rather than the corporate **Local Area Network (LAN)** — directly between the **production host (application server)** and a **backup device** (such as a tape library or virtual tape library). This architecture offloads backup traffic from the production Ethernet LAN, freeing it for user/application traffic and dramatically improving backup/restore performance.

### 🔑 Conceptual Analogy (Intuitive Explanation)

Imagine your **office LAN** is a **single-lane city road** shared by office workers, emails, internet, and once-a-day a **huge moving truck** that carries the entire office furniture to a warehouse for backup.

- In a **traditional (LAN-based) backup**, that moving truck drives on the same single-lane road, **bringing office work to a complete halt** (network congestion, slow user response).
- In a **LAN-Free Backup**, the company builds a **dedicated highway (the SAN)**, and the moving truck uses *that* highway directly from the office building (production host) to the warehouse (tape library). The city road (LAN) is now free for the office workers.

**The SAN acts as a dedicated, high-speed "backup highway"** — built on Fibre Channel (typically **2 Gbps, 4 Gbps, 8 Gbps, 16 Gbps, or 32 Gbps** links) or iSCSI — that bypasses the Ethernet LAN entirely for data movement during backup windows.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology Used in KTU 2024 Scheme:**
> - **Production Host / Application Server:** The server whose data is being backed up.
> - **Backup Server / Media Server:** The orchestration engine that runs the backup software.
> - **Backup Device:** Typically a **Tape Library** (LTO Ultrium: **LTO-7, LTO-8, LTO-9**) or **Virtual Tape Library (VTL)** backed by disk.
> - **SAN (Storage Area Network):** A dedicated high-speed network (Fibre Channel or iSCSI) connecting servers to storage.
> - **LAN (Local Area Network):** The traditional Ethernet/IP network used for client-server communication.

### Why LAN-Free? — The Motivation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In traditional **LAN-based backup**, backup data flows: *Application Server → LAN → Backup Server → LAN → Tape Library*. This causes:
> 1. **LAN congestion** (bandwidth contention with user traffic).
> 2. **Longer backup windows** (LAN is typically **1 Gbps or 10 Gbps**, but shared).
> 3. **Degraded application performance** during backups.
> 4. **Scalability bottleneck** — adding more servers to back up means more LAN traffic.

LAN-Free Backup solves this by allowing the **production host to write directly to the backup device over the SAN**, while the **backup server** only sends control/metadata commands over the LAN (or even the SAN).

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 LAN-Based Backup — The Baseline

In a **LAN-based backup**, the data path is:

$$
\text{Application Host} \xrightarrow{\text{Data (LAN)}} \text{Backup Server} \xrightarrow{\text{Data (LAN)}} \text{Tape Library}
$$

- The **Backup Server** acts as both the orchestrator AND the data mover.
- All backup data **must traverse the LAN** (typically **1 Gbps / 10 Gbps Ethernet**).
- The LAN is shared with user/application traffic.

## 2.2 LAN-Free Backup — The Architecture

In a **LAN-Free Backup**, the data path becomes:

$$
\text{Application Host} \xrightarrow{\text{Data (SAN)}} \text{Backup Device (Tape/VTL)}
$$

- The **Backup Server** sends only **control commands** (start, stop, catalog) over the LAN.
- **Backup data travels over the SAN** (typically **4 Gbps / 8 Gbps / 16 Gbps FC**, or **10 GbE iSCSI**).
- The LAN is **free** of bulk backup data traffic.

### 2.3 Components of a LAN-Free Backup Architecture

| # | Component | Role in LAN-Free Backup |
|---|-----------|-------------------------|
| 1 | **Application/Production Host** | Source of data; runs backup **client agent** that reads data from its disks. |
| 2 | **SAN Fabric (FC Switches / iSCSI)** | Dedicated high-speed network carrying backup data to the backup device. |
| 3 | **Backup Server (Media Server)** | Orchestrates the backup; sends control commands; in some designs also acts as a data mover. |
| 4 | **Backup Device (Tape Library / VTL)** | Destination where backup data is written. Must be **SAN-attached** (not LAN-attached). |
| 5 | **Backup Software** | Manages scheduling, deduplication, catalog, restore (e.g., NetBackup, TSM, CommVault, Veeam). |
| 6 | **SAN-Attached Storage (Disk Array)** | Source disks holding production data, also on the SAN. |

## 2.4 Operational Steps (LAN-Free Backup)

> [!NOTE]
> Below is the **step-by-step data flow** that KTU examiners love to test.

1. **Backup Initiation:** Backup server sends a "start backup" control command over the LAN to the application host's backup client agent.
2. **Device Allocation:** The backup server reserves a tape drive or VTL slot in the SAN-attached backup device.
3. **Data Read (Host side):** The application host reads data blocks from its **SAN-attached production disks** over the SAN.
4. **Data Write (SAN transfer):** The host writes these blocks **directly across the SAN** to the allocated tape drive or VTL — **bypassing the LAN entirely**.
5. **Catalog Update:** The backup server receives metadata (file names, timestamps, checksums) over the LAN and updates its catalog.
6. **Completion:** Tape is finalized, write-protected, and indexed.

## 2.5 Variants of LAN-Free Backup

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 expects students to know these three variants:**

### Variant A: **Server-Based LAN-Free Backup**
- A **dedicated backup server (media server)** is attached to the SAN.
- The application host's data is sent over the SAN to the **backup server**, which then writes it to the tape library over the SAN.
- The backup server participates in the data path.

### Variant B: **Serverless LAN-Free Backup** *(often called "Server-Free Backup")*
- The application host writes data **directly** to the tape device over the SAN.
- A **backup server still exists** but only for control/orchestration.
- Uses **SCSI Extended Copy (XCOPY)** or **ODM (Offload Data Move)** commands.
- The application host's CPU is **NOT** involved in the data movement — a SAN-attached storage controller or appliance does the actual copy using **block-level snapshots + replication**.

### Variant C: **NDMP-Based LAN-Free Backup** (Network Data Management Protocol)
- Used for **NAS (filer) backups** (e.g., NetApp, EMC Isilon).
- The NAS filer reads data from its own disks and writes directly to a SAN-attached tape library.
- A backup server orchestrates via **TCP/IP (port 10000)** for control only.
- **Backup data never crosses the LAN** — only control commands do.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Symbol / Value | Unit | Notes |
|-----------|---------------|------|-------|
| Backup window (LAN-based) | $T_{LAN}$ | hours | Function of data size & LAN bandwidth |
| Backup window (LAN-Free) | $T_{LF}$ | hours | Function of data size & SAN bandwidth |
| LAN bandwidth (typical) | $B_{LAN}$ | **1 Gbps / 10 Gbps** | Shared with user traffic |
| SAN bandwidth (typical) | $B_{SAN}$ | **4 / 8 / 16 / 32 Gbps FC** or 10/25 GbE iSCSI | Dedicated to storage traffic |
| LTO tape native throughput | $R_{tape}$ | **LTO-8: 360 MB/s, LTO-9: 400 MB/s** | Per drive |
| LTO tape capacity (native) | $C_{tape}$ | **LTO-8: 12 TB, LTO-9: 18 TB** | Per cartridge |
| Theoretical backup time | $t = \dfrac{D}{B \times \eta}$ | hours | $D$=data, $B$=bandwidth, $\eta$=efficiency (0.6–0.8) |
| Throughput with N parallel drives | $T_{total} = \dfrac{D}{N \times R_{tape} \times \eta}$ | hours | $N$ = number of tape drives |
| FC link speeds (FC) | — | **1, 2, 4, 8, 16, 32, 64 Gbps** | Doubles every gen |
| iSCSI speeds | — | **1, 10, 25, 40, 100 Gbps** | Runs on Ethernet |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical exam note:** When KTU asks *"Why is LAN-Free backup faster than LAN-based?"*, the canonical answer is:
> 1. **Higher SAN bandwidth** vs. shared LAN.
> 2. **No contention** with user/application traffic.
> 3. **Offloads data movement** from backup server (in serverless mode).
> 4. **Parallelism** — multiple hosts can back up concurrently to multiple tape drives.

## 2.7 Real-World Utility

- **Enterprise data centers** (banking, telecom, healthcare) use LAN-Free backup to meet strict **RPO/RTO** Service Level Agreements (SLAs).
- **Regulatory compliance** (SEBI, HIPAA, GDPR) mandates frequent backups — LAN-Free enables nightly/weekly backups without impacting business hours.
- Enables **backup of multi-TB databases** (Oracle, SQL Server, SAP HANA) within tight windows.
- Foundation for modern **disk-based backup** (VTL) and **snapshot-based instant recovery**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Problem: Backup Window Estimation

> [!NOTE]
> KTU frequently asks: *"Given a data size, LAN/SAN bandwidth, and number of tape drives, compute the backup window."*

### 📘 Worked Example (Model KTU 14-Mark Style)

**Problem:** A company has **20 TB** of data to back up. They use **LTO-9 tape drives**, each with a **native throughput of 400 MB/s**. The SAN infrastructure is **16 Gbps Fibre Channel** with an **end-to-end efficiency $\eta = 0.70$**. The system uses **4 parallel tape drives** in a tape library.

**Compute:**
1. The **maximum sustained backup throughput** across all drives.
2. The **total backup window** in hours and minutes.
3. Compare it with a **LAN-based backup** over a **10 Gbps Ethernet** link with the same efficiency.

---

### 🧮 Solution — Step-by-Step Derivation

#### Part (a): Maximum sustained backup throughput

**Step 1 — Convert tape throughput to a common unit (MB/s).**

We have $R_{tape} = 400 \text{ MB/s}$ per drive.

**Step 2 — Account for SAN efficiency.**

End-to-end SAN efficiency includes FC encoding (8b/10b), protocol overhead, and tape device overhead. We are given $\eta = 0.70$.

$$
R_{effective\_per\_drive} = R_{tape} \times \eta = 400 \times 0.70 = 280 \text{ MB/s per drive}
$$

**Step 3 — Multiply by the number of parallel drives (N = 4).**

$$
R_{total} = N \times R_{effective\_per\_drive}
$$

$$
R_{total} = 4 \times 280 = 1120 \text{ MB/s}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert MB/s to a more engineering-friendly unit (GB/hour).**

$$
R_{total\_GBph} = 1120 \text{ MB/s} \times 3600 \text{ s/hr} \times \frac{1 \text{ GB}}{1024 \text{ MB}}
$$

$$
R_{total\_GBph} = \dfrac{1120 \times 3600}{1024} \text{ GB/hr}
$$

$$
R_{total\_GBph} = \dfrac{4{,}032{,}000}{1024} \approx 3937.5 \text{ GB/hr}
$$

$$
\boxed{R_{total} \approx 3.94 \text{ TB/hr}}
$$

#### Part (b): Total backup window

**Step 1 — Use the fundamental backup time formula:**

$$
T_{backup} = \frac{D}{R_{total}}
$$

where $D$ = total data = **20 TB**, $R_{total}$ in TB/hr.

**Step 2 — Convert units consistently.**

$$
T_{backup} = \frac{20 \text{ TB}}{3.94 \text{ TB/hr}}
$$

$$
T_{backup} \approx 5.08 \text{ hours}
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{backup} \approx 5 \text{ hours } 5 \text{ minutes}}
$$

#### Part (c): Comparison with LAN-based backup (10 GbE)

**Step 1 — LAN bandwidth in MB/s.**

10 Gbps Ethernet raw bit rate ≈ 10,000 Mbps. However, due to Ethernet framing and TCP/IP overhead:

$$
B_{LAN\_effective} = 10 \text{ Gbps} \times 0.70 = 7 \text{ Gbps}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert to MB/s.**

$$
B_{LAN\_MBps} = \frac{7 \times 1000}{8} \approx 875 \text{ MB/s}
$$

(We use 1000 here for "Mbps-to-MBps" since Gbps is decimal.)

**Step 3 — But the LAN is shared!** Effective backup throughput is reduced further because user traffic competes. Realistic assumption: **dedicated-to-backup portion = 50%**.

$$
B_{LAN\_backup} = 875 \times 0.50 \approx 437.5 \text{ MB/s}
$$

**Step 4 — LAN backup window (single stream, no parallel drives assumed).**

$$
T_{LAN} = \frac{20 \text{ TB} \times 1024 \text{ GB/TB}}{437.5 \text{ MB/s} \times 3600 \text{ s/hr}}
$$

$$
T_{LAN} = \frac{20480 \text{ GB}}{1{,}575{,}000 \text{ MB/hr}} = \frac{20{,}480{,}000 \text{ MB}}{1{,}575{,}000 \text{ MB/hr}}
$$

$$
T_{LAN} \approx 13.0 \text{ hours}
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{LAN} \approx 13 \text{ hours} \quad \text{vs.} \quad T_{LF} \approx 5 \text{ hours}}
$$

#### Part (d): Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Metric | LAN-Based (10 GbE) | LAN-Free (16 Gbps FC, 4 drives) | Improvement Factor |
|--------|-------------------|---------------------------------|---------------------|
| Effective bandwidth (backup only) | ~437.5 MB/s | 1120 MB/s | **2.56×** |
| Backup window for 20 TB | ~13 hours | ~5 hours | **2.6× faster** |
| LAN impact on users | High (heavy) | Negligible | **Significant relief** |
| Tape drive utilization | 1 stream | 4 parallel streams | **4× parallelism** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Even with a single 16 Gbps FC link (≈1600 MB/s raw, ~1120 MB/s effective), the **parallelism of 4 LTO-9 drives** is the dominant factor. The SAN doesn't just provide speed — it provides **isolated, predictable bandwidth** that the LAN cannot guarantee.

## 3.2 Server-Free vs. Server-Based LAN-Free — Decision Matrix

| Criterion | Server-Based LAN-Free | Serverless (Server-Free) LAN-Free |
|-----------|----------------------|------------------------------------|
| Data path | App Host → SAN → Backup Server → SAN → Tape | App Host → SAN → Tape (via snapshot replication) |
| Backup server in data path? | **Yes** | **No** (only control plane) |
| Host CPU involvement | High | Minimal (snapshot-based) |
| Requires special hardware? | No (just SAN + tape) | Yes (storage array with snapshot/replication) |
| Scales to thousands of hosts? | Limited by backup server | Highly scalable |
| Implementation complexity | Moderate | High |
| Best for | Mid-size environments | Large enterprises with intelligent arrays |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram A — Traditional LAN-Based Backup Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PROD["PRODUCTION ENVIRONMENT"]
        APP1["Application Server 1"]
        APP2["Application Server 2"]
        APP3["Application Server N"]
    end

    subgraph LAN["CORPORATE LAN - ETHERNET 1 to 10 Gbps"]
        SW1["LAN Switch / Router"]
    end

    subgraph BKPSYS["BACKUP INFRASTRUCTURE"]
        BSVR["Backup Server - Media Server"]
        TAPE1["Tape Library - LTO 9"]
    end

    APP1 -- "Data and Control - LAN" --> SW1
    APP2 -- "Data and Control - LAN" --> SW1
    APP3 -- "Data and Control - LAN" --> SW1
    SW1 -- "Backup Data - LAN" --> BSVR
    BSVR -- "Backup Data - LAN" --> TAPE1

    classDef lanStyle fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#ff8c00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef serverStyle fill:#e1f0ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef tapeStyle fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class SW1 lanStyle
    class BSVR serverStyle
    class TAPE1 tapeStyle
```

> **Issue highlighted:** Both **data and control** traverse the same LAN → causes congestion.

## 4.2 Diagram B — LAN-Free Backup Architecture (Server-Based)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SANFAB["SAN FABRIC - 16 Gbps Fibre Channel"]
        FCSW1["FC Switch Zone A"]
        FCSW2["FC Switch Zone B"]
    end

    subgraph PRODHOSTS["PRODUCTION HOSTS - SAN ATTACHED"]
        H1["Application Host 1 - HBA"]
        H2["Application Host 2 - HBA"]
        H3["Application Host N - HBA"]
        DISK1["Production Disk Array"]
    end

    subgraph BKPINFRA["BACKUP INFRASTRUCTURE"]
        MSVR["Media Server - SAN Attached"]
        LIB["Tape Library - LTO 9 - SAN Attached"]
    end

    subgraph CLAN["LAN - CONTROL PLANE ONLY"]
        CSVR["Central Backup Server - Orchestrator"]
        LAN1["LAN Switch"]
    end

    H1 -- "Backup Data - SAN" --> FCSW1
    H2 -- "Backup Data - SAN" --> FCSW1
    H3 -- "Backup Data - SAN" --> FCSW1
    DISK1 -- "Read Source Data - SAN" --> FCSW1
    FCSW1 -- "SAN Fabric" --> FCSW2
    FCSW2 -- "Backup Data - SAN" --> MSVR
    MSVR -- "Backup Data - SAN" --> LIB

    CSVR -- "Control Commands Only - LAN" --> LAN1
    LAN1 -- "Control Only" --> H1
    LAN1 -- "Control Only" --> H2
    LAN1 -- "Control Only" --> MSVR

    classDef sanStyle fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#c2185b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef dataPath fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0d47a1,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef ctrlPath fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class FCSW1,FCSW2 sanStyle
    class H1,H2,H3,MSVR,LIB dataPath
    class CSVR,LAN1 ctrlPath
```

> **Key insight:** **Pink = SAN (data path)**, **Yellow = LAN (control only)**. Backup data never crosses the LAN.

## 4.3 Diagram C — Serverless (Server-Free) LAN-Free Backup

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph NET["NDMP or SCSI Extended Copy Control - LAN only"]
        CTRL["Backup Server - Control Plane"]
    end

    subgraph SANZ["SAN FABRIC"]
        SW["FC Switch"]
    end

    subgraph SRC["PRODUCTION STORAGE - INTELLIGENT ARRAY"]
        ARR["Disk Array with Snapshot and XCOPY Engine"]
        SNAP["Point in Time Snapshot"]
    end

    subgraph DST["BACKUP TARGET - SAN ATTACHED"]
        LIB2["Tape Library"]
    end

    CTRL -- "1. Initiate Backup - LAN" --> ARR
    ARR -- "2. Create Snapshot - Internal" --> SNAP
    SNAP -- "3. Replicate to Tape - SAN - XCOPY" --> SW
    SW -- "4. Direct Write - SAN" --> LIB2
    ARR -- "5. Catalog and Confirm - LAN" --> CTRL

    classDef ctrlStyle fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef sanData fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef srcStyle fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0d47a1,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class CTRL ctrlStyle
    class SW,LIB2 sanData
    class ARR,SNAP srcStyle
```

> **Crucial point:** The application host's CPU is **bypassed entirely**. The storage array's internal engine moves data from snapshot → tape using **SCSI XCOPY** or array-native replication.

## 4.4 Diagram D — Complete Decision Flow: Which Backup Method?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START(["Start - Need to back up production data"]) --> Q1{"Is data on a NAS filer?"}
    Q1 -- "Yes" --> NDMP["Use NDMP Based LAN Free Backup"]
    Q1 -- "No" --> Q2{"Do you have an intelligent storage array with snapshot capability?"}
    Q2 -- "Yes" --> Q3{"Is application host CPU a bottleneck?"}
    Q3 -- "Yes" --> SLF["Use Serverless Server Free LAN Free Backup"]
    Q3 -- "No" --> SB["Use Server Based LAN Free Backup"]
    Q2 -- "No" --> LAN["Use Traditional LAN Based Backup"]

    NDMP --> END(["Backup Complete"])
    SLF --> END
    SB --> END
    LAN --> END

    classDef decision fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef process fill:#e8eaf6,stroke:#283593,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef terminal fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class Q1,Q2,Q3 decision
    class NDMP,SLF,SB,LAN process
    class START,END terminal
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**
*Define LAN-Free Backup. State two key advantages over traditional LAN-based backup.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** LAN-Free Backup is a backup architecture in which backup data is transferred between the **production host** and the **backup device** over a **Storage Area Network (SAN)**, instead of traversing the corporate LAN.

> **Advantages (1 mark each, any two):**
> 1. **Reduced LAN congestion** — backup data no longer competes with user/application traffic.
> 2. **Faster backup windows** — SAN provides dedicated, high-speed bandwidth (4/8/16/32 Gbps FC) compared to shared 1/10 GbE LAN.
> 3. **Improved application performance** during backup windows.
> 4. **Greater scalability** — more hosts can be backed up concurrently without affecting LAN users.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
*Differentiate between **Server-Based LAN-Free Backup** and **Serverless (Server-Free) LAN-Free Backup** in 3 points.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Server-Based LAN-Free | Serverless LAN-Free |
|--------|----------------------|----------------------|
| Data path | App Host → SAN → Backup Server → SAN → Tape | App Host → SAN → Tape (via array snapshot) |
| Backup server role | **In data path** (data mover) | **Only in control path** (orchestrator) |
| Host CPU usage | High (host reads + sends) | Minimal (array does the copy) |

---

## 5.2 📝 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

### ❓ **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Server-Based vs. Serverless*

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a **Server-Based LAN-Free Backup** environment. List any **four advantages** of this approach over traditional LAN-based backup. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **Server-Based** and **Serverless (Server-Free) LAN-Free Backup**. Describe a real-world scenario where each is most appropriate. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Question A

### Part (a) — Server-Based LAN-Free Backup Architecture

**Block Diagram (2 marks):**

The diagram should show:

$$
\text{Application Host} \xrightarrow{\text{SAN}} \text{Backup Server (Media Server)} \xrightarrow{\text{SAN}} \text{Tape Library}
$$

- The Backup Server is **SAN-attached** via an HBA.
- The application host reads its data from **SAN-attached production disks** and writes it over the SAN to the media server.
- The media server then writes the data to a **SAN-attached tape library**.
- The **LAN is used only for control** (orchestration commands from a central backup server).

**Four Advantages (1 mark each, any 4):**

1. **Eliminates LAN congestion** — bulk backup data stays on the SAN.
2. **High throughput** — 4/8/16/32 Gbps FC bandwidth dedicated to backup.
3. **No impact on user traffic** — LAN remains free for application/client traffic.
4. **Scalability** — additional hosts can be backed up by adding tape drives, not LAN bandwidth.
5. **Supports parallel streams** — multiple tape drives can be written to simultaneously.

---

### Part (b) — Server-Based vs. Serverless Comparison (7 marks)

| Feature | Server-Based LAN-Free | Serverless LAN-Free |
|---------|----------------------|---------------------|
| Backup server in data path? | **Yes** — acts as data mover | **No** — only control plane |
| Where does data movement happen? | Through the backup/media server | **Inside the storage array** (using snapshots + XCOPY/ODM) |
| Application host CPU load | **High** (reads + transfers data) | **Low** (only triggers snapshot) |
| Required infrastructure | Standard SAN + tape library | **Intelligent array** with snapshot & replication capability |
| Scalability | Limited by backup server I/O | **Highly scalable** — many hosts can share array |
| Implementation complexity | **Lower** — easier to deploy | **Higher** — needs array features |
| Cost | Lower | Higher (intelligent array licensing) |
| Backup impact on app | Moderate (host CPU usage) | **Minimal** (near-zero impact) |

**Real-World Scenarios (2 marks for scenarios):**

- **Server-Based LAN-Free — best for:** *Mid-sized enterprises* with **5–50 application servers**, a standard FC SAN already deployed, and a need for **moderate-cost** backup consolidation. Example: A regional bank with 20 branch servers backing up to a centralized EMC DD tape library.

- **Serverless LAN-Free — best for:** *Large enterprises* with **hundreds of application servers**, very large databases (10+ TB), and strict **RTO/RPO SLAs** (e.g., < 1 hour RPO). Example: A telecom operator running SAP HANA on a Hitachi VSP array with array-based snapshots replicating to a remote tape library within minutes.

---

### ❓ **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**
