**(a)** Explain the **NDMP-based LAN-Free Backup** architecture used in NAS environments. How does it differ from a host-based LAN-Free backup? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A company needs to back up **30 TB** of data nightly. The backup uses **6 LTO-9 tape drives** (native throughput **400 MB/s** each) on a **16 Gbps FC SAN** with end-to-end efficiency of **0.65**. Calculate:
   1. The **effective per-drive throughput** in MB/s.
   2. The **total backup throughput** in TB/hour.
   3. The **total backup window** in hours. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Question B

### Part (a) — NDMP-Based LAN-Free Backup (7 marks)

**Definition (1 mark):** **NDMP (Network Data Management Protocol)** is an open standard protocol (TCP port **10000**) used to back up **NAS filers** (e.g., NetApp FAS, EMC Isilon) over a SAN without sending data through the LAN.

**Architecture (3 marks):**

$$
\text{NAS Filer} \xrightarrow{\text{Backup Data - SAN}} \text{Tape Library}
$$

$$
\text{Backup Server} \xrightarrow{\text{NDMP Control - TCP Port 10000 - LAN}} \text{NAS Filer}
$$

- The **NAS filer** reads data from its **own internal disks** (or SAN-attached shelves).
- Data is written **directly to a SAN-attached tape library** using NDMP's "**direct-to-tape**" or "**3-way NDMP**" modes.
- The backup server only sends **control commands** over the LAN using NDMP.

**Three NDMP Modes:**
1. **Local NDMP** — filer backs up to its own locally attached tape.
2. **3-Way NDMP** — filer sends data to a *remote* tape library via SAN (most common LAN-Free scenario).
3. **Direct NDMP** — filer talks to a tape server that controls a SAN-attached library.

**Differences from Host-Based LAN-Free (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Host-Based LAN-Free | NDMP-Based LAN-Free |
|--------|--------------------|--------------------|
| Source of data | Application server's disks | NAS filer's file system |
| Backup agent needed on host? | **Yes** | **No** (NDMP is built into filer) |
| Protocol | Vendor-specific backup software | **Open standard NDMP (RFC 4xxx)** |
| Best for | Application servers (DB, mail) | NAS filers (file shares, archives) |
| Data traversal | App Host → SAN → Tape | Filer → SAN → Tape |

---

### Part (b) — Numerical Backup Window Calculation (7 marks)

**Given:** $D = 30 \text{ TB}$, $N = 6$ drives, $R_{tape} = 400 \text{ MB/s}$, $\eta = 0.65$.

#### Step 1 — Effective per-drive throughput (2 marks)

$$
R_{eff} = R_{tape} \times \eta = 400 \times 0.65 = 260 \text{ MB/s per drive}
$$

#### Step 2 — Total throughput across 6 drives (2 marks)

$$
R_{total} = N \times R_{eff} = 6 \times 260 = 1560 \text{ MB/s}
$$

Convert to GB/hour:

$$
R_{total\_GBph} = 1560 \times 3600 \div 1024 = 5{,}616{,}000 \div 1024 \approx 5484.4 \text{ GB/hr}
$$

Convert to TB/hour:

$$
R_{total\_TBph} = 5484.4 \div 1024 \approx 5.36 \text{ TB/hr}
$$

$$
\boxed{R_{total} \approx 5.36 \text{ TB/hr}}
$$

#### Step 3 — Backup window (3 marks)

$$
T_{backup} = \frac{D}{R_{total}} = \frac{30 \text{ TB}}{5.36 \text{ TB/hr}} \approx 5.60 \text{ hours}
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{backup} \approx 5 \text{ hours } 36 \text{ minutes}}
$$

> **Engineering interpretation:** A 30 TB nightly backup is comfortably completed in under 6 hours, leaving 18 hours for production workloads and incremental backups. This is a typical enterprise-grade SLA compliance scenario.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes students make in LAN-Free Backup questions (and lose 2–3 marks):**
>
> 1. **Confusing "LAN-Free" with "Serverless"** — These are NOT the same. LAN-Free = data goes over SAN (server may or may not be in data path). Serverless = server is *never* in data path. Always clarify which is being asked.
> 2. **Forgetting the role of the backup server in control plane** — Even in LAN-Free, a backup server exists for orchestration. Students often say "no backup server needed" → lose 1 mark.
> 3. **Unit conversion errors** — MB/s vs. MBps vs. Mbps vs. MiB/s. **1 GB = 1024 MB** (binary) or **1000 MB** (decimal). KTU expects **1024** for storage calculations. Always show the unit conversion step.
> 4. **Missing the "efficiency factor" $\eta$** — The KTU key always expects $\eta$ in the formula. Raw bandwidth is unrealistic; effective throughput = $B \times \eta$.
> 5. **Forgetting NDMP port number** — In KTU questions about NAS backup, **TCP port 10000** is expected as a 1-mark recall point.
> 6. **Not mentioning tape parallelism** — When asked "how to speed up backup", students often say "use faster tape" but forget **N parallel drives** → marks lost.

---

## 5.4 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — LAN-Free Backups**

- ✅ **Definition:** Backup data travels over the **SAN**, NOT the LAN.
- ✅ **Key Components:** Production Host, SAN Fabric (FC/iSCSI), Backup/Media Server, Tape Library/VTL, Backup Software.
- ✅ **Three Variants:** (1) Server-Based, (2) Serverless/Server-Free, (3) NDMP-Based.
- ✅ **Standard Fibre Channel Speeds:** **1, 2, 4, 8, 16, 32, 64 Gbps** (each gen doubles).
- ✅ **LTO-9 Specs:** **18 TB native capacity, 400 MB/s native throughput** per drive.
- ✅ **NDMP Port:** **TCP 10000** (control plane only).
- ✅ **Backup Time Formula:** $T = \dfrac{D}{B \times \eta \times N}$
- ✅ **Serverless uses:** SCSI Extended Copy (**XCOPY**), ODM, or array-based snapshot replication.
- ✅ **Advantages:** No LAN congestion, faster backup, scalable, supports parallelism.
- ✅ **Limitations:** Requires SAN infrastructure (cost), FC switches/zoning complexity, SAN-attached backup device required.
- ✅ **Use case:** Enterprises with > 5 TB nightly backup, strict RPO/RTO SLAs, multi-server environments.
- ✅ **Comparison trap:** LAN-Free ≠ Serverless. LAN-Free is the broader category; Serverless is a *sub-type* where backup server is excluded from the data path.
- ✅ **Modern trend:** VTL (Virtual Tape Library) + deduplication + cloud tiering are evolving LAN-Free into hybrid architectures.
- ✅ **Real-world products:** NetBackup, IBM TSM/Spectrum Protect, CommVault, Veeam (for virtualized SANs like vSAN).
- ✅ **KTU one-liner:** *"In LAN-Free backup, the SAN acts as a dedicated backup highway, isolating bulk data movement from the corporate LAN and enabling parallel, high-throughput data protection."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Serverless Backups
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECST867 - STORAGE SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Serverless Backups # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Serverless Backup** is a cloud-native data protection paradigm in which backup operations — scheduling, deduplication, encryption, transfer, retention, and restoration — are executed without provisioning, patching, or operating dedicated backup servers or storage arrays. The underlying compute, scaling, and storage resources are abstracted away and managed entirely by the cloud provider under a consumption-based pricing model, while event-driven triggers (functions-as-a-service) initiate backup workflows. In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, serverless backups are positioned under the broader umbrella of **Backup as a Service (BaaS)** and are tightly coupled with **Function-as-a-Service (FaaS)** execution models such as AWS Lambda, Azure Functions, and Google Cloud Functions. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Terminology Watch:** Do not confuse "Serverless Backup" with "Agentless Backup." Serverless refers to the *absence of managed servers by the user*; agentless refers to the *absence of a software agent on the source host*. The two are independent architectural choices. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Electricity Grid" Model Imagine the historical days when every factory had to **build its own power generator**. Engineers had to purchase diesel, maintain turbines, manage voltage regulators, and replace parts. This is analogous to **traditional on-premise backups** where an organization provisions dedicated backup servers, tape libraries, SAN storage, and backup software. Now imagine the modern electricity grid: factories simply **plug into a socket** and pay only for the kilowatt-hours they consume. There is no generator on-site, no maintenance crew, and capacity scales invisibly. This is the **serverless backup model** — the cloud provider operates an invisible "grid" of compute and storage, and the consumer only pays for the backup operations actually executed. ## 1.3 Key Characteristics (Mandatory KTU Highlights) - **Event-Driven Execution:** Backups are triggered by cloud events (file upload, snapshot creation, schedule cron) via serverless functions. - **Auto-Scaling:** Concurrency scales from zero to thousands of parallel backup jobs without capacity planning. - **Pay-Per-Use Pricing:** Cost is computed per **invocation**, **GB-second of compute**, and **GB stored**. - **Zero Server Management:** No OS patching, runtime updates, or hardware refresh cycles. - **Stateless Function Logic:** Backup logic is encapsulated in ephemeral, stateless function code. - **State Externalization:** All persistent state is held in managed object storage (S3, Azure Blob, GCS). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Performance Metrics (Bolded for Exam Recall):** > - **RPO (Recovery Point Objective):** Measured in **seconds to minutes** (near-zero for continuous protection). > - **RTO (Recovery Time Objective):** Measured in **minutes** (cold start penalties apply). > - **Durability:** **99.999999999% (11 nines)** for standard object storage classes. > - **Availability SLA:** Typically **99.9% to 99.99%** depending on storage tier. ## 1.4 Placement in the Storage Stack Serverless backups occupy the **top tier** of the modern data-protection pyramid: $$ \text{Serverless Backup} \subset \text{Backup as a Service (BaaS)} \subset \text{Cloud Storage Services} \subset \text{Distributed Storage Infrastructure} $$ # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Architectural Decomposition — The Six Logical Tiers A serverless backup pipeline is decomposed into six cooperating logical tiers: 1. **Source Tier (Data Origin):** EC2 instances, EBS volumes, RDS databases, on-prem file servers, SaaS applications. 2. **Event Source Tier:** CloudWatch Events, EventBridge, S3 PUT notifications, SQS queues, SNS topics. 3. **Compute Tier (FaaS):** AWS Lambda / Azure Functions executing backup logic (snapshot creation, deduplication tagging, encryption). 4. **Orchestration Tier:** AWS Step Functions, Azure Logic Apps — coordinate multi-step backup state machines. 5. **Storage Tier (Target):** Object storage (S3, Blob) with lifecycle policies and versioning; cold tiers (Glacier, Archive). 6. **Governance Tier:** IAM, KMS, CloudTrail, Vault policies — security, auditing, compliance. ## 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic The backup workflow executes in this sequence: - **Step 1 — Event Trigger:** A cron schedule (EventBridge rule) or data-event (S3:ObjectCreated) invokes the FaaS function. - **Step 2 — Identity Verification:** The function assumes an IAM Role via STS (Secure Token Service) to gain least-privilege permissions. - **Step 3 — Snapshot Initiation:** For EBS-backed sources, an `CreateSnapshot` API call is made; for databases, a consistent snapshot or transaction-log flush is triggered. - **Step 4 — Data Movement:** Snapshot data is either copied directly to object storage or streamed via the function. - **Step 5 — Encryption:** Data is encrypted using **AWS KMS** (AES-256 envelope encryption) before persistence. - **Step 6 — Metadata Tagging:** Backup metadata (timestamp, source ID, retention class, checksum) is written as an S3 object alongside the backup. - **Step 7 — Lifecycle Transition:** A lifecycle rule transitions the object to Standard-IA → Glacier → Deep Archive based on age. - **Step 8 — Notification:** SNS topic publishes success/failure to administrators. ## 2.3 Why Serverless? The Engineering Motivation | Concern | Traditional Backup | Serverless Backup | |---|---|---| | Infrastructure provisioning | Manual capacity planning | **Zero provisioning** | | Idle cost | 24×7 server uptime | **Pay per invocation** | | Scalability ceiling | Hardware-bound | **Function concurrency** | | Patch management | Customer responsibility | **Provider-managed** | | Failover design | DR site required | **Multi-AZ inherent** | | Operational headcount | Backup admin team | **DevOps script owner** | ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Serverless Backup Economics > [!IMPORTANT] > **All formulas below are examinable. Memorize the units and the boundary conditions.** $$ C_{\text{total}} = C_{\text{compute}} + C_{\text{storage}} + C_{\text{requests}} + C_{\text{egress}} $$ $$ C_{\text{compute}} = N_{\text{invocations}} \times P_{\text{invoke}} + \sum_{i=1}^{N} t_i \times m_i \times P_{\text{GB-s}} $$ $$ C_{\text{storage}} = \sum_{j=1}^{T} S_j \times R_j \times \frac{D_j}{30} $$ $$ R_{\text{effective}} = \frac{D_{\text{uncompressed}}}{D_{\text{stored}}} \quad \text{(Deduplication Ratio)} $$ $$ S_{\text{stored}} = \frac{D_{\text{uncompressed}}}{R_{\text{effective}}} $$ $$ \text{Cold Start Penalty} \approx 200\text{ ms to 5 s} $$ $$ \text{Max Concurrent Backups} = \min(F_{\text{concurrency}}, \lfloor \frac{B_{\text{egress}}}{S_{\text{per-job}}} \rfloor) $$ ## 2.5 Symbol Reference Table | Symbol | Meaning | Typical Unit | |---|---|---| | $N_{\text{invocations}}$ | Number of function invocations per month | count | | $P_{\text{invoke}}$ | Price per invocation | USD / million | | $t_i$ | Duration of $i$-th execution | seconds | | $m_i$ | Memory allocated to $i$-th execution | GB | | $P_{\text{GB-s}}$ | Price per GB-second | USD | | $S_j$ | Stored data in tier $j$ | GB | | $R_j$ | Storage rate for tier $j$ | USD / GB / month | | $D_j$ | Days retained in tier $j$ | days | | $T$ | Number of storage tiers | count | | $R_{\text{effective}}$ | Deduplication effectiveness ratio | dimensionless (1.5 – 10) | | $F_{\text{concurrency}}$ | Function concurrency limit | count | | $B_{\text{egress}}$ | Egress bandwidth budget | MB/s | | $S_{\text{per-job}}$ | Bandwidth per backup job | MB/s | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Reality Check:** In KTU board problems, the most common mistake is forgetting that $\frac{D_j}{30}$ converts daily rates to monthly bills — always multiply retention days by the **per-GB-per-month** rate, not the per-day rate. ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility Serverless backups are deployed across industries for: - **Healthcare:** Encrypted HIPAA-compliant archival of PACS imaging data. - **Financial Services:** Immutable backup vaults for SOX/PCI-DSS audit trails. - **Media & Entertainment:** Petabyte-scale video asset preservation. - **E-Commerce:** Continuous protection of transaction logs with sub-minute RPO. - **Edge / IoT:** Aggregating device snapshots into centralized object stores. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Total Cost of Ownership (TCO) for a Serverless Backup Pipeline We derive the monthly cost closed-form, accounting for compute, storage, requests, and egress. This is the **canonical KTU numerical problem** type. ### Step 1 — Define the Variables Let: - $N$ = total backup invocations per month - $\bar{t}$ = mean function execution duration (seconds) - $m$ = memory allocated per invocation (GB) - $S$ = average backup object size stored per month (GB) - $R$ = retention period in days (e.g., 30) - $P_{\text{inv}}$ = price per million invocations = $0.20 - $P_{\text{gbs}}$ = price per GB-second = $0.0000166667 - $P_{\text{st}}$ = storage price per GB-month = $0.023 - $P_{\text{egr}}$ = egress price per GB = $0.09 ### Step 2 — Compute Cost Component The compute cost is the sum of invocation fees plus GB-second consumption. AWS Lambda bills the **rounded-up** memory to the nearest 1 MB and the **rounded-up** duration to the nearest 1 ms. For derivation simplicity, assume exact values: $$ C_{\text{compute}} = N \cdot P_{\text{inv}} + N \cdot \bar{t} \cdot m \cdot P_{\text{gbs}} $$ Substituting the AWS Lambda pricing constants: $$ C_{\text{compute}} = N \cdot \frac{0.20}{10^6} + N \cdot \bar{t} \cdot m \cdot \frac{0.0000166667}{1} $$ ### Step 3 — Compute Storage Cost Component Storage cost is proportional to the data stored and the retention duration: $$ C_{\text{storage}} = S \cdot P_{\text{st}} \cdot \frac{R}{30} $$ For a 30-day retention, the factor $\frac{R}{30} = 1$, simplifying to: $$ C_{\text{storage, 30-day}} = S \cdot P_{\text{st}} = S \cdot 0.023 $$ ### Step 4 — Compute Request and Egress Costs Request cost is a flat fee per 1000 PUT requests: $$ C_{\text{requests}} = \frac{N}{1000} \cdot P_{\text{PUT}} $$ Egress cost applies only during restore operations (not backups), and equals: $$ C_{\text{egress}} = S_{\text{restored}} \cdot P_{\text{egr}} $$ ### Step 5 — Combine into Final TCO $$ \boxed{ C_{\text{TCO}} = N \left( \frac{P_{\text{inv}}}{10^6} + \bar{t} \cdot m \cdot P_{\text{gbs}} + \frac{P_{\text{PUT}}}{1000} \right) + S \cdot P_{\text{st}} \cdot \frac{R}{30} + S_{\text{restored}} \cdot P_{\text{egr}} } $$ ### Step 6 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style) **Problem:** A serverless backup pipeline runs **5,000 invocations per month**. Each invocation executes for **60 seconds** with **512 MB** allocated. Average backup size is **2 GB** and retention is **90 days**. Find the monthly TCO assuming no restores. **Solution:** Compute invocation cost: $$ C_{\text{inv}} = 5000 \times \frac{0.20}{10^6} = 5000 \times 2 \times 10^{-7} = \$0.001 $$ Compute GB-second cost (512 MB = 0.5 GB): $$ C_{\text{gbs}} = 5000 \times 60 \times 0.5 \times 0.0000166667 $$ $$ C_{\text{gbs}} = 5000 \times 30 \times 0.0000166667 = 150000 \times 0.0000166667 = \$2.50 $$ Compute storage cost (90 days retention, 5000 × 2 GB = 10,000 GB): $$ C_{\text{storage}} = 10000 \times 0.023 \times \frac{90}{30} = 10000 \times 0.023 \times 3 = \$690 $$ Compute PUT request cost (assume $0.005 per 1000 PUTs): $$ C_{\text{PUT}} = \frac{5000}{1000} \times 0.005 = 5 \times 0.005 = \$0.025 $$ Total TCO: $$ C_{\text{TCO}} = 0.001 + 2.50 + 690 + 0.025 = \$692.526 $$ $$ \boxed{C_{\text{TCO}} \approx \$692.53 \text{ per month}} $$ **Interpretation:** Notice that **storage dominates (~99.6%)** of the bill. This is the canonical KTU insight — *compute is essentially free in serverless backups; storage tier selection is the primary cost lever.* ## 3.2 Derivation: Effective Deduplication Ratio Impact Given a deduplication ratio $R_{\text{effective}}$, the *stored* data after dedup is: $$ S_{\text{stored}} = \frac{S_{\text{logical}}}{R_{\text{effective}}} $$ For backups of virtual machines with high block similarity, $R_{\text{effective}}$ can range from 2 to 10. The **storage savings** in dollars per month are: $$ \text{Savings} = (S_{\text{logical}} - S_{\text{stored}}) \cdot P_{\text{st}} = S_{\text{logical}} \left(1 - \frac{1}{R_{\text{effective}}}\right) \cdot P_{\text{st}} $$ For $S_{\text{logical}} = 10{,}000$ GB and $R_{\text{effective}} = 4$: $$ \text{Savings} = 10000 \times \left(1 - \frac{1}{4}\right) \times 0.023 = 10000 \times 0.75 \times 0.023 = \$172.50/\text{month} $$ ## 3.3 Derivation: Concurrency-Limited Throughput A backup pipeline cannot exceed the lesser of the function concurrency limit or the egress bandwidth divided by per-job bandwidth. The maximum sustainable backup rate is: $$ T_{\text{max}} = \min \left( F_{\text{concurrency}}, \left\lfloor \frac{B_{\text{egress}}}{S_{\text{per-job}}} \right\rfloor \right) \text{ jobs/s} $$ For $F_{\text{concurrency}} = 1000$ and $\frac{B_{\text{egress}}}{S_{\text{per-job}}} = 250$: $$ T_{\text{max}} = \min(1000, 250) = 250 \text{ jobs/s} $$ $$ \boxed{ \text{Network egress, not function concurrency, is the binding constraint.} } $$ # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Serverless Backup Architecture — Block-Level Functional Flow The diagram below maps the end-to-end flow of a serverless backup pipeline. Every block represents a managed cloud service; arrows represent event or data dependencies. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Source Data Tier
EC2 EBS RDS OnPrem] --> B[Event Source Tier
EventBridge S3 Notification] B --> C[Compute Tier
AWS Lambda Function] C --> D[Identity Tier
IAM Role via STS] D --> E[Orchestration Tier
Step Functions State Machine] E --> F[Snapshot Tier
EBS Snapshot API] F --> G[Storage Tier Hot
S3 Standard] G --> H[Lifecycle Policy
Transition after 30 days] H --> I[Storage Tier Warm
S3 Standard IA] I --> J[Lifecycle Policy
Transition after 90 days] J --> K[Storage Tier Cold
S3 Glacier Deep Archive] C --> L[Security Tier
KMS Envelope Encryption] G --> L I --> L K --> L C --> M[Observability Tier
CloudWatch Logs Metrics] C --> N[Notification Tier
SNS Topic Email Alert] E --> O[Audit Tier
CloudTrail Immutable Log] ``` ## 4.2 Backup Data Lifecycle State Diagram This state diagram shows how a backup object transitions through storage tiers as it ages. ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Created : Lambda writes encrypted object Created --> Standard : Days 0 to 30 Standard --> StandardIA : Lifecycle rule fires day 30 StandardIA --> Glacier : Lifecycle rule fires day 90 Glacier --> DeepArchive : Lifecycle rule fires day 365 DeepArchive --> Restore : Restore request initiated Glacier --> Restore : Restore request initiated StandardIA --> Restore : Restore request initiated Standard --> Restore : Restore request initiated Restore --> [*] : Data egressed to target DeepArchive --> Deleted : Retention policy expires Glacier --> Deleted : Retention policy expires StandardIA --> Deleted : Retention policy expires Standard --> Deleted : Retention policy expires ``` ## 4.3 Backup Failure Recovery Decision Matrix | Failure Scenario | Detection Signal | Serverless Response | Recovery Time | |---|---|---|---| | Lambda timeout | CloudWatch metric `Duration` near limit | DLQ via SQS, retry with exponential backoff | Minutes | | KMS key disabled | API error code `AccessDeniedException` | SNS alert, fail-closed policy | Manual | | S3 throttling | HTTP 503 `SlowDown` | Step Functions retry with jitter | Seconds | | Snapshot copy failure | EC2 API error | EventBridge routes to remediation function | Minutes | | Lifecycle policy misconfiguration | CloudTrail `Transition` event missing | Config rule alarm, automated rollback | Hours | # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap > [!NOTE] > All questions below are aligned with **KTU 2024 Scheme Bloom's Taxonomy Levels**: Remember (L1), Understand (L2), Apply (L3), Analyze (L4), Evaluate (L5), Create (L6). --- ## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style] **Q: Define serverless backups and list three characteristics that differentiate them from traditional server-based backups.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1 mark):** Serverless backup is a cloud-native data protection model in which backup operations — scheduling, deduplication, encryption, transfer, and retention — are executed on infrastructure that is fully managed by the cloud provider, with the consumer paying only for the resources actually consumed during backup execution. **Three differentiating characteristics (2 marks — 1 each):** 1. **Zero server management:** No provisioning, patching, or hardware refresh; the cloud provider handles all infrastructure lifecycle. 2. **Event-driven execution:** Backups are triggered by cloud events (cron, object creation, snapshot completion) and not by polling schedulers running on dedicated hosts. 3. **Pay-per-use cost model:** Billing is based on invocation count, GB-seconds of compute, and GB stored — eliminating idle infrastructure cost. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often write "serverless means no servers" — this is incorrect and **loses 1 mark**. The correct phrasing is *"the customer does not manage or provision servers."* Servers absolutely exist; they are simply abstracted away. --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style] **Q: What is the role of AWS KMS in a serverless backup pipeline? Why is envelope encryption preferred over direct encryption?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Role of KMS (1.5 marks):** AWS Key Management Service (KMS) generates, stores, and controls the **Customer Master Keys (CMKs)** used to encrypt backup data. The Lambda function calls the `GenerateDataKey` API to receive a per-object **Data Encryption Key (DEK)**, which is used for symmetric AES-256 encryption of the backup payload. **Why envelope encryption (1.5 marks):** Envelope encryption uses a two-tier key hierarchy — the **DEK encrypts the data**, while the **CMK encrypts the DEK**. This is preferred because: - The CMK never leaves the KMS Hardware Security Module (HSM), reducing exposure. - Each backup object gets a unique DEK, limiting the blast radius of any single key compromise. - KMS automatically rotates CMKs annually without re-encrypting data, since only the wrapped DEK needs re-wrapping. --- ## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU 2024 Scheme Part B questions carry 14 marks with internal choice. Each question typically has two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. --- ### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper Style] **Q: (a)** Explain the complete architecture of a serverless backup system. Describe each tier with at least one cloud service example. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A startup stores **20 TB of logical data** with a deduplication ratio of **5**. The backup pipeline runs **10,000 Lambda invocations per month**, each lasting **45 seconds** with **1024 MB** allocated. Storage rate is **$0.023 per GB-month**, retention is **60 days**, and PUT request cost is **$0.005 per 1000 requests**. Compute the monthly TCO. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Answer — Part (a) [7 marks] The serverless backup architecture consists of **six cooperating tiers**: **Tier 1 — Source Tier (1 mark):** The origin of the data being backed up. Examples: EC2 instances with EBS volumes, RDS databases, on-premise VMware VMs syncing via AWS Storage Gateway, or SaaS platforms such as Salesforce. **Tier 2 — Event Source Tier (1 mark):** Generates the trigger that initiates backup logic. Examples: **Amazon EventBridge** for time-based cron rules (e.g., `rate(1 day)`), **S3 Event Notifications** for object-created events, or **SQS** for queued trigger messages from external schedulers. **Tier 3 — Compute Tier (1 mark):** The Function-as-a-Service execution environment that runs backup logic. Examples: **AWS Lambda** (Python or Node.js runtime), **Azure Functions**, or **Google Cloud Functions**. Functions are stateless, ephemeral, and auto-scaling. **Tier 4 — Orchestration Tier (1 mark):** Coordinates multi-step backup workflows. Examples: **AWS Step Functions** using Amazon States Language (ASL) to define retries, parallel branches, and human approval steps; or **Azure Logic Apps** for similar flow control. **Tier 5 — Storage Tier (2 marks):** The persistent target for backup artifacts. Includes: - **Hot tier:** S3 Standard — frequent access, low latency. - **Warm tier:** S3 Standard-IA — infrequent access, lower cost. - **Cold tier:** S3 Glacier / Glacier Deep Archive — archival, retrieval latency in hours. - **Backup-specific vault:** AWS Backup Vault with locked-immutable retention policies. **Tier 6 — Governance Tier (1 mark):** Cross-cutting concerns. **AWS KMS** for encryption, **IAM** for least-privilege access, **CloudTrail** for audit logging, **AWS Config** for compliance drift detection, and **SNS** for operator alerting. **Conclusion:** The six tiers are loosely coupled via asynchronous events, enabling the system to scale horizontally without manual capacity planning — a hallmark of serverless design. --- #### Model Answer — Part (b) [7 marks] **Given Data:** - $S_{\text{logical}} = 20\text{ TB} = 20{,}000\text{ GB}$ - $R_{\text{effective}} = 5$ - $N = 10{,}000$ invocations - $\bar{t} = 45\text{ s}$, $m = 1024\text{ MB} = 1\text{ GB}$ - $P_{\text{st}} = \$0.023/\text{GB-month}$ - $R = 60\text{ days}$ - $P_{\text{PUT}} = \$0.005/1000$ **Step 1 — Stored data after deduplication (1 mark):** $$ S_{\text{stored}} = \frac{S_{\text{logical}}}{R_{\text{effective}}} = \frac{20000}{5} = 4000\text{ GB} $$ **Step 2 — Invocation cost (1 mark):** $$ C_{\text{inv}} = N \cdot \frac{0.20}{10^6} = 10000 \times 2 \times 10^{-7} = \$0.002 $$ **Step 3 — GB-second cost (2 marks):** $$ C_{\text{gbs}} = N \cdot \bar{t} \cdot m \cdot P_{\text{gbs}} = 10000 \times 45 \times 1 \times 0.0000166667 $$ $$ C_{\text{gbs}} = 450000 \times 0.0000166667 = \$7.50 $$ **Step 4 — Storage cost (2 marks):** $$ C_{\text{storage}} = S_{\text{stored}} \cdot P_{\text{st}} \cdot \frac{R}{30} = 4000 \times 0.023 \times \frac{60}{30} $$ $$ C_{\text{storage}} = 4000 \times 0.023 \times 2 = \$184.00 $$ **Step 5 — PUT request cost (0.5 mark):** $$ C_{\text{PUT}} = \frac{N}{1000} \cdot 0.005 = \frac{10000}{1000} \times 0.005 = 10 \times 0.005 = \$0.05 $$ **Step 6 — Total TCO (0.5 mark):** $$ C_{\text{TCO}} = 0.002 + 7.50 + 184.00 + 0.05 = \$191.552 $$ $$ \boxed{C_{\text{TCO}} \approx \$191.55 \text{ per month}} $$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls:** > - **Common Error 1 (-2 marks):** Forgetting to apply the deduplication ratio before computing storage cost. The question says "logical 20 TB" — you must divide by $R_{\text{effective}}$. > - **Common Error 2 (-1 mark):** Forgetting the retention multiplier $\frac{R}{30}$. If retention is 60 days, multiply by 2, not 1. > - **Common Error 3 (-1 mark):** Using GB-month price directly without converting 1024 MB to **1.024 GB** or rounding to 1 GB — accept either as correct, but be consistent. > - **Common Error 4 (-0.5 mark):** Writing "compute is the major cost" — in reality, **storage dominates** here (~96%). Examiners reward the correct insight. --- ### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative to Question A) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper Style] **Q: (a)** Discuss the role of event-driven triggers, lifecycle policies, and immutable vaults in a serverless backup design. Why is immutability critical for ransomware protection? **(7 marks)** **(b)** Compare and contrast **S3 Standard**, **S3 Standard-IA**, **S3 Glacier**, and **S3 Glacier Deep Archive** for use in a tiered serverless backup strategy. Provide a recommendation matrix mapping data age to tier selection. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Answer — Part (a) [7 marks] **Event-driven triggers (2 marks):** Event-driven triggers decouple the backup initiation logic from the compute infrastructure. In AWS, services such as **Amazon EventBridge** accept cron-style schedules (`rate(1 day)`, `cron(0 2 * * ? *)`) and emit events to invoke Lambda functions. **S3 Event Notifications** can trigger a backup whenever a critical object is written, enabling **continuous data protection (CDP)** with RPO measured in seconds. The advantage over polling schedulers is that no VM must run 24×7 to "check" whether a backup is needed — the event itself contains all the context (timestamp, source bucket, object key). **Lifecycle policies (2.5 marks):** Lifecycle policies are declarative JSON/YAML rules attached to an S3 bucket that automatically transition objects between storage classes or expire them. A typical policy transitions backups: - From S3 Standard to **S3 Standard-IA** after 30 days (cost reduction ~40%). - From Standard-IA to **S3 Glacier** after 90 days (cost reduction ~80%). - From Glacier to **Glacier Deep Archive** after 365 days (cost reduction ~95%). - Expiration rule deletes backups after the regulatory retention period (e.g., 7 years for healthcare). Lifecycle policies execute **without invoking any Lambda function**, making them zero-cost and zero-overhead automation. **Immutable vaults (2.5 marks):** An immutable backup vault (such as AWS Backup Vault Lock or S3 Object Lock in Compliance mode) enforces **Write-Once-Read-Many (WORM)** semantics. Once a backup object is written, it cannot be modified or deleted — not even by the root account — until a predefined retention period elapses. **Why immutability matters for ransomware (key insight, 1 mark):** Modern ransomware strains (e.g., LockBit, BlackCat) explicitly target backup systems: they locate backup volumes, encrypt or delete them, and then demand ransom for the decryption key. If backups reside in a **mutable** S3 bucket, the attacker can erase them in seconds, leaving the victim with no recovery path. Immutable vaults defeat this by making deletion **cryptographically and operationally impossible** within the retention window — even if the attacker's IAM credentials are compromised, the vault lock policy blocks deletion. --- #### Model Answer — Part (b) [7 marks] **Tier Comparison Matrix (4 marks):** | Feature | S3 Standard | S3 Standard-IA | S3 Glacier | Glacier Deep Archive | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Storage cost ($/GB-month)** | $0.023 | $0.0125 | $0.004 | $0.00099 | | **Retrieval cost ($/GB)** | Free | $0.01 | $0.01 (expedited) | $0.02 (standard) | | **Retrieval latency** | Milliseconds | Milliseconds | Minutes to hours | 12+ hours | | **Minimum storage duration** | None | 30 days | 90 days | 180 days | | **Use case** | Recent / hot backups | Monthly backups | Quarterly backups | Annual / archival | | **Availability SLA** | 99.99% | 99.9% | 99.99% | 99.99% | | **Durability** | 11 nines | 11 nines | 11 nines | 11 nines | **Recommendation Matrix — Data Age to Tier Selection (3 marks):** $$ \text{Backup Age (days)} = t $$ | Age Range | Recommended Tier | Justification | |---|---|---| | $0 \le t < 30$ | **S3 Standard** | Frequent restore drills; instant access needed for operational recovery | | $30 \le t < 90$ | **S3 Standard-IA** | Lower cost for backups unlikely to be restored; 30-day minimum offsets churn | | $90 \le t < 365$ | **S3 Glacier** | Quarterly recovery scenario; minute-scale retrieval acceptable | | $t \ge 365$ | **Glacier Deep Archive** | Compliance-grade archival; 12-hour restore tolerable | **Engineering insight:** The transition thresholds should align with **business RTO requirements**. If the business requires same-day restore for any backup under 90 days old, S3 Standard-IA's millisecond retrieval makes it preferable to Glacier despite higher cost. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall — Part (b):** > - Writing only the storage prices **without retrieval latency** loses 2 marks — the latency is the primary engineering trade-off. > - Forgetting the **minimum storage duration penalty** — S3 charges for the full minimum duration even if the object is deleted earlier (e.g., deleting an S3 Standard-IA object on day 10 still bills for 30 days). This is a classic KTU trick question. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Scheme — Module 3, Serverless Backups:** - ✅ **Definition:** Serverless backups execute data protection logic on cloud-managed, event-driven compute (FaaS) with no customer-provisioned infrastructure. - ✅ **Core Tiers (memorize all six):** Source → Event → Compute → Orchestration → Storage → Governance. - ✅ **TCO Formula:** $C_{\text{TCO}} = C_{\text{compute}} + C_{\text{storage}} + C_{\text{requests}} + C_{\text{egress}}$. - ✅ **Storage dominates cost:** In typical pipelines, **>90% of monthly spend is storage** — tier selection matters more than compute tuning. - ✅ **Deduplication formula:** $S_{\text{stored}} = \frac{S_{\text{logical}}}{R_{\text{effective}}}$. - ✅ **Retention multiplier:** Always multiply GB-month price by $\frac{R}{30}$. - ✅ **AWS Lambda pricing constants:** $P_{\text{inv}} = \$0.20/\text{million}$, $P_{\text{gbs}} = \$0.0000166667/\text{GB-s}$. - ✅ **Standard durability:** **11 nines (99.999999999%)** for all S3 storage classes. - ✅ **Immutability:** WORM vaults defeat ransomware deletion; AWS Backup Vault Lock and S3 Object Lock Compliance mode are industry standards. - ✅ **Lifecycle policy transitions:** Standard → Standard-IA → Glacier → Deep Archive — declarative, zero-cost, automated. - ✅ **Envelope encryption:** DEK encrypts data, CMK encrypts DEK; CMK never leaves the KMS HSM. - ✅ **Concurrency limit:** $T_{\text{max}} = \min(F_{\text{concurrency}}, \lfloor \frac{B_{\text{egress}}}{S_{\text{per-job}}} \rfloor)$ — egress is usually the binding constraint. - ✅ **Common pitfall:** "Serverless" ≠ "no servers"; it means *no customer-managed servers*. - ✅ **Cold start penalty:** **200 ms to 5 s** — relevant for short-lived backup functions; mitigate with provisioned concurrency. - ✅ **RPO/RTO targets:** Serverless architectures achieve **RPO in seconds** and **RTO in minutes**, far superior to traditional tape-based backups (RPO in hours, RTO in days). - ✅ **Governance trio:** KMS + IAM + CloudTrail — every backup must be encrypted, authorized, and audited.

topics.title: NDMP
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST867 - STORAGE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** NDMP

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**NDMP (Network Data Management Protocol)** is an open, vendor-neutral, TCP/IP-based network protocol standardized for the unified control and coordination of **backup, recovery, and data management operations** between a **Data Management Application (DMA)** and an **NDMP server** embedded inside a Network-Attached Storage (NAS) device (also called a *filer*).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** NDMP was originally developed by **NetApp and PDC (now Legato/EMC)** and is now governed by the **SNIA (Storage Networking Industry Association)**. It operates as an **asymmetric client-server** protocol, with the DMA acting as the orchestrator and the NDMP server residing inside the filer.

The protocol defines standardized interfaces for:
- **Session Management** (control plane)
- **Data Movement** (transfer plane)
- **Tape Operations** (storage plane)
- **File History Tracking** (metadata plane)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a large **library warehouse** (the NAS filer) filled with millions of files. You hire a **logistics manager** (the DMA / backup software) to take daily copies of the books to a **secure vault** (the tape library) for safekeeping.

- Without NDMP: The manager would have to **physically carry every single book** to a copying machine on a separate floor, copy it, and walk it to the vault — slow, exhausting, and inefficient.
- **With NDMP:** The manager simply hands the warehouse a **printed set of instructions** ("copy shelf 3 to the vault"). The warehouse uses its **own internal copying machines** and **internal conveyor belts** to do the heavy lifting, and the manager gets a confirmation receipt. The manager never has to touch the books.

That instruction sheet is the **NDMP protocol**. It transforms the NAS filer from a passive storage box into an **active backup participant** capable of pushing its own data to tape without the DMA having to read the files itself.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** NDMP was created because traditional backup architectures forced backup servers to read files *over the LAN* from the NAS, consuming huge bandwidth and creating a single point of failure. NDMP lets the filer stream its own data to tape, freeing the LAN entirely.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Terminology

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **DMA** | Data Management Application (the backup software) |
| **NDMP Server** | NDMP service running inside the filer/NAS |
| **Tape Mover** | Module that moves data between memory buffer and tape device |
| **Data Mover** | Module that reads/writes file data from/to the filer's filesystem |
| **Mover** | Generic term for the entity transferring data to tape |
| **BNI** | Backup Network Interface (dedicated backup port) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Default NDMP TCP Port:** **10000** (well-known IANA-registered port).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Evolution of NDMP Versions

| Version | Year | Key Additions |
|---|---|---|
| **NDMP v1** | 1996 | Initial release by NetApp; tape-to-tape local backup only |
| **NDMP v2** | 1997 | Added **three-way** (LAN-free) backup; SCSI interface support |
| **NDMP v3** | 1999 | Introduced **MOVER interface**, **File History**, **P-to-P recovery** |
| **NDMP v4** | 2003 | Added **DAR (Direct Access Recovery)**, **Byte-Range Recoveries**, **SnapMirror-aware backups** |
| **NDMP v5** | 2005 | Added **IPv6**, **Snapshot Management**, **Multi-vendor file history extensions** |

## 2.2 Core NDMP Architecture Models

NDMP supports **three distinct operational models** for backup, each defining *who* moves the data and *where* the tape sits.

### Model 1: **Server-Free / Local Backup (Direct-Attached)**
- Tape device is **physically attached** to the filer.
- The **NDMP server (filer)** acts as both **Data Mover** and **Tape Mover**.
- DMA only sends control commands.
- **Zero LAN traffic** for backup data.

### Model 2: **Three-Way Backup (LAN-Free)**
- The tape library is attached to a **dedicated backup server** (not the filer).
- **Filer → DMA → Tape Server** control flow.
- **Filer → Tape Server** direct data flow (LAN-free).
- DMA orchestrates; filer's internal Data Mover pushes data directly to the tape server.

### Model 3: **Two-Way Backup**
- Used when filer cannot directly stream to tape.
- DMA reads data from filer and writes to tape (classic, LAN-consuming approach, **not recommended**).

## 2.3 NDMP Interface Architecture

NDMP is composed of **independent logical services** (called *interfaces*). Each interface has a set of well-defined messages. This modular design allows vendors to implement only what they need.

| Interface | Purpose | Plane |
|---|---|---|
| **Session Interface** | Establishes, maintains, and terminates NDMP connections | Control |
| **Connection Interface** | Manages TCP connections between NDMP hosts | Control |
| **MOVER Interface** | Controls the data transfer process (start, pause, abort, listen) | Data |
| **Data Interface** | Streams bulk data (read/write) | Data |
| **Tape Interface** | Controls tape operations (rewind, write filemark, position) | Storage |
| **SCSI Interface** | Issues low-level SCSI commands to the tape device | Storage |
| **File History Interface** | Reports metadata of backed-up files | Metadata |
| **Snapshot Interface (v5)** | Manages filesystem snapshots | Data |

## 2.4 Why NDMP Matters — Engineering Perspective

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** The three biggest engineering problems NDMP solves are:
> 1. **Bandwidth Bottleneck** — Removes backup data from the production LAN.
> 2. **Server Overload** — Backup server no longer needs to read every file.
> 3. **Vendor Lock-in** — Standardized protocol allows any compliant DMA to back up any compliant filer.

## 2.5 NDMP Operational Workflow — Backup

The DMA orchestrates the backup through a **defined sequence of NDMP messages**:

1. **Connection** — DMA opens TCP connection to NDMP server on port **10000**.
2. **Authentication & Negotiation** — DMA sends `NDMP_NOTIFY_CONNECTED`.
3. **Discover Resources** — DMA queries tape devices and filesystem via NDMP messages.
4. **Set Tape Parameters** — DMA configures block size, compression, etc.
5. **Initiate Backup** — DMA sends `NDMP_MOVER_LISTEN` on filer, `NDMP_DATA_START_BACKUP` to begin.
6. **Stream Data** — Filer's Data Mover reads file data; Tape Mover writes to tape.
7. **File History** — Filer sends `NDMP_FH_ADD_FILE` messages with metadata.
8. **Termination** — `NDMP_MOVER_ABORT` or `NDMP_MOVER_STOP` ends transfer.
9. **Tape Finalization** — Filemarks written; tape unloaded.

## 2.6 Recovery Workflow

Recovery reverses the data plane:
- DMA identifies files via **file history database**.
- Sends `NDMP_FH_Restore` with file list.
- Tape Mover reads from tape; Data Mover writes to filer filesystem.

> [!TIP]
> **DAR (Direct Access Recovery)** in NDMP v4 lets the DMA randomly position the tape to a specific file mark, **skipping** irrelevant data, dramatically reducing recovery time for individual files.

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| NDMP default port | **TCP 10000** | IANA registered |
| Three-way backup: LAN savings | $S_{LAN} = 100\% - (\text{metadata traffic only})$ | Backup data bypasses LAN |
| Backup window reduction | $T_{backup} \approx \frac{D_{fil}}{\min(R_{disk}, W_{tape})}$ | $D_{fil}$ = data size; $R_{disk}$ = filer read rate; $W_{tape}$ = tape write rate |
| NDMP message format | `\text{Header (4B ID + 4B Length)} + \text{Reply + Error Code}` | Each message has a request/reply pair |
| DAR efficiency | $T_{recover} \approx \frac{F_{target} \cdot B_{tape}}{V_{seek}}$ | $F_{target}$ = file position; $B_{tape}$ = block; $V_{seek}$ = tape seek rate |
| NDMP version → DAR support | $v \geq 4$ | Boolean: supported if $v \geq 4$ |
| IPv6 support | $v \geq 5$ | Boolean: supported if $v \geq 5$ |
| Backup window formula (parallel tapes) | $T_{par} = \frac{D_{fil}}{N_{tapes} \cdot W_{tape}}$ | $N_{tapes}$ = number of parallel tape drives |

> [!NOTE]
> **For complex NDMP architectures (e.g., distributed enterprise NAS), the backup window is the minimum of the three bottlenecks**: filer read rate, network SAN rate (in three-way), and tape write rate.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why Three-Way Backup Saves LAN Bandwidth

### Given
- $D_{fil}$: Total data to back up (bytes)
- $R_{LAN}$: Production LAN bandwidth (bytes/sec)
- $R_{SAN}$: Backup SAN/Fibre Channel bandwidth (bytes/sec)
- $R_{disk}$: Filer disk read rate (bytes/sec)
- $R_{tape}$: Tape write rate (bytes/sec)
- $T_{target}$: Maximum allowed backup window (sec)

### Goal
Compare LAN usage between **two-way** and **three-way** NDMP backup.

### Step 1: Two-Way Backup (DMA-mediated)

The DMA reads each file from the filer **across the LAN**, then writes to tape.

$$D_{LAN,\,2way} = D_{fil}$$

All $D_{fil}$ bytes traverse the production LAN. Time required:

$$T_{2way} = \max\left(\frac{D_{fil}}{R_{LAN}} + \frac{D_{fil}}{R_{tape}},\, \frac{D_{fil}}{R_{disk}}\right)$$

### Step 2: Three-Way Backup (LAN-Free)

The DMA sends **only control messages** (kilobytes, not megabytes) over the LAN. Bulk data flows from filer → tape server across the dedicated backup SAN.

$$D_{LAN,\,3way} = D_{ctrl} \approx 0.01\% \cdot D_{fil}$$

$$T_{3way} = \max\left(\frac{D_{fil}}{R_{SAN}} + \frac{D_{fil}}{R_{tape}},\, \frac{D_{fil}}{R_{disk}}\right)$$

### Step 3: Compute the LAN Bandwidth Savings Ratio

$$\eta_{LAN} = \frac{D_{LAN,\,2way} - D_{LAN,\,3way}}{D_{LAN,\,2way}} \times 100\%$$

$$\eta_{LAN} = \frac{D_{fil} - 0.0001 \cdot D_{fil}}{D_{fil}} \times 100\% = 99.99\%$$

### Step 4: Interpret the Result

**Approximately 99.99% of LAN bandwidth is freed** for production traffic, with only the small control-channel overhead remaining. The backup data is moved entirely across the dedicated backup infrastructure.

## 3.2 Derivation: Parallel Tape Backup Window (Analytical Matrix)

### Given
- $D_{fil} = 10\text{ TB}$
- $N_{tapes}$ = 1, 2, 4, 8 parallel tape drives
- $R_{tape} = 120\text{ MB/s}$ per drive

### Step 1: Aggregate Tape Throughput

$$R_{agg} = N_{tapes} \cdot R_{tape}$$

### Step 2: Time Required

$$T_{par} = \frac{D_{fil}}{R_{agg}}$$

### Step 3: Numerical Matrix

| $N_{tapes}$ | $R_{agg}$ (MB/s) | $T_{par}$ (sec) | $T_{par}$ (hours) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 120 | 87,381 | 24.27 |
| 2 | 240 | 43,691 | 12.14 |
| 4 | 480 | 21,845 | 6.07 |
| 8 | 960 | 10,923 | 3.03 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** Backup window scales **inversely** with the number of parallel tape drives. This is the core engineering justification for enterprise NDMP deployments with multiple tape drives.

## 3.3 Step-by-Step: DAR (Direct Access Recovery) Logic

### Goal
Recover a single file $F_x$ from a tape with $N$ files without reading all prior files.

### Step 1: Locate File in File History
The DMA queries its file history database and finds $F_x$ is the $k$-th file mark on the tape.

### Step 2: Issue Positioning Command
DMA sends `NDMP_TAPE_POSITION` with the filemark number $k$.

### Step 3: Tape Mover Repositions
The Tape Mover uses **fast seek** to position the tape head directly at filemark $k$.

### Step 4: Sequential Read of Single File
Data Mover reads the single file's blocks; Tape Mover streams them to the filer.

### Time Comparison

$$T_{linear} = \frac{k \cdot B_{file}}{V_{seek}} + \frac{B_{Fx}}{R_{tape}}$$

$$T_{DAR} = \frac{V_{position}}{V_{seek}} + \frac{B_{Fx}}{R_{tape}}$$

For $k = 1000$, $B_{file} = 100\text{ MB}$, $V_{seek} = 5\text{ m/s}$ tape, $V_{position} = 0.5\text{ s}$:

$$T_{linear} = \frac{1000 \cdot 100}{5} + \frac{100}{120} = 20{,}000.83\text{ sec} \approx 5.56\text{ hours}$$

$$T_{DAR} = \frac{0.5}{5} + \frac{100}{120} \approx 0.93\text{ sec}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **DAR achieves a speedup factor of approximately 20,000×** for selective file recovery, which is the engineering justification for using NDMP v4+ in production environments.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 NDMP High-Level Architecture (Three-Way Backup)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    appA[Backup Server DMA] -- Control messages over LAN --> fs1[NDMP Server on Filer]
    appA -- Control messages over LAN --> ts1[Tape Server with Library]
    fs1 -- Bulk data via SAN --> ts1
    fs1 -- File history metadata --> appA
    ts1 -- Tape control responses --> appA
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The DMA (top-left) sends control instructions over the LAN to both the filer and the tape server. The filer streams bulk data directly to the tape server across a SAN, **bypassing the LAN entirely**.

## 4.2 NDMP Internal Interface Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    app1[Application: Backup Job Orchestrator]
    subg1[NDMP Control Plane]
    subg2[NDMP Data Plane]
    subg3[NDMP Storage Plane]
    subg4[NDMP Metadata Plane]
    app1 --> subg1
    app1 --> subg2
    app1 --> subg3
    app1 --> subg4
    subg1 --> si[Session Interface]
    subg1 --> ci[Connection Interface]
    subg2 --> mi[MOVER Interface]
    subg2 --> di[Data Interface]
    subg3 --> ti[Tape Interface]
    subg3 --> sci[SCSI Interface]
    subg4 --> fhi[File History Interface]
    subg4 --> sni[Snapshot Interface v5]
```

## 4.3 NDMP Backup Operation Sequence

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant DMA as DMA Backup Server
    participant FLR as NDMP Filer
    participant TAP as Tape Server
    DMA->>FLR: NDMP Connect (TCP 10000)
    FLR-->>DMA: NDMP Notify Connected
    DMA->>FLR: NDMP Config Get Tapes
    FLR-->>DMA: List of tape devices
    DMA->>TAP: NDMP Mover Listen
    TAP-->>DMA: Mover Listen accepted
    DMA->>FLR: NDMP Data Start Backup
    FLR->>TAP: Stream file data blocks
    FLR-->>DMA: NDMP FH Add File (metadata)
    DMA->>FLR: NDMP Mover Stop
    FLR->>TAP: Write filemark, unload tape
    FLR-->>DMA: NDMP Mover Halted
    DMA->>FLR: NDMP Disconnect
```

## 4.4 NDMP Architecture Models — Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    m1[NDMP Architecture Models]
    m1 --> mod1[Model 1: Direct Attached]
    m1 --> mod2[Model 2: Three Way LAN Free]
    m1 --> mod3[Model 3: Two Way DMA Mediated]
    mod1 --> d1a[Filer holds tape]
    mod1 --> d1b[Filer acts as Data Mover and Tape Mover]
    mod2 --> d2a[DMA orchestrates]
    mod2 --> d2b[Filer streams to dedicated tape server]
    mod3 --> d3a[DMA reads files from filer]
    mod3 --> d3b[DMA writes to tape]
```

## 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture — NDMP Inside a Filer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    layerA[Application Layer: Backup Job and Schedule]
    layerB[NDMP Service Layer: Session, Connection, Mover, Data, Tape, SCSI, FH, Snapshot]
    layerC[File System Layer: WAFL, NTFS, EXT4, ZFS]
    layerD[Storage Hardware: SATA, SAS, SSD, RAID Groups]
    layerA --> layerB
    layerB --> layerC
    layerC --> layerD
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** This shows the *internal* layering inside an NDMP-aware filer. The vendor-specific filesystem (WAFL on NetApp, ZFS on Oracle, etc.) is hidden behind the standardized NDMP service layer, giving a uniform external interface to any DMA.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define NDMP. Mention its default TCP port.
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 (Model)]**

**Model Answer:**
**NDMP (Network Data Management Protocol)** is an open, vendor-neutral TCP/IP protocol that standardizes the communication between a **Data Management Application (DMA)** and an **NDMP server** embedded in a NAS filer for backup, recovery, and other data management operations.
- **Default TCP Port:** **10000**
- It is governed by the **SNIA** standards body.
- It enables **server-free / LAN-free backup** architectures.

### Question 2: Differentiate between the Data Mover and the Tape Mover in NDMP.
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)]**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Data Mover | Tape Mover |
|---|---|---|
| **Function** | Reads/Writes file data from/to the **filesystem** | Reads/Writes data to/from the **physical tape device** |
| **Resides in** | NDMP server (filer) | Either filer or dedicated tape server |
| **Operates on** | Filesystem blocks / file streams | Tape block, filemarks, scsi commands |
| **Control Plane** | `NDMP_DATA_*` messages | `NDMP_TAPE_*`, `NDMP_SCSI_*` messages |
| **Example Op** | `NDMP_DATA_START_BACKUP` | `NDMP_TAPE_WRITE`, `NDMP_TAPE_REWIND` |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain in detail the **three backup architectures** supported by NDMP with suitable diagrams. Compare their LAN bandwidth consumption. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Describe the **major NDMP interfaces** (Session, Mover, Data, Tape, SCSI, File History) and state the purpose of each. Mention the NDMP version that introduced the Snapshot interface. **[7 Marks]**

### Question B (14 Marks)

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **operation of three-way (LAN-free) backup** using NDMP. Explain the role of the DMA, the filer, and the tape server. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** What is **DAR (Direct Access Recovery)**? In which NDMP version was it introduced, and how does it reduce recovery time for individual files? Justify with a numerical example. **[7 Marks]**

---

## 5.3 Detailed Model Solutions

### Model Solution — Question A (a)

**NDMP Backup Architecture Models:**

**1. Direct-Attached / Server-Free (Local) Backup:**
- The **tape device is physically attached to the filer** (e.g., via SCSI, FC, or SAS).
- The filer acts as **both** the Data Mover and the Tape Mover.
- The DMA only exchanges control messages with the filer.
- **LAN Bandwidth Used:** **0%** of backup data traverses the LAN.

**2. Three-Way / LAN-Free Backup:**
- The tape library is connected to a **dedicated tape server** (not to the filer).
- Control flow: DMA ⇄ Filer; DMA ⇄ Tape Server.
- Data flow: Filer → Tape Server (over SAN/FC).
- The filer's **Data Mover** reads file data; the tape server's **Tape Mover** writes to tape.
- **LAN Bandwidth Used:** **<0.1%** (control messages only).

**3. Two-Way / DMA-Mediated Backup:**
- The DMA reads file data **from the filer over the LAN** and writes it to tape.
- This is the **legacy, non-LAN-free** approach.
- **LAN Bandwidth Used:** **100%** of backup data traverses the LAN.

**Comparison Table:**

| Architecture | LAN Use | Scalability | Filer Load | Best For |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Direct-Attached | 0% | Low | High | Small filers |
| Three-Way | ~0% | High | Medium | Enterprise NAS |
| Two-Way | 100% | Low | Low | Legacy systems |

**Diagram:** (Refer to Section 4.4)

---

### Model Solution — Question A (b)

**NDMP Interfaces:**

1. **Session Interface** — Manages the lifecycle of an NDMP connection. Initiates and terminates sessions. Used for authentication and capability negotiation.
2. **Connection Interface** — Establishes and tears down TCP connections between NDMP hosts.
3. **MOVER Interface** — Central control for the data transfer entity. Messages: `LISTEN`, `START`, `STOP`, `ABORT`, `PAUSE`, `CONTINUE`, `HALTED`.
4. **Data Interface** — Streams the actual file data. Messages: `READ`, `WRITE`, `START_BACKUP`, `START_RECOVER`, `GET_STATE`.
5. **Tape Interface** — High-level tape operations: `WRITE`, `READ`, `REWIND`, `WRITE_FILE_MARK`, `POSITION`.
6. **SCSI Interface** — Low-level pass-through for SCSI commands to the tape drive.
7. **File History Interface** — Reports metadata (path, size, inode, mtime) of each backed-up file via `FH_ADD_FILE`, `FH_ADD_DIR`, `FH_ADD_NODE`.
8. **Snapshot Interface** — Manages point-in-time snapshots. **Introduced in NDMP v5**.

---

### Model Solution — Question B (a)

**Three-Way (LAN-Free) Backup Operation — Block Diagram:**

*(Refer to Section 4.1)*

**Roles:**
- **DMA (Backup Server):** Orchestrator. Sends control commands, receives file history, manages tape catalog, schedules jobs.
- **Filer (NDMP Server):** Hosts the file data. Its internal **Data Mover** reads file streams. The **MOVER** interface controls the data flow direction.
- **Tape Server:** Holds the physical tape drives. Its **Tape Mover** receives data and writes to tape.

**Sequence:**
1. DMA establishes two NDMP connections — one to filer, one to tape server.
2. DMA issues `MOVER_LISTEN` to the tape server.
3. DMA issues `DATA_START_BACKUP` to the filer.
4. Filer's Data Mover streams data directly to the tape server's Tape Mover.
5. Filer sends `FH_ADD_FILE` messages to DMA with metadata.
6. DMA issues `MOVER_STOP` to terminate.

The **LAN carries only control and metadata**, not backup data.

---

### Model Solution — Question B (b)

**DAR (Direct Access Recovery):**
- **Definition:** DAR is a mechanism introduced in **NDMP v4** that allows a DMA to position the tape **directly** to the filemark of a specific file to be restored, without sequentially reading all prior files.
- **Mechanism:** The DMA looks up the file's filemark number from the file history database, sends `NDMP_TAPE_POSITION` with the filemark index, and the Tape Mover **fast-seeks** to that location.

**Numerical Justification:**
- Given: $k = 1000$ files before the target, $B_{file} = 100\text{ MB}$, $V_{seek} = 5\text{ m/s}$ (tape streaming head pass), $V_{position} = 0.5\text{ s}$, $R_{tape} = 120\text{ MB/s}$, $B_{Fx} = 100\text{ MB}$.

$$T_{linear} = \frac{k \cdot B_{file}}{V_{seek}} + \frac{B_{Fx}}{R_{tape}}$$

$$T_{linear} = \frac{1000 \cdot 100\text{ MB}}{5} + \frac{100}{120} = 20{,}000.83\text{ sec} \approx 5.56\text{ hours}$$

$$T_{DAR} = \frac{V_{position}}{V_{seek}} + \frac{B_{Fx}}{R_{tape}} = 0.1 + 0.83 = 0.93\text{ sec}$$

$$\text{Speedup Factor} = \frac{T_{linear}}{T_{DAR}} \approx 21{,}500\times$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Alert — Where students commonly lose marks:**
> 1. **Confusing Data Mover with Tape Mover** — Remember: Data Mover handles *files*; Tape Mover handles *tapes*.
> 2. **Forgetting the default port 10000** — frequently asked in 3-mark questions.
> 3. **Wrong NDMP version for DAR** — DAR is NDMP **v4** (NOT v3, NOT v5).
> 4. **Wrong NDMP version for Snapshot Interface** — Snapshot is NDMP **v5**.
> 5. **Misclassifying three-way backup as two-way** — Three-way means the filer streams **directly** to the tape server, NOT through the DMA.
> 6. **Forgetting DAR's time formula** — The speedup calculation is a high-value numerical question; practice it.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Topic: NDMP**

- [x] **NDMP = Network Data Management Protocol** — open standard for NAS backup, governed by **SNIA**.
- [x] **Default TCP Port = 10000**.
- [x] **Three architectural models:** Direct-Attached (Server-Free), **Three-Way (LAN-Free)** ← *most important*, and Two-Way (DMA-Mediated).
- [x] **Key actors:** DMA, NDMP Server (filer), Tape Server, Tape Library, **MOVER**.
- [x] **Data Mover** ↔ file data; **Tape Mover** ↔ tape device.
- [x] **Three-Way backup saves ~99.99% of LAN bandwidth** for bulk data.
- [x] **NDMP v4 introduced DAR (Direct Access Recovery)** — speedup of thousands of times for selective restores.
- [x] **NDMP v5 introduced Snapshot Interface** + IPv6.
- [x] **Key Interfaces:** Session, Connection, MOVER, Data, Tape, SCSI, File History, Snapshot.
- [x] **DAR formula:** $T_{DAR} = \frac{V_{position}}{V_{seek}} + \frac{B_{file}}{R_{tape}}$.
- [x] **Parallel tape window:** $T_{par} = \frac{D_{fil}}{N_{tapes} \cdot R_{tape}}$.
- [x] **File History Interface** sends metadata (path, size, mtime) — critical for cataloging.
- [x] **NDMP is asymmetric** — DMA is the master, filer/tape server are slaves.
- [x] **Bandwidth Savings Ratio** $\eta_{LAN} = \frac{D_{LAN,2way} - D_{LAN,3way}}{D_{LAN,2way}} \approx 99.99\%$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Backup Types - Full Backups
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECST867 - STORAGE SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Backup Types - Full Backups # Module 3: Backup Types - Full Backups ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Full Backup (Definition - KTU Syllabus Aligned):** > A **Full Backup** is a complete, comprehensive backup operation in which **every selected file, folder, data block, and system state** within the protected dataset is copied from the primary storage system to the backup media or secondary storage target, regardless of whether the data has been modified since the previous backup. It represents the **baseline copy** upon which all other incremental and differential backup strategies are anchored. In the context of enterprise storage systems governed by **Recovery Point Objective (RPO)** and **Recovery Time Objective (RTO)** Service Level Agreements (SLAs), a full backup is the most data-redundant but also the most resource-intensive backup methodology. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight:** > Under the **PECST867 - Storage Systems** syllabus, full backups form the foundational concept of the **Information Lifecycle Management (ILM)** module. Examiners frequently test the trade-off analysis between backup frequency, backup window, and storage capacity utilization. ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you have a **personal diary** that you write in every day. There are two ways you might preserve it: 1. **Full Backup Analogy:** Every Sunday, you photocopy the **entire diary from page 1 to the last written page** and store the copy in a safe. Even if only one line changed since last Sunday, the entire diary gets re-photocopied. 2. **The Result:** Restoration is **trivially easy** — you just take last Sunday's complete copy. But the cost is high — you consume a lot of paper, the photocopying takes a long time (large **backup window**), and the photocopy machine is busy (high **I/O load** on the production system). This is exactly how a **Full Backup** behaves in a storage environment: - ✅ **Pros:** Fastest recovery, simplest restore procedure, self-contained. - ❌ **Cons:** Largest storage footprint, longest backup window, highest network/IO bandwidth consumption. ### Key Standard Metrics - **RPO (Recovery Point Objective):** $\mathbf{0}$ to **24 hours** (depending on backup frequency). - **RTO (Recovery Time Objective):** Typically **lowest** among all backup types, because only one tape/image set is needed. - **De-duplication ratio (typical):** $\mathbf{2{:}1}$ to $\mathbf{20{:}1}$ when using post-process or inline dedup on full backups. - **Default compression ratio:** $\mathbf{1.5{:}1}$ to $\mathbf{2{:}1}$. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Workflow of a Full Backup The execution of a full backup follows a deterministic, sequential pipeline. Understanding this pipeline is critical for KTU exam questions on **backup architecture**. ### Step-by-Step Operational Logic: 1. **Snapshot Trigger** — A scheduled job, an administrator's manual command, or an event-driven trigger (e.g., pre-shutdown hook) initiates the backup agent. 2. **Metadata Enumeration** — The backup software scans the **file system tree** (or block-level volume) and builds an inventory list of all in-scope objects. 3. **Data Read Phase** — The agent reads data blocks from the **primary storage** through the production I/O path. 4. **Optional Processing Phase** — Inline operations may occur: - **Compression** (e.g., LZ4, ZSTD) - **Encryption** (AES-256) - **De-duplication** (hash-based, variable or fixed chunking) 5. **Data Write Phase** — The processed payload is transmitted over the **backup network** (LAN/SAN/dedicated backup VLAN) to the **backup target** (tape library, VTL, object store, cloud). 6. **Catalog Update** — The backup server's index database is updated with the new **backup set ID**, timestamp, and protected object map. 7. **Verification / Checksum** — Optional integrity check via CRC32, SHA-256, or MD5 hashes. > [!NOTE] > **Why "Full" Matters Architecturally:** > Because every full backup is **independently restorable**, it eliminates **restore-time dependency chains** — a problem that plagues incremental backups where you need the last full + every subsequent incremental in correct sequence. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet The following table summarizes the **mathematical relationships** essential for full backup problems in KTU examinations. | # | Parameter | Formula | Description | Typical Units | |---|-----------|---------|-------------|---------------| | 1 | Total Backup Size | $S_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} D_i$ | Sum of all protected data objects $D_i$ | Bytes, TB | | 2 | Compressed Backup Size | $S_{comp} = S_{total} \times (1 - C_r)$ | Where $C_r$ is the compression ratio (e.g., 0.5 for 50% reduction) | Bytes, TB | | 3 | Effective Backup Size (with Dedup) | $S_{eff} = S_{comp} / D_r$ | Where $D_r$ is the deduplication ratio | Bytes, TB | | 4 | Backup Window | $T_{window} = \frac{S_{total}}{T_{throughput}}$ | Time required to complete backup | Hours, Minutes | | 5 | Required Throughput | $T_{throughput} = \frac{S_{total}}{T_{window}}$ | Minimum backup pipeline bandwidth | MB/s, GB/hr | | 6 | Weekly Storage (Full Daily) | $S_{week} = 7 \times S_{full}$ | Seven daily full backups retained | TB | | 7 | Monthly Storage Growth | $S_{month} = 30 \times S_{full} \times C_{retention}$ | Total full backups stored over retention | TB | | 8 | Recovery Time (Full) | $RTO_{full} = \frac{S_{total}}{R_{throughput}} + T_{overhead}$ | Time to restore from one full backup | Hours | | 9 | RPO (Full Daily) | $RPO = T_{interval} = 24 \text{ hours}$ | Maximum data loss window | Hours | | 10 | Cost per GB | $C_{gb} = \frac{C_{media} + C_{ops}}{S_{total}}$ | Total Cost of Ownership per GB | $/GB | ### Real-World Engineering Utility In production data centers, full backups are used for: - **Bare-Metal Recovery (BMR)** — Restoring an entire server from scratch. - **Initial baseline** for incremental/differential backup chains. - **Quarterly / Yearly archival** snapshots for compliance (SOX, HIPAA, GDPR). - **Disaster Recovery (DR) site seeding** — Initial bulk data transfer to a remote site. - **Legal hold / Forensic preservation** — Court-admissible point-in-time copies. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Backup Window Calculation for a Full Backup > **KTU 2024 Typical Question Pattern:** > *"A storage administrator needs to back up 4 TB of data nightly within a 6-hour backup window. Calculate the minimum throughput required, and suggest a strategy if the existing infrastructure cannot meet this requirement."* ### Problem Setup Given: - Total data to back up: $S_{total} = 4 \text{ TB}$ - Allowed backup window: $T_{window} = 6 \text{ hours}$ - Current backup network link: 1 Gbps Ethernet - Compression capability: $C_r = 50\%$ (i.e., 0.5) - Deduplication ratio: $D_r = 3{:}1$ ### Step 1: Unit Standardization $$4 \text{ TB} = 4 \times 1024 \times 1024 \text{ MB} = 4{,}194{,}304 \text{ MB}$$ This follows the binary (TiB) convention used by backup software like **NetBackup**, **CommVault**, and **Veeam**. For network engineering, sometimes the **decimal TB** is used: $$4 \text{ TB (decimal)} = 4 \times 10^6 \text{ MB} = 4{,}000{,}000 \text{ MB}$$ ### Step 2: Required Throughput Before Optimization $$ \begin{aligned} T_{throughput} &= \frac{S_{total}}{T_{window}} \\[6pt] T_{throughput} &= \frac{4 \text{ TB}}{6 \text{ hours}} \\[6pt] T_{throughput} &= \frac{4{,}194{,}304 \text{ MB}}{6 \times 3600 \text{ seconds}} \\[6pt] T_{throughput} &= \frac{4{,}194{,}304 \text{ MB}}{21{,}600 \text{ s}} \\[6pt] T_{throughput} &\approx 194.18 \text{ MB/s} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 3: Convert to Gbps (Network Units) $$ \begin{aligned} 1 \text{ MB/s} &= 8 \text{ Mb/s} \\[4pt] T_{throughput} &= 194.18 \times 8 \text{ Mb/s} \\[4pt] T_{throughput} &\approx 1553.4 \text{ Mb/s} \approx 1.55 \text{ Gbps} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 4: Compare Against 1 Gbps Link The 1 Gbps Ethernet link can deliver at most (after protocol overhead of ~10%): $$ T_{max} = 1 \text{ Gbps} \times 0.9 = 900 \text{ Mb/s} = 112.5 \text{ MB/s} $$ This is **insufficient** for the raw data. We need compression and dedup. ### Step 5: Apply Compression + Deduplication $$ \begin{aligned} S_{eff} &= \frac{S_{total} \times (1 - C_r)}{D_r} \\[6pt] S_{eff} &= \frac{4 \text{ TB} \times 0.5}{3} \\[6pt] S_{eff} &= \frac{2 \text{ TB}}{3} \\[6pt] S_{eff} &\approx 0.667 \text{ TB} = 683 \text{ GB} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 6: Recompute Required Throughput (Post-Optimization) $$ \begin{aligned} T_{throughput}^{opt} &= \frac{S_{eff}}{T_{window}} \\[6pt] &= \frac{683 \text{ GB}}{6 \text{ hours}} \\[6pt] &= \frac{683{,}000 \text{ MB}}{21{,}600 \text{ s}} \\[6pt] &\approx 31.62 \text{ MB/s} \\[4pt] &\approx 253 \text{ Mb/s} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 7: Final Verdict $$ T_{throughput}^{opt} = 253 \text{ Mb/s} \quad \ll \quad T_{max} = 900 \text{ Mb/s} $$ **Conclusion:** The 1 Gbps link is **sufficient** if compression and dedup are enabled. ✅ ### Step 8: Headroom Analysis $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Headroom} &= T_{max} - T_{throughput}^{opt} \\[4pt] &= 900 - 253 = 647 \text{ Mb/s} \end{aligned} $$ The system has $\approx 72\%$ bandwidth headroom, which is healthy for a production backup pipeline. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Monthly Backup Storage Consumption > **Use Case:** Calculate storage capacity required for a data center running **daily full backups** with a **30-day retention policy** plus **monthly archives kept for 1 year**. ### Step 1: Daily Backup Footprint $$ S_{daily} = S_{full} = 4 \text{ TB (post-dedup/compression: 683 GB)} $$ ### Step 2: 30-Day Rolling Window $$ \begin{aligned} S_{30} &= 30 \times S_{daily} \\[4pt] &= 30 \times 683 \text{ GB} \\[4pt] &= 20{,}490 \text{ GB} \approx 20.5 \text{ TB} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 3: 12 Monthly Archives $$ \begin{aligned} S_{12} &= 12 \times S_{daily} \\[4pt] &= 12 \times 683 \text{ GB} \approx 8.2 \text{ TB} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 4: Total Storage Demand $$ \begin{aligned} S_{total} &= S_{30} + S_{12} \\[4pt] &= 20.5 + 8.2 = 28.7 \text{ TB} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 5: Add 25% Slack for Overhead $$ S_{final} = 28.7 \times 1.25 \approx 35.9 \text{ TB} $$ > **Rule of Thumb:** Always provision **20–30% additional capacity** beyond the calculated requirement to accommodate backup metadata, snapshots, and growth. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Trade-Off Matrix (Full vs. Other Backup Types) > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU examiners love **comparison tables**. The following is the **definitive comparison** for backup-type questions. | Attribute | **Full Backup** | Incremental Backup | Differential Backup | |-----------|-----------------|--------------------|--------------------| | **Data Captured** | All data | Only changes since last backup (any type) | Changes since last **full** backup | | **Storage Space** | 🔴 **Highest** (baseline × frequency) | 🟢 Lowest | 🟡 Moderate (grows daily) | | **Backup Speed** | 🔴 Slowest | 🟢 Fastest | 🟡 Moderate | | **Restore Speed** | 🟢 **Fastest** (1 tape) | 🔴 Slowest (chain of N tapes) | 🟡 Fast (1 full + 1 diff) | | **Restore Complexity** | 🟢 Trivial | 🔴 Complex ordering | 🟡 Simple | | **Network Bandwidth** | 🔴 High | 🟢 Low | 🟡 Moderate | | **RPO** | 🟢 Best (short interval) | 🟡 Depends on frequency | 🟡 Depends on frequency | | **RTO** | 🟢 Best | 🔴 Worst | 🟡 Good | | **Cost (TCO)** | 🔴 High | 🟢 Low | 🟡 Medium | | **Best For** | Periodic baseline, BMR | Daily/hourly low-change data | Balance of speed + recovery | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Examiner's Insight:** The "best" backup type is always a **hybrid strategy** — typically **weekly full + daily incremental** (the "Grandfather-Father-Son" or GFS rotation scheme). --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Full Backup Data Flow Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram) > [!NOTE] > The following **Mermaid block diagram** illustrates the end-to-end data path of a full backup operation, from the production server through the backup infrastructure to the storage target. ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph PROD["PRODUCTION ENVIRONMENT"] A1[Application Server] --> A2[File System / Volume] A2 --> A3[Data Blocks
4 TB Total] end subgraph BKAGENT["BACKUP AGENT LAYER"] B1[Backup Client Agent] --> B2[Pre-Processing Engine] B2 --> B3{Optimization
Enabled?} B3 -- Yes --> B4[Compression Module
LZ4 or ZSTD] B4 --> B5[Deduplication Engine
Hash-Based Chunking] B5 --> B6[Encryption Layer
AES-256] B3 -- No --> B6 B6 --> B7[Backup Stream
683 GB Effective] end subgraph BKNET["BACKUP NETWORK"] C1[1 Gbps / 10 Gbps
Dedicated VLAN] --> C2[TLS / IPsec Tunnel] end subgraph BKSVR["BACKUP SERVER"] D1[Media Server] --> D2[Catalog Database
Metadata Index] D2 --> D3[Backup Set Registry] end subgraph BKTGT["BACKUP TARGETS"] E1[Dedup Appliance
e.g., DataDomain] E2[Tape Library
LTO-9] E3[Object Storage
S3-compatible] E4[Cloud Vault
AWS S3 Glacier] end A3 --> B1 B7 --> C1 C2 --> D1 D3 --> E1 D3 --> E2 D3 --> E3 D3 --> E4 ``` ## 4.2 Full Backup Lifecycle State Machine ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Idle Idle --> Scheduled: Cron / Calendar Trigger Idle --> Manual: Admin Command Scheduled --> Scanning: Job Started Manual --> Scanning: Job Started Scanning --> Reading: Inventory Built Reading --> Processing: Data Acquired Processing --> Writing: Compressed / Encrypted Writing --> Verifying: Checksum Computed Verifying --> Success: Integrity OK Verifying --> Failed: Checksum Mismatch Writing --> Failed: I/O Error / Disk Full Success --> CatalogUpdate: Index Updated CatalogUpdate --> Idle: Ready for Next Cycle Failed --> Alerting: Notify Admin Alerting --> Idle: Issue Resolved Failed --> [*]: Job Terminated ``` ## 4.3 Backup Type Comparison Schematic ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph FB["FULL BACKUP"] F1[Mon: ALL 4 TB] F2[Tue: ALL 4 TB] F3[Wed: ALL 4 TB] end subgraph IB["INCREMENTAL BACKUP"] I1[Mon: Full 4 TB] I2[Tue: +200 MB] I3[Wed: +150 MB] end subgraph DB["DIFFERENTIAL BACKUP"] D1[Mon: Full 4 TB] D2[Tue: +350 MB since Mon] D3[Wed: +500 MB since Mon] end FB --> RESTORE1[Restore: 1 Set Needed] IB --> RESTORE2[Restore: Mon + Tue + Wed] DB --> RESTORE3[Restore: Mon + Wed] ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap > [!NOTE] > The following questions are modeled precisely on **KTU 2024 Scheme End Semester Examination (ESE)** patterns for PECST867 - Storage Systems. Each question is tagged with a simulated KTU past-year reference and aligned with the relevant **Course Outcome (CO)** and **Bloom's Taxonomy Level**. --- ## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **CO3 / Bloom's Level: Remember** **Q: Define a Full Backup. List any two of its key advantages.** **Model Answer:** A **Full Backup** is a backup operation in which the **entire protected dataset** — every file, directory, and system block within the defined scope — is copied from primary storage to the backup target, irrespective of the last modification timestamp. **Two Key Advantages:** 1. **Simplest Recovery Procedure:** A full backup is self-contained. Restoration requires only the most recent full backup set, eliminating dependency chains and drastically reducing RTO. 2. **Maximum Data Integrity Baseline:** Since every byte is captured, full backups serve as a reliable **recovery anchor** for incremental and differential backup strategies, ensuring no cumulative drift in restore operations. *(Examiner's note: A 3-mark answer should have a clean definition + 2 distinct points. Avoid generic "it's safe" answers.)* --- ### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **CO3 / Bloom's Level: Understand** **Q: Why is a daily full backup strategy generally considered inefficient in large enterprises?** **Model Answer:** A daily full backup strategy is considered **inefficient** in large enterprises due to three primary reasons: 1. **Excessive Storage Consumption:** If the dataset is, say, 10 TB, then seven daily full backups consume 70 TB of backup storage per week, leading to rapid capacity exhaustion and high media costs. 2. **Prolonged Backup Window:** Copying the entire dataset daily consumes significant I/O bandwidth, which can conflict with production workloads during business hours and force backups into narrow off-peak windows. 3. **Redundant Data Transfer:** The majority of enterprise data (typically **80–90%**) does not change daily, meaning the same unmodified bytes are re-transmitted and re-stored repeatedly — wasting network, CPU, and storage resources. Hence, enterprises typically use a **hybrid strategy** (e.g., weekly full + daily incremental). --- ## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU 2024 Scheme Part B questions carry **14 marks** with internal choice. Each question typically has **two sub-parts (a) and (b) of 7 marks each**. Students must answer **either the full OR the half** of the question. --- ### **Question A (Choice 1)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]` **CO3 / CO4 / Bloom's Level: Apply + Analyze** **Q: (a)** Explain the operational workflow of a Full Backup in detail, including the role of the **backup agent**, **media server**, and **backup target**. Illustrate with a labeled block diagram. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A company's primary file server hosts **6 TB of user data**. The backup policy mandates a **daily full backup** with a maximum backup window of **5 hours**. The available network link is **2 Gbps Ethernet** with ~10% protocol overhead. The backup solution offers **2:1 compression** and **4:1 deduplication**. Calculate: 1. The raw throughput required **(2 marks)** 2. The effective throughput after compression and deduplication **(2 marks)** 3.
