Whether the 2 Gbps link is sufficient, with justification **(3 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — Workflow Explanation:** The operational workflow of a Full Backup consists of **six sequential stages**: 1. **Initialization:** A scheduled event (cron, Windows Task Scheduler, or backup orchestrator like **Veeam** or **NetBackup**) triggers the backup client agent on the production host. 2. **Enumeration / Inventory:** The backup agent scans the file system and builds a complete inventory of in-scope objects (files, folders, application data, system state). 3. **Data Acquisition:** The agent reads every data block from the production volume — this is the defining characteristic of a **full** backup, distinguishing it from incremental/differential methods. 4. **Processing Pipeline:** The data stream passes through optional stages: - **Compression** (LZ4, ZSTD) to reduce payload size. - **Deduplication** to eliminate redundant chunks. - **Encryption** (AES-256) for data-in-transit and at-rest security. 5. **Transport & Storage:** The processed stream is sent over the **backup network** to the **media server**, which then writes it to the **backup target** (tape library, VTL, disk appliance, or cloud object store). 6. **Catalog & Verification:** The backup server's catalog database is updated with metadata (timestamps, file lists, checksums), and integrity verification is performed. *(Block diagram: See Section 4.1 of these notes.)* **Part (b) — Numerical Solution:** Given: - $S_{total} = 6 \text{ TB} = 6{,}291{,}456 \text{ MB}$ - $T_{window} = 5 \text{ hours} = 18{,}000 \text{ seconds}$ - Link: 2 Gbps with 10% overhead - $C_r = 50\%$ → Compression factor = 0.5 - $D_r = 4{:}1$ **(i) Raw Throughput Required:** $$ \begin{aligned} T_{raw} &= \frac{6{,}291{,}456 \text{ MB}}{18{,}000 \text{ s}} \\[4pt] &\approx 349.5 \text{ MB/s} \\[4pt] &\approx 2796 \text{ Mb/s} \approx 2.80 \text{ Gbps} \end{aligned} $$ **(ii) Effective Throughput After Compression + Dedup:** $$ \begin{aligned} S_{eff} &= \frac{6 \text{ TB} \times 0.5}{4} = 0.75 \text{ TB} = 768{,}000 \text{ MB} \\[4pt] T_{eff} &= \frac{768{,}000 \text{ MB}}{18{,}000 \text{ s}} \\[4pt] &\approx 42.67 \text{ MB/s} \\[4pt] &\approx 341.3 \text{ Mb/s} \end{aligned} $$ **(iii) Sufficiency Check:** Available throughput on 2 Gbps link: $$ T_{available} = 2 \text{ Gbps} \times 0.9 = 1.8 \text{ Gbps} = 1800 \text{ Mb/s} $$ Comparison: $$ T_{eff} = 341.3 \text{ Mb/s} \quad \ll \quad T_{available} = 1800 \text{ Mb/s} $$ **Verdict:** ✅ The 2 Gbps link is **more than sufficient** to back up 6 TB within the 5-hour window when compression (2:1) and deduplication (4:1) are enabled. The pipeline operates at only **~19% of available capacity**, leaving ample headroom. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Unit Conversion Mistakes:** Forgetting to convert MB/s to Mb/s (multiply by 8). This alone can cost **2–3 marks**. > 2. **Skipping protocol overhead:** Not accounting for TCP/IP, Ethernet frame, and storage protocol overhead (~8–12%) shows incomplete understanding. > 3. **Decimal vs. Binary TB confusion:** Always state which convention you are using (1 TB = 1024 GB or 1000 GB). > 4. **Forgetting to state assumptions** (e.g., "Assuming 90% efficiency of the link"). --- ### **Question B (Choice 2 — Internal Choice Alternative)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]` **CO3 / Bloom's Level: Analyze + Evaluate** **Q: (a)** Compare and contrast **Full Backup**, **Incremental Backup**, and **Differential Backup** strategies with respect to: storage requirement, backup speed, restore speed, and recovery complexity. Use a suitable comparison table in your answer. **(7 marks)** **(b)** An enterprise has **20 TB of critical data**. The IT team must choose between two backup strategies: - **Strategy 1:** Daily full backups with 7-day retention. - **Strategy 2:** Weekly full + daily incremental backups with 4-week retention. Assume an average **5% daily change rate** in the dataset. Calculate the **total weekly backup storage** for each strategy and recommend the better approach with justification. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — Comparison Table:** | Parameter | Full Backup | Incremental Backup | Differential Backup | |-----------|-------------|--------------------|--------------------| | Data Captured per Run | 100% of dataset | Only changes since last backup | Changes since last full backup | | Daily Storage (20 TB, 5% change) | 20 TB | ~1 TB | Growing 1→2→3→4→5→6→7 TB | | Weekly Storage | $7 \times 20 = 140$ TB | $20 + 6 \times 1 = 26$ TB | $20 + 21 = 41$ TB | | Backup Speed | Slowest | Fastest | Moderate | | Restore Speed | Fastest (1 set) | Slowest (chain of 7) | Fast (1 full + 1 diff) | | Recovery Complexity | Lowest | Highest | Moderate | | Best Use Case | Baseline, archival | Frequent backups, minimal change | Balance of speed + simplicity | **Part (b) — Numerical Analysis:** Given: - Dataset size $D = 20 \text{ TB}$ - Daily change rate = 5% → $\Delta = 0.05 \times 20 = 1 \text{ TB/day}$ **Strategy 1: Daily Full Backup (7-day retention)** $$ S_{S1} = 7 \times 20 \text{ TB} = 140 \text{ TB} $$ **Strategy 2: Weekly Full + Daily Incremental (4-week retention)** In one week: - 1 full backup = 20 TB - 6 incremental backups = $6 \times 1 \text{ TB} = 6 \text{ TB}$ - **Weekly total** = $20 + 6 = 26 \text{ TB}$ In 4 weeks: - 4 weekly full backups = $4 \times 20 = 80 \text{ TB}$ - 24 daily incremental backups (4 weeks × 6 days) = $24 \times 1 = 24 \text{ TB}$ - **Total retention storage** = $80 + 24 = 104 \text{ TB}$ However, the question asks for **total weekly backup storage** (i.e., a 1-week comparison): $$ S_{S2, \text{week}} = 26 \text{ TB} $$ **Comparison and Recommendation:** | Strategy | Weekly Storage | Pros | Cons | |----------|----------------|------|------| | Strategy 1 (Daily Full) | 140 TB | Simple restore, independent sets | Massive storage cost | | Strategy 2 (Weekly Full + Daily Incr.) | 26 TB | ~81% storage savings | Slower, more complex restore | **Recommendation:** **Strategy 2 is strongly recommended** because it provides an **81.4% storage reduction** ($1 - 26/140 = 0.814$) while still maintaining a **weekly recovery baseline**. The slightly more complex restore procedure is a worthwhile trade-off for the massive TCO savings. > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes in Comparison Questions:** > 1. **Forgetting cumulative growth** in differential backups (it grows daily until the next full). > 2. **Mixing up incremental and differential definitions** — this is the #1 reason students lose marks. > 3. **Not justifying the recommendation** with quantified metrics. > 4. **Ignoring restore complexity** when comparing strategies. --- ## 5. Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize for KTU Exams:** ### Core Concepts - ✅ A **Full Backup** copies **100% of the protected data** every time it runs, regardless of modification status. - ✅ It is the **baseline** for all incremental and differential backup strategies. - ✅ Full backups offer the **fastest RTO** but the **highest storage cost** and **longest backup window**. ### Key Formulas - ✅ **Backup Window:** $T_{window} = S_{total} / T_{throughput}$ - ✅ **Compressed + Dedup Size:** $S_{eff} = S_{total} \times (1 - C_r) / D_r$ - ✅ **Weekly Storage (Daily Full):** $S_{week} = 7 \times S_{full}$ - ✅ **Throughput Conversion:** $1 \text{ MB/s} = 8 \text{ Mb/s}$ ### Critical Trade-offs - ✅ **Full vs. Incremental:** Full = high storage, fast restore. Incremental = low storage, slow restore. - ✅ **Full vs. Differential:** Full = independent sets, high cost. Differential = grows daily, moderate cost. - ✅ **Rule of 80/20:** In most enterprises, only **10–20% of data changes daily** → full backups are **inefficient** at high frequency. ### Real-World Architectures - ✅ **GFS (Grandfather-Father-Son):** Monthly (grandfather) + Weekly (father) + Daily (son) — industry standard. - ✅ **Tower of Hanoi:** Sophisticated rotation scheme optimizing tape usage. - ✅ **3-2-1 Rule:** 3 copies of data, on 2 different media, with 1 offsite. ### Common KTU Traps - ✅ Always convert units explicitly (MB ↔ Mb, TB ↔ GB). - ✅ Always state assumptions (compression ratio, dedup ratio, overhead %). - ✅ Always include a **block diagram** in long-answer questions for full marks. - ✅ Remember: **Backup Window ≠ Restore Window** — they are inverse relationships in incremental backups. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** > For 14-mark questions on full backups, structure your answer as: > **(a)** Definition + Workflow + Diagram (7 marks) > **(b)** Numerical Problem with full unit conversions + Verdict (7 marks) > Always end with a **practical recommendation** — examiners reward applied thinking. ---

topics.title: Incremental Backups
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECST867 - STORAGE SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Incremental Backups # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition An **Incremental Backup** is a data protection strategy that captures and stores only the data blocks or files that have changed since the **most recent backup operation of any type** (whether that previous backup was a full backup or a prior incremental backup). Each successive incremental contains only the *delta* — the precise set of new or modified data — making it the most storage-efficient backup methodology in the enterprise storage hierarchy. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight — PECST867 / Module 3** > Incremental Backups form the backbone of modern **Data Protection & Disaster Recovery** strategies, sitting alongside full, differential, and synthetic full backups. The defining invariant of an incremental is: *"It stores the *change* since the last backup event, not the last full backup."* The original KTU definition (per the 2024 Scheme syllabus descriptor) reads: > *"An incremental backup copies only the data that has changed since the last backup (full or incremental), providing optimal storage utilization at the cost of a more complex restore procedure."* ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Photo Album Story Imagine you maintain a **photo album** of your life's memories: - A **Full Backup** is like reprinting the *entire album* from scratch every Sunday — safe, but hugely wasteful of paper, ink, and time. - A **Differential Backup** is like keeping the original album and then, every week, adding *all photos taken since the very first album was created* — the "diff" from the original grows cumulatively. - An **Incremental Backup** is the smartest strategy: you keep the original album, and *each week you only add the photos taken since last Sunday*. If you took 3 photos Monday, 1 on Tuesday, and 2 on Wednesday, this week's incremental contains just those **6 new photos** — nothing more. To rebuild the album from incremental backups, you must: 1. Take the **full album** (the foundation). 2. Lay down **Incremental 1** (this week's photos). 3. Lay down **Incremental 2** (this week's new photos). 4. … and so on, in **strict chronological order**, until the most recent incremental. Skip a step, and the picture is **incomplete**. ## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics The following standard metrics characterize an incremental backup system: - **Change Rate (CR):** Fraction of the data set modified between two backup windows. Typical production databases exhibit **2 %–10 %** daily change rates. - **Backup Window (BW):** Maximum time allowed for the backup job to complete. Often expressed in **minutes** (e.g., **60 min**). - **Recovery Point Objective (RPO):** Maximum acceptable data loss, expressed in time units. - **Recovery Time Objective (RTO):** Maximum acceptable downtime before service restoration. > [!NOTE] > **Critical Distinction (Frequently Tested in KTU):** > *Incremental* backups capture changes since the **last backup of any kind** (including the previous incremental). *Differential* backups capture changes since the **last full backup**. Confusing these two is the single most common error in KTU exam answers on this topic. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Mechanics — How an Incremental Backup Works A true incremental backup operates in the following sequence: 1. **Timestamping & Tracking** The backup client/server maintains a **high-water mark** — a timestamp or bit-map (often called a *Change Block Tracker* or **CBT**) recording which blocks were modified after the previous successful backup. 2. **Differential Scan (or CBT Lookup)** The agent either scans the file system's archive bit / mtime stamps, or consults the CBT bitmap (used by hypervisors like VMware vSphere, Hyper-V, and modern databases) to identify modified extents. 3. **Selective Read** Only the changed blocks/files are read from the source LUN or volume — drastically reducing I/O pressure on production storage. 4. **Deduplication Window** Many backup targets (e.g., Data Domain, StoreOnce) apply **inline deduplication** within the incremental stream, achieving additional reduction ratios of **10:1 to 30:1**. 5. **Catalog Update** The backup catalog records the *backup instance ID*, *chain position*, *parent reference*, and *byte count* for future restoration. 6. **Synthetic Merge (Optional)** Some environments (e.g., NetApp SnapProtect, Veeam) later consolidate the latest full + all incrementals into a **synthetic full backup** for fast restores, without disturbing the production host. ## 2.2 Levels of Incremental Backups KTU examiners often test the *multi-level* taxonomy used by Oracle RMAN and enterprise backup suites: | Level | Scope Captured | Typical Use | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Level 0 (L0)** | Equivalent to a full backup — the **base reference** | Weekly/monthly anchor | | **Level 1 Cumulative** | All changes since the last L0 (i.e., a *differential*) | Mid-week consolidated | | **Level 1 Differential / Incremental** | All changes since the last L1 (cascading) | Daily deltas | > [!NOTE] > **Why "Why" Matters:** Incremental backups exist to solve two problems — *limited backup windows* (you cannot back up terabytes every night) and *limited storage cost* (you cannot retain 365 full copies of a petabyte dataset). Incrementals are the engineering compromise. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **No vertical bars ( `|` ) are used in any cell below — the LaTeX vertical-bar symbol `\vert` is used to prevent markdown table breakage.** | # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | Total backup storage for $n$ incrementals after one full | $S_{\text{total}} = F + \sum_{i=1}^{n} d_i$ | Bytes; $F$ = full size, $d_i$ = incremental $i$ | | 2 | Average incremental size | $\bar{d} = F \cdot CR$ | Bytes; $CR$ = daily change rate | | 3 | Approx. total storage (uniform CR) | $S_{\text{total}} \approx F(1 + n \cdot CR)$ | Bytes | | 4 | Storage vs. full-every-night ratio | $\eta = \dfrac{S_{\text{total}}}{n \cdot F} = \dfrac{1 + n \cdot CR}{n}$ | Dimensionless efficiency | | 5 | Recovery Time Objective (sequential apply) | $RTO = T_{\text{full-restore}} + \sum_{i=1}^{n} T_{d_i}$ | Seconds / minutes | | 6 | Recovery Point Objective | $RPO = T_{\text{backup-interval}}$ | Time units | | 7 | Backup Window consumed (incremental) | $BW = \dfrac{\bar{d}}{T_{\text{throughput}}}$ | Minutes | | 8 | Chain length penalty factor | $C = 1 + \alpha n$ where $\alpha \in [0.02, 0.05]$ | Dimensionless restore-time growth | | 9 | Deduplicated incremental size | $d_i^{\text{dedup}} = d_i \cdot (1 - DR)$ | $DR$ = dedup ratio (e.g., **0.9** for 10:1) | | 10 | Forever-incremental chain size (after $k$ fulls) | $S_{\text{forever}} = k \cdot F + (365 k - k) \cdot F \cdot CR$ | Bytes per year | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Incremental backups underpin the **3-2-1 Backup Rule** (3 copies, 2 media types, 1 offsite) used in nearly every KTU-referenced enterprise deployment: AWS snapshots, Azure Backup, Veeam, NetApp SnapVault, Commvault, and Oracle RMAN all leverage incremental mechanics. They enable **forever-incremental retention** schemes, where the original full is retained and only small deltas accumulate, allowing long-term archival at near-constant storage cost. > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Trade-off Triangle:** > - **Storage Cost** ↓ (good with incrementals) > - **Restore Speed** ↑ / ↓ (worse — long chains = slow) > - **RPO** ↓ (good — small frequent jobs) > KTU questions frequently ask you to *justify* the trade-off triangle for a given workload. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Total Storage of an Incremental Chain **Given:** - A full backup of size $F$ (bytes). - $n$ successive incremental backups, each of size $d_i$. - Assume uniform change rate $CR$ (so $d_i = F \cdot CR$ for all $i$). **Goal:** Derive the closed-form expression for total storage footprint. ### Step 1 — Define the incremental chain Each incremental captures the data changed since the **immediately preceding** backup. The size of the $i$-th incremental is therefore: $$ d_i = F \cdot CR_i $$ where $CR_i$ is the change rate observed between backups $i-1$ and $i$. ### Step 2 — Sum the chain The total storage consumed by the full + all incrementals is: $$ S_{\text{total}} = F + \sum_{i=1}^{n} d_i $$ ### Step 3 — Apply the uniform-change assumption Substituting $d_i = F \cdot CR$: $$ S_{\text{total}} = F + \sum_{i=1}^{n} F \cdot CR $$ ### Step 4 — Factor the constant Since $F \cdot CR$ does not depend on $i$, the summation collapses: $$ S_{\text{total}} = F + n \cdot F \cdot CR $$ ### Step 5 — Final factored form $$ S_{\text{total}} = F \cdot (1 + n \cdot CR) $$ **Interpretation:** For a **1 TB** full backup with **5 % daily change rate** over a **7-day week** ($n = 7$): $$ S_{\text{total}} = 1\,\text{TB} \cdot (1 + 7 \cdot 0.05) = 1\,\text{TB} \cdot 1.35 = 1.35\,\text{TB} $$ Compared to **7 full backups = 7 TB**, this is a **~5.2× reduction**. ## 3.2 Derivation: Recovery Time for a Multi-Level Chain **Given:** - Time to restore the full backup: $T_F$ (proportional to $F$). - Time to apply each incremental: $T_{d_i}$ (proportional to $d_i$). **Step 1 — Sequential dependency** The restore engine *must* apply the full backup first, then incrementals in **strict chain order** (L1 → L2 → … → Ln). The total restore time is the sum: $$ RTO = T_F + \sum_{i=1}^{n} T_{d_i} $$ **Step 2 — Express in terms of data size** Assuming linear read performance with throughput $R$ (bytes/sec): $$ T_F = \frac{F}{R}, \quad T_{d_i} = \frac{d_i}{R} $$ **Step 3 — Substitute** $$ RTO = \frac{F}{R} + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{d_i}{R} = \frac{1}{R}\left( F + \sum_{i=1}^{n} d_i \right) = \frac{S_{\text{total}}}{R} $$ **Step 4 — Conclusion** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Restore time is proportional to the *total* chain size**, not just the most recent incremental. This is why long incremental chains are discouraged; a **synthetic full** or **differential base + weekly consolidations** is preferred when RTO is critical. ## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Full vs. Incremental vs. Differential | Property | Full Backup | Incremental Backup | Differential Backup | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Data Captured | Entire dataset | Changes since *last backup* | Changes since *last full* | | Daily Size | $F$ (constant) | $F \cdot CR$ (small) | $F \cdot CR \cdot i$ (grows) | | Weekly Storage (7 d) | $7F$ | $F(1 + 7 \cdot CR)$ | $F \cdot (1 + 28 \cdot CR)$ | | Restore Complexity | Apply 1 set | Apply $1 + n$ sets in order | Apply 1 full + 1 diff | | Restore Speed | **Fastest** | **Slowest** (chain dependency) | **Moderate** | | Backup Window | Largest | Smallest | Medium (grows) | | RPO | Poor (until next full) | Excellent | Good | | Storage Cost | Highest | Lowest | Medium | | Tape/Media Wear | Highest | Lowest | Medium | | Failure Impact (lost piece) | None (self-contained) | Total loss after that point | Re-apply only last diff | **Numerical sanity check** (assume $F = 1\,\text{TB}$, $CR = 0.05$, $n = 7$): $$ S_{\text{full}} = 7\,\text{TB}, \quad S_{\text{incr}} = 1.35\,\text{TB}, \quad S_{\text{diff}} = 1 \cdot (1 + 4 \cdot 0.05 \cdot 7) = 2.4\,\text{TB} $$ This confirms the **storage ordering**: *Full > Differential > Incremental*. ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Standard) **Problem:** A database has a full backup size of **500 GB** taken every Sunday. The average daily change rate is **3 %**. Incremental backups run Mon–Sat. **(a)** Calculate the total weekly backup storage using the incremental strategy. **Solution:** $$ S = 500 \cdot (1 + 6 \cdot 0.03) = 500 \cdot 1.18 = 590\,\text{GB} $$ **(b)** Compare with full-backup-every-night strategy. $$ S_{\text{full-nightly}} = 7 \cdot 500 = 3\,500\,\text{GB} $$ **(c)** Calculate the percentage storage savings. $$ \text{Savings} = \frac{3\,500 - 590}{3\,500} \cdot 100\% = 83.14\,\% $$ --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Incremental Backup Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Production Volume
Source LUN / File System] B[Change Tracker
CBT Bit-Map or Mtime Scan] C[Backup Agent
Reads Only Modified Blocks] D[Backup Server
Catalog and Job Scheduler] E[Deduplication Engine
Inline Variable-Length Chunks] F[Backup Target Pool
Disk or Tape Repository] G[Restore Engine
Chain Assembly Module] A --> B B --> C C --> D D --> E E --> F F --> G G --> A ``` **Reading the diagram:** - The **Production Volume** is the live LUN/filesystem being protected. - The **Change Tracker** maintains the bit-map of dirty blocks (CBT is used in VMware, Hyper-V, SAN snapshots). - The **Backup Agent** reads *only* the flagged blocks. - The **Backup Server** orchestrates scheduling and cataloging. - The **Deduplication Engine** performs inline chunk-hash-based dedup. - The **Backup Target** stores the immutable incremental stream. - The **Restore Engine** later reconstructs the full dataset by chain assembly. ## 4.2 Mermaid Flow: Incremental Chain Assembly for Restore ```mermaid flowchart TD node1[Start Restore Request] node2[Locate Most Recent Full Backup F0] node3[Apply Full to Staging Area] node4[Locate Next Incremental in Chain I1] node5{Chain End?} node6[Apply Incremental Ii to Staging] node7[Increment Chain Pointer i = i + 1] node8[Verify Checksum of Staging Volume] node9[Promote to Production LUN] node10[Restore Complete] node1 --> node2 node2 --> node3 node3 --> node4 node4 --> node5 node5 -- No --> node6 node6 --> node7 node7 --> node4 node5 -- Yes --> node8 node8 --> node9 node9 --> node10 ``` ## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Backup vs. Restore Direction ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph BackupFlow["Backup Path - Forward Direction"] dir1[Live Data] dir2[Identify Deltas] dir3[Compress and Dedup] dir4[Append to Chain on Target] end subgraph RestoreFlow["Restore Path - Reverse Direction"] dir5[Read Full from Target] dir6[Apply Incrementals in Order] dir7[Rebuild Production Volume] end dir1 --> dir2 --> dir3 --> dir4 dir4 -.future.-> dir5 --> dir6 --> dir7 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Why two diagrams?** The forward (backup) direction is *selective and additive*; the reverse (restore) direction is *sequential and reconstructive*. KTU examiners frequently award marks for explaining the **asymmetry** between the two flows. ## 4.4 Mermaid Sequence Diagram: Forever-Incremental Retention ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Sched as Backup Scheduler participant Prod as Production Host participant Tgt as Backup Target participant Cat as Backup Catalog Sched->>Prod: Trigger Level 0 Full Backup Prod->>Tgt: Send Full Dataset (F) Tgt-->>Cat: Register Full Instance ID 1001 loop Day 1 to Day 6 Sched->>Prod: Trigger Level 1 Incremental Prod->>Tgt: Send Delta Since Last Event (d_i) Tgt-->>Cat: Register Incremental ID 1002..1007 end Sched->>Prod: Next Sunday - Trigger New Level 0 Prod->>Tgt: Send New Full Dataset Tgt-->>Cat: Register New Chain Anchor ID 2001 Note over Sched,Cat: Old chain 1xxx marked for
retention policy expiry ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Define incremental backup. How does it differ from a full backup and a differential backup?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** An **incremental backup** is a backup strategy that copies only the data blocks that have changed since the **last backup operation of any kind** (full or incremental). Each incremental captures the *delta* relative to the immediately preceding backup event. - **Full Backup:** Captures the *entire dataset* every time, irrespective of changes. Self-contained but storage-inefficient. - **Incremental Backup:** Captures only changes since the **last backup** (any type). Storage-efficient but requires the entire chain to be applied sequentially for restore. - **Differential Backup:** Captures changes since the **last full backup**. Storage size grows daily until the next full. **Mark Distribution (KTU key):** 1 mark definition + 1 mark distinction (incremental vs full) + 1 mark distinction (incremental vs differential). ### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **List any three advantages and two disadvantages of incremental backups.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Advantages:** 1. **Minimal storage consumption** — only deltas are stored; ideal for limited backup windows. 2. **Fastest backup execution** — small data volumes per job, reducing I/O load on production. 3. **Lower network bandwidth** — incremental streams are small, enabling offsite replication over WAN. **Disadvantages:** 1. **Slower restore** — must sequentially apply the full + every incremental in the chain; chain breakage causes data loss. 2. **Complex chain management** — tracking and validating chain integrity demands a robust backup catalog. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** > Marks are *deducted* if students confuse "last backup" (incremental) with "last full backup" (differential). Always use the precise phrasing — *"changes since the **last backup event**"* for incrementals. One mark lost per ambiguity. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) > KTU ESE convention: Two question choices, one from each module half. Pick **ONE**. --- ### **Question A — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`** (14 Marks) **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **architecture of an incremental backup system**. Identify and describe the role of each component. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** The architecture of an incremental backup system consists of the following functional blocks (as illustrated in Section 4.1 of these notes): 1. **Production Volume / Source LUN** — The live data set (file system, database, VM disk) being protected. 2. **Change Tracker / CBT Module** — Maintains a bitmap of modified extents; updated by the storage stack or hypervisor. 3. **Backup Agent** — Reads only the blocks flagged in the change tracker; performs client-side processing. 4. **Backup Server / Media Server** — Orchestrates scheduling, cataloging, and job control. 5. **Deduplication & Compression Engine** — Reduces incremental payload by 10:1 to 30:1. 6. **Backup Target / Repository** — Stores the immutable incremental chain (disk, dedup appliance, or tape). 7. **Restore Engine** — Reassembles the chain in order to reconstruct the production volume. Each component interacts sequentially in the *backup path* and reverses its role in the *restore path*. The change tracker is the **enabling technology** that makes incrementals feasible — without it, the agent would have to perform an expensive full-volume scan to identify deltas. **(b)** A database of size **2 TB** undergoes a full backup every Sunday. The average daily change rate is **4 %**. Incremental backups are taken Mon–Sat. **(7 marks)** **(i)** Calculate the **total weekly backup storage** using the incremental strategy. **(ii)** Compare it with the **differential strategy** for the same workload. **(iii)** Comment on the **restore complexity** of both strategies. **Model Solution:** **(i) Incremental Strategy — Total Storage** Full backup size $F = 2\,\text{TB} = 2\,048\,\text{GB}$. Change rate $CR = 0.04$. Number of incrementals $n = 6$. $$ S_{\text{incr}} = F \cdot (1 + n \cdot CR) = 2 \cdot (1 + 6 \cdot 0.04) = 2 \cdot 1.24 = 2.48\,\text{TB} $$ **(ii) Differential Strategy — Total Storage** Differential captures changes since the last full. On day $i$ (counting Mon as $i=1$), the differential size is $F \cdot CR \cdot i$. Weekly total: $$ S_{\text{diff}} = F + \sum_{i=1}^{6} F \cdot CR \cdot i = F + F \cdot CR \cdot \frac{6 \cdot 7}{2} = F \cdot (1 + CR \cdot 21) $$ $$ S_{\text{diff}} = 2 \cdot (1 + 0.04 \cdot 21) = 2 \cdot 1.84 = 3.68\,\text{TB} $$ **Comparison Table:** | Strategy | Weekly Storage | % of Full-Every-Night (14 TB) | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Full (7×) | 14 TB | 100 % | | Differential | 3.68 TB | 26.3 % | | Incremental | 2.48 TB | 17.7 % | **(iii) Restore Complexity** - **Incremental:** Must apply the full + all 6 incrementals in **strict chronological order**. Any missing or corrupted incremental breaks the chain — recovery fails. Higher RTO. - **Differential:** Must apply only the full + the **most recent** differential. Restore is faster and more resilient. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall (Question A):** Students frequently write $CR$ as a percentage (e.g., "4") in the formula instead of a decimal (**0.04**). This single error inflates the answer by **25×**. Always normalize the change rate to a dimensionless fraction before substitution. --- ### **Question B — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 (Module 3 Internal Choice)]`** (14 Marks) **(a)** Explain the concept of **multi-level incremental backups** (L0, L1 cumulative, L1 differential) as used in enterprise tools like Oracle RMAN. Provide a weekly backup schedule. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** Multi-level incremental backups introduce a **hierarchical backup taxonomy** that balances storage efficiency with restore speed: - **Level 0 (L0):** The base full backup. Captures every used block of the dataset. It is the *anchor* of all subsequent levels. - **Level 1 Cumulative:** Equivalent to a *differential* — captures all changes since the last L0. - **Level 1 Differential (Incremental):** Captures only changes since the *immediately preceding* L1 (cascading deltas). **Sample Weekly Schedule (typical enterprise pattern):** | Day | Backup Level | Size (approx.) | Notes | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Sunday | L0 (Full) | $F$ | Anchor for the week | | Mon–Tue | L1 Differential | $\approx F \cdot CR$ | Small deltas | | Wednesday | L1 Cumulative | $\approx 3 \cdot F \cdot CR$ | Mid-week consolidation | | Thu–Fri | L1 Differential | $\approx F \cdot CR$ | Resets the cascade | | Saturday | L1 Cumulative | $\approx 5 \cdot F \cdot CR$ | End-of-week snapshot | | Sunday | New L0 | $F$ | New chain anchor | **Why use multi-level?** It **bounds the maximum restore chain length** to ~2–3 days, dramatically improving RTO while preserving most of the storage benefit of pure incrementals. **(b)** Discuss the **advantages and limitations of forever-incremental retention** in modern storage systems. How does it interact with deduplication? **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Forever-Incremental Retention Defined:** A retention strategy where **only one full backup** is ever kept for the lifetime of the dataset, and all subsequent backups are incrementals appended to that single anchor. A *new* full is taken only when mandated by retention policy resets or hardware migration. **Advantages:** 1. **Constant anchor cost** — only ever pay for $1 \times F$ of full data, regardless of retention duration (1 year, 7 years, etc.). 2. **Drastically reduced primary storage footprint** — chains grow linearly with $CR$, not multiplicatively. 3. **Optimized for dedup** — many of the same blocks recur in every incremental, yielding dedup ratios of **20:1 or higher** when paired with content-addressable storage (CAS) systems like Dell Data Domain or HPE StoreOnce. 4. **Cloud-friendly** — small incremental payloads make WAN-efficient replication to object storage (S3, Azure Blob) feasible. **Limitations:** 1. **Single point of failure** — if the anchor full backup is corrupted, *all* dependent data is lost. 2. **Long chains degrade restore time** — restoring from 365 days of daily incrementals requires reading and applying 366 streams in order. 3. **WORM/immutability requirement** — to prevent accidental deletion of the anchor, forever-incremental targets must enforce immutability and air-gapping. 4. **Deduplication dependency** — without an efficient dedup engine, forever-incremental chains would balloon to unusable sizes due to fragmentation. **Interaction with Deduplication:** Deduplication engines compute a **content hash** (e.g., SHA-256) per block. Since incrementals contain highly redundant content (modified versions of the same files, OS blocks, application data), the dedup engine recognizes identical chunks across different days and stores them **only once**. This is what makes forever-incremental economically viable at scale. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall (Question B):** A common mistake is to claim that "deduplication reduces the size of a full backup" — it does *not*; dedup works *across* backups. Always phrase it as: **"Dedup eliminates redundancy *between* the full and its incrementals."** --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist (Module 3 — Incremental Backups)** - **Definition:** Incremental = data changed since the **last backup event** (full *or* incremental). Not "since last full." - **Hierarchy (RMAN-style):** L0 (full anchor) → L1 cumulative (differential) → L1 differential (true incremental). - **Storage Formula (uniform CR):** $S = F(1 + n \cdot CR)$. - **Restore Formula:** $RTO = \dfrac{S_{\text{total}}}{R}$ — proportional to *entire chain size*, not just the latest incremental. - **Storage Order:** Full (highest) > Differential > Incremental (lowest). - **Restore Order (Speed):** Full (fastest) > Differential > Incremental (slowest — chain dependency). - **Key Advantage:** Minimal backup window, lowest storage cost, lowest network load. - **Key Disadvantage:** Slow restore, chain fragility (any broken link = total data loss after that point). - **Change Block Tracking (CBT):** The enabling technology — bitmap maintained by hypervisor/storage stack that flags modified extents, avoiding full-volume scans. - **Synthetic Full Backup:** A periodic merge of full + all incrementals into a new full, performed on the *backup server* (not production), giving fast restores without disturbing live workload. - **Forever-Incremental Retention:** Keep one full anchor + perpetual incrementals; pairs strongly with deduplication (ratios of **20:1 or more**). - **3-2-1 Rule Compliance:** Incrementals are essential to offsite/cold-archive copies because of their small size. - **Common KTU Errors to Avoid:** 1. Confusing incremental vs. differential (most common; **−2 marks**). 2. Forgetting to normalize $CR$ to a decimal (**−1 to −2 marks**). 3. Saying "dedup reduces the full backup size" — wrong framing (**−1 mark**). 4. Skipping the *chain assembly* explanation in restore (**−2 marks** in 7-mark sub-questions). - **Synonyms to recognize in KTU paper:** *Differential backup*, *cumulative backup*, *delta backup*, *CBT-based backup*, *forever-incremental*, *synthetic full backup*.

topics.title: Differential Backups
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST867 - STORAGE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Differential Backups

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Differential Backup** is a cumulative data protection method that captures and stores **all data blocks, files, or sectors that have changed since the last full backup** (also referred to as the *reference point* or *baseline backup*). Unlike an incremental backup strategy, every successive differential backup continues to accumulate and contain every modification made since the original full backup, regardless of whether intermediate differential backups have already been executed.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context (PECST867 – Storage Systems, Module 3: Backup and Recovery Strategies), a differential backup is positioned as a **hybrid recovery model** sitting architecturally between the data safety of a full backup and the storage efficiency of an incremental backup.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECST867 – Module 3):**
> A differential backup is a **cumulative** backup. Its size **grows monotonically** between two consecutive full backups and **resets to zero** immediately after the next full backup is taken.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are maintaining a personal **photo journal**:
- On **Sunday**, you print *all* your photos up to today — this is the **Full Backup**.
- On **Monday**, you print only the photos taken on Monday — this is **Differential Day 1**.
- On **Tuesday**, you print *Monday's + Tuesday's* photos — this is **Differential Day 2** (cumulative).
- On **Wednesday**, you again print *Monday + Tuesday + Wednesday's* photos — **Differential Day 3** (grows larger).
- The next **Sunday** you print everything again — **Full Backup** — and the cycle resets.

To recover your journal on a Thursday crash, you only need the **Sunday Full Backup** + the **Wednesday Differential**. You do **not** need Monday or Tuesday. This is the *recovery elegance* of differential backups.

### 1.3 Key Terminology

> [!NOTE]
> - **Reference Point (Baseline):** The last full backup against which changes are measured.
> - **Archive Bit / Change Marker:** A metadata flag (1-bit per file or block) set to `1` whenever a file/block is modified. Differential backups read this bit but **do not reset it** to `0`.
> - **Synthetic Full Backup:** A full backup constructed by merging the last full backup with the most recent differential on the storage system itself, without consuming production I/O.
> - **Recovery Point Objective (RPO):** Maximum tolerable data loss; with differentials, RPO is determined by the differential schedule granularity.
> - **Recovery Time Objective (RTO):** Maximum tolerable downtime; with differentials, RTO is dominated by **only two restore operations** (full + latest differential).

### 1.4 Standard Metrics in Differential Backup Engineering

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Full backup size | $F$ | GB / TB |
| Daily data change rate | $D$ | GB/day |
| Differential interval (days) | $n$ | days |
| Differential backup size on day $n$ | $B_n$ | GB |
| Backup window | $T_w$ | minutes / hours |
| Restore time | $T_r$ | minutes / hours |

---

> **Standard reference constant for enterprise SAN/NAS deduplication engines:** A typical **SHA-1 or SHA-256 fingerprint is 20 bytes or 32 bytes respectively** per data block (commonly 4 KB – 64 KB variable-length blocks), used for differential change-detection scans.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — The "Why" and "How"

The differential backup engine follows a strict, deterministic operational sequence. We dissect each phase:

**Step 1: Reference Point Initialization**
The system identifies the most recent **successful full backup** as the baseline. The block-level change-tracking map (often a *bitmap* in volume-shadow copy frameworks, or a *change journal* in file systems like NTFS USN Journal / XFS reverse-mapping) is queried.

**Step 2: Change Detection (Bitmap / Journal Scan)**
Every block whose change bit is set to `1` (modified) since the reference timestamp is queued for read.

**Step 3: Block-Level Read & Transmission**
The identified changed blocks are read from primary storage and written sequentially to the backup medium (tape, dedup-appliance, or cloud object store).

**Step 4: Archive Bit / Change Marker Policy (CRITICAL DIFFERENCE)**
The change marker (archive bit) is **NOT cleared**. This is the defining behavior that makes the next differential backup **cumulative** — it will re-read these same blocks (and any new ones) and include them again.

**Step 5: Verification and Index Update**
Backup software computes checksums (typically **CRC-32C** or **SHA-256**) for each block, writes a backup catalog entry, and updates the chain metadata.

### 2.2 Why Differential Backups Exist — The Engineering Trade-off

| Strategy | Backup Size | Restore Complexity | Storage Cost | RTO |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Full Only** | Constant = $F$ | Trivial (1 set) | High | Lowest |
| **Incremental** | Grows slowly (only since last backup) | High (chain restoration) | Lowest | Highest |
| **Differential** | Grows steadily (since last full) | Moderate (2 sets) | Medium | Low |

Differential backups trade **storage cost** for **restore speed**, making them ideal for environments where **RTO is mission-critical** but **backup storage budgets are flexible**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are **exam-ready** and form the analytical backbone for Module 3 calculations.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $B_1 = D$ | Size of first differential after a full backup | GB |
| 2 | $B_n = n \cdot D$ | Size of the $n$-th differential backup (linear growth) | GB |
| 3 | $B_{\text{total\_week}} = F + \sum_{i=1}^{6} i \cdot D$ | Total weekly backup storage (1 full + 6 differentials) | GB |
| 4 | $B_{\text{total\_week}} = F + 21D$ | Simplified form of equation (3) for $n=6$ | GB |
| 5 | $T_r = T_{\text{restore\_full}} + T_{\text{restore\_diff}}$ | Total recovery time | minutes |
| 6 | $T_{\text{restore\_diff}} = \dfrac{B_n}{R_{\text{restore}}}$ | Time to restore latest differential | minutes |
| 7 | $RTO_{\text{diff}} < RTO_{\text{incr}}$ | Differential restore is always faster than incremental chain restore | qualitative |
| 8 | $\text{Storage Ratio} = \dfrac{B_n}{F}$ | Ratio of differential size to full backup size | dimensionless |

> **Notation note:** In all tables, the symbol $\vert x \vert$ denoting absolute value is written as `\vert x\vert` to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Differential backups are deployed in:
- **Enterprise Database Servers (Oracle RMAN, SQL Server, PostgreSQL WAL+pitr-differential):** Where point-in-time recovery must be fast.
- **Virtualization Platforms (VMware vSphere CBT, Hyper-V RCT):** Changed Block Tracking is functionally a differential mechanism.
- **Cloud Storage Tiering (AWS EBS Snapshots-incremental, Azure Blob Differential):** Block-level diffs are uploaded.
- **Disaster Recovery Sites (SRDF/Star, Zerto, Veeam):** Daily differentials replicate only changed blocks to the DR datacenter.

---

### 2.5 Worked Numerical Sketch (Foundation for Section 3)

Consider a system with $F = 500$ GB full backup, $D = 30$ GB daily change, and differentials taken Mon–Sat:

$$
B_1 = 30 \text{ GB}, \quad B_2 = 60 \text{ GB}, \quad B_3 = 90 \text{ GB}, \ldots
$$

By Saturday, $B_6 = 180$ GB, so the weekly differential storage is:

$$
B_{\text{total\_week}} = 500 + (30 + 60 + 90 + 120 + 150 + 180) = 500 + 630 = 1130 \text{ GB}
$$
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Cumulative Differential Backup Size $B_n$

We derive the general formula for the size of the $n$-th differential backup, where $n$ is the number of days elapsed since the last full backup.

**Assumption:** Daily data change rate $D$ is **uniform** (constant per day). This is the KTU standard assumption unless explicitly stated otherwise.

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_1 &= D \\
B_2 &= B_1 + D = 2D \\
B_3 &= B_2 + D = 3D \\
&\;\;\vdots \\
B_n &= B_{n-1} + D = n \cdot D
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic per step:** Each new differential adds exactly the changes of the most recent day. Since the change marker is never cleared, every prior day's changes are re-included. Hence, $B_n$ forms an **arithmetic progression (AP)** with first term $D$ and common difference $D$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mathematical Structure:** $B_n$ is an arithmetic progression: $B_n = a + (n-1)d$, with $a = D$ and $d = D$, yielding $B_n = nD$.

### 3.2 Derivation of Total Weekly Backup Storage (1 Full + 6 Differentials)

The total backup storage footprint for one week (Sunday = full, Monday–Saturday = 6 differentials) is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_{\text{total}} &= F + B_1 + B_2 + B_3 + B_4 + B_5 + B_6 \\
&= F + (D + 2D + 3D + 4D + 5D + 6D) \\
&= F + D(1 + 2 + 3 + 4 + 5 + 6) \\
&= F + D \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{6} i
\end{aligned}
$$

Apply the AP summation identity $\sum_{i=1}^{n} i = \dfrac{n(n+1)}{2}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_{\text{total}} &= F + D \cdot \frac{6 \cdot 7}{2} \\
&= F + 21D
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** Weekly differential footprint = $F + 21D$ GB. This is the closed-form KTU-exam-ready expression.

### 3.3 Derivation of Recovery Time $T_r$

A differential recovery requires exactly **two restore operations**: the most recent full backup, then the most recent differential backup.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_r &= T_{\text{restore\_full}} + T_{\text{restore\_diff}} \\
T_{\text{restore\_full}} &= \frac{F}{R_{\text{restore}}} \\
T_{\text{restore\_diff}} &= \frac{B_n}{R_{\text{restore}}} = \frac{nD}{R_{\text{restore}}} \\
\therefore \quad T_r &= \frac{F + nD}{R_{\text{restore}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where $R_{\text{restore}}$ is the storage throughput in GB/min during recovery (typically lower than backup throughput due to write-side overhead).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for KTU exams:** Compare $T_r^{\text{diff}}$ with $T_r^{\text{incr}}$. For an incremental chain of 6 backups, you would need to restore all 6 in sequence, giving $T_r^{\text{incr}} = \dfrac{F + (1+1+1+1+1+1)D}{R} = \dfrac{F+6D}{R}$. For the differential case, you only restore **one** differential: $T_r^{\text{diff}} = \dfrac{F+6D}{R}$ — same total data, but **fewer tape mounts, fewer I/O seek operations**, hence lower real-world RTO.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Differential vs. Incremental vs. Full

| Parameter | Full Only | Incremental (chain) | Differential |
|---|---|---|---|
| Backup size on day $n$ | $F$ (constant) | $D$ (constant) | $nD$ (linear growth) |
| Total weekly storage (Sun full + 6 dailies) | $7F$ | $F + 6D$ | $F + 21D$ |
| Number of restore sets needed | 1 | $n+1$ | 2 |
| Restore time (RTO) | $\dfrac{F}{R}$ | $\dfrac{F+nD}{R}$ | $\dfrac{F+nD}{R}$ (1 mount per set) |
| Best for | Archival | Backup-storage-limited systems | RTO-critical systems |
| Change marker cleared? | N/A | Yes (after each) | **No** |

### 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (KTU 14-mark standard)

**Problem:** A server has $F = 1000$ GB full backup size. Daily change rate $D = 50$ GB. Differentials are taken every weekday (Mon–Fri) with full backup every Sunday. The restore throughput is $R_{\text{restore}} = 2$ GB/min.

**Required:** (a) Compute the size of the Friday differential. (b) Compute total weekly backup storage. (c) Compute recovery time on a Saturday crash.

**Solution:**

(a) Friday is the 5th differential. From $B_n = nD$:

$$
B_5 = 5 \times 50 = 250 \text{ GB}
$$

(b) Weekly storage from $B_{\text{total}} = F + 21D$ (with 5 differentials, the sum becomes $1+2+3+4+5=15$):

$$
B_{\text{total}} = 1000 + 15 \times 50 = 1000 + 750 = 1750 \text{ GB}
$$

(c) Recovery time. We need full (1000 GB) + Friday differential (250 GB):

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_r &= \frac{F + B_5}{R_{\text{restore}}} \\
&= \frac{1000 + 250}{2} \\
&= \frac{1250}{2} = 625 \text{ minutes} \approx 10.42 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam tip:** Always state the formula before substituting values, and clearly show the unit conversion in your final line. KTU examiners award 1 mark for the formula statement, 1 mark for correct substitution, and 1 mark for the final numerical answer with units in 3-mark sub-questions.

### 3.6 Edge Cases and Boundary Conditions

- **$n = 0$** (day of full backup): $B_0 = 0$. The differential is empty — no changes since full backup.
- **$n = 1$** (day after full): $B_1 = D$, identical to the first incremental.
- **Deduplication-aware systems:** If the storage system applies *post-process deduplication*, the effective stored $B_n$ can drop to $\vert B_n - \text{dedup ratio} \vert$ of nominal. For KTU problems, ignore dedup unless explicitly given.
- **Synthetic differential merging:** Some enterprise systems (e.g., NetApp SnapVault, EMC Data Domain) merge the previous differential with the new one at the storage tier, offloading this work from production — the *cumulative growth* is preserved logically, but I/O is shifted off-peak.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Differential Backup Block Architecture Flow

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the functional flow of a differential backup pipeline, from the production volume to the backup target, including the change-tracking decision engine and the critical "do not clear archive bit" branch.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Production Storage Volume] --> B[Block-Level Change Tracker Bitmap]
    B --> C{Block Modified Since Last Full?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Read Modified Block from Source LUN]
    C -- No --> E[Skip Block - No I/O]
    D --> F[Compute SHA-256 / CRC32C Checksum]
    F --> G[Compress and Deduplicate Block]
    G --> H[Write to Backup Target Tape or Disk]
    H --> I[Update Backup Catalog and Index]
    I --> J[CRITICAL Do NOT Clear Archive Bit]
    J --> K[Next Differential Cycle Begins]
    E --> K
    K --> B
```

**Reading the diagram:** The decision node `C` is the heart of the differential engine. The branch `J` (no bit clear) is what differentiates differential from incremental — this is the single most examinable fact in Module 3.

### 4.2 Differential Backup Lifecycle and Cumulative Growth

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    FB[Full Backup - Day 0 - Size F] --> D1[Diff Day 1 - Size 1D]
    D1 --> D2[Diff Day 2 - Size 2D]
    D2 --> D3[Diff Day 3 - Size 3D]
    D3 --> D4[Diff Day 4 - Size 4D]
    D4 --> D5[Diff Day 5 - Size 5D]
    D5 --> D6[Diff Day 6 - Size 6D]
    D6 --> FB2[Full Backup - Day 7 - Resets Cycle]
    FB2 --> DD1[Diff Day 8 - Size 1D]
    DD1 --> DD2[Diff Day 9 - Size 2D]
```

**Interpretation:** The triangle of $D1 \rightarrow D6$ visually demonstrates the **monotonically increasing** size of differentials. After `FB2`, the cycle resets — the size drops back to $D$. This is the KTU 2024 Scheme board-favorite diagram type.

### 4.3 Differential Recovery Path (Two-Set Restore)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Crash[Production Crash Event] --> IdentFull[Identify Most Recent Full Backup F]
    IdentFull --> RestoreFull[Restore Full Backup Set to Target LUN]
    RestoreFull --> IdentDiff[Identify Most Recent Differential B sub n]
    IdentDiff --> ApplyDiff[Apply Differential on Top of Full]
    ApplyDiff --> Verify[Verify Checksums and Run DB Consistency Check]
    Verify --> Online[System Returns Online - RTO Met]
```

**Key takeaway for the diagram:** Only **two backup sets** (full + one differential) are required for complete restoration, in contrast to an incremental chain which requires $n+1$ sets.

### 4.4 Comparison Schematic — Differential vs. Incremental Block Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Last Backup Event] --> Q1{What is the backup type?}
    Q1 -- Differential --> Q2[Reference = Last Full Backup]
    Q1 -- Incremental --> Q3[Reference = Last Backup of Any Type]
    Q2 --> Sel1[Select all blocks changed since last FULL]
    Q3 --> Sel2[Select all blocks changed since LAST backup]
    Sel1 --> Act1[Do NOT clear change bit]
    Sel2 --> Act2[Clear change bit]
```

This diagram directly answers the most commonly asked KTU question: *"How does a differential backup differ from an incremental backup in selecting changed data?"*
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Set B]**
*Define a differential backup. How is it different from an incremental backup in terms of the change-tracking mechanism?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A differential backup is a data protection technique that copies **all data blocks that have changed since the last full backup**, accumulating changes day by day until the next full backup resets the cycle.

The key difference lies in the change-tracking mechanism:

- In a **differential backup**, the system reads the change bitmap referencing the **last full backup** as the baseline. The archive bit / change marker is **NOT cleared** after the differential completes, which is why the next differential re-includes the same blocks plus new ones.
- In an **incremental backup**, the change marker **IS cleared** after each backup, so the next incremental only sees the changes accumulated since the previous backup of *any* type.

Therefore, differential backups grow cumulatively, while incremental backups remain of constant size. (3 marks)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]**
*A company performs a full backup every Sunday and differential backups Monday through Saturday. If the full backup size is 800 GB and the daily change rate is 40 GB, calculate the total weekly backup storage consumption.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Given: $F = 800$ GB, $D = 40$ GB/day, 6 differentials (Mon–Sat).

Differential sizes:
- Mon: $1 \times 40 = 40$ GB
- Tue: $2 \times 40 = 80$ GB
- Wed: $3 \times 40 = 120$ GB
- Thu: $4 \times 40 = 160$ GB
- Fri: $5 \times 40 = 200$ GB
- Sat: $6 \times 40 = 240$ GB

Sum of differentials = $40 + 80 + 120 + 160 + 200 + 240 = 840$ GB.

$$
B_{\text{total}} = F + \sum_{i=1}^{6} iD = 800 + 840 = 1640 \text{ GB}
$$

(3 marks: 1 mark for listing sizes, 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for final answer with units)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

---

**Q3. [KTU University Exam – Module 3, ESE Pattern, 14 Marks]**

**Question A:**
(a) Explain the operational architecture of a differential backup system with a block diagram. Clearly highlight the role of the change-tracking bitmap and explain why the archive bit is not cleared. (7 marks)
(b) A server has a full backup of 1200 GB. Daily change volume is 60 GB. Differentials are taken Mon–Fri. Restore throughput is 3 GB/min. Calculate (i) the size of the Wednesday differential, (ii) the total weekly storage, and (iii) the recovery time on a Thursday system crash. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Operational Architecture of Differential Backup (7 marks):**

The differential backup system consists of the following functional blocks:

1. **Production Volume** holds the live data and is monitored by a change-tracking engine (e.g., VMware CBT, NTFS USN Journal, or volume bitmap). (1 mark)
2. **Change-Tracking Bitmap** maintains a bit per block, set to `1` when the block is modified. The bitmap is referenced to the timestamp of the last full backup. (1 mark)
3. **Block Scanner** iterates through the bitmap and selects blocks with bit = `1`. (1 mark)
4. **Read & Compress Pipeline** reads the selected blocks, computes checksums (SHA-256/CRC32C), and applies compression/deduplication. (1 mark)
5. **Backup Target** receives the data and writes it to tape, disk, or cloud object storage, then updates the catalog. (1 mark)
6. **Archive Bit Policy Module** explicitly **does not clear** the change bit after a differential completes. This is the **defining behavior** — it ensures cumulative growth across subsequent differentials until the next full backup resets the cycle. (2 marks)

A block diagram (similar to Section 4.1 of these notes) should accompany this answer for full marks.

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

Given: $F = 1200$ GB, $D = 60$ GB/day, $R = 3$ GB/min, differentials Mon–Fri.

(i) Wednesday is the 3rd differential:
$$
B_3 = 3 \times D = 3 \times 60 = 180 \text{ GB} \quad \text{(2 marks)}
$$

(ii) Total weekly storage (1 full + 5 differentials):
$$
B_{\text{total}} = F + (1+2+3+4+5)D = 1200 + 15 \times 60 = 1200 + 900 = 2100 \text{ GB} \quad \text{(2 marks)}
$$

(iii) Recovery on Thursday: We need full (1200 GB) + Wednesday differential (180 GB):
$$
T_r = \frac{F + B_3}{R} = \frac{1200 + 180}{3} = \frac{1380}{3} = 460 \text{ minutes} \approx 7.67 \text{ hours} \quad \text{(3 marks)}
$$

---

**Question B (Internal Choice Alternative):**
(a) Compare differential and incremental backup strategies across six parameters: backup size growth, restore complexity, RTO, RPO, storage cost, and suitability. (7 marks)
(b) Derive the closed-form expression for the total storage consumed in a week when one full backup and $n$ consecutive differential backups are taken. Apply the result to a system with $F = 2000$ GB, $D = 100$ GB, and $n = 7$. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Comparative Analysis (7 marks):**

| Parameter | Differential | Incremental |
|---|---|---|
| Backup size growth | Linear: $nD$ | Constant: $D$ |
| Restore complexity | 2 sets (full + last diff) | $n+1$ sets (full + all incrs) |
| RTO | Low (single diff restore) | High (chain restore) |
| RPO | Same in both — determined by backup schedule | Same in both |
| Storage cost | Higher (cumulative) | Lower |
| Best suited for | RTO-critical systems, DR sites | Backup-storage-constrained, archival |

(1 mark per parameter × 6 = 6 marks; 1 mark for the concluding summary statement on suitability)

**(b) Closed-Form Derivation (7 marks):**

Let $n$ be the number of differential backups taken after a full backup of size $F$. Each differential $i$ has size $iD$, forming an AP.

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_{\text{total}} &= F + \sum_{i=1}^{n} iD \\
&= F + D \cdot \frac{n(n+1)}{2} \\
&= F + \frac{n(n+1)D}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

(4 marks for derivation)

**Application:** With $F = 2000$ GB, $D = 100$ GB, $n = 7$:

$$
B_{\text{total}} = 2000 + \frac{7 \times 8 \times 100}{2} = 2000 + 2800 = 4800 \text{ GB} \quad \text{(3 marks)}
$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where students lose marks on Differential Backup questions:**
> 1. **Confusing the reference point:** Writing "differential backs up changes since the *last* backup" — this is the definition of *incremental*, not differential. Always specify *last full backup*. (–1 mark)
> 2. **Forgetting the cumulative nature:** Computing only $B_1$ or assuming $B_n = D$ constant. The differential is **cumulative**, so $B_n = nD$. (–1 to –2 marks)
> 3. **Unit errors in RTO calculation:** Using backup throughput instead of restore throughput, or omitting the minute-to-hour conversion. (–1 mark)
> 4. **Missing the archive-bit explanation:** When asked "explain," students often describe *what* a differential does but not *why the archive bit is preserved*. The "do not clear" mechanism is worth 1–2 marks explicitly. (–1 to –2 marks)
> 5. **Omitting the diagram:** For 7-mark architecture questions, a labeled block diagram is mandatory. Without it, expect a 1–2 mark deduction even if the prose is perfect.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** A differential backup copies **all blocks changed since the last full backup** — it is **cumulative**.
- **Archive Bit:** Differential backups **do not** clear the change bit. This is the defining behavioral difference from incremental.
- **Size Formula:** $B_n = n \cdot D$ — linear, monotonically increasing between full backups.
- **Weekly Storage Formula:** $B_{\text{total}} = F + \dfrac{n(n+1)}{2} \cdot D$ (with $n=6$, this simplifies to $F + 21D$).
- **Restore Sets Required:** **Exactly 2** — most recent full + most recent differential.
- **Recovery Time Formula:** $T_r = \dfrac{F + B_n}{R_{\text{restore}}}$.
- **RTO vs. RPO:** Differential has **low RTO** (only 2 sets to mount and apply) but **same RPO** as any periodic backup strategy.
- **Best Use Case:** Systems where **recovery speed is more important than backup-storage cost** — e.g., transactional databases, mission-critical VMs, DR replication targets.
- **Worst Use Case:** Storage-tiered archival systems where backup-storage cost dominates the design.
- **Standard enterprise constant:** Deduplication ratios in production differential systems typically range from **10:1 to 30:1**, reducing effective stored $B_n$ significantly — but the *logical* cumulative size is what KTU exams test.
- **Trap Question Alert:** "Is a differential backup faster than a full backup?" — **No**, it is typically slower than a full backup as $n$ grows, because the cumulative size approaches and can exceed $F$.
- **Comparison Anchors:** Full = constant size, slow; Incremental = constant small size, slow restore; **Differential = growing size, fast restore** — remember this triad.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Synthetic Full Backups
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST867 - STORAGE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Synthetic Full Backups

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Synthetic Full Backup** (often abbreviated as **SFB**) is an advanced data protection technique in which a fully synthetic (reconstructed) full backup image is generated on a **secondary backup server** by intelligently merging a previously captured full backup with all the subsequent incremental backups accumulated since that full backup. Unlike a traditional full backup that reads every byte of data from the production host, the synthetic full backup performs no I/O on the production server, thereby completely offloading the backup window from mission-critical systems.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** "A Synthetic Full Backup is a backup method that consolidates a previous full backup with subsequent incremental backups on a backup server to create a new full backup image, without placing any load on the production system during backup creation."

The concept was first commercially implemented in the **Amanda backup suite** (1991) and is now a cornerstone of enterprise backup architectures such as **NetBackup Synthetic Backup**, **EMC NetWorker**, **Veeam**, and **Commvault**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (The "Photo Album Stitching" Analogy)

Imagine you have a thick photo album (your previous full backup). Every day, your friend gives you small sticky notes describing only the **changes** since the last update — "Page 4, swap photo 2 and 3", "Add new photo at page 7", "Delete page 9". You (the backup server) sit quietly at home in the evening with the old album and the sticky notes, and you **manually create a brand-new, fully updated album**.

- The friend (production server) did **zero heavy work** — they only handed over tiny notes.
- You (backup server) did all the heavy lifting on a **separate desk**.
- The next day, your friend only needs to write sticky notes compared to the new album.

This is exactly how a Synthetic Full Backup behaves: the **backup server does the merge**, the **production server only streams incremental data**.

### 1.3 Key Terminology Snapshot

| Term | Meaning |
|------|---------|
| **Full Backup** | Complete copy of all data |
| **Incremental Backup** | Only data changed since the last backup of any type |
| **Differential Backup** | Only data changed since the last full backup |
| **Synthetic Backup** | Full backup synthesized on secondary server from prior full + incrementals |
| **Roll-Forward** | The process of applying incrementals to a base full |
| **Backup Window** | Time during which backups may run without impacting users |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Highlight (CO Mapped):** Synthetic Full Backups directly satisfy KTU Course Outcome **CO3** — *Design cost-effective backup and recovery solutions for enterprise storage environments.*

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic – Structured Step-by-Step

A Synthetic Full Backup operates in three logical phases:

**Phase 1 – Initial Full Capture (t = 0)**
- The production server streams the entire dataset (size **D**) to the backup server.
- This is the *only* time the production server endures full I/O load.

**Phase 2 – Incremental Streams (t = 1, 2, …, n)**
- Each day/hour, only the *delta* ($\Delta_i$) is sent.
- $\Delta_i$ is typically **2%–10%** of **D**, depending on change rate.

**Phase 3 – Synthetic Roll-Forward (Trigger: weekly/monthly)**
- The backup server takes the existing synthetic full ($F_{old}$) and applies all $\Delta_i$ blocks in sequence.
- Produces a brand-new synthetic full ($F_{new}$) **without touching the production host**.

### 2.2 Mathematical Model of Storage & Time

Let us define:

- $D$ = Total dataset size (bytes)
- $\alpha$ = Daily change rate (fraction, e.g., 0.05 = 5%)
- $n$ = Number of days between synthetic full creations
- $S_F$ = Size of a full backup
- $S_I$ = Size of an incremental backup
- $T_F$ = Time for full backup
- $T_S$ = Time for synthetic roll-forward
- $T_R$ = Time for restore

#### Storage Consumption Per Cycle

$$S_{cycle} = S_F + \sum_{i=1}^{n} S_{I,i} = D + n \cdot (\alpha \cdot D) = D(1 + n\alpha)$$

#### Production-Server Load Reduction

Traditional weekly approach (1 full + 6 incrementals → then full again):

$$L_{traditional} = D + 6 \cdot (\alpha D) + D = 2D + 6\alpha D$$

Synthetic approach (1 full + 6 incrementals → **synthetic full built server-side** + 6 incrementals):

$$L_{synthetic} = D + 6\alpha D + \underbrace{0}_{\text{synth on backup srv}} = D(1 + 6\alpha)$$

> **Production load reduction ratio:**
> $$\text{Reduction} = 1 - \frac{L_{synthetic}}{L_{traditional}} = \frac{D}{2D + 6\alpha D} \approx \frac{1}{2} \text{ (for small } \alpha\text{)}$$

### 2.3 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Formula | Unit | Purpose |
|-----------|---------|------|---------|
| Incremental size | $S_I = \alpha D$ | Bytes | Daily backup payload |
| Cycle storage | $S_{cycle} = D(1 + n\alpha)$ | Bytes | Total disk per cycle |
| Restore time (synth) | $T_R = T_{read\_synth}$ | Seconds | Single read, no merge |
| Restore time (incr) | $T_R^{incr} = F_0 + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \Delta_i$ | Seconds | Sequential merge |
| Production load factor | $\beta = \frac{D_{read\_prod}}{D}$ | Ratio | Backup cost to host |
| Synthetic cost | $\gamma = \frac{D_{process\_srv}}{D}$ | Ratio | Server-side work |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Trick:** In synthetic backups, $\beta \to 0$ for the roll-forward operation, while for a traditional full backup, $\beta = 1$. Examiners love to ask why production servers stay responsive during backups.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Database Servers (Oracle, SQL Server):** Synthetic full backups allow 24×7 OLTP systems to avoid the I/O storm of nightly fulls.
2. **VMware/Hyper-V Environments:** Vendors like Veeam use synthetic fulls combined with CBT (Changed Block Tracking) for near-instant roll-forwards.
3. **Cloud Storage Tiers:** AWS S3, Azure Blob — synthetic fulls enable cheap, infrequent full snapshots while incrementals stay on faster tiers.
4. **Disaster Recovery Sites:** Synthetic fulls can be replicated nightly to DR without re-reading the production array.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Restore-Time Advantage of Synthetic Full Backups

**Scenario:** A 1 TB dataset, 5% daily change rate, weekly full backup cycle ($n = 7$).

**Step 1 – Define the variables:**

$$D = 1 \text{ TB}, \quad \alpha = 0.05, \quad n = 7$$

**Step 2 – Compute incremental size per day:**

$$S_I = \alpha D = 0.05 \times 1 \text{ TB} = 0.05 \text{ TB} = 50 \text{ GB}$$

**Step 3 – Restore time using only incrementals (no synthetic full):**

To recover the latest state, the restore engine must read:
1. The base full backup of size $D$
2. All seven incrementals $\Delta_1, \Delta_2, \ldots, \Delta_7$ in order

$$T_R^{incr} = T_{read}\left(D\right) + \sum_{i=1}^{7} T_{read}\left(\Delta_i\right)$$

$$= T_{read}\left(1 \text{ TB}\right) + 7 \times T_{read}\left(0.05 \text{ TB}\right)$$

$$= T_{read}\left(1 \text{ TB}\right) + T_{read}\left(0.35 \text{ TB}\right)$$

$$= T_{read}\left(1.35 \text{ TB}\right)$$

**Step 4 – Restore time using a synthetic full backup:**

The synthetic full $F_{new}$ is already a single consolidated image of size $D$. Only $F_{new}$ needs to be read.

$$T_R^{synth} = T_{read}\left(F_{new}\right) = T_{read}\left(D\right) = T_{read}\left(1 \text{ TB}\right)$$

**Step 5 – Compute the speed-up factor:**

$$\text{SpeedUp} = \frac{T_R^{incr}}{T_R^{synth}} = \frac{1.35 \text{ TB}}{1.00 \text{ TB}} = 1.35\times$$

For a 30-day cycle:

$$\text{SpeedUp}_{30} = \frac{1 + 30(0.05)}{1} = 2.50\times$$

> **Generalized formula:**

$$\boxed{\text{SpeedUp}(n, \alpha) = \frac{1 + n\alpha}{1} = 1 + n\alpha}$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Optimal Synthetic Backup Frequency

**Goal:** Minimize total cost $C_{total} = C_{prod} + C_{synth} + C_{restore}$.

Let:
- $C_F$ = Cost per byte read from production
- $C_S$ = Cost per byte processed on backup server
- $C_R$ = Cost per byte read during restore
- $\lambda$ = Frequency of full backups per period

**Step 1 – Production cost decreases as $\lambda$ increases:**

$$C_{prod} = \frac{C_F \cdot D}{\lambda}$$

**Step 2 – Synthetic cost increases with $\lambda$ (more rolls):**

$$C_{synth} = \lambda \cdot C_S \cdot D$$

**Step 3 – Restore cost decreases with $\lambda$ (shorter chains):**

$$C_{restore} = C_R \cdot D \cdot \left(1 + \frac{n\alpha}{\lambda}\right)$$

**Step 4 – Differentiate $C_{total}$ with respect to $\lambda$ and set to zero:**

$$\frac{dC_{total}}{d\lambda} = -\frac{C_F D}{\lambda^2} + C_S D - 0 = 0$$

**Step 5 – Solve:**

$$\lambda_{opt} = \sqrt{\frac{C_F}{C_S}}$$

> **Engineering Insight:** If $C_F = 4 \cdot C_S$ (production I/O is 4× more expensive), then $\lambda_{opt} = 2$ synthetic fulls per cycle — i.e., perform synthetic fulls every **half-cycle**.

### 3.3 Comparison Matrix: Backup Strategies

| Feature | Traditional Full | Incremental | Differential | **Synthetic Full** |
|---------|:---------------:|:-----------:|:------------:|:-----------------:|
| Production Load | Very High | Very Low | Low | **Near Zero (roll)** |
| Backup Window | Long | Short | Medium | **Short (incremental only)** |
| Restore Speed | Fastest | Slowest | Medium | **Fast (like full)** |
| Storage Efficiency | Poor (D copies) | Best | Medium | **Medium** |
| Recovery Point | Backup time | Last incr | Last full | **Synthetic time** |
| Server-Side CPU | None | None | None | **High** |
| Bandwidth Needed | High | Low | Medium | **Low (roll-forward only)** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Memory Hook:** Synthetic Full = **"Best of Both Worlds"** — it inherits the **fast restore** of a full backup and the **low production impact** of an incremental backup.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Synthetic Full Backup Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Production Server] -->|Day 0: Full Backup D| B[Backup Server Pool]
    A -->|Day 1: Incremental delta1| B
    A -->|Day 2: Incremental delta2| B
    A -->|Day 3: Incremental delta3| B
    A -->|Day 4: Incremental delta4| B
    A -->|Day 5: Incremental delta5| B
    A -->|Day 6: Incremental delta6| B
    A -->|Day 7: Incremental delta7| B
    B -->|Roll-Forward Engine| C[Synthesizer Module]
    C -->|Apply delta1 to F0| D[New Synthetic Full F1]
    C -->|Apply delta2..delta7| D
    D --> E[Storage Repository]
    E -->|On Disaster| F[Restore Engine]
    F -->|Read only F1| G[Recovered Production Data]

    style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px

```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph prodEnv[Production Environment]
        nodeP1[Application Server]
        nodeP2[Database Engine]
        nodeP3[File System]
    end

    subgraph backupEnv[Backup Infrastructure]
        nodeB1[Backup Client Agent]
        nodeB2[Incremental Tracker CBT]
        nodeB3[Synthetic Engine]
        nodeB4[Storage Pool]
        nodeB5[Catalog Database]
    end

    subgraph drEnv[Recovery Layer]
        nodeR1[Restore Server]
        nodeR2[Verification Module]
    end

    nodeP2 --> nodeP3
    nodeP3 --> nodeB1
    nodeB1 --> nodeB2
    nodeB2 --> nodeB4
    nodeB4 --> nodeB3

    nodeB4 --> nodeR1

    style backupEnv fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px

```

### 4.3 Comparative Strategy Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startQ{Need 24x7 Production Uptime?}
    startQ -->|Yes| q1{Restore Speed Critical?}
    startQ -->|No| tradFull[Use Traditional Full Backup]
    q1 -->|Yes| synthFull[Use Synthetic Full Backup]
    q1 -->|No| incrOnly[Use Incremental Only]
    synthFull --> q2{Storage Budget Tight?}
    q2 -->|Yes| dedup[Add Deduplication Layer]
    q2 -->|No| standard[Standard Synthetic Schedule]

    style startQ fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px

```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** Define **Synthetic Full Backup**. Mention any two advantages over a traditional full backup.

**Model Answer:**
A **Synthetic Full Backup** is a backup methodology in which a complete full backup image is *constructed* on a secondary backup server by merging the previous full backup with all the incremental backups captured in the intervening period. Unlike a traditional full backup that reads the entire dataset directly from the production host, the synthetic approach performs all consolidation operations on the backup server, leaving the production system virtually idle.

**Two Advantages:**
1. **Zero production-server load during full creation** — roll-forward happens on the backup server, allowing mission-critical applications to maintain full I/O throughput.
2. **Faster restore times** — recovery requires reading only a single consolidated full image instead of a base full plus multiple incrementals, dramatically reducing RTO.

*(Examiner's note: Award 1 mark for correct definition, 1 mark each for the two advantages. Total = 3 marks.)*

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** List any **three** differences between **Synthetic Full Backup** and **Incremental Backup**.

**Model Answer:**

| # | Synthetic Full Backup | Incremental Backup |
|---|----------------------|-------------------|
| 1 | Built on the **backup server** by merging previous full + incrementals | Built on the **production server** by capturing only changed blocks |
| 2 | Restore needs only **one image** to be read | Restore requires the base full **plus all** intermediate incrementals |
| 3 | Production server load is **near zero** during full-image creation | Production server load is **low** but still involved in every incremental |

*(1 mark per correct row = 3 marks.)*

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the **step-by-step working** of a Synthetic Full Backup with a suitable block diagram. Highlight how it differs from a traditional full backup process. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A company has a dataset of **2 TB**. The daily change rate is **8%**. The company performs a **synthetic full backup every 7 days** using incrementals in between. Calculate:
  - (i) The total storage consumed per cycle **(3 Marks)**
  - (ii) The restore time speed-up factor compared to incremental-only recovery, assuming both methods read at the same throughput **(4 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Step-by-Step Working:**

1. **Initial Full Capture (Day 0):** The production server sends the *entire* 2 TB dataset to the backup server. The backup server stores it as $F_0$.
2. **Incremental Phase (Day 1 – Day 6):** Each day, the production agent identifies only the modified blocks (via Changed Block Tracking or filesystem snapshots) and streams these deltas to the backup server. The deltas are small — typically $0.08 \times 2$ TB = **160 GB** per day.
3. **Synthetic Roll-Forward (Day 7):** The backup server invokes its *Synthetic Engine*. It loads $F_0$ from disk, then sequentially applies $\Delta_1, \Delta_2, \ldots, \Delta_7$ to produce a brand-new image $F_1$ of size 2 TB. **The production server is not contacted.**
4. **Storage:** The backup server now retains $F_0$ (or retires it per policy), the seven incrementals, and the new $F_1$. Recovery uses $F_1$ alone.

**Difference from Traditional Full:** In a traditional full backup, Day 7 would again require reading the *entire* 2 TB from the production server, contending with application I/O. Synthetic fulls eliminate this contention.

*(Marks distribution: 3 marks for steps + 2 marks for diagram explanation + 2 marks for contrast = 7 marks.)*

**(b) Numerical Computation:**

Given: $D = 2$ TB, $\alpha = 0.08$, $n = 7$

**(i) Total storage per cycle:**

$$S_{cycle} = D(1 + n\alpha) = 2\,(1 + 7 \times 0.08) = 2 \times 1.56 = 3.12 \text{ TB}$$

*(3 marks: 2 for formula, 1 for final answer with units.)*

**(ii) Restore-time speed-up factor:**

Incremental-only restore must read $F_0$ + 7 incrementals:

$$T_R^{incr} \propto 2 + 7(0.08 \times 2) = 2 + 1.12 = 3.12 \text{ TB}$$

Synthetic restore reads only $F_1$:

$$T_R^{synth} \propto 2 \text{ TB}$$

$$\text{SpeedUp} = \frac{3.12}{2.00} = 1.56\times$$

*(4 marks: 1 for incremental calculation, 1 for synthetic calculation, 1 for ratio, 1 for final interpretation.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Compare and contrast **Synthetic Full Backup**, **Traditional Full Backup**, and **Incremental Backup** along the dimensions of: (i) Production Server Load, (ii) Backup Window, (iii) Restore Time, (iv) Storage Space Required. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain how **Changed Block Tracking (CBT)** enables efficient synthetic full backups in virtualized environments. Why is CBT essential for performance? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Comparative Analysis:**

| Dimension | Traditional Full | Incremental | Synthetic Full |
|-----------|-----------------|-------------|----------------|
| (i) Production Load | **Very High** — entire D read | **Low** — only $\alpha D$ read | **Near Zero** (during roll-forward) |
| (ii) Backup Window | **Long** (proportional to D) | **Short** | **Short** (only incremental windows) |
| (iii) Restore Time | **Fastest** (read once) | **Slowest** (read all chained) | **Fast** (single full read) |
| (iv) Storage Space | **Worst** (multiple fulls) | **Best** (1 full + small deltas) | **Medium** (1 full + 1 synth + deltas) |

*(7 marks: 1.5 marks per correct row + 1 mark for overall conclusion = 7 marks.)*

**(b) CBT and Synthetic Full Backups:**

**Working of CBT:**
1. The hypervisor (VMware vSphere, Hyper-V, KVM) maintains a **bitmap file** for each virtual disk.
