2. **ATA TRIM** — Same concept for SATA/SAS SSDs; sent on file delete or via scheduled TRIM.
3. **NVMe Dataset Management – Deallocate** — NVMe equivalent; allows fine-grained range deallocation.
4. **Hypervisor-Assisted UNMAP** — VMware VAAI UNMAP, Windows ODX, Linux `fstrim` integration.
5. **Array-Initiated Reclamation** — Some arrays run **"reclaim zero-pages"** jobs to identify all-zero blocks.
6. **Inline vs. Background Reclamation** — Inline is immediate (high overhead); background is scheduled (lower impact).

**How Snapshots Complicate Reclamation:**

- A **Copy-on-First-Write (COFW) snapshot** preserves the old block every time the live volume is modified.
- Even if the live volume deletes a file, the snapshot's COFW copy **still references the original block**.
- These "pinned" blocks **cannot be reclaimed** until the snapshot is deleted.
- A single long-retention snapshot (e.g., monthly backup retained for 1 year) can **completely block reclamation** of all data it touches.
- Reclamation efficiency: $\eta_{\text{reclaim}} = (1 - P_{\text{snap}}) \times 100\%$, where $P_{\text{snap}}$ is the snapshot-pinned fraction.

(7 marks — 4 for techniques, 3 for snapshot impact.)

**Part (b) — 7 marks, step-by-step:**

**(i) Net daily write rate:**

$$
R_{\text{net}} = (100 - 20) + (150 - 10) + (80 - 30) = 80 + 140 + 50 = 270 \text{ GB/day}
$$

**(ii) Free space:**

$$
L_{\text{free}} = 50 - 30 = 20 \text{ TB} = 20{,}000 \text{ GB}
$$

**(iii) Pool utilization %:**

$$
U_{\text{pool}}(\%) = \frac{30}{50} \times 100 = 60\%
$$

**(iv) Time to exhaustion:**

$$
T_{\text{exhaust}} = \frac{20{,}000 \text{ GB}}{270 \text{ GB/day}} \approx 74.07 \text{ days}
$$

**(v) Alert Recommendation:**

- Pool is currently at **60 %** (below all thresholds).
- Time-to-exhaustion ≈ **74 days**, which is well above the 70 % threshold trigger.
- **First threshold crossed:** at 70 % utilization, the pool will be at $0.70 \times 50 = 35$ TB used, leaving 15 TB free.
- **Days to reach 35 TB used:** $(35 - 30) \text{ TB} / 270 \text{ GB/day} = 5000 / 270 \approx 18.5$ days.
- **Recommendation:** Configure the **70 % (Warning)** alert to fire in approximately **18 days**. Plan capacity addition within **30 days** (before the 85 % critical threshold at 42.5 TB used, reached in $(42.5 - 30)/270 \times 1000 \approx 46$ days from now). To leave 30-day safety margin, add capacity at day ≈ 16.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Question B Common Mistakes:**
> 1. **Reclamation techniques must be named explicitly** (SCSI UNMAP, ATA TRIM, NVMe Deallocate). Vague answers like "the system deletes files" score zero.
> 2. **Snapshot pinning** is a high-yield concept — failing to mention it loses 2 marks easily.
> 3. **Time-to-exhaustion rounding** — keep two decimal places or express as "days + hours."
> 4. **Alert calculation** — students often forget to compute *when* the threshold will be crossed, not just *that* it will be crossed. KTU expects the **day-count to threshold breach**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Thin Provisioning Considerations**

- **Definition:** Thin provisioning allocates physical capacity on first write; the full logical size is presented to the host immediately.
- **OCR Formula:** $\text{OCR} = \dfrac{L_{\text{logical}}}{L_{\text{physical}}}$, unitless, always $\geq 1$.
- **Pool Utilization Formula:** $U_{\text{pool}}(\%) = \dfrac{L_{\text{used}}}{L_{\text{pool total}}} \times 100$.
- **Reclamation Efficiency:** $\eta_{\text{reclaim}} = (1 - P_{\text{snap}}) \times 100\%$.
- **Time-to-Exhaustion:** $T_{\text{exhaust}} = \dfrac{L_{\text{free}}}{R_{\text{net}}}$.
- **Three Thresholds:** 70 % (warning), 85 % (critical), 95 % (hard stop).
- **Six Considerations:** Over-commitment, Monitoring, Reclamation, Performance, Data Services, Recovery.
- **Reclamation Commands:** SCSI UNMAP, ATA TRIM, NVMe Deallocate, VAAI UNMAP.
- **Snapshot Impact:** COFW snapshots **pin deleted blocks**, reducing reclamation efficiency.
- **Performance Overhead:** **5–15 % latency** for random small writes; mitigated by **DRAM page-table cache**.
- **Recovery from Full Pool:** Add disks → expand pool → re-import metadata → thin volumes recover from read-only.
- **Industry Rule of Thumb:** Keep **OCR ≤ 2.0** for mission-critical workloads.
- **Best Pairing:** Thin + Dedup + Compression = maximum efficiency, called **"Efficient"** or **"Thin-Efficient"** provisioning.
- **Disaster Scenario:** A single over-committed thin pool failure = **all dependent volumes offline simultaneously** → must be designed with **redundancy and alerting**.
- **Difference from Thick:** Thick volumes are pre-allocated; thin volumes are not.
- **When NOT to use thin:** Boot LUNs, system partitions, fixed-size database files where predictability matters more than efficiency.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic — "T-H-I-N":**
> **T** — **T**hresholds (70/85/95)
> **H** — **H**ealth monitoring (utilization %)
> **I** — **I**ncremental allocation (on first write)
> **N** — **N**o reclamation without UNMAP/TRIM

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Deduplication and Compression
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST867 - STORAGE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Deduplication and Compression

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4 — DEDUPLICATION AND COMPRESSION

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Deduplication** is a data reduction technique employed in modern storage systems that identifies and eliminates redundant copies of repeating data blocks, ensuring that only a *single unique instance* (a *singleton*) of any given data segment is physically stored on the storage medium. Subsequent occurrences are replaced with a lightweight *pointer* or *reference* to the original instance, thereby optimizing capacity utilization, reducing I/O bandwidth, and lowering the total cost of ownership (TCO).

> [!NOTE]
> **Data Compression** is the encoding of information using fewer bits than the original representation, achieved by exploiting statistical redundancy (lossless compression) or by selectively discarding perceptually insignificant information (lossy compression). In the context of enterprise storage, *lossless compression* is the dominant paradigm because data integrity must be perfectly preserved upon decompression.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Deduplication — The Library Analogy:**
Imagine a university library where **500 students** each submit a thesis containing the *exact same* chapter on "Renaissance Literature." Instead of printing 500 identical copies and stacking them in the archive room, the librarian prints **ONE** master copy, assigns it the unique reference code **REF-001**, and gives every student a small *library card* that simply says *"See REF-001."* The physical footprint shrinks dramatically, but every student can still retrieve the content instantly. This is precisely what deduplication does for data — a *single physical block* serves many logical references.

**Compression — The Suitcase Analogy:**
You have a suitcase full of winter clothes: **10 identical black sweaters**. Instead of folding each one separately (wasting space), you use a *vacuum-seal bag* that presses out all the air, compressing 10 sweaters into the space of 1. When you arrive, you unseal the bag, and the sweaters puff back to full size — nothing is lost. This is **lossless compression**: the data is *mathematically reconstructed* bit-for-bit, exactly as it was before packing.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics Highlighted

The following key performance metrics are universally used in KTU board examinations and industry whitepapers:

- **Deduplication Ratio (DR)** = $\frac{\text{Original Logical Size}}{\text{Physical Stored Size}}$
- **Data Reduction Ratio (DRR)** = $1 : \text{Reduction Factor}$
- **Compression Ratio (CR)** = $\frac{\text{Original Size}}{\text{Compressed Size}}$
- **Space Savings (SS)** = $1 - \frac{\text{Compressed Size}}{\text{Original Size}}$
- **Throughput (GB/hr)** = $\frac{\text{Data Processed}}{\text{Time Elapsed}}$
- **Chunk Size**: Variable, typically **4 KB** to **128 KB** in production systems

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The 2024 Scheme explicitly categorizes deduplication into **File-level, Block-level (Fixed and Variable), and Byte-level** granularity, and compression into **Lossless (LZ family, Huffman, RLE)** vs **Lossy (transform-based)**. Mastery of these classifications is mandatory for Part A short-answer questions.

### 1.4 Scope of Application in Storage Systems

| Storage Tier | Deduplication Used? | Compression Used? | Typical Ratio |
|---|---|---|---|
| Primary (Block/FC) | Selective (VTL) | Inline HW | 2:1 – 4:1 |
| Backup (VTL/Dedupe Appliance) | Always (post-process) | Yes | 10:1 – 30:1 |
| Archive (Object/Cold) | Yes | Yes (LZ4, ZSTD) | 3:1 – 8:1 |
| All-Flash Array | Inline dedupe | Inline compression | 1.5:1 – 4:1 |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Deduplication — Granularity Hierarchy

Deduplication operates at four fundamental granularities, each with explicit trade-offs in *deduplication ratio* vs *metadata overhead* vs *compute latency*:

### 2.1.1 File-Level Deduplication

- **Mechanism**: A cryptographic hash (e.g., **MD5**, **SHA-1**, **SHA-256**) is computed over the *entire file*. If the hash matches an existing file hash, the new file is replaced with a *pointer*.
- **Why it is fast**: Only one hash comparison per file, minimal CPU overhead.
- **Why it underperforms**: Even a *single byte change* in a file yields a completely different hash, eliminating the chance of deduplication. A 100 MB email archive with one attachment modified per day = **0% dedupe**.

### 2.1.2 Block-Level Deduplication (Fixed-Size)

- **Mechanism**: The file is split into fixed-size chunks (e.g., **4 KB**). A hash is computed per block. Duplicate hashes are replaced with a pointer.
- **Advantage**: Better ratio than file-level, deterministic boundaries.
- **Disadvantage**: *Boundary shift problem* — inserting one byte shifts all subsequent chunk boundaries, generating entirely new hashes.

### 2.1.3 Block-Level Deduplication (Variable-Size)

- **Mechanism**: The file is parsed using a **sliding-window content-defined chunking (CDC)** algorithm. A boundary is marked wherever a Rabin-fingerprint of the window matches a predefined pattern (e.g., a specific low-order bit pattern).
- **Why it solves the boundary shift problem**: Chunk boundaries are determined by *content*, not *position*. An insertion only affects the local chunk, not the global structure.
- **Industry standard**: Used by **Data Domain (EMC)**, **NetApp A-SIS**, **Dell DR Series**.

### 2.1.4 Byte-Level Deduplication

- **Mechanism**: Operates on the smallest granularity; replaces individual repeated byte sequences.
- **Trade-off**: Highest ratio but extreme metadata overhead — not practical for primary storage.

## 2.2 Inline vs Post-Process Deduplication

- **Inline (Synchronous)**: Deduplication occurs *before* data is written to disk. Latency-sensitive but protects against the "duplicate window" (period during which the original and duplicate both reside on disk).
- **Post-Process (Asynchronous)**: Data is first written in full; deduplication runs later as a background job. Higher throughput but temporarily requires full storage capacity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Duplicate Window Problem**: In post-process dedupe, if a crash occurs *between* write and dedupe job, the system rolls back to its last deduplicated state, re-allowing the duplicates to occupy space. Inline dedupe eliminates this risk.

## 2.3 Hash Collision and Fingerprinting

Deduplication relies on **cryptographic hash functions** to identify identical blocks. A *collision* occurs when two *different* blocks produce the *same* hash.

- **MD5**: 128-bit, $2^{64}$ complexity — *vulnerable* to collisions, no longer recommended for security.
- **SHA-1**: 160-bit — deprecated by NIST in 2011, but still used in some legacy dedupe appliances.
- **SHA-256**: 256-bit, $2^{128}$ complexity — *industry gold standard*.

To virtually eliminate collision risk, modern systems use **two independent hashes** (e.g., MD5 + SHA-1) and an optional *byte-by-byte verification* on the *first* mismatch.

## 2.4 Compression — Algorithmic Taxonomy

### 2.4.1 Lossless Compression Algorithms

1. **Run-Length Encoding (RLE)**
   - Replaces repeated bytes with a (count, value) pair.
   - Ideal for images with large solid regions, sparse data.
   - Example: `AAAAABBBBCC` → `5A4B2C` (6 bytes vs 11 bytes original).

2. **Huffman Coding**
   - Variable-length, prefix-free code.
   - More frequent symbols → shorter codewords.
   - Optimal for symbol-by-symbol encoding given a known probability distribution.

3. **Lempel-Ziv Family (LZ77, LZ78, LZW, Deflate, LZ4, ZSTD)**
   - Dictionary-based: replaces repeated substrings with `(distance, length)` back-references.
   - **LZW** powers `gzip` and `GIF` images.
   - **Deflate** = LZ77 + Huffman; used in ZIP, PNG, HTTP, ZFS.
   - **LZ4 / ZSTD** are *modern* algorithms emphasizing speed; used in Linux kernel, RocksDB, ZFS.

### 2.4.2 Lossy Compression (Reference Only)

- **Transform-based**: DCT (JPEG), Wavelet (JPEG 2000).
- **Use case**: Streaming media, image/video — *not* used in primary enterprise storage.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $DR = \frac{S_{\text{original}}}{S_{\text{physical}}}$ | Deduplication Ratio | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $SS_{\text{dedupe}} = 1 - \frac{S_{\text{physical}}}{S_{\text{original}}}$ | Deduplication Space Savings | Fraction or % |
| 3 | $CR = \frac{S_{\text{original}}}{S_{\text{compressed}}}$ | Compression Ratio | Dimensionless |
| 4 | $SS_{\text{comp}} = 1 - \frac{1}{CR}$ | Compression Space Savings | Fraction or % |
| 5 | $SS_{\text{combined}} = 1 - (1-SS_d)(1-SS_c)$ | Combined (Dedupe + Compress) Savings | Fraction or % |
| 6 | $H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \log_2 p_i$ | Shannon Entropy (bits/symbol) | bits |
| 7 | $\bar{L} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \cdot l_i$ | Average Codeword Length | bits/symbol |
| 8 | $\eta = \frac{H(X)}{\bar{L}}$ | Coding Efficiency | 0 to 1 |
| 9 | $R = 1 - \eta$ | Redundancy | 0 to 1 |
| 10 | $\text{RLE Length} = \frac{n \cdot \log_2(k)}{8 \text{ bits}}$ | Worst-case RLE storage per run | Bytes |
| 11 | $\text{Metadata Overhead} = N_{\text{chunks}} \times H_{\text{size}}$ | Total dedupe index memory | Bytes |
| 12 | $\text{Kolmogorov Bound: } \bar{L} \geq H(X)$ | No code is shorter than entropy | bits/symbol |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Tip:** Equations 1, 3, 5, 6, 7, 8, and 9 appear in **Part A (3 marks)** and **Part B (14 marks)** numerical questions almost every semester. Memorize them with the symbol-to-meaning mapping.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Backup & Disaster Recovery**: Dedupe is the *de-facto* standard in VTL (Virtual Tape Library) and backup targets — EMC Data Domain achieves **30:1 reduction** on real workloads.
- **Primary Storage Deduplication**: VMware VAAI, Windows Server 2016+, and ZFS use *inline block-level* dedupe.
- **Compression in Databases**: Oracle Exadata, SAP HANA use *Hybrid Columnar Compression* combining RLE + dictionary encoding.
- **Genomics & Scientific Data**: ZSTD and LZ4 are critical in genomic data pipelines due to their high decompression speed.
- **Cloud Storage**: AWS S3, Azure Blob, and Google Cloud Storage apply *per-object compression* transparently.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Combined Space Savings Formula

**Problem Statement:** A backup workload is first deduplicated with ratio $DR_1 = 10:1$, and the resulting unique data is then compressed with ratio $CR = 2.5:1$. Derive the *combined* space savings formula and compute the total savings for a **100 GB** logical dataset.

**Logical Walkthrough (Exhaustive):**

**Step 1** — Identify the stages of data transformation.

After deduplication, the *physical* data stored is:

$$
S_{\text{post-dedupe}} = \frac{S_{\text{original}}}{DR_1}
$$

**Step 2** — Apply compression to the deduplicated dataset.

After compression, the *final physical* size becomes:

$$
S_{\text{final}} = \frac{S_{\text{post-dedupe}}}{CR} = \frac{S_{\text{original}}}{DR_1 \cdot CR}
$$

**Step 3** — Express the combined space savings as a fraction.

$$
SS_{\text{combined}} = 1 - \frac{S_{\text{final}}}{S_{\text{original}}} = 1 - \frac{1}{DR_1 \cdot CR}
$$

**Step 4** — Algebraically expand the savings to a sum of individual savings.

Multiplying out, the identity emerges:

$$
SS_{\text{combined}} = 1 - \left(1 - SS_d\right)\left(1 - SS_c\right)
$$

where $SS_d = 1 - \frac{1}{DR_1}$ and $SS_c = 1 - \frac{1}{CR}$.

**Step 5** — Verify the derivation is dimensionally and logically correct.

- If $DR_1 = 1$ (no dedupe), the formula reduces to $SS_c$ ✓
- If $CR = 1$ (no compression), the formula reduces to $SS_d$ ✓
- If both are applied, savings are *multiplicatively* amplified, not merely additive.

**Step 6** — Numerical evaluation for the given problem.

$$
S_{\text{original}} = 100 \text{ GB}, \quad DR_1 = 10, \quad CR = 2.5
$$

After deduplication:

$$
S_{\text{post-dedupe}} = \frac{100}{10} = 10 \text{ GB}
$$

After compression:

$$
S_{\text{final}} = \frac{10}{2.5} = 4 \text{ GB}
$$

Combined space savings:

$$
SS_{\text{combined}} = 1 - \frac{4}{100} = 0.96 = 96\%
$$

Combined data reduction ratio:

$$
DR_{\text{combined}} = \frac{100}{4} = 25:1
$$

**Verification using expanded form:**

$$
SS_d = 1 - \frac{1}{10} = 0.9, \quad SS_c = 1 - \frac{1}{2.5} = 0.6
$$

$$
SS_{\text{combined}} = 1 - (1-0.9)(1-0.6) = 1 - (0.1)(0.4) = 1 - 0.04 = 0.96 \checkmark
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight for KTU students**: When both dedupe and compression are stacked, the savings are *multiplicative in their complements*, not additive. This is a frequently tested trap in numerical questions.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Shannon Entropy & Huffman Efficiency Bound

**Problem Statement:** A storage system encodes the symbols $\{A, B, C, D, E\}$ with the probability distribution $P = \{0.40, 0.20, 0.15, 0.15, 0.10\}$. Derive the Shannon Entropy, propose a Huffman code, compute the average codeword length, and find the efficiency.

**Step 1** — Compute the Shannon Entropy $H(X)$.

$$
H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{5} p_i \log_2 p_i
$$

$$
H(X) = -[0.40 \log_2 0.40 + 0.20 \log_2 0.20 + 0.15 \log_2 0.15 + 0.15 \log_2 0.15 + 0.10 \log_2 0.10]
$$

Computing each term:

- $0.40 \log_2 0.40 = 0.40 \times (-1.3219) = -0.5288$
- $0.20 \log_2 0.20 = 0.20 \times (-2.3219) = -0.4644$
- $0.15 \log_2 0.15 = 0.15 \times (-2.7370) = -0.4105$
- $0.10 \log_2 0.10 = 0.10 \times (-3.3219) = -0.3322$

Sum of magnitudes:

$$
H(X) = 0.5288 + 0.4644 + 0.4105 + 0.4105 + 0.3322 = 2.1464 \text{ bits/symbol}
$$

**Step 2** — Build a Huffman Tree.

Combine the two lowest probabilities iteratively:

| Iteration | Symbols | Probabilities | Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\{E, D\}$ | $0.10 + 0.15 = 0.25$ | Combine E+D |
| 2 | $\{CD, ED\}$ | $0.15 + 0.25 = 0.40$ | Combine C with (ED) |
| 3 | $\{B, (CD(ED))\}$ | $0.20 + 0.40 = 0.60$ | Combine B with (CD(ED)) |
| 4 | $\{A, BCD(ED)\}$ | $0.40 + 0.60 = 1.00$ | Root |

**Step 3** — Assign Codewords (left=0, right=1 convention).

| Symbol | Probability | Codeword | Length $l_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| A | 0.40 | `0` | 1 |
| B | 0.20 | `10` | 2 |
| C | 0.15 | `110` | 3 |
| D | 0.15 | `1110` | 4 |
| E | 0.10 | `1111` | 4 |

**Step 4** — Compute Average Codeword Length $\bar{L}$.

$$
\bar{L} = \sum_{i=1}^{5} p_i \cdot l_i = 0.40(1) + 0.20(2) + 0.15(3) + 0.15(4) + 0.10(4)
$$

$$
\bar{L} = 0.40 + 0.40 + 0.45 + 0.60 + 0.40 = 2.25 \text{ bits/symbol}
$$

**Step 5** — Verify the Kraft Inequality (prefix-free condition).

$$
K = \sum_{i=1}^{5} 2^{-l_i} = 2^{-1} + 2^{-2} + 2^{-3} + 2^{-4} + 2^{-4}
$$

$$
K = 0.5 + 0.25 + 0.125 + 0.0625 + 0.0625 = 1.000 \leq 1 \checkmark
$$

**Step 6** — Compute Coding Efficiency and Redundancy.

$$
\eta = \frac{H(X)}{\bar{L}} = \frac{2.1464}{2.25} = 0.9540 = 95.40\%
$$

$$
R = 1 - \eta = 0.0460 = 4.60\%
$$

**Step 7** — Compression ratio if symbols are stored naively in 3-bit fixed length.

$$
CR = \frac{3.00}{2.25} = 1.33 : 1
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Huffman's Optimality Theorem**: For any symbol set with known probability distribution, Huffman coding produces a code whose average length $\bar{L}$ satisfies $H(X) \leq \bar{L} < H(X) + 1$. Our result of $\bar{L} = 2.25$ lies within $[2.1464, 3.1464]$ ✓ — the optimality bound is satisfied.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Deduplication Granularities

| Property | File-Level | Block-Level (Fixed) | Block-Level (Variable) | Byte-Level |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Chunking Logic** | Whole file | Fixed offset | Content fingerprint | Single byte |
| **Hash Computations per MB** | $\sim 1$ | $\sim 256$ (for 4 KB) | $\sim 256$ | $\sim 10^6$ |
| **Dedupe Ratio (Typical)** | 2:1 – 5:1 | 5:1 – 15:1 | 10:1 – 30:1 | 20:1 – 50:1 |
| **Metadata Memory Overhead** | Very Low | Moderate | Moderate | Extreme |
| **CPU Cost** | Low | Moderate | Moderate–High | Extreme |
| **Boundary Shift Resilience** | N/A | Poor | Excellent | Excellent |
| **Production Use** | VTL, archive | VTL | Enterprise dedupe | Research only |
| **Industry Product** | Symantec NetBackup | Windows Server | EMC Data Domain | (None) |

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — RLE Storage

**Problem:** A 1 GB volume contains a 16 KB block where each sector is `0x00`. Compute the RLE-encoded storage.

**Step 1** — Identify the run.

A 16 KB block = **16384 bytes** of `0x00`. One run only.

**Step 2** — Determine the RLE tuple size.

If we use a 16-bit count + 8-bit value:

$$
\text{Encoded size} = 2 \text{ bytes (count)} + 1 \text{ byte (value)} = 3 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 3** — Compute the compression ratio.

$$
CR = \frac{16384}{3} \approx 5461 : 1
$$

**Step 4** — Compute savings percentage.

$$
SS = 1 - \frac{3}{16384} \approx 99.98\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** RLE is a *trivial* lossless algorithm; KTU questions test whether students recognize that RLE *expands* data when no runs exist. Example: `ABCDEFGH` (8 bytes) → `1A1B1C1D1E1F1G1H` (16 bytes) — a **negative compression ratio of 0.5:1**! This is a classic trick question in Part A.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 3 — Metadata Memory Budget for a Dedupe Index

**Problem:** A 500 TB backup target uses 8 KB average chunk size with SHA-256 hashes (32 bytes) + 8-byte pointer. Calculate the *in-memory index* size.

**Step 1** — Number of unique chunks.

$$
N_{\text{chunks}} = \frac{500 \text{ TB}}{8 \text{ KB}} = \frac{500 \times 2^{40}}{8 \times 2^{10}} = 6.5536 \times 10^{10} \text{ chunks}
$$

**Step 2** — Bytes per index entry.

$$
E_{\text{entry}} = 32 \text{ (hash)} + 8 \text{ (pointer)} + 4 \text{ (refcount)} = 44 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 3** — Total memory required.

$$
M_{\text{index}} = 6.5536 \times 10^{10} \times 44 \approx 2.88 \text{ TB of RAM}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Implication**: This is why enterprise dedupe appliances ship with **1 TB+ of flash-backed RAM** or use *Bloom filters* and *disk-resident hash tables*. The metadata cost is the *Achilles heel* of fine-grained dedupe — a frequently asked KTU question.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Deduplication Processing Pipeline (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Client Data Stream] --> B{Inline Mode?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Chunk Slicer]
    B -- No --> D[Raw Write to Disk]
    D --> E[Background Scheduler]
    E --> C
    C --> F[Hash Engine SHA-256]
    F --> G[Hash Index Lookup]
    G -- Match Found --> H[Increment Refcount]
    G -- No Match --> I[Write Unique Chunk]
    I --> J[Insert Hash into Index]
    H --> K[Replace Block with Pointer]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Update Container Map]
    L --> M[Storage Media]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram**: Every incoming block is *fingerprinted*. A lookup against the in-memory index determines whether the block is a duplicate. Duplicates become a tiny pointer; unique blocks are persisted and indexed. This is the canonical dataflow of *Data Domain* and *NetApp A-SIS*.

---

## 4.2 Content-Defined Chunking (CDC) Sliding Window

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph "Input Byte Stream"
        A0[Byte 0] --> A1[Byte 1] --> A2[Byte 2] --> A3[Byte 3] --> A4[Byte 4] --> A5[Byte 5] --> A6[Byte 6] --> A7[Byte 7] --> A8[Byte 8] --> A9[Byte 9] --> A10[Byte 10] --> A11[Byte 11]
    end
    subgraph "Sliding Window of 48 Bytes"
        W1[Window 1] -.-> H1{Rabin Hash mod 2 to the 12}
    end
    subgraph "Chunk Boundary Detection"
        H1 -- Pattern Match --> BCut[Mark Chunk Boundary]
    end
    subgraph "Output Chunks"
        BCut --> C1[Chunk 1 Size 4 KB avg]
        C1 --> C2[Chunk 2 Size 4 KB avg]
        C2 --> C3[Chunk 3 Size 4 KB avg]
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CDC Insight**: The window slides byte-by-byte. A boundary is marked *only* when the hash matches a pattern. Inserting a byte shifts only the local window, not the global structure — solving the boundary shift problem.

---

## 4.3 Block-Level vs File-Level Deduplication Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph "File-Level Dedupe"
        F1[File A Hash 1] --> FMatch1{Hash Exists?}
        FMatch1 -- Yes --> FP1[Pointer to Existing]
        FMatch1 -- No --> FS1[Store Full File]
        F2[File B Modified 1 Byte Hash 2] --> FMatch2{Different Hash}
        FMatch2 --> FS2[Store Full File B]
    end
    subgraph "Block-Level Dedupe"
        B1[Chunk 1] --> BH1{Hash Match?}
        B1 --> BX1[Chunk 2]
        B1 --> BX2[Chunk 3]
        B2[File B Chunk 1] --> BH1
        B2 --> BX1
        B2 --> BX3[Chunk 3 NEW]
        BH1 -- Yes --> BPtr[Pointer]
        BX3 -- No --> BStore[Store New Chunk]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram**: File-level fails to deduplicate when even one byte changes. Block-level detects per-chunk equality, so only the modified chunk requires new storage — a dramatic efficiency improvement.

---

## 4.4 Compression Pipeline (Deflate / LZ77 + Huffman)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Raw Input Bytes] --> B[Sliding Window Search]
    B --> C{Match Length >= Min Match}
    C -- Yes --> D[Output Distance Length Pair]
    C -- No --> E[Output Literal Byte]
    D --> F[Token Stream]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Huffman Encode Tokens]
    G --> H[Huffman Encode Literals]
    H --> I[Bit Packed Output]
    I --> J[Compressed Block]
    J --> K[Container with Header]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Deflate's Two-Stage Strategy**: (1) LZ77 finds repeated substrings and emits (distance, length) tuples. (2) Huffman encodes both the literal bytes and the tuple metadata. The combined approach achieves typical 2:1 – 3:1 ratios on text and 1.5:1 on binary.

---

## 4.5 Functional Architecture — Inline Dedupe + Compression in a Modern Storage Array

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Host I O Path"
        H1[Host Application] --> H2[HBA NIC] --> H3[Storage Controller Front End]
    end
    H3 --> P1[Pre Processing Cache]
    P1 --> P2[Dedupe Engine Block Splitter]
    P2 --> P3[Hash Lookup]
    P3 -- Duplicate --> P4[Pointer Only]
    P3 -- Unique --> P5[Compression Engine LZ4 or ZSTD]
    P5 --> P6[Encryption Engine AES 256 XTS]
    P6 --> P7[RAID 6 Parity Compute]
    P7 --> P8[Back End SAS or NVMe]
    P8 --> P9[SSD Pool]
    P8 --> P10[HDD Pool]
    P4 --> P7
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Mapping**: This block diagram maps to the architecture of **NetApp AFF**, **Dell PowerStore**, and **HPE Primera** systems. Dedupe → Compression → Encryption → RAID is the canonical processing order in modern all-flash arrays.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Differentiate between file-level and block-level deduplication. List two advantages of block-level over file-level.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | File-Level Dedupe | Block-Level Dedupe |
|---|---|---|
| **Granularity** | Entire file is hashed as one unit | File is split into chunks (4 KB – 128 KB), each hashed |
| **Hash Count per File** | One | Many (one per chunk) |
| **Sensitivity to Modification** | Any change → new hash → no dedupe | Only modified chunk is unique; rest dedupes |
| **Metadata Cost** | Low | High |

**Two advantages of block-level over file-level** (2 marks):

1. **Higher deduplication ratio** — partial file modifications still allow the unchanged blocks to be deduplicated.
2. **Better storage efficiency for similar files** — two files differing by even one byte share a large proportion of blocks.

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Block-level dedupe is the industry standard for backup and primary storage due to its superior ratio despite higher metadata overhead.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Define Shannon Entropy. Why is it the theoretical lower bound for lossless compression?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Shannon Entropy, $H(X)$, quantifies the average information content per symbol in a source with probability distribution $P = \{p_1, p_2, \dots, p_n\}$:

$$
H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \log_2 p_i \quad \text{bits/symbol}
$$

**Why it is the lower bound (2 marks):**

1. **Source Coding Theorem (Shannon 1948):** No lossless code can have an average length $\bar{L} < H(X)$.
2. **Intuition:** If a symbol carries only $H(X)$ bits of information on average, we cannot represent it with fewer bits without losing information. A shorter code would imply *more than one* symbol mapping to the same bit string, which violates the lossless condition.

**Conclusion:** Huffman coding achieves $\bar{L} \in [H(X), H(X)+1)$, making it asymptotically optimal for symbol-by-symbol coding.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

#### (a) [7 Marks] Explain the architecture of content-defined chunking (CDC). How does it overcome the boundary shift problem in fixed-size block-level deduplication? Use a diagram to illustrate.

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Content-Defined Chunking (CDC) is a variable-size block-level deduplication technique where chunk boundaries are determined by the *content* of the data, not by *positional offsets*. It is implemented using a *sliding-window content fingerprint* (typically a Rabin fingerprint) over the input byte stream.

**Algorithm (3 marks):**

1. A sliding window of fixed size $W$ (typically **48 bytes**) is moved byte-by-byte across the input.
2. For each window position, a *Rabin hash* $R$ is computed.
3. The chunk boundary is marked when the low-order $k$ bits of $R$ match a predefined pattern (e.g., $R \mod 2^k = 0$).
4. The expected chunk size is $2^k$ bytes (e.g., $k=12 \Rightarrow$ average **4096 bytes**).
5. After a boundary is marked, the window resets and the process repeats.

**Boundary Shift Problem Explanation (2 marks):**

In *fixed-size* chunking, inserting one byte at the start of the file shifts all subsequent chunk boundaries by one byte. Every chunk after the insertion point becomes *entirely different* in content and produces a *different hash*, destroying dedupe efficiency.

In *CDC*, the chunk boundary at position $p$ depends only on the byte content of the window ending at $p$. An insertion in the *middle* of a file only affects the *local* window region; the next boundary is determined by *content*, not by *absolute position*. Thus, the unaffected chunks *retain their original hashes* and remain deduplicable.

**Diagrammatic Illustration (1 mark):**
Refer to **Section 4.2** for the CDC sliding-window diagram. The expected chunk size and boundary location are functions of the data content, ensuring resilience to insertions and deletions.

**Conclusion (1 mark):** CDC is the foundation of modern enterprise dedupe (Data Domain, NetApp) because it gracefully handles modifications without losing deduplication ratio.

---

#### (b) [7 Marks] A backup system uses deduplication followed by compression. Original data is 1 TB. After deduplication, 200 GB remains, and after compression, 120 GB remains. Calculate the deduplication ratio, compression ratio, and combined space savings.

**Model Answer:**

**Given (1 mark):**
- $S_{\text{original}} = 1 \text{ TB} = 1024 \text{ GB}$
- $S_{\text{post-dedupe}} = 200 \text{ GB}$
- $S_{\text{final}} = 120 \text{ GB}$

**Step 1 — Deduplication Ratio (2 marks):**

$$
DR_1 = \frac{S_{\text{original}}}{S_{\text{post-dedupe}}} = \frac{1024}{200} = 5.12 : 1
$$

**Step 2 — Compression Ratio (2 marks):**

$$
CR = \frac{S_{\text{post-dedupe}}}{S_{\text{final}}} = \frac{200}{120} = 1.667 : 1
$$

**Step 3 — Combined Space Savings (2 marks):**

$$
SS_{\text{combined}} = 1 - \frac{S_{\text{final}}}{S_{\text{original}}} = 1 - \frac{120}{1024} = 1 - 0.1172 = 0.8828 = 88.28\%
$$

**Verification using multiplicative identity:**

$$
SS_d = 1 - \frac{1}{5.12} = 0.8047, \quad SS_c = 1 - \frac{1}{1.667} = 0.4002
$$

$$
SS_{\text{combined}} = 1 - (1-0.8047)(1-0.4002) = 1 - (0.1953)(0.5998) = 1 - 0.1171 \approx 0.8829 \checkmark
$$

**Conclusion:** The system achieves an **88.28% space saving**, equivalent to a combined data reduction ratio of approximately **8.53:1**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

#### (a) [7 Marks] Compare lossless and lossy compression. With examples, explain the LZ77 algorithm and its role in the Deflate algorithm.

**Model Answer:**

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Property | Lossless | Lossy |
|---|---|---|
| **Data Integrity** | Perfect reconstruction | Approximate reconstruction |
| **Compression Ratio** | 1.5:1 – 4:1 typical | 10:1 – 100:1 possible |
| **Use Case** | Databases, executable files, backup | Images, audio, video |
| **Examples** | Huffman, LZ77, LZW, ZSTD | JPEG, MP3, MPEG, AAC |
| **Predictability of Output** | Deterministic, bit-exact | Quality-dependent |
| **Storage Use** | Primary & secondary storage | Streaming & media archives |

**LZ77 Algorithm Explanation (3 marks):**

Lempel-Ziv 1977 (LZ77) is a *dictionary-based* sliding-window compression algorithm. Its core idea is:

1. Maintain a **sliding window** consisting of a *search buffer* (recently seen data) and a *look-ahead buffer* (data to be encoded).
2. Scan the look-ahead buffer for the **longest match** in the search buffer.
3. Emit a triple: `(distance, length, next_symbol)` where:
   - `distance` = how far back the match starts
   - `length` = how many bytes match
   - `next_symbol` = the byte immediately following the match
4. Slide the window forward by `length + 1` bytes and repeat.
5. If no match is found, emit `(0, 0, literal)`.

**Example:** Encoding `abababab` with window size 4:

| Position | Match | Triple |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | none | `(0,0,'a')` |
| 1 | none | `(0,0,'b')` |
| 2 | `ab` at distance 2 | `(2,2,'a')` |
| 5 | `ab` at distance 2 | `(2,2,'b')` |

**Role in Deflate (1 mark):** Deflate = **LZ77 + Huffman coding**. LZ77 produces a stream of literal bytes and (distance, length) tuples. Huffman then entropy-codes the literals and the tuple fields. Deflate is the core of `gzip`, `zlib`, `ZIP`, `PNG`, and HTTP content encoding.

**Conclusion (1 mark):** LZ77 is the most influential compression algorithm in history, forming the foundation of virtually all modern lossless compressors.

---

#### (b) [7 Marks] A source emits symbols $\{A, B, C, D\}$ with probabilities $\{0.5, 0.25, 0.125, 0.125\}$. Construct the Huffman code, compute the average codeword length, coding efficiency, and redundancy.

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Build Huffman Tree (2 marks):**

| Step | Symbols Combined | New Probability |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\{C, D\}$ | $0.125 + 0.125 = 0.25$ |
| 2 | $\{B, CD\}$ | $0.25 + 0.25 = 0.50$ |
| 3 | $\{A, BCD\}$ | $0.50 + 0.50 = 1.00$ |

**Step 2 — Assign Codewords (1 mark):**

| Symbol | $p_i$ | Codeword | $l_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| A | 0.500 | `0` | 1 |
| B | 0.250 | `10` | 2 |
| C | 0.125 | `110` | 3 |
| D | 0.125 | `111` | 3 |

**Step 3 — Average Codeword Length (1 mark):**

$$
\bar{L} = 0.5(1) + 0.25(2) + 0.125(3) + 0.125(3) = 0.5 + 0.5 + 0.375 + 0.375 = 1.75 \text{ bits/symbol}
$$

**Step 4 — Shannon Entropy (1 mark):**

$$
H(X) = -[0.5 \log_2 0.5 + 0.25 \log_2 0.25 + 0.125 \log_2 0.125 + 0.125 \log_2 0.125]
$$

$$
H(X) = -[0.5(-1) + 0.25(-2) + 0.125(-3) + 0.125(-3)] = 0.5 + 0.5 + 0.375 + 0.375 = 1.75 \text{ bits/symbol}
$$

**Step 5 — Efficiency and Redundancy (1 mark):**

$$
\eta = \frac{H(X)}{\bar{L}} = \frac{1.75}{1.75} = 1.000 = 100\%
$$

$$
R = 1 - \eta = 0.00\%
$$

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Because the probabilities follow an exact geometric series ($p_i = 2^{-i}$), the Huffman code achieves the **absolute theoretical optimum** — the average code length equals the entropy exactly, with zero redundancy. This is a rare and elegant result.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks (Examiner Insights)**
>
> 1. **Conflating dedupe ratio with compression ratio.** A *5:1 dedupe* + *2:1 compression* does NOT yield *10:1 combined*. The correct formula is $1 - (1-\frac{1}{5})(1-\frac{1}{2}) = 0.60$ savings, i.e., **2.5:1** — a classic valuation trap.
>
> 2. **Forgetting units in Shannon Entropy.** The result is in *bits per symbol* — never "bits" alone. Loss of 1 mark in Part A.
>
> 3. **Assuming RLE always compresses.** RLE *expands* data when no runs exist. Examiners love to test this with `ABCDEFGH`.
>
> 4. **Confusing inline vs post-process dedupe.** Inline occurs *before* the write, blocking the duplicate window; post-process runs *after*, exposing the system to disk-full risk during the dedupe job.
>
> 5. **Forgetting the boundary shift problem.** Students often describe block-level dedupe without mentioning the *insertion byte destroys all subsequent chunks* phenomenon. Always contrast with CDC.
>
> 6. **Using $2^{12}$ instead of $2^{40}$ in TB-to-bytes conversion.** KTU strictly uses $1 \text{ TB} = 2^{40} \text{ bytes}$ in numerical problems.
>
> 7. **Writing code in non-CS branches.** Programming snippets in storage-related modules of other branches cause *full-mark cancellation* — keep algorithms descriptive.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### ⚡ Rapid Revision Checklist

- [x] **Deduplication** identifies and stores *one unique copy* of repeated data; duplicates become *pointers*.
- [x] **Granularity**: File → Block (Fixed) → Block (Variable/CDC) → Byte. Higher granularity = better ratio, higher cost.
- [x] **Content-Defined Chunking (CDC)** uses Rabin fingerprints to mark boundaries — solves the *boundary shift problem*.
- [x] **Inline dedupe** = low-latency, no duplicate window. **Post-process** = high-throughput, exposes duplicate window.
- [x] **Hash functions** used: MD5 (legacy), SHA-1 (deprecated), SHA-256 (modern). Two-hash + byte-verify for collision safety.
- [x] **Compression** types: **Lossless** (Huffman, LZ77, LZW, Deflate, ZSTD) vs **Lossy** (DCT, Wavelet).
- [x] **LZ77** = dictionary + sliding window. **Deflate** = LZ77 + Huffman. **LZW** = dictionary with growing table.
- [x] **Huffman coding** is *prefix-free*; satisfies the Kraft inequality $\sum 2^{-l_i} \leq 1$.
- [x] **Shannon Entropy** $H(X) = -\sum p_i \log_2 p_i$ is the *theoretical lower bound* for $\bar{L}$.
- [x] **Efficiency** $\eta = H(X)/\bar{L}$; **Redundancy** $R = 1 - \eta$.
- [x] **Dedupe Ratio** $DR = S_{\text{orig}}/S_{\text{phys}}$; **Compression Ratio** $CR = S_{\text{orig}}/S_{\text{comp}}$.
- [x] **Combined Savings** = $1 - (1 - SS_d)(1 - SS_c)$ — multiplicative in *complements*, not additive.
- [x] **RLE** compresses runs of identical symbols; *expands* data without runs.
- [x] **Metadata memory** is the *Achilles heel* of dedupe: 500 TB @ 8 KB chunks = ~2.88 TB of RAM just for the index.
- [x] **Typical production ratios**: File dedupe (2:1–5:1), Block dedupe (5:1–30:1), LZ4 compression (1.5:1–3:1).
- [x] **Industry implementations**: EMC Data Domain (CDC), NetApp A-SIS, ZFS (inline block-level), Veeam (post-process), Windows Server 2016+ (inline).

### 🎯 Must-Memorize Numerical Constants

| Symbol | Value | Context |
|---|---|---|
| $1 \text{ TB}$ | $2^{40}$ bytes | Capacity calculations |
| $1 \text{ KB}$ | $2^{10}$ bytes | Chunk size |
| $\log_2 2$ | $1$ | Entropy of uniform binary |
| MD5 hash length | $128$ bits | Legacy dedupe |
| SHA-256 hash length | $256$ bits | Modern dedupe |
| Rabin window | $48$ bytes | CDC default |
| LZ4 compression speed | $\sim 500 \text{ MB/s}$ | Modern arrays |
| ZSTD compression speed | $\sim 400 \text{ MB/s}$ | Linux kernel default |

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU 2024 Tip**: In Part A, definitions of *Deduplication*, *Compression Ratio*, *Shannon Entropy*, and *CDC* are the most frequently asked. In Part B, the highest-scoring answers combine a *conceptual explanation* with a *numerical problem* — practice the combined-savings formula and Huffman efficiency calculation until they become second nature.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Quotas and Archiving
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST867 - STORAGE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Quotas and Archiving

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 Module 4: Quotas and Archiving — Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are Quotas in Storage Systems?

A **storage quota** is an administrative limit imposed by a storage administrator that restricts the amount of disk space a user, group, or project can consume on a given filesystem, volume, or storage pool. Quotas are enforced by the **operating system kernel** (in software-defined storage stacks) or by the **storage array controller** (in enterprise SAN/NAS systems) to prevent any single entity from monopolizing shared storage resources.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition:**
> *A quota is a pre-defined resource allocation policy that caps the maximum allowable data consumption (measured in blocks, bytes, files, or inodes) for a given principal (user, group, directory tree) on a logical storage container. Quotas may be classified as **hard quotas** (enforced strictly — writes fail when the limit is reached) or **soft quotas** (warnings issued when a configurable grace period is exceeded).*

### 1.2 What is Archiving?

**Data archiving** is the process of moving data that is no longer in active operational use from primary, high-performance storage tiers to **secondary, lower-cost storage media** (such as object stores, tape libraries, or cold cloud buckets) for long-term retention, regulatory compliance, and disaster recovery. Unlike backups, archives are typically indexed, cataloged, and searchable, but they are not designed for rapid restoration of operational workloads.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PECST867):** Archiving is positioned within the broader framework of **Information Lifecycle Management (ILM)** and is closely tied to **Hierarchical Storage Management (HSM)**, where migration decisions are policy-driven rather than manual.

---

## 1.3 🧠 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

### The "Apartment Building with a Mailbox" Analogy for Quotas

Imagine a large apartment building where every resident shares a single massive mailbox room. Without rules, one resident could fill the entire room with parcels, leaving none for the others. To prevent this:

- The building manager installs a **mailbox size limit** for each apartment (this is a **quota**).
- If a resident exceeds the limit, the system either **refuses new deliveries** (hard quota) or **sends a polite warning** and gives a few days to clean out (soft quota).
- The building manager also periodically **moves old, uncollected parcels to a remote warehouse** (this is **archiving**), keeping the active mailbox room fast and uncluttered.

In the storage world:
- The **mailbox room** = a shared LUN, filesystem, or volume.
- The **resident** = a user, group, or application.
- The **mailbox size limit** = the quota policy.
- The **remote warehouse** = an archive tier (tape, object store, cold cloud).

---

## 1.4 Key Metrics & Physical Constants (Bolded Standards)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Standard Storage Capacity Units used in Quota Engineering:
> - **1 KiB (Kibibyte) = 2¹⁰ bytes = 1,024 bytes**
> - **1 MiB (Mebibyte) = 2²⁰ bytes = 1,048,576 bytes**
> - **1 GiB (Gibibyte) = 2³⁰ bytes = 1,073,741,824 bytes**
> - **1 TiB (Tebibyte) = 2⁴⁰ bytes = 1,099,511,627,776 bytes**
> - **1 PiB (Pebibyte) = 2⁵⁰ bytes = 1,125,899,906,842,624 bytes**

> **Enterprise storage vendors typically quote quotas in Tebibytes (TiB) for capacity planning, while cloud providers (AWS, Azure, GCP) bill in decimal units (TB = 10¹² bytes).** This is a **common exam pitfall** — always confirm the unit base (binary vs decimal) before solving numerical problems.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ⚙️ Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Quotas

Storage quotas in KTU-aligned enterprise systems are categorized along **three orthogonal axes**:

### A. By Scope of Principal
| Quota Type | Description | Typical Use Case |
|------------|-------------|------------------|
| **User Quota** | Limits data owned by a single UID | Home directories, personal mailboxes |
| **Group Quota** | Limits aggregate data of all members of a GID | Shared project directories |
| **Project / Directory Tree Quota** | Limits data under a specific directory subtree | Department shares, container volumes |

### B. By Enforcement Strictness
| Type | Behavior on Limit Breach | Engineering Trade-off |
|------|--------------------------|----------------------|
| **Hard Quota** | Write operations are **immediately rejected** with an `EDQUOT` error | Strict fairness; risk of application downtime |
| **Soft Quota** | Warnings issued; writes continue until **grace period** expires | Forgiving; allows temporary bursts |

### C. By Resource Dimension
- **Block Quota**: Limits raw disk space (measured in KiB / MiB / GiB).
- **Inode Quota**: Limits the number of files/directories (protects filesystem metadata tables).
- **File Count Quota**: Hybrid — tracks both size and count.

---

## 2.2 The Operational "Why" Behind Quotas

1. **Capacity Assurance (Capacity Engineering):** Guarantees that mission-critical workloads (databases, VMs) always have headroom.
2. **Cost Allocation & Chargeback:** Quotas enable **departmental billing** — each group is billed for the storage it consumes.
3. **Performance Isolation:** Prevents noisy-neighbor scenarios where one user fills a LUN and degrades IOPS for everyone.
4. **Compliance & Retention:** Pairs with archiving to enforce data sovereignty (GDPR, HIPAA) by capping local retention.

---

## 2.3 Theoretical Foundations of Archiving

Archiving is governed by three policy frameworks:

### (i) Information Lifecycle Management (ILM)
ILM is a **policy-driven framework** that defines how data transitions across storage tiers throughout its useful life. Stages: **Create → Active → Near-line → Archive → Disposition**.

### (ii) Hierarchical Storage Management (HSM)
HSM is the **automated implementation** of ILM. It continuously monitors file access patterns and migrates cold files from primary disks to secondary tape/object storage based on configurable thresholds (e.g., "files not accessed for 90 days").

### (iii) Data Archiving vs. Backup — A Critical Distinction

| Attribute | Backup | Archive |
|-----------|--------|---------|
| Purpose | Recovery from loss/corruption | Long-term retention & compliance |
| Retention | Short-to-medium term (days–weeks) | Long-term (years–decades) |
| Mutability | Overwritten cyclically | Immutable (WORM: Write Once Read Many) |
| Indexing | Minimal | Fully indexed & searchable |
| RTO/RPO | Low (minutes–hours) | High (hours–days acceptable) |
| Media | Disk, dedup appliances | Tape, optical, cold object storage |

---

## 2.4 📐 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Symbolic Form | Description |
|---|---------------------|---------------|-------------|
| 1 | Total Usable Capacity | $C_{usable} = C_{raw} \times (1 - O_{overhead})$ | Where $O_{overhead}$ includes RAID parity, filesystem metadata, and snapshot reserve. |
| 2 | Effective Quota per User | $Q_{eff} = \lfloor C_{usable} / N_{users} \rfloor \times F_{fair}$ | Fair-share allocation with fairness factor $F_{fair} \in (0,1]$ |
| 3 | Quota Utilization Ratio | $U_{q} = \frac{D_{used}}{Q_{limit}} \times 100\%$ | Expressed as a percentage; warning at **80%**, critical at **95%** |
| 4 | Block-to-Byte Conversion | $B_{bytes} = B_{blocks} \times S_{block}$ | Where $S_{block}$ is typically **4096 bytes (4 KiB)** for modern filesystems (ext4, XFS, NTFS) |
| 5 | Inode Density | $I_{density} = \frac{N_{files}}{C_{bytes}}$ | Files per byte; critical for workloads with many small files (mail servers) |
| 6 | Archive Compression Ratio | $R_{comp} = \frac{C_{original}}{C_{compressed}}$ | Typical values: 1.5:1 (documents), 3:1 (logs), 1.05:1 (already-compressed media) |
| 7 | Archive Capacity Required | $C_{archive} = \frac{D_{cold} \times (1 + G_{annual})}{R_{comp}}$ | Where $G_{annual}$ is the expected annual data growth rate |
| 8 | Storage TCO per TiB | $TCO = C_{capex} + C_{opex} \times Y_{life}$ | Sum of capital expenditure and operational expenditure over $Y_{life}$ years |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Note on $S_{block}$:** Modern enterprise filesystems (ext4, XFS, NTFS, ZFS) default to a **4 KiB block size**. However, large-file workloads (video, genomics, backups) often reformat with **64 KiB or 1 MiB blocks** to reduce metadata overhead. Always confirm the block size when converting quota units.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Cloud Storage Gateways (AWS Storage Gateway, Azure Archive Blob):** Quotas prevent runaway cloud bills; archives use **Glacier / Archive Blob** tiers costing fractions of a cent per GiB-month.
- **Enterprise NAS (NetApp ONTAP, Isilon, Qumulo):** Quotas are enforced at the **flexvol/WAFL layer** with sub-file granularity and real-time reporting.
- **HPC & Research Computing:** Project-tree quotas prevent a single research group from filling the parallel scratch filesystem, which would crash the batch scheduler.
- **Media & Entertainment:** Archives store Petabytes of raw camera footage on LTO tape, indexed by MAM (Media Asset Management) systems for decades.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Problem 1: Fair-Share Quota Allocation (7-step derivation)

**Problem Statement:**
A research organization has a storage array with **24 raw TiB** of usable capacity. The RAID-6 configuration consumes **2 TiB** for parity. The filesystem metadata reserve is **5%** of the post-RAID capacity. The organization has **8 research groups**, and the storage administrator wants to enforce a **fair-share quota** with a fairness factor $F_{fair} = 0.9$. Compute the effective quota per group.

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1: Calculate post-RAID usable capacity.**

$$C_{postRAID} = C_{raw} - C_{parity}$$

$$C_{postRAID} = 24 \text{ TiB} - 2 \text{ TiB} = 22 \text{ TiB}$$

**Step 2: Calculate filesystem metadata overhead.**

$$O_{meta} = C_{postRAID} \times 0.05$$

$$O_{meta} = 22 \text{ TiB} \times 0.05 = 1.1 \text{ TiB}$$

**Step 3: Calculate final usable capacity for data.**

$$C_{usable} = C_{postRAID} - O_{meta}$$

$$C_{usable} = 22 \text{ TiB} - 1.1 \text{ TiB} = 20.9 \text{ TiB}$$

**Step 4: Calculate raw fair-share per group (equal division).**

$$Q_{raw} = \frac{C_{usable}}{N_{groups}}$$

$$Q_{raw} = \frac{20.9 \text{ TiB}}{8} = 2.6125 \text{ TiB per group}$$

**Step 5: Apply the fairness factor $F_{fair}$.**

$$Q_{eff} = Q_{raw} \times F_{fair}$$

$$Q_{eff} = 2.6125 \times 0.9 = 2.35125 \text{ TiB per group}$$

**Step 6: Round down to a practical block-aligned value.**

Since most storage controllers allocate in units of GiB, we round down:

$$Q_{final} = 2.35 \text{ TiB} = 2.35 \times 1024 \text{ GiB} = 2406.4 \text{ GiB}$$

The administrator will set **2.35 TiB (≈ 2,406 GiB)** as the hard quota per group.

**Step 7: Set the soft quota at 80% of hard quota (industry best practice).**

$$Q_{soft} = Q_{final} \times 0.80$$

$$Q_{soft} = 2.35 \text{ TiB} \times 0.80 = 1.88 \text{ TiB}$$

When a group crosses **1.88 TiB**, the system sends a warning email. When it crosses **2.35 TiB**, writes are blocked.

---

## 3.2 Problem 2: Archive Capacity Planning (6-step derivation)

**Problem Statement:**
A financial firm has **50 TiB** of cold compliance data that must be archived for **7 years**. The data is expected to grow at **15% annually**. The compression algorithm achieves a **2.5:1 ratio** on financial documents. Compute the total archive capacity required at the end of year 7.

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1: Apply the growth factor over 7 years (compound growth).**

$$D_{final} = D_{initial} \times (1 + G_{annual})^{Y_{life}}$$

$$D_{final} = 50 \text{ TiB} \times (1.15)^{7}$$

**Step 2: Compute $(1.15)^{7}$ step-by-step.**

$$(1.15)^{2} = 1.3225$$

$$(1.15)^{4} = (1.3225)^{2} = 1.74900625$$

$$(1.15)^{6} = 1.74900625 \times 1.3225 = 2.31306076...$$

$$(1.15)^{7} = 2.31306076... \times 1.15 = 2.66001987...$$

**Step 3: Substitute back into Step 1.**

$$D_{final} = 50 \text{ TiB} \times 2.66002 \approx 133.00 \text{ TiB}$$

**Step 4: Apply compression ratio.**

$$C_{archive} = \frac{D_{final}}{R_{comp}}$$

$$C_{archive} = \frac{133.00 \text{ TiB}}{2.5} = 53.20 \text{ TiB}$$

**Step 5: Add a safety buffer of 10% for index/catalog metadata.**

$$C_{total} = C_{archive} \times 1.10$$

$$C_{total} = 53.20 \times 1.10 = 58.52 \text{ TiB}$$

**Step 6: Final answer.**

The organization must provision **≈ 58.52 TiB** of archive storage capacity (e.g., two LTO-9 cartridges of 18 TiB native each, plus one for rotation).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Quota Threshold Decision Tree

| Trigger Condition | System Action | Configuration Variable |
|-------------------|---------------|------------------------|
| Usage < 70% of $Q_{soft}$ | Normal operation | None |
| 70% ≤ Usage < 80% | **INFO log** written | Logging verbosity |
| 80% ≤ Usage < $Q_{soft}$ | **Warning email** to user/admin | SMTP gateway |
| $Q_{soft}$ ≤ Usage < $Q_{hard}$ | **Soft grace period** countdown begins | $T_{grace}$ (e.g., 7 days) |
| Usage ≥ $Q_{hard}$ | **Hard block** — all writes return `EDQUOT` | Enforced by kernel/array |

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Archive Tier Selection

| Data Temperature | Access Pattern | Recommended Tier | Cost/GiB-Month (Approx.) |
|------------------|----------------|------------------|---------------------------|
| **Hot** | Accessed daily | NVMe SSD | $0.10 – $0.30 |
| **Warm** | Accessed weekly | SAS HDD / Standard cloud | $0.02 – $0.05 |
| **Cold** | Accessed quarterly | Near-line SAS / S3 IA | $0.01 – $0.02 |
| **Frozen / Archive** | Accessed annually or less | LTO Tape / Glacier Deep Archive | $0.0001 – $0.004 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Quota Enforcement Flow (Mermaid Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Initiates Write Request] --> B{Quota Engine Lookup}
    B --> C[Check User UID in Quota Database]
    C --> D{Compute Current Usage + Request Size}
    D --> E{Is Total <= Q_soft?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Allow Write]
    F --> G[Update Usage Counter]
    G --> H[Return Success to User]
    E -- No --> I{Is Total <= Q_hard?}
    I -- Yes --> J[Allow Write + Start Grace Timer]
    J --> K[Send Warning Notification]
    K --> G
    I -- No --> L{Is Grace Period Expired?}
    L -- No --> M[Block Write + Warning]
    L -- Yes --> N[Block Write + Critical Alert]
    M --> O[Return EDQUOT Error]
    N --> O
```

## 4.2 Archive Tier Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Primary[Tier 1: Primary Storage]
        A1[Application Server] --> A2[High-Performance SSD Pool]
    end
    subgraph Secondary[Tier 2: Near-Line Storage]
        B1[Policy Engine] --> B2[SAS HDD Pool]
    end
    subgraph Archive[Tier 3: Archive Storage]
        C1[HSM Migrator] --> C2[Tape Library / Object Store]
    end
    subgraph Catalog[Metadata Catalog]
        D1[Archive Index DB] --> D2[Search and Retrieval API]
    end
    A2 --> B1
    B1 --> C1
    C1 --> D1
    D1 --> D2
    D2 --> A1
```

## 4.3 Information Lifecycle Management (ILM) State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Created
    Created --> Active: Data Written
    Active --> NearLine: 30 days no access
    NearLine --> Archive: 90 days no access
    Archive --> Disposition: Retention period expired
    Disposition --> [*]
    Archive --> Active: User Retrieval Request
    NearLine --> Active: User Retrieval Request
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`
**Differentiate between a hard quota and a soft quota in a storage system. Under what circumstances would an administrator prefer a soft quota over a hard quota?**

#### Model Answer (3 marks):
A **hard quota** is a strict upper bound on storage consumption. Once a user reaches the hard quota limit, the system immediately rejects any further write operations and returns an `EDQUOT` (or equivalent) error to the application. In contrast, a **soft quota** acts as a warning threshold — the system allows writes to continue beyond the soft limit for a configurable **grace period** (typically 7 days), after which the quota effectively becomes hard.

An administrator would prefer a **soft quota** in environments where brief capacity overruns are tolerable (for example, during large batch compilations, overnight ETL jobs, or research data ingestion). Soft quotas provide **forgiveness** while still alerting administrators to capacity planning issues.

*(Award 1.5 marks for the distinction, 1 mark for the enforcement behavior, 0.5 mark for the practical justification.)*

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`
**List any three differences between data backup and data archiving.**

#### Model Answer (3 marks):

| Parameter | Backup | Archive |
|-----------|--------|---------|
| **Purpose** | Recovery from data loss or corruption | Long-term retention and compliance |
| **Mutability** | Cyclic — old backups are overwritten | Immutable — Write Once Read Many (WORM) |
| **Retention Period** | Days to weeks | Years to decades |

*(Award 1 mark per correct difference, up to 3 marks.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B: Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of **Information Lifecycle Management (ILM)** with a neat block diagram. Describe how ILM differs from traditional backup strategies. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An enterprise has **100 TiB** of primary storage. The overhead due to RAID-5 parity and filesystem metadata is **20%**. The storage is shared among **10 departments**. The administrator sets a fairness factor of **0.85** and reserves **15%** of usable capacity for system snapshots. Compute the effective hard quota per department and the soft quota (set at 80% of hard quota). **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — ILM Explanation (7 marks):**

**Information Lifecycle Management (ILM)** is a policy-driven, comprehensive approach to managing data throughout its useful lifetime, from creation to final disposition. ILM defines rules based on the **business value, age, access frequency, and regulatory requirements** of data, automatically migrating it across storage tiers.

**Stages of ILM:**
1. **Creation:** Data is born on the highest-performance tier (NVMe SSD).
2. **Active Phase:** Data is accessed frequently and resides on primary storage.
3. **Near-line Phase:** After 30–90 days of inactivity, data is migrated to cheaper HDD tiers.
4. **Archive Phase:** After 1+ year of inactivity, data is moved to tape or cold object storage.
5. **Disposition Phase:** After the retention policy expires (e.g., 7 years), data is securely destroyed.

**Key Differences from Traditional Backup:**
- **Backup is reactive** (recovery-oriented); **ILM is proactive** (lifecycle-oriented).
- **Backup is cyclic** and short-term; **ILM is continuous** and long-term.
- **Backup uses a single media type**; **ILM uses tiered storage** matched to value.
- **ILM integrates with compliance** and legal hold frameworks; backup does not.

*(Award 3 marks for the ILM explanation, 2 marks for the block diagram, 2 marks for the differences.)*

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Compute overhead-adjusted capacity.**

$$C_{overhead} = 100 \text{ TiB} \times 0.20 = 20 \text{ TiB}$$

$$C_{postOverhead} = 100 \text{ TiB} - 20 \text{ TiB} = 80 \text{ TiB}$$

**Step 2: Subtract snapshot reserve.**

$$C_{usable} = 80 \text{ TiB} \times (1 - 0.15)$$

$$C_{usable} = 80 \text{ TiB} \times 0.85 = 68 \text{ TiB}$$

**Step 3: Fair division among 10 departments.**

$$Q_{raw} = \frac{68 \text{ TiB}}{10} = 6.8 \text{ TiB per department}$$

**Step 4: Apply fairness factor.**

$$Q_{hard} = 6.8 \text{ TiB} \times 0.85 = 5.78 \text{ TiB per department}$$

**Step 5: Compute soft quota.**

$$Q_{soft} = 5.78 \text{ TiB} \times 0.80 = 4.624 \text{ TiB per department}$$

**Final Answer:**
- **Hard Quota per department = 5.78 TiB (≈ 5,919 GiB)**
- **Soft Quota per department = 4.624 TiB (≈ 4,735 GiB)**

*(Award 1.5 marks for Step 1, 1 mark for Step 2, 1.5 marks for Step 3, 1.5 marks for Step 4, 1.5 marks for Step 5. Total 7 marks.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`

**(a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the working of a **Hierarchical Storage Management (HSM)** system. Compare HSM with manual archival processes. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A university research data archive currently holds **8 TiB** of archived data. The data grows at **20%** per year. The archive uses LTO-9 tapes with **18 TiB native capacity each** and achieves a **2:1 compression ratio**. The retention period is **10 years**. Compute: (i) the total raw uncompressed data after 10 years, (ii) the number of LTO-9 tapes required (without compression), (iii) the number of LTO-9 tapes required with compression. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — HSM Explanation (7 marks):**

**Hierarchical Storage Management (HSM)** is the automated implementation of ILM policies. It uses a **policy engine** and a **migrator daemon** that continuously scans file access metadata (atime, ctime) and migrates cold files to lower-cost tiers.

**Working:**
1. The **file system agent** intercepts file access requests.
2. The **policy engine** evaluates the file against thresholds (e.g., "last access > 90 days").
3. If the threshold is met, the **migrator** copies the file to the next tier and replaces the original with a **stub file** (a small placeholder containing the file's location).
4. When the user accesses the stub, HSM transparently recalls the file from the archive tier.

**HSM vs. Manual Archival:**

| Parameter | HSM | Manual |
|-----------|-----|--------|
| Automation | Fully automated | Requires administrator action |
| Speed | Real-time migration | Slow, error-prone |
| Cost efficiency | Optimized tier usage | Often over-provisioned |

*(Award 3 marks for HSM explanation, 2 marks for diagram, 2 marks for comparison.)*

---

**Part (b) — Tape Calculation (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Compute raw uncompressed data after 10 years.**

$$D_{10y} = 8 \text{ TiB} \times (1.20)^{10}$$

**Step 2: Compute $(1.20)^{10}$ step-by-step.**

$$(1.20)^{2} = 1.44$$

$$(1.20)^{4} = (1.44)^{2} = 2.0736$$

$$(1.20)^{5} = 2.0736 \times 1.20 = 2.48832$$

$$(1.20)^{10} = (2.48832)^{2} = 6.1917364224$$

**Step 3: Final raw data after 10 years.**

$$D_{10y} = 8 \times 6.1917 \approx 49.53 \text{ TiB}$$

**Step 4: Number of LTO-9 tapes without compression.**

$$N_{noComp} = \lceil \frac{49.53}{18} \rceil = \lceil 2.752 \rceil = 3 \text{ tapes}$$

**Step 5: Effective capacity per LTO-9 with 2:1 compression.**

$$C_{tapeCompressed} = 18 \text{ TiB} \times 2 = 36 \text{ TiB per tape}$$

**Step 6: Number of LTO-9 tapes with compression.**

$$N_{comp} = \lceil \frac{49.53}{36} \rceil = \lceil 1.376 \rceil = 2 \text{ tapes}$$

**Final Answer:**
- (i) **Raw data after 10 years ≈ 49.53 TiB**
- (ii) **Tapes without compression = 3**
- (iii) **Tapes with 2:1 compression = 2**

*(Award 2 marks for (i), 2 marks for (ii), 3 marks for (iii). Total 7 marks.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit Confusion:** Students often confuse **TB (10¹²)** with **TiB (2⁴⁰)**. When the question says "TiB," use 1024-based conversion. When it says "TB" or "terabytes" without the 'i', use 1000-based. Mixing these costs **1–2 marks**.
> 2. **Forgetting Overheads:** Always subtract RAID parity, filesystem metadata, and snapshot reserves **before** dividing by the number of users. Skipping this step is a **3-mark deduction** in numerical problems.
> 3. **Ceiling vs. Rounding:** For tape/drive counts, always use the **ceiling function** (round UP), not standard rounding. You cannot store partial data on a tape.
> 4. **Hard vs. Soft Confusion:** Students sometimes define soft quota as "no enforcement" — this is wrong. Soft quotas have a **grace period**; they are enforced, just delayed.

---

## 5.3 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ A **quota** is a policy limit on storage consumption, enforced at the **block level** (size) or **inode level** (file count).
- ✅ **Hard quotas** block writes immediately; **soft quotas** allow temporary overruns within a **grace period**.
- ✅ Quotas serve four purposes: **capacity assurance, cost chargeback, performance isolation, and compliance**.
- ✅ **Archiving** is the long-term retention of cold data on cheap, immutable media — it is **not** a substitute for backup.
- ✅ **ILM** is the policy framework; **HSM** is the automated engine that implements ILM.
- ✅ Standard capacity units in KTU exams: **KiB, MiB, GiB, TiB, PiB** (binary) — remember $1 \text{ TiB} = 1024 \text{ GiB}$.
- ✅ Standard block size in modern filesystems: **4 KiB**.
- ✅ **Compression ratios** are workload-dependent: documents compress better than pre-compressed media.
- ✅ **LTO tape generations** evolve roughly every 2–3 years (LTO-8: 12 TiB, LTO-9: 18 TiB, LTO-10: 30 TiB native).
- ✅ Always use the **ceiling function** when calculating the number of tapes or drives required.
- ✅ The standard warning threshold in industry is **80%** of quota; the critical threshold is **95%**.
- ✅ For ILM, typical migration windows are: **0–30 days = Hot, 30–90 days = Warm, 90+ days = Cold/Archive**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Showback and Chargeback
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECST867 - STORAGE SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Showback and Chargeback # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of enterprise storage systems and IT Service Management (ITSM) under the KTU 2024 syllabus, **Showback** and **Chargeback** are the two principal methodologies of **IT Cost Allocation** used to assign the expenses of shared storage infrastructure to the consuming business units, departments, or projects. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Showback (IT Showback / Cost Visibility Reporting):** A *non-financial* reporting mechanism in which the IT organization (or storage administrator) periodically reports the consumption of storage resources — capacity, IOPS, throughput, and tier usage — and the associated cost to each internal consumer, **without** actually transferring funds between budgets. It is fundamentally a *transparency* and *awareness* mechanism aligned with ITIL's "Strategy & Design" financial management practices. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Chargeback (IT Chargeback):** A *financial* billing mechanism in which the IT organization calculates the exact cost of storage services consumed by each business unit and **bills** that unit's budget. Funds are actually transferred from the consumer's operating/capital budget to the IT department's budget. It is a *cost-recovery* and *accountability* mechanism. The storage cost pool that is typically distributed includes: - **Capital Expenditure (CapEx):** SAN/NAS hardware, SSDs, HDDs, controllers, HBAs, switches, backup tape libraries, and storage software licenses. - **Operational Expenditure (OpEx):** Power, cooling, datacenter floor space, storage administrator salaries, maintenance contracts, and cloud-storage egress/ingress fees. - **Depreciation:** The amortized value of the storage assets over their useful life (typically **3 to 5 years** for enterprise arrays). ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition Imagine a **college hostel mess (cafeteria)**: - **Showback** is like the mess manager putting up a notice board every month saying: *"Computer Science department ate 1,200 plates, Electronics department ate 850 plates, Mechanical department ate 600 plates. Estimated cost per plate is ₹80."* The departments now *know* how much they consume and roughly what it costs them, but **no money actually moves** between budgets. The goal is **awareness** — if Mechanical sees they're using a lot, they may self-regulate. - **Chargeback** is like the mess manager sending an **actual invoice** to each department's principal: *"Computer Science owes ₹96,000 for 1,200 plates, Electronics owes ₹68,000, Mechanical owes ₹48,000. Please remit payment by the 10th."* Now **real money flows** from the department's budget back to the mess budget. The goal is **fair, enforceable cost recovery**. In a storage environment, the "plates" are **gigabytes, IOPS, and tier-classes** (Gold SSD, Silver SAS, Bronze NL-SAS, Tape), and the "diners" are the **business units** (Finance, HR, R&D, Sales, Engineering). > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight for KTU Exams:** Showback is a *prerequisite mindset* for Chargeback. Most enterprises begin with Showback to build cost-awareness culture, and then graduate to Chargeback once consumption tracking matures and political will exists. ## 1.3 Core Storage Metrics Used > [!IMPORTANT] > The standard unit of measure for storage cost allocation is **Cost per GB (₹/GB or $/GB)**. However, modern tiered systems also use **Cost per IOPS**, **Cost per MB/s of throughput**, and **Service-Level Tier Multipliers** (e.g., Gold = 4× base rate, Silver = 2× base rate, Bronze = 1× base rate). These metrics form the foundation of every showback/chargeback report. A typical KTU question may ask you to compute a department's monthly bill given its tier-mix and consumption. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Strategic "Why" — Drivers of Showback/Chargeback Storage infrastructure in modern enterprises is **shared, consolidated, and expensive**. Without a cost-allocation strategy, several problems arise: 1. **The "Free Rider" Problem:** Departments over-provision storage because they don't see the cost. A 10 TB LUN is requested for a 1 TB dataset because "it's free." 2. **Inefficient Tier Usage:** Critical databases run on cheap NL-SAS, while test data sits on expensive SSD — purely because the consumer doesn't know the cost differential. 3. **No Capital Justification:** When IT asks for a ₹50 lakh storage upgrade, there's no per-department consumption data to justify which unit should bear the cost. 4. **No Demand Forecasting:** Without historical consumption data, capacity planning becomes guesswork. Showback addresses the **awareness** problem; Chargeback addresses the **accountability** problem. ## 2.2 The Operational "How" — Implementation Lifecycle A robust storage showback/chargeback system follows this **5-stage pipeline**: - **Stage 1 — Cost Pool Identification:** Catalogue every cost element. Group into CapEx (depreciated monthly), OpEx, and allocated overhead (power, cooling, floor space). - **Stage 2 — Resource Metering:** Use storage array management tools (e.g., NetApp OnCommand, EMC Unisphere, Hitachi Ops Center, Huawei OceanStor DeviceManager) to capture per-LUN, per-volume, per-share consumption. For software-defined storage, use tools like OpenStack Cinder, Ceph RADOSGW billing, or VMware vRealize. - **Stage 3 — Rate Definition:** Define the billing rate. The simplest is a **flat rate per GB per month**; the most sophisticated is a **tiered, SLG-driven** (Storage Level Guarantee) rate. - **Stage 4 — Allocation Engine:** Map each LUN/volume to its owning business unit via a CMDB (Configuration Management Database) tag, ownership metadata, or filesystem ACL analysis. - **Stage 5 — Reporting / Invoicing:** For Showback, produce a PDF/dashboard report. For Chargeback, generate an invoice and debit the cost center. ## 2.3 Rate Models — The Spectrum of Sophistication | **Rate Model** | **Formula / Logic** | **When Used** | **Complexity** | |---|---|---|---| | **Flat Rate (Single Pool)** | $\text{Rate} = \dfrac{\text{Total Monthly Storage Cost}}{\text{Total Provisioned GB}}$ | Homogeneous storage pools, small enterprises | Low | | **Tiered Rate** | $\text{Rate}_{\text{tier}} = \text{Base} \times \text{Tier Multiplier}$ (Gold=4×, Silver=2×, Bronze=1×) | Hybrid arrays with SSD + SAS + NL-SAS | Medium | | **Usage-Based (True Consumption)** | $\text{Cost} = \text{Used GB} \times \text{Rate} + (\text{Provisioned GB} - \text{Used GB}) \times \text{Idle Rate}$ | Cloud-like models, mature IT | High | | **Service-Level (SLA-Driven)** | $\text{Cost} = \text{Capacity Cost} + \text{Performance Cost (IOPS)} + \text{Availability Cost}$ | Mission-critical, multi-tenant storage | Very High | | **Activity-Based Costing (ABC)** | $\text{Cost} = \sum (\text{Activity Driver} \times \text{Cost Driver Rate})$ | Complex shared platforms with replication, snapshots, dedup | Research-grade | ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > Memorize these formulas — they appear frequently in KTU Module 4 numerical and 14-mark questions. | **#** | **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Unit** | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Flat Storage Rate | $R = \dfrac{C_{\text{total}}}{G_{\text{provisioned}}}$ | ₹ / GB / month | | 2 | Department Bill (Flat) | $B_{\text{dept}} = G_{\text{dept}} \times R$ | ₹ / month | | 3 | Tiered Bill | $B_{\text{dept}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} G_{i,\text{dept}} \times R_{i}$ | ₹ / month | | 4 | Tier Rate | $R_{i} = R_{\text{base}} \times M_{i}$ | ₹ / GB / month | | 5 | Capital Recovery Factor | $CRF = \dfrac{r(1+r)^{n}}{(1+r)^{n}-1}$ | dimensionless | | 6 | Monthly Depreciation | $D = \dfrac{P - S}{N \times 12}$ | ₹ / month | | 7 | Annualized CapEx | $A = P \times CRF$ | ₹ / year | | 8 | Storage Utilization | $U = \dfrac{G_{\text{used}}}{G_{\text{provisioned}}} \times 100\%$ | % | | 9 | Wasted Capacity Cost | $W = (G_{\text{prov}} - G_{\text{used}}) \times R$ | ₹ | | 10 | Total Cost of Ownership (TCO) per GB/month | $TCO_{\text{per GB}} = \dfrac{C_{\text{CapEx}} + C_{\text{OpEx}}}{G_{\text{usable}} \times 12 \times \text{Years}}$ | ₹ / GB / month | | 11 | Effective Cost After Dedup/Compression | $C_{\text{eff}} = \dfrac{C_{\text{raw}}}{DR}$ where $DR$ = Data Reduction ratio | ₹ / GB | **Notation key:** $C_{\text{total}}$ = total monthly storage cost, $G$ = capacity in GB, $M_i$ = tier multiplier, $P$ = purchase price, $S$ = salvage value, $N$ = useful life in years, $r$ = discount/interest rate, $DR$ = dedup ratio. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Cloud Service Providers (AWS, Azure, GCP):** Use the *Usage-Based* model with extreme granularity — ₹/GB-month for S3 Standard, ₹/GB-month for S3 Glacier, separate charges for GET/POST requests and data egress. - **Telecom & BFSI (Indian Banks like SBI, HDFC):** Use tiered chargeback to allocate SAN costs across business units (Retail Banking, Corporate Banking, Treasury, IT) with regulatory audit trails. - **Higher Education & Research (IIT/IIIT campus clusters):** Often use **showback only** to encourage prudent use of the centralized HPC storage tier without creating inter-departmental billing friction. - **Managed Service Providers (MSPs):** Use chargeback as a customer-facing billing mechanism, identical to a utility bill. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Deriving the Flat Storage Rate **Problem Setup:** An enterprise SAN has the following monthly cost pool: - Hardware depreciation: ₹2,50,000 - Power & cooling: ₹60,000 - Storage admin salaries (allocated): ₹1,20,000 - Maintenance contract: ₹40,000 - Software license renewal: ₹30,000 Total provisioned capacity = **500 TB**. **Step 1 — Sum the cost pool components.** $$ \begin{aligned} C_{\text{total}} &= C_{\text{depr}} + C_{\text{power}} + C_{\text{admin}} + C_{\text{maint}} + C_{\text{license}} \\ C_{\text{total}} &= 250{,}000 + 60{,}000 + 120{,}000 + 40{,}000 + 30{,}000 \\ C_{\text{total}} &= 500{,}000 \text{ ₹ / month} \end{aligned} $$ > *Text:* Adding all five monthly cost line items yields a total monthly cost pool of ₹5,00,000. **Step 2 — Convert provisioned capacity to GB.** $$ \begin{aligned} G_{\text{provisioned}} &= 500 \text{ TB} \times 1024 \text{ GB / TB} \\ G_{\text{provisioned}} &= 512{,}000 \text{ GB} \end{aligned} $$ > *Text:* Since the rate formula requires GB, we convert 500 TB to 512,000 GB using the binary 1 TB = 1024 GB convention typical of storage systems. **Step 3 — Compute the flat rate per GB per month.** $$ \begin{aligned} R &= \frac{C_{\text{total}}}{G_{\text{provisioned}}} \\ R &= \frac{500{,}000}{512{,}000} \\ R &= 0.9766 \text{ ₹ / GB / month} \end{aligned} $$ > *Text:* The unit rate is approximately ₹0.98 per GB per month.
