This is the flat chargeback rate. **Step 4 — Bill a department that owns 20 TB.** $$ \begin{aligned} B_{\text{dept}} &= G_{\text{dept}} \times R \\ G_{\text{dept}} &= 20 \times 1024 = 20{,}480 \text{ GB} \\ B_{\text{dept}} &= 20{,}480 \times 0.9766 \\ B_{\text{dept}} &= ₹19{,}998.72 \approx ₹20{,}000 \text{ / month} \end{aligned} $$ > *Text:* The department's monthly chargeback invoice is approximately ₹20,000. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Tiered Chargeback with Multipliers **Problem Setup:** The same enterprise (total cost pool ₹5,00,000/month) now has a **tiered** structure: - **Gold Tier** (SSD, mission-critical DBs): 50 TB provisioned, multiplier $M_{\text{Gold}} = 4$ - **Silver Tier** (SAS 10K, application servers): 150 TB provisioned, $M_{\text{Silver}} = 2$ - **Bronze Tier** (NL-SAS, file shares, backups): 300 TB provisioned, $M_{\text{Bronze}} = 1$ **Step 1 — Compute the weighted average base unit.** The base rate is computed by dividing the total cost by the *effective weighted capacity* (sum of capacity × multiplier): $$ \begin{aligned} G_{\text{effective}} &= \sum_{i} G_{i} \times M_{i} \\ G_{\text{effective}} &= (50{,}720 \times 4) + (153{,}600 \times 2) + (307{,}200 \times 1) \\ G_{\text{effective}} &= 202{,}880 + 307{,}200 + 307{,}200 \\ G_{\text{effective}} &= 817{,}280 \text{ GB-equivalent} \end{aligned} $$ > *Text:* Each tier's provisioned GB is multiplied by its tier multiplier, then summed. The Gold tier "consumes" 4× the base cost per GB because of its premium hardware cost. **Step 2 — Compute the base rate.** $$ \begin{aligned} R_{\text{base}} &= \frac{C_{\text{total}}}{G_{\text{effective}}} \\ R_{\text{base}} &= \frac{500{,}000}{817{,}280} \\ R_{\text{base}} &= 0.6118 \text{ ₹ / GB-equivalent / month} \end{aligned} $$ > *Text:* The base rate is the cost per "weighted GB." All tier rates are derived from this. **Step 3 — Compute each tier's actual rate.** $$ \begin{aligned} R_{\text{Gold}} &= 0.6118 \times 4 = 2.4472 \text{ ₹ / GB / month} \\ R_{\text{Silver}} &= 0.6118 \times 2 = 1.2236 \text{ ₹ / GB / month} \\ R_{\text{Bronze}} &= 0.6118 \times 1 = 0.6118 \text{ ₹ / GB / month} \end{aligned} $$ > *Text:* Gold storage is ~₹2.45/GB, Silver is ~₹1.22/GB, Bronze is ~₹0.61/GB. The ratio 4:2:1 mirrors the hardware cost gradient. **Step 4 — Bill a department using 2 TB Gold + 5 TB Silver + 10 TB Bronze.** $$ \begin{aligned} B_{\text{dept}} &= (2{,}048 \times 2.4472) + (5{,}120 \times 1.2236) + (10{,}240 \times 0.6118) \\ B_{\text{dept}} &= 5{,}011.86 + 6{,}264.83 + 6{,}264.83 \\ B_{\text{dept}} &= ₹17{,}541.52 \text{ / month} \end{aligned} $$ > *Text:* The total invoice is approximately ₹17,542 per month for this mixed-tier consumption. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Capital Recovery Factor for Annualized CapEx When the storage array was purchased for **₹60,00,000** with a **5-year** useful life, **zero salvage value**, and the enterprise's cost-of-capital is **8% per annum**, the *monthly* capital recovery must be calculated properly. **Step 1 — Apply the Capital Recovery Factor (CRF) formula.** $$ \begin{aligned} CRF &= \frac{r(1+r)^{n}}{(1+r)^{n} - 1} \\ CRF &= \frac{0.08 \times (1.08)^{5}}{(1.08)^{5} - 1} \\ (1.08)^{5} &= 1.46933 \\ CRF &= \frac{0.08 \times 1.46933}{1.46933 - 1} \\ CRF &= \frac{0.11755}{0.46933} \\ CRF &= 0.25046 \end{aligned} $$ > *Text:* The CRF of 0.25046 means that to recover the principal + 8% interest over 5 years, the annual payment must be 25.046% of the original purchase price. **Step 2 — Compute the annualized CapEx.** $$ \begin{aligned} A &= P \times CRF \\ A &= 60{,}00{,}000 \times 0.25046 \\ A &= ₹15{,}02{,}760 \text{ / year} \end{aligned} $$ > *Text:* The annual capital recovery is ₹15,02,760. **Step 3 — Convert to monthly depreciation for inclusion in the cost pool.** $$ \begin{aligned} D_{\text{monthly}} &= \frac{A}{12} \\ D_{\text{monthly}} &= \frac{15{,}02{,}760}{12} \\ D_{\text{monthly}} &= ₹1{,}25{,}230 \text{ / month} \end{aligned} $$ > *Text:* This ₹1,25,230/month is added to the cost pool in Derivation 1 as the "hardware depreciation" line item, replacing the rough ₹2,50,000 estimate. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Showback vs Chargeback | **Parameter** | **Showback** | **Chargeback** | |---|---|---| | **Money Movement** | None — informational only | Actual inter-departmental transfer | | **Implementation Complexity** | Low–Medium | High | | **Political Resistance** | Low | High (departments resist being billed) | | **Behavioral Impact** | Soft influence via awareness | Hard accountability via budget impact | | **Data Granularity Required** | Moderate | Very high (audit-grade) | | **Tooling Required** | Reporting dashboards (Grafana, Power BI) | Billing engine + ERP integration (SAP CO, Oracle GL) | | **Audit Trail** | Optional | Mandatory | | **Time to Implement** | 1–3 months | 6–18 months | | **Best Suited For** | Initial cost-culture programs, regulated environments | Mature IT, multi-tenant clouds, MSPs | | **Reversibility** | Easy to roll back | Difficult — contractual and budget-linked | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Diagram 1 — The Showback Information Flow This diagram illustrates the **one-way informational reporting pipeline** of a Showback system. Data flows from the storage infrastructure up through metering, aggregation, and reporting — but no funds flow back. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Storage Array
SAN or NAS] --> B[Storage Management Tool
NetApp OnCommand or Unisphere] B --> C[Capacity and IOPS
Metering Engine] C --> D[Cost Allocation Database
Department and Tier Mapping] D --> E[Reporting Engine
Power BI or Grafana] E --> F[Showback Report
PDF or Web Dashboard] F --> G[Department Heads
View Only No Payment] G --> H[Behavior Change
Self Regulation] H -.Awareness Feedback.-> A ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** The arrow from `G` back to `A` is *dashed* because it represents *behavioral influence*, not a physical data or money flow. This is the core distinction of Showback. ## 4.2 Diagram 2 — The Chargeback Financial Flow This diagram illustrates the **bidirectional flow** of Chargeback: storage data flows up to generate invoices, and money flows back from departmental budgets to the central IT budget. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Storage Array
SAN or NAS] --> B[Capacity Metering
Per LUN Per Volume] B --> C[Allocation Engine
Cost Pool to Tier Rates] C --> D[Invoice Generator
Monthly Bill Per Dept] D --> E[Departmental Budget
Cost Center] E -->|Actual Money Transfer| F[Central IT Budget
Recovery Account] F -->|Reinvestment| G[Storage Upgrades
and Expansion] G -.New Capacity.-> A D --> H[ERP System
SAP or Oracle GL Posting] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** The solid arrow from `E` to `F` represents the **actual fund transfer** — the defining feature of Chargeback. The ERP posting at `H` is mandatory for accounting audit trails. ## 4.3 Diagram 3 — The Showback-to-Chargeback Maturity Ladder This diagram depicts the **4-stage maturity model** that enterprises typically follow when transitioning from no cost allocation to full chargeback. ```mermaid flowchart TD L1[Stage 1
No Visibility
IT as Cost Center] --> L2[Stage 2
Showback Introduced
Monthly Reports] L2 --> L3[Stage 3
Soft Chargeback
Internal Billing No Money] L3 --> L4[Stage 4
Hard Chargeback
Full Budget Transfer] L1 -.Political and Tooling Barriers.-> L4 L2 -.After 6 to 12 months.-> L3 L3 -.After 12 to 24 months.-> L4 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Maturity Insight:** The KTU syllabus specifically emphasizes this **graduated transition**. Skipping stages (e.g., going directly from Stage 1 to Stage 4) almost always fails due to political resistance and immature metering. ## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Tiered Storage Cost Allocation Architecture This block diagram shows how a hybrid storage array's three tiers feed into the cost allocation engine, and how per-department bills are generated based on tier-mix consumption. ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Tier1[Gold Tier SSD Pool] T1A[LUN 1 DB1] --> T1P[50 TB Provisioned] T1B[LUN 2 DB2] --> T1P end subgraph Tier2[Silver Tier SAS Pool] T2A[Volume 1 App1] --> T2P[150 TB Provisioned] T2B[Volume 2 App2] --> T2P end subgraph Tier3[Bronze Tier NL-SAS Pool] T3A[Share 1 Files] --> T3P[300 TB Provisioned] T3B[Share 2 Backup] --> T3P end T1P --> ALLOC[Cost Allocation Engine] T2P --> ALLOC T3P --> ALLOC ALLOC --> DEPT1[Finance Dept Bill] ALLOC --> DEPT2[HR Dept Bill] ALLOC --> DEPT3[Engineering Dept Bill] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]` **Define IT Showback and IT Chargeback. List any two key differences between them.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **IT Showback (1 mark):** A cost-allocation reporting mechanism in which the IT department periodically reports the storage (and other IT) resource consumption and associated cost to each consuming business unit **without actually transferring any funds** between budgets. Its purpose is to create cost awareness and influence user behavior. - **IT Chargeback (1 mark):** A cost-allocation billing mechanism in which the IT department computes the exact cost of storage services consumed by each business unit and **bills that unit's budget**, resulting in actual money transferring from the consumer's budget to the IT department's budget. - **Key Differences (1 mark for any two):** 1. *Money movement:* Showback has no fund transfer; Chargeback has actual fund transfer. 2. *Behavioral impact:* Showback relies on soft awareness; Chargeback enforces hard budget accountability. 3. *Implementation effort:* Showback is simpler; Chargeback requires metering-grade precision and ERP integration. ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]` **Explain the concept of "tiered chargeback" in the context of enterprise storage systems.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Tiered chargeback is a cost-allocation model in which the storage infrastructure is divided into multiple performance/cost tiers — typically **Gold (SSD)**, **Silver (SAS)**, and **Bronze (NL-SAS/Tape)** — and each tier has a distinct per-GB billing rate derived from a **tier multiplier** ($M_i$) applied to a base rate $R_{\text{base}}$. The Gold tier, being the most expensive hardware, has the highest multiplier (typically 3×–5×), while Bronze has the lowest (1×). The rate for tier $i$ is given by $R_i = R_{\text{base}} \times M_i$, and a department's total bill is the sum of consumption across all tiers: $B_{\text{dept}} = \sum G_{i,\text{dept}} \times R_i$. This approach encourages users to place hot data on premium tiers only when justified. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]` **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture and operational workflow of a **Storage Showback system** in a large enterprise. Discuss its advantages and limitations. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A company's enterprise storage has the following cost pool per month: - Depreciation of array hardware: ₹1,80,000 - Maintenance contract: ₹50,000 - Power and cooling: ₹40,000 - Administrator salaries (allocated): ₹90,000 - Backup software license: ₹40,000 The total provisioned capacity is **400 TB**, distributed as: - **Gold (SSD):** 40 TB - **Silver (SAS):** 120 TB - **Bronze (NL-SAS):** 240 TB Tier multipliers: Gold = 5, Silver = 2, Bronze = 1. Compute the **base rate**, the **per-tier rates**, and the **monthly chargeback bill** for a department that uses 2 TB Gold + 4 TB Silver + 8 TB Bronze. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks - **Architecture diagram (3 marks):** Draw a block diagram with the following blocks in sequence: *Storage Array (SAN/NAS) → Metering Tool (e.g., NetApp OnCommand) → Cost Allocation DB → Reporting Engine (Power BI/Grafana) → Showback Report (PDF/Dashboard) → Department Head (view only, no payment)*. (Use a horizontal flowchart with at least 6 blocks, each labeled.) - **Operational workflow (2 marks):** (1) Meter per-LUN, per-volume, per-share consumption monthly. (2) Map each LUN to its owning business unit using CMDB tags or ACL analysis. (3) Multiply consumption by tier rate. (4) Generate a graphical report showing consumption and cost per department. (5) Distribute to department heads; no money transfers. - **Advantages (1 mark):** Creates cost awareness, encourages self-regulation, politically low-resistance, simple to implement, useful first step toward full chargeback. - **Limitations (1 mark):** No enforceable accountability, no fund recovery for IT, may be ignored by departments, does not reduce IT's budget pressure. #### Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks **Step 1 — Compute total monthly cost pool.** $$ \begin{aligned} C_{\text{total}} &= 1{,}80{,}000 + 50{,}000 + 40{,}000 + 90{,}000 + 40{,}000 \\ C_{\text{total}} &= ₹4{,}00{,}000 \text{ / month} \end{aligned} $$ > *Text:* The five cost line items sum to ₹4,00,000 per month. **(1 mark)** **Step 2 — Convert capacities to GB and compute effective weighted capacity.** $$ \begin{aligned} G_{\text{Gold}} &= 40 \times 1024 = 40{,}960 \text{ GB} \\ G_{\text{Silver}} &= 120 \times 1024 = 1{,}22{,}880 \text{ GB} \\ G_{\text{Bronze}} &= 240 \times 1024 = 2{,}45{,}760 \text{ GB} \\ G_{\text{effective}} &= (40{,}960 \times 5) + (1{,}22{,}880 \times 2) + (2{,}45{,}760 \times 1) \\ &= 2{,}04{,}800 + 2{,}45{,}760 + 2{,}45{,}760 \\ &= 6{,}96{,}320 \text{ GB-equivalent} \end{aligned} $$ > *Text:* Each tier's GB is multiplied by its tier multiplier, then summed. **(2 marks)** **Step 3 — Compute base rate and per-tier rates.** $$ \begin{aligned} R_{\text{base}} &= \frac{4{,}00{,}000}{6{,}96{,}320} = ₹0.5745 \text{ / GB / month} \\ R_{\text{Gold}} &= 0.5745 \times 5 = ₹2.8725 \text{ / GB / month} \\ R_{\text{Silver}} &= 0.5745 \times 2 = ₹1.1490 \text{ / GB / month} \\ R_{\text{Bronze}} &= 0.5745 \times 1 = ₹0.5745 \text{ / GB / month} \end{aligned} $$ > *Text:* The base rate is ₹0.5745/GB; Gold is 5× of base, Silver is 2×, Bronze is 1×. **(2 marks)** **Step 4 — Compute the department's monthly bill.** $$ \begin{aligned} G_{\text{dept,Gold}} &= 2 \times 1024 = 2{,}048 \text{ GB} \\ G_{\text{dept,Silver}} &= 4 \times 1024 = 4{,}096 \text{ GB} \\ G_{\text{dept,Bronze}} &= 8 \times 1024 = 8{,}192 \text{ GB} \\ B_{\text{dept}} &= (2{,}048 \times 2.8725) + (4{,}096 \times 1.1490) + (8{,}192 \times 0.5745) \\ &= 5{,}882.88 + 4{,}706.30 + 4{,}706.30 \\ &= ₹15{,}295.48 \text{ / month} \end{aligned} $$ > *Text:* The department's monthly chargeback invoice is approximately **₹15,295.48**. **(2 marks)** --- ### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]` **(a)** Explain the **5-stage implementation lifecycle** of a storage chargeback system. For each stage, identify the key activities and typical tools used. **(7 marks)** **(b)** An enterprise SAN was purchased for **₹80,00,000** with a **5-year** useful life, **zero salvage value**, and a cost-of-capital of **10% per annum**. The OpEx per year is **₹12,00,000** (power, cooling, admin, maintenance combined). Total provisioned capacity is **600 TB**. Compute: **(i)** the monthly capital recovery using the CRF formula, **(ii)** the monthly total cost pool, **(iii)** the flat rate per GB per month, and **(iv)** the monthly bill for a department using 25 TB. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks - **Stage 1 — Cost Pool Identification (1.5 marks):** Catalogue all CapEx (hardware, software) and OpEx (power, cooling, salaries, maintenance). Allocate shared overhead using activity-based costing. - **Stage 2 — Resource Metering (1.5 marks):** Use array-native tools (NetApp OnCommand Insight, Dell EMC Unisphere, Huawei OceanStor DeviceManager) to capture per-LUN/per-volume capacity and IOPS. For software-defined storage, use OpenStack Ceilometer, Ceph RGW usage, or VMware vRealize. - **Stage 3 — Rate Definition (1 mark):** Choose a rate model (flat, tiered, usage-based, SLA-driven) based on business maturity and tool capability. - **Stage 4 — Allocation Engine (1.5 marks):** Map each storage object (LUN, volume, share) to a cost center using CMDB tags, ownership metadata, or filesystem ACL analysis. - **Stage 5 — Reporting and Invoicing (1.5 marks):** Generate showback dashboards (Power BI, Grafana) for awareness OR chargeback invoices (PDF + ERP posting to SAP/Oracle GL) for hard billing. #### Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks **Step (i) — Compute the Capital Recovery Factor.** $$ \begin{aligned} CRF &= \frac{0.10 \times (1.10)^{5}}{(1.10)^{5} - 1} \\ (1.10)^{5} &= 1.61051 \\ CRF &= \frac{0.10 \times 1.61051}{1.61051 - 1} \\ CRF &= \frac{0.16105}{0.61051} \\ CRF &= 0.26380 \end{aligned} $$ > *Text:* The CRF is 0.26380. **(1 mark)** **Step (i cont.) — Compute the monthly capital recovery.** $$ \begin{aligned} A &= 80{,}00{,}000 \times 0.26380 = ₹21{,}10{,}400 \text{ / year} \\ D_{\text{monthly,CapEx}} &= \frac{21{,}10{,}400}{12} = ₹1{,}75{,}867 \text{ / month} \end{aligned} $$ > *Text:* Monthly CapEx recovery is ₹1,75,867. **(1 mark)** **Step (ii) — Compute the total monthly cost pool.** $$ \begin{aligned} D_{\text{monthly,OpEx}} &= \frac{12{,}00{,}000}{12} = ₹1{,}00{,}000 \text{ / month} \\ C_{\text{total}} &= 1{,}75{,}867 + 1{,}00{,}000 = ₹2{,}75{,}867 \text{ / month} \end{aligned} $$ > *Text:* The total monthly cost pool is ₹2,75,867. **(1 mark)** **Step (iii) — Compute the flat rate per GB per month.** $$ \begin{aligned} G_{\text{provisioned}} &= 600 \times 1024 = 6{,}14{,}400 \text{ GB} \\ R &= \frac{2{,}75{,}867}{6{,}14{,}400} = ₹0.4490 \text{ / GB / month} \end{aligned} $$ > *Text:* The flat rate is ₹0.449/GB/month. **(2 marks)** **Step (iv) — Compute the department's monthly bill.** $$ \begin{aligned} G_{\text{dept}} &= 25 \times 1024 = 25{,}600 \text{ GB} \\ B_{\text{dept}} &= 25{,}600 \times 0.4490 = ₹11{,}494.40 \text{ / month} \end{aligned} $$ > *Text:* The department's monthly invoice is approximately **₹11,494**. **(2 marks)** --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common places students LOSE marks in this topic:** > > 1. **Unit Conversion Error (loses 1–2 marks):** Forgetting to convert TB to GB before applying the rate formula. Always multiply TB × 1024 (binary) or TB × 1000 (decimal, used by drive vendors). KTU exam questions usually expect **TB × 1024 = GB**. > 2. **Confusing Showback and Chargeback definitions (loses 1 mark):** Writing that Showback "also bills departments" or that Chargeback "is just a report" — examiners deduct heavily for swapped definitions. > 3. **Skipping the CRF derivation (loses 2 marks):** For capital recovery, students often write $D = P / (N \times 12)$ — this is *straight-line* depreciation, which **ignores the time value of money**. When the cost-of-capital $r$ is given, you MUST use the CRF formula. > 4. **Forgetting the ERP integration in Chargeback (loses 1 mark):** A complete answer must mention that the invoice is *posted* to the ERP system (SAP/Oracle GL) for accounting audit. Just printing a PDF is Showback, not Chargeback. > 5. **Not drawing the architecture block diagram (loses 1–2 marks):** In 7-mark sub-parts, the diagram is worth 2–3 marks. A textual description without a flowchart is incomplete. > 6. **Mixing up tier multipliers (loses 1 mark):** Tier multipliers apply to *rates*, not to *costs*. Compute the base rate first, then multiply. Do not multiply the total cost by multipliers. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Showback and Chargeback:** - **Showback = Report Only, No Money.** Chargeback = Actual Fund Transfer. - The standard cost metric is **₹ (or $) per GB per month**. - The **flat rate** formula is $R = C_{\text{total}} / G_{\text{provisioned}}$. - The **tiered rate** formula is $R_i = R_{\text{base}} \times M_i$, where $M_i$ is the tier multiplier (Gold > Silver > Bronze). - A department's bill is $B = \sum G_i \times R_i$ (sum across all tiers it consumes). - The **Capital Recovery Factor (CRF)** is $CRF = r(1+r)^n / [(1+r)^n - 1]$ — used to annualize capital costs considering the **time value of money**. - Always convert **TB to GB** (multiply by 1024) before applying per-GB rates. - The **5-stage implementation lifecycle** is: (1) Cost Pool Identification, (2) Resource Metering, (3) Rate Definition, (4) Allocation Engine, (5) Reporting/Invoicing. - The **4-stage maturity ladder** is: No Visibility → Showback → Soft Chargeback → Hard Chargeback. - Chargeback requires **ERP integration** (SAP CO, Oracle GL) for audit; Showback does not. - **Tier multipliers** reflect the cost gradient of underlying hardware: SSD > SAS > NL-SAS > Tape. - **Key tools** for metering: NetApp OnCommand Insight, EMC Unisphere, Hitachi Ops Center, VMware vRealize. - **Key reporting tools:** Power BI, Grafana, Tableau. - **Cloud parallels:** AWS S3, Azure Blob, and GCP Cloud Storage all use **usage-based chargeback** with per-GB-month rates and separate request/egress charges. - **Showback advantage:** politically low-resistance, easy to roll out, builds cost culture. - **Chargeback advantage:** enforceable accountability, full cost recovery, fair inter-departmental cost allocation. - **Chargeback disadvantage:** politically contentious, complex metering, requires ERP integration, high implementation cost. - The **effective cost** after data reduction (dedup/compression) is $C_{\text{eff}} = C_{\text{raw}} / DR$. - The **storage utilization** metric is $U = G_{\text{used}} / G_{\text{provisioned}} \times 100\%$; low utilization (over-provisioning) inflates the cost pool and effectively penalizes all consumers in a flat-rate model. - **Activity-Based Costing (ABC)** is the most sophisticated rate model — used in research papers and very mature IT organizations, not typically expected in KTU numericals.

topics.title: Performance Management - Latency/Response Time
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST867 - STORAGE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Performance Management - Latency/Response Time

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of KTU 2024 Scheme (Course Code: **PECST867 – Storage Systems**, Module 4), **Performance Management** is the systematic engineering discipline of measuring, modeling, and optimizing the temporal behavior of a storage subsystem under varying workload conditions. The two primary temporal metrics of interest are:

- **Latency**: The time taken by a single storage component (device, channel, or controller) to complete a discrete internal operation. It is purely a *device-internal* timing measurement and excludes the time spent waiting in a service queue.
- **Response Time**: The end-to-end wall-clock time measured from the moment an I/O request is issued by the host until the application receives confirmation that the operation has completed. It is a *system-level* metric and **includes** queuing delays, host overheads, and network/storage-fabric transit times.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Box:** Response Time = Service Time + Queuing Delay. Latency is *always a subset* of Response Time. Latency is intrinsic to the device; Response Time is extrinsic and depends on system load.

The Relationship Hierarchy (from innermost to outermost) is:

$$\text{Latency} \;\subseteq\; \text{Service Time} \;\subseteq\; \text{Response Time}$$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you walk into a **Government RTO office** to renew your driving licence.

- **Latency** is the time the *single clerk* takes to type your details, scan your documents, and print the card once it is your turn (a fixed, inherent processing time).
- **Queueing Delay** is the 90 minutes you spent standing in the queue waiting for the previous 27 people to finish.
- **Response Time** is the **total time** from when you entered the building to when you walked out with the licence (≈ 95 minutes).

The clerk cannot type faster than his inherent *latency*, but the *response time* you experience depends on how many people are in the queue (system load). Storage systems work exactly the same way: the disk platters have a fixed physical **rotational latency** and **seek latency**, but the I/O response time balloons when many requests pile up in the controller's queue.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU Units to Remember:**
> - **Latency** is measured in **milliseconds (ms)** or **microseconds (µs)**.
> - **Throughput** is measured in **IOPS** (I/O Operations Per Second) or **MB/s**.
> - **Bandwidth** is measured in **MB/s** or **GB/s**.

## 1.3 Why This Topic is a KTU High-Weight Module

Module 4 in **PECST867** sits at the intersection of *Queueing Theory*, *Operating Systems*, and *Disk Physics*. KTU examiners frequently test the following high-yield themes:

1. Derivation of the **M/M/1 queueing response time formula**.
2. Application of **Little's Law** to storage subsystems.
3. Decomposition of disk service time ($T_s = T_{seek} + T_{rot} + T_{transfer}$).
4. Trade-off analysis between **IOPS** and **sequential throughput**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Decomposition of Disk I/O Service Time

For a **rotational Hard Disk Drive (HDD)**, the total service time $T_s$ experienced by a single I/O request at the device is decomposed into three physical components:

1. **Seek Time ($T_{seek}$)** — The time required to move the actuator arm so that the read/write head is positioned over the correct track. It is a *mechanical* operation dependent on the distance travelled (≈ **3 ms to 12 ms** for modern enterprise HDDs, with an *average seek* of ~4 ms).

2. **Rotational Latency ($T_{rot}$)** — The time spent waiting for the correct sector to rotate under the head. For a disk spinning at rotational speed $N$ revolutions per minute:

$$T_{rot,\text{avg}} = \frac{1}{2} \times \frac{60}{N} \text{ seconds}$$

> [!NOTE]
> For a **7,200 RPM** drive, average $T_{rot}$ = 4.17 ms. For a **15,000 RPM** drive, average $T_{rot}$ = 2.0 ms. SSD rotational latency is **zero**.

3. **Data Transfer Time ($T_{transfer}$)** — The time to actually move the bits under the head:

$$T_{transfer} = \frac{\text{Request Size (bytes)}}{\text{Transfer Rate (bytes/sec)}}$$

**Total Device Service Time:**

$$T_s = T_{seek} + T_{rot} + T_{transfer}$$

## 2.2 Queuing Theory Foundations (M/M/1 Model)

Storage subsystems are modeled as a **single-server queue** where:
- **M** = Markovian (Poisson) arrival process
- **M** = Markovian (exponential) service times
- **1** = Single server

Let $\lambda$ = arrival rate (requests/sec) and $\mu$ = service rate (requests/sec). The **traffic intensity** (or utilization) is:

$$\rho = \frac{\lambda}{\mu}, \quad \text{where } 0 \leq \rho < 1$$

For a stable system, the arrival rate must be strictly less than the service rate.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **M/M/1 Response Time ($R$)** equation is the most frequently tested formula in this module:

$$R = \frac{1}{\mu - \lambda} = \frac{1}{\mu(1 - \rho)}$$

The **Average Queue Length ($L_q$)** and **Total Number in System ($L$)** are:

$$L_q = \frac{\rho^2}{1 - \rho} = \frac{\lambda^2}{\mu(\mu - \lambda)}$$

$$L = \frac{\rho}{1 - \rho} = \frac{\lambda}{\mu - \lambda}$$

## 2.3 Little's Law — The Golden Theorem

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Little's Law** is universally applicable to any stable queuing system in equilibrium:

$$L = \lambda \times W$$

Where:
- $L$ = average number of items in the system
- $\lambda$ = average arrival rate (items/sec)
- $W$ = average time an item spends in the system (response time)

**Real-World Engineering Utility:** Little's Law is used by SAN administrators to:
- Estimate the number of in-flight I/O requests from a given response-time SLA.
- Validate storage-array benchmark results (e.g., SPEC SFS).
- Capacity-plan front-end ports and back-end disk shelves.

## 2.4 IOPS vs Throughput vs Latency — The Performance Triangle

| Metric | Definition | Unit | Optimization Goal |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **IOPS** | I/O Operations Per Second | operations/sec | Maximize for small random workloads (OLTP databases) |
| **Throughput / Bandwidth** | Data volume transferred per second | MB/s or GB/s | Maximize for sequential workloads (video streaming, backups) |
| **Latency** | Time for one operation to complete | ms or µs | Minimize for latency-sensitive apps (stock trading) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Hard Trade-off:** A disk can deliver either high IOPS *or* high throughput, but rarely both simultaneously. Increasing the request size (e.g., 64 KB instead of 4 KB) inflates throughput by ~16× but reduces IOPS by the same factor, while keeping latency roughly constant.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL TABLE NOTICE:** This table uses `\vert` and `\mid` delimiters for absolute-value and conditional notation to **avoid breaking the markdown table syntax** (per the engine's anti-pipe rule).

| Concept | Formula | Variables | Typical Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Rotational Latency (avg) | $T_{rot} = \dfrac{30}{N}$ | $N$ = disk RPM | ms |
| Transfer Time | $T_{tr} = \dfrac{B}{R_d}$ | $B$ = bytes, $R_d$ = transfer rate | seconds |
| Total Service Time | $T_s = T_{seek} + T_{rot} + T_{tr}$ | Sum of all device delays | seconds |
| Utilization | $\rho = \dfrac{\lambda}{\mu}$ | $\lambda$ = arrival rate, $\mu$ = service rate | dimensionless |
| M/M/1 Response Time | $R = \dfrac{1}{\mu(1-\rho)}$ | $0 \le \rho < 1$ | seconds |
| M/M/1 Avg Queue Length | $L_q = \dfrac{\rho^2}{1-\rho}$ | Pure queue, excludes server | requests |
| Little's Law | $L = \lambda \cdot W$ | Holds for any stable system | mixed |
| IOPS from Throughput | $\text{IOPS} = \dfrac{\text{Throughput (MB/s)}}{\text{Request Size (MB)}}$ | Bandwidth/Size | ops/sec |
| Effective Service Time | $T_{\text{eff}} = \dfrac{T_s}{1 - \rho}$ | Open-queue M/M/1 form | seconds |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

In enterprise environments, performance management directly informs:
- **Storage Tiering:** Placing hot data on low-latency SSDs (≈ 100 µs) and cold data on high-capacity HDDs (≈ 5–10 ms).
- **RAID Configuration:** RAID-5/6 introduces write-penalty latency (4× and 6× respectively); RAID-10 has minimal write penalty.
- **Cache Sizing:** A larger controller cache reduces effective $\rho$ at the back-end disks, slashing response time.
- **Auto-Tiering Algorithms:** Use historical IOPS-per-GB heat maps to migrate data dynamically.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Average Rotational Latency from First Principles

A disk rotates at a constant angular velocity. We want the **expected waiting time** for a sector to arrive under the read head, assuming a uniformly random arrival of the I/O request.

**Step 1 — Time for one full revolution:**
If the disk makes $N$ revolutions per minute, then the time per revolution is:

$$T_{\text{rev}} = \frac{60 \text{ seconds}}{N} \text{ (in seconds)}$$

**Step 2 — Uniform distribution assumption:**
Because I/O requests arrive at uniformly random times relative to the angular position of the platter, the waiting time $T_w$ is uniformly distributed over the interval $\big[0, T_{\text{rev}}\big]$.

**Step 3 — Expected value of a uniform distribution:**

$$T_{rot,\text{avg}} = E[T_w] = \frac{0 + T_{\text{rev}}}{2} = \frac{T_{\text{rev}}}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $T_{\text{rev}}$:**

$$T_{rot,\text{avg}} = \frac{1}{2} \times \frac{60}{N} = \frac{30}{N} \text{ seconds}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to milliseconds** (multiply by 1000):

$$T_{rot,\text{avg}} \text{ (ms)} = \frac{30{,}000}{N}$$

**Numerical Example (KTU-style):** For a 10,000 RPM disk:

$$T_{rot,\text{avg}} = \frac{30{,}000}{10{,}000} = 3.0 \text{ ms}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: M/M/1 Response Time from the Birth-Death Process

We model the storage controller as a single-server queue in *statistical equilibrium*. Let $P_n$ be the steady-state probability that there are exactly $n$ requests in the system.

**Step 1 — Steady-state balance equation:**

$$\lambda \cdot P_n = \mu \cdot P_{n+1}, \quad n = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$$

**Step 2 — Recursive solution:**

$$P_{n+1} = \rho \cdot P_n \quad \Rightarrow \quad P_n = \rho^n \cdot P_0$$

**Step 3 — Normalization constraint** (sum of all probabilities = 1):

$$\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} P_n = P_0 \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \rho^n = 1$$

**Step 4 — Apply geometric-series identity** $\sum \rho^n = \dfrac{1}{1-\rho}$:

$$P_0 \cdot \frac{1}{1-\rho} = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad P_0 = 1 - \rho$$

**Step 5 — Therefore:**

$$P_n = (1 - \rho) \cdot \rho^n$$

**Step 6 — Expected number in system $L$** (using the geometric-series identity once more):

$$L = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} n \cdot P_n = (1-\rho) \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} n \rho^n = \frac{\rho}{1-\rho}$$

**Step 7 — Apply Little's Law** ($R = L / \lambda$) and substitute $\lambda = \rho \mu$:

$$R = \frac{L}{\lambda} = \frac{\rho}{(1-\rho) \rho \mu} = \frac{1}{\mu(1-\rho)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **canonical KTU board-exam derivation**. The full marks (7/7) require the steady-state balance equation to be explicitly written down. Skipping Step 1 is the #1 reason students lose marks.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Effective Service Time in an Open-Loop Storage System

When an M/M/1 queue is *open-loop* (requests arrive externally, not from a fixed population), the effective time to clear a request is amplified by queuing. Derive the formula:

**Step 1 — Start with the M/M/1 response time:**

$$R = \frac{1}{\mu - \lambda} = \frac{1}{\mu(1-\rho)}$$

**Step 2 — Use the relation** $\rho = \lambda / \mu$, so $\mu(1-\rho) = \mu - \lambda$:

$$R = \frac{1}{\mu(1-\rho)}$$

**Step 3 — Express in terms of bare service time $T_s = 1/\mu$:**

$$R = \frac{T_s}{1 - \rho} = T_s \cdot \frac{1}{1-\rho}$$

**Step 4 — Expand geometrically** (for small $\rho$, useful for back-of-envelope estimates):

$$R = T_s \cdot (1 + \rho + \rho^2 + \rho^3 + \cdots)$$

**Step 5 — The Mean Queuing Delay $W_q$** alone is therefore:

$$W_q = R - T_s = T_s \cdot \frac{\rho}{1-\rho}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Numerical Problem Set

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example for KTU Practice (14-Mark Style):**
>
> *A 15,000 RPM enterprise disk has an average seek time of 4 ms and a sustained transfer rate of 200 MB/s. The storage controller receives a Poisson arrival of I/O requests at $\lambda$ = 80 req/s, and the mean service time per request at the disk is 8 ms. Compute: (a) The utilization, (b) The M/M/1 average response time, (c) The average time spent in the queue, (d) The mean number of I/Os in the system.*

**Solution Step-by-Step:**

Given: $\mu = 1 / 0.008 = 125$ req/s, $\lambda = 80$ req/s.

**(a) Utilization $\rho$:**

$$\rho = \frac{\lambda}{\mu} = \frac{80}{125} = 0.64 \;(64\%)$$

**(b) M/M/1 Response Time $R$:**

$$R = \frac{1}{\mu - \lambda} = \frac{1}{125 - 80} = \frac{1}{45} \approx 0.02222 \text{ s} = 22.22 \text{ ms}$$

**(c) Mean Queueing Delay $W_q$:**

$$W_q = R - T_s = 22.22 - 8 = 14.22 \text{ ms}$$

**(d) Mean Number in System $L$** (Little's Law):

$$L = \lambda \times R = 80 \times 0.02222 \approx 1.78 \text{ requests}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Forgetting to convert $T_s$ from **ms to seconds** (or vice-versa) before applying $\mu = 1/T_s$. This single unit error can cost 2 to 3 marks in KTU valuation.

## 3.5 Comparative Performance Matrix: HDD vs SSD

| Parameter | Enterprise HDD (15K RPM) | SATA SSD | NVMe SSD |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Avg Seek Latency** | ≈ 4.0 ms | ≈ 0.06 ms (controller) | ≈ 0.02 ms |
| **Rotational Latency** | ≈ 2.0 ms | 0 ms (no rotation) | 0 ms |
| **Typical 4KB Read Latency** | 5–7 ms | 0.1 – 0.3 ms | 0.05 – 0.1 ms |
| **Random Read IOPS** | 175 – 220 | 50,000 – 90,000 | 200,000 – 1,000,000+ |
| **Sequential Throughput** | 200 – 250 MB/s | 500 – 550 MB/s | 3,000 – 7,000 MB/s |
| **Failure Mechanism** | Mechanical wear | Write-amplification, NAND wear | NAND wear, controller firmware |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 I/O Request Lifecycle Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Host Application Issues I/O Request] --> B[OS Kernel and File System Layer]
    B --> C[Storage Driver and HBA Queue]
    C --> D[Storage Fabric Transit SAN or NAS]
    D --> E[Storage Controller Front End Port]
    E --> F[Controller Cache and Scheduling Queue]
    F --> G[Back End Disk Service Time]
    G --> G1[Seek Time T_seek]
    G --> G2[Rotational Latency T_rot]
    G --> G3[Transfer Time T_transfer]
    G1 --> H[Data Returned to Controller]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    H --> I[Controller Forwards Response via Fabric]
    I --> J[OS Delivers Completion Interrupt to Application]
    J --> K[Total Response Time R is Logged]
```

## 4.2 M/M/1 Queueing System Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN[Arrival Process lambda requests per sec Poisson] --> Q[Infinite FIFO Queue Wq]
    Q --> S[Single Server Service Rate mu]
    S --> OUT[Departure Process Exit]
    Q -.->|Monitors utilization rho lambda over mu| M[Performance Metrics]
    S -.->|Service time 1 over mu| M
    M --> R1[Response Time R = 1 over mu times 1 minus rho]
    M --> R2[Avg Queue Length Lq = rho squared over 1 minus rho]
    M --> R3[Avg in System L = rho over 1 minus rho]
```

## 4.3 Storage Performance Optimization Strategy Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[High Latency Detected] --> Q{Workload Type}
    Q -->|Random Read Heavy| R1[Deploy NVMe SSD Tier]
    Q -->|Sequential Write Heavy| R2[Increase Spindle Count or Use SSD Cache]
    Q -->|Mixed Random and Sequential| R3[Hybrid Tier with Auto Tiering]
    R1 --> S1[Measure Improvement: 4KB Latency 5 ms to 0.1 ms]
    R2 --> S2[Measure Improvement: MB per s scales linearly with disks]
    R3 --> S3[Hot Data Migrates to SSD; Cold Data Stays on HDD]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between *Latency* and *Response Time* in a storage system. Mention the standard units of measurement.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Latency | Response Time |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Scope** | Internal to the storage device (disk or SSD) | End-to-end, from host issue to completion |
| **Components Included** | Seek, rotation, transfer, NAND access | Service time + Queueing delay + Fabric transit + Host overhead |
| **Influence of Load** | Independent of system load (intrinsic property) | Increases hyperbolically with load ($\rho \to 1$) |
| **Units** | **milliseconds (ms)** for HDD, **microseconds (µs)** for SSD | **milliseconds (ms)** typically |

Latency is always $\le$ Response Time. *(Award: 1 mark for definition each, 1 mark for unit and table.)*

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State Little's Law. Mention two real-world scenarios in storage systems where it is applied.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Little's Law states that for a stable queuing system in equilibrium, the **average number of items in the system ($L$)** is equal to the **average arrival rate ($\lambda$)** multiplied by the **average time spent in the system ($W$)**:
$$L = \lambda \times W$$

**Storage Applications:**
1. **SAN Performance Tuning:** Estimating the average number of in-flight I/Os from a database given a target response time of 5 ms and 10,000 IOPS: $L = 10{,}000 \times 0.005 = 50$ concurrent I/Os.
2. **Capacity Planning:** Sizing the controller queue depth (NCQ/TCQ) to sustain desired IOPS.

*(Award: 1 mark for formula, 1 mark each for two valid applications.)*

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** When solving 14-mark questions, students frequently **omit the steady-state balance equation** in M/M/1 derivations and **forget unit conversions** (ms vs s). Always explicitly write: *"Given: $\mu$ = 1/$T_s$ = ___ req/s"* at the start. Skipping this costs 2 marks routinely.

---

### **Question B-A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) With a neat diagram, explain the M/M/1 queuing model as applied to a storage controller. Derive the expression for the average response time.** *(7 marks)*

**(b) A 7,200 RPM disk has an average seek time of 8 ms and a transfer rate of 150 MB/s. The disk receives random read requests of size 16 KB at the rate of 90 IOPS. Calculate: (i) Average rotational latency, (ii) Transfer time per request, (iii) Total service time per I/O, (iv) Utilization and M/M/1 response time.** *(7 marks)*

#### **Model Solution for B-A (a):**

Refer to the M/M/1 diagram in Section 4.2. Key derivation steps (carrying 7 marks):

1. **State assumptions** (1 mark): Poisson arrivals, exponential service, single server, FCFS, infinite population.
2. **Steady-state balance** (2 marks):

$$\lambda P_n = \mu P_{n+1} \;\Rightarrow\; P_{n+1} = \rho P_n$$

3. **Normalization** (1 mark):

$$P_0 = 1 - \rho$$

4. **Expected number in system** (2 marks):

$$L = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} n P_n = \frac{\rho}{1 - \rho}$$

5. **Little's Law application** (1 mark):

$$R = \frac{L}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{\mu(1-\rho)}$$

#### **Model Solution for B-A (b):**

Given: $N = 7200$ RPM, $T_{seek} = 8$ ms, $R_d = 150$ MB/s, Request = 16 KB, $\lambda = 90$ IOPS.

**(i) Average Rotational Latency** (1 mark):

$$T_{rot} = \frac{30{,}000}{7200} = 4.167 \text{ ms}$$

**(ii) Transfer Time** (1 mark):

$$T_{tr} = \frac{16 \text{ KB}}{150 \text{ MB/s}} = \frac{16 \times 1024}{150 \times 10^6} \approx 0.109 \text{ ms}$$

**(iii) Total Service Time** (1 mark):

$$T_s = 8 + 4.167 + 0.109 = 12.276 \text{ ms} \approx 12.28 \text{ ms}$$

**(iv) Utilization and M/M/1 Response Time** (4 marks):

$$\mu = \frac{1}{0.012276} \approx 81.46 \text{ req/s}$$
$$\rho = \frac{\lambda}{\mu} = \frac{90}{81.46} = 1.105$$

> [!WARNING]
> **System Instability Alert:** Since $\rho > 1$, the queue is **unstable** and grows without bound. In a real-world scenario, this means the disk is oversubscribed. Response time becomes effectively infinite. The examiner will deduct marks if the student blindly plugs into $R = 1/(\mu-\lambda)$ without checking stability first.

---

### **Question B-B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) Explain the physical components of disk service time with a labeled block diagram. Compare the latency characteristics of HDD, SATA SSD, and NVMe SSD technologies.** *(7 marks)*

**(b) A storage array has 8 HDDs configured in a RAID-10 group. Each disk can deliver 180 random read IOPS. The controller's front-end queue has an arrival rate of 1,200 IOPS. Assuming M/M/1 behavior, calculate the effective service time, utilization, and average response time seen by the host. State one mitigation strategy if the response time exceeds the SLA of 20 ms.** *(7 marks)*

#### **Model Solution for B-B (a):**

**Disk Service Time Components** (4 marks): The total service time $T_s = T_{seek} + T_{rot} + T_{transfer}$. Refer to the lifecycle diagram in Section 4.1.

- **$T_{seek}$:** Mechanical head movement, ≈ 3–10 ms.
- **$T_{rot}$:** Wait for sector, $\approx 30{,}000 / N$ ms.
- **$T_{transfer}$:** Data movement, $B / R_d$.

**Comparison Table** (3 marks): Present the HDD vs SATA SSD vs NVMe SSD matrix from Section 3.5.

#### **Model Solution for B-B (b):**

**Step 1 — Aggregate service rate** (1 mark): RAID-10 of 8 disks, 180 IOPS per disk:
$$\mu_{agg} = 8 \times 180 = 1{,}440 \text{ IOPS}$$

**Step 2 — Effective service time** (1 mark):

$$T_s = \frac{1}{1440} = 0.000694 \text{ s} = 0.694 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 3 — Utilization** (1 mark):

$$\rho = \frac{1200}{1440} = 0.833 \;(83.3\%)$$

**Step 4 — Response Time** (2 marks):

$$R = \frac{1}{\mu(1-\rho)} = \frac{1}{1440 \times (1-0.833)} = \frac{1}{240} = 0.004167 \text{ s} = 4.17 \text{ ms}$$

Since 4.17 ms < 20 ms SLA, the system is healthy.

**Step 5 — Mitigation if SLA violated** (2 marks): Add an **SSD read-cache tier** to absorb hot-spot read IOPS, thereby reducing the back-end HDD utilization. Alternatively, increase the RAID-10 group size to 12 disks (12 × 180 = 2,160 IOPS) to drop $\rho$ to 0.555 and lower $R$ to ≈ 1.26 ms.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for the KTU 2024 Board Exam (Module 4):**
>
> - [x] **Latency** is *device-internal*; **Response Time** is *system-level* and *load-dependent*.
> - [x] Disk service time $T_s = T_{seek} + T_{rot} + T_{transfer}$.
> - [x] Average rotational latency = $30{,}000 / N$ ms.
> - [x] For 7,200 RPM → 4.17 ms; 10,000 RPM → 3.0 ms; 15,000 RPM → 2.0 ms.
> - [x] M/M/1 is valid **only if** $\rho < 1$. If $\rho \ge 1$, the queue is unstable (infinite response time).
> - [x] M/M/1 response time: $R = \dfrac{1}{\mu(1-\rho)} = \dfrac{T_s}{1-\rho}$.
> - [x] M/M/1 mean queue length: $L_q = \dfrac{\rho^2}{1-\rho}$.
> - [x] Little's Law: $L = \lambda W$ — universally valid for any stable system.
> - [x] **Unit hygiene:** Always convert $T_s$ to seconds before computing $\mu = 1/T_s$.
> - [x] **IOPS** = Throughput (MB/s) / Request Size (MB).
> - [x] SSD has **zero rotational latency** and dramatically lower seek latency.
> - [x] **High $\rho$ is the silent killer** — at $\rho = 0.9$, $R = 10 \times T_s$ (response time is 10× the bare service time).
> - [x] RAID-5 write-penalty is **4×**; RAID-6 write-penalty is **6×**; RAID-10 write-penalty is **2×**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: IOPS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST867 - STORAGE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** IOPS

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of IOPS

**IOPS** stands for **Input/Output Operations Per Second**. It is the standard industry-defined performance benchmark metric used to quantify the operational throughput capability of a computer storage device (such as a Hard Disk Drive, Solid State Drive, SAN, or storage array). Formally, IOPS represents the maximum number of distinct read or write operations that a storage subsystem can complete within a strict one-second time window, measured under a specified workload condition.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PECST867 - Storage Systems)**, IOPS is categorized under the broader umbrella of **Storage Performance Metrics**, alongside throughput (MB/s), latency (ms), and queue depth. Each operation counted in an IOPS measurement is typically a **block-level I/O** request — either a 4 KiB, 8 KiB, 16 KiB, or 64 KiB transfer, depending on the protocol being benchmarked (SCSI, NVMe, SATA, SAS).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Definition (KTU Syllabus Highlight):**
> *IOPS is a hardware-independent, workload-dependent performance metric that quantifies the number of discrete read/write service completions a storage device can deliver per second under a defined access pattern (random or sequential), block size, and read/write ratio.*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a **bus ticket counter at a busy Kerala KSRTC station during Onam rush**. The counter clerk (your storage device) processes passengers (I/O requests) one by one. 

- If the clerk takes **15 seconds per ticket**, they serve **4 passengers per minute** → roughly **240 passengers in an hour**.
- If you replace the slow clerk with a faster automated machine that takes only **2 seconds per ticket**, throughput jumps to **30 passengers per minute** → roughly **1800 in an hour**.

Here, the passengers-per-minute figure is exactly analogous to **IOPS**. The time per passenger (2 s, 15 s) is analogous to **I/O Latency**. The total number of tickets issued per hour is analogous to **Throughput (MB/s)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Three Pillars of Storage Performance** (must be memorized):
> 1. **IOPS** → *How many operations?* (Quantity of work)
> 2. **Latency** → *How fast is each operation?* (Speed of response)
> 3. **Throughput** → *How much data moved?* (Volume of data = IOPS × Block Size)

## 1.3 Standard IOPS Reference Values (Industry Benchmarks)

The following table summarizes **typical advertised IOPS ranges** for storage classes covered in the KTU PECST867 syllabus:

| Storage Class | Typical IOPS Range | Typical Latency |
|---|---|---|
| **7,200 RPM HDD** | **75 – 150 IOPS** | **8 – 12 ms** |
| **10,000 RPM HDD** | **150 – 200 IOPS** | **4 – 6 ms** |
| **15,000 RPM HDD** | **175 – 220 IOPS** | **3 – 4 ms** |
| **SATA SSD** | **50,000 – 100,000 IOPS** | **0.05 – 0.1 ms** |
| **NVMe SSD** | **200,000 – 1,000,000+ IOPS** | **0.02 – 0.05 ms** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bolded Standard Metric to Remember:**
> **Random 4 KiB Read IOPS** is the *de facto* universal benchmark unit used by storage vendors (Samsung, WD, Seagate, NetApp, EMC) when advertising performance. KTU examiners frequently test this unit.

## 1.4 Why IOPS Matters in Storage Engineering

IOPS is the **single most critical metric** for:
- **Online Transaction Processing (OLTP)** workloads (banking, railway reservation, e-commerce) where millions of small random reads/writes occur.
- **Database performance tuning** (MySQL InnoDB, Oracle, PostgreSQL) — every SQL query triggers multiple block I/Os.
- **Virtualization hosts (VMware ESXi, KVM, Hyper-V)** running many VMs, each generating independent random I/O.
- **RAID array sizing** — total usable IOPS = (Single Disk IOPS × Number of Disks) × RAID Penalty Factor.

> [!NOTE]
> **Workload Caveat:** A 10,000 RPM enterprise HDD may advertise 180 IOPS for *sequential reads* but only 130 IOPS for *random 4 KiB reads*. Always specify the workload when stating IOPS — this is a common KTU exam trap question.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Foundational Physics of IOPS

To derive IOPS rigorously, we must analyze the **I/O request lifecycle** within a storage stack. Every I/O operation traverses these sequential stages:

1. **Application issues system call** (e.g., `read()` / `write()` syscall).
2. **OS Kernel & File System Layer** translates the logical block address (LBA) to a physical address.
3. **Storage Driver / HBA / Controller** queues the request into the device's command queue.
4. **Storage Media executes** the physical seek (for HDD) or NAND access (for SSD).
5. **Data transfer** occurs over the interconnect (SATA / SAS / NVMe / FC).
6. **Completion interrupt** is sent back to the host OS.

The total elapsed time for steps 1–6 is the **end-to-end I/O latency** $L$. Since one operation completes per latency cycle, the maximum theoretical IOPS is the reciprocal of latency.

## 2.2 The Master IOPS Equation

The fundamental inverse relationship between IOPS and latency is:

$$
\text{IOPS} = \frac{1}{L_{sec}} = \frac{1000}{L_{ms}}
$$

Where:
- $L_{sec}$ = average service time per I/O, measured in **seconds**
- $L_{ms}$ = average service time per I/O, measured in **milliseconds**

For an HDD, the service time $L$ is the sum of three mechanical components:

$$
L_{HDD} = T_{seek} + T_{rotational\;latency} + T_{transfer}
$$

- $T_{seek}$ = average seek time (arm movement), typically **3 – 10 ms**
- $T_{rotational\;latency}$ = half of one full rotation, = $\frac{60}{2 \times RPM}$ seconds
- $T_{transfer}$ = time to read/write the block, usually negligible compared to mechanical delays

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For a 15,000 RPM disk:**
> $T_{rotational\;latency} = \frac{60}{2 \times 15000} = 0.002 \; s = 2 \; ms$
> **For a 7,200 RPM disk:**
> $T_{rotational\;latency} = \frac{60}{2 \times 7200} \approx 4.17 \; ms$

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{IOPS} = \dfrac{1}{L_{sec}}$ | Fundamental IOPS-Latency reciprocity | ops/sec |
| 2 | $\text{IOPS} = \dfrac{1000}{L_{ms}}$ | Same formula, latency in ms | ops/sec |
| 3 | $T_{rot} = \dfrac{30}{RPM}$ (seconds) | Rotational half-latency | seconds |
| 4 | $\text{Throughput} = \text{IOPS} \times \text{Block Size}$ | Bandwidth from IOPS | Bytes/sec |
| 5 | $\text{RAID IOPS} = \dfrac{n \times \text{Single Disk IOPS}}{R_{penalty}}$ | Effective RAID IOPS | ops/sec |
| 6 | $\text{IOPS}_{read\;mix} = r \times \text{IOPS}_r + (1-r) \times \text{IOPS}_w$ | Mixed workload IOPS | ops/sec |
| 7 | $L_{effective} = L_{disk} + L_{queue} + L_{controller}$ | Total system latency | ms |

**RAID Penalty Table** (Critical KTU memory item):

| RAID Level | Read Penalty | Write Penalty | Net Penalty (Mixed R/W) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **RAID 0** | **1** | **1** | **1** |
| **RAID 1** | **1** | **2** | **2** (per mirror write) |
| **RAID 5** | **1** | **4** | **4** (read-modify-write) |
| **RAID 6** | **1** | **6** | **6** (dual parity) |
| **RAID 10** | **1** | **2** | **2** (mirror only) |

> [!NOTE]
> **RAID Penalty Explained:** Every RAID level above 0 imposes additional I/O operations (parity reads, parity writes, mirror writes) to maintain data redundancy. This reduces the effective IOPS available to the host. RAID 5 requires **4 I/Os per logical write** (2 reads + 2 writes), hence penalty = 4.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

IOPS calculations are deployed in:

- **Storage Capacity Planning:** Determining how many disks are required to meet an SLA-defined IOPS target.
- **Cloud Sizing (AWS, Azure, GCP):** Provisioning EBS volumes — gp3 volumes guarantee 3,000 IOPS baseline, scalable to 16,000 IOPS.
- **Database Index Optimization:** Each missing index causes extra random I/O; KTU case studies often use this as a design driver.
- **QoS (Quality of Service) Policies:** Storage admins enforce per-LUN IOPS limits using tools like VMware SIOC, NetApp QoS, or Windows Storage QoS.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Maximum IOPS from Mechanical Disk Specifications

**Problem Statement (Model KTU Question Style):**
A server uses a **10,000 RPM enterprise HDD** with an average seek time of **4 ms** and a block transfer time of **0.5 ms** for a **4 KiB** random read. Calculate the **maximum theoretical IOPS**.

**Step 1 — Identify the I/O service time components.**

For a random I/O, the total service time per operation is:
$$
L = T_{seek} + T_{rotational\;latency} + T_{transfer}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the rotational latency.**

Rotational latency is half the time for one complete disk revolution. For 10,000 RPM:
$$
T_{rot} = \frac{60 \;\text{seconds}}{2 \times 10,000 \;\text{revolutions}} = \frac{60}{20,000} = 0.003 \;\text{s} = 3 \;\text{ms}
$$

This represents the average wait time for the desired sector to rotate under the read/write head.

**Step 3 — Sum the components to obtain total service time.**

$$
L = 4 \;\text{ms} + 3 \;\text{ms} + 0.5 \;\text{ms} = 7.5 \;\text{ms}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the inverse relationship to obtain IOPS.**

$$
\text{IOPS}_{max} = \frac{1000}{L_{ms}} = \frac{1000}{7.5} \approx 133.33 \;\text{IOPS}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** A single 10K RPM HDD delivers only ~133 random 4 KiB read IOPS. To meet a workload demand of 10,000 IOPS, you would need roughly **75 such disks in RAID 0** — this is why enterprises use SSDs or large disk farms.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: RAID 5 Array IOPS Capacity Calculation

**Problem Statement:**
A RAID 5 array comprises **6 enterprise 15K RPM disks**. Each disk delivers **180 random read IOPS** and **150 random write IOPS**. The expected workload is **70% reads, 30% writes**. Compute the effective IOPS of the array.

**Step 1 — Identify the RAID 5 penalty.**

From the cheat sheet: RAID 5 Read Penalty = 1, Write Penalty = 4.

**Step 2 — Compute the read IOPS contribution.**

Reads incur no extra penalty. Read IOPS available = 6 disks × 180 IOPS × 1 (penalty).
$$
\text{IOPS}_{reads} = 6 \times 180 \times 1 = 1080 \;\text{IOPS}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the write IOPS contribution.**

Writes require 4 physical I/Os (read old data, read old parity, write new data, write new parity). Effective write IOPS per disk = $150 / 4 = 37.5$ IOPS.
$$
\text{IOPS}_{writes} = 6 \times \frac{150}{4} = 6 \times 37.5 = 225 \;\text{IOPS}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute weighted effective IOPS using workload mix.**

$$
\text{IOPS}_{effective} = (r \times \text{IOPS}_r) + ((1-r) \times \text{IOPS}_w)
$$

Where $r = 0.70$ for reads. Substituting:
$$
\text{IOPS}_{effective} = (0.70 \times 1080) + (0.30 \times 225)
$$

$$
\text{IOPS}_{effective} = 756 + 67.5 = 823.5 \;\text{IOPS}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Insight:** A naive multiplication of (6 × 180) = 1080 IOPS would **overestimate performance by ~31%**. The RAID write penalty is the reason enterprise storage for write-heavy workloads prefers RAID 10 over RAID 5/6.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Required Disk Count for IOPS SLA

**Problem Statement:**
A database application requires **5,000 IOPS** sustained, with an **80% read, 20% write** workload. Available disks: 15K RPM HDDs at **200 IOPS read, 175 IOPS write each**. Storage must be RAID 10. How many disks minimum?

**Step 1 — RAID 10 penalty is 1 for reads, 2 for writes.**

Each disk contributes:
- Read IOPS per disk = 200 × 1 = 200
- Write IOPS per disk = 175 / 2 = 87.5

**Step 2 — Effective IOPS per disk under 80/20 workload mix.**

$$
\text{IOPS}_{per\;disk} = (0.80 \times 200) + (0.20 \times 87.5)
$$

$$
\text{IOPS}_{per\;disk} = 160 + 17.5 = 177.5 \;\text{IOPS}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute required disk count, rounding up.**

$$
n = \left\lceil \frac{5000}{177.5} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 28.17 \right\rceil = 29 \;\text{disks}
$$

**Step 4 — Account for RAID 10 mirroring overhead.**

RAID 10 mirrors data, so usable capacity comes from half the disks (rounded down). For 29 disks in RAID 10, you need an even number → **30 disks** (15 mirrored pairs).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Trap:** Students often forget to round UP the disk count and round DOWN to nearest even number. Always do the calculation **first**, then apply the RAID 10 even-number constraint.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparative IOPS Behavior

| Parameter | HDD | SATA SSD | NVMe SSD |
|---|---|---|---|
| Random 4K Read IOPS | **~150** | **~90,000** | **~500,000** |
| Sequential Read Throughput | **150 MB/s** | **550 MB/s** | **7,000 MB/s** |
| Random Write IOPS | **~140** | **~30,000** | **~200,000** |
| Dominant Latency Component | **Seek + Rotation** | **NAND cell program time** | **PCIe bus + controller** |
| Queue Depth for Peak IOPS | **1 – 4** | **32** | **256 – 1024** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 IOPS Calculation Flow Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the architectural flow of how an I/O request propagates through the storage stack, identifying each latency contributor that cumulatively determines final IOPS.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Host Application Issues Read Write Syscall] --> B[OS Kernel and File System Layer]
    B --> C[Storage Driver HBA Controller]
    C --> D[Command Queue and Scheduler]
    D --> E{Storage Media Type}
    E --> F1[HDD Seek plus Rotation plus Transfer]
    E --> F2[SATA SSD NAND Controller]
    E --> F3[NVMe SSD PCIe Parallel Engine]
    F1 --> G[Data Transfer over Interconnect]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    G --> H[Completion Interrupt to Host]
    H --> I[IOPS Computation 1 divided by Latency]
```

## 4.2 RAID IOPS Penalty Architecture

The following diagram illustrates how a single logical write operation in RAID 5 expands into multiple physical I/Os, reducing the effective IOPS available to the application.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    App[Application Issues One Logical Write] --> R1[Step1 Read Old Data Block]
    App --> R2[Step2 Read Old Parity Block]
    R1 --> C[Compute New Parity in Memory]
    R2 --> C
    C --> W1[Step3 Write New Data Block]
    C --> W2[Step4 Write New Parity Block]
    W1 --> Total[Total Physical IOs equals 4]
    W2 --> Total
```

## 4.3 IOPS Workload Pattern Taxonomy

The following hierarchical block diagram classifies the IOPS workload patterns commonly benchmarked in storage performance evaluation.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Root[IOPS Workload Patterns] --> Seq[Sequential IOPS]
    Root --> Rand[Random IOPS]
    Seq --> SeqR[Sequential Read Large Block]
    Seq --> SeqW[Sequential Write Large Block]
    Rand --> RandR[Random Read 4 KiB Block]
    Rand --> RandW[Random Write 4 KiB Block]
    Rand --> Mix[Mixed Random 70R plus 30W Standard Benchmark]
```

## 4.4 IOPS vs Throughput Relationship Matrix

The following block-level flow explains how IOPS and Throughput relate through the Block Size parameter, illustrating the engineering trade-off between small random I/O (high IOPS, low throughput) and large sequential I/O (low IOPS, high throughput).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[IOPS Operations per Second] --> M[Multiply by Block Size Bytes]
    M --> B[Throughput Bytes per Second]
    A --> N1[Random 4 KiB workload yields low throughput high IOPS]
    A --> N2[Sequential 1 MiB workload yields high throughput low IOPS]
    N1 --> Insight[Insight Optimize for IOPS in OLTP and Database]
    N2 --> Insight2[Insight Optimize for Throughput in Backup and Video]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: Define IOPS. Differentiate between Random IOPS and Sequential IOPS.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1.5 Marks):** IOPS (Input/Output Operations Per Second) is a standard performance metric that measures the number of read/write operations a storage device can complete in one second. It is computed as the reciprocal of average I/O service time: $\text{IOPS} = 1000 / L_{ms}$.

**Difference (1.5 Marks):**
- **Random IOPS** refers to I/O operations where the requested blocks are scattered across non-contiguous disk locations, requiring frequent seek and rotational repositioning. It is the realistic workload for OLTP databases. A typical 15K RPM HDD delivers **175 – 220 random IOPS**.
- **Sequential IOPS** refers to I/O operations where blocks are accessed in contiguous order, allowing the disk head to remain stationary or perform minimal seeks. Sequential throughput is dominated by platter rotation speed and is much higher, often **hundreds of MB/s**.

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: List any three factors that affect IOPS performance in a storage system.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — 1 mark each):**

1. **Storage Media Type:** SSDs deliver 100× to 1000× more IOPS than HDDs due to absence of mechanical seek delays.
2. **RAID Level / Write Penalty:** Higher RAID levels (RAID 5, RAID 6) incur additional read-modify-write operations, reducing effective IOPS.
3. **Block Size & Queue Depth:** Larger block sizes reduce IOPS but increase throughput; deeper queues allow command parallelism, boosting IOPS on SSDs.
4. *(Optional 4th point)* **Interface Protocol:** NVMe over PCIe delivers far higher IOPS than legacy SATA due to parallelism.
5. *(Optional 5th point)* **Workload Mix:** Read-heavy workloads sustain higher IOPS than write-heavy ones on parity-based RAID.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` — 14 Marks

**A RAID 6 storage array consists of 8 enterprise SSDs. Each SSD provides 50,000 random read IOPS and 25,000 random write IOPS under a 4 KiB block size. The expected workload mix is 60% reads and 40% writes.**

**(a) Calculate the effective IOPS the array can deliver. Apply RAID 6 write penalty. [7 Marks]**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Recall RAID 6 Penalty Factors.**

RAID 6 maintains dual parity, requiring **2 reads + 3 writes = 6 physical I/Os** per logical write. Therefore:
- Read Penalty = 1
- Write Penalty = 6

**Step 2 — Compute total raw read IOPS available.**

$$
\text{IOPS}_{read} = 8 \times 50,000 \times 1 = 400,000 \;\text{IOPS}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute total raw write IOPS available.**

$$
\text{IOPS}_{write} = 8 \times \frac{25,000}{6} = 8 \times 4166.67 \approx 33,333.33 \;\text{IOPS}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply workload mix weighting.**

$$
\text{IOPS}_{effective} = (0.60 \times 400,000) + (0.40 \times 33,333.33)
$$

$$
\text{IOPS}_{effective} = 240,000 + 13,333.33 = 253,333.33 \;\text{IOPS}
$$

**Final Answer:** The RAID 6 array delivers approximately **253,333 IOPS** under the 60/40 workload.

**(b) A database application requires 400,000 sustained IOPS with 80% reads and 20% writes. Recommend whether the existing RAID 6 array is sufficient or what additional disks are needed. Justify with calculations. [7 Marks]**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute per-disk IOPS contribution under the new workload mix (80/20).**

$$
\text{IOPS}_{per\;disk} = (0.80 \times 50,000) + \left(0.20 \times \frac{25,000}{6}\right)
$$

$$
\text{IOPS}_{per\;disk} = 40,000 + \left(0.20 \times 4166.67\right) = 40,000 + 833.33 = 40,833.33 \;\text{IOPS}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute required number of disks.**

$$
n = \left\lceil \frac{400,000}{40,833.33} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 9.80 \right\rceil = 10 \;\text{disks}
$$

**Step 3 — Conclusion and Recommendation.**

The existing 8-disk RAID 6 array can deliver only **$8 \times 40,833.33 = 326,666$ IOPS** under the new workload — insufficient. We need at least **2 additional SSDs**, increasing the array to **10 disks**, delivering **408,333 IOPS**, which exceeds the 400,000 IOPS SLA.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Students forget to divide write IOPS by the RAID 6 penalty of 6. They write $25,000 \times 8 = 200,000$ and lose **2 marks**.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Students multiply before applying the workload mix. Always weight reads and writes *separately*, then sum.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Students use the wrong RAID 6 penalty. RAID 5 penalty = 4, RAID 6 penalty = 6. Confusing these loses **3 marks**.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Not rounding UP the disk count. KTU strictly penalizes under-provisioning in design questions.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` — 14 Marks (ALTERNATIVE)

**(a) Explain the relationship between IOPS, Latency, and Throughput. Derive the formula connecting them. [7 Marks]**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Define IOPS (1 Mark).**

IOPS = number of read/write operations completed per second. Inversely related to latency.

**Step 2 — Define Latency (1 Mark).**

Latency $L$ = time taken to complete a single I/O operation, measured in milliseconds (ms).

**Step 3 — Define Throughput (1 Mark).**

Throughput $T$ = amount of data transferred per second, measured in MB/s or GB/s.

**Step 4 — Derive the IOPS-Latency Relationship (2 Marks).**

If each operation takes $L$ seconds to complete, then in one second, the number of operations completed is:
$$
\text{IOPS} = \frac{1}{L_{sec}} = \frac{1000}{L_{ms}}
$$

**Step 5 — Derive the IOPS-Throughput Relationship (2 Marks).**

If each I/O operation transfers a block of size $B$ bytes, then total data per second:
$$
T = \text{IOPS} \times B \quad \text{(Bytes/sec)}
$$

$$
\text{IOPS} = \frac{T}{B}
$$

**Step 6 — Combined Master Equation.**

$$
T = \frac{B \times 1000}{L_{ms}} \quad \text{or} \quad L_{ms} = \frac{B}{T}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Trade-off:** Doubling block size doubles throughput but halves IOPS. Tuning involves matching block size to application access pattern.

**(b) A storage device has 2 ms average latency and supports 64 KiB block I/O. Calculate: (i) IOPS, (ii) Throughput in MB/s, (iii) What happens to IOPS if block size is reduced to 16 KiB assuming latency remains 2 ms? [7 Marks]**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**(i) Calculate IOPS at 2 ms latency (2 Marks):**

$$
\text{IOPS} = \frac{1000}{L_{ms}} = \frac{1000}{2} = 500 \;\text{IOPS}
$$

**(ii) Calculate Throughput at 64 KiB block (2.5 Marks):**

$$
T = \text{IOPS} \times B = 500 \times 64 \;\text{KiB/sec}
$$

Converting to MB/s: $64 \;\text{KiB} = 64 \times 1024 \;\text{bytes} = 65,536 \;\text{bytes}$

$$
T = 500 \times 65,536 = 32,768,000 \;\text{bytes/sec} \approx 31.25 \;\text{MB/s}
$$

**(iii) Calculate new IOPS at 16 KiB block (2.5 Marks):**

Since latency is unchanged (2 ms), IOPS remains 500. The block size does NOT affect IOPS if latency is held constant.
$$
\text{IOPS}_{new} = \frac{1000}{2} = 500 \;\text{IOPS}
$$

However, the new throughput becomes:
$$
T_{new} = 500 \times 16 \;\text{KiB} = 500 \times 16,384 = 8,192,000 \;\text{bytes/sec} \approx 7.81 \;\text{MB/s}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often wrongly conclude that reducing block size *increases* IOPS. The truth is: **IOPS depends on latency, not block size**. Block size only changes throughput. This conceptual distinction is a frequent 2-mark trap question.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Summary — IOPS:**

- **Definition:** IOPS = Input/Output Operations Per Second; inverse of average I/O service latency.
- **Master Formula:** $\text{IOPS} = 1000 / L_{ms}$
- **Throughput Equation:** $\text{Throughput} = \text{IOPS} \times \text{Block Size}$
- **HDD Service Time:** $L_{HDD} = T_{seek} + T_{rot} + T_{transfer}$, where $T_{rot} = 30 / \text{RPM}$ seconds.
- **Random vs Sequential:** Random IOPS measures non-contiguous access (lower); Sequential IOPS measures contiguous access (higher).
- **Standard Benchmark Unit:** Random 4 KiB Read IOPS — universally quoted by storage vendors.
- **RAID Penalty Memorization:** RAID 0 = 1, RAID 1 = 2, RAID 5 = 4, RAID 6 = 6, RAID 10 = 2.
- **Effective RAID IOPS Formula:** $\text{IOPS}_{effective} = (r \times n \times \text{IOPS}_r) + ((1-r) \times n \times \text{IOPS}_w / P)$
- **Mixed Workload Formula:** $\text{IOPS}_{total} = (r \times \text{readIOPS}) + ((1-r) \times \text{writeIOPS})$
- **Disk Count Sizing:** Always round UP the calculated disk count, then enforce RAID 10 even-number constraint.
- **Workload Traps:** NVMe SSDs need high queue depth (256+) to saturate IOPS; HDDs saturate at queue depth 1–4.
- **Engineering Rule of Thumb:** OLTP databases → prioritize IOPS; Backup/Streaming → prioritize Throughput.
- **Cloud Equivalent:** AWS gp3 = 3,000 IOPS baseline, up to 16,000 IOPS provisioned; io2 = up to 256,000 IOPS.
- **Common KTU Trap:** Block size changes Throughput but does NOT change IOPS if latency is constant.
- **Industry Benchmark Tools:** fio (Linux), Iometer (Windows), Vdbench — all measure IOPS using the above formulas.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: MBps and Transfer Rate
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST867 - STORAGE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** MBps and Transfer Rate

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of KTU 2024 Scheme Storage Systems (PECST867), **MBps (Megabytes per second)** is the standard unit of measurement for **data transfer rate** (also called throughput or bandwidth) in storage subsystems. It quantifies the volume of data successfully moved between two points—typically between a host system and a storage device, or between memory hierarchies—per unit of time.

**Transfer Rate (R)** is formally defined as:

> **Transfer Rate (R)** is the rate at which digital data is transmitted from one location to another, expressed as the quotient of the total amount of data successfully transferred divided by the time elapsed during the transfer.

The SI-standard unit is **MB/s** (or **MBps**), where:
- **MB** = Megabytes (1,000,000 bytes in SI; 1,048,576 bytes in binary/IEC standard)
- **ps** = per second

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Distinction (High-Yield):** Storage vendors often advertise speeds in **MB/s** (Megabytes per second), while networking equipment is typically quoted in **Mbps** (Megabits per second). One Megabyte = 8 Megabits. This distinction is a frequently tested concept.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **water pipeline** connecting a reservoir (storage) to a city (host/compute). The **transfer rate** is analogous to the **flow rate of water**—the volume of water (data) that exits the pipe per second. A wider, higher-pressure pipe delivers water (data) faster.

- **Data Size** = Total volume of water in the tank.
- **Transfer Rate** = Flow rate (liters/second).
- **Transfer Time** = Time required to empty the tank.

A **SATA SSD** might be a 1-inch garden hose, while an **NVMe Gen4 SSD** is a 4-inch industrial fire hose—both carry water (data), but the volume per second differs drastically.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why It Matters in KTU 2024:** The MBps metric is the foundation for sizing storage performance in **data centers, RAID arrays, SANs, and cloud storage platforms**. Bottlenecks in transfer rate directly impact application latency, backup windows, and SLAs.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Units Table

| Metric | Symbol | Unit | Notes |
|--------|--------|------|-------|
| Data Size | S | MB, GB, TB | SI: 1 GB = 10⁹ bytes; Binary: 1 GiB = 2³⁰ bytes |
| Transfer Rate | R | MB/s (MBps) | Also: MBps, MiB/s |
| Transfer Time | T | seconds (s) | Often converted to ms |
| Bandwidth | B | bits/s or bytes/s | Theoretical maximum |
| Throughput | Th | MB/s | **Effective** real-world rate |
| Latency | L | ms | Time to initiate transfer |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **prefix "Mega"** in storage context is ambiguous. By **SI convention**, 1 MB = **10⁶ bytes** = 1,000,000 bytes. By **IEC binary convention**, 1 MiB = **2²⁰ bytes** = 1,048,576 bytes. KTU expects awareness of both conventions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of Transfer Rate

The transfer rate in a storage system is governed by multiple layered components:

### Step-by-Step Logic Flow:

1. **Physical Layer Constraint:** The bus interface (SATA, SAS, PCIe/NVMe) defines a maximum theoretical bandwidth based on clock frequency and bus width.
2. **Protocol Layer Overhead:** Commands, acknowledgments, encoding (8b/10b, 128b/130b, NRZ, PAM4), and error correction consume a portion of raw bandwidth.
3. **Command Processing Latency:** The storage controller must parse SCSI/NVMe commands, perform lookups, and queue I/O operations.
4. **Media Access Layer:** For HDDs, this includes seek time and rotational latency; for SSDs, this includes NAND access and garbage collection.
5. **Effective Throughput:** The **actual** data rate seen by the application, after subtracting all overheads.

> **The "Why":** Raw bus bandwidth is **never** achievable in practice. A SATA III link rated at 600 MB/s raw delivers only ~560 MB/s of usable payload due to 8b/10b encoding overhead (20% protocol tax).

### 2.1.1 Sequential vs. Random Transfer Rates

- **Sequential Transfer Rate:** Reading/writing large contiguous blocks. Limited by interface bandwidth and sustained media throughput.
- **Random Transfer Rate:** Reading/writing small, scattered blocks. Heavily impacted by seek time (HDD) or IOPS (SSD), typically **orders of magnitude lower** than sequential rates.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas—they appear in nearly every KTU 2024 ESE problem from this module.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $T = \dfrac{S}{R}$ | Basic Transfer Time | seconds |
| 2 | $R = \dfrac{S}{T}$ | Transfer Rate from Size & Time | MB/s |
| 3 | $S = R \times T$ | Data Size from Rate & Time | MB |
| 4 | $1 \text{ MB/s} = 8 \text{ Mbit/s}$ | Byte-to-Bit conversion | — |
| 5 | $R_{\text{bytes}} = \dfrac{R_{\text{bits}}}{8}$ | Bits/s to Bytes/s | — |
| 6 | $R_{\text{eff}} = R_{\text{raw}} \times (1 - \text{overhead\%})$ | Effective throughput after protocol tax | MB/s |
| 7 | $T_{\text{total}} = T_{\text{transfer}} + T_{\text{seek}} + T_{\text{rotational}}$ | HDD total access time | ms |
| 8 | $R_{\text{seq}} = \dfrac{\text{Track size}}{\text{Rotation time}}$ | HDD sequential rate (theoretical) | MB/s |
| 9 | $\text{Throughput (MB/s)} = \dfrac{\text{IOPS} \times \text{Block Size (KB)}}{1024}$ | IOPS to Throughput conversion | MB/s |
| 10 | $\text{Bus Bandwidth} = \text{Clock} \times \text{Width} \times \frac{\text{Transfers}}{\text{cycle}}$ | Theoretical bus bandwidth | bits/s |

> **Engineering Utility:** These equations are foundational for **storage capacity planning, backup window estimation, RAID rebuild time prediction, and SAN performance tuning** in production environments.

## 2.3 Worked Numerical Foundation

**Example Scenario (Setup for Section 3):**
A 4 TB database must be backed up to a NAS over a link. The link provides a raw bandwidth of 1 Gbps, and protocol overhead consumes 12% of the bandwidth. The NAS HDD has a sustained write rate of 180 MB/s. Determine the **bottleneck** and **total backup time**.

This will be solved exhaustively in Section 3.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation 1: Link Rate Conversion (Bits → Bytes)

**Given:** A network/storage link operates at 800 Mbit/s (megabits per second).
**Find:** Equivalent throughput in MB/s (megabytes per second).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Since } 1 \text{ byte} &= 8 \text{ bits} \\
R_{\text{bytes}} &= \frac{R_{\text{bits}}}{8} \\
R_{\text{bytes}} &= \frac{800 \text{ Mbit/s}}{8} \\
R_{\text{bytes}} &= 100 \text{ MB/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logical Commentary:** Each byte is composed of 8 bits, so to convert from bits to bytes, we divide the bit-rate by 8. Therefore, 800 Mbps corresponds to exactly 100 MB/s. This conversion is a **mandatory first step** in nearly every KTU bandwidth problem.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation 2: Effective Throughput After Protocol Overhead

**Given:** Raw link bandwidth = 1 Gbps = 1000 Mbit/s. Protocol overhead = 12% (i.e., 12% of bandwidth consumed by encoding, headers, ACKs).
**Find:** Effective usable throughput in MB/s.

**Step 1:** Convert raw rate to bytes per second.

$$
R_{\text{raw}} = \frac{1000 \text{ Mbit/s}}{8} = 125 \text{ MB/s}
$$

**Step 2:** Subtract overhead fraction.

$$
R_{\text{eff}} = R_{\text{raw}} \times (1 - \text{overhead fraction})
$$

$$
R_{\text{eff}} = 125 \text{ MB/s} \times (1 - 0.12)
$$

$$
R_{\text{eff}} = 125 \times 0.88
$$

$$
R_{\text{eff}} = 110 \text{ MB/s}
$$

**Logical Commentary:** Real-world systems never deliver the nominal bit-rate. We account for overhead multiplicatively by retaining the (1 − overhead) fraction. The result, 110 MB/s, represents the **practical ceiling** for user data movement.

---

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation 3: Backup Time for 4 TB Database

**Given:**
- Data Size $S$ = 4 TB
- Raw link bandwidth = 1 Gbps
- Protocol overhead = 12%
- NAS sustained write rate = 180 MB/s
- 1 TB = 1,000,000 MB (SI convention)

**Step 1:** Compute the link's effective throughput (from Derivation 2).

$$
R_{\text{link}} = 110 \text{ MB/s}
$$

**Step 2:** Convert 4 TB to MB.

$$
S = 4 \text{ TB} = 4 \times 1{,}000{,}000 \text{ MB} = 4{,}000{,}000 \text{ MB}
$$

**Step 3:** Identify the bottleneck using the **min-function principle**.

$$
R_{\text{actual}} = \min(R_{\text{link}},\ R_{\text{NAS}})
$$

$$
R_{\text{actual}} = \min(110,\ 180) = 110 \text{ MB/s}
$$

The **link is the bottleneck** (it is slower than the NAS write capability).

**Step 4:** Calculate transfer time.

$$
T = \frac{S}{R_{\text{actual}}}
