$$

$$
T = \frac{4{,}000{,}000 \text{ MB}}{110 \text{ MB/s}}
$$

$$
T \approx 36{,}363.64 \text{ seconds}
$$

**Step 5:** Convert to hours.

$$
T = \frac{36{,}363.64}{3600} \approx 10.10 \text{ hours}
$$

**Logical Commentary:** A real-world backup window of ~10.1 hours is the practical result. If the link were upgraded to 10 Gbps (~1100 MB/s effective), the bottleneck would shift to the NAS at 180 MB/s, dramatically extending the time—demonstrating why **end-to-end pipeline analysis** is critical in storage engineering.

---

## 3.4 Exhaustive Derivation 4: HDD Sequential Transfer Rate (Theoretical)

**Given:** An HDD with 5400 RPM spindle speed, 256 sectors per track, 512 bytes per sector.

**Step 1:** Calculate rotation period.

$$
T_{\text{rot}} = \frac{60 \text{ s}}{5400 \text{ RPM}} = \frac{60}{5400} = 0.01111 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 2:** Calculate bytes per track.

$$
\text{Bytes/track} = 256 \text{ sectors} \times 512 \text{ bytes} = 131{,}072 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute sequential read rate (one full track per rotation).

$$
R_{\text{seq}} = \frac{\text{Bytes/track}}{T_{\text{rot}}}
$$

$$
R_{\text{seq}} = \frac{131{,}072 \text{ bytes}}{0.01111 \text{ s}}
$$

$$
R_{\text{seq}} \approx 11{,}796{,}396 \text{ bytes/s} \approx 11.25 \text{ MB/s}
$$

**Logical Commentary:** A 5400 RPM consumer HDD delivers roughly 11 MB/s sequential read at the outermost track (where one rotation yields the maximum data). Real-world rates are often lower due to inner-track recording zones and firmware overhead.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Storage Transfer Rates

| Storage Media | Interface | Sequential Read | Sequential Write | Random 4K Read (IOPS) |
|---------------|-----------|-----------------|------------------|------------------------|
| 5400 RPM HDD | SATA III | ~100 MB/s | ~90 MB/s | ~100 |
| 7200 RPM HDD | SATA III | ~180 MB/s | ~170 MB/s | ~150 |
| SATA SSD | SATA III (6 Gbps) | ~550 MB/s | ~520 MB/s | ~95,000 |
| NVMe Gen3 SSD | PCIe 3.0 x4 | ~3,500 MB/s | ~3,000 MB/s | ~500,000 |
| NVMe Gen4 SSD | PCIe 4.0 x4 | ~7,000 MB/s | ~6,500 MB/s | ~1,000,000 |
| NVMe Gen5 SSD | PCIe 5.0 x4 | ~12,000 MB/s | ~11,000 MB/s | ~1,500,000 |

> [!NOTE]
> These values represent typical real-world benchmarks as of 2024–2025. KTU exam questions often use **theoretical maxima** (e.g., SATA III raw = 600 MB/s, but with 8b/10b encoding = 480 MB/s usable).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Data Transfer

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **end-to-end data path** in a typical storage system, identifying each component that contributes to the effective transfer rate:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Application Layer: Request 4 TB Backup] --> B[OS File System: Splits into Blocks]
    B --> C[Storage Driver: Issues Read/Write Commands]
    C --> D[Host Bus Adapter: HBA / NVMe Controller]
    D --> E[Interface Layer: SATA / SAS / PCIe]
    E --> F[Storage Controller: Command Queueing and Scheduling]
    F --> G[Cache Layer: DRAM Cache Hit or Miss]
    G --> H[Media Access: HDD Platter or NAND Flash]
    H --> I[Return Path: Reverse Pipeline]
    I --> J[Effective Throughput Calculation]
    J --> K[MBps Reported to User]
    
    L[Raw Bandwidth: Interface Limit] --> J
    M[Protocol Overhead: Encoding and Headers] --> J
    N[Seek and Rotational Latency: HDD] --> J
    O[Controller Queue Depth] --> J
    P[NAND Program and Erase Latency: SSD] --> J
```

**Interpretation of the Flow:**
- The **raw bandwidth** of the interface (e.g., 600 MB/s for SATA III) is the **upper bound**.
- Each stage in the pipeline **subtracts** overhead (encoding, queuing, latency).
- The final **effective throughput** is what the user observes as MBps.

---

## 4.2 Transfer Rate vs. Data Size Relationship (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S[Data Size S: in MB] --> T[Transfer Time T: in seconds]
    R[Transfer Rate R: in MB per second] --> T
    T --> Result[Result: T equals S divided by R]
    
    S2[Increase S] --> Effect1[T increases linearly with S]
    R2[Increase R] --> Effect2[T decreases inversely with R]
```

**Mathematical Relationship Summary:**

$$
T \propto S \quad \text{and} \quad T \propto \frac{1}{R}
$$

- Doubling the data size doubles the transfer time.
- Doubling the transfer rate halves the transfer time.

---

## 4.3 Bottleneck Identification Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Compute all component rates] --> Q1{Is link rate less than media rate}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Bottleneck1[Link is the bottleneck]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Is controller rate less than media rate}
    Q2 -- Yes --> Bottleneck2[Controller is the bottleneck]
    Q2 -- No --> Bottleneck3[Media is the bottleneck]
    Bottleneck1 --> Final[Effective Rate equals min of all components]
    Bottleneck2 --> Final
    Bottleneck3 --> Final
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: Differentiate between MBps and Mbps. Why is this distinction important in storage system performance evaluation?**

**Model Answer:**

**MBps (Megabytes per second):**
- Unit of data transfer rate in **bytes** per second.
- 1 MBps = 1,000,000 bytes/second (SI) or 1,048,576 bytes/second (binary).
- Commonly used to describe **storage device** performance (SSDs, HDDs, RAID arrays).

**Mbps (Megabits per second):**
- Unit of data transfer rate in **bits** per second.
- 1 Mbps = 1,000,000 bits/second.
- Commonly used to describe **network link** speeds (Ethernet, Fibre Channel, WAN).

**Conversion:** $1 \text{ MBps} = 8 \text{ Mbps}$.

**Importance:** A storage device advertised as "1000 Mbps" delivers only 125 MB/s effective throughput. Confusing these units leads to a **factor-of-8 error** in performance estimates, which is critical in capacity planning, backup window calculations, and SLA compliance.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**Q: Define effective transfer rate. List any two factors that reduce the effective transfer rate below the theoretical maximum.**

**Model Answer:**

**Effective Transfer Rate** is the actual data throughput achieved in real-world operation, accounting for all system-level overheads, latencies, and inefficiencies. It is always less than or equal to the theoretical (raw) bandwidth.

**Two factors that reduce effective transfer rate:**

1. **Protocol Overhead:** Encoding schemes (8b/10b, 128b/130b), command headers, acknowledgments, and error-correction data consume 10–20% of raw bandwidth.
2. **Media Access Latency:** For HDDs, seek time (3–15 ms) and rotational latency (4–8 ms) reduce the rate for random I/O. For SSDs, NAND program/erase cycles and garbage collection introduce delays.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students often quote the **raw interface bandwidth** (e.g., 600 MB/s for SATA III) without accounting for the 8b/10b encoding loss, leading to a **20% overestimation** of throughput. Always apply the encoding tax in numerical problems.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Full-Length Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> **Module 4 Internal Choice Note:** As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, students answer **one** of two alternatives. Both options are provided below for comprehensive preparation.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Modified]`

**A(a)** Explain the concept of transfer rate in storage systems. Discuss the difference between sequential and random transfer rates with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)**

**A(b)** A 2 TB external HDD is connected via USB 3.0 (theoretical 5 Gbps). The protocol overhead is 15%, and the HDD's sustained sequential write speed is 120 MB/s. Calculate:
- (i) The effective throughput of the USB link in MB/s.
- (ii) The bottleneck component.
- (iii) The total time to transfer 2 TB of data in minutes. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for A(a):

**Concept of Transfer Rate:**
Transfer rate is the **speed at which data moves** between a host and a storage medium, measured in MB/s. It is determined by the **slowest component** in the I/O path (the bottleneck).

**Sequential Transfer Rate:**
- Reading or writing **large, contiguous blocks** of data.
- Limited primarily by the **interface bandwidth** and **media sustained throughput**.
- **Example:** Copying a 50 GB movie file to an SSD. Modern NVMe SSDs achieve 5,000–12,000 MB/s sequentially.

**Random Transfer Rate:**
- Reading or writing **small, scattered blocks** (typically 4 KB) at non-contiguous locations.
- Heavily impacted by **seek time (HDD)** or **IOPS (SSD)**.
- **Example:** Running an OS with thousands of small file accesses. A 7200 RPM HDD may deliver only 1–2 MB/s randomly, while an NVMe SSD delivers 500–2,000 MB/s.

**Key Insight:** Sequential rates can be **10× to 1000× higher** than random rates, especially for HDDs. This is why SSDs dominate workloads with high random I/O (databases, VMs).

---

#### Model Solution for A(b):

**Given:**
- Data size $S$ = 2 TB = 2,000,000 MB (SI)
- USB 3.0 raw rate = 5 Gbps = 5000 Mbit/s
- Protocol overhead = 15%
- HDD sustained write = 120 MB/s

**(i) Effective USB throughput:**

Convert to bytes:

$$
R_{\text{raw}} = \frac{5000 \text{ Mbit/s}}{8} = 625 \text{ MB/s}
$$

Apply overhead:

$$
R_{\text{USB, eff}} = 625 \times (1 - 0.15) = 625 \times 0.85 = 531.25 \text{ MB/s}
$$

**(ii) Bottleneck identification:**

$$
R_{\text{actual}} = \min(531.25,\ 120) = 120 \text{ MB/s}
$$

**The HDD is the bottleneck.**

**(iii) Total transfer time:**

$$
T = \frac{S}{R_{\text{actual}}} = \frac{2{,}000{,}000 \text{ MB}}{120 \text{ MB/s}} = 16{,}666.67 \text{ s}
$$

Convert to minutes:

$$
T = \frac{16{,}666.67}{60} \approx 277.78 \text{ minutes} \approx 4 \text{ hours } 38 \text{ minutes}
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified]`

**B(a)** With a neat diagram, describe the layered architecture of a storage I/O path and explain how each layer affects the effective transfer rate. **(7 Marks)**

**B(b)** An enterprise RAID-6 array consists of 8 HDDs, each with a sequential read rate of 200 MB/s. The RAID controller has an internal bandwidth of 2 GB/s. The host connection is a 16 Gbps Fibre Channel link with 10% overhead. Calculate:
- (i) The aggregate sequential read capacity of the RAID array (in MB/s).
- (ii) The effective host link rate in MB/s.
- (iii) The end-to-end user-perceived transfer rate. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for B(a):

**Layered Storage I/O Architecture:**

1. **Application Layer:** Issues read/write requests (e.g., database query). May aggregate small I/Os.
2. **File System Layer:** Maps file operations to logical block addresses.
3. **OS Storage Driver / HBA:** Translates commands to interface-specific protocols (SCSI, NVMe).
4. **Interface Layer (SATA/SAS/PCIe/FC):** Provides raw bandwidth; subject to encoding overhead.
5. **Storage Controller:** Performs RAID calculations, command queuing, caching.
6. **Cache (DRAM):** Accelerates repeated reads and write coalescing.
7. **Physical Media (HDD/SSD):** Final source/sink of data.

**Effect on Transfer Rate:**
- Each layer introduces **latency** (queueing, processing) and may impose **bandwidth limits**.
- The **effective rate** is constrained by the **minimum** of all layer bandwidths.
- For example, an HDD can sustain 200 MB/s, but a SATA II interface caps it at 300 MB/s, and a busy controller may further reduce it.

*(Refer to Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1 for visual support.)*

---

#### Model Solution for B(b):

**Given:**
- 8 HDDs × 200 MB/s each (RAID-6 read = all disks contribute, no parity penalty for reads)
- Controller bandwidth = 2 GB/s = 2000 MB/s
- FC link = 16 Gbps, 10% overhead

**(i) Aggregate array read:**

In RAID-6, reads can be served by **all 8 disks in parallel** (no parity recomputation needed):

$$
R_{\text{array}} = 8 \times 200 = 1600 \text{ MB/s}
$$

**(ii) Effective FC link rate:**

$$
R_{\text{raw, FC}} = \frac{16{,}000 \text{ Mbit/s}}{8} = 2000 \text{ MB/s}
$$

$$
R_{\text{FC, eff}} = 2000 \times (1 - 0.10) = 2000 \times 0.90 = 1800 \text{ MB/s}
$$

**(iii) End-to-end user rate:**

$$
R_{\text{user}} = \min(R_{\text{array}},\ R_{\text{controller}},\ R_{\text{FC, eff}})
$$

$$
R_{\text{user}} = \min(1600,\ 2000,\ 1800) = 1600 \text{ MB/s}
$$

**The RAID array is the bottleneck.** Despite the FC link supporting 1800 MB/s, the disks collectively limit throughput to 1600 MB/s.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning – Common Mistakes:**
> 1. **RAID-6 read penalty confusion:** Some students incorrectly apply a "read penalty" to RAID-6 reads. *Read penalty applies only to writes* (due to parity calculation). Reads scale linearly with disk count.
> 2. **Unit confusion:** Mixing GB/s and MB/s. Remember: 1 GB = 1000 MB (SI).
> 3. **Forgetting the `min` function:** Students often average rates instead of taking the minimum. The **bottleneck (slowest component)** dictates the end-to-end rate.
> 4. **Overhead miscalculation:** Overhead is *subtracted* from the raw rate, not added. Use $R_{\text{eff}} = R_{\text{raw}} \times (1 - \text{OH})$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> **Print this section and revise 5 minutes before the exam.**

- ✅ **MBps = Megabytes/second** (storage metric); **Mbps = Megabits/second** (network metric). **1 MBps = 8 Mbps.**
- ✅ **Core formula:** $T = \dfrac{S}{R}$. Master all three rearrangements.
- ✅ **Effective rate** = Raw rate × (1 − overhead fraction). Protocol overhead typically 10–20%.
- ✅ **Bottleneck principle:** End-to-end throughput = **minimum** of all component rates.
- ✅ **HDD access time** = Seek time + Rotational latency + Transfer time.
- ✅ **HDD sequential rate** = (Bytes per track) / (Rotation time in seconds).
- ✅ **IOPS to Throughput:** $\text{MB/s} = \dfrac{\text{IOPS} \times \text{Block Size (KB)}}{1024}$.
- ✅ **SATA III raw = 600 MB/s**, but **8b/10b encoding reduces usable to ~480 MB/s** (20% tax).
- ✅ **NVMe Gen4 x4 = ~7 GB/s**; **Gen5 x4 = ~12 GB/s** (real-world, not theoretical).
- ✅ **Sequential >> Random** for HDDs (1000× gap); gap is smaller (~10×) for SSDs.
- ✅ **RAID read scaling:** RAID-0, RAID-1, RAID-5, RAID-6 all scale reads linearly with disk count (no read penalty). **Writes** have penalties in parity-based RAID.
- ✅ **SI vs Binary:** 1 MB (SI) = 10⁶ bytes; 1 MiB (binary) = 2²⁰ bytes. KTU accepts both but expects awareness.
- ✅ **Always convert bits to bytes** (divide by 8) when network specs are given for storage problems.
- ✅ **End-to-end pipeline analysis** is the key engineering skill tested in 14-mark questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Factors Affecting Storage Performance
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST867 - STORAGE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Factors Affecting Storage Performance

# 📘 FACTORS AFFECTING STORAGE PERFORMANCE

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 📌 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Storage Performance** is a composite measure of how efficiently a storage subsystem can service data access requests from host applications, characterized by three interdependent engineering dimensions: **latency** (response time per request), **throughput** (volume of data moved per unit time), and **availability** (fraction of time the system is operationally usable). It is the result of the dynamic interaction between **device-level characteristics**, **channel/interface parameters**, **host-side software stack**, and **workload behavioral patterns**.

Mathematically, performance is a *function* rather than a single number:

$$P_{storage} = f(\text{Device}, \text{Interface}, \text{Host}, \text{Workload}, \text{Environment})$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Performance is **NOT** a property of the storage device alone. The *same* disk can yield wildly different IOPS when paired with different controllers, drivers, queue depths, and workloads. Examiners frequently test this nuance.

---

### 🧠 Conceptual Analogy — The "Library & Librarian" Model

Imagine a **central library** with millions of books, attended by **one librarian**.

| Storage Element | Library Analogy |
|---|---|
| **Storage Media (HDD/SSD/Tape)** | The book stacks and warehouse |
| **Capacity (TB/PB)** | Total number of books the building can hold |
| **Seek Time** | The time for the librarian to *walk* from the current shelf to the desired one |
| **Rotational Latency** | The time the librarian spends *turning around* to face the right shelf |
| **Throughput** | How many books the librarian can hand out per minute |
| **IOPS** | How many individual *requests* are completed per second |
| **Cache (DRAM Buffer)** | The librarian's personal *desk* where frequently-returned books are kept |
| **Interface (SATA/SAS/NVMe)** | The **width of the doorway** through which books leave the library |
| **Controller CPU** | The librarian's *brain* and *decision-making speed* |
| **Queue Depth** | The number of patrons standing in line waiting |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Core Insight:** A library (storage device) with **huge capacity** is useless if the **doorway is narrow** (slow interface) or the **librarian is slow** (poor controller). Likewise, a fast doorway cannot help if the librarian is *searching* for a misplaced book (high seek time). **Performance is the bottleneck of the weakest link in the chain.**

> [!TIP]
> **Why does this matter for KTU exams?** Because the question "List the factors affecting storage performance" is not asking for a *shopping list*. It is asking you to **classify** them into categories and explain **how each one mathematically alters latency, throughput, or availability**. Memorize the categories first, then the *mechanism* of impact.

---

### 🎯 Taxonomy of Performance Factors (Four Pillars)

The KTU 2024 Scheme expects students to classify factors into the following four engineering domains:

1. **Device-Level Physical Factors** — Capacity, RPM, NAND type, density
2. **Interface & Connectivity Factors** — Bus width, protocol overhead, link speed
3. **Host-Side Software Factors** — File system, driver stack, OS scheduler, controller firmware
4. **Workload & Operational Factors** — Read/write mix, sequential/random pattern, queue depth, block size, temperature, vibration, power

We will dissect every pillar in **SECTION 2**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Pillar I — Device-Level Physical Factors

These are intrinsic to the **storage medium itself** and cannot be altered without replacing the hardware.

### 2.1.1 Storage Capacity & Areal Density
- **Capacity** = Total bytes storable. Modern 3.5″ HDDs reach **20 TB+** via **Helium-filled** chambers and **Shingled Magnetic Recording (SMR)**.
- **Areal Density** (bits per square inch) is the *real* driver. Higher areal density → more data per track → higher sequential throughput.
- Performance impact: **Larger capacity generally improves sequential throughput** (more data per rotation passes under the head) but does **NOT** improve random IOPS.

### 2.1.2 Rotational Speed (RPM) — For HDDs
- Standard enterprise RPMs: **5400, 7200, 10000, 15000**.
- Higher RPM → **Lower rotational latency** and **higher transfer rate**.
- A 15K RPM drive has rotational latency of **2.0 ms** vs. **4.17 ms** for a 7.2K RPM drive.

### 2.1.3 Average Seek Time
- Time for the actuator arm to move the read/write head from one track to a randomly chosen destination track.
- Typical values: **3 ms (enterprise) to 12 ms (consumer)**.
- Seek time is **dominated by mechanical inertia** and is the **single largest component of HDD latency**.

### 2.1.4 NAND Flash Type (For SSDs)
- **SLC (Single-Level Cell):** 1 bit/cell → fastest, highest endurance (~100K P/E cycles)
- **MLC (Multi-Level Cell):** 2 bits/cell → moderate speed, moderate endurance (~3K–10K P/E)
- **TLC (Triple-Level Cell):** 3 bits/cell → slower, lower endurance (~1K–3K P/E)
- **QLC (Quad-Level Cell):** 4 bits/cell → slowest, lowest endurance (~1K P/E)
- Higher bits/cell → **more voltage-level distinctions needed** → **slower program/erase operations**.

### 2.1.5 Cache/Buffer Size
- Onboard **DRAM cache** (typically **32 MB to 512 MB** in HDDs; **1 GB to 4 GB** in enterprise SSDs).
- Acts as a **write coalescing buffer** and a **read-ahead prefetch buffer**.

---

## 2.2 Pillar II — Interface & Connectivity Factors

The **pipe** through which data flows between the storage device and the host.

| Interface | Theoretical Bandwidth | Protocol Overhead | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| SATA III | 6 Gbps (~600 MB/s) | High (ATA command set) | Consumer HDDs/SSDs |
| SAS 3.0 | 12 Gbps | Moderate (SCSI) | Enterprise HDDs |
| SAS 4.0 | 22.5 Gbps | Moderate | Data center |
| NVMe (PCIe 4.0 x4) | ~7 GB/s | Low (designed for flash) | High-performance SSDs |
| NVMe (PCIe 5.0 x4) | ~14 GB/s | Low | Cutting-edge SSDs |
| Fibre Channel 32G | 32 Gbps | Low | SAN environments |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why NVMe beats SATA for SSDs:** SATA was designed for **spinning disks** with queue depth of 1 command. NVMe supports **64K queues × 64K commands each** (over 4 billion parallel commands). This is the *primary reason* an NVMe SSD delivers **10× the IOPS** of an identical flash chip behind a SATA bridge.

---

## 2.3 Pillar III — Host-Side Software Factors

The **invisible performance killers** that sit *between* the application and the disk.

1. **Storage Controller Firmware** — Implements caching, wear-leveling, GC (garbage collection)
2. **Host Bus Adapter (HBA) Driver** — Translates OS I/O requests to interface commands
3. **OS I/O Scheduler** — Reorders and merges requests (CFQ, Deadline, NOOP)
4. **File System Journaling** — Adds write overhead for crash recovery (ext4, NTFS, XFS)
5. **File System Block Size** — Misalignment between FS block size and physical sector size causes **read-modify-write penalties**
6. **Page Cache / Buffer Cache** — Host-side DRAM cache that absorbs reads

---

## 2.4 Pillar IV — Workload & Operational Factors

The **behavior of the request stream** itself. The same disk behaves *completely differently* under different workloads.

| Workload Trait | Impact on Performance |
|---|---|
| **Sequential vs. Random** | Sequential I/O leverages prefetch; random I/O pays full seek + rotational penalty |
| **Read vs. Write Mix** | Writes are slower on HDDs (need head to write-enable) and on SSDs (program > read latency) |
| **Block Size** | Larger blocks → higher throughput, lower IOPS. Smaller blocks → higher IOPS, lower throughput |
| **Queue Depth (QD)** | HDDs saturate at QD=1–4. SSDs scale linearly up to QD=32 or more |
| **Concurrency** | Multiple threads/IOs in flight allow the controller to optimize ordering |
| **I/O Burstiness** | Bursty workloads can be absorbed by cache; sustained loads cannot |

---

## 2.5 Environmental Factors
- **Temperature:** High temps → increased read errors, thermal throttling in SSDs, accelerated media degradation
- **Vibration:** Severely degrades HDD seek accuracy (rotational vibration in dense disk shelves)
- **Power Quality:** Voltage sags cause write loss, controller resets
- **Humidity:** Causes head-disc stiction in HDDs

---

## 2.6 📊 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (HIGH-YIELD)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are examinable. Use `\vert` (not `|`) for absolute values per the KTU math convention.**

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Average Rotational Latency** | $L_{rot} = \dfrac{1}{2} \times \dfrac{60}{RPM}$ | Seconds; half a full rotation on average |
| 2 | **Average Access Time** | $T_{access} = T_{seek} + L_{rot} + T_{transfer}$ | Seconds; total per I/O |
| 3 | **Theoretical IOPS** | $IOPS = \dfrac{1}{T_{access}}$ | Operations per second |
| 4 | **Data Transfer Rate (HDD)** | $DTR = \dfrac{B_{track} \times RPM}{60}$ | Bytes/second; $B_{track}$ = bytes per track |
| 5 | **Throughput (Bandwidth)** | $Th = IOPS \times B_{block}$ | Bytes/second; $B_{block}$ = I/O block size |
| 6 | **Cache Hit Ratio** | $H = \dfrac{N_{hits}}{N_{hits} + N_{misses}}$ | Dimensionless (0 to 1) |
| 7 | **Effective Access Time (2-level cache)** | $T_{eff} = H_1 T_{L1} + (1-H_1)\big[H_2 T_{L2} + (1-H_2) T_{storage}\big]$ | Weighted average access time |
| 8 | **System Availability** | $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | Fraction (0 to 1); often expressed as "nines" |
| 9 | **Unavailability (Downtime)** | $U = 1 - A$ | Fraction of year/quarter down |
| 10 | **Amdahl's Law (Storage Scaling)** | $S_{overall} = \dfrac{1}{(1-F) + \dfrac{F}{N}}$ | $F$ = parallelizable fraction, $N$ = number of disks |
| 11 | **RAID 0 Read IOPS** | $IOPS_{R} = N \times IOPS_{disk}$ | $N$ = number of disks in stripe |
| 12 | **RAID 1 Write IOPS** | $IOPS_{W} = \dfrac{N}{2} \times IOPS_{disk}$ | Each write hits both mirrors |
| 13 | **RAID 5 Write Penalty** | $IOPS_{W} = \dfrac{N \times IOPS_{disk}}{4}$ | 4 I/O operations per logical write (2 reads + 2 writes) |
| 14 | **RAID 6 Write Penalty** | $IOPS_{W} = \dfrac{N \times IOPS_{disk}}{6}$ | 6 I/O operations per logical write (3 reads + 3 writes) |
| 15 | **SSD Program/Erase Cycles (P/E)** | $Endurance = Type_{factor} \times Capacity$ | SLC ≈ 100K, MLC ≈ 10K, TLC ≈ 3K, QLC ≈ 1K per cell |
| 16 | **Queue Response Time (M/G/1)** | $R = \dfrac{S}{1 - \rho}$ | $S$ = service time, $\rho$ = utilization |
| 17 | **Sequential vs Random Speed Ratio (HDD)** | $Ratio \approx 50{:}1$ to $100{:}1$ | SSDs reduce this to ~5:1 |

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Cloud Architects (AWS, Azure, GCP):** Choose storage tiers (EBS gp3 vs. io2 Block Express) based on the **IOPS-vs-throughput trade-off** dictated by workload.
- **Database Engineers:** Calculate **RAID 5/6 write penalty** when sizing OLTP databases to avoid I/O bottlenecks.
- **Backup Engineers:** Apply **Amdahl's Law** to size parallel backup streams for tape libraries.
- **Embedded Systems Designers:** Use **SLC flash** for industrial controllers (high endurance) and **QLC** for consumer USB drives (cost-driven).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Industrial Context:** Modern hyper-converged infrastructure (HCI) like Nutanix and vSAN dynamically tunes these factors in real time using AI/ML. The "factors" in your syllabus are the **inputs** to these real systems.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Mandate:** Every step below is fully expanded. No algebraic transition is skipped. Memorize the **logic** behind each step, not just the final expression.

---

## 3.1 Derivation #1 — Average Rotational Latency

**Premise:** When a random I/O request arrives, the disk platter is at an **unknown angular position**. We want the *expected* wait time until the desired sector rotates under the head.

### Step 1 — Define the random variable
Let $X$ = angular offset of desired sector from current head position (in degrees). Since the sector is equally likely to be anywhere on the circle:

$$X \sim \text{Uniform}(0, 360)$$

### Step 2 — Convert to time
The time to wait is proportional to the angular distance. The full rotation takes $\dfrac{60}{RPM}$ seconds. So:

$$T_{wait} = X \times \frac{60}{360 \times RPM} = X \times \frac{1}{6 \times RPM}$$

### Step 3 — Compute the Expected Value $E[T_{wait}]$
For a uniform distribution on $[0, 360]$:

$$E[X] = \frac{0 + 360}{2} = 180 \text{ degrees}$$

### Step 4 — Substitute into the time equation

$$L_{rot} = E[T_{wait}] = 180 \times \frac{1}{6 \times RPM} = \frac{30}{RPM} \text{ minutes?}$$

> Wait — units must be reconciled. $\dfrac{60}{RPM}$ is in **seconds per rotation**. So $E[X]$ in degrees, divided by 360 degrees, gives a **fraction of a rotation**, then multiplied by total seconds per rotation:

$$L_{rot} = \frac{180 \text{ degrees}}{360 \text{ degrees}} \times \frac{60 \text{ sec}}{RPM}$$

### Step 5 — Simplify the expression

$$L_{rot} = \frac{1}{2} \times \frac{60}{RPM} = \frac{30}{RPM} \text{ seconds}$$

### ✅ Final Result:

$$\boxed{L_{rot} = \frac{30}{RPM} \text{ seconds}}$$

**Numerical Sanity Check:**
- For a **7200 RPM** drive: $L_{rot} = \dfrac{30}{7200} = 0.00417$ s = **4.17 ms** ✓
- For a **15000 RPM** drive: $L_{rot} = \dfrac{30}{15000} = 0.002$ s = **2.0 ms** ✓

---

## 3.2 Derivation #2 — Theoretical Maximum IOPS

**Premise:** The maximum number of random I/O operations a disk can complete per second is the reciprocal of the average time per I/O.

### Step 1 — Write the access time equation

$$T_{access} = T_{seek} + L_{rot} + T_{transfer}$$

### Step 2 — Approximate the transfer time
For random I/O, only **one sector** is read/written per I/O. If the sector size is $S$ bytes and the disk has $N$ sectors per track:

$$T_{transfer} = \frac{S}{DTR} = \frac{S \times 60}{B_{track} \times RPM}$$

For *random* I/O, $T_{transfer}$ is negligible (microseconds) compared to $T_{seek}$ and $L_{rot}$ (milliseconds). So:

$$T_{access} \approx T_{seek} + L_{rot}$$

### Step 3 — Take the reciprocal

$$IOPS_{max} = \frac{1}{T_{access}}$$

### Step 4 — Worked Numerical Example
A 7200 RPM enterprise HDD has $T_{seek} = 8$ ms (average) and $L_{rot} = 4.17$ ms.

$$T_{access} = 8 + 4.17 = 12.17 \text{ ms}$$

$$IOPS_{max} = \frac{1}{0.01217} \approx 82 \text{ IOPS}$$

### ✅ Final Result:

$$\boxed{IOPS = \frac{1000}{T_{seek} + L_{rot}} \text{ (when } T_{access} \text{ in ms)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Note:** 80–180 IOPS is the **theoretical maximum** for a single 7.2K RPM HDD. Real-world benchmarks yield 60–120 IOPS due to OS overhead, file system journaling, and queue starvation.

---

## 3.3 Derivation #3 — Effective Access Time with Cache

**Premise:** A 2-level cache hierarchy exists. A fraction $H_1$ of requests are satisfied by L1 (fast cache). The rest go to L2, where a further fraction $H_2$ hit. Misses reach main storage.

### Step 1 — Define the access times
- $T_{L1}$ = L1 cache access time
- $T_{L2}$ = L2 cache access time
- $T_{storage}$ = Main storage (disk) access time
- Convention: $T_{L1} < T_{L2} < T_{storage}$

### Step 2 — Compute the L1 miss penalty
If the L1 cache is missed (probability $1 - H_1$), we pay $T_{L1}$ (already spent) **plus** the time to access L2. The time to access L2 is:

$$T_{L2,eff} = H_2 \cdot T_{L2} + (1 - H_2) \cdot (T_{L2} + T_{storage})$$

This collapses to:

$$T_{L2,eff} = T_{L2} + (1 - H_2) \cdot T_{storage}$$

### Step 3 — Build the overall effective access time

$$T_{eff} = H_1 \cdot T_{L1} + (1 - H_1) \cdot (T_{L1} + T_{L2,eff})$$

### Step 4 — Expand the L2 effective time

$$T_{eff} = H_1 \cdot T_{L1} + (1 - H_1) \cdot \left[ T_{L1} + T_{L2} + (1 - H_2) \cdot T_{storage} \right]$$

### Step 5 — Distribute the $(1 - H_1)$ term

$$T_{eff} = H_1 \cdot T_{L1} + (1 - H_1) \cdot T_{L1} + (1 - H_1) \cdot T_{L2} + (1 - H_1)(1 - H_2) \cdot T_{storage}$$

The first two terms collapse to $T_{L1}$:

$$T_{eff} = T_{L1} + (1 - H_1) \cdot T_{L2} + (1 - H_1)(1 - H_2) \cdot T_{storage}$$

### ✅ Final Result (the **canonical** formula for KTU):

$$\boxed{T_{eff} = T_{L1} + (1 - H_1) \cdot T_{L2} + (1 - H_1)(1 - H_2) \cdot T_{storage}}$$

### Step 6 — Numerical Worked Example
- $T_{L1}$ = 0.1 ms (SSD cache)
- $T_{L2}$ = 5 ms (SSD over SAS)
- $T_{storage}$ = 12 ms (spinning HDD)
- $H_1$ = 0.6 (60% of reads hit L1)
- $H_2$ = 0.5 (50% of L1-misses hit L2)

$$T_{eff} = 0.1 + (0.4)(5) + (0.4)(0.5)(12)$$
$$= 0.1 + 2.0 + 2.4 = 4.5 \text{ ms}$$

**Effective IOPS** = $\dfrac{1000}{4.5} \approx 222$ IOPS — a **170% improvement** over the bare HDD's 82 IOPS!

---

## 3.4 Derivation #4 — RAID 5 Write Penalty ("Read-Modify-Write")

**Premise:** A RAID 5 array has $N$ disks with distributed parity. Every logical write requires updating both the data block **and** the parity block.

### Step 1 — Identify the four sub-operations
When a single data block is written to a RAID 5 volume:
1. **Read the OLD data** block (to compute the new parity) — 1 read I/O
2. **Read the OLD parity** block (from a different disk) — 1 read I/O
3. **Write the NEW data** block — 1 write I/O
4. **Write the NEW parity** block — 1 write I/O

### Step 2 — Count total physical I/Os
Total physical I/Os per logical write = **4**

### Step 3 — Express the effective IOPS
For $N$ disks in the array, total physical IOPS available = $N \times IOPS_{disk}$. The effective *logical* write IOPS are:

$$IOPS_{W, RAID5} = \frac{N \times IOPS_{disk}}{4}$$

### Step 4 — Worked Numerical Example
A RAID 5 array with **8 disks**, each capable of **180 IOPS**:

$$IOPS_{W, RAID5} = \frac{8 \times 180}{4} = 360 \text{ logical write IOPS}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **The "Free Capacity" Trap:** RAID 5 has 1-disk-equivalent of parity overhead ($N-1$ usable disks of $N$ total). Despite this, the **write penalty is 4×**, not 1.25× as students often assume.

### ✅ Final Result:

$$\boxed{IOPS_{W, RAID5} = \frac{N \times IOPS_{disk}}{4}}$$

---

## 3.5 Derivation #5 — Amdahl's Law for Storage Scaling

**Premise:** Adding more disks in parallel can speed up I/O — but only for the *parallelizable* portion of the workload. The serial portion is unaffected.

### Step 1 — Define the components
Let the total workload be normalized to 1. Let $F$ be the fraction that *can* be parallelized across $N$ disks. Then $(1 - F)$ is the *serial* portion (e.g., metadata I/O, lock management, log writes).

### Step 2 — Compute the new execution time
- Time for serial part with $N$ disks = $(1 - F) \times T_{orig}$
- Time for parallel part with $N$ disks = $\dfrac{F \times T_{orig}}{N}$

$$T_{new} = (1 - F) T_{orig} + \frac{F \cdot T_{orig}}{N} = T_{orig} \left[ (1 - F) + \frac{F}{N} \right]$$

### Step 3 — Compute speedup $S = T_{orig} / T_{new}$

$$S = \frac{1}{(1 - F) + \dfrac{F}{N}}$$

### Step 4 — The fundamental limit
As $N \to \infty$:

$$\lim_{N \to \infty} S = \frac{1}{1 - F}$$

### ✅ Final Result (KTU High-Yield):

$$\boxed{S_{max} = \frac{1}{1 - F} \text{ (as } N \to \infty\text{)}}$$

### Step 5 — Numerical Example
If 80% of a database workload is parallelizable ($F = 0.8$):

$$S_{max} = \frac{1}{1 - 0.8} = \frac{1}{0.2} = 5 \times \text{speedup (no matter how many disks you add!)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Amdahl's Law is the *theoretical ceiling*. The **latency** of the slowest disk in the array, the **controller bottleneck**, and **queue contention** all reduce the *actual* speedup well below this theoretical maximum.

---

## 3.6 📋 Analytical Summary Matrix (Quick-Reference for Exam Day)

| Factor | Mechanism | Effect on Latency | Effect on Throughput | Effect on IOPS |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| ↑ RPM (HDD) | Lower $L_{rot}$ | ↓ Strongly | ↑ | ↑ |
| ↑ Capacity (HDD) | Higher areal density | ↔ (no change) | ↑ (sequential) | ↔ |
| SSD vs HDD | No mechanical delay | ↓↓↓ Drastically | ↑↑ | ↑↑↑ |
| ↑ Cache Size | Higher $H$ | ↓↓ | ↑ | ↑ |
| ↑ Queue Depth | Better overlap | ↑ (per request) | ↑↑ | ↑↑ |
| ↑ Block Size | Less overhead/I/O | ↓ per byte | ↑↑ | ↓ |
| RAID 5 vs RAID 0 | Write penalty | ↑ for writes | ↓ for writes | ↓ for writes |
| SATA vs NVMe | Lower protocol overhead | ↓ | ↑ | ↑ |
| ↑ Vibration | Mis-tracking, re-reads | ↑↑ | ↓ | ↓ |
| File system journaling | Extra write barrier | ↑ for writes | ↓ for writes | ↓ for writes |

## 4.2 Data Flow Block Diagram — How a Single I/O Request Encounters Each Factor

```mermaid
graph LR
    APP["APPLICATION LAYER\nGenerates Read or Write"]

    SCHED["OS I/O SCHEDULER\nMerges and Reorders"]
    HBA["HOST BUS ADAPTER\nDriver Translation"]
    CTRL["STORAGE CONTROLLER\nCache and RAID Engine"]
    INT["INTERFACE LAYER\nSATA SAS NVMe"]
    DEV["STORAGE DEVICE\nHDD SSD or Hybrid"]
    CACHE["ON-DEVICE CACHE\nDRAM Buffer"]
    MEDIA["PHYSICAL MEDIA\nPlatters or NAND"]

    APP -->|User I/O Call| FS
    FS -->|File I/O Request| SCHED
    SCHED -->|Scatter Gather List| HBA
    HBA -->|Protocol Command| CTRL
    CTRL -->|Direct or Cached| CACHE
    CACHE -->|On Miss| MEDIA
    MEDIA -->|Raw Bytes| INT
    INT -->|Acknowledgment| HBA
    HBA -->|Completion| SCHED
    SCHED -->|Elevator Sort| FS
    FS -->|Return to Caller| APP
```

## 4.4 RAID Performance Trade-off Bar Chart (Conceptual)

```mermaid
graph LR
    R0["RAID 0\nWrite Speed: 100 percent\nRead Speed: 100 percent\nRedundancy: NONE"]
    R1["RAID 1\nWrite Speed: 50 percent\nRead Speed: 100 percent\nRedundancy: 1x Mirror"]
    R5["RAID 5\nWrite Speed: 25 percent per disk\nRead Speed: Near 100 percent\nRedundancy: 1 disk parity"]
    R6["RAID 6\nWrite Speed: 17 percent per disk\nRead Speed: Near 100 percent\nRedundancy: 2 disk parity"]
    R10["RAID 10\nWrite Speed: 50 percent\nRead Speed: 200 percent\nRedundancy: 1x Mirror Plus Stripe"]

    R0 --- R1
    R1 --- R5
    R5 --- R6
    R6 --- R10
```

> [!TIP]
> **Visual Mnemonic for KTU Exams:** When asked to compare RAID performance, draw a simple "ladder" like the diagram above. Examiners reward visual clarity, even in written exams.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper Set B]`
**"Differentiate between IOPS and Throughput as measures of storage performance. Under what workload conditions does each become the critical metric?"**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks — ~150 words):

**IOPS (Input/Output Operations Per Second)** measures the *number* of discrete I/O requests a storage system can service per second. It is a *frequency* metric with units of **operations/s**.

**Throughput (Bandwidth)** measures the *volume of data* transferred per unit time. It is a *rate* metric with units of **MB/s** or **GB/s**.

The relationship is:

$$Th = IOPS \times B_{block}$$

where $B_{block}$ is the average I/O block size.

- **IOPS is critical** for **transactional workloads** (OLTP databases, email servers, virtualization) that issue many small (4 KB – 16 KB), random reads/writes.
- **Throughput is critical** for **streaming workloads** (video editing, backups, big-data analytics, scientific computing) that move large (1 MB+) sequential files.

> *Awarding Breakdown:* 1 mark for definitions, 1 mark for the formula, 1 mark for workload conditions.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, S6 CSE Elective]`
**"What is the significance of the Cache Hit Ratio in determining storage performance? Derive the formula for effective access time in a single-level cache system."**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks — ~150 words):

The **Cache Hit Ratio (H)** is the fraction of all memory/storage accesses that are satisfied from the faster cache layer, without needing to access the slower main storage. It is a value between 0 and 1, defined as:

$$H = \frac{N_{hits}}{N_{hits} + N_{misses}}$$

A **higher** H means **fewer** expensive trips to the backing storage, directly reducing average latency.

**Derivation for a single-level cache:**
Let $T_{cache}$ be the cache access time and $T_{storage}$ be the main storage access time. The expected access time is the weighted average:

$$T_{eff} = H \cdot T_{cache} + (1 - H) \cdot T_{miss}$$

Since on a miss we pay $T_{cache}$ to discover the miss, **plus** $T_{storage}$ to fetch the data from main storage:

$$T_{miss} = T_{cache} + T_{storage}$$

Substituting:

$$\boxed{T_{eff} = T_{cache} + (1 - H) \cdot T_{storage}}$$

> *Awarding Breakdown:* 1 mark for H definition and significance, 1 mark for the derivation, 1 mark for the boxed final formula.

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU Rule:** *"Answer any ONE full question from this module. Each question carries 14 marks split as (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks."* Both alternatives below are independent full-questions.

---

### ✏️ QUESTION A `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper Set A, 14 Marks]`

**"Storage performance is determined by the interplay of multiple device, interface, host, and workload factors."**
**(a)** Classify the **factors affecting storage performance** into four engineering domains. For each domain, list **at least three factors** and explain the **mechanism** by which they influence **latency, throughput, or availability**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A data center deploys a **RAID 5 array of 8 enterprise HDDs**, each rated at **180 IOPS** with a **7200 RPM** spindle speed, an **average seek time of 8 ms**, and **6 Gbps SATA** interface. Calculate:
  - (i) The **average rotational latency** of each drive.
  - (ii) The **average access time** per I/O.
  - (iii) The **theoretical maximum IOPS** of one drive.
  - (iv) The **effective read IOPS** and **effective write IOPS** of the RAID 5 array.
  - (v) Comment on the **bottleneck** if the workload is **random 4 KB writes**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Part (a) — 7 Marks

The four engineering domains of factors affecting storage performance are:

**1. Device-Level Physical Factors** (intrinsic to the storage media)
- **Capacity & Areal Density:** Larger capacity increases sequential throughput (more bytes per track); does *not* affect random IOPS.
- **Rotational Speed (RPM) [HDD only]:** Higher RPM → lower rotational latency → lower overall access time → higher IOPS. A 15K RPM drive delivers ~2.4× the IOPS of a 5.4K RPM drive.
- **Average Seek Time [HDD]:** Time to position the read/write head. Reducing seek time from 10 ms to 4 ms nearly *doubles* the IOPS ceiling.
- **NAND Flash Type [SSD]:** SLC > MLC > TLC > QLC in both speed and endurance due to fewer voltage-level distinctions per cell.
- **Onboard DRAM Cache:** Absorbs bursts of writes via write-coalescing and serves hot reads without media access.

**2. Interface & Connectivity Factors** (the data pipe)
- **Protocol Overhead (SATA vs. NVMe):** NVMe has up to **4 billion** concurrent command slots vs. SATA's **1 queue × 32 commands**.
- **Raw Link Bandwidth:** SATA III = 600 MB/s; PCIe 4.0 NVMe = 7,000 MB/s — over **10×** headroom.
- **Queue Depth Capability:** HDDs saturate at QD=1–4; SSDs scale to QD=32+.

**3. Host-Side Software Factors** (the invisible stack)
- **OS I/O Scheduler:** Reorders requests to minimize seek storms (e.g., Linux Deadline scheduler).
- **File System Journaling:** Adds a write barrier — every logical write becomes 2–3 physical writes.
- **Block Size Mismatch:** FS block size = 4 KB, physical sector = 4 KB is optimal. Misalignment triggers *read-modify-write* penalties.

**4. Workload & Environmental Factors** (the request pattern)
- **Sequential vs Random I/O:** Sequential I/O can be 50–100× faster than random I/O on HDDs.
- **Read/Write Mix:** Writes incur parity updates in RAID and program latency in NAND.
- **Block Size:** Larger blocks → higher throughput, lower IOPS.
- **Temperature, Vibration, Power Quality:** All can throttle the device or induce re-reads.

> *Awarding Breakdown:* 1 mark per domain for listing factors, 1 mark for mechanism explanation per domain (3 domains × 1 + 1 domain extra = 4 marks), 3 marks for depth of mechanism.

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:** 8 HDDs in RAID 5, each at 7200 RPM, $T_{seek} = 8$ ms, $IOPS_{disk} = 180$.

**(i) Average Rotational Latency**

$$L_{rot} = \frac{30}{RPM} = \frac{30}{7200} = 0.004167 \text{ s} = 4.167 \text{ ms}$$

**(ii) Average Access Time**

$$T_{access} = T_{seek} + L_{rot} = 8 + 4.167 = 12.167 \text{ ms}$$

(Transfer time for a 4 KB random read is negligible at the microsecond level.)

**(iii) Theoretical Maximum IOPS per Drive**

$$IOPS = \frac{1000}{T_{access}} = \frac{1000}{12.167} \approx 82.18 \text{ IOPS}$$

> Note: The *rated* IOPS of 180 represents a typical *marketing* figure under sequential or queued conditions, not the theoretical random-IOPS ceiling. In this problem, we use the *measured* $T_{access}$ of 12.167 ms to derive the realistic random IOPS of ~82.

**(iv) RAID 5 Effective IOPS**

- **Read IOPS** (parallel across all disks, no parity operation):

$$IOPS_{R, RAID5} = N \times IOPS_{disk} = 8 \times 180 = 1440 \text{ read IOPS}$$

- **Write IOPS** (4× penalty per logical write):

$$IOPS_{W, RAID5} = \frac{N \times IOPS_{disk}}{4} = \frac{8 \times 180}{4} = 360 \text{ write IOPS}$$

**(v) Bottleneck Analysis for Random 4 KB Writes**

The bottleneck is **multifold**:
1. **Random seek overhead** dominates: each write requires 12.167 ms of mechanical positioning.
2. **RAID 5 write penalty** quadruples the physical I/O count, reducing the effective write IOPS to just **360** for the *entire* array of 8 disks.
3. **SATA queue depth limit** of 32 commands prevents the controller from reordering I/Os to minimize seeks.
4. **Journaling** (e.g., ext4) adds an additional ~2 physical writes per logical write.

**Recommendation:** For random 4 KB write workloads (typical of OLTP), replace the RAID 5 with **RAID 10** (eliminates parity penalty) and migrate to **SSDs** (eliminates seek/latency bottleneck). The IOPS gain is typically **50–100×**.

> *Awarding Breakdown:* 1 mark each for (i) and (ii), 1 mark for (iii), 2 marks for (iv) with both calculations, 2 marks for (v) bottleneck commentary with engineering insight.

---

### ✏️ QUESTION B `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper Set B, 14 Marks]` *(Alternative to Question A)*

**(a)** Explain the **RAID write penalty** for RAID levels 0, 1, 5, and 6. For each level, derive the **effective write IOPS** formula and explain the **trade-off** between redundancy and performance. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A storage system uses a **two-level cache hierarchy**:
- L1 cache: access time $T_{L1} = 0.05$ ms, hit ratio $H_1 = 0.7$
- L2 cache: access time $T_{L2} = 0.5$ ms, hit ratio $H_2 = 0.6$
- Main storage: access time $T_{storage} = 10$ ms

Calculate:
  - (i) The **effective access time** $T_{eff}$ of the system.
  - (ii) The **effective IOPS** the system can deliver.
  - (iii) The **percentage improvement** over a no-cache baseline.
  - (iv) What happens to $T_{eff}$ if $H_1$ drops to 0.4 (e.g., after a workload change)? Recalculate and comment. **(7 marks)**

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Part (a) — 7 Marks

**RAID Write Penalty Analysis:**

| RAID Level | Operations per Logical Write | Effective Write IOPS Formula | Redundancy | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| RAID 0 | 1 (striping only) | $IOPS_W = N \times IOPS_{disk}$ | None | Temp scratch, caches |
| RAID 1 | 2 (data + mirror) | $IOPS_W = \dfrac{N \times IOPS_{disk}}{2}$ | 1× mirror | Critical small DBs |
| RAID 5 | 4 (2 reads + 2 writes) | $IOPS_W = \dfrac{N \times IOPS_{disk}}{4}$ | 1 disk parity | General purpose |
| RAID 6 | 6 (3 reads + 3 writes) | $IOPS_W = \dfrac{N \times IOPS_{disk}}{6}$ | 2 disk parity | Large capacity arrays |

**Trade-off Discussion:**
- **RAID 0:** Maximum performance, zero fault tolerance — one disk failure = total data loss.
- **RAID 1:** Moderate write performance (50%), excellent read performance (N reads in parallel), excellent redundancy.
- **RAID 5:** Best *capacity efficiency* (only 1-disk overhead), but write-heavy workloads suffer the **4× penalty** — the dominant cost is the *old data read + old parity read* required to compute the new parity.
- **RAID 6:** Same as RAID 5 but tolerates 2 simultaneous failures; the **6× write penalty** makes it unsuitable for write-intensive OLTP.

> *Awarding Breakdown:* 2 marks for the table, 2 marks for the formula derivations, 3 marks for the trade-off commentary with engineering use cases.

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:** $T_{L1} = 0.05$ ms, $H_1 = 0.7$, $T_{L2} = 0.5$ ms, $H_2 = 0.6$, $T_{storage} = 10$ ms.

**(i) Effective Access Time**

Using the derived formula:

$$T_{eff} = T_{L1} + (1 - H_1) \cdot T_{L2} + (1 - H_1)(1 - H_2) \cdot T_{storage}$$

Substituting:

$$T_{eff} = 0.05 + (1 - 0.7)(0.5) + (1 - 0.7)(1 - 0.6)(10)$$

$$T_{eff} = 0.05 + (0.3)(0.5) + (0.3)(0.4)(10)$$

$$T_{eff} = 0.05 + 0.15 + 1.20 = 1.40 \text{ ms}$$

**(ii) Effective IOPS**

$$IOPS_{eff} = \frac{1000}{T_{eff}} = \frac{1000}{1.40} \approx 714 \text{ IOPS}$$

**(iii) Percentage Improvement over No-Cache Baseline**

Baseline (no cache) IOPS:

$$IOPS_{baseline} = \frac{1000}{T_{storage}} = \frac{1000}{10} = 100 \text{ IOPS}$$

Percentage improvement:

$$\Delta\% = \frac{714 - 100}{100} \times 100 = 614\%$$

> In other words, the cache hierarchy delivers a **7.14× speedup**.

**(iv) Sensitivity Analysis with $H_1 = 0.4$**

Recalculate $T_{eff}$:

$$T_{eff} = 0.05 + (1 - 0.4)(0.5) + (1 - 0.4)(1 - 0.6)(10)$$

$$T_{eff} = 0.05 + (0.6)(0.5) + (0.6)(0.4)(10)$$

$$T_{eff} = 0.05 + 0.30 + 2.40 = 2.75 \text{ ms}$$

New IOPS = $1000 / 2.75 \approx 364$ IOPS.

**Comment:** A drop in the L1 hit ratio from 0.7 to 0.4 (e.g., due to a workload change that defeats the prefetch heuristics) **nearly halves** the effective IOPS (from 714 to 364). The system is **highly sensitive** to L1 hit ratio because every L1 miss costs an extra 0.5 ms at L2 *and* a 60% chance of paying 10 ms at storage. **Engineering takeaway:** Invest in **better cache eviction policies (ARC, LIRS)** and **workload-aware prefetching** to maintain high $H_1$.

> *Awarding Breakdown:* 2 marks for (i), 1 mark for (ii), 1 mark for (iii), 3 marks for (iv) with both recalculation and engineering commentary.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students commonly lose marks on this topic:**
>
> 1. **Confusing IOPS with Throughput** — IOPS is *count* (ops/sec); Throughput is *volume* (MB/s). Mixing units in calculations is an instant 1-mark deduction.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the "1/2" in rotational latency** — Students often write $L_{rot} = \frac{60}{RPM}$ instead of $\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{60}{RPM}$. Always remember: it's *half* a rotation on average, not a full rotation.
>
> 3. **Misapplying RAID write penalty** — RAID 0 has *no* write penalty (penalty = 1, not 0). RAID 5 penalty is **4×** (not 2×). Memorize the table in Q-A above.
>
> 4. **Confusing seek time with access time** — Seek time is *only* the head movement. Access time = seek + rotational latency + transfer.
>
> 5. **Skipping units in derivations** — KTU examiners deduct 0.5 marks for missing units (ms vs. s) in intermediate steps. Always carry units.
>
> 6. **Forgetting that performance = f(Workload)** — A common essay question asks "Why is the same disk faster in benchmark than in production?" The answer is **workload behavior**, not the disk.
>
> 7. **Treating cache as a "magic" speedup** — Cache is *only* effective if the workload has **temporal or spatial locality**. Random-access workloads gain almost nothing from cache — KTU loves to test this nuance.

---

## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** Storage performance = $f(\text{Device}, \text{Interface}, \text{Host}, \text{Workload}, \text{Environment})$ — NOT a property of the disk alone.
- **Four Pillars:** (1) Device Physical, (2) Interface, (3) Host Software, (4) Workload/Environment.
- **Rotational Latency:** $L_{rot} = \dfrac{30}{RPM}$ seconds. Always divide by 2 first.
- **Access Time:** $T_{access} = T_{seek} + L_{rot} + T_{transfer}$.
- **IOPS vs Throughput:** $Th = IOPS \times B_{block}$. Use IOPS for small random I/O, Throughput for large sequential I/O.
- **Cache Formula (1-level):** $T_{eff} = T_{cache} + (1 - H) \cdot T_{storage}$.
- **Cache Formula (2-level):** $T_{eff} = T_{L1} + (1 - H_1) T_{L2} + (1 - H_1)(1 - H_2) T_{storage}$.
- **Availability:** $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$. "Five nines" = 99.999% = 5.26 min/year downtime.
- **Amdahl's Law:** $S = \dfrac{1}{(1-F) + F/N}$. Maximum possible speedup = $\dfrac{1}{1-F}$ regardless of $N$.
- **RAID Write Penalties:** RAID 0 = 1, RAID 1 = 2, RAID 5 = 4, RAID 6 = 6, RAID 10 = 2.
- **NVMe advantage:** 64K queues × 64K commands vs. SATA's 1 queue × 32 commands — orders-of-magnitude parallelism.
- **NAND Hierarchy:** SLC > MLC > TLC > QLC (in speed and endurance).
- **HDD Sequential-vs-Random Ratio:** ~50–100× (this collapses to ~5× in SSDs).
- **Workload Impact:** Random I/O is the *real-world killer* — always ask "what is the I/O pattern?"
- **Key Mnemonic for the 4 Pillars:** **"D-I-H-W"** — **D**evice, **I**nterface, **H**ost, **W**orkload.
- **Engineering Intuition:** Performance = bottleneck of the *weakest link* in the chain. Upgrading one component rarely helps if another is the chokepoint.
- **Exam Day Tip:** Always carry **units** in derivations. Always specify *which* RAID level when computing effective IOPS. Always state the *workload assumption* (random vs. sequential, read vs. write).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Management Protocols and Interfaces
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST867 - STORAGE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Management Protocols and Interfaces

# Management Protocols and Interfaces

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Management Protocols and Interfaces** in a storage system refer to the standardized communication frameworks, command sets, and software abstractions that enable a Storage Area Network (SAN) or storage controller to be **discovered, configured, monitored, administered, and provisioned** by administrators and management software, both locally and remotely.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the management stack is stratified into three cohesive layers:

1. **Protocol Layer (Wire-Level)**: Defines how commands and data traverse the physical medium (e.g., **SCSI Primary Command Set (SPCS)**, **ATA/SATA**, **NVMe**).
2. **Management/Service Layer**: Defines *what* information is exchanged about the storage resource (e.g., **CIM**, **SMI-S**, **SNMP MIBs**).
3. **Interface Layer (API/Console)**: Defines *how* administrators interact with the system (e.g., **REST APIs, CLI, GUI, Out-of-Band BMC/IPMI**).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The course code **PECST867** treats "Management Protocols and Interfaces" as the bridge between *raw device control* (Module 2/3 — RAID, FC, iSCSI) and *enterprise serviceability* (Module 5 — Backup, BC/DR). Mastery here guarantees a 14-mark question in the End Semester Examination (ESE).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Taxonomy for Examiners:**
> - **In-Band Management**: Control commands travel *over the same path* as user data (e.g., SCSI enclosure services — SES).
> - **Out-of-Band Management**: Control commands travel over a *dedicated side-channel* (e.g., IPMI over Ethernet, separate management LAN).
> - **Firmware/BMC Interface**: The lowest-level hardware controller that runs even when the OS is down (e.g., **IPMI 2.0**, **Redfish**).

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Building" Model

Imagine a large commercial skyscraper (your **Storage Array**):

- **Data Path (Tenant Movement)**: Tenants walk in and out via the main elevators daily — this is the **SCSI / NVMe read-write traffic**.
- **Management Protocols (Building Automation)**: A separate Building Management System (BMS) tracks elevator health, monitors HVAC, controls lights, and runs security cameras — this is **SNMP / SMI-S**.
- **Interfaces (Reception Desk & Mobile App)**: A reception desk (CLI) and a mobile app (GUI/REST API) let the building owner (admin) check status and order maintenance.

| Building Element | Storage Equivalent | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Main Elevator | SCSI / NVMe | Carries user data |
| Fire Alarm Panel | SNMP Traps | Pushes alerts |
| Maintenance Engineer (phone) | Out-of-Band BMC (IPMI) | Direct admin access |
| Building Management App | SMI-S / CIMOM | Centralized provisioning |
| Wi-Fi Calling System | iSCSI / FC over Ethernet | Encapsulated commands |

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition Builder:** A storage system with *no management protocol* is like a car with no dashboard — you can drive it, but you cannot see fuel, speed, or engine temperature. The richer the management interface, the higher the **Total Operational Efficiency (TOE)** of the data center.

---

### 1.3 Physical Constants, Standard Metrics & Bus Parameters

- **SCSI SAM-5 (SCSI Architecture Model — 5)** – the architectural foundation for **ANSI T10** standards.
- **NVMe 2.0** baseline throughput per queue: **128 × 64 KB entries = 8 KB of outstanding commands per submission queue** (default).
- **SMI-S** standardized on **DMTF DSP0226 (CIM)** with **WBEM** (Web-Based Enterprise Management) over **HTTP/HTTPS** (port 5988/5989).
- **SNMP**: Default UDP ports **161** (polling) and **162** (traps).
- **IPMI 2.0** default UDP ports: **623** (RMCP), leveraging the **Intelligent Platform Management Bus (IPMB)** at **I²C 100 kHz / 400 kHz**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Layered Architecture of Storage Management

The KTU 2024 scheme expects a layered model. Every layer exposes a **well-defined contract (interface)** to the layer above it.

### Layer 0 — Device & Bus Layer
- Raw electrical/physical transport: SAS/SATA, PCIe, FC, Ethernet.
- The disk or SSD responds to **block-level primitives**: Read(10), Write(10), Read(16), etc.

### Layer 1 — Command/Protocol Layer
- **SCSI Primary Command Set (SPCS)** and **ATA Command Set**.
- Defines *opcodes* and *Control Data Blocks (CDB)* — the binary "verb" sent to a device.
- Example: SCSI opcode `0x1E` = `PREVENT ALLOW MEDIUM REMOVAL` (used by SAN locking).

### Layer 2 — Transport Layer
- Encapsulates SCSI over networks: **FCoE (FC over Ethernet)**, **iSCSI (SCSI over TCP/IP)**, **NVMe-oF (NVMe over RDMA/TCP/FC)**.

### Layer 3 — Service/Management Layer
- **CIM (Common Information Model)** by DMTF.
- **SMI-S (Storage Management Initiative Specification)** by SNIA — built on top of CIM.
- **SNMP MIBs** (e.g., **FC-MIB**, **ISCSI-MIB**, **ENTITY-SENSOR-MIB**).

### Layer 4 — Interface/Presentation Layer
- **CLI** (vendor-specific), **GUI** (e.g., Unisphere, OnCommand), **RESTful APIs** (Redfish, vCenter APIs), **Web Portals**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** SMI-S was an attempt to make *heterogeneous* arrays (NetApp, EMC, Hitachi) manageable by *one* orchestrator. Adoption peaked around 2012–2018. Modern hyper-scalers have largely replaced it with **REST/Redfish + vendor-specific APIs**, but SMI-S remains a **key academic and competitive-exam topic**.

---

## 2.2 In-Band vs Out-of-Band Management — The Decisive Trade-Off

| Property | In-Band (e.g., SES, SCSI ENCL) | Out-of-Band (e.g., IPMI, Redfish) |
|---|---|---|
| Path Used | Same I/O fabric | Dedicated management LAN / side-band |
| Visibility when OS crashes | ❌ Lost | ✅ Preserved |
| Bandwidth Cost | Eats user I/O bandwidth | Isolated, no contention |
| Security Exposure | Same fabric as data | Isolated, easier to firewall |
| Latency | Higher (contended) | Lower (predictable) |
| KTU Example | SCSI Enclosure Services (SES) | IPMI 2.0 over Ethernet |

**Operational Rule of Thumb:**

$$\text{Availability}_{System} = 1 - (1 - A_{Data}) \times (1 - A_{Mgmt})$$

If **A_Data = 0.999** and **A_Mgmt = 0.999**, the compound availability is:

$$A_{System} = 1 - (0.001)^2 = 0.999999 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{"Five Nines of Diagnostics."}$$

---

## 2.3 SCSI Enclosure Services (SES) — The Heart of In-Band Management

SES is a **SCSI device type 0x0D (PROCESSOR device)** that is embedded in a **JBOD (Just a Bunch of Disks)** or intelligent enclosure. It exposes:

- **Element descriptors** (slots, power supplies, fans, temperature sensors).
- **Status descriptors** (each 4 bytes: element type code, status, severity).
- **Diagnostic pages**: `0x0A` (Additional Element Status), `0x0E` (Download Microcode), `0x07` (Enclosure Status).

**SES Command Flow:**

$$\text{Host} \xrightarrow{\text{RECEIVE DIAGNOSTIC RESULTS (10)}} \text{SES} \xrightarrow{\text{Status Page}} \text{Host}$$
$$\text{Host} \xrightarrow{\text{SEND DIAGNOSTIC (10)}} \text{SES} \xrightarrow{\text{Control Page}} \text{SES}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why KTU loves SES:** It allows the **RAID controller to monitor disk health (PFA — Predicted Failure Analysis), fan RPM, and PSU state** without requiring a separate Ethernet port on every JBOD — saving cost and complexity.

---

## 2.4 SNMP — The Network Management Workhorse

**SNMP (Simple Network Management Protocol)** uses a **Manager–Agent** model:

1. The **Manager** (NMS — e.g., Zabbix, SolarWinds) periodically *polls* the **Agent** (running on the storage controller) using **GET/GET-NEXT** requests.
2. The **Agent** holds an **MIB (Management Information Base)** — a hierarchical object tree defined in **ASN.1 / SMI** notation.
3. The **Agent** can proactively send **TRAPS** (UDP 162) on threshold breaches (e.g., `ifInErrors` rising, temperature exceeding 55 °C).

**SNMP Versions:**

| Version | Security | KTU 2024 Note |
|---|---|---|
| SNMPv1 | Community string (cleartext) | Obsolete |
| SNMPv2c | Community string (cleartext) | Bulk GET (GETBULK) |
| SNMPv3 | USM (auth + priv) — MD5/SHA + AES | **Industry standard today** |

**SNMP PDU Format (BER-encoded):**

| Field | Size (bytes) | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Version | 1 | 0=v1, 1=v2c, 3=v3 |
| Community / Security Params | Variable | Authentication context |
| PDU Type | 1 | 0xA0=GET, 0xA1=GET-NEXT, 0xA6=SET, 0xA7=TRAP, 0xA8=INFORM |
| Request-ID | 4 | Correlation |
| Error-Index | 1 | Points to failed varbind |
| VarBind List | Variable | OID + Value pairs |

---

## 2.5 CIM and SMI-S — The Object-Oriented Management Stack

**Common Information Model (CIM)** is a DMTF standard that defines a **schema** (a set of classes) for representing managed resources. It is purely **schema**, not a wire protocol.

**SMI-S (SNIA's Storage Management Initiative Specification)** is a **profile** over CIM that defines *which* CIM classes are mandatory, *how* they relate, and *which* operations are exposed.

**WBEM (Web-Based Enterprise Management)** is the *wire protocol* used to access CIM repositories. A server implementing WBEM is called a **CIMOM (CIM Object Manager)**.

**Workflow:**

$$\text{App (client)} \xrightarrow{\text{HTTP/XML}} \text{CIMOM} \xrightarrow{\text{Provider (C/C++)}} \text{Storage Array}$$

**Key SMI-S Profile (v1.7+) Classes:**

- `CIM_ComputerSystem` — represents the array.
- `CIM_StorageExtent` — represents a LUN.
- `CIM_StoragePool` — represents the unallocated capacity.
- `CIM_StorageVolume` — represents an allocated LUN.
- `CIM_StorageSetting` — represents a desired provisioning policy.
- `CIM_ConcreteJob` — tracks async operations (e.g., LUN creation).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** SMI-S enables a hypervisor (e.g., VMware vCenter) to *automatically* discover LUNs, create LUNs for a new VM, and apply QoS policies — all from a single vSphere screen. This is called **"Single-Pane-of-Glass Management."**

---

## 2.6 IPMI and Redfish — Modern Out-of-Band Standards

### IPMI 2.0
- Runs on the **Baseboard Management Controller (BMC)**.
- Independent of CPU, OS, and BIOS.
- Functions: power on/off, sensor reading, event log, SOL (Serial-over-LAN), SDR (Sensor Data Records), FRU inventory.

### Redfish (DMTF DSP0266)
- Successor to IPMI, but **RESTful (JSON over HTTP/HTTPS)**.
- Uses standard **HTTP verbs** (`GET`, `POST`, `PATCH`, `DELETE`).
- Examples:
  - `GET /redfish/v1/Systems` — list servers.
  - `PATCH /redfish/v1/Managers/BMC` — change BMC settings.
  - `POST /redfish/v1/Systems/{Id}/Actions/ComputerSystem.Reset` — power cycle.

**Redfish Data Model (OData $metadata):**

```json
{
  "@odata.id": "/redfish/v1/Systems/1",
  "Id": "1",
  "Name": "Storage-Controller-A",
  "PowerState": "On",
  "Status": {"Health": "OK", "State": "Enabled"},
  "StoragePools": {"@odata.id": "/redfish/v1/Systems/1/StoragePools"}
}
```

---

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet & Performance Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Metric | Expression | Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | SNMP Polling Cycle Time | $T_{poll} = N_{OIDs} \times \frac{P_{size}}{B_{link}} + RTT$ | seconds | $N$ = number of OIDs polled |
| 2 | Effective iSCSI Throughput | $T_{eff} = \frac{L_{payload}}{L_{payload} + H_{TCP} + H_{iSCSI} + H_{Eth}}$ | % | $H_{TCP}=20B,\; H_{iSCSI}=48B$ (BHS) |
| 3 | SCSI Command Latency | $L = t_{queue} + t_{protocol} + t_{xfer} + t_{controller}$ | ms | Sum of 4 sub-latencies |
| 4 | NVMe Queue Depth Utilization | $U_{q} = \frac{QD_{active}}{QD_{max}}$ | ratio | $QD_{max}=65536$ per namespace |
| 5 | SMI-S Job Polling | $J_{poll} = \frac{JobTime_{est}}{PollingInterval}$ | dimensionless | Adaptive throttling |
| 6 | Out-of-Band Availability Compound | $A_{total} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} A_i$ | ratio | Independent mgmt paths |
| 7 | MIB Walk Time | $T_{walk} = \sum_{j=1}^{M} \frac{S_{obj_j}}{R_{MIB}}$ | seconds | $S$=size of each object |
| 8 | SES Polling Overhead | $O_{SES} = \frac{P_{SES}}{T_{SES\_period}} \times P_{io}$ | % of I/O | $P_{SES}$ = bytes per poll |
| 9 | Effective SAN Bandwidth (with mgmt) | $B_{eff} = B_{raw} \times (1 - O_{mgmt})$ | MB/s | Subtract mgmt overhead |
| 10 | Redfish REST Round-Trip | $T_{req} = 2 \times RTT + \frac{S_{payload}}{B_{TLS}}$ | seconds | TLS adds crypto cost |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Tip:** Numerical problems in this module usually revolve around **iSCSI encapsulation overhead (Formula 2)** and **compound availability of redundant management paths (Formula 6)**. Memorize both with their boundary conditions.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — iSCSI Encapsulation Overhead (Worked)

> **Problem:** A 4 MB database page is transmitted over iSCSI. The iSCSI BHS is **48 bytes**, the TCP header is **20 bytes**, the IP header is **20 bytes**, and the Ethernet frame is **18 bytes** (header + trailer). Compute the **Protocol Efficiency** $\eta$.

### Step 1 — Identify every layer's contribution

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_{total} &= H_{iSCSI\_BHS} + H_{TCP} + H_{IP} + H_{Eth} \\
H_{total} &= 48 + 20 + 20 + 18 \\
H_{total} &= 106 \;\text{bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** Each header is added by its respective layer; the totals are summed in bytes.

### Step 2 — Convert payload to the same unit

$$
L_{payload} = 4 \;\text{MB} = 4 \times 1024 \times 1024 = 4\,194\,304 \;\text{bytes}
$$

### Step 3 — Apply the encapsulation formula

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta &= \frac{L_{payload}}{L_{payload} + H_{total}} \\
\eta &= \frac{4\,194\,304}{4\,194\,304 + 106} \\
\eta &= \frac{4\,194\,304}{4\,194\,410} \\
\eta &\approx 0.9999747 \\
\eta_{\%} &\approx 99.9975\%
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Interpret

The overhead is **only ≈ 0.0025%** because the payload is *enormous* compared to fixed headers. For **small I/O (4 KB)**:

$$
\eta_{4KB} = \frac{4096}{4096 + 106} = \frac{4096}{4202} \approx 97.48\%
$$

> **Conclusion:** iSCSI efficiency is **inversely proportional to payload size** for small packets. This is why database designers issue **large sequential I/Os** to amortize header cost.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Compound Availability of Redundant Management Paths

> **Problem:** A storage controller has two **independent** out-of-band management ports: a **1 Gbps Ethernet BMC** and a **serial console** with individual availabilities $A_1 = 0.999$ and $A_2 = 0.99$. Compute the probability that **at least one** path is alive.

### Step 1 — Compute failure probability of each path

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_1 &= 1 - A_1 = 1 - 0.999 = 0.001 \\
F_2 &= 1 - A_2 = 1 - 0.99 = 0.01
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute joint failure (independent events)

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{joint} &= F_1 \times F_2 = 0.001 \times 0.01 = 0.00001
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Compound availability

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{system} &= 1 - F_{joint} \\
A_{system} &= 1 - 0.00001 \\
A_{system} &= 0.99999 \;\;\text{(Four Nines plus)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Convert to downtime per year

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{year} &= (1 - A_{system}) \times 365 \times 24 \times 60 \;\text{minutes} \\
D_{year} &= 0.00001 \times 525\,600 \\
D_{year} &\approx 5.256 \;\text{minutes/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conclusion:** The redundancy improved availability by **a factor of 100** (from 0.999 to 0.99999), reducing expected downtime from **~8.76 hours/year** to **~5.26 minutes/year**. This is why dual management paths are mandatory in Tier-III/IV data centers.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — SNMP Polling Time for a Storage MIB

> **Problem:** A network manager polls a storage array's MIB containing **N = 250 OIDs**. Each `GET-REQUEST` fetches **20 OIDs**, and the round-trip time on a 1 Gbps link with average packet size **P = 200 bytes** is **RTT = 0.5 ms**. Compute total polling time $T_{poll}$.

### Step 1 — Number of round-trips required

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{RT} &= \left\lceil \frac{N_{OIDs}}{OIDs_{per\_req}} \right\rceil \\
N_{RT} &= \left\lceil \frac{250}{20} \right\rceil = \lceil 12.5 \rceil = 13
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Wire-time per packet

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{wire} &= \frac{P_{size}}{B_{link}} = \frac{200 \times 8}{10^9} \\
t_{wire} &= \frac{1600}{10^9} = 1.6 \;\mu s \;\text{(negligible vs. RTT)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Total polling time

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{poll} &= N_{RT} \times (RTT + t_{wire}) \\
T_{poll} &= 13 \times (0.5 \times 10^{-3} + 1.6 \times 10^{-6}) \\
T_{poll} &= 13 \times 0.5016 \times 10^{-3} \\
T_{poll} &\approx 6.521 \;\text{ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Effective polling rate

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{poll} &= \frac{N_{OIDs}}{T_{poll}} = \frac{250}{6.521 \times 10^{-3}} \\
R_{poll} &\approx 38\,335 \;\text{OIDs/second}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conclusion:** Modern 1 Gbps links support **tens of thousands of OID polls per second**, making fine-grained monitoring of large storage arrays feasible. Older 100 Mbps links would limit this to **~3,800 OIDs/second**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing a Management Protocol

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — Reasoning Question Gold Mine:** You will often be asked *"Which protocol is best for X scenario?"* Use the matrix below to justify your answer with **two-column reasoning**.

| Scenario | Recommended Protocol | Justification (Why) |
|---|---|---|
| Remote data center with intermittent network | Out-of-Band IPMI + Cellular modem | Works without OS or main fabric |
| Heterogeneous multi-vendor SAN | SMI-S / CIMOM | Vendor-neutral schema |
| Real-time disk failure alerts | SES + SNMP Trap | Push-based, low-latency |
| Cloud-native orchestration | Redfish REST API | JSON, integrates with Kubernetes/IaC |
| Legacy UNIX server management | SNMPv3 | Ubiquitous, mature tooling |
| Hypervisor-integrated provisioning | vCenter API (over CIM) | Deep integration with VMware |
| Low-cost JBOD without BMC | SES (in-band) | No extra silicon required |
| Container storage orchestration | CSI (Container Storage Interface) | gRPC-based, K8s-native |

> [!TIP]
> **Examination Heuristic:** The answer is *almost always* **"It depends on the operational requirement, vendor ecosystem, and SLA"** — but the **table above** gives you a concrete justification in one line per column. Examiners reward this kind of structured reasoning.

---

## 3.5 Comparative Latency of Storage Management Operations

The following **measured-order-of-magnitude latency table** is high-yield:

| Operation | Protocol | Typical Latency | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| SES Status Read | In-Band SCSI | **1–5 ms** | Contended with user I/O |
| SNMP GET (single OID) | UDP/IP | **0.5–2 ms** | LAN only |
| SNMP WALK (1000 OIDs) | UDP/IP | **300–800 ms** | Sequential polling |
| CIM GET (one instance) | HTTP/XML | **5–20 ms** | XML parsing overhead |
| CIM ENUMERATE (deep) | HTTP/XML | **50–500 ms** | Schema traversal |
| Redfish GET | HTTP/JSON | **3–10 ms** | Smaller payload than CIM |
| IPMI Get Sensor Reading | IPMB (I²C) | **1–10 ms** | Local bus, not LAN |
| Redfish PATCH (config) | HTTPS/JSON | **20–50 ms** | TLS crypto overhead |

> **Engineering Insight:** Notice how **Redfish (JSON)** is **~2× faster than CIM (XML)** for the same logical operation due to payload size. This is a key driver of the industry shift from SMI-S to Redfish post-2018.
