# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question 1. Define SCSI Enclosure Services (SES). List any four enclosure elements it can monitor. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**SCSI Enclosure Services (SES)** is an in-band management interface defined in the **ANSI T10 SES-3 standard** that allows a host to query the **operational status** and **control** the physical elements of a disk enclosure (JBOD or intelligent shelf) using standard **SCSI commands** (`RECEIVE DIAGNOSTIC RESULTS (10)` and `SEND DIAGNOSTIC (10)`) sent to a virtual **SCSI device of type 0x0D (PROCESSOR)**.

The four elements it can monitor are:
1. **Disk drives / slots** (presence, fault LED, activity LED).
2. **Power Supply Units (PSUs)** — input voltage, current, redundancy.
3. **Cooling fans** — RPM and fault status.
4. **Temperature sensors** — inlet/exhaust temperature thresholds.

> [!NOTE]
> SES is **vendor-neutral** and runs over the **same physical cable (SAS/FC)** as user data — making it the **most economical** monitoring solution for dense JBOD shelves.

---

### Question 2. Differentiate between In-Band and Out-of-Band storage management. State one advantage of each. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

| Property | In-Band Management | Out-of-Band Management |
|---|---|---|
| **Path** | Same fabric as data I/O | Dedicated management side-channel |
| **Examples** | SES, SCSI enclosure services, in-band CIM | IPMI 2.0, Redfish, separate mgmt LAN |
| **OS Dependency** | Requires host OS to issue SCSI commands | Independent of OS / hypervisor |
| **Advantage** | No extra hardware cost — uses existing cables | Continues to work **even when the server OS is crashed** |

**In-Band Advantage:** Zero additional hardware — works through the existing SAS/FC cabling.  
**Out-of-Band Advantage:** **Always-on management** — admin can reboot, collect logs, or re-image a server whose OS is unresponsive.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

### ⭐ Question A. (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **layered architecture of storage management**. List the role of each layer with a representative protocol. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **SMI-S architecture** in detail. Show how **CIM classes, CIMOM, and WBEM** interoperate to provision a LUN. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Storage management** is conventionally stratified into **four logical layers**, each providing services to the layer above it:

1. **Device/Bus Layer** — physical transport (SAS, SATA, FC, PCIe, NVMe).
2. **Command/Protocol Layer** — defines opcodes and CDBs (**SCSI SPC-5**, **ATA/ATAPI**, **NVMe 2.0**).
3. **Transport Layer** — encapsulates commands over networks (**iSCSI**, **FCoE**, **NVMe-oF**).
4. **Management/Service Layer** — describes the resource (**CIM**, **SMI-S**, **SNMP MIBs**).
5. **Interface Layer** — admin interaction (**CLI**, **GUI**, **REST API**, **Redfish**).

**Block Diagram (textual representation):**

```
┌────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│   Layer 5: Interface  (GUI / CLI / REST API)   │
├────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│   Layer 4: Management (SMI-S / CIM / SNMP)     │
├────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│   Layer 3: Transport   (iSCSI / FCoE / NVMe-oF) │
├────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│   Layer 2: Command     (SCSI SPC-5 / NVMe)      │
├────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│   Layer 1: Device/Bus  (SAS / FC / PCIe)       │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**Significance:** This layering ensures **separation of concerns** — a vendor can swap the transport (say, FC to iSCSI) without changing the management interface. The **reference diagram** in **Section 4.1** of these notes visualizes the same stack using Mermaid.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**SMI-S (Storage Management Initiative Specification)** is a **SNIA-defined profile** built on top of the **DMTF CIM schema**. Its purpose is to enable **multi-vendor, multi-array** management from a single application (e.g., VMware vCenter, Microsoft SCVMM).

**Three-Pillar Architecture:**

1. **CIM Schema** — the *vocabulary* (classes like `CIM_StorageVolume`, `CIM_StoragePool`).
2. **CIMOM (CIM Object Manager)** — the *server* that exposes the schema over the wire.
3. **WBEM (Web-Based Enterprise Management)** — the *wire protocol* (HTTP/HTTPS carrying XML CIM operations).

**LUN Provisioning Workflow (8 steps):**

1. Admin's tool sends an HTTP `POST` to the **CIMOM** asking to enumerate `CIM_ComputerSystem` instances → discovers available arrays.
2. CIMOM forwards the request to the **SMI-S Provider** (a vendor-supplied C/C++ shim).
3. Provider queries the array's internal **SOAP/REST API** for capacity info.
4. Provider returns `CIM_StoragePool` instances with available capacity.
5. Admin selects a pool and requests creation of a 100 GB LUN.
6. CIMOM invokes the **method `CreateOrModifyStorageVolume`** on the provider.
7. Array creates the LUN; provider returns a **`CIM_ConcreteJob`** with a Job-URI.
8. Admin **polls** the job until `JobState` becomes `Completed` (or subscribes via `CIM_Indication`).

**Engineering Benefit:** vCenter can use this single workflow to provision LUNs on **NetApp, EMC, and Hitachi** arrays simultaneously, achieving **"Single-Pane-of-Glass"** data center automation.

---

### ⭐ Question B. (14 Marks) — Alternative `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Explain the **SNMP protocol stack** with a neat block diagram. List any **four** standard MIBs relevant to storage management. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **IPMI 2.0** and **Redfish** as out-of-band management interfaces. Which is more suitable for a **modern hyperscale data center** and why? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**SNMP Architecture (Four Components):**

1. **Managed Device** — the storage controller running an SNMP **Agent** daemon.
2. **SNMP Agent** — software that holds the **MIB** and responds to manager requests.
3. **Network Management Station (NMS)** — the manager application (e.g., Zabbix).
4. **Management Information Base (MIB)** — a hierarchical **OID tree** of managed objects.

**Block Diagram (textual):**

```
┌──────────┐    GET / TRAP (UDP 161/162)   ┌──────────┐
│   NMS    │ <--------------------------->  │  Agent   │
│  Manager │                                │  (Array) │
└────┬─────┘                                └────┬─────┘
     │                                           │
     ▼                                           ▼
  MIB-II                                     Local MIB
  FC-MIB                                     ENTITY-SENSOR-MIB
  IF-MIB                                     ISCSI-MIB
```

**Four Storage-Relevant MIBs:**

| MIB Name | OID Prefix | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **FC-MIB** | `1.3.6.1.2.1.142` | Fibre Channel port counters |
| **ISCSI-MIB** | `1.3.6.1.2.1.141` | iSCSI session/target stats |
| **ENTITY-SENSOR-MIB** | `1.3.6.1.2.1.99` | Temperature, fan, voltage sensors |
| **HOST-RESOURCES-MIB** | `1.3.6.1.2.1.25` | CPU, memory, storage volumes |

**SNMP Operations (PDU types):** `GET`, `GET-NEXT`, `GET-BULK` (v2c+), `SET`, `RESPONSE`, `TRAP`, `INFORM`.

**Security:** **SNMPv3** adds **USM (User-based Security Model)** with **MD5/SHA** authentication and **AES/DES** privacy. It is the *only* version recommended by KTU for modern deployments.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

| Property | IPMI 2.0 | Redfish (DSP0266) |
|---|---|---|
| **Standard Body** | Intel-led (1998) | DMTF (2015) |
| **Wire Protocol** | RMCP over UDP 623, binary | **REST over HTTP/HTTPS, JSON** |
| **Data Format** | Binary SDR records | **JSON / OData** |
| **Transport** | Dedicated mgmt LAN + IPMB (I²C) | Standard Ethernet, no extra bus |
| **Authentication** | HMAC + password | TLS + sessions + OAuth (optional) |
| **Tooling** | `ipmitool`, vendor CLIs | `curl`, Python, Ansible, Kubernetes |
| **Extensibility** | Vendor OEM commands | Standard `OEM` resource extensions |
| **Payload Size** | Tiny (~50 B per sensor) | Medium (~300 B per resource) |
| **Schema Definition** | Hard-coded in firmware | Machine-readable **OData $metadata** |
| **Latency (typical)** | 1–10 ms | 3–15 ms (slightly higher due to TLS) |

**Verdict for a Modern Hyperscale Data Center: ✅ Redfish wins.**

**Justification (2-column reasoning):**

| Reason | Impact |
|---|---|
| **JSON + HTTP** integrates natively with **Ansible, Terraform, Python, and Kubernetes Operators** | Eliminates brittle custom CLIs |
| **OData $metadata** exposes a **discoverable, machine-readable schema** | Auto-generates client SDKs |
| **TLS + OAuth** is **cloud-grade** secure | Aligns with zero-trust models |
| **Event Service (SSE/WebSocket)** supports **streaming telemetry** | Enables AIOps and real-time dashboards |
| **Industry adoption by Dell, HPE, Lenovo, Supermicro** | Vendor-agnostic — no lock-in |

IPMI 2.0 remains ubiquitous in legacy hardware and is **still useful for low-level firmware management** (e.g., updating BIOS via `ipmitool chassis bootdev pxe`), but **Redfish is the strategic choice for new deployments**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read This Carefully!**
>
> 1. **Do NOT confuse SES with SMA** (Storage Management Appliance). SES is a *device*; an SMA is a *server* running CIMOM.
> 2. **Do NOT equate "In-Band" with "Internal HDD"** — In-Band refers to the **management traffic path**, not the device location.
> 3. **Do NOT claim "SMI-S uses REST/JSON"** — SMI-S uses **WBEM (HTTP+XML)**, not REST. Only **Redfish** is RESTful.
> 4. **In SNMP questions, always mention the UDP port** (161 for polling, 162 for traps). Examiners deduct 1 mark if missing.
> 5. **In availability questions, show the joint-failure formula explicitly**: $A_{series} = A_1 \times A_2$; $A_{parallel} = 1 - (1-A_1)(1-A_2)$.
> 6. **Avoid using "SMI" alone** — say **"SMI-S"** (Storage Management Initiative Specification) to avoid confusion with **SMI** (Structure of Management Information, an SNMP concept).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- [x] **Storage management has 4–5 layers**: Device, Command, Transport, Management, Interface.
- [x] **In-Band** = same cable as data (e.g., **SES over SAS/FC**); **Out-of-Band** = dedicated side-channel (e.g., **IPMI, Redfish**).
- [x] **SES (SCSI Enclosure Services)** uses SCSI `RECEIVE DIAGNOSTIC RESULTS` and `SEND DIAGNOSTIC` commands; **SCSI device type 0x0D**.
- [x] **SNMP** uses **UDP 161** (GET) and **UDP 162** (TRAP); **SNMPv3** = only secure version (USM, SHA, AES).
- [x] **MIB** = object tree, polled by NMS; **TRAP** = unsolicited alert pushed by agent.
- [x] **CIM** = schema; **CIMOM** = server; **WBEM** = wire protocol (HTTP+XML).
- [x] **SMI-S** = SNIA profile over CIM; enables multi-vendor **Single-Pane-of-Glass** management.
- [x] **IPMI 2.0** runs on the **BMC**; uses **RMCP over UDP 623**; independent of OS.
- [x] **Redfish (DSP0266)** is **REST + JSON**; uses standard **HTTP verbs**; supports TLS, OData, and streaming events.
- [x] **iSCSI overhead** = `H_{total} = H_{iSCSI} + H_{TCP} + H_{IP} + H_{Eth}`; efficiency drops for small payloads.
- [x] **Compound availability** of independent paths: $A_{system} = 1 - \prod (1 - A_i)$.
- [x] **Redfish latency ≈ 3–10 ms**; **SMI-S latency ≈ 5–500 ms** (XML parsing overhead).
- [x] **KTU 2024 favourite 14-mark pattern:** *"Compare X and Y. Justify which is suitable for scenario Z."*
- [x] **Always cite standard numbers** in answers: T10, DMTF, SNIA, ANSI — adds examiner confidence marks.
- [x] **Modern trend (post-2018):** Redfish is **displacing** SMI-S in hyperscale environments; legacy IPMI still in use for BMC.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic — "S-C-R-I-M" for the 5 layers:**
> **S**CSI command set → **C**IM/SMI-S schema → **R**edfish/REST interface → **I**PMI out-of-band → **M**anagement GUI/CLI.
> Walk top-down in the exam and the answer will flow naturally.
